topics.title: Introduction to Chemical Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Chemical Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Chemical Engineering** is the branch of engineering that applies the principles of **chemistry, physics, mathematics, biology, and economics** to design, produce, and use chemicals, materials, energy, and products in a safe, economical, and environmentally sustainable manner. A *Chemical Engineer* transforms raw materials (feedstocks) into useful products through carefully controlled physical, chemical, and biological processes.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (Course Code: **PBSFT304 – Chemical Process Principles**), the discipline is introduced as the science of *changing the identity, composition, or condition of matter* by orchestrating molecular-, phase-, and energy-level transformations at an industrial scale.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Chemical Engineering

The discipline rests on three fundamental pillars:

1. **Mass Transfer** – Movement of chemical species from one phase to another (e.g., distillation, absorption).
2. **Heat Transfer** – Movement of thermal energy across process equipment (e.g., heat exchangers, reboilers).
3. **Momentum Transfer (Fluid Mechanics)** – Movement of fluids and resultant forces (e.g., pumping, mixing).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The introductory module emphasizes the *broad role* of chemical engineering — its origin, evolution, and modern-day scope. Students must be able to differentiate between **Unit Operations** and **Unit Processes**, identify the **industries served by chemical engineers**, and articulate the **socio-economic and environmental responsibilities** of the profession.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for a First-Time Learner)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Modern Kitchen Chef!**
> Imagine you are a chef running a *cloud kitchen* serving 10,000 customers daily. You don't grow the vegetables (that's the *chemist/agro-scientist*), but you must:
> - Pick the *right ingredients* in the correct ratio (**feedstock selection**),
> - Slice, boil, fry, and mix them using *standard appliances* — a chopper, an oven, a mixer (**Unit Operations**),
> - Ensure the taste changes chemically (caramelization, fermentation) at the *right temperature and time* (**Unit Processes / Reaction Engineering**),
> - Maintain hygiene, follow a recipe rigorously (**Process Control & Safety**),
> - And deliver the food cheaply enough to make a profit (**Economics**).
> A Chemical Engineer is essentially an *industrial-scale chef of matter* — converting crude raw materials into refined, useful products via standardized "kitchen tools" (unit operations) and controlled chemical transformations (unit processes).

## 1.4 Historical Evolution (Brief Timeline)

| Era | Milestone | Significance |
|-----|-----------|--------------|
| ~2000 BCE | Mesopotamian perfume distillation | Earliest recorded separation technique |
| 1790s | Nicolas Leblanc Soda Ash Process | First *industrial chemical* process |
| 1880s | George E. Davis coined "Chemical Engineering" | Manchester, UK — birth of the discipline |
| 1888 | *Unit Operations* concept formalized (Davis) | Foundation of modern ChE education |
| 1923 | A.D. Little coined "Unit Operations" | MIT-led curriculum revolution |
| 1901–1920 | First ChE departments (MIT, 1888; UMIST, 1887) | Global academic recognition |
| 1940s | Petroleum refining boom | Mass-transfer & reactor design matured |
| 1970s | Biochemical & Polymer Engineering | Diversification beyond bulk chemicals |
| 2000s+ | Sustainability, Green Chemistry, AI-driven process design | Modern scope |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Scope and Definition Breakdown

Chemical engineering as taught in KTU is structured around the **transformation of matter and energy**. To systematically analyze a chemical process, engineers typically follow the *process flow*:

- **Raw Material → Pre-treatment → Reactor → Separation → Purification → Product**

A chemical engineer must answer, for every step:

1. *What* is happening physically or chemically? (**Phenomenon identification**)
2. *How fast* is it happening? (**Rate / Kinetics**)
3. *How much* energy is required? (**Thermodynamics**)
4. *How large* must the equipment be? (**Sizing via material & energy balances**)

## 2.2 Unit Operations vs. Unit Processes — The Core Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Operation (UO):** A *physical* change in the system. No new chemical species is formed. Examples: distillation, filtration, evaporation, drying, mixing, crushing, sedimentation.
>
> **Unit Process (UP):** A *chemical* change in the system. New molecular species are formed. Examples: oxidation, reduction, polymerization, nitration, hydrolysis, fermentation.

| Aspect | Unit Operation | Unit Process |
|---|---|---|
| Nature of change | Physical | Chemical |
| New molecules formed? | No | Yes |
| Examples | Distillation, Drying, Filtration | Combustion, Cracking, Polymerization |
| Governing laws | Mass/Heat/Momentum transfer | Reaction kinetics, Thermodynamics |
| Type of equipment | Heat exchangers, Columns, Pumps | Reactors, Furnaces, Fermenters |

## 2.3 The Six (Six + New) Major Unit Operations in KTU Curriculum

The KTU 2024 syllabus clusters unit operations into the following families:

1. **Fluid Mechanics Operations** — Pumping, mixing, fluidization
2. **Heat Transfer Operations** — Heat exchangers, evaporators, condensers
3. **Mass Transfer Operations** — Distillation, absorption, extraction, drying, humidification
4. **Mechanical Separations** — Filtration, sedimentation, centrifugation
5. **Reactor Operations** (Unit Processes) — CSTR, PFR, batch reactors
6. **Process Control Operations** — Sensors, controllers, feedback loops

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Conceptual Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> While the *Introduction* topic is mostly conceptual, the foundational *Process Variables* introduced here are reused throughout the course. Memorize the conversions below.

| Concept / Variable | Symbol / Formula | Units (SI) | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Molar mass | $M$ | kg / kmol | Mass per kmol of substance |
| Mass fraction of species *i* | $x_i^{(m)} = \dfrac{m_i}{\sum m_j}$ | dimensionless | Sum over all species = **1** |
| Mole fraction of species *i* | $x_i = \dfrac{n_i}{\sum n_j}$ | dimensionless | Sum = **1** |
| Average molecular weight of mixture | $M_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum (n_i \cdot M_i)}{\sum n_i} = \dfrac{1}{\sum (x_i^{(m)} / M_i)}$ | kg / kmol | Critical for ideal gas calculations |
| Density | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | kg / m³ | Mass per unit volume |
| Specific gravity | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{substance}}{\rho_{water @ 4^\circ C}}$ | dimensionless | Water density = **1000 kg/m³** |
| Specific volume | $\hat{V} = \dfrac{1}{\rho} = \dfrac{V}{m}$ | m³ / kg | Inverse of density |
| Molar flow rate | $\dot{n} = \dfrac{dn}{dt}$ | kmol / s | Used in material balances |
| Volumetric flow rate | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{dV}{dt}$ | m³ / s | Used in fluid mechanics |
| Mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \dot{V}$ | kg / s | Conservation: $\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}$ (steady state) |
| Flow rate conversion | $\dot{n} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{M_{avg}}$ | kmol / s | Bridge between mass and molar flow |
| Temperature conversion | $T(K) = T(^\circ C) + 273.15$ | K | Absolute scale for gas equations |
| Pressure conversion | $1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 760 \text{ mm Hg}$ | kPa | Standard atmospheric pressure = **101.325 kPa** |
| Universal gas constant | $R = 8.314$ | kJ / (kmol · K) | Used in ideal gas law: $PV = nRT$ |
| Ideal Gas Law | $PV = nRT$ | Pa · m³ = J | Valid at low pressure / high T |
| Specific gravity of gas | $SG_{gas} = \dfrac{M_{gas}}{M_{air}} = \dfrac{M_{gas}}{28.97}$ | dimensionless | Air molar mass ≈ **28.97 kg/kmol** |
| Standard Temperature & Pressure (STP) | $T = 273.15$ K, $P = 101.325$ kPa | — | 1 kmol ideal gas occupies **22.414 m³** at STP |
| Normal Temperature & Pressure (NTP) | $T = 293.15$ K, $P = 101.325$ kPa | — | 1 kmol occupies **24.05 m³** at NTP |

> [!WARNING]
> **Memorization Trap:** Students often confuse **STP** and **NTP**. KTU board questions frequently use NTP (293.15 K) for chemical engineering calculations. Always check the temperature value given in the problem statement.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of These Concepts

- **Petroleum Refineries:** Crude oil is fractionated using *distillation* (UO) and *cracking* (UP).
- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** *Batch reactors* (UP) and *crystallization* (UO) dominate.
- **Food Processing:** *Pasteurization* (UO) and *fermentation* (UP) are cornerstones.
- **Water Treatment:** *Sedimentation, filtration* (UO) and *chlorination* (UP) are essential.
- **Semiconductor Manufacturing:** *Chemical Vapor Deposition* (CVD) is a Unit Process.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The *Introduction* module is largely conceptual, but the following **analytical derivations** are the foundational mathematics you will use in every chapter of PBSFT304. They are also common **2-mark/3-mark derivation-style questions** in KTU university exams.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between Mass Fraction and Mole Fraction

Let a mixture contain $c$ components. Define:

- $n_i$ = number of moles of component $i$
- $m_i = n_i \cdot M_i$ = mass of component $i$
- $M_i$ = molar mass of component $i$

**Total mass and total moles of mixture:**

$$M_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} n_i \cdot M_i$$

$$n_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} n_i$$

**Mass fraction of component $i$:**

$$x_i^{(m)} = \dfrac{m_i}{M_{total}} = \dfrac{n_i \cdot M_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{c} n_j \cdot M_j}$$

**Mole fraction of component $i$:**

$$x_i = \dfrac{n_i}{n_{total}} = \dfrac{n_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{c} n_j}$$

**Conversion (mass fraction → mole fraction):**

Divide numerator and denominator of the mass fraction expression by $\sum n_j$:

$$x_i^{(m)} = \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{n_i}{\sum n_j}\right) \cdot M_i}{\left(\dfrac{\sum n_j M_j}{\sum n_j}\right)} = \dfrac{x_i \cdot M_i}{M_{avg}}$$

Rearranging for the mole fraction:

$$x_i = \dfrac{x_i^{(m)} / M_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{c} \left(x_j^{(m)} / M_j\right)}$$

This is the *bridge equation* that you will use repeatedly.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average Molecular Weight of a Mixture

Starting from the definition of molar mass, $M_i = \dfrac{m_i}{n_i}$:

$$M_{avg} = \dfrac{m_{total}}{n_{total}} = \dfrac{\sum n_i M_i}{\sum n_i}$$

Substituting $n_i = x_i \cdot n_{total}$:

$$M_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum x_i \cdot n_{total} \cdot M_i}{n_{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} x_i \cdot M_i$$

**In terms of mass fractions** (substitute $x_i = \dfrac{x_i^{(m)} / M_i}{\sum x_j^{(m)} / M_j}$):

$$M_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{c} \left(x_i^{(m)} / M_i\right)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Conversion of Mass Flow Rate ↔ Molar Flow Rate

For a stream with mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ and average molecular weight $M_{avg}$:

$$\dot{m} = \dot{n} \cdot M_{avg}$$

Therefore:

$$\dot{n} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{M_{avg}}$$

For a gas stream, using ideal gas law in the differential flow form, $P\dot{V} = \dot{n}RT$:

$$\dot{n} = \dfrac{P \dot{V}}{RT} = \dfrac{y_i P \dot{V}}{RT}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-Step Logic Recap:** This is the *rate analog* of the static ideal gas law. Pressure $P$, volumetric flow rate $\dot{V}$, and temperature $T$ of the stream combine to give molar flow rate $\dot{n}$ directly. KTU frequently tests this with a stream table.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical Stream Table Format (KTU Board Convention)

| Component | $M_i$ (kg/kmol) | $\dot{n}_{in}$ (kmol/h) | $x_{i,in}$ | $\dot{m}_{in}$ (kg/h) | $x_{i,in}^{(m)}$ | $\dot{n}_{out}$ | $x_{i,out}$ | $\dot{m}_{out}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | … | … | … | … | … | … | … | … |
| 2 | … | … | … | … | … | … | … | … |
| Total | $M_{avg}$ | $\sum \dot{n}$ | 1.00 | $\sum \dot{m}$ | 1.00 | $\sum \dot{n}$ | 1.00 | $\sum \dot{m}$ |

This table is the *first step* in any material balance problem you will solve in subsequent modules.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Demonstration (Concept Application)

**Problem:** A gas mixture contains 30 mol% N₂, 50 mol% O₂, and 20 mol% CO₂. Calculate:
(a) The average molecular weight of the mixture.
(b) The density of the mixture at 25 °C and 101.325 kPa.

**Solution:**

Molar masses: $M_{N_2} = 28$ kg/kmol, $M_{O_2} = 32$ kg/kmol, $M_{CO_2} = 44$ kg/kmol.

**Part (a):**

$$M_{avg} = \sum x_i M_i = (0.30)(28) + (0.50)(32) + (0.20)(44)$$

$$M_{avg} = 8.4 + 16.0 + 8.8 = 33.2 \text{ kg/kmol}$$

**Part (b):** Using the ideal gas law in molar density form:

$$\rho = \dfrac{P \cdot M_{avg}}{R \cdot T} = \dfrac{101.325 \text{ kPa} \times 33.2 \text{ kg/kmol}}{8.314 \text{ kJ/(kmol·K)} \times 298.15 \text{ K}}$$

$$\rho = \dfrac{3363.99}{2478.83} = 1.357 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always show units in *every step*. Board examiners will deduct marks if a unit is missing in any intermediate line. A 14-mark numerical typically gives 2 marks for the *correct setup* with units and 2–3 marks for the *correct final numerical answer*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Scope of Chemical Engineering (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["FEEDSTOCKS"] --> nodeB["PRE TREATMENT UNIT"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["REACTOR"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["SEPARATION TRAIN"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["PURIFICATION"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["FINAL PRODUCT"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["QUALITY CONTROL LAB"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["PACKAGING AND STORAGE"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["MARKET DISTRIBUTION"]

    nodeJ["RECYCLE STREAM"] -.-> nodeC
    nodeD -.-> nodeJ
    nodeK["WASTE TREATMENT"] -.-> nodeF
    nodeC -.-> nodeK
    nodeL["ENERGY MANAGEMENT"] -.-> nodeC
    nodeC -.-> nodeL
    nodeL -.-> nodeD
```

**Caption:** The *block-level process architecture* of a typical chemical plant. Solid arrows denote the *main process flow*, while dashed arrows denote *ancillary flows* (recycle, waste, energy) that the chemical engineer must design, integrate, and optimize.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Unit Operations vs. Unit Processes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["CHEMICAL ENGINEERING DISCIPLINE"] --> nodeB["UNIT OPERATIONS PHYSICAL CHANGES"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["UNIT PROCESSES CHEMICAL CHANGES"]

    nodeB --> nodeB1["FLUID MECHANICS"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["HEAT TRANSFER"]
    nodeB --> nodeB3["MASS TRANSFER"]
    nodeB --> nodeB4["MECHANICAL SEPARATIONS"]

    nodeC --> nodeC1["REACTION KINETICS"]
    nodeC --> nodeC2["REACTOR DESIGN"]
    nodeC --> nodeC3["CATALYSIS"]
    nodeC --> nodeC4["BIOPROCESSES"]
```

**Caption:** The two major sub-domains of Chemical Process Principles. KTU module structure follows this classification — *Unit Operations* form the bulk of Modules 2–4, while *Unit Processes / Reaction Engineering* constitute Module 5.

## 4.3 Application Domains of a Chemical Engineer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["CHEMICAL ENGINEER ROLES"] --> nodeB["PETROLEUM AND ENERGY"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["PHARMACEUTICALS"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["FOOD AND AGRI PROCESSING"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["WATER AND ENVIRONMENT"]
    nodeA --> nodeF["POLYMERS AND MATERIALS"]
    nodeA --> nodeG["SEMICONDUCTORS AND ELECTRONICS"]
    nodeA --> nodeH["BIOTECHNOLOGY AND NANOTECH"]
```

**Caption:** The *diverse industrial landscape* a chemical engineer can work in. KTU board questions often ask: *"List the major industries served by chemical engineers"* — this diagram gives a complete reference.

## 4.4 The Chemical Engineer's Tool-Kit (Modern Perspective)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["CORE CHEMICAL ENGINEERING TOOLKIT"] --> nodeB["MATERIAL AND ENERGY BALANCES"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["THERMODYNAMICS"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["UNIT OPERATIONS AND PROCESSES"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["PROCESS CONTROL AND SAFETY"]
    nodeA --> nodeF["COMPUTATIONAL TOOLS ASPEN HYSYS MATLAB"]
    nodeA --> nodeG["SUSTAINABILITY AND GREEN CHEMISTRY"]
    nodeA --> nodeH["ECONOMICS AND PROJECT MANAGEMENT"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Schematic Rationale (Why this architecture?):** The above diagrams use *block-level functional architecture* rather than physical-equipment drawings, because the introduction topic spans a *very wide* scope — equipment-level schematics would be too narrow. Block diagrams convey the *systems-level perspective* that KTU examiners expect in 14-mark answer scripts.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Chemical Engineering. Discuss the role of a chemical engineer in modern industry. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Chemical Engineering** is the branch of engineering that deals with the design, operation, and optimization of industrial processes in which raw materials undergo physical, chemical, or biological changes to yield value-added products. The discipline integrates principles of **chemistry, physics, mathematics, biology, and economics**.

**Role of a Chemical Engineer in modern industry (3 key points):**
1. **Process Design:** Develops efficient, safe, and economically viable manufacturing processes for chemicals, fuels, pharmaceuticals, food, and materials.
2. **Plant Operation & Optimization:** Monitors, troubleshoots, and improves running plants to maximize yield, minimize energy consumption, and ensure product quality.
3. **Sustainability & Safety:** Implements green chemistry principles, designs effluent treatment systems, enforces process safety standards (OSHA, HAZOP), and ensures compliance with environmental regulations.

### Question 2: Differentiate between Unit Operations and Unit Processes. Give two examples of each. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| S. No. | Unit Operation | Unit Process |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Involves **physical** change only | Involves **chemical** change |
| 2 | No new molecular species formed | New molecular species formed |
| 3 | Governed by transfer phenomena (mass, heat, momentum) | Governed by reaction kinetics & thermodynamics |
| 4 | Examples: **Distillation, Filtration, Evaporation, Drying** | Examples: **Combustion, Polymerization, Neutralization, Cracking** |
| 5 | Equipment: Heat exchangers, Columns | Equipment: Reactors, Furnaces |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part B questions in a module are answered in **choice pairs**. Below we provide the two alternative questions: **Question A** *or* **Question B**.

### Question A (14 Marks): Detailed Answer

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain in detail the historical evolution of Chemical Engineering as a discipline. Discuss the contributions of George E. Davis, Arthur Amos Noyes, and Warren K. Lewis. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe with neat block diagrams the role of unit operations and unit processes in a typical chemical industry, and discuss their importance with reference to the petroleum refining industry. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Historical Evolution of Chemical Engineering:**

**Step 1 — Industrial Chemistry Era (Pre-1880):** The earliest chemical processes were driven by empirical knowledge, with *Leblanc's soda ash process (1791)* and *Solvay process (1861s)* being prominent examples. Engineering was largely artisanal.

**Step 2 — George E. Davis (Manchester, 1880s):** George Davis delivered a series of 12 lectures in **1887** at the Manchester School of Technology, systematically grouping chemical industry processes. He is widely regarded as the *father of chemical engineering*. His 1901 book *"A Handbook of Chemical Engineering"* formalized the discipline.

**Step 3 — Arthur Amos Noyes & Warren K. Lewis (USA, 1908–1920):** Noyes at MIT recognized that *chemical engineering was not applied chemistry, but a discipline of its own*. Lewis, in collaboration with *William H. McAdams* and *Edwin R. Gilliland*, authored the landmark textbook *"Principles of Chemical Engineering"* (1923) which introduced *material and energy balances* as the foundation. They formalized the **Unit Operations** concept as the curriculum backbone.

**Step 4 — Modern Era (Post-1950):** The discipline expanded to include *biochemical engineering, polymer engineering, process control, computer-aided design (ASPEN, HYSYS), and sustainable/green process engineering*.

| Pioneer | Country | Year | Key Contribution |
|---|---|---|---|
| George E. Davis | UK | 1887 | Coined "Chemical Engineering" |
| Arthur Amos Noyes | USA | 1920 | Identified ChE as a *distinct* discipline |
| Warren K. Lewis | USA | 1923 | "Principles of Chemical Engineering" textbook |

**(b) Block Diagram of Unit Operations & Unit Processes in Petroleum Refining:**

**Crude Oil → Desalting (UO) → Atmospheric Distillation (UO) → Vacuum Distillation (UO) → Cracking (UP – Thermal/Catalytic) → Reforming (UP) → Treating (UO) → Blending (UO) → Refined Products**

**Importance:**
- *Distillation (UO)* is the largest *energy-consuming* operation in a refinery.
- *Catalytic Cracking (UP)* converts heavy hydrocarbons into gasoline, which is a *value-addition* step.
- Without the synergy of UO and UP, a refinery cannot meet octane number, sulfur content, or yield specifications.
- A typical modern refinery integrates *over 30 unit operations and 10+ unit processes* in a single integrated plant.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**(a)** Define the terms *mass fraction*, *mole fraction*, and *average molecular weight*. Derive the relationship between mass fraction and mole fraction for a multi-component mixture. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A gas mixture contains 40 mol% Methane (CH₄) and 60 mol% Ethane (C₂H₆). Calculate:
   (i) The average molecular weight of the mixture.
   (ii) The density of the mixture at 30 °C and 105 kPa.
   (iii) The mass fraction of each component. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Definitions and Derivation:**

**Mass fraction ($x_i^{(m)}$):** Ratio of the mass of component $i$ to the total mass of the mixture.

$$x_i^{(m)} = \dfrac{m_i}{\sum m_j}$$

**Mole fraction ($x_i$):** Ratio of the moles of component $i$ to the total moles.

$$x_i = \dfrac{n_i}{\sum n_j}$$

**Average molecular weight ($M_{avg}$):** Total mass divided by total moles of the mixture.

**Derivation (mass fraction → mole fraction):**

Starting from $m_i = n_i \cdot M_i$ and dividing numerator and denominator by $\sum n_j$:

$$x_i^{(m)} = \dfrac{n_i \cdot M_i}{\sum n_j \cdot M_j} = \dfrac{x_i \cdot M_i}{M_{avg}}$$

Solving for the mole fraction:

$$x_i = \dfrac{x_i^{(m)} / M_i}{\sum_j \left(x_j^{(m)} / M_j\right)}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $x_{CH_4} = 0.40$, $x_{C_2H_6} = 0.60$, $M_{CH_4} = 16$ kg/kmol, $M_{C_2H_6} = 30$ kg/kmol, $T = 303.15$ K, $P = 105$ kPa, $R = 8.314$ kJ/(kmol·K).

**(i) Average molecular weight:**

$$M_{avg} = (0.40)(16) + (0.60)(30) = 6.4 + 18.0 = 24.4 \text{ kg/kmol}$$

**(ii) Density using ideal gas law:**

$$\rho = \dfrac{P \cdot M_{avg}}{R \cdot T} = \dfrac{105 \times 24.4}{8.314 \times 303.15}$$

$$\rho = \dfrac{2562}{2520.42} = 1.0165 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**(iii) Mass fractions:**

For CH₄: $x_{CH_4}^{(m)} = \dfrac{x_{CH_4} \cdot M_{CH_4}}{M_{avg}} = \dfrac{0.40 \times 16}{24.4} = \dfrac{6.4}{24.4} = 0.2623$

For C₂H₆: $x_{C_2H_6}^{(m)} = \dfrac{0.60 \times 30}{24.4} = \dfrac{18.0}{24.4} = 0.7377$

**Verification:** $0.2623 + 0.7377 = 1.0000$ ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in 'Introduction to Chemical Engineering' questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating STP and NTP (–1 to –2 marks):** Many students use 22.414 m³/kmol for NTP. The correct NTP molar volume at 20 °C is 24.05 m³/kmol. *Always re-read the problem statement.*
> 2. **Forgetting units in intermediate steps (–1 mark per KTU's strict marking scheme):** A calculation without units is considered incomplete. Show units on *every* line.
> 3. **Mixing up mass and mole fractions (–2 marks):** A 14-mark derivation question can lose half its marks if the student confuses which fraction is dimensionless. Memorize the *bridge equation*.
> 4. **Writing generic historical answers (–1 to –2 marks):** Examiners reward *specific years and names* (Davis 1887, Lewis & McAdams 1923). Vague statements like "Chemical engineering was developed in the 19th century" fetch partial credit only.
> 5. **Omitting block diagrams in 14-mark answers (–2 to –3 marks):** KTU examiners explicitly award marks for neat, labeled flow diagrams. Always include at least one block diagram in a 14-mark answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Introduction to Chemical Engineering"**

- ✅ **Definition:** Chemical Engineering = design of processes that transform matter and energy at an industrial scale using chemistry + physics + economics.
- ✅ **Three Pillars:** Mass transfer, Heat transfer, Momentum transfer.
- ✅ **Unit Operation (UO):** Physical change — examples: distillation, filtration, evaporation, drying.
- ✅ **Unit Process (UP):** Chemical change — examples: combustion, polymerization, cracking.
- ✅ **Father of Chemical Engineering:** George E. Davis (1887 lectures, Manchester).
- ✅ **Landmark Textbook:** *"Principles of Chemical Engineering"* by Lewis, McAdams & Gilliland (1923).
- ✅ **Mole fraction sum:** $\sum x_i = 1$.
- ✅ **Mass fraction sum:** $\sum x_i^{(m)} = 1$.
- ✅ **Average molecular weight:** $M_{avg} = \sum x_i M_i$ (mole basis).
- ✅ **Inverse relation:** $M_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{\sum \left(x_i^{(m)} / M_i\right)}$ (mass basis).
- ✅ **Conversion bridge:** $x_i^{(m)} = \dfrac{x_i \cdot M_i}{M_{avg}}$.
- ✅ **Ideal Gas Law:** $PV = nRT$, with $R = 8.314$ kJ/(kmol·K).
- ✅ **STP:** 273.15 K, 101.325 kPa, molar volume = **22.414 m³/kmol**.
- ✅ **NTP:** 293.15 K, 101.325 kPa, molar volume = **24.05 m³/kmol**.
- ✅ **Flow rate conversion:** $\dot{n} = \dot{m} / M_{avg} = P\dot{V}/(RT)$.
- ✅ **Industries served:** Petroleum, Pharma, Food, Water, Polymers, Semiconductors, Biotechnology.
- ✅ **Modern tool-kit:** Material & energy balances, thermodynamics, process control, ASPEN/HYSYS, sustainability.

> **End of Module 1 — Introduction to Chemical Engineering. Proceed to Module 2: Material Balances for next set of notes.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Chemical Calculations - Mole Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Chemical Calculations - Mole Concept

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mole (Unit: mol):** A mole is the SI unit of the amount of substance. One mole of any substance contains exactly **6.02214076 × 10²³** elementary entities (atoms, molecules, ions, electrons, or other particles). This number is the fixed numerical value of the **Avogadro constant (Nₐ)** when expressed in mol⁻¹.

The term **"mole"** (symbol: **mol**) was adopted by the International System of Units (SI) in 1971 and is the seventh base quantity. It is defined as:

$$1 \text{ mol} = 6.02214076 \times 10^{23} \text{ particles}$$

This definition is **exact** (no uncertainty) since 2019 when SI base units were redefined in terms of fundamental constants.

### Key Related Terms

- **Avogadro's Number (Nₐ):** The numerical value of Avogadro's constant ≈ **6.022 × 10²³**
- **Avogadro's Constant (Nₐ):** Same numerical value with units of **mol⁻¹**
- **Amount of Substance (n):** The number of moles of a given sample
- **Molar Mass (M):** Mass of one mole of a substance, expressed in **g/mol** or **kg/kmol**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The numerical value 6.022 × 10²³ is the *exact* value by definition. Students must not write it as 6.023 × 10²³ (older approximation) in the 2024 scheme.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Chemist's Dozen"

Imagine you walk into a grocery store to buy eggs. You don't say "give me 12 eggs" — you say "give me **one dozen**." A *dozen* is just a counting group of 12.

Similarly, a **mole is simply a counting group for atoms and molecules**, but the group size is astronomically larger — **6.022 × 10²³**.

| Everyday Unit | Group Size | Used For |
|---------------|------------|----------|
| Pair | 2 | Shoes, socks |
| Dozen | 12 | Eggs, bananas |
| Gross | 144 | Pencils |
| Ream | 500 | Paper sheets |
| **Mole** | **6.022 × 10²³** | **Atoms, molecules, ions** |

> [!TIP]
> **Why such a huge number?** Because atoms and molecules are *incredibly* tiny. To get a weighable amount (in grams), you need a colossal number of them. This is why chemistry works in "moles" — it bridges the invisible world of atoms with the tangible world of grams.

### Real-World Scale of a Mole
- 1 mole of water (H₂O) = **18 mL** (about a small shot glass)
- 1 mole of table salt (NaCl) = **58.44 g** (about 9 teaspoons)
- 1 mole of gold (Au) = **197 g** (a small gold coin)
- 1 mole of tennis balls would cover the Earth in a layer several km thick!

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Relationships of the Mole Concept

### 2.1.1 Mass–Mole–Particle Triad

The mole sits at the center of three interchangeable quantities:

**Step 1 — Moles from Mass:**
$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$
where $m$ = mass of substance (g), $M$ = molar mass (g/mol)

**Step 2 — Particles from Moles:**
$$N = n \times N_A$$
where $N$ = number of particles, $N_A$ = Avogadro's number

**Step 3 — Mass from Particles:**
$$m = \frac{N \times M}{N_A}$$

### 2.1.2 Molar Mass Calculation Principles

- **Atomic Mass (A):** Mass of a single atom in atomic mass units (u). On the periodic table, atomic masses are given.
- **Molecular Mass (Mₘ):** Sum of atomic masses of all atoms in a molecule.
- **Molar Mass (M):** Numerically equal to molecular mass but expressed in **g/mol**.

**Example: H₂SO₄**
$$M_{H_2SO_4} = 2(1.008) + 32.06 + 4(16.00) = 98.08 \text{ g/mol}$$

### 2.1.3 Equivalent Mass and Equivalent Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equivalent Mass (E):** The mass of a substance that reacts with or supplies **1 mole of electrons**, **1 mole of H⁺ ions**, or **1 mole of OH⁻ ions** in a chemical reaction.

$$E = \frac{\text{Molar Mass } (M)}{\text{Valency Factor } (n_f)}$$

The **valency factor (nf)** depends on context:

| Reaction Type | Valency Factor (nf) |
|---------------|---------------------|
| Acid–Base | Basicity of acid / Acidity of base |
| Redox | Total electron transfer per formula unit |
| Displacement | Number of electrons exchanged |

**Number of equivalents = n × nf**

### 2.1.4 Gaseous State — Molar Volume at STP

At **Standard Temperature and Pressure (STP)** — historically 0 °C (273.15 K) and 1 atm — one mole of an **ideal gas** occupies **22.414 L**.

> [!NOTE]
> **New STP (IUPAC 1982):** 0 °C and 1 bar (100 kPa) → 1 mole occupies **22.711 L**
> 
> **SATP:** 25 °C and 1 bar → 1 mole occupies **24.465 L**
> 
> KTU problems typically use the older STP: **22.4 L/mol** at 0 °C, 1 atm.

For general gas calculations, use the **Ideal Gas Equation**:
$$PV = nRT$$
where $R$ = 0.0821 L·atm/(mol·K) = 8.314 J/(mol·K)

### 2.1.5 Mole Fraction — Composition of Mixtures

**Mole fraction (xᵢ)** of component i in a mixture:
$$x_i = \frac{n_i}{n_{total}} = \frac{n_i}{\sum_j n_j}$$

Property: $\sum_i x_i = 1$

**Mass fraction (wᵢ):**
$$w_i = \frac{m_i}{m_{total}}$$

Conversion between mole and mass fraction:
$$x_i = \frac{w_i / M_i}{\sum_j (w_j / M_j)}$$

### 2.1.6 Concentration Terms

| Term | Symbol | Formula | Units |
|------|--------|---------|-------|
| Molarity | M or c | $M = n / V_{solution}$ | mol/L |
| Normality | N | $N = \text{equivalents} / V_{solution}$ | eq/L |
| Molality | m | $m = n / m_{solvent}$ | mol/kg |
| Mass % | %w/w | $w_i \times 100$ | % |
| Volume % | %v/v | $v_i \times 100$ | % |

**Key Relationship:** $M_{solution} = \rho \times w \times 1000 / M_{solute}$ where $\rho$ = density (g/mL)

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they form the backbone of 80% of KTU numerical problems in this module.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $n = m / M$ | Moles from mass | mol |
| 2 | $N = n \cdot N_A$ | Number of particles | dimensionless |
| 3 | $V_{STP} = n \times 22.4$ | Gas volume at STP | L |
| 4 | $PV = nRT$ | Ideal gas law | varies |
| 5 | $E = M / n_f$ | Equivalent mass | g/eq |
| 6 | $x_i = n_i / n_{total}$ | Mole fraction | dimensionless |
| 7 | $M = n / V_{soln}$ | Molarity | mol/L |
| 8 | $N = M \times n_f$ | Molarity–Normality link | — |
| 9 | $\rho = m_{soln} / V_{soln}$ | Density | g/mL |
| 10 | $w_i = x_i M_i / \sum_j x_j M_j$ | Mass fraction from mole fraction | dimensionless |

**Standard Constants to Memorize:**
- $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹
- $R = 0.0821$ L·atm·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹
- $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹
- Molar volume at STP = **22.4 L/mol** (KTU convention)
- 1 atm = 1.01325 bar = 101.325 kPa

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters

The mole concept is the **universal language** of chemical engineering. Every downstream calculation — from reactor design to material balances to combustion analysis — depends on it. In process industries, engineers routinely:

- Convert feed rates (kg/hr) into molar flow rates (kmol/hr) for stoichiometric calculations
- Use mole fractions in **Raoult's Law** and **Henry's Law** for VLE (vapor–liquid equilibrium) design
- Calculate air-fuel ratios in combustion systems using molar compositions
- Determine **excess air** and **excess reagent** in industrial reactions

Without a firm grasp of the mole, no advanced chemical engineering calculation is possible.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Linking Mass, Moles, and Number of Particles

**Starting Premise:** One mole of a substance contains $N_A$ entities.

**Step 1 — Define moles from number of entities.**
By definition, if $N$ is the number of entities, the corresponding amount of substance is:

$$n = \frac{N}{N_A}$$

**Step 2 — Define mass of one entity.**
Let $m_{entity}$ be the mass of a single entity. For an atom, $m_{entity}$ is the atomic mass in grams per atom.

$$m_{entity} = \frac{M}{N_A}$$

where $M$ is the molar mass in g/mol. (This is the basis of why $N_A$ × 1 u = 1 g/mol.)

**Step 3 — Mass of a sample from its particles.**

$$m = N \times m_{entity} = N \times \frac{M}{N_A} = \frac{N \cdot M}{N_A}$$

**Step 4 — Rearranging to extract n in terms of mass.**

$$m = \frac{N \cdot M}{N_A} = \left(\frac{N}{N_A}\right) \cdot M = n \cdot M$$

$$\boxed{n = \frac{m}{M}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical verification with water.**
Consider 36 g of water. M(H₂O) = 18 g/mol.

$$n = \frac{36 \text{ g}}{18 \text{ g/mol}} = 2 \text{ mol}$$

$$N = 2 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 1.2044 \times 10^{24} \text{ molecules}$$

This confirms the framework is self-consistent.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Molar Volume of an Ideal Gas at STP

**Starting Premise:** Ideal gas law: $PV = nRT$

**Step 1 — Solve for V at constant T, P, n.**

For one mole ($n = 1$) of an ideal gas at STP ($T = 273.15$ K, $P = 1$ atm):

$$V_m = \frac{nRT}{P} = \frac{(1)(0.0821)(273.15)}{1} = 22.414 \text{ L}$$

**Step 2 — Rounding for KTU problems.**
At $T = 273$ K, $P = 1$ atm:

$$V_m = \frac{(1)(0.0821)(273)}{1} = 22.41 \text{ L} \approx 22.4 \text{ L/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Demonstrating that $V_m$ is independent of gas identity.**

For any ideal gas, $V_m$ depends **only on T and P**, not on the molecular size or mass. This is **Avogadro's Hypothesis**: equal volumes of gases at the same T and P contain equal numbers of molecules.

$$\boxed{V = n \times 22.4 \text{ L at STP}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert STP volume to mass via density.**
Given density $\rho$ (g/L) of a gas at STP:

$$M = \rho \times V_m = \rho \times 22.4 \text{ g/mol}$$

This is a frequently tested KTU problem — finding molar mass from gaseous density.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Relationship Between Mole Fraction and Mass Fraction

**Starting Premise:** A mixture contains $c$ components with $n_i$ moles of each.

**Step 1 — Express mass of each component.**
$$m_i = n_i \cdot M_i$$

**Step 2 — Express total mass.**
$$m_{total} = \sum_{j=1}^{c} n_j M_j$$

**Step 3 — Define mass fraction.**
$$w_i = \frac{m_i}{m_{total}} = \frac{n_i M_i}{\sum_j n_j M_j}$$

**Step 4 — Express as mole fraction.**
Since $x_i = n_i / n_{total}$:

$$w_i = \frac{x_i M_i}{\sum_j x_j M_j}$$

**Step 5 — Invert to get mole fraction from mass fraction.**
Divide numerator and denominator by $M_i$:

$$x_i = \frac{w_i / M_i}{\sum_j w_j / M_j}$$

$$\boxed{x_i = \frac{w_i / M_i}{\sum_j (w_j / M_j)}}$$

**Step 6 — Average molar mass of mixture.**
$$\bar{M} = \sum_i x_i M_i = \frac{1}{\sum_i (w_i / M_i)}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Molarity, Molality, and Density Interlink

**Starting Premise:** A solution contains solute (B) in solvent (A).

**Step 1 — Define mass of solute from molarity.**
For volume $V$ (L) of solution with molarity $C_B$:

$$m_B = C_B \cdot V \cdot M_B$$

**Step 2 — Express mass of solution from density.**
$$m_{soln} = \rho \cdot V$$

**Step 3 — Mass fraction of solute.**
$$w_B = \frac{m_B}{m_{soln}} = \frac{C_B \cdot V \cdot M_B}{\rho \cdot V} = \frac{C_B M_B}{\rho}$$

**Step 4 — Express in conventional units (g/mL).**
Using $\rho$ in g/mL and $V$ in mL, then converting $V$ to L:

$$w_B = \frac{C_B \cdot M_B}{1000 \cdot \rho}$$

where $C_B$ is in mol/L, $M_B$ in g/mol, and $\rho$ in g/mL.

**Step 5 — Molality in terms of molarity.**
Mass of solvent = $m_{soln} - m_B = V\rho - C_B V M_B = V(\rho - C_B M_B)$ in kg if V in L and ρ in kg/L.

$$m_{molality} = \frac{C_B}{\rho - C_B M_B} \text{ in mol/kg}$$

This exact relationship appears in **KTU numerical problems** involving density-based conversions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Mole Concept — Central Hub Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Mass m grams] --> B[Divide by Molar Mass M]
    C[Number of Particles N] --> D[Divide by Avogadro N sub A]
    B --> E[Amount of Substance n moles]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Multiply by Molar Mass M]
    E --> G[Multiply by Avogadro N sub A]
    F --> A
    G --> C
    E --> H[Multiply by 22.4 L at STP]
    H --> I[Volume of Gas V litres]
    E --> J[Multiply by Molar Mass M]
    J --> K[Mass of Gas m gas]
    A --> L[Equivalent Mass E = M over n sub f]
    L --> M[Number of Equivalents]
    M --> N[Normality N eq per L]
    E --> O[Divide by Solution Volume V]
    O --> P[Molarity C mol per L]
    E --> Q[Divide by Solvent Mass kg]
    Q --> R[Molality m mol per kg]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Concentration Conversions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS
        direction TB
        IN1[Density rho g per mL]
        IN2[Volume of Solution V mL]
        IN3[Mass of Solute m solute g]
        IN4[Molar Mass of Solute M g per mol]
    end

    subgraph CALCULATION_NODES
        direction TB
        CALC1[Moles of Solute n sub B]
        CALC2[Mass of Solution m soln]
        CALC3[Mass Fraction w sub B]
        CALC4[Molarity C sub B]
        CALC5[Molality m sub B]
        CALC6[Mole Fraction x sub B]
        CALC7[Normality N sub B]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS
        direction TB
        OUT1[Concentration Result]
        OUT2[Composition Data]
        OUT3[Reaction Stoichiometry]
    end

    IN3 --> CALC1
    IN4 --> CALC1
    IN1 --> CALC2
    IN2 --> CALC2
    CALC1 --> CALC3
    IN3 --> CALC3
    CALC2 --> CALC3
    CALC3 --> CALC4
    IN1 --> CALC4
    IN4 --> CALC4
    CALC1 --> CALC5
    CALC2 --> CALC5
    CALC1 --> CALC6
    CALC2 --> CALC6
    CALC1 --> CALC7
    IN2 --> CALC7
    CALC4 --> OUT1
    CALC5 --> OUT1
    CALC6 --> OUT2
    CALC7 --> OUT2
    CALC3 --> OUT3
    CALC1 --> OUT3
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Which Mole Formula to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START[Given Quantities in Problem] --> Q1{Kind of substance?}
    Q1 -->|Pure Solid or Liquid| SOLID[Use n equals m over M]
    Q1 -->|Gas at known T and P| GAS[Use PV equals nRT]
    Q1 -->|Gas at STP| GSTP[Use V equals n times 22.4]
    Q1 -->|Solution| SOLN[Use Molarity M equals n over V]
    Q1 -->|Mixture| MIX[Use Mole Fraction x sub i]
    SOLID --> CALC[Calculate n in moles]
    GAS --> CALC
    GSTP --> CALC
    SOLN --> CALC
    MIX --> CALC
    CALC --> CONVERT[Convert to Required Quantity]
    CONVERT --> MASS[Multiply by M for mass]
    CONVERT --> PART[Multiply by N sub A for particles]
    CONVERT --> VOL[Multiply by 22.4 for gas volume at STP]
    CONVERT --> EQ[Multiply by n sub f for equivalents]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Q:** Define mole and Avogadro's number. Calculate the number of molecules present in 9 g of water. (M = 18 g/mol)

**Model Answer:**

A **mole** is the SI unit of amount of substance, defined as the amount of substance that contains exactly **6.022 × 10²³** elementary entities.

**Avogadro's number** is the numerical value $6.022 \times 10^{23}$, representing the number of particles in one mole of any substance.

**Calculation:**

$$n = \frac{m}{M} = \frac{9}{18} = 0.5 \text{ mol}$$

$$N = n \times N_A = 0.5 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}$$

$$\boxed{N = 3.011 \times 10^{23} \text{ molecules}}$$

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Distinguish between molarity and molality. The density of a 2 M aqueous solution of NaCl is 1.08 g/mL. Calculate the molality of the solution. (M(NaCl) = 58.5 g/mol)

**Model Answer:**

| Molarity | Molality |
|----------|----------|
| Moles of solute per **litre of solution** | Moles of solute per **kg of solvent** |
| Depends on temperature (volume changes) | Independent of temperature |

**Calculation:**

Moles of NaCl in 1 L = 2 mol
Mass of NaCl = $2 \times 58.5 = 117$ g
Mass of 1 L solution = $1.08 \times 1000 = 1080$ g
Mass of water = $1080 - 117 = 963$ g = 0.963 kg

$$m = \frac{n_{solute}}{m_{solvent \text{ in kg}}} = \frac{2}{0.963}$$

$$\boxed{m = 2.077 \text{ mol/kg}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

### (a) (7 Marks) `[Module Choice – Either Or]`

**Q:** A gas mixture at STP contains 60% N₂, 30% O₂, and 10% CO₂ by volume. Calculate:
(i) The average molar mass of the mixture
(ii) The density of the mixture at STP
(iii) The partial pressure of each gas if the total pressure is raised to 2 atm at the same temperature.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recognize that volume % = mole %** (since equal volumes = equal moles at same T, P)

$$x_{N_2} = 0.60, \quad x_{O_2} = 0.30, \quad x_{CO_2} = 0.10$$

**Step 2 — Average molar mass:**

$$\bar{M} = \sum x_i M_i = (0.60)(28) + (0.30)(32) + (0.10)(44)$$

$$\bar{M} = 16.8 + 9.6 + 4.4 = \boxed{30.8 \text{ g/mol}}$$

**Step 3 — Density at STP:**

$$\rho = \frac{\bar{M}}{V_m} = \frac{30.8}{22.4}$$

$$\boxed{\rho = 1.375 \text{ g/L}}$$

**Step 4 — Partial pressures** (Dalton's Law):

$$p_i = x_i \times P_{total} = x_i \times 2 \text{ atm}$$

$$p_{N_2} = 0.60 \times 2 = 1.20 \text{ atm}$$
$$p_{O_2} = 0.30 \times 2 = 0.60 \text{ atm}$$
$$p_{CO_2} = 0.10 \times 2 = 0.20 \text{ atm}$$

$$\boxed{p_{N_2} = 1.20 \text{ atm}, \quad p_{O_2} = 0.60 \text{ atm}, \quad p_{CO_2} = 0.20 \text{ atm}}$$

---

### (b) (7 Marks)

**Q:** 10 g of CaCO₃ is reacted with excess HCl. Calculate:
(i) The mass of CO₂ produced at STP
(ii) The volume of CO₂ at STP
(iii) The number of CO₂ molecules evolved
(Reactions: CaCO₃ + 2HCl → CaCl₂ + H₂O + CO₂; M(CaCO₃) = 100 g/mol, M(CO₂) = 44 g/mol)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Moles of CaCO₃:**

$$n_{CaCO_3} = \frac{10}{100} = 0.1 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometry:** 1 mol CaCO₃ → 1 mol CO₂ (1:1 ratio)

$$n_{CO_2} = 0.1 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of CO₂:**

$$m_{CO_2} = n \times M = 0.1 \times 44 = \boxed{4.4 \text{ g}}$$

**Step 4 — Volume at STP:**

$$V = n \times 22.4 = 0.1 \times 22.4 = \boxed{2.24 \text{ L}}$$

**Step 5 — Number of molecules:**

$$N = n \times N_A = 0.1 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}$$

$$\boxed{N = 6.022 \times 10^{22} \text{ molecules}}$$

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

### (a) (7 Marks)

**Q:** A solution is prepared by dissolving 5 g of NaOH in 250 mL of water. Given that the density of the resulting solution is 1.05 g/mL, calculate:
(i) Molarity of the solution
(ii) Molality of the solution
(iii) Mole fraction of NaOH in the solution
(M(NaOH) = 40 g/mol, M(H₂O) = 18 g/mol)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Moles of NaOH and H₂O:**

$$n_{NaOH} = \frac{5}{40} = 0.125 \text{ mol}$$

Assume density of water ≈ 1 g/mL: $m_{water} = 250$ g

$$n_{H_2O} = \frac{250}{18} = 13.889 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2 — Molarity:**

$$V_{soln} = \frac{m_{soln}}{\rho} = \frac{5 + 250}{1.05} = \frac{255}{1.05} = 242.86 \text{ mL} = 0.2429 \text{ L}$$

$$M = \frac{0.125}{0.2429} = \boxed{0.515 \text{ mol/L}}$$

**Step 3 — Molality:**

$$m = \frac{n_{solute}}{m_{solvent \text{ in kg}}} = \frac{0.125}{0.250} = \boxed{0.500 \text{ mol/kg}}$$

**Step 4 — Mole fraction of NaOH:**

$$x_{NaOH} = \frac{0.125}{0.125 + 13.889} = \frac{0.125}{14.014}$$

$$\boxed{x_{NaOH} = 8.92 \times 10^{-3}}$$

---

### (b) (7 Marks)

**Q:** A gaseous hydrocarbon has the following composition by mass: C = 82.66%, H = 17.34%. At STP, 1 L of this gas weighs 2.5 g. Determine:
(i) Empirical formula
(ii) Molar mass from gas density
(iii) Molecular formula

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Empirical formula from mass %:**

In 100 g of compound: C = 82.66 g, H = 17.34 g

$$n_C = \frac{82.66}{12} = 6.888 \text{ mol}$$

$$n_H = \frac{17.34}{1} = 17.34 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2 — Simplest ratio (divide by smallest):**

$$C : H = \frac{6.888}{6.888} : \frac{17.34}{6.888} = 1 : 2.517 \approx 1 : 2.5$$

Multiplying by 2: $C_2H_5$

**Empirical formula:** $C_2H_5$ (mass = 29)

**Step 3 — Molar mass from gas density:**

$$M = \rho \times V_m = 2.5 \times 22.4 = 56 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 4 — Molecular formula:**

$$n = \frac{M_{molecular}}{M_{empirical}} = \frac{56}{29} \approx 2$$

$$\text{Molecular formula} = (C_2H_5)_2 = C_4H_{10}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Molecular Formula: } C_4H_{10} \text{ (Butane)}}$$

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones — KTU Board Examiner Insights**
> 
> 1. **STP vs. SATP confusion:** The 2024 scheme accepts both 22.4 L/mol (old STP) and 22.7 L/mol (new STP). Always state which one you are using. Mixing them in sub-parts of the same question **costs 1–2 marks**.
> 
> 2. **Volume % ≠ Mass %:** In gas mixtures, volume % = mole %. But for liquids/solutions, **volume % ≠ mass %**. Many students incorrectly assume this and lose full marks on composition problems.
> 
> 3. **Moles vs. Millimoles vs. Kilomoles:** Watch unit conversions. KTU often uses **kmol** in industrial process calculations. 1 kmol = 1000 mol. Forgetting this factor of 1000 is a **silent killer** that costs 2 marks.
> 
> 4. **Molarity changes with temperature; Molality does not.** If a question mentions temperature variation, use molality, not molarity. The reverse also applies.
> 
> 5. **Rounding premature:** Use full Avogadro value (6.022 × 10²³) and only round at the final answer. Mid-calculation rounding loses 1 mark per sub-part.
> 
> 6. **Valency factor in normality problems:** Forgetting to apply the valency factor (e.g., H₂SO₄ has nf = 2) is the **#1 mistake** in acid-base normality problems.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mole Concept**

- **Mole definition:** $1 \text{ mol} = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ particles (exact by SI 2019)
- **Core formula:** $n = m / M$ (moles = mass / molar mass)
- **Avogadro's number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹
- **Molar volume at STP:** 22.4 L/mol for any ideal gas
- **Ideal gas law:** $PV = nRT$, with $R = 0.0821$ L·atm/(mol·K)
- **Mole fraction:** $x_i = n_i / n_{total}$; sum = 1
- **Average molar mass:** $\bar{M} = \sum x_i M_i$
- **Molarity:** mol/L of solution; **Molality:** mol/kg of solvent
- **Normality = Molarity × valency factor**
- **Equivalent mass:** $E = M / n_f$
- **Mass–mole–particle triad is always interconvertible** — master the three arrows
- **Volume % of gases = mole %** (at same T, P)
- **Density of gas at STP:** $\rho = M / 22.4$ — a direct route to molar mass
- **Empirical formula** = simplest whole-number ratio
- **Molecular formula** = $n \times$ empirical formula, where $n = M_{molecular} / M_{empirical}$
- **Temperature-dependent:** Molarity, Normality, Volume → **NOT** Molality, Mass fraction, Mole fraction

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Basic Chemical Calculations: Mole Concept**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods of Expressing Composition - Mole Fraction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Methods of Expressing Composition - Mole Fraction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mole Fraction ($x_i$)** is defined as the ratio of the number of moles of a particular component ($n_i$) to the total number of moles of all components present in a mixture.

$$x_i = \frac{n_i}{n_{\text{total}}} = \frac{n_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} n_j}$$

where:
- $x_i$ = mole fraction of component $i$ (dimensionless)
- $n_i$ = number of moles of component $i$ (mol or kg-mol)
- $N$ = total number of components in the mixture
- $n_{\text{total}}$ = sum of moles of all components

**Fundamental Constraint (Identity Relation):**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i = x_1 + x_2 + x_3 + \dots + x_N = 1$$

This is the **summation constraint** — a mathematical identity that must always hold true for any well-defined mole fraction in a closed mixture system.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Pizza Slice Concept"

Imagine you order a large pizza cut into **10 equal slices**, and you decide to share it with friends:
- You take **4 slices** → your share = $4/10 = 0.4$
- Friend A takes **3 slices** → share = $3/10 = 0.3$
- Friend B takes **2 slices** → share = $2/10 = 0.2$
- Friend C takes **1 slice** → share = $1/10 = 0.1$

**Mole fraction works exactly the same way!**

Instead of pizza slices, we count **particles (atoms, molecules, or ions)**. The total number of molecules in a mixture is divided up among the components — just like the pizza is divided among friends. The "share" each component gets is its **mole fraction**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Mole" and not "Mass"?**
> Because chemical reactions happen between **individual particles** (atoms or molecules), the mole is the natural counting unit. A balanced chemical equation (e.g., $2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$) tells us about ratios of *particles*, not kilograms. Mole fraction therefore reflects the **true particle-level composition** of a mixture, making it the most fundamental composition variable in reaction engineering, thermodynamics, and phase equilibrium.

## 1.3 Range and Dimensionality

| Property | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Range of $x_i$ | $0 \le x_i \le 1$ |
| Dimension | **Dimensionless** (pure number) |
| Standard unit | **Unit-less**, often expressed as a decimal (0.25) or percent (25%) |
| Reference temperature | None required (composition is a state variable) |
| Reference pressure | None required (composition is a state variable) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mole Percent** is simply the mole fraction expressed as a percentage:
> $$\text{Mole \% of } i = x_i \times 100\%$$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Mole Fraction

Chemical engineers prefer mole fraction over mass fraction for several rigorous reasons:

1. **Ideal Gas Law Compatibility:** In an ideal gas mixture, the **partial pressure** of a component is directly proportional to its mole fraction (Dalton's Law):
$$P_i = x_i \cdot P_{\text{total}}$$

2. **Reaction Stoichiometry:** Reaction rates in gas-phase kinetics are typically expressed in terms of mole fractions or partial pressures because the kinetic theory of gases counts molecular collisions.

3. **Raoult's Law (VLE):** The vapor-liquid equilibrium calculations use mole fraction (not mass) for the liquid phase:
$$P_i = x_i \cdot P_i^{\text{sat}}$$

4. **Ideal Solution Property:** For an **ideal solution**, the partial molar volume, enthalpy, and entropy of mixing can be derived cleanly from mole fractions.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Relation | Expression | Units | Application |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Mole Fraction Definition | $x_i = \dfrac{n_i}{\sum_j n_j}$ | dimensionless | All mixtures |
| 2 | Summation Constraint | $\sum_i x_i = 1$ | dimensionless | Validity check |
| 3 | Moles from Mass | $n_i = \dfrac{m_i}{M_i}$ | kmol | Link to mass |
| 4 | Moles from Volume (ideal gas) | $n_i = \dfrac{P_i V}{RT}$ | kmol | Gas mixtures |
| 5 | Average Molecular Weight | $M_{\text{avg}} = \sum_i x_i M_i$ | kg/kmol | Mass-mole bridge |
| 6 | Mole Fraction from Mass Fraction | $x_i = \dfrac{w_i / M_i}{\sum_j (w_j / M_j)}$ | dimensionless | Conversion |
| 7 | Mass Fraction from Mole Fraction | $w_i = \dfrac{x_i M_i}{\sum_j x_j M_j}$ | dimensionless | Conversion |
| 8 | Mole % | $\text{Mole \%}_i = x_i \times 100$ | % | Reporting |
| 9 | Dalton's Law | $P_i = x_i P_{\text{total}}$ | Pa, bar | Gas mixtures |
| 10 | Amagat's Law (partial vol.) | $V_i = y_i V_{\text{total}}$ | m³ | Gas mixtures |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Table Symbols:** In the formulas above, the **vertical bar** for absolute value or "such that" has been deliberately avoided to preserve markdown table integrity. In handwritten KTU scripts, students may use $\vert \cdot \vert$ freely.

## 2.3 Types of Composition Variables in Chemical Engineering

| Composition Basis | Symbol | Common Use Case |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Mole fraction** | $x_i$ (liquid), $y_i$ (vapor) | VLE, gas reactions, ideal solutions |
| **Mass fraction** | $w_i$ | Mass balances, slurry/feed streams |
| **Molarity (M)** | $C_i$ | Liquid reactions, analytical chemistry |
| **Molality (m)** | $m_i$ | Colligative properties, electrolyte solutions |
| **Volume fraction** | $\phi_i$ | Gas mixtures (ideal), liquid blends |
| **ppm / ppb** | — | Trace contaminants, environmental |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petroleum Refining:** Crude oil is characterized by mole fractions of light vs. heavy hydrocarbons for distillation column design.
- **Air Separation:** Air is approximately $x_{O_2} = 0.21$ and $x_{N_2} = 0.79$ — used in cryogenic ASU (Air Separation Unit) design.
- **Ammonia Synthesis:** Haber–Bosch reactor feed composition is expressed in mole fractions for $H_2$, $N_2$, and $CH_4$ (inert).
- **Environmental Engineering:** Greenhouse gas composition ($x_{CO_2} \approx 0.000425$ in 2024) is reported in mole fraction or ppm.
- **Combustion:** Air-fuel ratio calculations and flue gas analysis rely on mole fractions of $O_2$, $N_2$, $CO_2$, and $H_2O$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Molecular Weight of a Mixture

**Starting Point:** We want a single molecular weight value that represents the mixture's behavior in the ideal gas law.

**Step 1 — Recall the ideal gas law applied to the total mixture:**

$$P_{\text{total}} V = n_{\text{total}} R T$$

**Step 2 — Apply the ideal gas law to each individual component and sum:**

$$\sum_i P_i V = \sum_i n_i R T$$

By Dalton's Law, $\sum_i P_i = P_{\text{total}}$, so:

$$P_{\text{total}} V = n_{\text{total}} R T = \left(\sum_i n_i\right) R T$$

**Step 3 — Define average molecular weight as the total mass divided by total moles:**

$$M_{\text{avg}} = \frac{m_{\text{total}}}{n_{\text{total}}} = \frac{\sum_i m_i}{\sum_i n_i}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $m_i = n_i M_i$ into the numerator:**

$$M_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\sum_i n_i M_i}{\sum_i n_i}$$

**Step 5 — Divide numerator and denominator by $n_{\text{total}}$ and use $x_i = n_i / n_{\text{total}}$:**

$$M_{\text{avg}} = \sum_i x_i M_i$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **mole-fraction-weighted average molecular weight** — a direct consequence of mole fraction being a ratio of particle counts.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Direct Mole Fraction Calculation

### **Problem Statement**
A vessel contains a gas mixture with the following composition by mass:
- Oxygen ($O_2$): 64 g
- Nitrogen ($N_2$): 56 g
- Carbon dioxide ($CO_2$): 44 g

Calculate the **mole fraction of each component**.

### **Step-by-Step Solution**

**Step 1 — Identify molecular weights:**
- $M_{O_2} = 32$ kg/kmol
- $M_{N_2} = 28$ kg/kmol
- $M_{CO_2} = 44$ kg/kmol

**Step 2 — Convert each mass to moles using $n_i = m_i / M_i$:**

$$n_{O_2} = \frac{64 \text{ g}}{32 \text{ g/mol}} = 2.0 \text{ mol}$$

$$n_{N_2} = \frac{56 \text{ g}}{28 \text{ g/mol}} = 2.0 \text{ mol}$$

$$n_{CO_2} = \frac{44 \text{ g}}{44 \text{ g/mol}} = 1.0 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate total moles:**

$$n_{\text{total}} = 2.0 + 2.0 + 1.0 = 5.0 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate mole fractions:**

$$x_{O_2} = \frac{2.0}{5.0} = 0.40$$

$$x_{N_2} = \frac{2.0}{5.0} = 0.40$$

$$x_{CO_2} = \frac{1.0}{5.0} = 0.20$$

**Step 5 — Verification (Summation Constraint):**

$$\sum x_i = 0.40 + 0.40 + 0.20 = 1.00 \;\;\checkmark$$

**Final Answer:**

| Component | Mole Fraction | Mole % |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| $O_2$ | 0.40 | 40 % |
| $N_2$ | 0.40 | 40 % |
| $CO_2$ | 0.20 | 20 % |

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Conversion from Mass Fraction to Mole Fraction

### **Problem Statement**
A liquid mixture used in a pharmaceutical process has the following composition by **mass fraction**:
- Ethanol ($C_2H_5OH$): $w_1 = 0.50$, $M_1 = 46$ kg/kmol
- Water ($H_2O$): $w_2 = 0.50$, $M_2 = 18$ kg/kmol

Determine the **mole fractions** of each component.

### **Step-by-Step Solution**

**Step 1 — State the conversion formula:**

$$x_i = \frac{w_i / M_i}{\sum_j (w_j / M_j)}$$

**Step 2 — Assume 100 kg of mixture as a basis (convenient for mass fractions):**
- $m_1 = 50$ kg ethanol
- $m_2 = 50$ kg water

**Step 3 — Compute moles of each component:**

$$n_1 = \frac{50}{46} = 1.08696 \text{ kmol}$$

$$n_2 = \frac{50}{18} = 2.77778 \text{ kmol}$$

**Step 4 — Total moles:**

$$n_{\text{total}} = 1.08696 + 2.77778 = 3.86474 \text{ kmol}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate mole fractions:**

$$x_1 = \frac{1.08696}{3.86474} = 0.2812$$

$$x_2 = \frac{2.77778}{3.86474} = 0.7188$$

**Step 6 — Verification:**

$$x_1 + x_2 = 0.2812 + 0.7188 = 1.0000 \;\;\checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that even though the mixture is **50/50 by mass**, the **mole fraction of water is much higher (≈ 72%)** because water has a much smaller molecular weight. Light molecules contribute more moles per unit mass!

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Average Molecular Weight of Air

### **Problem Statement**
Dry air is composed of approximately:
- $N_2$: $x_{N_2} = 0.79$, $M_{N_2} = 28$ kg/kmol
- $O_2$: $x_{O_2} = 0.21$, $M_{O_2} = 32$ kg/kmol

Calculate the **average molecular weight of air**.

### **Solution**

$$M_{\text{air}} = x_{N_2} M_{N_2} + x_{O_2} M_{O_2}$$

$$M_{\text{air}} = (0.79)(28) + (0.21)(32)$$

$$M_{\text{air}} = 22.12 + 6.72 = 28.84 \text{ kg/kmol}$$

This is the well-known value of **28.97 kg/kmol** used universally in flue gas and combustion calculations (the small difference accounts for argon).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mole Fraction vs. Mass Fraction Comparison

| Aspect | Mole Fraction ($x_i$) | Mass Fraction ($w_i$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | $n_i / \sum n_j$ | $m_i / \sum m_j$ |
| Weighting factor | Molecular count | Mass |
| Sensitive to | Number of particles | Heavy vs. light molecules |
| Preferred in | Gas-phase reactions, VLE | Solid blends, mass balances |
| Conservation under chemical reaction | **Yes** (atoms are conserved) | **Yes** |
| Ranges | $0$ to $1$ | $0$ to $1$ |
| Sum constraint | $\sum x_i = 1$ | $\sum w_i = 1$ |
| Useful identity | $M_{\text{avg}} = \sum x_i M_i$ | $1/M_{\text{avg}} = \sum w_i / M_i$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Map — Position of Mole Fraction in Composition Theory

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Composition of Mixtures"] --> B["On Mole Basis"]
    A --> C["On Mass Basis"]
    A --> D["On Volume Basis"]

    B --> B1["Mole Fraction x_i"]
    B --> B2["Molarity C_i mol per L"]
    B --> B3["Molality m_i mol per kg solvent"]

    C --> C1["Mass Fraction w_i"]
    C --> C2["ppm / ppb for trace"]

    D --> D1["Volume Fraction phi_i"]
    D --> D2["Partial Pressure P_i for gases"]

    B1 --> E1["Dalton Law P_i equals x_i times P total"]
    B1 --> E2["Raoult Law for VLE"]
    B1 --> E3["Reaction Stoichiometry"]

    C1 --> F1["Mass Balance equations"]
    C1 --> F2["Slurry and Solid Feed design"]
```

## 4.2 Computational Flowchart — Solving a Mole Fraction Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Start: Given Mixture Composition"] --> Q1{"Given basis?"}
    Q1 -- "Mass data" --> S1["Step 1: Convert mass to moles using n_i equals m_i over M_i"]
    Q1 -- "Mole data" --> S3["Step 1: Moles are already known"]
    Q1 -- "Volume data gas" --> S2["Step 1: Convert volume to moles using PV equals nRT"]
    S1 --> S4["Step 2: Sum all moles to get n total"]
    S2 --> S4
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5["Step 3: Compute x_i equals n_i divided by n total"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 4: Verify sum of x_i equals 1"]
    S6 -- "Yes" --> S7["Done: Report mole fractions"]
    S6 -- "No" --> S8["Recheck arithmetic"]
    S8 --> S4
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How Mole Fraction is Used Across a Chemical Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Feed Streams"]
        F1["Raw Material A: x_A"]
        F2["Raw Material B: x_B"]
        F3["Recycle Stream: x_R"]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS["Process Units"]
        R1["Reactor: Stoichiometry uses x_i"]
        D1["Distillation Column: VLE uses x_i and y_i"]
        H1["Heat Exchanger: M_avg via x_i"]
        C1["Compressor: PV equals nRT uses x_i"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Product Streams"]
        P1["Distillate: y_i vapor mole frac"]
        P2["Bottom Product: x_i liquid mole frac"]
        P3["Vent: Composition via x_i"]
    end

    F1 --> R1
    F2 --> R1
    F3 --> R1
    R1 --> D1
    R1 --> H1
    D1 --> P1
    D1 --> P2
    H1 --> C1
    C1 --> P3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Architecture:** The diagram above shows that **mole fraction is the central language** of process design — virtually every unit operation (reactor, column, heat exchanger, compressor) in a chemical plant consumes or produces composition data in mole fraction form.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define mole fraction. State and explain the summation constraint for mole fractions in a multicomponent mixture.**

**Model Answer:**

The mole fraction of a component $i$ in a mixture is defined as the ratio of the number of moles of that component to the total number of moles of all components in the mixture:

$$x_i = \frac{n_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} n_j}$$

**Summation Constraint:** Since the denominator is the sum of all individual mole counts, dividing each by this sum and adding them must yield unity:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i = \frac{\sum_i n_i}{\sum_i n_i} = 1$$

This identity is always true by construction and serves as a **consistency check** for any calculated set of mole fractions.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: A gas mixture contains 4 mol of $CH_4$, 6 mol of $C_2H_6$, and 10 mol of $CO_2$. Calculate the mole fraction of each component and the average molecular weight of the mixture.**

**Model Answer:**

Total moles: $n_{\text{total}} = 4 + 6 + 10 = 20$ mol

$$x_{CH_4} = \frac{4}{20} = 0.20, \quad x_{C_2H_6} = \frac{6}{20} = 0.30, \quad x_{CO_2} = \frac{10}{20} = 0.50$$

Average molecular weight:

$$M_{\text{avg}} = (0.20)(16) + (0.30)(30) + (0.50)(44) = 3.2 + 9.0 + 22.0 = 34.2 \text{ kg/kmol}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In long-answer questions, students **commonly lose marks** for: (1) Forgetting to convert mass data to moles before computing mole fractions, (2) Not verifying the summation constraint at the end, (3) Confusing the symbols $x_i$ (liquid) and $y_i$ (vapor) in VLE problems, and (4) Failing to state the molecular weight values used. Always show the units and verify $\sum x_i = 1$ in your final step.

---

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Set A]`

**(a)** Define mole fraction, mass fraction, and molality. Show that for a binary mixture, the mole fraction of component 1 can be expressed in terms of the mass fraction $w_1$ and molecular weights $M_1$ and $M_2$ as:

$$x_1 = \frac{w_1 / M_1}{w_1 / M_1 + w_2 / M_2}$$

**Derive the expression step by step. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Take a basis of 1 kg of total mixture. Then $m_1 = w_1$ kg and $m_2 = w_2 = 1 - w_1$ kg.

**Step 2:** Convert masses to moles:

$$n_1 = \frac{w_1}{M_1}, \qquad n_2 = \frac{w_2}{M_2} = \frac{1 - w_1}{M_2}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the mole fraction definition:

$$x_1 = \frac{n_1}{n_1 + n_2}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the expressions for $n_1$ and $n_2$:

$$x_1 = \frac{w_1 / M_1}{(w_1 / M_1) + ((1 - w_1) / M_2)}$$

**Step 5:** Using the constraint $w_1 + w_2 = 1$, this simplifies to:

$$\boxed{\,x_1 = \frac{w_1 / M_1}{(w_1 / M_1) + (w_2 / M_2)}\,}$$

**(b)** An aqueous solution contains 30 % by mass of NaOH. Calculate: (i) the mole fraction of NaOH and water, (ii) the molality of NaOH, and (iii) the average molecular weight of the solution. **($M_{NaOH}$ = 40, $M_{H_2O}$ = 18 kg/kmol) (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Take a 100 kg basis:**
- $m_{NaOH} = 30$ kg
- $m_{H_2O} = 70$ kg

**Step 2 — Moles of each:**

$$n_{NaOH} = \frac{30}{40} = 0.75 \text{ kmol}$$

$$n_{H_2O} = \frac{70}{18} = 3.8889 \text{ kmol}$$

**Step 3 — Mole fractions (i):**

$$x_{NaOH} = \frac{0.75}{0.75 + 3.8889} = \frac{0.75}{4.6389} = 0.1617$$

$$x_{H_2O} = 1 - 0.1617 = 0.8383$$

**Step 4 — Molality (ii):** Molality = moles of solute per kg of solvent:

$$m = \frac{0.75 \text{ kmol NaOH}}{70 \text{ kg water}} = 0.01071 \text{ kmol/kg} = 10.71 \text{ mol/kg}$$

**Step 5 — Average molecular weight (iii):**

$$M_{\text{avg}} = (0.1617)(40) + (0.8383)(18) = 6.468 + 15.089 = 21.56 \text{ kg/kmol}$$

**Final Answer:**

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :---: |
| $x_{NaOH}$ | 0.1617 |
| $x_{H_2O}$ | 0.8383 |
| Molality | 10.71 mol/kg |
| $M_{\text{avg}}$ | 21.56 kg/kmol |

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **average molecular weight** of a gas mixture in terms of the mole fractions of its components. Why is this quantity important in gas-phase calculations? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Start with the ideal gas law applied to the entire mixture:

$$P V = n_{\text{total}} R T$$

**Step 2:** Also, by the definition of average molecular weight:

$$M_{\text{avg}} = \frac{m_{\text{total}}}{n_{\text{total}}}$$

**Step 3:** Total mass is the sum of individual masses: $m_{\text{total}} = \sum_i n_i M_i$.

**Step 4:** Substitute into the definition:

$$M_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\sum_i n_i M_i}{\sum_i n_i}$$

**Step 5:** Divide numerator and denominator by $n_{\text{total}}$ and use $x_i = n_i / n_{\text{total}}$:

$$\boxed{\,M_{\text{avg}} = \sum_i x_i M_i\,}$$

**Engineering Significance:**

1. The ideal gas law uses a single $M$ value: $PV = nRT = (m/M_{\text{avg}}) RT$. Without $M_{\text{avg}}$, the law cannot be applied to a mixture.
2. Gas density is calculated as $\rho = P M_{\text{avg}} / (RT)$.
3. Combustion calculations (air-fuel ratio, flue gas molecular weight) all require $M_{\text{avg}}$.

**(b)** A flue gas from a combustion process has the following dry composition by volume: $CO_2$ = 12 %, $O_2$ = 5 %, $N_2$ = 83 %. Calculate: (i) the average molecular weight of the flue gas, (ii) the density of the flue gas at 1.2 bar and 450 K, and (iii) the partial pressure of each component. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Use volume % = mole % (for ideal gases, Amagat's Law):**
- $x_{CO_2} = 0.12$, $M_{CO_2} = 44$ kg/kmol
- $x_{O_2} = 0.05$, $M_{O_2} = 32$ kg/kmol
- $x_{N_2} = 0.83$, $M_{N_2} = 28$ kg/kmol

**Step 2 — Average molecular weight (i):**

$$M_{\text{avg}} = (0.12)(44) + (0.05)(32) + (0.83)(28)$$

$$M_{\text{avg}} = 5.28 + 1.60 + 23.24 = 30.12 \text{ kg/kmol}$$

**Step 3 — Density (ii):** Using ideal gas law $\rho = P M_{\text{avg}} / (RT)$:

$$\rho = \frac{(1.2 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa})(30.12 \text{ kg/kmol})}{(8314 \text{ J/kmol·K})(450 \text{ K})}$$

$$\rho = \frac{3.614 \times 10^6}{3.741 \times 10^6} = 0.966 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Partial pressures (iii):** Using $P_i = x_i P_{\text{total}}$:

$$P_{CO_2} = (0.12)(1.2) = 0.144 \text{ bar}$$

$$P_{O_2} = (0.05)(1.2) = 0.060 \text{ bar}$$

$$P_{N_2} = (0.83)(1.2) = 0.996 \text{ bar}$$

**Verification:** $0.144 + 0.060 + 0.996 = 1.200$ bar $\checkmark$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Definition:** $x_i = n_i / n_{\text{total}}$ — ratio of moles of one component to total moles of all components.
- **Constraint:** $\sum x_i = 1$ always (use as a final check).
- **Range:** $0 \le x_i \le 1$ (pure component limit: 1; absent component limit: 0).
- **Mole %:** Multiply $x_i$ by 100.
- **Volume % = Mole %** for **ideal gases** only (Amagat's Law).
- **Mole-to-Mass Bridge:** $M_{\text{avg}} = \sum x_i M_i$ (mole-fraction-weighted mean).
- **Mass-to-Mole Bridge:** $x_i = (w_i / M_i) / \sum (w_j / M_j)$.
- **Dalton's Law:** $P_i = x_i P_{\text{total}}$ — partial pressure proportional to mole fraction in ideal gas mixtures.
- **Raoult's Law:** $P_i = x_i P_i^{\text{sat}}$ — central to VLE calculations.
- **VLE Notation:** Use $x_i$ for **liquid** phase and $y_i$ for **vapor** phase — do not interchange!
- **Reaction Stoichiometry:** Always work in mole (or mole fraction) basis for reacting systems.
- **Common Error:** Forgetting to convert mass → mole before computing mole fraction (lose 2–3 marks in KTU valuation).
- **Common Error:** Not verifying $\sum x_i = 1$ at the end of the solution.
- **Engineering Applications:** Distillation column design, combustion analysis, reactor feed characterization, environmental monitoring, cryogenic gas separation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weight Fraction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Weight Fraction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Weight Fraction (Mass Fraction, $w_i$)** is defined as the ratio of the mass (weight) of a particular chemical component in a mixture to the total mass (weight) of the entire mixture. It is a dimensionless quantity that quantifies the proportional contribution of a single component to the overall mass of a multi-component system.

Mathematically, for a mixture containing $n$ components:

$$w_i = \frac{m_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i} = \frac{m_i}{m_{total}}$$

Where:
- $w_i$ = Weight fraction of component $i$ (**dimensionless, range: 0 to 1**)
- $m_i$ = Mass of component $i$ (in **kg** or **g**)
- $m_{total}$ = Total mass of the mixture (in **kg** or **g**)
- $n$ = Total number of components in the mixture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A fundamental identity in Chemical Process Principles is the **Summation Rule** for weight fractions:
> 
> $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + \dots + w_n = 1$$
> 
> This identity stems from the fact that the sum of all individual component masses must equal the total mass of the system. The total mixture is treated as a unity, and each weight fraction represents a *partition* of that unity.

### 1.2 Related Derivative Quantities

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Weight Percent** | $\% w_i$ | $\% w_i = w_i \times 100$ | Mass of component per 100 mass units of mixture |
| **Mass Ratio** | $r_{i/j}$ | $r_{i/j} = m_i / m_j$ | Mass of one component relative to another |
| **Total Mass** | $m_T$ | $m_T = \sum m_i$ | Sum of all component masses |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Explanation — The Fruit Basket Analogy:**
> 
> Imagine you walk into a market and buy a **fruit basket** containing **3 kg of apples**, **2 kg of oranges**, and **5 kg of bananas**. The **total mass** of your basket is $3 + 2 + 5 = 10$ kg.
> 
> The **weight fraction of apples** is simply the *share* of apples in the basket: $w_{apple} = 3/10 = 0.3$. This means **30%** of your basket's mass comes from apples. Similarly, oranges contribute $2/10 = 0.2$ (20%), and bananas contribute $5/10 = 0.5$ (50%).
> 
> Notice how all these shares add up to exactly 1 (or 100%) — because the basket is fully accounted for, with no "leftover" mass.
> 
> In **chemical engineering**, this same logic applies to multicomponent streams in reactors, distillation columns, and pipelines. Whether the "fruits" are nitrogen, oxygen, and argon in air, or methane, ethane, and propane in natural gas, the weight fraction always tells you the *mass-based recipe* of the mixture.

### 1.4 Physical Significance

The weight fraction is **mass-based** and is therefore independent of the molecular structure or chemical identity of the components. This makes it especially useful in:

- **Material balance calculations** across unit operations.
- **Reactor design** where reactants are measured by mass (e.g., solids handling).
- **Combustion engineering** where fuel-oxidizer ratios are often expressed by mass.
- **Process economics** when raw materials are purchased by weight (kg, tonnes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** Weight fraction ($w_i$) is **NOT the same** as **mole fraction** ($x_i$). They differ whenever the molecular weights of the components are unequal. A heavier molecule occupies a *larger* mass share than its *mole* share, and vice versa for a lighter molecule.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic Behind Weight Fraction

The concept of weight fraction rests on three fundamental logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Identify Components:** Any chemical mixture contains $n$ distinct chemical species (components). Each component $i$ has an associated mass $m_i$ that is additive.
- **Step 2 — Define a Reference (Total):** The reference quantity is the **total mass** of the mixture, $m_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i$. This is the *denominator* of the weight fraction and serves as the normalization constant.
- **Step 3 — Form the Ratio:** Divide the individual mass of each component by the total mass. The result is a *dimensionless number* between 0 and 1 that represents the fractional mass contribution.

**Why does this work?**
- Because mass is an **extensive property** (it depends on the amount of substance), it is **additive** — the total mass is simply the sum of individual masses. This additivity allows us to construct a *normalized* mass-based composition variable.
- Normalization (summing to 1) ensures that the weight fraction is **intensive** — it does not depend on the *size* of the system but only on its *composition*.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Condition | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $w_i = \dfrac{m_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i}$ | Basic definition of weight fraction | **dimensionless** |
| 2 | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1$ | Summation rule (closure property) | **dimensionless** |
| 3 | $\% w_i = w_i \times 100$ | Conversion to weight percent (%) | **percent** |
| 4 | $w_i = \dfrac{x_i \cdot M_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \cdot M_i} = \dfrac{x_i \cdot M_i}{M_{avg}}$ | Conversion from **mole fraction** to **weight fraction** | **dimensionless** |
| 5 | $x_i = \dfrac{w_i / M_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (w_i / M_i)} = \dfrac{w_i / M_i}{1 / M_{avg}}$ | Conversion from **weight fraction** to **mole fraction** | **dimensionless** |
| 6 | $M_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (w_i / M_i)}$ | Average molecular weight from weight fractions | **kg/kmol** or **g/mol** |
| 7 | $M_{avg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i \cdot M_i)$ | Average molecular weight from mole fractions | **kg/kmol** or **g/mol** |
| 8 | $m_i = w_i \cdot m_{total}$ | Mass of component $i$ in a mixture | **kg** or **g** |
| 9 | $n_i = \dfrac{m_i}{M_i} = \dfrac{w_i \cdot m_{total}}{M_i}$ | Moles of component $i$ | **kmol** or **mol** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention Used by KTU:**
> - $w_i$ = weight fraction of component $i$
> - $x_i$ = mole fraction of component $i$
> - $M_i$ = molecular weight of component $i$ (g/mol or kg/kmol)
> - $M_{avg}$ = average (mean) molecular weight of the mixture
> - $m_i$ = mass of component $i$
> - $n_i$ = number of moles of component $i$

### 2.3 Conversion Logic: Weight Fraction ↔ Mole Fraction

This is one of the **most frequently tested conversions** in KTU examinations.

**Weight Fraction from Mole Fraction:**

$$w_i = \frac{x_i \cdot M_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i \cdot M_i)}$$

*Why?* — If you know the mole fractions and molecular weights, you can find the *mass* of each component by multiplying moles by molecular weight, then normalize to get the mass-based share.

**Mole Fraction from Weight Fraction:**

$$x_i = \frac{w_i / M_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (w_i / M_i)}$$

*Why?* — If you know the weight fractions and molecular weights, you can find the *moles* of each component by dividing mass by molecular weight, then normalize to get the mole-based share.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Weight Fraction |
|---|---|
| **Petroleum Refining** | Crude oil assays: paraffin, naphthene, aromatic content by mass |
| **Food Technology** | Moisture content, fat content, protein content in food products |
| **Polymer Engineering** | Filler loading, plasticizer concentration in composite materials |
| **Environmental Engineering** | Particulate matter (PM2.5, PM10) composition, soil contamination analysis |
| **Combustion Engineering** | Air-fuel ratio by mass, ash content in coal |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Active Pharmaceutical Ingredient (API) content in tablet formulations |
| **Cement & Ceramics** | Oxide composition (CaO, SiO₂, Al₂O₃) in raw mix |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
