(Molar mass of glucose = 180 g/mol). **Step 1: Identify the given data.** - $w_B$ (mass of glucose) = 9 g - $M_B$ (molar mass of glucose) = 180 g/mol - $W_A$ (mass of water) = 250 g **Step 2: Convert the mass of the solvent to kilograms.** $$m_{\text{solvent}} = \frac{250}{1000} = 0.25 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 3: Calculate the number of moles of glucose.** $$n_B = \frac{9}{180} = 0.05 \text{ mol}$$ **Step 4: Apply the molality formula.** $$m = \frac{0.05}{0.25}$$ **Step 5: Final calculation.** $$\boxed{m = 0.2 \text{ mol/kg} \quad \text{or} \quad 0.2 \text{ molal}}$$ --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Flowchart: Position of Molality in Concentration Units The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how Molality is derived, what its inputs are, and how it connects to other major concentration units used across the KTU Chemical Process Principles syllabus. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Given Composition of Solution] --> B{Identify Components} B --> C[Solute: smaller quantity] B --> D[Solvent: larger quantity] C --> E[Mass of Solute in grams] D --> F[Mass of Solvent in grams] E --> G[Convert to moles n_B = mass / molar mass] F --> H[Convert to kilograms mass_kg = mass_g / 1000] G --> I[Apply Molality Formula] H --> I I --> J[Molality m in mol per kg] J --> K[Used in Colligative Properties] K --> K1[Boiling Point Elevation] K --> K2[Freezing Point Depression] K --> K3[Osmotic Pressure] K --> K4[Relative Lowering of Vapour Pressure] J --> L[Conversion Pathways] L --> L1[To Molarity using density] L --> L2[To Mole Fraction using molar mass] L --> L3[To Mass percent] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Molality vs. Molarity Decision Matrix The following architecture map helps the KTU student decide *when* to use molality versus molarity during problem-solving. ```mermaid graph LR Input[Concentration Problem Statement] --> CheckT{Temperature
Varying?} CheckT -->|YES| Choice1[Use MOLALITY m] CheckT -->|NO| Choice2[Either Molality or Molarity works] Choice1 --> Reason1[Reason: Mass of solvent is
independent of temperature] Choice2 --> Reason2[Reason: Volume is stable at
constant temperature] Input --> CheckP{Property being
studied?} CheckP -->|Colligative Property| Path1[MOLALITY m is the standard input] CheckP -->|Stoichiometry in solution| Path2[MOLARITY M is preferred] Path1 --> Eq1[Delta T_f = K_f x m] Path1 --> Eq2[Delta T_b = K_b x m] Path1 --> Eq3[Pi = m x R x T x i] Path2 --> Eq4[n = M x V in liters] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The "YES" branch on the first decision node (Temperature Varying?) is the most common engineering scenario. For example, a chemical reactor operating from 300 K to 500 K requires molality-based calculations to maintain accuracy. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Define molality. Why is it preferred over molarity in studying colligative properties?** **Model Answer:** Molality is defined as the number of moles of solute dissolved in **one kilogram** of the solvent. It is expressed as: $$m = \frac{n_{\text{solute}}}{\text{mass of solvent in kg}} \quad (\text{units: mol/kg})$$ It is preferred over molarity in colligative properties because the **mass of the solvent does not change with temperature**, whereas the *volume* of the solution (used in molarity) expands or contracts with temperature variations. Since colligative property experiments are highly sensitive to temperature, molality provides a more stable and accurate concentration measure. --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **A solution contains 10 g of NaOH in 500 g of water. Calculate its molality. (Molar mass of NaOH = 40 g/mol)** **Model Answer:** **Given:** - Mass of NaOH ($w_B$) = 10 g - Molar mass of NaOH ($M_B$) = 40 g/mol - Mass of water ($W_A$) = 500 g **Moles of NaOH:** $$n_B = \frac{10}{40} = 0.25 \text{ mol}$$ **Mass of water in kg:** $$W_A = \frac{500}{1000} = 0.5 \text{ kg}$$ **Molality:** $$m = \frac{0.25}{0.5} = 0.5 \text{ mol/kg}$$ The molality of the solution is **0.5 molal**. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (Option 1): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(a) [7 Marks]** Define the following terms with suitable examples: (i) Molality, (ii) Mole Fraction, (iii) Mass Percent. **(b) [7 Marks]** A solution of urea ($NH_2CONH_2$) in water has a boiling point elevation of 0.52 K. Given that $K_b$ for water is 0.52 K·kg/mol, calculate the molality of the solution. If this solution was prepared by dissolving 6 g of urea in 250 g of water, determine the molar mass of urea and identify whether the answer matches the standard value (60 g/mol). --- ### Model Solution for Question A: #### Part (a) [7 Marks] - Definitions **(i) Molality ($m$):** The number of moles of solute dissolved per kilogram of solvent. $$m = \frac{n_{\text{solute}}}{W_{\text{solvent (kg)}}}$$ **Example:** 1 molal NaCl solution = 1 mole NaCl in 1 kg water. **(ii) Mole Fraction ($x$):** The ratio of the number of moles of a particular component to the total number of moles of all components in the solution. It is dimensionless. $$x_i = \frac{n_i}{n_{\text{total}}}$$ **Example:** In a binary mixture of 1 mole ethanol and 3 moles water, $x_{\text{ethanol}} = 0.25$ and $x_{\text{water}} = 0.75$. **(iii) Mass Percent ($w/w\%$):** The mass of the solute expressed as a percentage of the total mass of the solution. $$\% w/w = \frac{\text{mass of solute}}{\text{mass of solution}} \times 100$$ **Example:** A 10% (w/w) NaCl solution contains 10 g NaCl in 100 g of solution. #### Part (b) [7 Marks] - Numerical Solution **Step 1: Use the boiling point elevation formula to find molality.** $$\Delta T_b = K_b \times m$$ **Step 2: Substitute the known values.** $$0.52 = 0.52 \times m$$ **Step 3: Solve for molality.** $$m = \frac{0.52}{0.52} = 1.0 \text{ mol/kg}$$ **Step 4: Use the molality formula to find the molar mass.** Recall: $m = \dfrac{1000 \times w_B}{M_B \times W_A}$ **Step 5: Substitute the given data.** - $w_B = 6$ g (urea) - $W_A = 250$ g (water) - $m = 1.0$ mol/kg $$1.0 = \frac{1000 \times 6}{M_B \times 250}$$ **Step 6: Simplify the equation.** $$M_B = \frac{1000 \times 6}{1.0 \times 250} = \frac{6000}{250} = 24 \text{ g/mol}$$ **Step 7: Re-evaluate the calculation.** $$M_B = \frac{6000}{250} = 24 \text{ g/mol}$$ **Step 8: Compare with the standard molar mass of urea.** The actual molar mass of urea ($NH_2CONH_2$) is: - N: $2 \times 14 = 28$ - H: $4 \times 1 = 4$ - C: $1 \times 12 = 12$ - O: $1 \times 16 = 16$ - **Total = 60 g/mol** The calculated value of **24 g/mol** does **NOT match** the standard value of 60 g/mol. This indicates an experimental error or a misprint in the problem's data (the actual mass of urea that would yield $m = 1.0$ mol/kg in 250 g of water would be 15 g, not 6 g). --- ### Question B (Option 2): [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the relationship between molarity ($M$) and molality ($m$) of a solution. State clearly all assumptions used. **(b) [7 Marks]** The density of a 2.0 molal aqueous solution of NaCl is 1.08 g/mL. Calculate: (i) the molarity of the solution, and (ii) the mole fraction of NaCl in the solution. (Molar mass of NaCl = 58.5 g/mol, Molar mass of water = 18 g/mol). --- ### Model Solution for Question B: #### Part (a) [7 Marks] - Derivation **Assumption:** Consider **1 liter (1000 mL) of the solution** as the reference state. Let the solution contain a single solute dissolved in a pure solvent. **Step 1:** Let the density of the solution be $\rho$ (g/mL). Mass of 1 L of solution = $1000 \rho$ g. **Step 2:** Let the molarity be $M$ mol/L. In 1 L, moles of solute = $M$. Mass of solute = $M \times M_B$ g. **Step 3:** Mass of solvent (using conservation of mass): $$W_A = 1000\rho - M \times M_B \quad \text{(in grams)}$$ **Step 4:** Convert to kg: $W_A = \dfrac{1000\rho - M M_B}{1000}$ kg. **Step 5:** Apply molality formula: $m = \dfrac{n_{\text{solute}}}{W_{\text{solvent (kg)}}}$ **Step 6:** Substitute and simplify. $$m = \frac{M \times 1000}{1000\rho - M \times M_B}$$ This is the required relationship. #### Part (b) [7 Marks] - Numerical Solution **Given:** $m = 2.0$ mol/kg, $\rho = 1.08$ g/mL, $M_B$ (NaCl) = 58.5 g/mol, $M_A$ (water) = 18 g/mol. **(i) Molarity Calculation:** **Step 1:** Use the relation $m = \dfrac{1000 \times M}{1000\rho - M \times M_B}$ **Step 2:** Substitute values: $$2.0 = \frac{1000 \times M}{1000 \times 1.08 - M \times 58.5}$$ **Step 3:** Cross-multiply: $$2.0 \times (1080 - 58.5 M) = 1000 M$$ $$2160 - 117 M = 1000 M$$ $$2160 = 1117 M$$ **Step 4:** Solve: $$M = \frac{2160}{1117} = 1.934 \text{ mol/L}$$ **(ii) Mole Fraction of NaCl:** **Step 1:** Calculate moles of NaCl in 1 kg of water (= 1000 g of water). - Moles of water = $\dfrac{1000}{18} = 55.56$ mol - Moles of NaCl (from molality) = $2.0 \times 1 = 2.0$ mol **Step 2:** Total moles = $55.56 + 2.0 = 57.56$ mol **Step 3:** Mole fraction of NaCl: $$x_{\text{NaCl}} = \frac{2.0}{57.56} = 0.0347$$ The molarity is **1.934 mol/L** and the mole fraction of NaCl is **0.0347**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Unit Conversion Trap:** Forgetting to convert the mass of the solvent from **grams to kilograms** is the #1 reason students lose 1-2 marks per question. The denominator MUST be in kg. > 2. **Solute vs. Solvent Confusion:** In solutions with multiple components, always identify which is the solute (smaller quantity). Using the *solution* mass instead of *solvent* mass is a fatal error. > 3. **Formula Mismatch:** Using the molality formula $m = \dfrac{1000 w_B}{M_B W_A}$ in a problem where mass of solvent is already given in kg requires conversion; conversely, using $m = \dfrac{n_B}{W_A}$ (kg) when grams are given leads to answers 1000x too large. > 4. **Colligative Property Units:** The unit of $K_b$ and $K_f$ is **K·kg/mol** (not K/mol). Multiplying by molality gives the correct temperature elevation in Kelvin. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Molality ($m$)** = moles of solute per **kilogram of solvent** (not solution). - The SI unit is **mol/kg**, also called **molal**. - It is **temperature-independent**, making it the gold standard for colligative property calculations. - Master the compact form: $m = \dfrac{1000 \times w_B}{M_B \times W_A}$ (with mass in grams). - For interconversion: $m = \dfrac{1000 \times M}{1000\rho - M \times M_B}$. - Mole fraction to molality: $m = \dfrac{x_B \times 1000}{x_A \times M_A}$ (where $M_A$ is in g/mol). - Always identify the **solute (minor component)** and **solvent (major component)** before applying any formula. - Density ($\rho$) is the **bridge unit** between molarity (volume-based) and molality (mass-based). - Standard textbook value: A 1 molal aqueous solution contains **1 mole** of solute in **1000 g (= 1 kg)** of water. - **Boiling point elevation:** $\Delta T_b = K_b \times m$ — KTU favorite numerical. - **Freezing point depression:** $\Delta T_f = K_f \times m$ — KTU favorite numerical. - **Osmotic pressure:** $\pi = m \times R \times T \times i$ (where $i$ is the van't Hoff factor). - **Key distinction to memorize:** Molarity uses **volume of solution**; Molality uses **mass of solvent**. - Do not confuse the **lowercase italic $m$ (molality)** with the **bold M (molar mass)** or **uppercase M (molarity)**.

topics.title: Normality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Normality # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Normality (N)** is defined as the **number of gram equivalents of solute dissolved per litre of solution**. It is a measure of the **concentration** of a solution expressed in terms of the reactive (available) species per unit volume. Mathematically, normality is expressed as: $$N = \frac{\text{Number of gram equivalents of solute}}{\text{Volume of solution in litres (L)}}$$ Or equivalently, $$N = \frac{\text{Mass of solute (g)}}{\text{Equivalent weight (E)} \times V(\text{L})}$$ The **SI unit** of normality is **eq/L** (equivalents per litre) or **N** (a dimensionless label since 1 N = 1 eq/L). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of a water tank filled with workers, where each worker can do **one unit of work**. If you have only "specialist workers" (each capable of doing 5 units of work), fewer workers are needed to do the same job. **Normality counts the total work-units, not just the number of workers.** > > - **Molarity** = counts the *number of workers* (moles). > - **Normality** = counts the *work capacity* (moles × n-factor). So, **1 N H₂SO₄** delivers **twice** the chemical "reacting power" of **1 N HCl** per litre, even if both contain 1 mole of acid, because H₂SO₄ provides **2 replaceable H⁺ ions**. ## 1.3 Equivalent Weight and the n-Factor > [!IMPORTANT] > The **equivalent weight (E)** of a substance is the mass of the substance that will react with or supply **1 mole of electrons (for redox)** or **1 mole of H⁺ / OH⁻ ions (for acid-base reactions)**. $$E = \frac{\text{Molar Mass (M)}}{n\text{-factor}}$$ The **n-factor (valency factor)** depends on the type of reaction: - **For acids:** Number of replaceable H⁺ ions per molecule (e.g., HCl → 1, H₂SO₄ → 2, H₃PO₄ → 3). - **For bases:** Number of replaceable OH⁻ ions per molecule (e.g., NaOH → 1, Ca(OH)₂ → 2). - **For salts:** Total positive (or negative) charge of the cation (or anion) (e.g., NaCl → 1, Na₂SO₄ → 2, AlCl₃ → 3). - **For redox reactions:** Total number of electrons gained or lost per molecule. - **For oxidising/reducing agents in specific reactions:** Change in oxidation number per molecule. ## 1.4 Standards Highlighted in the KTU 2024 Syllabus - **Atomic Mass Unit (amu) = 1.6605 × 10⁻²⁴ g** - **1 Faraday (F) = 96487 C ≈ 96500 C** (charge of 1 mole of electrons) - Normality is **reaction-specific** — a solution may have different normalities in different reactions. - Normality **decreases** with dilution but **never increases**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework 1. **Identify the type of reaction** (acid-base, redox, precipitation) — this dictates the n-factor. 2. **Compute the molar mass (M)** of the solute using standard atomic weights. 3. **Determine the n-factor** from the balanced chemical equation. 4. **Calculate equivalent weight:** E = M / n. 5. **Weigh the solute** (mass in grams) and **dissolve** in a known volume (V in L). 6. **Apply the normality formula:** N = (m / E) / V. 7. **Verify** by cross-checking with **N = Molarity × n-factor**. ## 2.2 Why and How Behind Each Step - **Why identify the reaction type?** Because the n-factor is not an intrinsic property of the molecule; it depends on how the molecule participates. KMnO₄ can have n = 5 in acid, 3 in neutral, and 1 in strongly alkaline medium. - **Why is normality reaction-specific?** Because the "reactive capacity" of one mole of KMnO₄ is different in acidic vs. basic media. - **Why is N = Molarity × n-factor?** Because 1 mole contains n gram-equivalents. Therefore, multiplying molarity by n gives the normality. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **S.No** | **Formula / Relation** | **Description / Condition** | **Units** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $N = \dfrac{\text{equivalents}}{V(\text{L})}$ | Primary definition of normality | eq/L or N | | 2 | $N = \dfrac{m}{E \cdot V}$ | Mass-based normality (m = mass in g, V in L) | N | | 3 | $E = \dfrac{M}{n\text{-factor}}$ | Equivalent weight from molar mass | g/eq | | 4 | $N = M \times n\text{-factor}$ | Relation between Normality and Molarity | N | | 5 | $N_{1} V_{1} = N_{2} V_{2}$ | Dilution / titration equation (same substance) | — | | 6 | $N_{a} V_{a} = N_{b} V_{b}$ | Acid-base neutralization equivalence | — | | 7 | $\text{equivalents} = N \times V(\text{L})$ | Total equivalents in a given volume | eq | | 8 | $\text{equivalents} = \dfrac{m}{E}$ | Equivalents from mass | eq | | 9 | $\text{Normality} = \dfrac{\text{Strength (g/L)}}{E}$ | Strength (g/L) ÷ equivalent weight | N | | 10 | $N_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{N_{1} V_{1} + N_{2} V_{2}}{V_{1} + V_{2}}$ | Mixing of two solutions of same solute | N | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL SYLLABUS NOTE:** The equation **N₁V₁ = N₂V₂** is valid **only when both solutions refer to the same solute or same type of reaction**. For cross-reactions, the principle is **equivalents of acid = equivalents of base**. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Water Treatment Plants:** Normality of coagulants (alum, lime) and pH adjusters is critical for dose calculations. - **Chemical Process Industries (CPI):** Titrimetric analysis in quality control of acids, bases, and oxidising agents. - **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Preparation of standard solutions for assay testing. - **Effluent Treatment:** Determining COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand) and hardness using normality-based titrations. - **Electrochemical Cells:** Faraday's laws use normality concepts for current–mass calculations. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: N = Molarity × n-factor **Starting Point:** We know that **1 mole of a substance contains n gram-equivalents**, where n is the valency factor. By definition: $$\text{Molarity} (M) = \frac{\text{Number of moles of solute}}{\text{Volume of solution in L}}$$ $$\text{Normality} (N) = \frac{\text{Number of gram-equivalents of solute}}{\text{Volume of solution in L}}$$ Now, if the number of moles = x, then the number of gram-equivalents = x × n. Substituting into the normality equation: $$N = \frac{x \cdot n}{V(\text{L})}$$ Factor rearrangement: $$N = n \cdot \frac{x}{V(\text{L})}$$ Recognising that x / V = Molarity: $$\boxed{N = M \times n\text{-factor}}$$ ## 3.2 Worked Example — KTU Board Style **Problem:** A solution is prepared by dissolving **4.9 g of H₂SO₄** in water to make **500 mL** of solution. Calculate its **normality**. > **[KTU University Exam – May 2022, Model Problem]** ### Given Data - Mass of H₂SO₄, m = 4.9 g - Volume, V = 500 mL = 0.5 L - Molar mass of H₂SO₄, M = 98 g/mol - n-factor of H₂SO₄ (acid) = 2 ### Step 1: Calculate Equivalent Weight $$E = \frac{M}{n} = \frac{98}{2} = 49 \;\text{g/eq}$$ ### Step 2: Calculate Normality $$N = \frac{m}{E \times V(\text{L})}$$ $$N = \frac{4.9}{49 \times 0.5}$$ $$N = \frac{4.9}{24.5}$$ $$\boxed{N = 0.2 \;\text{N}}$$ ### Step 3: Verification Using N = M × n $$\text{Moles} = \frac{m}{M} = \frac{4.9}{98} = 0.05 \;\text{mol}$$ $$M = \frac{0.05}{0.5} = 0.1 \;\text{M}$$ $$N = 0.1 \times 2 = 0.2 \;\text{N} \;\;\checkmark$$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: n-Factor of Common Reagents | **Solute** | **Molar Mass (g/mol)** | **Reaction Type** | **n-factor** | **Equivalent Weight** | |---|---|---|---|---| | HCl | 36.5 | Acid-base | 1 | 36.5 | | H₂SO₄ | 98 | Acid-base | 2 | 49 | | HNO₃ | 63 | Acid-base | 1 | 63 | | H₃PO₄ | 98 | Acid-base | 3 | 32.67 | | NaOH | 40 | Acid-base | 1 | 40 | | KOH | 56 | Acid-base | 1 | 56 | | Ca(OH)₂ | 74 | Acid-base | 2 | 37 | | Na₂CO₃ (vs HCl) | 106 | Acid-base | 2 | 53 | | KMnO₄ (acidic) | 158 | Redox | 5 | 31.6 | | KMnO₄ (neutral) | 158 | Redox | 3 | 52.67 | | K₂Cr₂O₇ (acidic) | 294 | Redox | 6 | 49 | | FeSO₄ (acidic) | 152 | Redox | 1 | 152 | ## 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Problem — Titration Equivalence **Problem:** In a titration, **25 mL of an unknown HCl solution** requires **30 mL of 0.1 N NaOH** for complete neutralisation. Find the normality of the HCl solution. ### Given - V(acid) = 25 mL, V(base) = 30 mL, N(base) = 0.1 N ### Step 1: Apply Equivalence Law At the end-point, equivalents of acid = equivalents of base. $$N_{a} \cdot V_{a} = N_{b} \cdot V_{b}$$ ### Step 2: Substitute Values $$N_{a} \times 25 = 0.1 \times 30$$ $$N_{a} = \frac{0.1 \times 30}{25}$$ $$N_{a} = \frac{3}{25}$$ $$\boxed{N_{a} = 0.12 \;\text{N}}$$ ### Step 3: Molarity Cross-Check (HCl has n = 1) $$M = N / n = 0.12 / 1 = 0.12 \;\text{M} \;\;\checkmark$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Normality Calculation Flowchart (Mermaid Block Diagram) The following diagram illustrates the systematic decision flow for determining the normality of any solution, which is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** suitable for the KTU board examination answer. ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start: Problem Statement]) --> stepB{Identify the
Solute Type} stepB -- Acid --> stepC1[Count Replaceable
H plus ions
n equals to H count] stepB -- Base --> stepC2[Count Replaceable
OH minus ions
n equals to OH count] stepB -- Salt --> stepC3[Total Positive
or Negative Charge
n equals to charge] stepB -- Oxidant Reductant --> stepC4[Total Electrons
Transferred per Molecule
n equals to electron count] stepC1 --> stepD[Compute Molar Mass
M of solute from
atomic weights] stepC2 --> stepD stepC3 --> stepD stepC4 --> stepD stepD --> stepE[Calculate Equivalent
Weight
E equals M divided by n] stepE --> stepF{Given
Information?} stepF -- Mass and Volume --> stepG[Apply Formula
N equals m divided by
E times V in litres] stepF -- Molarity Known --> stepH[Apply Relation
N equals M times n] stepF -- Titration Data --> stepI[Apply N1V1 equals N2V2
or equivalents balance] stepG --> stepJ([End: Normality
Value in eq per litre]) stepH --> stepJ stepI --> stepJ ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Role of Normality in a Process Plant ```mermaid flowchart LR rawA[Raw Acid or Base
Storage Tank] --> pumpA[Feed Pump] pumpA --> mixerA[Inline Static Mixer] mixerA --> titratorA[Online pH Titrator
Uses Normality Standards] titratorA --> reactorA[Main Reactor Vessel] reactorA --> ctrlA[PLC / DCS Control Loop
Adjusts Dose based on N] ctrlA -- Feedback Signal --> pumpA reactorA --> outletA[Treated Product Stream] ``` > [!NOTE] > The block diagram above depicts the **functional role of normality-based standard solutions** in a typical chemical dosing loop used in process industries. The titrator uses normality principles to determine residual acid/base concentration, and the controller adjusts the feed rate accordingly. ## 4.3 Conceptual Hierarchy Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD concA[Concentration Measures] --> molA[Molarity
moles per litre] concA --> normA[Normality
equivalents per litre] concA --> molalA[Molality
moles per kg solvent] concA --> strA[Strength
grams per litre] normA --> eqA[Equivalent Weight
M divided by n factor] eqA --> nfA[n factor rules] nfA --> nfa1[Acid: H plus count] nfA --> nfa2[Base: OH minus count] nfA --> nfa3[Redox: electron count] nfA --> nfa4[Salt: total charge] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Q1.a]** **Q1.** Define normality. State its SI unit. **Model Answer:** Normality (N) is the number of gram equivalents of solute dissolved per litre of solution. It is mathematically expressed as: $$N = \frac{\text{Number of gram equivalents}}{\text{Volume of solution (L)}}$$ **SI unit:** eq/L (equivalents per litre), commonly denoted simply as "N". --- > **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 1, Q1.b]** **Q2.** Calculate the normality of a solution containing **5.85 g of NaCl** in **500 mL** of solution. (Molar mass of NaCl = 58.5 g/mol, n-factor of NaCl = 1) **Model Answer:** Given: m = 5.85 g, V = 0.5 L, M = 58.5 g/mol, n = 1 $$E = \frac{M}{n} = \frac{58.5}{1} = 58.5 \;\text{g/eq}$$ $$N = \frac{m}{E \times V} = \frac{5.85}{58.5 \times 0.5} = \frac{5.85}{29.25}$$ $$\boxed{N = 0.2 \;\text{N}}$$ ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1, Q2]** ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** Derive the relationship between normality and molarity. Explain with a suitable example why the same solution can have **different normalities in different reactions**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** **20 mL of a H₂SO₄ solution** requires **30 mL of 0.5 N NaOH** for complete neutralisation. Calculate: **(i)** the normality of H₂SO₄, **(ii)** its molarity, and **(iii)** the mass of H₂SO₄ present in 1 litre of the solution. **(7 marks)** ### ✅ Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Derivation:** As shown in Section 3.1, $$N = M \times n\text{-factor}$$ **Reason for different normalities in different reactions:** Consider **KMnO₄**: - In **acidic medium:** MnO₄⁻ → Mn²⁺, n = 5 (gain of 5 electrons). - In **neutral/weakly basic medium:** MnO₄⁻ → MnO₂, n = 3. - In **strongly alkaline medium:** MnO₄⁻ → MnO₄²⁻, n = 1. For a **0.1 M KMnO₄ solution**: - In acid: N = 0.1 × 5 = **0.5 N** - In neutral: N = 0.1 × 3 = **0.3 N** - In alkali: N = 0.1 × 1 = **0.1 N** Hence, **normality is reaction-specific**, while molarity remains 0.1 M in all cases. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** V(acid) = 20 mL, V(base) = 30 mL, N(base) = 0.5 N **(i) Normality of H₂SO₄:** $$N_{a} V_{a} = N_{b} V_{b}$$ $$N_{a} \times 20 = 0.5 \times 30$$ $$N_{a} = \frac{15}{20} = 0.75 \;\text{N}$$ **(ii) Molarity of H₂SO₄ (n = 2):** $$M = \frac{N}{n} = \frac{0.75}{2} = 0.375 \;\text{M}$$ **(iii) Mass of H₂SO₄ per litre:** $$\text{Strength} = N \times E = 0.75 \times 49 = 36.75 \;\text{g/L}$$ --- ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** Explain the concept of **equivalent weight**. With suitable examples, derive the n-factor for: (i) H₃PO₄ in complete neutralisation, (ii) K₂Cr₂O₇ in acidic redox, and (iii) Na₂CO₃ when titrated against HCl. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A solution of **glacial acetic acid** has a density of **1.05 g/mL** and is **99% pure by mass**. Calculate the normality of this commercial acid. (Molar mass of CH₃COOH = 60 g/mol, n-factor = 1) **(7 marks)** ### ✅ Model Solution for Question B #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Equivalent weight** is the mass of a substance that reacts with or supplies 1 mole of H⁺, OH⁻, or 1 mole of electrons. It is given by E = M / n. **(i) H₃PO₄ (complete neutralisation):** $$\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 + 3\text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Na}_3\text{PO}_4 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ All 3 H⁺ are replaced, so **n = 3**, and E = 98 / 3 = **32.67 g/eq**. **(ii) K₂Cr₂O₇ (acidic redox):** $$\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-} + 14\text{H}^+ + 6\text{e}^- \rightarrow 2\text{Cr}^{3+} + 7\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ Each Cr changes by 3 units; 2 Cr atoms per molecule ⇒ **n = 6**, E = 294 / 6 = **49 g/eq**. **(iii) Na₂CO₃ (titrated with HCl):** $$\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow 2\text{NaCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2$$ Two moles of HCl react per mole of Na₂CO₃ ⇒ **n = 2**, E = 106 / 2 = **53 g/eq**. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** Density = 1.05 g/mL, Purity = 99% by mass, M = 60 g/mol, n = 1 **Step 1:** Mass of 1 L of solution = 1.05 × 1000 = 1050 g **Step 2:** Mass of pure acetic acid = 0.99 × 1050 = 1039.5 g **Step 3:** Number of moles = 1039.5 / 60 = 17.325 mol **Step 4:** Number of equivalents = moles × n = 17.325 × 1 = 17.325 eq **Step 5:** Normality = equivalents / volume (L) = 17.325 / 1 $$\boxed{N = 17.325 \;\text{N} \;\approx\; 17.33 \;\text{N}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Deduction Points:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert mL to L** in the volume term — this gives a 10× or 1000× error. > 2. **Using atomic mass instead of molar mass** in equivalent-weight calculations (e.g., taking Na = 23 instead of NaOH = 40). > 3. **Applying the wrong n-factor** — students often hardcode n = 1 for acids, missing the 2 for H₂SO₄. > 4. **Misapplying N₁V₁ = N₂V₂** when the solutes are different — it is valid **only at the equivalence point** of a titration; otherwise, use the equivalents balance. > 5. **Forgetting reaction conditions for KMnO₄** — examiners specifically test acidic vs. basic n-factors. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Normality (Module 1, PBSFT304)** - **Definition:** Normality = gram-equivalents of solute / litres of solution. - **SI unit:** eq/L or N (dimensionless label). - **Core relation:** $N = M \times n\text{-factor}$. - **Equivalent weight:** $E = M / n\text{-factor}$, expressed in g/eq. - **n-factor for acids** = number of replaceable H⁺ ions; **for bases** = number of OH⁻ ions; **for salts** = total positive/negative charge; **for redox** = number of electrons transferred. - **Titration equivalence:** At end-point, equivalents of acid = equivalents of base. - **Dilution law:** $N_1 V_1 = N_2 V_2$ (same substance). - **Mixing law:** $N_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{N_1 V_1 + N_2 V_2}{V_1 + V_2}$ (same solute type). - **Reaction-specific:** Normality changes with reaction type; molarity does not. - **Always convert volume to litres** before substituting in formulas. - **Use atomic weights** from the standard periodic table: H = 1, O = 16, Na = 23, S = 32, K = 39, Mn = 55, Cr = 52, C = 12. - **Faraday's constant** F = 96500 C is useful for redox normality problems. - **Normality is always ≤ Molarity × |highest valency|** — never fabricate n-factors exceeding the maximum oxidation-state change. - **Practical utility:** Widely used in titration, water softening, COD analysis, and process control dosing.

topics.title: PPM; Ideal Gases and Gas Mixtures - Ideal Gas Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** PPM; Ideal Gases and Gas Mixtures - Ideal Gas Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Ideal Gas — Formal Definition

An **ideal gas** is a hypothetical gas whose molecules occupy negligible space and experience no intermolecular forces of attraction or repulsion. The state of an ideal gas is completely described by four measurable variables: **Pressure (P)**, **Volume (V)**, **Temperature (T)**, and **Number of moles (n)**, related through the ideal gas equation:

$$P V = n R T$$

where **R** is the **universal gas constant** = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** = **0.08206 L·atm/(mol·K)** = **1.987 cal/(mol·K)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Real gases approach ideal behavior at **low pressure (P → 0)** and **high temperature (T → ∞)**. The ideal gas law serves as the foundational reference equation for all gas-phase stoichiometric and process calculations in this course.

### 1.2 Gas Mixture — Definition

A **gas mixture** is a physical combination of two or more gases that do not react chemically with each other. Each component gas retains its individual molecular identity and exerts its own pressure independently of the others, behaving exactly as if it alone occupied the entire container.

### 1.3 Parts Per Million (PPM) — Definition

**PPM (Parts Per Million)** is a dimensionless concentration unit that expresses the amount of a constituent (component) per one million parts of the total mixture. For gas mixtures, PPM is commonly expressed as **PPM by volume (ppmv)** or **PPM by mole (ppmm)**, and for ideal gases these are numerically equal to mole fraction × 10⁶.

$$y_i \,(\text{ppm}) = \frac{n_i}{n_{\text{total}}} \times 10^6 = \frac{V_i}{V_{\text{total}}} \times 10^6 = \frac{p_i}{P_{\text{total}}} \times 10^6$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is PPM used?** When dealing with trace pollutants (like SO₂, NOₓ, CO in flue gases) or impurities, mole fractions become extremely small numbers (e.g., 0.00005). Expressing them as **50 ppm** is far more practical and intuitive for engineers working in environmental, safety, and process industries.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bouncing Balls in a Box"

Imagine a large empty box (the container) in which two types of rubber balls are bouncing:
- **Red balls** (Gas A) — collide with walls and with each other but exert no "sticky" pull.
- **Blue balls** (Gas B) — behave the same way.

Each ball type contributes to the **total wall pressure** independently. If you removed all blue balls, the red balls alone would still exert a pressure (their **partial pressure**). The total pressure is simply the **sum** of these independent contributions. This is exactly how Dalton visualized gas mixtures, leading to **Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures**. PPM is then just a way of saying "for every 1 million balls in the box, how many are red?"

---

### 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Reference Conditions (KTU Standard)

| Parameter | Symbol | Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Universal Gas Constant | R | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| Universal Gas Constant | R | **0.08206 L·atm/(mol·K)** |
| Universal Gas Constant | R | **1.987 cal/(mol·K)** |
| Standard Temperature | T_std | **273.15 K (0 °C)** or **298.15 K (25 °C)** |
| Standard Pressure | P_std | **1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar = 760 mmHg = 14.696 psia** |
| Avogadro's Number | N_A | **6.022 × 10²³ molecules/mol** |
| Molar Volume at STP | V_m | **22.414 L/mol** (at 0 °C, 1 atm) |
| Molar Volume at SATP | V_m | **24.79 L/mol** (at 25 °C, 1 atm) |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Ideal Gas Law — Origin and Logic

The ideal gas law is a **combination of three empirical gas laws**:

1. **Boyle's Law** (P ∝ 1/V at constant T, n): $P V = \text{constant}$
2. **Charles's Law** (V ∝ T at constant P, n): $V/T = \text{constant}$
3. **Avogadro's Law** (V ∝ n at constant P, T): $V/n = \text{constant}$

Combining all three into a single proportional relationship gives: $V \propto \dfrac{nT}{P}$, and introducing the proportionality constant R yields:

$$P V = n R T$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind R:** The constant **R** is the *scaling factor* that converts the simple proportionality (V ∝ nT/P) into an equality. Its numerical value depends only on the **units chosen** for P, V, T, and n. **Always select R to match the units of P and V** in the problem.

### 2.2 Ideal Gas Mixture Laws — The Two Pillars

#### 2.2.1 Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures (Additivity of Pressures)

The **total pressure** of a gas mixture equals the **sum of the partial pressures** of the individual components, where the partial pressure of a component is the pressure it would exert if it alone occupied the container at the same T and V.

$$P_{\text{total}} = p_A + p_B + p_C + \cdots = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i$$

And the partial pressure of component *i* is:

$$p_i = y_i \, P_{\text{total}} = \frac{n_i}{n_{\text{total}}} \, P_{\text{total}}$$

where $y_i$ is the **mole fraction** of component i.

**Intuition (Why?):** Because ideal gas molecules have *no intermolecular forces*, each molecule "sees" only the walls. The collision frequency of A-molecules with the walls is unaffected by the presence of B-molecules. Hence, the *contributions* to wall pressure are strictly additive.

#### 2.2.2 Amagat's Law of Partial Volumes (Additivity of Volumes)

The **total volume** of a gas mixture equals the **sum of the partial volumes** of the individual components, where the partial volume of a component is the volume it would occupy at the same T and P as the mixture.

$$V_{\text{total}} = V_A + V_B + V_C + \cdots = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i$$

And the partial volume of component *i* is:

$$V_i = \tilde{y}_i \, V_{\text{total}} = \frac{n_i}{n_{\text{total}}} \, V_{\text{total}}$$

where $\tilde{y}_i$ is the **volume fraction** = **mole fraction** for ideal gases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** For ideal gases, **mole fraction = volume fraction = partial pressure fraction** at the same T and P.

### 2.3 Apparent (Average) Molecular Weight of a Gas Mixture

Since each gas contributes proportionally to the total mass based on its mole fraction and molecular weight:

$$M_{\text{avg}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i M_i$$

where $y_i$ is the mole fraction and $M_i$ is the molecular weight of component i.

This is essential because once we know $M_{\text{avg}}$, the gas mixture behaves as a **single pseudo-pure gas** with molecular weight $M_{\text{avg}}$, allowing direct application of the ideal gas law:

$$P V = \frac{m}{M_{\text{avg}}} R T$$

where $m$ is the total mass of the gas mixture.

### 2.4 Density of an Ideal Gas Mixture

$$\rho_{\text{mix}} = \frac{P \, M_{\text{avg}}}{R T}$$

### 2.5 PPM Conversions for Ideal Gas Mixtures

For an ideal gas at pressure P and temperature T, the molar concentration is $n/V = P/(RT)$. Therefore:

$$c_i \,(\text{mol/L}) = y_i \cdot \frac{P}{RT} = \frac{\text{ppm}_i \times 10^{-6} \times P}{RT}$$

$$\text{ppm}_i = y_i \times 10^6 = \frac{p_i \,(\text{in same units as } P)}{P_{\text{total}}} \times 10^6$$

$$\frac{\text{mg}}{\text{m}^3} = \frac{\text{ppm}_i \times M_i \times P}{R T \times 10^3}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (KTU Valuation Trap):** When converting **ppm by volume** to **mg/m³** (or vice versa), students often forget the **P/(RT)** factor, which is the *molar density*. Without it, the conversion is dimensionally invalid.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Variables / Units | Applicable Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ideal Gas Law | $P V = n R T$ | P [Pa/atm], V [m³/L], n [mol], T [K], R [8.314 or 0.08206] | Ideal gas behavior (low P, high T) |
| 2 | Density of Ideal Gas | $\rho = \dfrac{P M}{R T}$ | $\rho$ [kg/m³ or g/L] | Ideal gas, M = molecular weight |
| 3 | Dalton's Law | $P = \sum p_i$ | $p_i = y_i P$ | Ideal gas mixture |
| 4 | Amagat's Law | $V = \sum V_i$ | $V_i = \tilde{y}_i V$ | Ideal gas mixture |
| 5 | Mole Fraction | $y_i = \dfrac{n_i}{\sum n_j}$ | Dimensionless | $\sum y_i = 1$ |
| 6 | Apparent M.W. of Mixture | $M_{\text{avg}} = \sum y_i M_i$ | [kg/kmol or g/mol] | Ideal gas mixture |
| 7 | PPM by Volume | $\text{ppm}_i = y_i \times 10^6$ | Dimensionless (×10⁻⁶ scale) | Ideal gas mixture |
| 8 | Specific Gravity (Gas) | $SG = \dfrac{M}{29}$ (vs air) | Dimensionless | Air has M ≈ 28.97 g/mol |
| 9 | Mass-Mole Relation | $n_i = \dfrac{m_i}{M_i}$ | m [g or kg] | Always valid |
| 10 | Partial Pressure | $p_i = \dfrac{n_i R T}{V}$ | Same as P | Ideal gas |
| 11 | Combined Gas Law | $\dfrac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}$ | For a fixed mass of gas | Ideal gas |
| 12 | Molar Volume | $V_m = \dfrac{R T}{P}$ | L/mol at given T,P | Ideal gas |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These equations are the *backbone* of combustion calculations, flue gas analysis, air pollution control, petrochemical refinery design, cryogenics, HVAC systems, and process safety relief-valve sizing — all of which fall under Chemical Process Principles.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures

**Statement to Prove:** For an ideal gas mixture in a container of volume V at temperature T, the total pressure equals the sum of the individual partial pressures.

**Starting Point:** Apply the ideal gas law to each component gas i (treating it as if it alone occupies the volume V at temperature T):

$$p_i V = n_i R T$$

**Therefore, the partial pressure of component i is:**

$$p_i = \frac{n_i R T}{V}$$

**Summing over all components i = 1 to N:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{n_i R T}{V}$$

**Since R, T, and V are the same for all components, they can be factored out:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i = \frac{R T}{V} \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i$$

**But the total number of moles in the mixture is:**

$$n_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i$$

**Substituting and applying the ideal gas law to the total mixture (treating it as one gas):**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i = \frac{n_{\text{total}} R T}{V} = P_{\text{total}}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{total}} = p_A + p_B + p_C + \cdots = \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i}$$

This is **Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures**. ∎

### 3.2 Derivation of the Expression for Apparent Molecular Weight

**Starting point:** Total mass of the gas mixture is the sum of masses of all components:

$$m_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i = \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i M_i$$

**Dividing both sides by the total number of moles:**

$$\frac{m_{\text{total}}}{n_{\text{total}}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i M_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \frac{n_i}{n_{\text{total}}} \right) M_i$$

**Recognizing that $n_i / n_{\text{total}} = y_i$ (mole fraction):**

$$\frac{m_{\text{total}}}{n_{\text{total}}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i M_i$$

**The left side is, by definition, the apparent (average) molecular weight:**

$$\boxed{M_{\text{avg}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i M_i}$$

### 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Partial Pressure Calculation

**Problem:** A gas mixture contains **0.5 mol N₂**, **0.3 mol O₂**, and **0.2 mol CO₂** in a **10 L** container at **T = 300 K**. Calculate the partial pressure of each gas and the total pressure. (R = 0.08206 L·atm/(mol·K))

**Solution — Step 1: Compute Total Moles**

$$n_{\text{total}} = 0.5 + 0.3 + 0.2 = 1.0 \, \text{mol}$$

**Solution — Step 2: Compute Mole Fractions**

$$y_{N_2} = \frac{0.5}{1.0} = 0.5, \quad y_{O_2} = \frac{0.3}{1.0} = 0.3, \quad y_{CO_2} = \frac{0.2}{1.0} = 0.2$$

**Solution — Step 3: Compute Total Pressure Using Ideal Gas Law**

$$P = \frac{n_{\text{total}} R T}{V} = \frac{(1.0)(0.08206)(300)}{10}$$

$$P = 2.4618 \, \text{atm}$$

**Solution — Step 4: Compute Partial Pressures Using Dalton's Law**

$$p_{N_2} = y_{N_2} \cdot P = 0.5 \times 2.4618 = 1.2309 \, \text{atm}$$

$$p_{O_2} = y_{O_2} \cdot P = 0.3 \times 2.4618 = 0.7385 \, \text{atm}$$

$$p_{CO_2} = y_{CO_2} \cdot P = 0.2 \times 2.4618 = 0.4924 \, \text{atm}$$

**Verification (Sum):** $1.2309 + 0.7385 + 0.4924 = 2.4618$ atm ✓ (matches total pressure)

### 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Apparent Molecular Weight & Density

**Problem:** Dry air has the following composition by mole: **N₂ = 79%**, **O₂ = 21%**. Calculate (a) the apparent molecular weight of air, (b) the density of air at **T = 25 °C** and **P = 1 atm**.

**Given:** $M_{N_2} = 28 \, \text{g/mol}$, $M_{O_2} = 32 \, \text{g/mol}$, R = 0.08206 L·atm/(mol·K), T = 298.15 K.

**Solution — Part (a):**

$$M_{\text{air}} = y_{N_2} M_{N_2} + y_{O_2} M_{O_2} = (0.79)(28) + (0.21)(32)$$

$$M_{\text{air}} = 22.12 + 6.72 = 28.84 \, \text{g/mol}$$

(Note: More precise value used in engineering is **28.97 g/mol**, accounting for Ar, CO₂, etc.)

**Solution — Part (b):** Using $\rho = \dfrac{P M}{R T}$:

$$\rho_{\text{air}} = \frac{(1 \, \text{atm})(28.84 \, \text{g/mol})}{(0.08206 \, \text{L·atm/(mol·K)})(298.15 \, \text{K})}$$

$$\rho_{\text{air}} = \frac{28.84}{24.466} = 1.179 \, \text{g/L} = 1.179 \, \text{kg/m}^3$$

### 3.5 Worked Example 3 — PPM to Mole Fraction & Mass Concentration

**Problem:** A flue gas contains **200 ppmv** of SO₂ at **P = 1 atm** and **T = 450 K**. Calculate (a) the mole fraction of SO₂, (b) the partial pressure of SO₂, (c) the concentration in **mg/m³**. (M_SO₂ = 64 g/mol)

**Solution — Part (a): Mole Fraction**

$$y_{SO_2} = 200 \times 10^{-6} = 2.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Solution — Part (b): Partial Pressure**

$$p_{SO_2} = y_{SO_2} \cdot P = (2.0 \times 10^{-4})(1 \, \text{atm}) = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{atm}$$

$$p_{SO_2} = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 101.325 \, \text{kPa} = 0.02027 \, \text{kPa} = 20.27 \, \text{Pa}$$

**Solution — Part (c): Mass Concentration (mg/m³)**

**Step 1:** Molar concentration of the mixture:

$$c_{\text{mix}} = \frac{P}{RT} = \frac{101325 \, \text{Pa}}{(8.314 \, \text{J/(mol·K)})(450 \, \text{K})}$$

$$c_{\text{mix}} = \frac{101325}{3741.3} = 27.08 \, \text{mol/m}^3$$

**Step 2:** Molar concentration of SO₂:

$$c_{SO_2} = y_{SO_2} \cdot c_{\text{mix}} = (2.0 \times 10^{-4})(27.08)$$

$$c_{SO_2} = 5.416 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{mol/m}^3$$

**Step 3:** Convert to mg/m³:

$$\text{mass conc.} = c_{SO_2} \times M_{SO_2} \times 1000 \, \text{mg/g}$$

$$\text{mass conc.} = (5.416 \times 10^{-3})(64)(1000) = 346.6 \, \text{mg/m}^3$$

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Equation to Use?

| Problem Scenario | Primary Equation | Secondary Step |
|---|---|---|
| Find P given n, V, T | $P = nRT/V$ | Choose R based on units |
| Find V given m, T, P | $V = \dfrac{m R T}{M P}$ | Need molecular weight M |
| Find density of a gas | $\rho = \dfrac{PM}{RT}$ | Must know M |
| Partial pressure from mole fraction | $p_i = y_i P$ | First compute $y_i$ |
| PPM ↔ Mole fraction | $\text{ppm} = y_i \times 10^6$ | Direct for ideal gases |
| Apparent M of mixture | $M_{\text{avg}} = \sum y_i M_i$ | Weighted by mole fractions |
| PPM to mg/m³ | $\text{mg/m}^3 = \text{ppm} \times \dfrac{M \cdot P}{R T \times 10^3}$ | Requires M and T,P |
| Specific gravity of gas | $SG = M_{\text{gas}}/29$ | Reference: air (29) |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concept Map — Ideal Gases and Gas Mixtures

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IDEAL GAS SYSTEM] --> B[Pure Gas]
    A --> C[Gas Mixture]
    
    B --> B1[P V = n R T]
    B1 --> B2[Density: rho = PM/RT]
    B1 --> B3[Specific Gravity = M/29]
    
    C --> D[Dalton's Law]
    C --> E[Amagat's Law]
    C --> F[Concentration Units]
    
    D --> D1[P_total = sum of p_i]
    D1 --> D2[p_i = y_i P_total]
    
    E --> E1[V_total = sum of V_i]
    E1 --> E2[V_i = y_i V_total]
    
    F --> F1[Mole Fraction y_i]
    F --> F2[Mass Fraction w_i]
    F --> F3[PPM = y_i x 10^6]
    F --> F4[mg per cubic meter]
    
    C --> G[Apparent Molecular Weight]
    G --> G1[M_avg = sum of y_i M_i]
    G1 --> G2[Treat mixture as pseudo-pure gas]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — PPM Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Input: ppm value of component i] --> M1[Convert ppm to mole fraction y_i]
    M1 --> M2[Multiply by total pressure P]
    M2 --> OUT1[Output: Partial Pressure p_i]
    
    IN2[Input: Mole fractions y_i and M_i] --> M3[Compute M_avg = sum y_i M_i]
    M3 --> M4[Apply ideal gas law]
    M4 --> OUT2[Output: Density of mixture]
    
    IN3[Input: ppm_i, M_i, P, T] --> M5[Compute P over RT molar density]
    M5 --> M6[Multiply by ppm_i and M_i]
    M6 --> OUT3[Output: mg per cubic meter]
    
    IN4[Input: Total moles n_total and n_i] --> M7[Compute y_i = n_i over n_total]
    M7 --> M8[Multiply by 10 to power 6]
    M8 --> OUT4[Output: ppm_i]
```

### 4.3 Process Flow — Solving a Typical Gas Mixture Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Identify all components and given data] --> S2[Step 2: Compute mole fractions y_i]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Select appropriate gas law]
    S3 --> S4{Is the system pure gas or mixture?}
    S4 -->|Pure Gas| S5[Apply PV = nRT directly]
    S4 -->|Mixture| S6[Compute M_avg from mole fractions]
    S6 --> S7[Apply PV = n_total R T using M_avg]
    S5 --> S8[Step 4: Compute partial properties if needed]
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9[Step 5: Verify units and Dalton's Law sum]
```
