## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**State Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures. Under what conditions is it strictly applicable?**

**Model Answer:**

Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures states that **the total pressure exerted by a mixture of non-reacting gases is equal to the sum of the partial pressures of the individual component gases**, where the partial pressure of each component is the pressure it would exert if it alone occupied the container at the same temperature.

$$P_{\text{total}} = p_A + p_B + p_C + \cdots = \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i$$

**Conditions of Applicability:**
1. The gases must **not react chemically** with one another.
2. The gases must behave as **ideal gases** (i.e., no intermolecular forces, negligible molecular volume).
3. The law holds exactly in the **limit of low pressure and high temperature**.

**[3 Marks: 2 for statement, 1 for conditions]**

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Define Parts Per Million (PPM). How is it related to the mole fraction of a component in an ideal gas mixture?**

**Model Answer:**

**PPM (Parts Per Million)** is a dimensionless concentration unit representing the number of parts of a component present per one million parts of the total mixture.

**Relation to Mole Fraction:**

For an ideal gas mixture:

$$\text{ppm}_i = y_i \times 10^6 = \frac{n_i}{n_{\text{total}}} \times 10^6$$

Equivalently:

$$y_i = \frac{\text{ppm}_i}{10^6}$$

For ideal gases, **ppm by volume = ppm by mole = partial pressure fraction × 10⁶**, since volume fraction = mole fraction = pressure fraction under ideal behavior. PPM is especially useful for expressing concentrations of trace components such as pollutants (SO₂, NOₓ, CO).

**[3 Marks: 1 for definition, 2 for relation]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] — **14 Marks**

**(a)** A gas mixture has the following composition by volume: **H₂ = 30%, CO = 20%, CO₂ = 40%, N₂ = 10%**. The mixture is contained in a vessel of **5 m³** at a pressure of **2 atm** and temperature of **400 K**. Calculate:
   (i) The partial pressure of each component. **[3 Marks]**
   (ii) The apparent (average) molecular weight of the mixture. **[2 Marks]**
   (iii) The mass of each component in the mixture and the total mass. **[2 Marks]**

**(b)** A flue gas stream contains **350 ppm by volume** of NO. If the flue gas is at **1.2 atm** and **500 K**, calculate:
   (i) The mole fraction and partial pressure of NO. **[2 Marks]**
   (ii) The concentration of NO in **mg/m³** and **µg/m³**. **[3 Marks]**
   (iii) The molar concentration of the total gas mixture. **[2 Marks]**

**Given:** $M_{H_2} = 2$, $M_{CO} = 28$, $M_{CO_2} = 44$, $M_{N_2} = 28$, $M_{NO} = 30$ (all in g/mol). R = 0.08206 L·atm/(mol·K) or 8.314 J/(mol·K).

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a)(i) — Partial Pressures:**

Since volume % = mole % for ideal gases, mole fractions are:
$y_{H_2} = 0.30$, $y_{CO} = 0.20$, $y_{CO_2} = 0.40$, $y_{N_2} = 0.10$

Applying Dalton's Law:

$$p_{H_2} = 0.30 \times 2 = 0.60 \, \text{atm}$$

$$p_{CO} = 0.20 \times 2 = 0.40 \, \text{atm}$$

$$p_{CO_2} = 0.40 \times 2 = 0.80 \, \text{atm}$$

$$p_{N_2} = 0.10 \times 2 = 0.20 \, \text{atm}$$

**Verification:** $0.60 + 0.40 + 0.80 + 0.20 = 2.00$ atm ✓

**Part (a)(ii) — Apparent Molecular Weight:**

$$M_{\text{avg}} = \sum y_i M_i$$

$$M_{\text{avg}} = (0.30)(2) + (0.20)(28) + (0.40)(44) + (0.10)(28)$$

$$M_{\text{avg}} = 0.6 + 5.6 + 17.6 + 2.8 = 26.6 \, \text{g/mol}$$

**Part (a)(iii) — Mass of Each Component:**

Total moles from ideal gas law:

$$n_{\text{total}} = \frac{P V}{R T} = \frac{(2 \, \text{atm})(5000 \, \text{L})}{(0.08206)(400)}$$

$$n_{\text{total}} = \frac{10000}{32.824} = 304.7 \, \text{mol}$$

Moles of each component:

$$n_{H_2} = 0.30 \times 304.7 = 91.4 \, \text{mol}$$

$$n_{CO} = 0.20 \times 304.7 = 60.9 \, \text{mol}$$

$$n_{CO_2} = 0.40 \times 304.7 = 121.9 \, \text{mol}$$

$$n_{N_2} = 0.10 \times 304.7 = 30.5 \, \text{mol}$$

Mass of each:

$$m_{H_2} = 91.4 \times 2 = 182.8 \, \text{g}$$

$$m_{CO} = 60.9 \times 28 = 1705.2 \, \text{g}$$

$$m_{CO_2} = 121.9 \times 44 = 5363.6 \, \text{g}$$

$$m_{N_2} = 30.5 \times 28 = 854.0 \, \text{g}$$

Total mass:

$$m_{\text{total}} = 182.8 + 1705.2 + 5363.6 + 854.0 = 8105.6 \, \text{g} \approx 8.11 \, \text{kg}$$

**Cross-check using $M_{\text{avg}}$:**

$$m_{\text{total}} = n_{\text{total}} \times M_{\text{avg}} = 304.7 \times 26.6 = 8105.0 \, \text{g} \quad ✓$$

**Part (b)(i) — Mole Fraction & Partial Pressure of NO:**

$$y_{NO} = 350 \times 10^{-6} = 3.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$p_{NO} = y_{NO} \times P_{\text{total}} = (3.5 \times 10^{-4})(1.2 \, \text{atm})$$

$$p_{NO} = 4.2 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{atm} = 0.0425 \, \text{kPa}$$

**Part (b)(ii) — Concentration in mg/m³ and µg/m³:**

Molar concentration of mixture:

$$c_{\text{mix}} = \frac{P}{RT} = \frac{(1.2 \times 101325 \, \text{Pa})}{(8.314)(500)} = \frac{121590}{4157} = 29.25 \, \text{mol/m}^3$$

Molar concentration of NO:

$$c_{NO} = y_{NO} \times c_{\text{mix}} = (3.5 \times 10^{-4})(29.25) = 1.024 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{mol/m}^3$$

Mass concentration:

$$\rho_{NO} = c_{NO} \times M_{NO} = (1.024 \times 10^{-2})(30 \, \text{g/mol}) = 0.3071 \, \text{g/m}^3$$

$$\rho_{NO} = 307.1 \, \text{mg/m}^3 = 3.071 \times 10^5 \, \mu\text{g/m}^3$$

**Part (b)(iii) — Molar Concentration of Total Mixture:**

Already computed: $c_{\text{mix}} = 29.25 \, \text{mol/m}^3$

---

**Question B.** [KTU University Exam — June 2023] — **14 Marks**

**(a)** State and derive the **Ideal Gas Law** from the combined statement of Boyle's Law and Charles's Law. Show that the universal gas constant R is consistent across units. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A **2 L** cylinder contains a mixture of **8 g CH₄** and **12 g C₂H₆** at **27 °C**. Calculate:
   (i) The mole fraction of each component. **[2 Marks]**
   (ii) The total pressure exerted by the gas mixture. **[2 Marks]**
   (iii) The partial pressure of each component using Dalton's Law. **[2 Marks]**
   (iv) The density of the gas mixture. **[1 Mark]**

**Given:** $M_{CH_4} = 16$, $M_{C_2H_6} = 30$ g/mol. R = 0.08206 L·atm/(mol·K).

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of Ideal Gas Law:**

**Statement of Boyle's Law:** At constant temperature and fixed mass, the volume of a gas is inversely proportional to pressure:

$$V \propto \frac{1}{P} \quad \Rightarrow \quad PV = k_1 \, (\text{at constant } T)$$

**Statement of Charles's Law:** At constant pressure and fixed mass, the volume of a gas is directly proportional to absolute temperature:

$$V \propto T \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{V}{T} = k_2 \, (\text{at constant } P)$$

**Combining both proportionalities:**

$$V \propto \frac{T}{P} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V = k_3 \cdot \frac{T}{P}$$

Introducing **Avogadro's Law** (V ∝ n at constant T, P):

$$V = R \cdot \frac{n T}{P}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{P V = n R T}$$

**Consistency of R across units:**

Since PV/nT must yield the same physical dimension (energy per mole per kelvin), the numerical value of R changes with the units chosen for P and V:

$$R = 8.314 \, \frac{\text{J}}{\text{mol·K}} = 0.08206 \, \frac{\text{L·atm}}{\text{mol·K}} = 1.987 \, \frac{\text{cal}}{\text{mol·K}}$$

Verification using 1 J = 0.009869 L·atm:
$0.08206 / 8.314 = 0.00987$ ✓

**Part (b)(i) — Mole Fractions:**

$$n_{CH_4} = \frac{8 \, \text{g}}{16 \, \text{g/mol}} = 0.5 \, \text{mol}$$

$$n_{C_2H_6} = \frac{12 \, \text{g}}{30 \, \text{g/mol}} = 0.4 \, \text{mol}$$

$$n_{\text{total}} = 0.5 + 0.4 = 0.9 \, \text{mol}$$

$$y_{CH_4} = \frac{0.5}{0.9} = 0.5556, \quad y_{C_2H_6} = \frac{0.4}{0.9} = 0.4444$$

**Part (b)(ii) — Total Pressure:**

T = 27 °C = 300 K

$$P = \frac{n_{\text{total}} R T}{V} = \frac{(0.9)(0.08206)(300)}{2}$$

$$P = \frac{22.156}{2} = 11.08 \, \text{atm}$$

**Part (b)(iii) — Partial Pressures:**

$$p_{CH_4} = y_{CH_4} \cdot P = (0.5556)(11.08) = 6.156 \, \text{atm}$$

$$p_{C_2H_6} = y_{C_2H_6} \cdot P = (0.4444)(11.08) = 4.924 \, \text{atm}$$

Verification: $6.156 + 4.924 = 11.08$ atm ✓

**Part (b)(iv) — Density:**

Total mass = 8 + 12 = 20 g

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V} = \frac{20 \, \text{g}}{2 \, \text{L}} = 10 \, \text{g/L} = 10 \, \text{kg/m}^3$$

Cross-check via $M_{\text{avg}}$:

$$M_{\text{avg}} = (0.5556)(16) + (0.4444)(30) = 8.89 + 13.33 = 22.22 \, \text{g/mol}$$

$$\rho = \frac{P M_{\text{avg}}}{RT} = \frac{(11.08)(22.22)}{(0.08206)(300)} = \frac{246.2}{24.62} = 10.0 \, \text{g/L} \quad ✓$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert °C to K (add 273.15), or cm³ to L, or kPa to atm. *Always state conversions explicitly.*
> 2. **Wrong R Selection (–2 marks):** Using R = 0.08206 when V is in m³, or R = 8.314 when V is in L. *Match R to P-V units.*
> 3. **PPM to Mole Fraction (–2 marks):** Forgetting the 10⁻⁶ factor when converting ppm to mole fraction or vice versa.
> 4. **Partial Pressure Misapplication (–2 marks):** Using $p_i = n_i RT/V$ directly without realizing it gives the same answer as $y_i P$ — both are valid for ideal gases, but KTU expects you to *show* which principle you used.
> 5. **Mole fraction ≠ volume fraction at high pressure:** This is only true for ideal gases; in real gases at high P, this assumption breaks down.
> 6. **No unit on mole fraction / PPM:** Always state that these are *dimensionless* for clarity.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember — At a Glance:**

- ✅ **Ideal Gas Equation:** $P V = n R T$ — the *master equation*; memorize it, and know **three values of R** for the three common unit systems.
- ✅ **Mole Fraction:** $y_i = n_i / n_{\text{total}}$, always dimensionless; **sum of all yᵢ = 1**.
- ✅ **Dalton's Law:** $P_{\text{total}} = \sum p_i$; valid for ideal gases / non-reacting gases; partial pressure $p_i = y_i P$.
- ✅ **Amagat's Law:** $V_{\text{total}} = \sum V_i$; valid for ideal gases; partial volume $V_i = y_i V$.
- ✅ **For Ideal Gases:** Mole fraction = Volume fraction = Pressure fraction.
- ✅ **Apparent Molecular Weight:** $M_{\text{avg}} = \sum y_i M_i$ — a gas mixture can be treated as a *single pseudo-component* with this M.
- ✅ **Density of Gas:** $\rho = PM/RT$ — applies to pure gases and gas mixtures using $M_{\text{avg}}$.
- ✅ **PPM Definition:** $\text{ppm}_i = y_i \times 10^6$ — used for **trace components**.
- ✅ **PPM to mg/m³ conversion:** $\text{mg/m}^3 = \text{ppm}_i \times M_i \times P / (R T \times 10^3)$ — remember the $P/(RT)$ molar density factor.
- ✅ **Specific Gravity of Gas:** $SG = M_{\text{gas}} / 29$ (since air ≈ 29 g/mol).
- ✅ **Standard Conditions:**
   - **STP:** T = 273.15 K, P = 1 atm, Vₘ = 22.414 L/mol.
   - **SATP:** T = 298.15 K, P = 1 atm, Vₘ = 24.79 L/mol.
- ✅ **Validity of Ideal Gas Law:** Most accurate at **low P, high T**; real gases deviate near condensation, at high pressures (>10 atm for many gases), and near critical points.
- ✅ **Always convert temperature to Kelvin** before substituting into any gas-law equation.
- ✅ **Always verify:** Sum of partial pressures = total pressure; sum of mole fractions = 1.

---

**End of Notes — Module 1, Topic: PPM; Ideal Gases and Gas Mixtures — Ideal Gas Law**
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme | PBSFT304 — Chemical Process Principles*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Amagat's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Amagat's Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Amagat's Law of Additive Volumes (also known as Leduc's Law):**
> *"The total volume occupied by a mixture of non-reacting, non-ideal (or ideal) gases is exactly equal to the sum of the individual partial volumes that each pure gas would occupy when existing alone at the same temperature **T** and pressure **P** as the mixture."*

Mathematically, for a mixture of **N** components:

$$V_{\text{mix}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i \quad \text{(at constant T and P)}$$

where:
- $V_i$ = partial volume of component *i* in the mixture
- $V_{\text{mix}}$ = total volume of the gaseous mixture
- The summation is performed at **constant T and P**

> [!NOTE]
> **Partial Volume ($V_i$):** The volume that component *i* would occupy if it existed alone at the same temperature and pressure as the mixture. It is a hypothetical construct used to mathematically decompose the mixture volume.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **large transparent jar** filled with marbles, ping-pong balls, and tiny beads — all mixed together. Now imagine the jar, the air inside, and a flexible membrane that can perfectly isolate each marble, ball, or bead.

Amagat's Law says something beautifully simple:

> *"If you gave each type of ball its OWN jar, but kept the temperature and air pressure exactly the same as the original big jar, the total volume of all those individual jars would be IDENTICAL to the volume of the original big jar."*

In other words, **volumes add up like Lego blocks** at constant T and P. The gas molecules do not "notice" each other (in the ideal case), so the total volume is just the sum of individual contributions.

**Real-world industrial analogy:** A chemical engineer designing a multi-component gas absorption column (e.g., absorbing $CO_2$ from a mixture of $CO_2 + N_2$) relies on Amagat's Law to **add the volumes** of each gas stream entering the absorber to predict the total volumetric flow rate at column conditions.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Temperature (KTU/Reference):** $T = 273.15\ \text{K} = 0\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- **Standard Pressure (KTU/Reference):** $P = 1\ \text{atm} = 101.325\ \text{kPa} = 1.01325\ \text{bar}$
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 0.082057\ \text{L·atm·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1} = 8.3145\ \text{J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$
- **Avogadro's Number:** $N_A = 6.02214076 \times 10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Volume Fraction vs. Mole Fraction — A Key KTU Insight:**
> For gases that obey (or closely approximate) the ideal gas law, the **volume fraction** of a component is numerically equal to its **mole fraction**:
> $$y_i = \frac{V_i}{V_{\text{mix}}} = \frac{n_i}{n_{\text{total}}}$$
> This is **only** true because $V \propto n$ at fixed T and P (from the ideal gas equation).

---

## 1.4 Syllabus Context: Why KTU Includes Amagat's Law

Amagat's Law is the **complementary counterpart to Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures**. While Dalton's Law tells engineers how pressures add up at constant T and V, Amagat's Law tells them how **volumes add up at constant T and P**. Together, they form the foundational toolkit for handling multi-component gas mixtures in:

- **Material balances** on reactors and separators
- **Humidification and psychrometric calculations**
- **Compressibility factor** treatments (Amagat's Law forms the basis for defining pseudo-reduced volumes for mixtures)
- **Gas absorption / stripping** column design
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

The operational logic of Amagat's Law can be broken down into **five coherent steps**:

- **Step 1 — Postulate Component Independence:** Each gas in the mixture behaves as though it alone occupies the entire volume at the given T and P. The total volume is the **sum of independent contributions**.

- **Step 2 — Define Partial Volume:** The partial volume of component *i* is defined as:
$$V_i = \frac{n_i R T}{P} \quad \text{(ideal gas case)}$$
where $n_i$ is the moles of component *i*, evaluated at the mixture's T and P.

- **Step 3 — Sum the Partial Volumes:** Apply the additive principle:
$$V_{\text{mix}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{n_i R T}{P} = \frac{R T}{P} \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i = \frac{R T \cdot n_{\text{total}}}{P}$$

- **Step 4 — Recover Ideal Gas Law:** The summation yields exactly the ideal gas law applied to the total mixture. This shows **internal self-consistency**: Amagat's Law is **automatically satisfied** for ideal gas mixtures.

- **Step 5 — Extend to Real Gases via Compressibility:** For **real (non-ideal)** gases, Amagat's Law is reformulated using the **Amagat rule of mixtures** for the compressibility factor $Z_m$ of the mixture:
$$Z_m V_m = \sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i Z_i V_i \quad \text{(at constant T and P)}$$
where $Z_i$ is the compressibility factor of pure component *i* and $y_i$ is its mole (or volume) fraction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why" matters in KTU valuations:** Examiners frequently award partial credit for the "why" of each step. Students who can articulate *why* volumes add linearly (molecules don't interact in ideal case) typically score higher than those who merely write the formula.

---

## 2.2 Dalton's Law vs. Amagat's Law — The Critical Distinction

| Feature | Dalton's Law | Amagat's Law |
|---|---|---|
| **Quantity that adds** | Partial **pressures** | Partial **volumes** |
| **Conditions held constant** | Constant **T** and **V** | Constant **T** and **P** |
| **Mathematical form** | $P = \sum p_i$ | $V = \sum V_i$ |
| **Defines** | Partial pressure $p_i$ | Partial volume $V_i$ |
| **Mole fraction relation** | $y_i = p_i / P$ (ideal) | $y_i = V_i / V$ (ideal) |
| **Strict validity** | Strictly true for ideal gases; approximates real gases at low P | Strictly true for ideal gases; useful for real gases with Kay's mixing rule |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For ideal gas mixtures, BOTH laws are EXACTLY equivalent.** They are two different ways of slicing the same ideal gas pie. The choice depends on whether P or V is the convenient variable.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Units (SI) | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = \sum V_i$ | Amagat's Law — total volume is sum of partial volumes | $m^3$ | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | $V_i = n_i R T / P$ | Partial volume of component *i* (ideal gas) | $m^3$ | ★★★★★ |
| 3 | $y_i = V_i / V = n_i / n_{\text{tot}}$ | Volume fraction = mole fraction (ideal) | dimensionless | ★★★★★ |
| 4 | $V = n_{\text{tot}} R T / P$ | Mixture ideal gas equation | $m^3$ | ★★★★★ |
| 5 | $p_i = y_i P$ | Partial pressure (Dalton's analogue) | Pa | ★★★★★ |
| 6 | $Z_m = \sum y_i Z_i$ | Amagat rule for mixture compressibility (Kay's rule) | dimensionless | ★★★★ |
| 7 | $P_c,\text{mix} = \sum y_i P_{c,i}$ | Pseudo-critical pressure (Kay's rule) | Pa | ★★★★ |
| 8 | $T_c,\text{mix} = \sum y_i T_{c,i}$ | Pseudo-critical temperature (Kay's rule) | K | ★★★★ |

> **KTU Note on Kay's Rule:** The pseudo-critical constants ($P_{c,\text{mix}}$ and $T_{c,\text{mix}}$) obtained via Kay's rule are then used to look up a single mixture $Z_m$ from generalized compressibility charts — this is a KTU-favourite 14-mark problem.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Liquefaction of air:** Engineers use Amagat's Law to compute the combined volume of $N_2$ and $O_2$ streams before mixing and feeding a cryogenic distillation column.
- **Flue gas analysis:** Power plant engineers add the volumes of $CO_2$, $H_2O$, $N_2$, and $O_2$ in flue gas to determine the volumetric flow rate for designing pollution-control equipment.
- **Compressibility correction:** When handling natural gas ($CH_4 + C_2H_6 + C_3H_8 + \dots$), the Amagat rule (Kay's) provides the mixture $Z$ used in real gas flow equations.
- **Biogas engineering:** $CH_4 + CO_2$ mixtures are routinely treated using Amagat's additive volume principle for digester gas mass balance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Amagat's Law from First Principles (Ideal Gas Case)

We begin with the **ideal gas law** applied to each component *i* in the mixture, evaluated at the **mixture temperature T** and **mixture pressure P**.

**Starting point** — Component *i* in the mixture has partial volume $V_i$:

$$V_i = \frac{n_i R T}{P}$$

**Step 1** — Sum partial volumes of all N components:

$$V_{\text{mix}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i$$

**Step 2** — Substitute the ideal gas expression for each $V_i$:

$$V_{\text{mix}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{n_i R T}{P}$$

**Step 3** — Since **R, T, and P** are **identical** for all terms in the summation, factor them out:

$$V_{\text{mix}} = \frac{R T}{P} \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i$$

**Step 4** — Recognize that $\sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i = n_{\text{total}}$:

$$V_{\text{mix}} = \frac{R T \cdot n_{\text{total}}}{P}$$

**Step 5** — This is the **ideal gas law** for the total mixture. Thus, Amagat's Law is **derived rigorously** from the ideal gas law. $\blacksquare$

> **Transition logic explanation:** The key insight is that T and P are common to ALL components in the sum. Factoring them out converts a sum of N terms into a single algebraic expression in $n_{\text{total}}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Proof that Volume Fraction = Mole Fraction (Ideal Gases)

**Start** with the definition of volume fraction:

$$f_i \equiv \frac{V_i}{V_{\text{mix}}}$$

**Step 1** — Substitute $V_i = n_i R T / P$ in the numerator:

$$\text{Numerator: } V_i = \frac{n_i R T}{P}$$

**Step 2** — Substitute $V_{\text{mix}} = n_{\text{tot}} R T / P$ in the denominator:

$$\text{Denominator: } V_{\text{mix}} = \frac{n_{\text{tot}} R T}{P}$$

**Step 3** — Form the ratio:

$$f_i = \frac{\dfrac{n_i R T}{P}}{\dfrac{n_{\text{tot}} R T}{P}}$$

**Step 4** — Cancel $R T / P$ from numerator and denominator (the common factor):

$$f_i = \frac{n_i}{n_{\text{tot}}} \equiv y_i$$

**Step 5** — Conclusion: **volume fraction = mole fraction** for ideal gas mixtures. $\blacksquare$

> **Transition logic explanation:** The R T / P terms cancel cleanly because both numerator and denominator are evaluated at the same T and P. This is why Amagat's Law is "exact" for ideal gases but only approximate for real gases.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III: Amagat's Rule for Real Gas Mixture Compressibility (Kay's Rule)

**Start** with the real gas law applied to component *i*:

$$P V_i = Z_i n_i R T$$

**Step 1** — Solve for the partial volume $V_i$:

$$V_i = \frac{Z_i n_i R T}{P}$$

**Step 2** — Sum over all N components (Amagat's principle):

$$V_{\text{mix}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{Z_i n_i R T}{P}$$

**Step 3** — Express moles in terms of mole fraction: $n_i = y_i \cdot n_{\text{tot}}$:

$$V_{\text{mix}} = \frac{R T}{P} \sum_{i=1}^{N} Z_i \cdot y_i \cdot n_{\text{tot}} = \frac{n_{\text{tot}} R T}{P} \sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i Z_i$$

**Step 4** — Apply the real gas law to the mixture as a whole: $P V_{\text{mix}} = Z_m n_{\text{tot}} R T$, which gives:

$$\frac{V_{\text{mix}} P}{n_{\text{tot}} R T} = Z_m$$

**Step 5** — Equate the two expressions for $V_{\text{mix}} / (n_{\text{tot}} R T / P)$:

$$Z_m = \sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i Z_i$$

**Step 6** — This is **Amagat's rule (Kay's mixing rule)** for mixture compressibility. $\blacksquare$

> **Transition logic explanation:** The $P$, $R$, and $T$ cancel because we are evaluating both pure-component and mixture volumes at the **same T and P** — the cornerstone of Amagat's Law. The result is a **mole-fraction-weighted linear average** of pure-component compressibilities.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A gas mixture contains $0.4\ \text{mol}\ CH_4$, $0.35\ \text{mol}\ C_2H_6$, and $0.25\ \text{mol}\ CO_2$ at $T = 350\ \text{K}$ and $P = 10\ \text{bar}$. Calculate: (a) the total volume using Amagat's Law, (b) the partial volume of each component, and (c) the volume (mole) fractions.

**Given:**
- $n_{CH_4} = 0.4\ \text{mol}$, $n_{C_2H_6} = 0.35\ \text{mol}$, $n_{CO_2} = 0.25\ \text{mol}$
- $T = 350\ \text{K}$, $P = 10\ \text{bar} = 10 \times 10^5\ \text{Pa} = 10^6\ \text{Pa}$
- $R = 8.314\ \text{J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$

**Solution (a) — Total volume:**

**Step 1** — Total moles:
$$n_{\text{tot}} = 0.4 + 0.35 + 0.25 = 1.00\ \text{mol}$$

**Step 2** — Apply ideal gas law (Amagat's Law result):
$$V_{\text{mix}} = \frac{n_{\text{tot}} R T}{P}$$

**Step 3** — Substitute values:
$$V_{\text{mix}} = \frac{1.00 \times 8.314 \times 350}{10^6}$$

**Step 4** — Compute:
$$V_{\text{mix}} = \frac{2909.9}{10^6} = 2.9099 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3 = 2.91\ \text{L}$$

**Solution (b) — Partial volumes:**

**Step 1** — Use $V_i = n_i R T / P$:

**Step 2** — For $CH_4$:
$$V_{CH_4} = \frac{0.4 \times 8.314 \times 350}{10^6} = \frac{1163.96}{10^6} = 1.164 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3 = 1.164\ \text{L}$$

**Step 3** — For $C_2H_6$:
$$V_{C_2H_6} = \frac{0.35 \times 8.314 \times 350}{10^6} = \frac{1018.45}{10^6} = 1.018 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3 = 1.018\ \text{L}$$

**Step 4** — For $CO_2$:
$$V_{CO_2} = \frac{0.25 \times 8.314 \times 350}{10^6} = \frac{727.48}{10^6} = 7.275 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3 = 0.7275\ \text{L}$$

**Step 5** — Verify Amagat's Law:
$$V_{\text{mix}} = 1.164 + 1.018 + 0.7275 = 2.910\ \text{L} \checkmark$$

**Solution (c) — Volume (= mole) fractions:**

**Step 1** — For $CH_4$:
$$y_{CH_4} = \frac{1.164}{2.910} = 0.400$$

**Step 2** — For $C_2H_6$:
$$y_{C_2H_6} = \frac{1.018}{2.910} = 0.350$$

**Step 3** — For $CO_2$:
$$y_{CO_2} = \frac{0.7275}{2.910} = 0.250$$

**Step 4** — Sum check:
$$0.400 + 0.350 + 0.250 = 1.000 \checkmark$$

> **KTU Valuer's Note:** Always verify the sum of fractions equals 1.000 — this is a free 1-mark check that examiners love to award.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flowchart: Amagat's Law Operational Logic

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **logical pipeline** of how Amagat's Law is applied in a chemical engineering problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify gas mixture components] --> B[Note mixture T and P]
    B --> C[Compute moles of each component]
    C --> D[Apply ideal gas law: Vi equals ni RT over P]
    D --> E[Sum partial volumes: Vmix equals sum of Vi]
    E --> F[Compute volume fraction: yi equals Vi divided by Vmix]
    F --> G{Real gas correction needed?}
    G -- No --> H[Use Amagat result directly for material balance]
    G -- Yes --> I[Look up Z_i for each pure component]
    I --> J[Apply Kay mixing rule: Zmix equals sum of yi Zi]
    J --> K[Compute Vmix real equals Zmix ntot RT over P]
    K --> L[Report final mixture volume to process design]
```

> **Reading guide:** Start from node `A` (top-left) and follow the arrows downward. The diamond-shaped decision node `G` branches the calculation into **ideal gas** (right exit) or **real gas with Kay's rule** (left exit) paths.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Amagat vs. Dalton in Process Design

This diagram shows how Amagat's Law (volume-additive) and Dalton's Law (pressure-additive) are **two complementary views** of the same mixture, deployed in different unit operations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT STREAMS]
        IG1[Pure gas 1: T, P, n1]
        IG2[Pure gas 2: T, P, n2]
        IG3[Pure gas N: T, P, nN]
    end

    subgraph MIX[MIXING ZONE]
        AG[Amagat Law: Vmix equals sum of Vi at constant T and P]
        DL[Dalton Law: Pmix equals sum of pi at constant T and V]
    end

    subgraph OUT[OUTPUT APPLICATIONS]
        OM1[Reactor material balance]
        OM2[Compressor design]
        OM3[Absorption column sizing]
        OM4[Cryogenic distillation feed]
    end

    IG1 --> AG
    IG2 --> AG
    IG3 --> AG
    AG --> OM1
    AG --> OM4
    AG --> OM3
    DL --> OM2
    DL --> OM4
```

> **Reading guide:** Multiple input streams (left block) feed into both the **Amagat** and **Dalton** law modules (center block). The output applications (right block) select whichever law is most convenient for the given T, P, or V constraint of the unit operation.

---

## 4.3 State-Transition Diagram: Constant T, P Constraint of Amagat's Law

This diagram emphasizes the **invariant T and P** that defines the Amagat's Law framework, contrasted with Dalton's framework.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Mixture state: Tmix, Pmix, Vmix, ni set] --> S1[Decompose into pure component states]
    S1[Decompose into pure component states] --> S2[Component i hypothetical state: Tmix, Pmix, Vi]
    S2[Component i hypothetical state: Tmix, Pmix, Vi] --> S3[Sum partial volumes]
    S3[Sum partial volumes] --> S4[Recover mixture state Vmix]
    S4[Recover mixture state Vmix] --> S0[Mixture state Tmix Pmix Vmix ni set]
```

> **Reading guide:** The cycle emphasizes that the **T and P of each hypothetical pure component are identical to the T and P of the mixture**. Only V (and the mole count $n_i$) varies during the decomposition and recombination.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State Amagat's Law of additive volumes. Write its mathematical form and define partial volume.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Statement (2 marks):** Amagat's Law states that the total volume of a gaseous mixture at constant temperature **T** and pressure **P** is equal to the sum of the **partial volumes** of the individual component gases, where each partial volume is the volume that the component would occupy if it existed alone at the same T and P as the mixture.
- **Mathematical form (1 mark):**
$$V_{\text{mix}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i \quad \text{(at constant T and P)}$$

---

### Question A2
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**For an ideal gas mixture, show that the volume fraction of a component equals its mole fraction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Step 1 (1 mark):** For component *i*, $V_i = n_i R T / P$ and $V_{\text{mix}} = n_{\text{tot}} R T / P$.
- **Step 2 (1 mark):** Volume fraction $f_i = V_i / V_{\text{mix}}$.
- **Step 3 (1 mark):** Substituting and cancelling $R T / P$: $f_i = n_i / n_{\text{tot}} = y_i$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — INTERNAL CHOICE (14 Marks)

### Question A (Option 1)

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Paper 1]`

**(a)** Derive Amagat's Law from the ideal gas law for an N-component gas mixture. Show all intermediate steps and state the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gas mixture at $T = 400\ \text{K}$ and $P = 5\ \text{bar}$ contains the following: $0.6\ \text{kmol}$ of $N_2$, $0.3\ \text{kmol}$ of $O_2$, and $0.1\ \text{kmol}$ of $CO_2$. Using Amagat's Law, calculate the **total volume** of the mixture, the **partial volume** of each component, and verify the additive property. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A(a)

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
- Mixture behaves as an ideal gas.
- Components do not react chemically with each other.
- T and P are uniform throughout the mixture.

**Derivation (6 marks):**

**Step 1:** For the *i*-th component, ideal gas law gives:
$$P V_i = n_i R T \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_i = \frac{n_i R T}{P}$$

**Step 2:** Amagat's principle (additive volumes):
$$V_{\text{mix}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i$$

**Step 3:** Substitute partial volume expression:
$$V_{\text{mix}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{n_i R T}{P} = \frac{R T}{P} \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i$$

**Step 4:** Recognize $\sum n_i = n_{\text{tot}}$:
$$V_{\text{mix}} = \frac{n_{\text{tot}} R T}{P}$$

**Step 5:** This is precisely the ideal gas law for the total mixture, confirming Amagat's Law. $\blacksquare$

---

### Model Solution — Question A(b)

**Given:** $n_{N_2} = 0.6\ \text{kmol}$, $n_{O_2} = 0.3\ \text{kmol}$, $n_{CO_2} = 0.1\ \text{kmol}$, $T = 400\ \text{K}$, $P = 5 \times 10^5\ \text{Pa}$, $R = 8.314\ \text{kJ·kmol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$.

**Step 1:** Total moles:
$$n_{\text{tot}} = 0.6 + 0.3 + 0.1 = 1.0\ \text{kmol}$$

**Step 2:** Total volume (Amagat's Law / ideal gas law):
$$V_{\text{mix}} = \frac{n_{\text{tot}} R T}{P} = \frac{1.0 \times 8.314 \times 400}{5 \times 10^5} = \frac{3325.6}{5 \times 10^5} = 6.651 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{mix}} = 6.651 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3 = 6.651\ \text{L}}$$

**Step 3:** Partial volume of $N_2$:
$$V_{N_2} = \frac{0.6 \times 8.314 \times 400}{5 \times 10^5} = 3.991 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3 = 3.991\ \text{L}$$

**Step 4:** Partial volume of $O_2$:
$$V_{O_2} = \frac{0.3 \times 8.314 \times 400}{5 \times 10^5} = 1.995 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3 = 1.995\ \text{L}$$

**Step 5:** Partial volume of $CO_2$:
$$V_{CO_2} = \frac{0.1 \times 8.314 \times 400}{5 \times 10^5} = 6.651 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3 = 0.665\ \text{L}$$

**Step 6:** Verification of additive property:
$$V_{N_2} + V_{O_2} + V_{CO_2} = 3.991 + 1.995 + 0.665 = 6.651\ \text{L} = V_{\text{mix}}\ \checkmark$$

**Step 7 (Bonus — volume fractions):**
$$y_{N_2} = 3.991/6.651 = 0.600,\quad y_{O_2} = 1.995/6.651 = 0.300,\quad y_{CO_2} = 0.665/6.651 = 0.100$$
Sum: $0.600 + 0.300 + 0.100 = 1.000\ \checkmark$

---

### Question B (Option 2 — Alternative Internal Choice)

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Paper 2]`

**(a)** State Dalton's Law of partial pressures and Amagat's Law of additive volumes. Compare them in a tabular form, clearly highlighting the constant variables in each law. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A real gas mixture at $T = 500\ \text{K}$ and $P = 30\ \text{bar}$ consists of $40\%\ CH_4$ and $60\%\ C_2H_6$ on a mole basis. The compressibility factors of the pure gases at their respective pseudo-reduced states are $Z_{CH_4} = 0.95$ and $Z_{C_2H_6} = 0.82$. Using **Amagat's rule (Kay's mixing rule)**, calculate the mixture compressibility factor $Z_m$ and the actual molar volume of the mixture. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B(a)

**Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures (3 marks):**
The total pressure of a mixture of non-reacting ideal gases is the sum of the partial pressures of the individual components. Each partial pressure is the pressure that the gas would exert if it occupied the volume of the mixture alone **at the same temperature T**.

$$P_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \quad \text{(at constant T and V)}$$

**Amagat's Law of Additive Volumes (3 marks):**
The total volume of a mixture of non-reacting ideal gases is the sum of the partial volumes of the individual components. Each partial volume is the volume that the gas would occupy at the **same temperature T and pressure P** as the mixture.

$$V_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i \quad \text{(at constant T and P)}$$

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Dalton's Law | Amagat's Law |
|---|---|---|
| Additive quantity | Pressures $p_i$ | Volumes $V_i$ |
| Constants held | $T, V$ | $T, P$ |
| Governing relation | $p_i = y_i P$ | $V_i = y_i V$ |

---

### Model Solution — Question B(b)

**Given:** $y_{CH_4} = 0.40$, $y_{C_2H_6} = 0.60$, $Z_{CH_4} = 0.95$, $Z_{C_2H_6} = 0.82$, $T = 500\ \text{K}$, $P = 30\ \text{bar} = 3 \times 10^6\ \text{Pa}$, $R = 8.314\ \text{J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$.

**Step 1:** Apply Amagat's rule (Kay's mixing rule) for mixture compressibility:
$$Z_m = \sum y_i Z_i = y_{CH_4} \cdot Z_{CH_4} + y_{C_2H_6} \cdot Z_{C_2H_6}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:
$$Z_m = (0.40)(0.95) + (0.60)(0.82)$$

**Step 3:** Compute each term:
$$Z_m = 0.380 + 0.492 = 0.872$$

$$\boxed{Z_m = 0.872}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the **real-gas molar volume** using the real gas equation:
$$P V_m = Z_m R T \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_m = \frac{Z_m R T}{P}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute values:
$$V_m = \frac{0.872 \times 8.314 \times 500}{3 \times 10^6}$$

**Step 6:** Compute numerator:
$$0.872 \times 8.314 \times 500 = 3624.9\ \text{J/mol}$$

**Step 7:** Divide by pressure:
$$V_m = \frac{3624.9}{3 \times 10^6} = 1.208 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3/\text{mol}$$

$$\boxed{V_m = 1.208 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3/\text{mol} = 1.208\ \text{L/mol}}$$

**Step 8 (Comparison check):** Ideal gas molar volume at same T and P:
$$V_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{R T}{P} = \frac{8.314 \times 500}{3 \times 10^6} = 1.386 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3/\text{mol}$$

The real value is smaller, indicating **attractive intermolecular forces** dominate, consistent with $Z_m < 1$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (loses 2 marks):** Mixing $P$ in bar with $R$ in J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ without conversion. Always convert P to Pa (SI) when using R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹.
> 2. **Confusing Dalton with Amagat (loses 3 marks):** Writing $V = \sum p_i$ or $P = \sum V_i$. Memorize: **Dalton = pressures add**; **Amagat = volumes add**.
> 3. **Forgetting to sum partial volumes to verify (loses 1 mark):** Always do the additive check $V_{\text{mix}} = \sum V_i$ — examiners reward verification.
> 4. **Applying Amagat's Law to reacting mixtures (loses 2 marks):** Amagat's Law assumes **no chemical reaction** between components. For reacting systems, you must first do a **mole balance** to find the equilibrium composition.
> 5. **Skipping assumptions in derivations (loses 1 mark):** Always explicitly state: (i) ideal gas behaviour, (ii) no chemical reaction, (iii) uniform T and P, (iv) closed system equilibrium.
> 6. **Kay's rule units confusion (loses 1 mark):** $Z_m$ is **dimensionless**. Do NOT write units in the final box.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Amagat's Law (Module 1)**

- **Core statement:** Total volume of a gas mixture = sum of partial volumes, at constant **T** and **P**.
- **Math form:** $V_{\text{mix}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i$
- **Partial volume definition:** $V_i = n_i R T / P$ (ideal gas) — volume component *i* would occupy alone at mixture's T and P.
- **Ideal gas result:** $V_{\text{mix}} = n_{\text{tot}} R T / P$ — fully consistent with the ideal gas law.
- **Volume fraction identity:** $y_i = V_i / V_{\text{mix}} = n_i / n_{\text{tot}}$ (only for ideal gases).
- **Dalton vs. Amagat:** Dalton → pressures add at constant T, V. Amagat → volumes add at constant T, P.
- **Real gas extension (Kay's rule):** $Z_m = \sum y_i Z_i$ — mole-fraction-weighted average of pure-component Z's.
- **Validity:** Strictly true for ideal gases; approximation for real gases at **low to moderate** pressures; replace with EOS (e.g., Peng–Robinson) at high pressures.
- **Applicability:** Non-reacting gas mixtures only — reacting systems require mole balance first.
- **Standard reference conditions:** $T = 273.15\ \text{K}$, $P = 1\ \text{atm} = 101.325\ \text{kPa}$.
- **Engineer's use:** Material balances, compressor design, absorption column sizing, flue gas analysis, cryogenic air separation, biogas engineering.
- **KTU hot-button:** Always state assumptions before applying; always verify the additive check $\sum V_i = V_{\text{mix}}$ in numerical answers.
- **Common pitfall to avoid:** Do NOT apply Amagat's Law to liquids (it is specifically for gases where $V \propto n$ at fixed T, P).
- **Cross-connection:** Amagat's Law combined with **Kay's rule** allows generalized compressibility charts to be used for real gas mixtures — a 14-mark KTU favourite.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dalton's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dalton's Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures** (also known as Dalton's Law of Additive Pressures) is a fundamental gas law formulated by the English chemist and physicist **John Dalton** in **1801**. The law states that:

> *"The total pressure exerted by a non-reacting mixture of ideal gases in a closed container is equal to the algebraic sum of the partial pressures of the individual gases."*

Mathematically, for a mixture containing $n$ components:

$$P_{total} = p_1 + p_2 + p_3 + \dots + p_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i$$

Where:
- $P_{total}$ = Total pressure of the gas mixture
- $p_i$ = Partial pressure of the $i^{th}$ component
- $n$ = Total number of components in the mixture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Partial Pressure ($p_i$)**: The partial pressure of an individual gas in a mixture is defined as the pressure that the gas would exert if it occupied the entire volume of the mixture alone at the same temperature. In other words, it is the hypothetical pressure contribution of a single gas component.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: Dalton's Law is a cornerstone of Module 1 (Stoichiometry & Ideal Gases) and is directly applied in problems involving humidification, gas absorption, distillation, and combustion calculations. Students are expected to derive partial pressure relations using both mole fraction and volumetric approaches.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **large classroom** filled with students from different departments — Mechanical, Civil, Electrical, and Computer Science — all running around in random directions (mimicking gas molecules in constant motion).

- If **only Mechanical students** were in the room, the "pressure" on the walls (collisions per unit area per unit time) would be a certain value, say $p_M$.
- If **only Civil students** were in the room, the pressure would be $p_C$.
- If **only Electrical students** were in the room, the pressure would be $p_E$.

Now, when **all four groups** are mixed together in the same room (at the same temperature), each group behaves **independently** and contributes its own individual pressure. The total pressure on the walls is simply the **sum** of each group's individual contribution:

$$P_{total} = p_M + p_C + p_E + p_{CS}$$

This is the essence of **Dalton's Law** — gases in a mixture do not "interfere" with each other; they coexist independently, and their pressure contributions simply add up. The key assumptions are:
1. **No chemical reaction** occurs between the gases.
2. The gases behave **ideally** (negligible intermolecular forces, negligible molecular volume).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy**: Consider opening multiple cans of compressed air in a sealed room. Each can contributes its own "share" of pressure, and the total room pressure is the sum of all contributions — much like Dalton's Law.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants are **essential** for solving Dalton's Law problems in KTU examinations:

- **Universal Gas Constant**: $R = 8.314 \, \text{J/(mol·K)} = 0.08206 \, \text{L·atm/(mol·K)} = 10.7316 \, \text{psia·ft}^3\text{/(lbmol·°R)}$
- **Standard Temperature and Pressure (STP)**: $T = 273.15 \, \text{K}$, $P = 1 \, \text{atm} = 101.325 \, \text{kPa} = 14.696 \, \text{psia}$
- **Standard Conditions (IUPAC, formerly NTP)**: $T = 273.15 \, \text{K}$, $P = 100 \, \text{kPa}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip**: Always explicitly state the **assumptions** (ideal gas behavior, no chemical reaction, constant temperature) before applying Dalton's Law. Examiners award partial marks for proper assumption statements.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown of Dalton's Law

Dalton's Law is derived from the **kinetic theory of gases** and can be understood through the following structured logic:

1. **Molecular Independence Assumption**
   - In an ideal gas mixture, each gas molecule moves independently of the others.
   - Intermolecular forces between unlike molecules are negligible.
   - Each gas occupies the **entire volume** of the container and is at the **same temperature** as the mixture.

2. **Pressure as a Molecular Collision Phenomenon**
   - Pressure is generated by molecular collisions with the container walls.
   - The frequency of collisions per unit area determines the pressure exerted.

3. **Additivity of Collision Frequencies**
   - The total collision frequency = Sum of individual collision frequencies of each gas.
   - Therefore, total pressure = Sum of individual (partial) pressures.

4. **Connection to the Ideal Gas Law**
   - For component $i$ alone occupying volume $V$ at temperature $T$:
   $$p_i V = n_i R T$$
   - Summing over all components:
   $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i V = \sum_{i=1}^{n} n_i R T = (RT/V) \sum_{i=1}^{n} n_i$$
   - Since $\sum p_i = P_{total}$ and $\sum n_i = n_{total}$:
   $$P_{total} V = n_{total} R T$$

5. **Definition of Partial Pressure in Terms of Mole Fraction**
   - Dividing $p_i V = n_i R T$ by $P_{total} V = n_{total} R T$:
   $$\frac{p_i}{P_{total}} = \frac{n_i}{n_{total}} = y_i$$
   - Therefore:
   $$\boxed{p_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}}$$

6. **Definition of Partial Pressure in Terms of Volume Fraction (Amagat's Law Equivalent)**
   - For ideal gases, the **mole fraction** equals the **volume fraction**.
   - If $V_i$ is the partial volume (volume the gas would occupy at $P$ and $T$):
   $$p_i = y_i \cdot P = \frac{V_i}{V_{total}} \cdot P$$

### 2.2 Why Dalton's Law Works (The "Why" Behind the Concept)

The fundamental reason Dalton's Law holds is rooted in the **kinetic molecular theory**:

- **Temperature Equality**: All gases in a mixture rapidly equilibrate to the same temperature through elastic collisions. Since $T$ is uniform, the average kinetic energy of each gas is identical, regardless of its identity.
- **Pressure Independence**: Each gas "sees" the container walls as if it were alone. The presence of other gases does not alter the average molecular velocity or the collision dynamics of a given gas species.
- **Linear Superposition**: Because pressure is a linear function of the number of molecules (at constant $T$ and $V$), the pressures add linearly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU**: Dalton's Law is **exact for ideal gases** and an excellent **approximation for real gases at low to moderate pressures** (typically below **10 atm**) and **high temperatures**. At high pressures or near the critical point, deviations occur due to intermolecular forces and finite molecular volumes.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Units / Conditions** |
|---|---|---|
| $P_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i$ | Dalton's Law (basic form) | $P$ in Pa, atm, or kPa |
| $p_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}$ | Partial pressure via mole fraction | $y_i$ is dimensionless |
| $y_i = \frac{n_i}{n_{total}}$ | Mole fraction definition | Dimensionless |
| $p_i = \frac{n_i R T}{V}$ | Partial pressure via ideal gas law | $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K) |
| $\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i = 1$ | Mole fraction sum constraint | Dimensionless |
| $y_i = \frac{V_i}{V_{total}}$ | Volume fraction (ideal gas only) | Dimensionless |
| $P_{total} V = n_{total} R T$ | Total mixture ideal gas equation | Applies to entire mixture |
| $M_{avg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i M_i$ | Average molecular weight of mixture | kg/kmol or g/mol |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for KTU Examinations**: In the formula $p_i = y_i \cdot P$, the mole fraction $y_i$ is calculated using **moles**, not masses. A common student error is using mass fractions — always verify which quantity is given in the problem.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

Dalton's Law is foundational in numerous chemical engineering applications:

1. **Humidification and Air-Conditioning**: The total pressure of humid air is the sum of the partial pressure of dry air and water vapor. This is essential for psychrometric calculations, dehumidifier design, and cooling tower analysis.

2. **Gas Absorption and Stripping**: In absorption columns (e.g., removing $\text{CO}_2$ from natural gas using amine solutions), the driving force for mass transfer depends on the **partial pressure difference** of the solute gas.

3. **Combustion Engineering**: In furnace and boiler design, the partial pressure of oxygen in flue gas determines combustion efficiency and emission rates.

4. **Distillation Columns**: Vapor-liquid equilibrium calculations in distillation (Raoult's Law and modified Raoult's Law) require partial pressures of each component.

5. **Medical and Respiratory Engineering**: Understanding partial pressures of $\text{O}_2$, $\text{CO}_2$, and $\text{N}_2$ in blood and lungs is critical for anesthesia machines, ventilators, and hyperbaric chambers.

6. **Vacuum Technology and Leak Detection**: Helium leak detection relies on the partial pressure buildup of helium in vacuum systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Dalton's Law from the Ideal Gas Law

**Objective**: Derive $P_{total} = \sum p_i$ starting from the ideal gas equation applied to each component.

**Given**:
- A closed container of volume $V$ at temperature $T$.
- A mixture of $n$ ideal gases with mole numbers $n_1, n_2, \dots, n_n$.

**Step 1**: Apply the ideal gas law to component $i$ alone (treating it as if it occupies volume $V$ alone):

$$p_i V = n_i R T$$

**Step 2**: Solve for the partial pressure $p_i$:

$$p_i = \frac{n_i R T}{V}$$

**Step 3**: Apply the ideal gas law to the **total mixture** (with total moles $n_{total} = \sum n_i$):

$$P_{total} V = n_{total} R T = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} n_i\right) R T$$

**Step 4**: Expand the summation:

$$P_{total} V = n_1 R T + n_2 R T + n_3 R T + \dots + n_n R T$$

**Step 5**: Factor out $R T / V$:

$$P_{total} = \frac{n_1 R T}{V} + \frac{n_2 R T}{V} + \frac{n_3 R T}{V} + \dots + \frac{n_n R T}{V}$$

**Step 6**: Recognize that each term is the partial pressure $p_i$ from Step 2:

$$P_{total} = p_1 + p_2 + p_3 + \dots + p_n$$

**Step 7**: Express in compact summation form:

$$\boxed{P_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i}$$

This completes the derivation. ∎

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Relation Between Partial Pressure and Mole Fraction

**Objective**: Derive the relation $p_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}$.

**Given**:
- Same setup as Derivation 1.
- Mole fraction $y_i$ is defined as $y_i = n_i / n_{total}$.

**Step 1**: Start with the ideal gas law for component $i$:

$$p_i V = n_i R T$$

**Step 2**: Divide both sides by $n_{total}$:

$$\frac{p_i V}{n_{total}} = \frac{n_i}{n_{total}} R T$$

**Step 3**: Recognize $n_i / n_{total} = y_i$:

$$\frac{p_i V}{n_{total}} = y_i R T$$

**Step 4**: Multiply both sides by $n_{total} / V$:

$$p_i = y_i \cdot \frac{n_{total} R T}{V}$$

**Step 5**: From the ideal gas law for the total mixture, $P_{total} V = n_{total} R T$, so $n_{total} R T / V = P_{total}$:

$$p_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}$$

**Step 6**: Rearrange to confirm the mole fraction definition:

$$y_i = \frac{p_i}{P_{total}}$$

**Final Result**:

$$\boxed{p_i = y_i \cdot P_{total} \quad \text{and} \quad y_i = \frac{p_i}{P_{total}}}$$

This relation is **the most frequently used form** of Dalton's Law in KTU problems. ∎

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Verification of the Mole Fraction Sum Constraint

**Objective**: Show that $\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i = 1$ as a direct consequence of Dalton's Law.

**Step 1**: Sum the partial pressures of all components:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \cdot P_{total}$$

**Step 2**: Factor out the constant $P_{total}$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i = P_{total} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i$$

**Step 3**: By Dalton's Law, the left-hand side equals $P_{total}$:

$$P_{total} = P_{total} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i$$

**Step 4**: Divide both sides by $P_{total}$ (assuming $P_{total} \neq 0$):

$$1 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i$$

**Final Result**:

$$\boxed{\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i = 1}$$

This constraint is a **powerful consistency check** when solving KTU problems. ∎

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem**: A gas mixture contains **2.0 mol** of $\text{N}_2$, **3.0 mol** of $\text{O}_2$, and **5.0 mol** of $\text{CO}_2$ in a 10 L container at **300 K**. Calculate:
1. The mole fraction of each component.
2. The partial pressure of each component.
3. The total pressure of the mixture.

Assume ideal gas behavior. Use $R = 0.08206 \, \text{L·atm/(mol·K)}$.

**Step 1: Calculate total moles**

$$n_{total} = 2.0 + 3.0 + 5.0 = 10.0 \, \text{mol}$$

**Step 2: Calculate mole fractions**

$$y_{N_2} = \frac{2.0}{10.0} = 0.20$$

$$y_{O_2} = \frac{3.0}{10.0} = 0.30$$

$$y_{CO_2} = \frac{5.0}{10.0} = 0.50$$

**Verification**: $0.20 + 0.30 + 0.50 = 1.00$ ✓

**Step 3: Calculate total pressure using ideal gas law**

$$P_{total} = \frac{n_{total} R T}{V} = \frac{10.0 \times 0.08206 \times 300}{10}$$

$$P_{total} = \frac{246.18}{10} = 24.618 \, \text{atm}$$

**Step 4: Calculate partial pressures using $p_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}$**

$$p_{N_2} = 0.20 \times 24.618 = 4.9236 \, \text{atm} \approx 4.92 \, \text{atm}$$

$$p_{O_2} = 0.30 \times 24.618 = 7.3854 \, \text{atm} \approx 7.39 \, \text{atm}$$

$$p_{CO_2} = 0.50 \times 24.618 = 12.309 \, \text{atm} \approx 12.31 \, \text{atm}$$

**Step 5: Verify Dalton's Law**

$$P_{total} = p_{N_2} + p_{O_2} + p_{CO_2} = 4.92 + 7.39 + 12.31 = 24.62 \, \text{atm}$$

This matches the calculated $P_{total}$ from Step 3 (minor rounding difference) ✓

**Final Answers**:
- $y_{N_2} = 0.20$, $y_{O_2} = 0.30$, $y_{CO_2} = 0.50$
- $p_{N_2} = 4.92 \, \text{atm}$, $p_{O_2} = 7.39 \, \text{atm}$, $p_{CO_2} = 12.31 \, \text{atm}$
- $P_{total} = 24.62 \, \text{atm}$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Problem Patterns and Solution Strategies

| **Problem Type** | **Given Data** | **Required Output** | **Key Formula** | **Solution Strategy** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mole fraction from moles | $n_i$ for each gas | $y_i$ | $y_i = n_i / n_{total}$ | Sum all moles, then divide each by total |
| Partial pressure from mole fraction | $y_i$ and $P_{total}$ | $p_i$ | $p_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}$ | Multiply mole fraction by total pressure |
| Total pressure from partial pressures | $p_i$ for each gas | $P_{total}$ | $P_{total} = \sum p_i$ | Sum all partial pressures |
| Composition from partial pressures | $p_i$ and $P_{total}$ | $y_i$ | $y_i = p_i / P_{total}$ | Divide each partial pressure by total |
| Mixture using ideal gas law | $n_{total}$, $V$, $T$ | $P_{total}$ | $PV = nRT$ | Direct substitution |
| Average molecular weight | $y_i$ and $M_i$ | $M_{avg}$ | $M_{avg} = \sum y_i M_i$ | Weighted average of molar masses |
| Partial pressure in humid air | $P_{total}$ and RH | $p_{H_2O}$ | $p_{H_2O} = y_{H_2O} P$ | $y_{H_2O} = \text{RH} \cdot (p^*_{H_2O} / P)$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Dalton's Law Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Gas Mixture in Closed Container] --> B[Identify Components]
    B --> C[Component 1: n1 moles]
    B --> D[Component 2: n2 moles]
    B --> E[Component 3: n3 moles]
    B --> F[Component n: nn moles]
    C --> G[Apply Ideal Gas Law p1V equals n1RT]
    D --> H[Apply Ideal Gas Law p2V equals n2RT]
    E --> I[Apply Ideal Gas Law p3V equals n3RT]
    F --> J[Apply Ideal Gas Law pnV equals nnRT]
    G --> K[Sum All Partial Pressures]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Daltons Law: Ptotal equals p1 plus p2 plus ... plus pn]
    L --> M[Partial Pressure Formula: pi equals yi times Ptotal]
    M --> N[Final Result: Total Pressure of Mixture]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Partial Pressure Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputLayer[INPUT LAYER]
        A1[Mole Numbers n1, n2, n3]
        A2[Temperature T]
        A3[Volume V]
    end
    subgraph processingLayer[PROCESSING LAYER]
        B1[Calculate Total Moles ntotal]
        B2[Calculate Mole Fractions yi]
        B3[Apply Ideal Gas Law]
        B4[Compute Partial Pressures pi]
    end
    subgraph outputLayer[OUTPUT LAYER]
        C1[Partial Pressures p1, p2, p3]
        C2[Total Pressure Ptotal]
        C3[Mole Fraction Vector y1, y2, y3]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A2 --> B3
    A3 --> B3
    B1 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B2 --> B4
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    B2 --> C3
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Three-Component Gas Mixture Pressure Contributions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph container[SEALED CONTAINER V, T]
        G1[Gas A: 2 moles]
        G2[Gas B: 3 moles]
        G3[Gas C: 5 moles]
    end
    G1 --> P1[Partial Pressure pA]
    G2 --> P2[Partial Pressure pB]
    G3 --> P3[Partial Pressure pC]
    P1 --> SUM[Daltons Law Summation]
    P2 --> SUM
    P3 --> SUM
    SUM --> RESULT[Total Pressure Ptotal]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Decision Tree: When to Apply Dalton's Law

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Problem Given: Gas Mixture] --> Q1{Is the mixture ideal?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is there chemical reaction?}
    Q1 -->|No Approximation| Q3{Is Pressure below 10 atm?}
    Q2 -->|No Reaction| APPLY[Apply Daltons Law]
    Q2 -->|Reaction Occurs| REVISED[Use Equilibrium Methods]
    Q3 -->|Yes| APPLY
    Q3 -->|No High Pressure| REVISED
    APPLY --> SUCCESS[Calculate Partial Pressures]
    REVISED --> COMPLEX[Use Fugacity or Activity Coefficients]
    SUCCESS --> DONE[Solution Valid]
    COMPLEX --> ADVANCED[Advanced Thermodynamics Required]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: State Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures. Define partial pressure with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer**:

Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures states that the total pressure exerted by a mixture of non-reacting ideal gases in a closed container is equal to the sum of the partial pressures of the individual gases.

$$P_{total} = p_1 + p_2 + p_3 + \dots + p_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i$$

**Partial Pressure** is defined as the pressure that an individual gas in a mixture would exert if it occupied the container alone at the same temperature.

**Example**: In a mixture of oxygen and nitrogen in a container, the partial pressure of oxygen ($p_{O_2}$) is the pressure oxygen would exert if it alone occupied the container at the same temperature.

**Mark Distribution (as per KTU pattern)**:
- Statement of Dalton's Law: **2 marks**
- Definition of partial pressure with example: **1 mark**

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: A gas mixture contains 60% N₂ and 40% O₂ by mole. If the total pressure is 2 atm, calculate the partial pressure of each gas.**

**Model Answer**:

**Given**:
- Mole fraction of N₂: $y_{N_2} = 0.60$
- Mole fraction of O₂: $y_{O_2} = 0.40$
- Total pressure: $P_{total} = 2 \, \text{atm}$

**Formula**: $p_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}$

**Calculation**:

$$p_{N_2} = y_{N_2} \times P_{total} = 0.60 \times 2 = 1.2 \, \text{atm}$$

$$p_{O_2} = y_{O_2} \times P_{total} = 0.40 \times 2 = 0.8 \, \text{atm}$$

**Verification**: $p_{N_2} + p_{O_2} = 1.2 + 0.8 = 2.0 \, \text{atm} = P_{total}$ ✓

**Mark Distribution (as per KTU pattern)**:
- Identification of given data and formula: **1 mark**
- Correct calculations: **1.5 marks**
- Verification: **0.5 marks**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks - Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**Q: A gas mixture consists of three components: 4.0 mol of CH₄, 6.0 mol of C₂H₆, and 10.0 mol of CO₂ contained in a 20 L vessel at a temperature of 350 K.**

**(a)** Calculate the mole fraction of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the partial pressure of each component and the total pressure of the mixture. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Part (a) - Mole Fraction Calculation**:

**Step 1**: Calculate total moles in the mixture.

$$n_{total} = n_{CH_4} + n_{C_2H_6} + n_{CO_2} = 4.0 + 6.0 + 10.0 = 20.0 \, \text{mol}$$

**Step 2**: Calculate mole fraction of each component using $y_i = n_i / n_{total}$.

$$y_{CH_4} = \frac{4.0}{20.0} = 0.20$$

$$y_{C_2H_6} = \frac{6.0}{20.0} = 0.30$$

$$y_{CO_2} = \frac{10.0}{20.0} = 0.50$$

**Step 3**: Verify the mole fraction sum.

$$\sum y_i = 0.20 + 0.30 + 0.50 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark$$

**Part (b) - Partial Pressure and Total Pressure Calculation**:

**Step 1**: Calculate total pressure using the ideal gas law.

$$P_{total} = \frac{n_{total} R T}{V}$$

$$P_{total} = \frac{20.0 \times 0.08206 \times 350}{20}$$

$$P_{total} = 0.08206 \times 350 = 28.721 \, \text{atm}$$

**Step 2**: Calculate partial pressure of each component using $p_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}$.

$$p_{CH_4} = 0.20 \times 28.721 = 5.7442 \, \text{atm} \approx 5.74 \, \text{atm}$$

$$p_{C_2H_6} = 0.30 \times 28.721 = 8.6163 \, \text{atm} \approx 8.62 \, \text{atm}$$

$$p_{CO_2} = 0.50 \times 28.721 = 14.3605 \, \text{atm} \approx 14.36 \, \text{atm}$$

**Step 3**: Verify Dalton's Law.

$$P_{total} = p_{CH_4} + p_{C_2H_6} + p_{CO_2} = 5.74 + 8.62 + 14.36 = 28.72 \, \text{atm} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answers**:
- Mole fractions: $y_{CH_4} = 0.20$, $y_{C_2H_6} = 0.30$, $y_{CO_2} = 0.50$
- Partial pressures: $p_{CH_4} = 5.74 \, \text{atm}$, $p_{C_2H_6} = 8.62 \, \text{atm}$, $p_{CO_2} = 14.36 \, \text{atm}$
- Total pressure: $P_{total} = 28.72 \, \text{atm}$

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2 - Alternative)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]

**Q: A container of volume 5 L holds a mixture of gases at 400 K. The partial pressures of the gases are: p_H₂ = 3.0 atm, p_N₂ = 2.0 atm, and p_CO₂ = 5.0 atm.**

**(a)** Calculate the total pressure of the mixture and the mole fraction of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the total number of moles of gas in the container and the partial volume of each component. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Part (a) - Total Pressure and Mole Fraction**:

**Step 1**: Apply Dalton's Law to find total pressure.

$$P_{total} = p_{H_2} + p_{N_2} + p_{CO_2} = 3.0 + 2.0 + 5.0 = 10.0 \, \text{atm}$$

**Step 2**: Calculate mole fraction of each component using $y_i = p_i / P_{total}$.

$$y_{H_2} = \frac{3.0}{10.0} = 0.30$$

$$y_{N_2} = \frac{2.0}{10.0} = 0.20$$

$$y_{CO_2} = \frac{5.0}{10.0} = 0.50$$

**Step 3**: Verify mole fraction sum.

$$\sum y_i = 0.30 + 0.20 + 0.50 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark$$

**Part (b) - Total Moles and Partial Volumes**:

**Step 1**: Calculate total moles using ideal gas law.

$$n_{total} = \frac{P_{total} V}{R T} = \frac{10.0 \times 5.0}{0.08206 \times 400}$$

$$n_{total} = \frac{50.0}{32.824} = 1.5233 \, \text{mol} \approx 1.52 \, \text{mol}$$

**Step 2**: Calculate moles of each component using $n_i = y_i \cdot n_{total}$.

$$n_{H_2} = 0.30 \times 1.5233 = 0.4570 \, \text{mol} \approx 0.457 \, \text{mol}$$

$$n_{N_2} = 0.20 \times 1.5233 = 0.3047 \, \text{mol} \approx 0.305 \, \text{mol}$$

$$n_{CO_2} = 0.50 \times 1.5233 = 0.7617 \, \text{mol} \approx 0.762 \, \text{mol}$$

**Step 3**: Calculate partial volumes using $V_i = y_i \cdot V_{total}$ (for ideal gas, mole fraction = volume fraction).

$$V_{H_2} = 0.30 \times 5.0 = 1.5 \, \text{L}$$

$$V_{N_2} = 0.20 \times 5.0 = 1.0 \, \text{L}$$

$$V_{CO_2} = 0.50 \times 5.0 = 2.5 \, \text{L}$$

**Step 4**: Verify partial volume sum.

$$\sum V_i = 1.5 + 1.0 + 2.5 = 5.0 \, \text{L} = V_{total} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answers**:
- Total pressure: $P_{total} = 10.0 \, \text{atm}$
- Mole fractions: $y_{H_2} = 0.30$, $y_{N_2} = 0.20$, $y_{CO_2} = 0.50$
- Total moles: $n_{total} = 1.52 \, \text{mol}$
- Partial volumes: $V_{H_2} = 1.5 \, \text{L}$, $V_{N_2} = 1.0 \, \text{L}$, $V_{CO_2} = 2.5 \, \text{L}$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**:
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency**: Students frequently mix units (e.g., using $P$ in atm but $R$ in J/(mol·K) or vice versa). Always check that the units of $P$, $V$, $T$, and $R$ are dimensionally consistent. For atm·L/mol·K, use $R = 0.08206 \, \text{L·atm/(mol·K)}$.
>
> 2. **Mole Fraction vs. Mass Fraction**: A very common mistake is to use mass fraction (from a "percentage by weight" or "% by mass" statement) directly as mole fraction. Always convert mass fractions to mole fractions using the molecular weight: $y_i = (w_i / M_i) / \sum (w_i / M_i)$.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to Verify**: Students often lose 1-2 marks by not verifying the mole fraction sum equals 1 or the partial pressure sum equals the total pressure. Always include a verification step.
>
> 4. **Temperature Conversion**: Forgetting to convert °C to K (or vice versa) is a leading cause of arithmetic errors. KTU problems frequently give temperature in °C.
>
> 5. **Ideal Gas Assumption**: Marks are awarded for explicitly stating that "the gas behaves ideally" before applying the ideal gas law. Always begin your solution with a clear assumption statement.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Dalton's Law Statement**: The total pressure of a non-reacting gas mixture equals the sum of partial pressures: $P_{total} = \sum p_i$.
- **Partial Pressure**: The pressure an individual gas would exert if it alone occupied the entire volume at the same temperature.
- **Key Formula**: $p_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}$, where $y_i$ is the mole fraction of component $i$.
- **Mole Fraction Definition**: $y_i = n_i / n_{total}$ — always use **moles**, never mass.
- **Sum Constraint**: $\sum y_i = 1$ (mole fractions always sum to unity).
- **Ideal Gas Assumption**: Dalton's Law is **exact** for ideal gases and a good **approximation** for real gases at low to moderate pressures (<10 atm) and high temperatures.
- **Independence Principle**: Each gas in a mixture behaves as if the others are absent, occupying the full volume at the mixture's temperature.
- **Connection to Ideal Gas Law**: $p_i V = n_i R T$ for each component; $P_{total} V = n_{total} R T$ for the mixture.
- **Volume Fraction Equivalence**: For ideal gases, the mole fraction equals the volume fraction: $y_i = V_i / V_{total}$.
- **Average Molecular Weight**: $M_{avg} = \sum y_i M_i$ — used when mixture density or specific gravity is required.
- **Practical Applications**: Humidification, gas absorption, combustion, distillation, respiratory engineering, and vacuum technology.
- **Verification Steps**: Always verify $\sum y_i = 1$ and $\sum p_i = P_{total}$ as a consistency check.
- **KTU Common Errors**: Unit mismatches, mass/mole confusion, temperature conversion errors, and missing assumption statements.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Henry's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Henry's Law # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) **Henry's Law** states that *at a constant temperature, the partial pressure of a gas (P_A) in the vapor phase in equilibrium with a dissolved solute (A) in a liquid is directly proportional to the mole fraction (x_A) of that solute in the liquid solution.* Mathematically, the limiting form is expressed as: $$P_A = H_A \cdot x_A$$ Where: - **P_A** = Partial pressure of the solute gas **A** in the gas phase (units: **Pa**, **bar**, or **atm**) - **H_A** = Henry's Law constant for solute **A** in a given solvent (units: **Pa** or **bar**) - **x_A** = Mole fraction of dissolved gas **A** in the liquid phase (dimensionless) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Expectation):** > *"At constant temperature and low concentrations, the partial pressure of a gas in equilibrium with a dissolved solute in a liquid is directly proportional to the mole fraction of the solute in the solution."* > The phrase *"at low concentrations"* and *"at constant temperature"* are mandatory qualifying clauses in any KTU answer. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Fizzy Soda Bottle" Imagine opening a cold bottle of **Coca-Cola**. Before opening, the bottle is sealed, and **CO₂** gas is dissolved in the liquid under high pressure (above 1 atm). The amount of CO₂ held in the liquid is directly tied to the pressure pushing down on it. - **High pressure inside (sealed bottle)** → more CO₂ stays dissolved. - **Sudden pressure drop (opening the cap)** → CO₂ rushes out as bubbles to restore equilibrium. This everyday phenomenon is **Henry's Law in action** — gases dissolve into liquids in proportion to the pressure exerted on the gas-liquid interface. The colder the soda, the more CO₂ it can hold (which is why warm soda goes flat faster — gas solubility decreases with temperature). > [!NOTE] > **Key Intuition:** Think of the gas-liquid interface as a "two-way membrane" where molecules constantly escape and re-enter. At equilibrium, the *rate of escape = rate of return*. Increasing gas-phase pressure pushes more molecules into the liquid, increasing dissolved concentration proportionally. --- ## 1.3 Scope and Limitations (Board-Favorite Concept) > [!WARNING] > **When does Henry's Law FAIL?** > Henry's Law is a **limiting law** valid for: > 1. **Dilute (ideal) solutions** — typically **x_A < 0.01 to 0.02** > 2. **No chemical reaction** between gas and solvent (e.g., NH₃ + H₂O does NOT obey Henry's Law due to ionization) > 3. **No association/dissociation** of solute in solvent > 4. **Constant temperature** strictly maintained > 5. **Non-electrolyte solutes** preferred If any of these conditions are violated, the P–x relationship becomes **non-linear**, and engineers must use **activity-based models** or **separation process correlations** instead. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The "Why" Behind Henry's Law Henry's Law emerges from **thermodynamic equilibrium** between the gas and liquid phases. At the molecular level: 1. **Dynamic Equilibrium:** Gas molecules continuously transfer across the liquid interface. At equilibrium, the *chemical potential (μ) of the solute in the gas phase equals that in the liquid phase.* 2. **Ideal Gas Assumption:** For the gas phase at low to moderate pressures, we apply Dalton's Law and ideal gas behavior. 3. **Ideal Dilute Solution Assumption:** The liquid phase is treated as ideal (Raoult's Law fails for the solute, but Henry's Law describes it). 4. **Reference Fugacity:** The standard state for the dissolved solute is defined such that fugacity equals Henry's constant when mole fraction → 1 (pure component reference). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Raoult's Law vs. Henry's Law — The Crucial Distinction:** > - **Raoult's Law** describes the **solvent** behavior: $P_{solvent} = x_{solvent} \cdot P^{sat}_{solvent}$ > - **Henry's Law** describes the **solute** behavior: $P_{solute} = x_{solute} \cdot H_{solute}$ > - The **Henry's constant (H)** is determined **experimentally**, unlike $P^{sat}$ which is a pure-component property. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description / Form | Units | KTU Frequency | |---|---------|-------------------|-------|---------------| | 1 | $P_A = H_A \cdot x_A$ | Basic Henry's Law (Pressure form) | Pa, bar, atm | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 2 | $P_A = H_A^{cp} \cdot C_A$ | Concentration form (molarity-based) | Pa·m³/mol | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 3 | $y_A \cdot P_{total} = H_A \cdot x_A$ | Vapor-Liquid Equilibrium form | Pa, bar | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 4 | $H_A^{cp} = H_A / (C_{total} \cdot M_{solvent})$ | Conversion between H and $H^{cp}$ | Pa·m³/mol | ⭐⭐⭐ | | 5 | $H_A^{\phi} = y_A \cdot P_{total} / x_A$ | Dimensionless Henry's constant | Dimensionless | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 6 | $\ln\left(\dfrac{H_2}{H_1}\right) = \dfrac{\Delta H_{sol}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | Temperature dependence (van't Hoff) | J/mol, K | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 7 | $\dfrac{d \ln H}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{sol}}{R T^2}$ | Differential form of temperature effect | J/mol, K | ⭐⭐⭐ | | 8 | $H_A = P \cdot y_A / x_A$ | General vapor-liquid definition | bar, atm | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL NOTATION DISTINCTION (Board loves to test this):** > - $H_A$ → has units of **pressure** (bar, Pa, atm, psi) > - $H_A^{cp}$ → has units of **pressure·volume/mole** (bar·m³/mol, atm·L/mol) > - $H_A^{\phi}$ (or $H_A^{dim}$) → **dimensionless** > These three forms are **numerically different** and **NOT interchangeable** without unit conversion. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Engineering Application | Use of Henry's Law | |------------------------|--------------------| | **Absorption Columns (Gas Treating)** | Designing $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ removal from natural gas using amines/water | | **Aeration & Oxygenation** | Calculating dissolved $\text{O}_2$ in water for wastewater treatment (BOD/COD analysis) | | **Blood Oxygenation (Medical/Pharma)** | Modeling $\text{O}_2$/$\text{CO}_2$ transport in blood during anesthesia | | **Carbonated Beverages** | Determining $\text{CO}_2$ pressure for desired fizz levels | | **Scuba Diving / Decompression Sickness** | Predicting $\text{N}_2$ release during ascent | | **Distillation at High Pressure** | Estimating light-gas solubility in condensers | | **Environmental Engineering** | Modeling volatile organic compound (VOC) release from contaminated water | > [!TIP] > **High-Yield Insight:** In KTU problems on **gas absorption using solvent**, Henry's constant directly determines the **volatility** of the solute. A *small* H means *high solubility* (good for absorption); a *large* H means *low solubility* (the gas prefers to stay in vapor phase). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Thermodynamic Equilibrium to Henry's Law ### Step 1: Start with Phase Equilibrium Condition At constant temperature and pressure, the criterion for vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE) of component **A** is the equality of **fugacities** (escaping tendencies): $$\hat{f}_A^{vapor} = \hat{f}_A^{liquid}$$ > *Reasoning:* At equilibrium, the chemical potential of A is equal in both phases, which translates to equal fugacities (a practical thermodynamic surrogate for chemical potential). --- ### Step 2: Express Fugacity in the Vapor Phase Assuming the gas phase behaves ideally (low to moderate pressure): $$\hat{f}_A^{vapor} = y_A \cdot P_{total}$$ Where: - $y_A$ = mole fraction of A in vapor - $P_{total}$ = total system pressure > *Reasoning:* For an ideal gas mixture, fugacity equals partial pressure, and partial pressure = mole fraction × total pressure (Dalton's Law). --- ### Step 3: Express Fugacity in the Liquid Phase (Lewis-Randall Framework) For the dissolved solute in a dilute solution, we use the **Henry's Law reference state** rather than the pure component fugacity (which is used in Raoult's Law): $$\hat{f}_A^{liquid} = x_A \cdot \mathcal{H}_A$$ Where: - $\mathcal{H}_A$ is the **Henry's Law constant** (with units of pressure) - $x_A$ is the mole fraction of A in the liquid > *Reasoning:* This is the defining equation for an "ideal dilute solution" — the reference fugacity $\mathcal{H}_A$ replaces the pure-component vapor pressure $P_A^{sat}$ that appears in Raoult's Law. The key idea is that since the solute cannot exist as a pure liquid at the system temperature (or is at infinite dilution), we define a hypothetical reference state. --- ### Step 4: Equate the Two Fugacities $$y_A \cdot P_{total} = x_A \cdot \mathcal{H}_A$$ > *Reasoning:* This is the fundamental VLE condition, now recast in terms of the Henry's constant. --- ### Step 5: Rearrange to Obtain the Standard Form $$\boxed{P_A = y_A \cdot P_{total} = x_A \cdot \mathcal{H}_A}$$ > *Reasoning:* By definition, the partial pressure of A in the gas phase is $P_A = y_A \cdot P_{total}$. Substituting this gives the textbook form $P_A = \mathcal{H}_A \cdot x_A$. --- ### Step 6: Derive the Concentration Form Starting from the mole fraction definition: $$x_A = \dfrac{n_A}{n_A + n_{solvent}} \approx \dfrac{n_A}{n_{solvent}} = \dfrac{C_A \cdot V}{n_{solvent}}$$ For a dilute solution (1 liter of water as solvent, $n_{water} = 1000/18.015 = 55.51$ mol): $$x_A \approx \dfrac{C_A}{55.51} \quad \text{(for water as solvent)}$$ Substituting into Henry's Law: $$P_A = H_A \cdot \dfrac{C_A}{55.51} = H_A^{cp} \cdot C_A$$ Where: $$H_A^{cp} = \dfrac{H_A}{55.51} \quad \text{(in bar·L/mol or atm·L/mol)}$$ > *Reasoning:* The concentration form $P_A = H_A^{cp} \cdot C_A$ is more convenient in **environmental engineering** and **biochemical applications** where concentrations are reported in mol/L rather than mole fractions. --- ### Step 7: Derive the Temperature Dependence (van't Hoff Equation) From thermodynamics, the temperature dependence of any equilibrium constant follows the **van't Hoff equation**: $$\dfrac{d \ln K}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{rxn}}{R T^2}$$ Since Henry's constant is itself an equilibrium constant (dimensionless form), and dissolution of gas in liquid is typically **exothermic** ($\Delta H_{sol} < 0$): $$\dfrac{d \ln H}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{sol}}{R T^2}$$ Integrating between two temperatures (assuming $\Delta H_{sol}$ is constant): $$\int_{H_1}^{H_2} d\ln H = \dfrac{\Delta H_{sol}}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \dfrac{dT}{T^2}$$ $$\ln\left(\dfrac{H_2}{H_1}\right) = \dfrac{\Delta H_{sol}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$$ > *Reasoning:* Since gas dissolution is exothermic, increasing temperature **increases** H (solubility decreases). This explains why warm soda loses fizz faster than cold soda. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem) > **Problem:** The Henry's constant for $\text{O}_2$ in water at 25°C is $4.01 \times 10^4$ bar. Calculate the mole fraction of $\text{O}_2$ dissolved in water when exposed to air at 1 bar total pressure. Air contains 21 mol% $\text{O}_2$. (Assume Henry's Law is valid.) ### **Solution:** **Step 1:** Identify known values: - $H_{O_2} = 4.01 \times 10^4$ bar - $P_{total} = 1$ bar - $y_{O_2} = 0.21$ **Step 2:** Calculate partial pressure of $\text{O}_2$: $$P_{O_2} = y_{O_2} \cdot P_{total} = 0.21 \times 1 \text{ bar} = 0.21 \text{ bar}$$ **Step 3:** Apply Henry's Law $P_{O_2} = H_{O_2} \cdot x_{O_2}$: $$\begin{aligned} 0.21 \text{ bar} &= (4.01 \times 10^4 \text{ bar}) \cdot x_{O_2} \\ x_{O_2} &= \dfrac{0.21}{4.01 \times 10^4} \\ x_{O_2} &= 5.24 \times 10^{-6} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4:** Express in ppm for clarity: $$x_{O_2} = 5.24 \text{ ppm (mole basis)}$$ **Verification:** Since $x_{O_2} \ll 0.01$, the dilute solution assumption holds, and Henry's Law is **valid**. ✓ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Henry's Law — Conceptual Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR GASPHASE["GAS PHASE P total"] --> PARTIALP["PARTIAL PRESSURE P_A = y_A x P"] PARTIALP -->|Equilibrium Interface| LIQUIDPHASE["LIQUID PHASE x_A mole fraction"] LIQUIDPHASE -->|Proportionality| HENRY["H_A Henry Constant bar"] PARTIALP --> HENRY HENRY -->|Valid only for| DILUTE["DILUTE SOLUTION x_A less than 0.02"] HENRY -->|Valid only for| ISOTHERMAL["ISOTHERMAL T constant"] HENRY -->|Valid only for| NOREACTION["NO CHEMICAL REACTION"] DILUTE --> OUTPUT["P_A = H_A x x_A"] ISOTHERMAL --> OUTPUT NOREACTION --> OUTPUT ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Forms of Henry's Constant — Conversion Matrix ```mermaid flowchart TD FORM_A["FORM 1: P_A = H_A x x_A
Units: bar or Pa"] FORM_B["FORM 2: P_A = H cp x C_A
Units: bar m3 per mol"] FORM_C["FORM 3: y_A x P = H phi x x_A
