$$Q = 10 \times 34.05 \times 500 = \boxed{170250 \text{ kJ} = 1.7025 \times 10^5 \text{ kJ}}$$

---

**(b) Reactive Gas Mixture Heat Capacity:**

When chemical reactions occur, the heat capacity calculation must account for **formation enthalpies** because the chemical composition changes.

**Non-reactive case:**

$$Q = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \sum n_i \bar{C}_{p,i} \, dT = n_{total} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \bar{C}_{p,mix} \, dT$$

**Reactive case:**

$$Q = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \sum n_i \bar{C}_{p,i} \, dT + \left[ \sum n_{products} \Delta H_f^\circ - \sum n_{reactants} \Delta H_f^\circ \right]$$

The second term accounts for the energy released or absorbed due to bond breaking and formation during reaction.

**Key Differences:**
1. Reactive case includes **standard heat of formation** terms.
2. Reference state matters — typically **elements at 25°C (298 K)**.
3. For combustion calculations, the heat of combustion is also incorporated.
4. $C_p$ values of individual species are summed based on **moles present**, not just inlet composition.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency**: Forgetting to convert $R = 1.987$ cal/(mol·K) to $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) loses **1 mark** easily.
> 2. **Mean vs True Confusion**: Using true $C_p$ at a single temperature for a wide range $\Delta T$ gives wrong answer. Use $\bar{C}_{p,m}$ averaged over the range.
> 3. **Integration Errors**: Polynomial integration with $T^{-2}$ term is frequently done incorrectly. Remember: $\int T^{-2} dT = -T^{-1}$.
> 4. **Mole vs Mass Basis**: KTU questions sometimes switch between $C_p$ and $\bar{C}_p$ — verify basis before calculation.
> 5. **Forgetting Formation Enthalpies**: In reactive mixtures, omitting $\Delta H_f^\circ$ terms costs **2-3 marks** in 14-mark questions.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Definition**: $C_p = \left(\dfrac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_p$ — heat capacity at constant pressure.
- **Mayer's Relation**: $C_p - C_v = R$ (ideal gas, molar) or $C_p - C_v = R/M$ (specific). **Most tested formula.**
- **Specific Heat Ratio**: $\gamma = C_p/C_v$. For air $\gamma = 1.4$, monatomic gases $\gamma = 1.67$.
- **Standard Values**:
  - Monatomic ideal gas: $\bar{C}_p = 2.5R$
  - Diatomic ideal gas: $\bar{C}_p = 3.5R$
  - Linear polyatomic: $\bar{C}_p = 3.5R$
- **Mean Heat Capacity**: $\bar{C}_{p,m} = \dfrac{\int_{T_1}^{T_2} C_p \, dT}{T_2 - T_1}$ — use for sensible heat calculations.
- **Polynomial Forms to Memorize**:
  - $C_p = a + bT + cT^2$
  - $C_p = a + bT + cT^{-2}$ (most common in KTU)
  - $C_p = a + bT + cT^{0.5}$
- **Mixture Rule** (non-reactive): $\bar{C}_{p,mix} = \sum y_i \bar{C}_{p,i}$
- **Reactive Case**: Add $\Delta H_f^\circ$ corrections for products minus reactants.
- **Units to Watch**:
  - $R = 8.314$ kJ/(kmol·K) = $1.987$ kcal/(kmol·K) = $1.987$ Btu/(lbmol·°R)
  - Always verify whether the question uses kJ or kcal.
- **Integration Cheat**: For $C_p = a + bT + cT^2$:
  - $\int C_p dT = aT + \dfrac{bT^2}{2} + \dfrac{cT^3}{3}$
  - For $C_p = a + bT + cT^{-2}$:
  - $\int C_p dT = aT + \dfrac{bT^2}{2} - \dfrac{c}{T}$
- **Engineering Rule of Thumb**: For most engineering approximations, $C_p$ of common gases can be taken as constant over moderate temperature ranges (100-200 K span). Use polynomial form only when high accuracy is needed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Capacity of Gaseous Mixtures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Heat Capacity of Gaseous Mixtures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Heat Capacity?

**Heat Capacity (C)** is a fundamental thermodynamic property that quantifies the amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of a given quantity of substance by **one degree** (1 K or 1 °C).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> Heat capacity is defined as the *rate of change of enthalpy (H) with respect to temperature at constant pressure* (for $C_p$), or the *rate of change of internal energy (U) with respect to temperature at constant volume* (for $C_v$).

The two principal forms used throughout Chemical Process Principles are:

- **$C_p$ — Heat Capacity at Constant Pressure** (J/(mol·K))
- **$C_v$ — Heat Capacity at Constant Volume** (J/(mol·K))

These are formally expressed as:

$$C_p = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_p$$

$$C_v = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_v$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Thermal Bucket" Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine heat energy as **water** and temperature as the **level of water in a bucket**. The heat capacity is the **cross-sectional area of the bucket**.
> - A **large bucket** (high heat capacity, like $CO_2$) needs *lots of water* (heat) to raise its level (temperature).
> - A **small bucket** (low heat capacity, like $He$) needs only a *little water* to raise its level.
> - **A gaseous mixture is a bucket containing many smaller buckets inside it** — each gas contributes its own thermal "size," and the total heat capacity is the weighted sum.

## 1.3 Heat Capacity of a Gaseous Mixture — The Core Idea

A gaseous mixture (e.g., flue gas, reformer gas, combustion products) is a combination of several pure gases. Each component contributes to the overall heat capacity **proportionally to its presence** in the mixture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Mixing Rule (KTU 2024 Highlight):**
> The molar heat capacity of an ideal gaseous mixture is the **mole-fraction-weighted arithmetic mean** of the pure-component heat capacities.

Mathematically, on a **molar basis**:

$$C_{p,\,mix} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \, C_{p,i}$$

$$C_{v,\,mix} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \, C_{v,i}$$

where $y_i$ is the mole fraction of component $i$, and $n$ is the total number of components.

On a **mass basis**, the expression is:

$$C_{p,\,mix}^{\text{mass}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \, C_{p,i}^{\text{mass}}$$

where $x_i$ is the mass fraction.

> [!NOTE]
> The mixture is assumed to behave as an **ideal gas mixture** (Amagat's Law / Dalton's Law applies) — this is the standard assumption in KTU-level problems unless explicitly stated otherwise.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Foundation — Why Does This Work?

The mixing rule for ideal gases rests on three pillars:

1. **Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures** — each gas behaves independently as if it alone occupied the volume.
2. **Additivity of Enthalpies** — the total enthalpy of an ideal gas mixture equals the sum of the partial enthalpies of the components.
3. **No Interaction Energy** — ideal gases exert no intermolecular forces, so the energy of the mixture is purely the sum of the energies of its constituents.

From these, the heat capacity — being a derivative of energy/enthalpy with respect to temperature — must also be additive in the same proportions.

## 2.2 Fundamental Relationships (Pure Component & Mixture)

For any **ideal gas** (pure or in a mixture):

$$C_p - C_v = R$$

$$\frac{C_p}{C_v} = \gamma \quad \text{(ratio of specific heats)}$$

Combining these:

$$C_p = \frac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1}$$

$$C_v = \frac{R}{\gamma - 1}$$

These relations hold **component-wise** and also hold for the **mixture as a whole**, because the mixture of ideal gases is itself thermodynamically an ideal gas.

## 2.3 Mean Heat Capacity (KTU High-Yield Topic)

### 2.3.1 True (Instantaneous) Heat Capacity
The values tabulated in textbooks (e.g., $C_p$ for $N_2 = 29.1$ J/mol·K at 298 K) are **true heat capacities** at a specific temperature.

### 2.3.2 Mean Heat Capacity
Often, engineers need the **average heat capacity over a temperature range** $T_1 \to T_2$. The mean molar heat capacity at constant pressure is defined by:

$$\bar{C}_p = \frac{1}{T_2 - T_1} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} C_p(T) \, dT$$

The mean heat capacity is used directly in the energy-balance equation:

$$Q = n \, \bar{C}_p \, (T_2 - T_1)$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a gaseous mixture, the mean heat capacity of the mixture is the mole-fraction-weighted mean of the component mean heat capacities over the same temperature range.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $C_p = (\partial H / \partial T)_p$ | Heat capacity at constant $P$ | J/(mol·K) |
| $C_v = (\partial U / \partial T)_v$ | Heat capacity at constant $V$ | J/(mol·K) |
| $C_p - C_v = R$ | Mayer's relation (ideal gas) | $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) |
| $C_p = \gamma R / (\gamma - 1)$ | $C_p$ in terms of $\gamma$ | $\gamma = C_p/C_v$ |
| $C_{p,\,mix} = \sum y_i C_{p,i}$ | Mole-basis mixing rule | $y_i$ = mole fraction |
| $C_{v,\,mix} = \sum y_i C_{v,i}$ | Mole-basis mixing rule | dimensionless $y_i$ |
| $C_{p,\,mix}^{mass} = \sum x_i C_{p,i}^{mass}$ | Mass-basis mixing rule | $x_i$ = mass fraction |
| $\bar{C}_p = \frac{1}{T_2 - T_1}\int_{T_1}^{T_2} C_p \, dT$ | Mean heat capacity definition | $T$ in K |
| $Q = n \bar{C}_p \Delta T$ | Sensible heat change | $Q$ in J |
| $H(T) = H^{o}_{ref} + \int_{T_{ref}}^{T} C_p \, dT$ | Enthalpy as a function of $T$ | Reference state $T_{ref}$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Role of Mixture Heat Capacity |
|---|---|
| **Combustion / Flue Gas Design** | Calculating heat lost in exhaust gases ($CO_2$, $H_2O$, $N_2$, $O_2$). |
| **Reactor Heat Duties** | Energy balance for exothermic/endothermic reactors with multi-component gas feeds. |
| **Compressor Work** | Isentropic temperature rise in multi-stage compressors depends on mixture $C_p/C_v$. |
| **Air-Separation Units (ASU)** | Cryogenic design of $N_2/O_2/Ar$ systems. |
| **Petrochemical Furnace Design** | Heat balance of cracking furnaces (hydrocarbon mixtures). |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: $C_p - C_v = R$ for an Ideal Gas

### Starting Point — The First Law of Thermodynamics

For a closed, non-flow system undergoing a quasi-equilibrium process:

$$dU = \delta Q - \delta W$$

For a reversible process, the work done by the gas on the surroundings is:

$$\delta W = p \, dV$$

So:

$$dU = \delta Q - p \, dV$$

### Case 1 — Process at Constant Volume ($dV = 0$)

At constant volume, no $P\text{-}V$ work is performed, so $\delta W = 0$. The heat transferred equals the change in internal energy:

$$\delta Q_v = dU$$

By the definition of $C_v$:

$$\delta Q_v = n \, C_v \, dT$$

Therefore:

$$dU = n \, C_v \, dT \quad \text{...(Eq. A)}$$

> *Key logic:* Even when the pressure is not constant, $U$ is a *state function* of $T$ alone for an ideal gas (Joule's Law). So $dU$ depends *only* on $dT$.

### Case 2 — Process at Constant Pressure

At constant pressure, work is done: $\delta W = p \, dV$. So:

$$\delta Q_p = dU + p \, dV$$

Substituting Eq. (A):

$$\delta Q_p = n \, C_v \, dT + p \, dV \quad \text{...(Eq. B)}$$

### Apply the Ideal Gas Law

For $n$ moles of an ideal gas:

$$p V = n R T$$

Differentiate both sides (with $n$ constant):

$$p \, dV + V \, dp = n R \, dT \quad \text{...(Eq. C)}$$

### Impose the Constant-Pressure Constraint

At constant pressure, $dp = 0$. Equation (C) reduces to:

$$p \, dV = n R \, dT$$

### Substitute Back into Eq. (B)

$$\delta Q_p = n \, C_v \, dT + n R \, dT = n \left(C_v + R\right) dT$$

By the *definition* of $C_p$:

$$\delta Q_p = n \, C_p \, dT$$

### Equate and Conclude

$$n \, C_p \, dT = n \left(C_v + R\right) dT$$

Dividing both sides by $n \, dT$ (neither is zero for a real temperature change):

$$\boxed{\,C_p - C_v = R\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This relation is **universal for all ideal gases** — pure components and mixtures alike — because a mixture of ideal gases behaves as a single ideal gas with an effective $C_p$ and $C_v$ obeying the same Mayer relation.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Mixing Rule for $C_{p,\,mix}$

For an ideal gas mixture, the total enthalpy is the sum of the partial enthalpies of the pure components (Lewis–Randall rule):

$$H_{mix} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} n_i \, H_i(T)$$

where $H_i(T)$ is the molar enthalpy of pure component $i$ at the mixture temperature $T$.

Differentiate with respect to $T$ at constant $P$ and composition:

$$\left(\frac{\partial H_{mix}}{\partial T}\right)_{P, n_i} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} n_i \left(\frac{\partial H_i}{\partial T}\right)_P = \sum_{i=1}^{n} n_i \, C_{p,i}$$

By definition, the total moles $n = \sum n_i$, and $y_i = n_i / n$. Divide through by $n$:

$$C_{p,\,mix} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} n_i \, C_{p,i} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \, C_{p,i}$$

$$\boxed{\,C_{p,\,mix} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \, C_{p,i}\,}$$

The same argument applied to $U$ yields the analogous rule for $C_v$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example (KTU Board-Standard)

**Problem (Example 1):**

A gaseous mixture has the following composition by volume:
- $N_2$: 60 %
- $CO_2$: 25 %
- $O_2$: 15 %

The true molar heat capacities at 350 K are:
- $C_{p,\,N_2} = 29.14$ J/(mol·K)
- $C_{p,\,CO_2} = 39.39$ J/(mol·K)
- $C_{p,\,O_2} = 29.46$ J/(mol·K)

**Calculate:** (a) $C_{p,\,mix}$ of the mixture. (b) $C_{v,\,mix}$ of the mixture. (c) The ratio $\gamma_{mix}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify mole fractions (Dalton's Law ⇒ volume % = mole %):**

$$y_{N_2} = 0.60, \quad y_{CO_2} = 0.25, \quad y_{O_2} = 0.15$$

Verification: $0.60 + 0.25 + 0.15 = 1.00$ ✓

**Step 2 — Compute $C_{v,i}$ for each component using $C_{v,i} = C_{p,i} - R$:**

$$C_{v,\,N_2} = 29.14 - 8.314 = 20.83 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$$

$$C_{v,\,CO_2} = 39.39 - 8.314 = 31.08 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$$

$$C_{v,\,O_2} = 29.46 - 8.314 = 21.15 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C_{p,\,mix}$ using the mole-fraction mixing rule:**

$$C_{p,\,mix} = (0.60)(29.14) + (0.25)(39.39) + (0.15)(29.46)$$

Evaluate each term:

$$\begin{aligned}
(0.60)(29.14) &= 17.484 \\
(0.25)(39.39) &= 9.8475 \\
(0.15)(29.46) &= 4.419 \\
\end{aligned}$$

Sum them:

$$C_{p,\,mix} = 17.484 + 9.8475 + 4.419 = 31.75 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $C_{v,\,mix}$:**

$$C_{v,\,mix} = (0.60)(20.83) + (0.25)(31.08) + (0.15)(21.15)$$

Evaluate each term:

$$\begin{aligned}
(0.60)(20.83) &= 12.498 \\
(0.25)(31.08) &= 7.770 \\
(0.15)(21.15) &= 3.1725 \\
\end{aligned}$$

Sum them:

$$C_{v,\,mix} = 12.498 + 7.770 + 3.1725 = 23.44 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$$

**Step 5 — Verify Mayer's relation for the mixture:**

$$C_{p,\,mix} - C_{v,\,mix} = 31.75 - 23.44 = 8.31 \text{ J/(mol·K)} \approx R \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Compute $\gamma_{mix}$:**

$$\gamma_{mix} = \frac{C_{p,\,mix}}{C_{v,\,mix}} = \frac{31.75}{23.44} = 1.355$$

### Final Results Summary

| Quantity | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{p,\,mix}$ | 31.75 | J/(mol·K) |
| $C_{v,\,mix}$ | 23.44 | J/(mol·K) |
| $\gamma_{mix}$ | 1.355 | dimensionless |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Situation | Correct Formula | Pitfall to Avoid |
|---|---|---|
| Mole-basis mixture $C_p$ | $\sum y_i C_{p,i}$ | Do **not** use mass fractions. |
| Mass-basis mixture $C_p$ | $\sum x_i C_{p,i}^{mass}$ | Convert $C_{p,mol}$ to $C_{p,mass}$ via $M_i$ first. |
| Mean $C_p$ over $T_1 \to T_2$ | $(1/\Delta T) \int C_p \, dT$ | Tabulated $C_p$ is usually *true*, not *mean*. |
| $C_v$ from $C_p$ | $C_p - R$ | Only for **ideal gases**. |
| $\gamma$ for mixture | $C_{p,mix} / C_{v,mix}$ | Do **not** average $\gamma_i$ directly. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Procedure for Calculating Mixture Heat Capacity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START: Given gas mixture composition]) --> B[Identify all components and their mole fractions yi]
    B --> C{Choose basis}
    C -->|Mole basis| D[Use Cp,mol,i for each component]
    C -->|Mass basis| E[Convert to mass fractions xi and Cp,mass,i]
    D --> F[Check temperature: tabulated Cp at right T?]
    E --> F
    F -->|True Cp| G[Apply mixing rule: Cp,mix = sum of yi times Cp,i]
    F -->|Need mean Cp| H[Integrate Cp,T dT over T1 to T2, then divide by T2 minus T1 for each component]
    H --> I[Apply mixing rule on mean values]
    G --> J{Cv required?}
    I --> J
    J -->|Yes| K[Apply Cv,i = Cp,i minus R for each component]
    J -->|No| L([OUTPUT: Cp,mix])
    K --> M[Apply mixing rule for Cv: Cv,mix = sum of yi times Cv,i]
    M --> N[Verify: Cp,mix minus Cv,mix equals R]
    N --> L
    L --> O{Gamma required?}
    O -->|Yes| P[Compute gamma = Cp,mix divided by Cv,mix]
    O -->|No| Q([END])
    P --> Q
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Mixture Heat Capacity

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT LAYER]
        A1[Component 1 with y1 and Cp1 at T]
        A2[Component 2 with y2 and Cp2 at T]
        A3[Component N with yN and CpN at T]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS[PROCESSING LAYER]
        B1[Weighted product: y1 times Cp1]
        B2[Weighted product: y2 times Cp2]
        B3[Weighted product: yN times CpN]
    end
    subgraph AGG[AGGREGATION LAYER]
        C1[Sum of all weighted products]
        C2[Apply Mayer relation: Cv,mix = Cp,mix minus R]
        C3[Compute gamma mix = Cp,mix divided by Cv,mix]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT LAYER]
        D1[Cp,mix in J per mol K]
        D2[Cv,mix in J per mol K]
        D3[gamma mix dimensionless]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B3
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> D2
    C3 --> D3
    C1 --> D1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagram:** The input layer captures the composition data. The processing layer applies the mole-fraction weighting. The aggregation layer combines the contributions, enforces Mayer's relation, and computes derived quantities. The output layer gives the final thermodynamic properties of the mixture.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define true and mean molar heat capacity of a gas. State the Mayer relation between $C_p$ and $C_v$ for an ideal gas.**

**Model Answer:**

- **True Molar Heat Capacity ($C_p$ or $C_v$):** The amount of heat required to raise the temperature of *one mole* of a gas by one degree at a specified reference temperature, with the process conducted either at constant pressure ($C_p$) or constant volume ($C_v$). It is a *point function* of temperature.
- **Mean Molar Heat Capacity ($\bar{C}_p$ or $\bar{C}_v$):** The constant value of heat capacity that, when multiplied by the temperature change $(T_2 - T_1)$, gives the same total heat transfer as the true (variable) heat capacity integrated over that range.

$$\bar{C}_p = \frac{1}{T_2 - T_1}\int_{T_1}^{T_2} C_p(T) \, dT$$

- **Mayer Relation:** $C_p - C_v = R$ where $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State the mixing rule for the molar heat capacity of an ideal gaseous mixture. Why is the mixture treated as an ideal gas in most engineering calculations?**

**Model Answer:**

**Mixing rule:** On a molar basis,

$$C_{p,\,mix} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \, C_{p,i}$$

and

$$C_{v,\,mix} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \, C_{v,i}$$

**Reason for ideal-gas assumption:**
1. At low to moderate pressures, intermolecular forces are negligible.
2. Dalton's Law applies exactly, so partial properties are additive.
3. Engineering calculations (e.g., combustion, furnace design) operate at pressures where real-gas deviations are within acceptable error limits (typically $< 5\%$ at pressures below **10 bar**).
4. It dramatically simplifies energy-balance equations for industrial process design.

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions in this module carry **14 marks each** with a typical split of **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** Define the terms *true* and *mean* molar heat capacity. Derive the relation $C_p - C_v = R$ for an ideal gas from the first law of thermodynamics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gaseous mixture contains 20 % $CO_2$, 30 % $O_2$ and 50 % $N_2$ by volume. At 400 K, the true molar heat capacities (J/(mol·K)) are: $C_{p,CO_2} = 41.30$, $C_{p,O_2} = 29.94$, $C_{p,N_2} = 29.46$. Calculate: (i) $C_{p,\,mix}$, (ii) $C_{v,\,mix}$, (iii) $\gamma_{mix}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part A(a)

Refer to the exhaustive derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The key steps to write in the answer script are:

1. First Law: $dU = \delta Q - \delta W$; for reversible: $\delta W = p\,dV$.
2. Constant-volume case: $\delta Q_v = dU = n C_v\,dT$.
3. Constant-pressure case: $\delta Q_p = dU + p\,dV = n C_v\,dT + p\,dV$.
4. Ideal gas law differentiation: $p\,dV + V\,dp = nR\,dT$.
5. At constant $P$: $p\,dV = nR\,dT$.
6. Substitute and obtain: $n C_p dT = n(C_v + R)dT \Rightarrow C_p - C_v = R$.

#### Model Solution — Part A(b)

**Step 1 — Mole fractions (volume % = mole %):**

$$y_{CO_2} = 0.20, \quad y_{O_2} = 0.30, \quad y_{N_2} = 0.50$$

**Step 2 — Compute $C_{v,i} = C_{p,i} - R$ for each:**

$$C_{v,CO_2} = 41.30 - 8.314 = 32.99 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$$

$$C_{v,O_2} = 29.94 - 8.314 = 21.63 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$$

$$C_{v,N_2} = 29.46 - 8.314 = 21.15 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C_{p,\,mix}$:**

$$C_{p,\,mix} = (0.20)(41.30) + (0.30)(29.94) + (0.50)(29.46)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
&= 8.260 + 8.982 + 14.730 \\
&= 31.97 \text{ J/(mol·K)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $C_{v,\,mix}$:**

$$C_{v,\,mix} = (0.20)(32.99) + (0.30)(21.63) + (0.50)(21.15)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
&= 6.598 + 6.489 + 10.575 \\
&= 23.66 \text{ J/(mol·K)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\gamma_{mix}$:**

$$\gamma_{mix} = \frac{C_{p,\,mix}}{C_{v,\,mix}} = \frac{31.97}{23.66} = 1.351$$

**Step 6 — Verification (Mayer's relation):**

$$C_{p,\,mix} - C_{v,\,mix} = 31.97 - 23.66 = 8.31 \approx R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)} \checkmark$$

**Final Answer:**

| Quantity | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{p,\,mix}$ | 31.97 | J/(mol·K) |
| $C_{v,\,mix}$ | 23.66 | J/(mol·K) |
| $\gamma_{mix}$ | 1.351 | dimensionless |

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]` (Internal Choice)

**(a)** Starting from the first law of thermodynamics, derive the Mayer relation $C_p - C_v = R$ for an ideal gas. State clearly any assumptions used in the derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how the molar heat capacity of a gaseous mixture is calculated from the heat capacities of its pure components. A natural-gas-like mixture contains 40 % $CH_4$, 30 % $C_2H_6$, and 30 % $C_3H_8$ by mole. At 350 K, the true molar heat capacities (J/(mol·K)) are: $C_{p,CH_4} = 35.69$, $C_{p,C_2H_6} = 52.71$, $C_{p,C_3H_8} = 73.51$. Calculate the mixture $C_p$, $C_v$, and $\gamma$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part B(a)

**Assumptions:**
1. The gas is **ideal** (so $pV = nRT$ exactly and internal energy depends only on $T$).
2. Process is **quasi-equilibrium** (reversible), so $W = \int p\,dV$.
3. System is **closed** (no mass flow in/out).
4. Only $P\text{-}V$ work is done (no electrical, shaft, or surface work).

**Derivation:** Follow the same six-step derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes, writing each equation clearly with a one-line verbal explanation. The final boxed result is:

$$C_p - C_v = R \quad \text{(Mayer's relation)}$$

#### Model Solution — Part B(b)

**Step 1 — Mole fractions:**

$$y_{CH_4} = 0.40, \quad y_{C_2H_6} = 0.30, \quad y_{C_3H_8} = 0.30$$

**Step 2 — Mixture $C_p$:**

$$C_{p,\,mix} = (0.40)(35.69) + (0.30)(52.71) + (0.30)(73.51)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
&= 14.276 + 15.813 + 22.053 \\
&= 52.14 \text{ J/(mol·K)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute individual $C_v$ values:**

$$C_{v,CH_4} = 35.69 - 8.314 = 27.38 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$$

$$C_{v,C_2H_6} = 52.71 - 8.314 = 44.40 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$$

$$C_{v,C_3H_8} = 73.51 - 8.314 = 65.20 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$$

**Step 4 — Mixture $C_v$:**

$$C_{v,\,mix} = (0.40)(27.38) + (0.30)(44.40) + (0.30)(65.20)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
&= 10.952 + 13.320 + 19.560 \\
&= 43.83 \text{ J/(mol·K)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\gamma$:**

$$\gamma_{mix} = \frac{52.14}{43.83} = 1.190$$

**Step 6 — Verification:**

$$C_{p,\,mix} - C_{v,\,mix} = 52.14 - 43.83 = 8.31 \approx R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)} \checkmark$$

**Final Answer:**

| Quantity | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{p,\,mix}$ | 52.14 | J/(mol·K) |
| $C_{v,\,mix}$ | 43.83 | J/(mol·K) |
| $\gamma_{mix}$ | 1.190 | dimensionless |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing mass fraction with mole fraction** in the mixing rule. Volume % = mole %, but weight % ≠ mole %. Always convert explicitly.
> 2. **Averaging $\gamma$ directly** as $\sum y_i \gamma_i$. This is **wrong** — the correct procedure is to average $C_p$ and $C_v$ *separately*, then take the ratio.
> 3. **Forgetting to subtract $R$** when going from $C_p$ to $C_v$. Many students write $C_v = C_p$ by mistake.
> 4. **Mixing up true and mean heat capacities.** Tabulated values are usually true; if the problem says "from 300 K to 800 K," you need the *mean* over that range.
> 5. **Not verifying** with $C_{p,mix} - C_{v,mix} \approx R$. This is a free 1-mark sanity check; skipping it costs easy marks.
> 6. **Unit errors** — J vs. kJ is the single most common mistake in numerical answers. Match units across the entire calculation.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Heat capacity** = slope of $H(T)$ at constant $P$ (for $C_p$) or $U(T)$ at constant $V$ (for $C_v$).
- [x] **Mayer's relation** $C_p - C_v = R$ holds for **every ideal gas**, pure or in a mixture.
- [x] **Ideal gas mixture $C_p$** = mole-fraction-weighted average: $C_{p,mix} = \sum y_i C_{p,i}$.
- [x] **Mass basis** uses mass fractions $x_i$ and mass heat capacities $C_{p,i}^{mass} = C_{p,i}^{mol} / M_i$.
- [x] **True vs Mean $C_p$** — True is at a single $T$; mean is averaged over $T_1 \to T_2$ via integration.
- [x] **Mole % = Volume %** for ideal gases (Avogadro's hypothesis).
- [x] $\gamma_{mix} \ne \sum y_i \gamma_i$ — always compute as $C_{p,mix} / C_{v,mix}$.
- [x] **Mixture obeys the same Mayer relation** as pure gases because an ideal-gas mixture *is* an ideal gas.
- [x] **Engineering uses:** combustion gases, reformer gas, flue gas, hydrocarbon mixtures, compressor design.
- [x] **Final sanity check:** Always confirm $C_{p,mix} - C_{v,mix} \approx 8.314$ J/(mol·K) before submitting.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Latent Heats
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Latent Heats

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Latent Heat** is the quantity of heat energy that is **absorbed or released** by a unit mass (or mole) of a substance during a **phase change** occurring at **constant temperature** and **constant pressure**.

The term *latent* originates from the Latin word *latere*, meaning "to hide." During a phase transition, the supplied (or removed) heat energy becomes "hidden" because it does not manifest as a change in temperature — instead, it is utilized entirely to overcome (or form) the **intermolecular cohesive forces** holding the molecules together in their original phase.

Mathematically, for a mass $m$ undergoing a phase change:

$$Q = m \cdot \lambda$$

where:
- $Q$ = heat transferred during the phase change (kJ)
- $m$ = mass of the substance (kg)
- $\lambda$ = specific latent heat (kJ/kg)

The corresponding **molar latent heat** is:

$$\Lambda = M \cdot \lambda$$

where $M$ is the molar mass (kg/kmol), and $\Lambda$ has units of **kJ/kmol**.

## 1.2 Physical Interpretation & Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The Ice-Cube Story:**
> Imagine placing ice cubes (at **0 °C = 273.15 K**) in a beaker on a stove. You turn the burner to a *constant* setting. A thermometer in the beaker shows the temperature is **stuck at 0 °C** for several minutes — even though heat is continuously flowing in. Where did the energy go? It went into **breaking the rigid hydrogen-bonded crystalline lattice** of the ice, converting solid $\rightarrow$ liquid. Only **after** all the ice has melted does the temperature of the water start rising again.

This "hidden" energy is the **latent heat of fusion**. The same phenomenon occurs at **100 °C** when liquid water is converted to steam — the supplied heat is the **latent heat of vaporization**.

## 1.3 Classification of Latent Heats

Latent heats are classified according to the **type of phase transition** involved. The five principal types are summarized in the diagram below.

| # | Type of Latent Heat | Transition | Typical Symbol |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Latent Heat of Fusion** | Solid $\rightarrow$ Liquid | $\lambda_f$ |
| 2 | **Latent Heat of Solidification** | Liquid $\rightarrow$ Solid | $\lambda_f$ (same magnitude, opposite sign) |
| 3 | **Latent Heat of Vaporization** | Liquid $\rightarrow$ Vapor | $\lambda_v$ |
| 4 | **Latent Heat of Condensation** | Vapor $\rightarrow$ Liquid | $\lambda_v$ (same magnitude, opposite sign) |
| 5 | **Latent Heat of Sublimation** | Solid $\rightarrow$ Vapor | $\lambda_s$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Relationship:** The latent heat of sublimation is the **sum** of the latent heat of fusion and the latent heat of vaporization, because sublimation can be visualized as a two-step process (melt, then vaporize).
>
> $$\lambda_s = \lambda_f + \lambda_v$$

## 1.4 Why Latent Heats Matter in Chemical Engineering

In chemical process industries, latent heats govern:
- **Energy required for evaporation, distillation, drying, and crystallization** units.
- **Refrigeration cycle** design (latent heat of vaporization of refrigerant).
- **Process safety** calculations — runaway reactions often involve sudden release of latent heat.
- **Storage and transport** of cryogenic liquids (LNG, LPG, liquid N₂, liquid O₂).
- **Heat exchanger** sizing for two-phase flows (boiling/condensation).
- **Power generation** — Rankine cycle efficiency depends directly on $\lambda_v$ of the working fluid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Convention in KTU Examinations:**
> - Latent heat of fusion is usually reported at the **normal melting point (NMP) = 1 atm**.
> - Latent heat of vaporization is usually reported at the **normal boiling point (NBP) = 1 atm**.
> - Units used: **kJ/kg** (specific) or **kJ/kmol** (molar).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Latent Heat of Fusion ($\lambda_f$)

The latent heat of fusion is the energy required to convert **1 kg (or 1 kmol) of a solid into a liquid at its normal melting point**, under standard atmospheric pressure (**101.325 kPa**).

**Molecular basis:** Energy overcomes the **rigid crystalline lattice forces** but is insufficient to fully separate the molecules into the gaseous state.

| Substance | $\lambda_f$ (kJ/kg) | NMP (K) |
|---|---|---|
| Water (H₂O) | **333.5** | 273.15 |
| Ammonia (NH₃) | **332.0** | 195.4 |
| Benzene (C₆H₆) | **127.4** | 278.7 |
| Sodium (Na) | **113.0** | 371.0 |

## 2.2 Latent Heat of Vaporization ($\lambda_v$)

The latent heat of vaporization is the energy required to convert **1 kg (or 1 kmol) of a liquid into vapor at its normal boiling point**, under standard atmospheric pressure.

**Molecular basis:** Energy completely overcomes the **intermolecular cohesive forces** (van der Waals, hydrogen bonds, dipole-dipole), allowing molecules to escape into the vapor phase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** $\lambda_v$ is **always greater than $\lambda_f$** for the same substance, because vaporization requires complete separation of molecules, whereas fusion only requires partial loosening of the lattice.

## 2.3 Latent Heat of Sublimation ($\lambda_s$)

Sublimation is the direct conversion of a solid into vapor **without passing through the liquid phase** (e.g., dry ice $\rightarrow$ CO₂ vapor, or naphthalene balls disappearing in air). 

The latent heat of sublimation combines both fusion and vaporization steps:

$$\lambda_s = \lambda_f + \lambda_v$$

This is a **consequence of the First Law of Thermodynamics** (path-independence of enthalpy, which is a state function).

## 2.4 Variation of Latent Heat with Temperature — Watson's Equation

The latent heat of vaporization is **not a true constant** — it decreases as temperature approaches the **critical temperature** $T_c$, and becomes **zero at the critical point** (since liquid and vapor phases become indistinguishable).

> [!NOTE]
> **Watson's Equation (1943)** — Empirical correlation widely used in KTU problems:
>
> $$\frac{\lambda_{v2}}{\lambda_{v1}} = \left(\frac{T_c - T_2}{T_c - T_1}\right)^{0.38}$$
>
> where:
> - $\lambda_{v1}$ = latent heat at temperature $T_1$ (kJ/kg)
> - $\lambda_{v2}$ = latent heat at temperature $T_2$ (kJ/kg)
> - $T_c$ = critical temperature of the substance (K)
> - Exponent **0.38** is empirical and works well for most substances.

## 2.5 Clausius–Clapeyron Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most frequently tested equation** in KTU Module 2 questions on Latent Heats.

The Clausius–Clapeyron equation relates the **vapor pressure** of a substance to the **latent heat of vaporization** and temperature:

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\lambda_v}{T \cdot \Delta v}$$

For an **ideal gas vapor** in equilibrium with a liquid (and neglecting liquid molar volume compared to vapor):

$$\ln P = -\frac{\Lambda_v}{R}\cdot\frac{1}{T} + C$$

where:
- $\Lambda_v$ = molar latent heat of vaporization (J/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $C$ = integration constant
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $P$ = vapor pressure (Pa or kPa)

## 2.6 Trouton's Rule

Empirical rule for estimating the **molar entropy of vaporization** at the normal boiling point:

$$\frac{\Lambda_{v,\text{NBP}}}{T_{\text{NBP}}} \approx 88 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$$

This rule works well for **non-polar, non-associated liquids**, but fails for water and hydrogen-bonded substances (where the value is closer to 109 J/(mol·K)).

## 2.7 Kistyakowsky Equation (Improved Trouton)

> [!NOTE]
> A more accurate empirical correlation for $\Lambda_v$ at the normal boiling point $T_b$ (K):
>
> $$\frac{\Lambda_{v,\text{NBP}}}{T_{\text{NBP}}} = 36.63 + R \ln T_{\text{NBP}}$$
>
> where $\Lambda_v$ is in **J/mol** and $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = m \cdot \lambda$ | Total heat for phase change | kJ |
| 2 | $\lambda_s = \lambda_f + \lambda_v$ | Sublimation = Fusion + Vaporization | kJ/kg |
| 3 | $\dfrac{\lambda_{v2}}{\lambda_{v1}} = \left(\dfrac{T_c - T_2}{T_c - T_1}\right)^{0.38}$ | Watson's equation | kJ/kg |
| 4 | $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{\lambda_v}{T \cdot \Delta v}$ | Clapeyron equation (general) | Pa/K |
| 5 | $\ln P = -\dfrac{\Lambda_v}{R}\cdot\dfrac{1}{T} + C$ | Clausius–Clapeyron (integrated) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\dfrac{\Lambda_{v,\text{NBP}}}{T_{\text{NBP}}} \approx 88$ | Trouton's rule | J/(mol·K) |
| 7 | $\dfrac{\Lambda_{v,\text{NBP}}}{T_{\text{NBP}}} = 36.63 + R \ln T_{\text{NBP}}$ | Kistyakowsky equation | J/(mol·K) |
| 8 | $\Delta H = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat (NOT latent) | kJ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **latent heat** (phase change, no $\Delta T$) with **sensible heat** (temperature change, no phase change). In a problem where a substance is heated, cooled, *and* undergoes phase change, you must **combine both** contributions.

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Distillation columns** — Reboiler duty is dominated by $\lambda_v$.
- **Crystallization** — Crystallizer cooling duty requires $\lambda_f$.
- **Spray drying** — Latent heat of vaporization of water (≈ **2257 kJ/kg at 100 °C**) determines the air-flow rate.
- **LNG storage** — Boil-off gas is generated because $\lambda_v$ of methane is still non-zero at storage temperatures.
- **Fire safety** — Water's exceptionally high $\lambda_v$ makes it the best fire-extinguishing agent.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Clapeyron Equation

**Starting Point:** Consider a pure substance in **phase equilibrium** between liquid and vapor. Let the molar volume of liquid be $V_L$ and that of vapor be $V_V$. The Clapeyron equation originates from equating the **chemical potentials** (or Gibbs free energies) of the two phases.

**Step 1 — Infinitesimal change in Gibbs free energy along the saturation curve:**

For each phase, the differential of $G$ is given by $dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$. Along the phase boundary (saturation curve), the molar Gibbs free energies of the two phases are **equal**:

$$\mu_L(T, P) = \mu_V(T, P)$$

**Step 2 — Apply a differential shift along the coexistence curve:**

If temperature changes by $dT$, pressure must change by $dP$ to maintain equilibrium. Differentiating both sides:

$$d\mu_L = d\mu_V$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the expression for $d\mu$:**

For each phase, $d\mu = -\bar{S}\,dT + \bar{V}\,dP$ where $\bar{S}$ and $\bar{V}$ are the **molar entropy** and **molar volume** of the respective phase.

$$-\bar{S}_L\,dT + \bar{V}_L\,dP = -\bar{S}_V\,dT + \bar{V}_V\,dP$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange terms:**

Group the $dT$ and $dP$ terms on opposite sides:

$$(\bar{V}_V - \bar{V}_L)\,dP = (\bar{S}_V - \bar{S}_L)\,dT$$

**Step 5 — Introduce the change in molar volume and molar entropy:**

Let $\Delta \bar{V} = \bar{V}_V - \bar{V}_L$ and $\Delta \bar{S} = \bar{S}_V - \bar{S}_L$. Then:

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta \bar{S}}{\Delta \bar{V}}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Second Law equilibrium condition:**

At phase equilibrium, the change in molar Gibbs free energy is zero, so:

$$\Delta \bar{S} = \frac{\Delta \bar{H}}{T} = \frac{\Lambda_v}{T}$$

where $\Lambda_v = \Delta \bar{H}$ is the molar latent heat of vaporization (J/mol).

**Step 7 — Final Clapeyron Equation:**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Lambda_v}{T \cdot \Delta \bar{V}}\;}$$

This is the most general form, valid for **any** phase transition (fusion, vaporization, sublimation).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Clausius–Clapeyron Equation

**Step 1 — Start with the Clapeyron equation** from §3.1.

**Step 2 — Apply two simplifying assumptions:**

- (a) The molar volume of the liquid is **negligible** compared to the molar volume of the vapor: $\bar{V}_L \ll \bar{V}_V$.
- (b) The vapor behaves as an **ideal gas**: $\bar{V}_V = \dfrac{RT}{P}$.

Substituting (a) gives $\Delta \bar{V} \approx \bar{V}_V$. Combined with (b):

$$\Delta \bar{V} \approx \frac{RT}{P}$$

**Step 3 — Insert into the Clapeyron equation:**

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Lambda_v}{T \cdot (RT/P)} = \frac{\Lambda_v \cdot P}{R \cdot T^2}$$

**Step 4 — Separate variables:**

$$\frac{dP}{P} = \frac{\Lambda_v}{R} \cdot \frac{dT}{T^2} = \frac{\Lambda_v}{R} \cdot T^{-2}\,dT$$

**Step 5 — Assume $\Lambda_v$ is approximately constant** over a moderate temperature range. Then integrate from $(T_1, P_1)$ to $(T_2, P_2)$:

$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2}\frac{dP}{P} = \frac{\Lambda_v}{R}\int_{T_1}^{T_2}\frac{dT}{T^2}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the integrals:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \frac{\Lambda_v}{R}\left(-\frac{1}{T_2} + \frac{1}{T_1}\right) = \frac{\Lambda_v}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Final Integrated Clausius–Clapeyron Equation:**

$$\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \frac{\Lambda_v}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\;}$$

**Equivalent linearized form** (slope-intercept) — frequently used to extract $\Lambda_v$ from experimental $\ln P$ vs $1/T$ data:

$$\ln P = -\frac{\Lambda_v}{R}\cdot\frac{1}{T} + C$$

A plot of $\ln P$ (y-axis) versus $1/T$ (x-axis) gives a **straight line of slope** $-\Lambda_v/R$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Watson's Equation

**Step 1 — Start from the Clapeyron equation and assume ideal-gas behavior:**

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Lambda_v P}{RT^2}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to express $d\Lambda_v$:**

Using the thermodynamic identity $\dfrac{d\Lambda_v}{dT} = \Delta C_p = C_{p,V} - C_{p,L}$ (Kirchhoff's equation), and assuming vapor + liquid follow a constant-pressure heat-capacity model, one obtains an empirical relationship between $\Lambda_v$ and $T$.

**Step 3 — Empirical form of latent heat vs temperature:**

Studies of many substances show that the **molar latent heat of vaporization decreases monotonically** with $T$ and reaches **zero at the critical temperature** $T_c$ (because liquid–vapor distinction vanishes).

**Step 4 — Dühring-type empirical fit:**

$$\Lambda_v = A \left(1 - \frac{T}{T_c}\right)^n$$

For many substances, $n \approx 0.38$ gives the best fit.

**Step 5 — Ratio form** (eliminating the constant $A$):

$$\frac{\Lambda_{v2}}{\Lambda_{v1}} = \left(\frac{1 - T_2/T_c}{1 - T_1/T_c}\right)^{0.38} = \left(\frac{T_c - T_2}{T_c - T_1}\right)^{0.38}$$

**Step 6 — Final Watson's Equation:**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\lambda_{v2}}{\lambda_{v1}} = \left(\frac{T_c - T_2}{T_c - T_1}\right)^{0.38}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Mini-Example for Student Practice:**
> Latent heat of vaporization of water at 100 °C is 2257 kJ/kg. Estimate $\lambda_v$ at 200 °C. Given $T_c$ of water = 647.1 K.
>
> Solution: $T_1 = 373.15$ K, $T_2 = 473.15$ K.
>
> $$\frac{\lambda_{v2}}{2257} = \left(\frac{647.1 - 473.15}{647.1 - 373.15}\right)^{0.38} = \left(\frac{173.95}{273.95}\right)^{0.38}$$
>
> Computing the base: $173.95 / 273.95 \approx 0.6350$.
>
> $0.6350^{0.38} \approx 0.8340$ (use $\ln(0.6350) = -0.4540$, multiply by 0.38, exponentiate).
>
> $\lambda_{v2} = 2257 \times 0.8340 \approx 1882$ kJ/kg.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Trouton's Rule (Heuristic)

Trouton observed empirically that for many liquids, the ratio $\Lambda_v / T_b$ clusters around a value of **~88 J/(mol·K)**. The physical justification is based on the approximate **equipartition of energy** and similar molar entropy changes during vaporization across substances.

**Step 1 — Start with the entropy of vaporization:**

$$\Delta S_v = \frac{\Lambda_v}{T_b}$$

**Step 2 — Trouton's empirical observation** (statistical average from many substances):

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\Lambda_v}{T_b} \approx 88 \text{ J/(mol·K)}\;}$$

**Step 3 — Kistyakowsky's refinement** (adds a $T_b$-dependent correction):

$$\frac{\Lambda_v}{T_b} = 36.63 + R \ln T_b = 36.63 + 8.314 \ln T_b$$

For $T_b = 373$ K (water), this gives $36.63 + 8.314 \cdot \ln(373) = 36.63 + 8.314 \cdot 5.922 \approx 36.63 + 49.25 \approx 85.88$ J/(mol·K). Multiplying by $T_b$:

$$\Lambda_v \approx 85.88 \times 373 \approx 32{,}033 \text{ J/mol} \approx 32.0 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

The actual value is ~**40.7 kJ/mol**, showing the Kistyakowsky equation under-predicts for water due to strong hydrogen bonding.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — "When to Use What?"

| Problem Scenario | Applicable Formula | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| Calculate heat to vaporize a known mass | $Q = m \cdot \lambda_v$ | ★★★★★ |
| Estimate $\lambda_v$ at a different temperature | Watson's equation | ★★★★★ |
| Calculate vapor pressure from $\lambda_v$ | Clausius–Clapeyron | ★★★★ |
| Estimate $\Lambda_v$ at NBP from $T_b$ | Trouton or Kistyakowsky | ★★★ |
| Combined sensible + latent heat in heating/cooling | Step-by-step integration | ★★★★★ |
| Sublimation problems | $\lambda_s = \lambda_f + \lambda_v$ | ★★★ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flowchart — Latent Heat Classification & Energy Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Substance at Temperature T] --> B{Phase Transition Type}
    B --> C[Solid to Liquid]
    B --> D[Liquid to Vapor]
    B --> E[Solid to Vapor Direct]
    B --> F[Liquid to Solid]
    B --> G[Vapor to Liquid]

    C --> C1[Latent Heat of Fusion lambda_f]
    D --> D1[Latent Heat of Vaporization lambda_v]
    E --> E1[Latent Heat of Sublimation lambda_s]
    F --> F1[Latent Heat of Solidification minus lambda_f]
    G --> G1[Latent Heat of Condensation minus lambda_v]

    C1 --> H[Energy Required to overcome lattice forces]
    D1 --> I[Energy Required to overcome all intermolecular forces]
    E1 --> J[lambda_s equals lambda_f plus lambda_v]

    H --> K[Constant Temperature and Pressure]
    I --> K
    J --> K

    K --> L[Engineering Applications: Distillation, Drying, Refrigeration]
```

## 4.2 Process Block Diagram — Heating Curve with Phase Changes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Initial Solid at T_low] --> S1[Heating: Sensible Heat Q1 equals m times Cp_solid times delta T]
    S1 --> S2{At Melting Point}
    S2 --> S3[Phase Change: Latent Heat of Fusion Q2 equals m times lambda_f]
    S3 --> S4[Liquid at T_melt]
    S4 --> S5[Heating: Sensible Heat Q3 equals m times Cp_liquid times delta T]
    S5 --> S6{At Boiling Point}
    S6 --> S7[Phase Change: Latent Heat of Vaporization Q4 equals m times lambda_v]
    S7 --> S8[Final Vapor at T_high]

    style S0 fill:#E0F2FE
    style S2 fill:#FEF3C7
    style S3 fill:#FEF3C7
    style S6 fill:#FECACA
    style S7 fill:#FECACA
    style S8 fill:#D1FAE5
```

## 4.3 State-Function Block Diagram — Latent Heat Thermodynamic Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Phase 1: Solid] -->|Heat Q_f equals m times lambda_f| P2[Phase 2: Liquid]
    P2 -->|Heat Q_v equals m times lambda_v| P3[Phase 3: Vapor]
    P1 -->|Heat Q_s equals m times lambda_s| P3

    P1 -.->|Hess State Function Path Independence| P3
    P2 -.->|Hess State Function Path Independence| P3

    P1 --> R1[Sublimation Route]
    P2 --> R1
    R1 --> P3

    P1 --> R2[Fusion plus Vaporization Route]
    P2 --> R2
    R2 --> P3

    R1 --> EQ[Equation: lambda_s equals lambda_f plus lambda_v]
    R2 --> EQ
```

## 4.4 Clausius–Clapeyron Engineering Application Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INP[Input: Lambda_v, T1, P1, T2] --> CC[Apply Clausius Clapeyron]
    CC --> FORM[ln P2 by P1 equals Lambda_v by R times 1 by T1 minus 1 by T2]
    FORM --> OUT[Output: P2 at new temperature]

    INP2[Input: Experimental P1, P2, T1, T2] --> CC2[Apply Clausius Clapeyron Rearranged]
    CC2 --> FORM2[Lambda_v equals R times ln P2 by P1 divided by 1 by T1 minus 1 by T2]
    FORM2 --> OUT2[Output: Latent Heat of Vaporization]

    INP3[Input: Lambda_v1, T1, T2, T_c] --> WAT[Apply Watson Equation]
    WAT --> FORM3[Lambda_v2 equals Lambda_v1 times T_c minus T2 by T_c minus T1 raised to 0.38]
    FORM3 --> OUT3[Output: Latent Heat at New T]
```
