<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**Define the term "latent heat of vaporization." Why is it always greater than the latent heat of fusion for the same substance?**

**Model Answer:**

> Latent heat of vaporization is defined as the **quantity of heat required to convert one unit mass (or one kmol) of a liquid into vapor at its normal boiling point, under standard atmospheric pressure.**

It is always greater than the latent heat of fusion because:

1. **Vaporization requires complete separation** of molecules from each other, completely overcoming all intermolecular cohesive forces (van der Waals, hydrogen bonds, dipole-dipole, etc.).
2. **Fusion only requires loosening** of the rigid crystalline lattice — the molecules remain in close contact, still experiencing significant attractive forces.
3. Therefore, the energy input for vaporization must exceed that for fusion.

For example, for water at 1 atm:
- $\lambda_f = 333.5$ kJ/kg
- $\lambda_v = 2257$ kJ/kg
- $\lambda_v / \lambda_f \approx 6.77$

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**State Trouton's rule. Mention its limitations.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Trouton's Rule:** The molar entropy of vaporization at the normal boiling point is approximately **constant for many liquids**:
>
> $$\frac{\Lambda_{v,\text{NBP}}}{T_{\text{NBP}}} \approx 88 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$$

**Limitations of Trouton's Rule:**

1. **Fails for hydrogen-bonded liquids** like water, alcohols, and ammonia (where the ratio is higher, ~109 J/(mol·K) for water).
2. **Fails for highly associated liquids** with strong intermolecular forces.
3. **Fails for low-boiling-point substances** (e.g., He, H₂, Ne) where quantum effects dominate.
4. **Fails for highly polar liquids** with strong dipole-dipole interactions.

For such cases, the **Kistyakowsky equation** provides a better estimate:
$$\dfrac{\Lambda_{v,\text{NBP}}}{T_{\text{NBP}}} = 36.63 + R \ln T_{\text{NBP}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **INSTRUCTIONS (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### 🔷 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper Style]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

A chemical plant receives **5000 kg/h** of benzene at **25 °C**. It needs to be heated, vaporized, and superheated to **150 °C** before entering a reactor. Calculate the **total heat duty** required.

**Given Data:**
- Specific heat of liquid benzene, $C_{p,L} = 1.74$ kJ/(kg·K)
- Specific heat of benzene vapor, $C_{p,V} = 1.05$ kJ/(kg·K)
- Boiling point of benzene = **80.1 °C** (353.25 K)
- Latent heat of vaporization at 80.1 °C, $\lambda_v = 394$ kJ/kg

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sensible heat to raise liquid benzene from 25 °C to its boiling point (80.1 °C):**

$$Q_1 = m \cdot C_{p,L} \cdot \Delta T = 5000 \times 1.74 \times (80.1 - 25.0)$$

$$\Delta T = 55.1 \text{ K}$$

$$Q_1 = 5000 \times 1.74 \times 55.1 = 479{,}370 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Step 2 — Latent heat of vaporization at the boiling point:**

$$Q_2 = m \cdot \lambda_v = 5000 \times 394 = 1{,}970{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Step 3 — Sensible heat to superheat vapor from 80.1 °C to 150 °C:**

$$Q_3 = m \cdot C_{p,V} \cdot \Delta T = 5000 \times 1.05 \times (150.0 - 80.1)$$

$$\Delta T = 69.9 \text{ K}$$

$$Q_3 = 5000 \times 1.05 \times 69.9 = 366{,}975 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Step 4 — Total heat duty:**

$$Q_{\text{total}} = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3$$

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 479{,}370 + 1{,}970{,}000 + 366{,}975 = 2{,}816{,}345 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to kW (since 1 kW = 3600 kJ/h):**

$$Q_{\text{total}} = \frac{2{,}816{,}345}{3600} \approx 782.3 \text{ kW}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{Q_{\text{total}} \approx 782.3 \text{ kW}}$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

The latent heat of vaporization of a certain organic liquid at its normal boiling point of **350 K** is **30,000 J/mol**. Calculate the **vapor pressure** of this liquid at **400 K**, assuming it behaves as an ideal gas in the vapor phase. The vapor pressure at 350 K is **1 atm = 101.325 kPa**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the formula to use:** Clausius–Clapeyron equation:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \frac{\Lambda_v}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

- $\Lambda_v = 30{,}000$ J/mol
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- $T_1 = 350$ K, $P_1 = 101.325$ kPa
- $T_2 = 400$ K, $P_2 = ?$

**Step 3 — Compute the temperature term:**

$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{350} - \frac{1}{400} = \frac{400 - 350}{350 \times 400} = \frac{50}{140{,}000} = 3.5714 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the coefficient:**

$$\frac{\Lambda_v}{R} = \frac{30{,}000}{8.314} = 3608.85 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = 3608.85 \times 3.5714 \times 10^{-4} = 1.2889$$

**Step 6 — Exponentiate to find $P_2$:**

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = e^{1.2889} = 3.629$$

$$P_2 = 101.325 \times 3.629 \approx 367.7 \text{ kPa}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{P_2 \approx 367.7 \text{ kPa} \approx 3.63 \text{ atm}}$

---

### 🔷 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

The latent heat of vaporization of water at **100 °C** is **2257 kJ/kg**. Estimate its value at **200 °C** using **Watson's equation**. The critical temperature of water is **647.1 K**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Watson's equation:**

$$\frac{\lambda_{v2}}{\lambda_{v1}} = \left(\frac{T_c - T_2}{T_c - T_1}\right)^{0.38}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values:**

- $\lambda_{v1} = 2257$ kJ/kg at $T_1 = 373.15$ K
- $T_2 = 473.15$ K
- $T_c = 647.1$ K

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator term:**

$$T_c - T_2 = 647.1 - 473.15 = 173.95 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator term:**

$$T_c - T_1 = 647.1 - 373.15 = 273.95 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the ratio inside the bracket:**

$$\frac{T_c - T_2}{T_c - T_1} = \frac{173.95}{273.95} = 0.6350$$

**Step 6 — Raise to the power 0.38:**

$$0.6350^{0.38} = e^{0.38 \times \ln(0.6350)} = e^{0.38 \times (-0.4540)} = e^{-0.1725} = 0.8415$$

**Step 7 — Compute $\lambda_{v2}$:**

$$\lambda_{v2} = 2257 \times 0.8415 \approx 1899 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{\lambda_{v2} \approx 1899 \text{ kJ/kg at 200 °C}}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** The actual experimental value of $\lambda_v$ for water at 200 °C is approximately **1940 kJ/kg**. The Watson correlation slightly under-predicts because the exponent 0.38 is an empirical average that does not fit all substances perfectly.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

The normal boiling point of **ethanol** is **351.5 K**. Estimate its **molar latent heat of vaporization** using:
- (i) Trouton's rule
- (ii) Kistyakowsky equation

Comment on the accuracy of each estimate. The actual experimental value is **38,560 J/mol**.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Trouton's Rule:**

$$\Lambda_v = 88 \times T_b = 88 \times 351.5 = 30{,}932 \text{ J/mol}$$

**Part (ii) — Kistyakowsky Equation:**

$$\frac{\Lambda_v}{T_b} = 36.63 + R \ln T_b$$

$$\Lambda_v = T_b \left[36.63 + 8.314 \times \ln(351.5)\right]$$

Compute $\ln(351.5)$:

$$\ln(351.5) = \ln(350 \times 1.0043) = \ln 350 + \ln 1.0043 \approx 5.858 + 0.0043 = 5.862$$

Now:

$$36.63 + 8.314 \times 5.862 = 36.63 + 48.74 = 85.37 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$$

$$\Lambda_v = 351.5 \times 85.37 \approx 30{,}007 \text{ J/mol}$$

**Comparison with experimental value (38,560 J/mol):**

| Method | Estimated $\Lambda_v$ (J/mol) | % Error |
|---|---|---|
| Trouton's Rule | 30,932 | -19.8% |
| Kistyakowsky Equation | 30,007 | -22.2% |
| Experimental | 38,560 | — |

**Comment:** Both methods **significantly under-predict** the latent heat of vaporization of ethanol. This is because ethanol exhibits strong **hydrogen bonding** (–OH group), which is not accounted for in these empirical correlations. Trouton's rule, in particular, works best for **non-polar, non-associated liquids**. For associated substances, the actual ratio $\Lambda_v / T_b$ is much higher than 88 J/(mol·K).

**Final Answer:**
- Trouton: $\boxed{\Lambda_v \approx 30{,}932 \text{ J/mol}}$
- Kistyakowsky: $\boxed{\Lambda_v \approx 30{,}007 \text{ J/mol}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder:** Many students forget to convert °C to K (e.g., using 100 directly in Clausius–Clapeyron). The Clausius–Clapeyron and Watson equations **require absolute temperature (K)** — using Celsius will give a wrong answer and a minimum loss of 2–3 marks.
>
> 2. **Misidentifying Phase:** In combined sensible + latent problems, students often add latent heat at the *wrong* temperature or forget a phase change entirely. Always **draw a heating curve** first and label each segment.
>
> 3. **Sign Convention:** Latent heat of condensation is **negative** (heat released), while latent heat of vaporization is **positive** (heat absorbed). In heat-balance problems, account for sign.
>
> 4. **Watson Equation Exponent:** Using 0.38 is the standard; using 0.5 or 0.4 will yield wrong numerical answers.
>
> 5. **R Universal Gas Constant:** Always use $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) when $\Lambda_v$ is in J/mol. If $\Lambda_v$ is in kJ/mol, use $R = 8.314 \times 10^{-3}$ kJ/(mol·K). Mismatched units is the **#1 cause** of incorrect answers in KTU exams.
>
> 6. **For Trouton's Rule — Always Mention Limitations:** Examiners explicitly look for the limitations of Trouton's rule in 3-mark and 7-mark answers. Failing to mention them = 1–2 marks lost.
>
> 7. **Step-Marking in Long Problems:** Examiners award **partial marks** for correctly setting up the formula (1 mark), correct substitution (1 mark), correct intermediate calculation (1 mark), and final answer (1 mark). Even if the final number is wrong due to arithmetic, you can still get **2–3 marks** for the method.

---

## 5.3 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🎯 Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Latent Heats**

### A. Core Definitions (Must memorize)
- ☐ **Latent heat of fusion** $\lambda_f$ — solid → liquid at NMP
- ☐ **Latent heat of vaporization** $\lambda_v$ — liquid → vapor at NBP
- ☐ **Latent heat of sublimation** $\lambda_s$ — solid → vapor directly
- ☐ **Latent heat of condensation / solidification** — reverse processes (same magnitude, opposite sign)

### B. Fundamental Equations (Must memorize)
- ☐ $Q = m \cdot \lambda$ — total heat for phase change
- ☐ $\lambda_s = \lambda_f + \lambda_v$ — Hess's law for enthalpy
- ☐ Clapeyron: $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{\Lambda_v}{T \cdot \Delta \bar{V}}$
- ☐ Clausius–Clapeyron: $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \dfrac{\Lambda_v}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$
- ☐ Watson: $\dfrac{\lambda_{v2}}{\lambda_{v1}} = \left(\dfrac{T_c - T_2}{T_c - T_1}\right)^{0.38}$
- ☐ Trouton: $\dfrac{\Lambda_v}{T_b} \approx 88$ J/(mol·K)
- ☐ Kistyakowsky: $\dfrac{\Lambda_v}{T_b} = 36.63 + R \ln T_b$

### C. Critical Numerical Values to Remember
- ☐ $\lambda_f$ of water at 0 °C = **333.5 kJ/kg**
- ☐ $\lambda_v$ of water at 100 °C = **2257 kJ/kg**
- ☐ $T_c$ of water = **647.1 K**
- ☐ $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) = **8.314 × 10⁻³ kJ/(mol·K)**

### D. Conceptual Pitfalls
- ☐ Latent heat ≠ Sensible heat (constant $T$ vs. changing $T$)
- ☐ $\lambda_v$ is **temperature-dependent** — use Watson to correct
- ☐ At $T = T_c$, $\lambda_v \to 0$ (liquid and vapor become indistinguishable)
- ☐ Trouton's rule **fails** for H-bonded and highly polar liquids
- ☐ Clausius–Clapeyron assumes (a) $\bar{V}_L \ll \bar{V}_V$ and (b) ideal gas vapor

### E. Exam Strategy Reminders
- ☐ Always draw a **heating/cooling curve** before solving multi-stage problems
- ☐ Always use **absolute temperature (K)** in all gas-law and phase-equilibrium equations
- ☐ Always **state the assumptions** you are making (ideal gas, constant $\Lambda_v$, etc.)
- ☐ Always check **unit consistency** between $R$, $\Lambda_v$, and the result
- ☐ For KTU 14-mark questions, **label every step** clearly — partial marking is generous for neat, structured answers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Mnemonic — "LISA":**
> **L**atent = **I**sothermal + **S**tate change + **A**t constant pressure
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Enthalpy Changes Accompanying Chemical Reactions - Standard Heat of Formation and Standard Heat of Combustion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Enthalpy Changes Accompanying Chemical Reactions - Standard Heat of Formation and Standard Heat of Combustion # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Reaction Enthalpy The **Enthalpy Change Accompanying a Chemical Reaction (ΔH_rxn)** is the quantity of heat evolved (released) or absorbed (absorbed) when a chemical reaction takes place at constant temperature and constant pressure. Mathematically, it is defined as the difference between the sum of the enthalpies of the products and the sum of the enthalpies of the reactants, all evaluated at the same temperature and pressure. $$\Delta H_{rxn} = \sum H_{products} - \sum H_{reactants}$$ When ΔH_rxn is **negative**, the reaction is **exothermic** (releases heat to the surroundings). When ΔH_rxn is **positive**, the reaction is **endothermic** (absorbs heat from the surroundings). ## 1.2 Standard States and Reference Conditions All thermochemical data used in KTU problems are reported under the **Standard State Conditions**: - **Pressure:** 1 atm (101.325 kPa) - **Temperature:** 25 °C (**298.15 K**) - **Physical state:** The most stable form of the species at 1 atm and 25 °C The superscript "°" (small circle) denotes standard state. Thus **ΔH°** represents the standard enthalpy change. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner Convention:** Whenever you see ΔH°_f, ΔH°_c, or ΔH°_rxn in a numerical problem, ALWAYS assume the temperature is **298.15 K** and pressure is **1 atm** unless explicitly stated otherwise. Failing to write these conditions in your answer will cost you at least 1 mark. ## 1.3 Standard Heat of Formation (ΔH°_f) The **Standard Heat of Formation** of a compound is the enthalpy change accompanying the formation of **one mole** of the compound from its **constituent elements in their standard states** at 1 atm and 25 °C. Example: The standard heat of formation of water is based on the reaction: $$H_2(g) + \frac{1}{2}O_2(g) \rightarrow H_2O(l), \quad \Delta H^{\circ}_f = -285.8 \; kJ/mol$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Convention (Memorize This!):** The standard heat of formation of **every element in its standard state** is **ZERO**. > Examples: ΔH°_f of O₂(g) = 0, ΔH°_f of C(graphite) = 0, ΔH°_f of N₂(g) = 0, ΔH°_f of Fe(s) = 0. ## 1.4 Standard Heat of Combustion (ΔH°_c) The **Standard Heat of Combustion** of a substance is the enthalpy change accompanying the **complete combustion of one mole** of the substance with **excess (theoretical) oxygen** at 1 atm and 25 °C, with the products being the most stable oxides at those conditions. Example: For methane, the combustion reaction is: $$CH_4(g) + 2O_2(g) \rightarrow CO_2(g) + 2H_2O(l), \quad \Delta H^{\circ}_c = -890.4 \; kJ/mol$$ > [!NOTE] > **Combustion Product Rules (Frequently Tested in KTU):** > - **Carbon (C)** burns to **CO₂(g)** > - **Hydrogen (H)** burns to **H₂O(l)** — note it is *liquid*, not vapor > - **Sulfur (S)** burns to **SO₂(g)** > - **Nitrogen (N)** does not combust; it passes through as N₂(g) > - **Chlorine (Cl)** forms **HCl(g)** ## 1.5 Intuitive Real-World Analogy: The "Energy Bank Account" Imagine every chemical compound has an **"energy bank account"** in the sky. - **Standard Heat of Formation (ΔH°_f)** is like the **opening deposit** when a compound is "born" from its elements. Some compounds (like CO₂) are formed with a large energy release, so they have a **negative balance** (energy-rich and stable). Others (like NO) require energy input to form, so they have a **positive balance** (energy-poor and unstable). - **Standard Heat of Combustion (ΔH°_c)** is the **total withdrawal** when the compound is "cash out" back to its most stable oxidation products. This is the *useful* energy we extract in furnaces, engines, and power plants. - **Hess's Law** is the **bank statement rule**: the net change in the balance depends ONLY on the starting and ending accounts, not on how many transactions you made in between. This analogy is the key to solving all problems in this module. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hess's Law of Constant Heat Summation (Foundational Principle) **Statement:** If a chemical reaction can be expressed as the sum of two or more intermediate reactions, the enthalpy change of the overall reaction is equal to the **algebraic sum** of the enthalpy changes of the intermediate reactions. This is a direct consequence of the fact that **enthalpy is a state function** — it depends only on the initial and final states, not the path taken. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hess's Law is THE most heavily tested concept in Module 2 of PBSFT304.** Master it and you can solve 80% of the numerical questions. ## 2.2 The Two Master Formulas for Calculating Heat of Reaction ### Method 1: From Standard Heats of Formation The standard heat of any reaction is the difference between the sum of the standard heats of formation of the **products** and the sum of the standard heats of formation of the **reactants**, each multiplied by its stoichiometric coefficient. $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = \sum_{i} n_{p,i} \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_{f,i} \big(products\big) - \sum_{j} n_{r,j} \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_{f,j} \big(reactants\big)$$ Where: - n_{p,i} = stoichiometric coefficient of product i - n_{r,j} = stoichiometric coefficient of reactant j ### Method 2: From Standard Heats of Combustion The standard heat of any reaction is equal to the **negative** of the difference between the sum of the standard heats of combustion of the **products** and the sum of the standard heats of combustion of the **reactants**. $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = \sum_{j} n_{r,j} \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_{c,j} \big(reactants\big) - \sum_{i} n_{p,i} \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_{c,i} \big(products\big)$$ > [!WARNING] > **Sign Reversal Trap:** Notice the formulas are **mirror images** of each other! > - Heats of **FORMATION** → *Products MINUS Reactants* > - Heats of **COMBUSTION** → *Reactants MINUS Products* > Reversing the order in your exam is the #1 reason students lose marks. ## 2.3 Engineering Utility of These Concepts In real chemical engineering practice, the heat of reaction determines: - **Reactor design:** Whether to use an isothermal, adiabatic, or cooled/heated reactor - **Heat exchanger sizing:** Calculating the cooling duty or heating duty (Q = ΔH) - **Fuel selection:** Higher \vert \Delta H^{\circ}_c \vert = more energy per unit fuel - **Safety analysis:** Predicting runaway reactions - **Process simulation software (Aspen Plus, HYSYS):** All reaction blocks require ΔH_rxn as a primary input ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units & Conditions | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Reaction Enthalpy (General) | $\Delta H_{rxn} = \sum H_{products} - \sum H_{reactants}$ | kJ, constant T and P | | 2 | From Heat of Formation | $\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = \sum n_p \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(P) - \sum n_r \Delta H^{\circ}_{f}(R)$ | kJ/mol, 298.15 K, 1 atm | | 3 | From Heat of Combustion | $\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = \sum n_r \Delta H^{\circ}_{c}(R) - \sum n_p \Delta H^{\circ}_{c}(P)$ | kJ/mol, 298.15 K, 1 atm | | 4 | Reference State Rule | $\Delta H^{\circ}_f$ of element in standard state $= 0$ | Universal | | 5 | Exothermic Reaction | $\Delta H < 0$ | Heat released | | 6 | Endothermic Reaction | $\Delta H > 0$ | Heat absorbed | | 7 | Hess's Law | $\Delta H_{net} = \Delta H_1 + \Delta H_2 + \Delta H_3 + \ldots$ | Path independent | | 8 | Adiabatic Flame Temperature | $T_{ad} = T_{ref} + \frac{-\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn}}{\sum n_i C_{p,i}}$ | For energy balance | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of "Heat of Reaction from Heat of Formation" Formula **Starting Point:** Consider a general balanced chemical reaction: $$aA + bB \rightarrow cC + dD$$ where a, b, c, d are the stoichiometric coefficients. **Step 1:** Express the formation reactions for each compound. The standard heat of formation of compound C corresponds to: $$\text{Elements of C} \rightarrow C, \quad \Delta H^{\circ}_f(C)$$ **Step 2:** Apply Hess's Law. Imagine a hypothetical **two-step thermochemical pathway**: - **Pathway I (Direct Route):** Reactants → Products $$aA + bB \rightarrow cC + dD, \quad \Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = ?$$ - **Pathway II (Indirect Route via Elements):** - Step 1: Decompose reactants into their elements $$aA \rightarrow a \cdot (\text{elements of A}), \quad \Delta H_1 = -a \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_f(A)$$ $$bB \rightarrow b \cdot (\text{elements of B}), \quad \Delta H_2 = -b \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_f(B)$$ - Step 2: Form products from those elements $$c \cdot (\text{elements of C}) \rightarrow cC, \quad \Delta H_3 = +c \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_f(C)$$ $$d \cdot (\text{elements of D}) \rightarrow dD, \quad \Delta H_4 = +d \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_f(D)$$ **Step 3:** Apply Hess's Law (Path I = Path II): $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = \Delta H_1 + \Delta H_2 + \Delta H_3 + \Delta H_4$$ **Step 4:** Substitute the expressions: $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = -a\Delta H^{\circ}_f(A) - b\Delta H^{\circ}_f(B) + c\Delta H^{\circ}_f(C) + d\Delta H^{\circ}_f(D)$$ **Step 5:** Re-arrange into the standard "products minus reactants" form: $$\boxed{\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = \big[c\Delta H^{\circ}_f(C) + d\Delta H^{\circ}_f(D)\big] - \big[a\Delta H^{\circ}_f(A) + b\Delta H^{\circ}_f(B)\big]}$$ This matches the master formula given in Section 2.2. ## 3.2 Derivation of "Heat of Reaction from Heat of Combustion" Formula **Conceptual Foundation:** Combustion reactions convert everything to the most stable oxides. So if we **combust both the reactants AND the products**, we end up at the same final oxidized state from both directions. **Step 1:** Consider the same general reaction: $$aA + bB \rightarrow cC + dD$$ **Step 2:** Write combustion reactions for all species: $$A + O_2 \rightarrow \text{Oxides of A}, \quad \Delta H^{\circ}_c(A)$$ $$B + O_2 \rightarrow \text{Oxides of B}, \quad \Delta H^{\circ}_c(B)$$ $$C + O_2 \rightarrow \text{Oxides of C}, \quad \Delta H^{\circ}_c(C)$$ $$D + O_2 \rightarrow \text{Oxides of D}, \quad \Delta H^{\circ}_c(D)$$ **Step 3:** Build a Hess's Law cycle: - **Direct Path:** $aA + bB \rightarrow cC + dD$, giving $\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn}$ - **Indirect Path:** Burn reactants AND burn products (in reverse), both reaching the same "fully oxidized" final state. **Step 4:** Setting up the cycle algebraically — combust reactants (forward) then un-combust products (reverse): $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} + \big[c\Delta H^{\circ}_c(C) + d\Delta H^{\circ}_c(D)\big] = \big[a\Delta H^{\circ}_c(A) + b\Delta H^{\circ}_c(B)\big]$$ **Step 5:** Solve for $\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn}$: $$\boxed{\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = \big[a\Delta H^{\circ}_c(A) + b\Delta H^{\circ}_c(B)\big] - \big[c\Delta H^{\circ}_c(C) + d\Delta H^{\circ}_c(D)\big]}$$ This is the **reactants minus products** arrangement, which is the inverse of the formation formula. ## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example: Verification of Both Methods **Problem:** Calculate the standard heat of the reaction: $$C_2H_4(g) + H_2(g) \rightarrow C_2H_6(g)$$ **Given Data:** - $\Delta H^{\circ}_f$ of C₂H₄(g) = +52.3 kJ/mol - $\Delta H^{\circ}_f$ of H₂(g) = 0 (element in standard state) - $\Delta H^{\circ}_f$ of C₂H₆(g) = -84.7 kJ/mol - $\Delta H^{\circ}_c$ of C₂H₄(g) = -1411.0 kJ/mol - $\Delta H^{\circ}_c$ of H₂(g) = -285.8 kJ/mol - $\Delta H^{\circ}_c$ of C₂H₆(g) = -1559.7 kJ/mol ### Method 1: Using Heats of Formation **Step 1:** Identify products and reactants with coefficients (all = 1 here). **Step 2:** Apply the formula: $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = (1)\Delta H^{\circ}_f(C_2H_6) - (1)\Delta H^{\circ}_f(C_2H_4) - (1)\Delta H^{\circ}_f(H_2)$$ **Step 3:** Substitute numerical values: $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = (-84.7) - (+52.3) - (0)$$ **Step 4:** Calculate: $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = -84.7 - 52.3 = -137.0 \; kJ/mol$$ The reaction is **exothermic** (ΔH is negative). ### Method 2: Using Heats of Combustion (Verification) **Step 1:** Apply the combustion formula: $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = (1)\Delta H^{\circ}_c(C_2H_4) + (1)\Delta H^{\circ}_c(H_2) - (1)\Delta H^{\circ}_c(C_2H_6)$$ **Step 2:** Substitute numerical values: $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = (-1411.0) + (-285.8) - (-1559.7)$$ **Step 3:** Calculate step by step: $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = -1411.0 - 285.8 + 1559.7$$ $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = -1696.8 + 1559.7 = -137.1 \; kJ/mol$$ The small difference of 0.1 kJ/mol is due to rounding of tabulated data. **Both methods give the same answer**, confirming Hess's Law and validating the formulas. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Thermochemical Cycle: Heat of Formation Method (Block-Level Architecture) The following Mermaid flow diagram illustrates how Hess's Law connects the **formation reactions** of individual species to the **target reaction** through a conceptual "elements reservoir." ```mermaid flowchart TD A1["Reactant A"]:::reactant A2["Reactant B"]:::reactant ELE["Elements Reservoir
Standard States
O2, H2, C, N2"]:::element B1["Product C"]:::product B2["Product D"]:::product F1["Formation Reaction of A
dH_f_A"]:::formation F2["Formation Reaction of B
dH_f_B"]:::formation F3["Formation Reaction of C
dH_f_C"]:::formation F4["Formation Reaction of D
dH_f_D"]:::formation RXN["TARGET REACTION
dH_rxn = ?"]:::target A1 -- decomposes --> ELE A2 -- decomposes --> ELE ELE -- forms --> B1 ELE -- forms --> B2 F1 -.covers.-> A1 F2 -.covers.-> A2 F3 -.covers.-> B1 F4 -.covers.-> B2 A1 --> RXN A2 --> RXN RXN --> B1 RXN --> B2 classDef reactant fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000 classDef product fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#27ae60,color:#000 classDef element fill:#d6e9ff,stroke:#2980b9,color:#000 classDef formation fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#f39c12,color:#000 classDef target fill:#e8d6ff,stroke:#8e44ad,color:#000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** The horizontal direct path (A₁ + A₂ → B₁ + B₂) gives ΔH_rxn. The vertical detour (A₁ + A₂ → Elements → B₁ + B₂) involves ΔH_f values. By Hess's Law, both paths are equivalent, leading to the "products minus reactants" formula. ## 4.2 Thermochemical Cycle: Heat of Combustion Method ```mermaid flowchart LR R1["Reactant A"]:::reactant R2["Reactant B"]:::reactant P1["Product C"]:::product P2["Product D"]:::product OX["Combustion Products
CO2, H2O, SO2
Universal Final State"]:::oxide RXN2["TARGET REACTION
dH_rxn"]:::target R1 -- combust dH_c_A --> OX R2 -- combust dH_c_B --> OX P1 -- combust dH_c_C --> OX P2 -- combust dH_c_D --> OX R1 --> RXN2 R2 --> RXN2 RXN2 --> P1 RXN2 --> P2 P1 -. reverse combustion .-> OX P2 -. reverse combustion .-> OX classDef reactant fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000 classDef product fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#27ae60,color:#000 classDef oxide fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#555,color:#000 classDef target fill:#e8d6ff,stroke:#8e44ad,color:#000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** Both reactants and products ultimately reach the same "fully oxidized" state. Reactants go there via forward combustion; products must go there in reverse. The net energy balance yields the "reactants minus products" formula. ## 4.3 Method Selection Decision Matrix ```mermaid flowchart TD START["Given Data Type?"]:::start PATH1["Heat of Formation Data
dH_f values"]:::path PATH2["Heat of Combustion Data
dH_c values"]:::path FORMULA1["Use Formula:
dH_rxn = sum n_p dH_f_P - sum n_r dH_f_R"]:::formula FORMULA2["Use Formula:
dH_rxn = sum n_r dH_c_R - sum n_p dH_c_P"]:::formula ANSWER["Compute dH_rxn
Report sign and units"]:::result START --> PATH1 START --> PATH2 PATH1 --> FORMULA1 PATH2 --> FORMULA2 FORMULA1 --> ANSWER FORMULA2 --> ANSWER classDef start fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#f39c12,color:#000 classDef path fill:#d6e9ff,stroke:#2980b9,color:#000 classDef formula fill:#e8d6ff,stroke:#8e44ad,color:#000 classDef result fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#27ae60,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Define the following terms with one suitable example for each:** **(a)** Standard heat of formation **(b)** Standard heat of combustion **Model Answer:** **(a) Standard Heat of Formation (ΔH°_f):** It is defined as the enthalpy change accompanying the formation of one mole of a compound from its constituent elements in their standard states (1 atm, 25 °C). The standard heat of formation of an element in its standard state is conventionally taken as zero. *Example:* The standard heat of formation of water is the enthalpy change of the reaction $$H_2(g) + \frac{1}{2}O_2(g) \rightarrow H_2O(l), \quad \Delta H^{\circ}_f = -285.8 \; kJ/mol$$ **(b) Standard Heat of Combustion (ΔH°_c):** It is defined as the enthalpy change accompanying the complete combustion of one mole of a substance with excess (theoretical) oxygen at 1 atm and 25 °C, with products being the most stable oxides (CO₂ for C, H₂O for H, SO₂ for S, N₂ for N, HCl for Cl). *Example:* The standard heat of combustion of methane is the enthalpy change of $$CH_4(g) + 2O_2(g) \rightarrow CO_2(g) + 2H_2O(l), \quad \Delta H^{\circ}_c = -890.4 \; kJ/mol$$ **[3 Marks: 1.5 marks for each definition + example]** --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **State Hess's Law of Constant Heat Summation. Why is it applicable to all thermochemical calculations?** **Model Answer:** **Statement of Hess's Law:** If a chemical reaction can be carried out in multiple steps (either directly or through a series of intermediate reactions), the total enthalpy change of the overall reaction is equal to the **algebraic sum of the enthalpy changes of the individual steps**, provided the initial and final states (temperature, pressure, physical state) are the same. **Why it is applicable:** Hess's Law is universally valid because **enthalpy (H) is a state function**. A state function depends only on the initial and final states of the system, not on the path taken between them. Since ΔH is a state function (path-independent), the total enthalpy change for any cyclic process must be zero, and the enthalpy change between two fixed states is unique regardless of the path. **Significance:** This principle allows us to calculate enthalpy changes of reactions that cannot be measured experimentally (e.g., formation of CO from C and O₂, which is hard to isolate from CO₂ formation) by constructing thermochemical cycles from known formation or combustion data. **[3 Marks: 1.5 marks for statement + 1.5 marks for explanation]** --- ## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Module in the End Semester Exam contains a choice between Question A and Question B. Both questions are for 14 marks, typically split as 7 + 7 sub-parts. You only need to attempt **ONE** full question from the choice. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **(a)** The standard heats of formation of CO₂(g), H₂O(l), and CH₄(g) are **−393.5, −285.8, and −74.8 kJ/mol** respectively. Calculate the **standard heat of combustion of methane** at 298.15 K. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** The standard heats of combustion of ethylene (C₂H₄), ethane (C₂H₆), and hydrogen (H₂) are **−1411.0, −1559.7, and −285.8 kJ/mol** respectively. Calculate the **standard heat of reaction** for the hydrogenation of ethylene to ethane: $$C_2H_4(g) + H_2(g) \rightarrow C_2H_6(g)$$ Comment on the sign of ΔH. **[7 Marks]** --- #### Model Solution for Question A **Part (a) Solution:** **Step 1:** Write the balanced combustion reaction for methane: $$CH_4(g) + 2O_2(g) \rightarrow CO_2(g) + 2H_2O(l)$$ **Step 2:** Recall that ΔH°_f of O₂(g) = 0 (element in standard state). **Step 3:** Apply the heat of formation formula: $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = \sum n_p \Delta H^{\circ}_f(products) - \sum n_r \Delta H^{\circ}_f(reactants)$$ **Step 4:** Substitute values: $$\Delta H^{\circ}_c(CH_4) = \big[1 \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_f(CO_2) + 2 \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_f(H_2O)\big] - \big[1 \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_f(CH_4) + 2 \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_f(O_2)\big]$$ **Step 5:** Plug in numerical values: $$\Delta H^{\circ}_c(CH_4) = \big[1(-393.5) + 2(-285.8)\big] - \big[1(-74.8) + 2(0)\big]$$ **Step 6:** Calculate inside each bracket: $$\Delta H^{\circ}_c(CH_4) = \big[-393.5 - 571.6\big] - \big[-74.8\big]$$ $$\Delta H^{\circ}_c(CH_4) = (-965.1) - (-74.8) = -965.1 + 74.8$$ **Step 7:** Final answer: $$\boxed{\Delta H^{\circ}_c(CH_4) = -890.3 \; kJ/mol}$$ **Comment:** The large negative value indicates that methane releases a substantial amount of heat on combustion, which is why it is an excellent fuel. **[7 Marks: 1 for reaction, 2 for formula, 3 for calculation, 1 for final answer with units]** --- **Part (b) Solution:** **Step 1:** Identify the target reaction: $$C_2H_4(g) + H_2(g) \rightarrow C_2H_6(g)$$ **Step 2:** Apply the **heat of combustion formula** (note: reactants MINUS products): $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = \sum n_r \Delta H^{\circ}_c(reactants) - \sum n_p \Delta H^{\circ}_c(products)$$ **Step 3:** Substitute values: $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = \big[1 \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_c(C_2H_4) + 1 \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_c(H_2)\big] - \big[1 \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_c(C_2H_6)\big]$$ **Step 4:** Plug in numerical values: $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = \big[1(-1411.0) + 1(-285.8)\big] - \big[1(-1559.7)\big]$$ **Step 5:** Calculate: $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = (-1411.0 - 285.8) - (-1559.7)$$ $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = (-1696.8) + 1559.7$$ **Step 6:** Final answer: $$\boxed{\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = -137.1 \; kJ/mol}$$ **Comment on sign:** The negative sign indicates that the hydrogenation of ethylene to ethane is an **exothermic reaction**, meaning heat is liberated during the conversion. This is the basis of catalytic hydrogenation reactors used in the petroleum and food industries (e.g., margarine production). **[7 Marks: 1 for formula selection, 2 for substitution, 2 for calculation, 1 for sign comment, 1 for final boxed answer]** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(a)** The standard heats of the following reactions at 298.15 K are given: - (i) $C(graphite) + O_2(g) \rightarrow CO_2(g)$, $\Delta H^{\circ}_1 = -393.5 \; kJ$ - (ii) $H_2(g) + \frac{1}{2}O_2(g) \rightarrow H_2O(l)$, $\Delta H^{\circ}_2 = -285.8 \; kJ$ - (iii) $C_2H_6(g) + \frac{7}{2}O_2(g) \rightarrow 2CO_2(g) + 3H_2O(l)$, $\Delta H^{\circ}_3 = -1559.7 \; kJ$ Calculate the **standard heat of formation of ethane (C₂H₆)** at 298.15 K using Hess's Law. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** The standard heats of formation of NO(g), NO₂(g), and N₂O₄(g) are **+90.25, +33.18, and +9.16 kJ/mol** respectively. Calculate the standard heat of reaction for the dimerization of nitrogen dioxide: $$2NO_2(g) \rightarrow N_2O_4(g)$$ Is the reaction exothermic or endothermic? Justify. **[7 Marks]** --- #### Model Solution for Question B **Part (a) Solution:** **Step 1:** Write the **target formation reaction** for ethane (formation from elements in their standard states, 2 moles of C and 3 moles of H₂): $$2C(graphite) + 3H_2(g) \rightarrow C_2H_6(g), \quad \Delta H^{\circ}_f(C_2H_6) = ?$$ **Step 2:** Combine the given reactions using Hess's Law to get the target reaction: - Multiply (i) by 2: $2C + 2O_2 \rightarrow 2CO_2$, $\Delta H = 2 \times (-393.5) = -787.0 \; kJ$ - Multiply (ii) by 3: $3H_2 + \frac{3}{2}O_2 \rightarrow 3H_2O$, $\Delta H = 3 \times (-285.8) = -857.4 \; kJ$ - Reverse (iii): $2CO_2 + 3H_2O \rightarrow C_2H_6 + \frac{7}{2}O_2$, $\Delta H = +1559.7 \; kJ$ **Step 3:** Add the three modified reactions: $$[2C + 2O_2] + [3H_2 + \frac{3}{2}O_2] + [2CO_2 + 3H_2O] \rightarrow [2CO_2] + [3H_2O] + [C_2H_6] + \frac{7}{2}O_2$$ **Step 4:** Cancel species appearing on both sides: - 2CO₂: cancels - 3H₂O: cancels - O₂ on left = 2 + 1.5 = 3.5 = 7/2: cancels with right side **Net Result:** $2C + 3H_2 \rightarrow C_2H_6$ ✓ (matches the target formation reaction) **Step 5:** Sum the enthalpies: $$\Delta H^{\circ}_f(C_2H_6) = -787.0 + (-857.4) + 1559.7$$ $$\Delta H^{\circ}_f(C_2H_6) = -1644.4 + 1559.7$$ **Step 6:** Final answer: $$\boxed{\Delta H^{\circ}_f(C_2H_6) = -84.7 \; kJ/mol}$$ This value can be verified against standard thermodynamic tables. **[7 Marks: 1 for target reaction, 2 for reaction manipulation, 2 for cancellation, 1 for summation, 1 for final answer]** --- **Part (b) Solution:** **Step 1:** Write the target reaction: $$2NO_2(g) \rightarrow N_2O_4(g)$$ **Step 2:** Apply the **heat of formation formula**: $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = \sum n_p \Delta H^{\circ}_f(products) - \sum n_r \Delta H^{\circ}_f(reactants)$$ **Step 3:** Substitute values: $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = \big[1 \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_f(N_2O_4)\big] - \big[2 \cdot \Delta H^{\circ}_f(NO_2)\big]$$ **Step 4:** Plug in numerical values: $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = (1)(+9.16) - (2)(+33.18)$$ **Step 5:** Calculate: $$\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = 9.16 - 66.36$$ **Step 6:** Final answer: $$\boxed{\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = -57.20 \; kJ/mol}$$ **Justification:** The negative sign indicates that the dimerization of NO₂ to N₂O₄ is **exothermic** — heat is released when two NO₂ molecules combine to form the more stable dimer N₂O₄. This is why cooling brown NO₂ gas shifts the equilibrium toward the colorless N₂O₄ dimer (Le Chatelier's principle). **[7 Marks: 1 for formula, 2 for substitution, 2 for calculation, 2 for justification with equilibrium concept]** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:** > > 1. **Sign Reversal Mistake (Most Common — costs 2-3 marks):** Mixing up the "products minus reactants" and "reactants minus products" formulas. **Mnemonic trick:** Heat of **F**ormation follows the **P**roducts-then-**R**eactants pattern (**FPR**). Heat of **C**ombustion does the **R**everse (**CRR**). > > 2. **Forgetting the Negative Sign for Combustion:** The product terms in the combustion formula must be **subtracted**, not added. Writing $\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = \Delta H_c(R) + \Delta H_c(P)$ is WRONG and will fetch zero marks. > > 3. **Forgetting Stoichiometric Coefficients:** If a reaction has 2H₂O as product, the contribution is $2 \times \Delta H^{\circ}_f(H_2O)$, not just $1 \times \Delta H^{\circ}_f(H_2O)$. Always multiply by the coefficient from the balanced equation. > > 4. **Wrong Physical State of Water in Combustion:** The standard heat of combustion uses **liquid H₂O(l)**, not water vapor H₂O(g). Using the wrong state will cost you 1-2 marks. > > 5. **Not Stating Units:** Always write the final answer as **"kJ/mol"** or **"kJ"** (depending on whether the reaction is per mole). A numerically correct answer without units loses 0.5 mark. > > 6. **Cancellation Errors in Hess's Law:** When manipulating intermediate reactions, forgetting to **multiply the ΔH by the same factor** as the reaction coefficients, or forgetting to **reverse the sign** when flipping a reaction. Always show all three steps explicitly. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Module 2 — High-Yield Summary for Last-Minute Revision** - **Reaction Enthalpy Definition:** $\Delta H_{rxn} = \sum H_{products} - \sum H_{reactants}$ (state function) - **Standard State:** 1 atm pressure, 25 °C (298.15 K), most stable physical form - **Heat of Formation (ΔH°_f):** Enthalpy change for forming **1 mole** of compound from elements in standard states - **Heat of Combustion (ΔH°_c):** Enthalpy change for **complete combustion of 1 mole** with O₂ to most stable oxides - **Reference State Rule:** $\Delta H^{\circ}_f$ of **any element in its standard state = 0** (e.g., O₂, N₂, graphite, S₈, Fe) - **Master Formula 1 (Formation):** $\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = \sum n_p \Delta H^{\circ}_f(P) - \sum n_r \Delta H^{\circ}_f(R)$ - **Master Formula 2 (Combustion):** $\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = \sum n_r \Delta H^{\circ}_c(R) - \sum n_p \Delta H^{\circ}_c(P)$ - **Hess's Law:** Total ΔH is path-independent; cyclic ΔH = 0 - **Combustion Products to Memorize:** C → CO₂(g), H → H₂O(**l**), S → SO₂(g), N → N₂(g), Cl → HCl(g) - **Exothermic:** ΔH < 0 (heat released, products more stable) - **Endothermic:** ΔH > 0 (heat absorbed, products less stable) - **Method Selection:** Use formation data if given; use combustion data if all species are organic/combustible - **Units:** Always report in **kJ** or **kJ/mol** with proper sign and physical state assumptions - **Engineering Applications:** Reactor design (heat duty), fuel calorific value, process safety analysis, adiabatic flame temperature calculations - **Hess's Law Cycle Construction:** Always write (1) target reaction, (2) given reactions, (3) manipulation (multiply/divide/reverse), (4) verify cancellation, (5) sum ΔH values

## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Standard Heat of Reaction** ($\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ$) is defined as the amount of heat evolved or absorbed during a chemical reaction carried out at constant temperature and pressure, when **all reactants and products are in their standard states** and the reaction proceeds to the extent that one mole of a designated product (or reactant) is formed (or consumed).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard State Reference Conditions (as per IUPAC/KTU convention):**
> - **Temperature:** $T = 298.15 \, \text{K}$ (often written as $25^\circ \text{C}$)
> - **Pressure:** $P = 1 \, \text{atm}$ ($= 101.325 \, \text{kPa}$)
> - **Concentration (for solutions):** $C = 1 \, \text{mol/L}$
> - **Physical State:** Pure, stable form of the substance at $1 \, \text{atm}$ and $25^\circ \text{C}$

The superscript "$\circ$" denotes **standard state conditions**. Subscript "$rxn$" denotes a generic chemical reaction. By KTU sign convention:
- $\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ < 0$ → **Exothermic** reaction (heat released)
- $\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ > 0$ → **Endothermic** reaction (heat absorbed)

### 1.2 Mathematical Statement

For a generalized reaction:
$$aA + bB \rightarrow cC + dD$$

The standard heat of reaction is expressed as:
$$\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ = \sum_{i=\text{products}} \nu_i H_i^\circ - \sum_{j=\text{reactants}} \nu_j H_j^\circ$$

where $\nu_i$ and $\nu_j$ are the stoichiometric coefficients of the products and reactants respectively, and $H_i^\circ$ is the standard molar enthalpy of species $i$.

> [!NOTE]
> The absolute enthalpies of individual species are not measurable. Therefore, we use **reference states** to express all heats relative to a defined zero. This is the origin of the concepts of *Standard Heat of Formation* and *Standard Heat of Combustion*.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Balance" Picture

Imagine the **chemical energy** of a molecule as the balance in a bank account:

- **Reactants** are people walking *into* a bank with money.
- **Products** are people walking *out* of a bank with money.
- The **Standard Heat of Reaction** is the *net change* in the bank's total balance after the transaction.

To make this comparison fair, the bank measures everyone's balance at the **same reference moment** (this is the **standard state**: $298 \, \text{K}$, $1 \, \text{atm}$). Just as a bank cannot assign an "absolute" money value to a person without a starting reference, chemists cannot assign absolute enthalpies to elements. They assign every element in its natural form a balance of **zero** at standard state, and then express all compounds relative to this zero.

- If the products have **less** energy stored than reactants → energy is **released** as heat (exothermic → balance decreases).
- If the products have **more** energy stored than reactants → energy is **absorbed** (endothermic → balance increases).

### 1.4 Why the "Standard" State Matters in Chemical Engineering

In real chemical plants, reactions occur at:
- **High pressures** (e.g., Haber process at $200 \, \text{atm}$)
- **Elevated temperatures** (e.g., methanol synthesis at $500 \, \text{K}$)
- **Non-ideal concentrations**

However, all thermodynamic property databases ($\Delta H_f^\circ$, $\Delta H_c^\circ$, $C_p$) are tabulated at the **standard state**. The Standard Heat of Reaction therefore acts as the **universal reference** from which the actual heat of reaction at *any* operating condition can be calculated using:
1. **Hess's Law** (composition of reactions)
2. **Kirchhoff's Equation** (temperature correction)

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Reactor design, energy integration (pinch analysis), and safety studies (relief valve sizing) all begin with $\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ$ as the foundational value.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

The standard heat of reaction is a **state function**. This means its value depends *only* on the initial and final states of the system, not on the path taken. This single property gives rise to two of the most powerful computational tools in chemical thermodynamics:

#### 2.1.1 Hess's Law of Constant Heat Summation

**Statement:** *If a reaction can be expressed as the algebraic sum of two or more other reactions, then the standard heat of the overall reaction is the algebraic sum of the standard heats of the constituent reactions.*

This law is a direct consequence of the first law of thermodynamics applied to enthalpy (a state function). Hess's Law is the theoretical foundation for calculating $\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ$ using either heats of formation or heats of combustion.

#### 2.1.2 Standard Heat of Formation ($\Delta H_f^\circ$)

The standard heat of formation of a compound is the enthalpy change when **one mole** of the compound is formed from its constituent elements, with all species in their **standard states**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> By definition: $\Delta H_f^\circ$ of any element in its reference (most stable) form at $1 \, \text{atm}$ and $25^\circ \text{C}$ is **zero**.
> Examples: $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{O}_2, g) = 0$, $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{C, graphite}) = 0$, $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{N}_2, g) = 0$.

**Calculation Rule:**
$$\boxed{\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ = \sum_i \nu_i \Delta H_{f,i}^\circ \,\big(\text{products}\big) - \sum_j \nu_j \Delta H_{f,j}^\circ \,\big(\text{reactants}\big)}$$

#### 2.1.3 Standard Heat of Combustion ($\Delta H_c^\circ$)

The standard heat of combustion of a substance is the enthalpy change when **one mole** of the substance undergoes complete combustion with oxygen, with all species in their **standard states** and products being $\text{CO}_2(g)$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$, and other fully oxidized species.

**Calculation Rule:**
$$\boxed{\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ = \sum_j \nu_j \Delta H_{c,j}^\circ \,\big(\text{reactants}\big) - \sum_i \nu_i \Delta H_{c,i}^\circ \,\big(\text{products}\big)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the sign reversal in combustion method?** Heats of combustion are tabulated for *reactants burning*. For the products ($\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$), combustion is undefined (they are already at their oxidized state, so $\Delta H_c^\circ = 0$ by convention). The general formula is: (Sum of $\Delta H_c^\circ$ of species *that can burn* on the reactant side) minus (Sum on the product side).

#### 2.1.4 Temperature Dependence — Kirchhoff's Equation

In real reactors, $T \neq 298.15 \, \text{K}$. Kirchhoff's Law gives the standard heat of reaction at any temperature $T$:

$$\boxed{\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ(T) = \Delta H_{rxn}^\circ(298) + \int_{298}^{T} \Delta C_p \, dT}$$

where the net heat capacity change is:
$$\Delta C_p = \sum_i \nu_i C_{p,i}\,\big(\text{products}\big) - \sum_j \nu_j C_{p,j}\,\big(\text{reactants}\big)$$

For the common case of $C_p = a + bT + cT^2$:

$$\int_{298}^{T} \Delta C_p \, dT = \Delta a(T - 298) + \frac{\Delta b}{2}(T^2 - 298^2) + \frac{\Delta c}{3}(T^3 - 298^3)$$

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Boundary Condition / Use** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Standard Heat of Reaction | $\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ = \sum \nu_i H_i^\circ\big(\text{prod}\big) - \sum \nu_j H_j^\circ\big(\text{react}\big)$ | Reference state: $298.15 \, \text{K}$, $1 \, \text{atm}$ |
| Heat of Formation Method | $\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ = \sum \nu_i \Delta H_{f,i}^\circ(\text{prod}) - \sum \nu_j \Delta H_{f,j}^\circ(\text{react})$ | Elements in standard state: $\Delta H_f^\circ = 0$ |
| Heat of Combustion Method | $\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ = \sum \nu_j \Delta H_{c,j}^\circ(\text{react}) - \sum \nu_i \Delta H_{c,i}^\circ(\text{prod})$ | Combustion products: $\text{CO}_2(g)$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$ |
| Kirchhoff's Equation | $\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ(T_2) = \Delta H_{rxn}^\circ(T_1) + \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \Delta C_p \, dT$ | Assumes no phase change between $T_1$ and $T_2$ |
| Mean Heat Capacity | $\overline{C_p} = \frac{1}{T_2 - T_1}\int_{T_1}^{T_2} C_p \, dT$ | Used for average-value approximations |
| $\Delta C_p$ Definition | $\Delta C_p = \sum \nu_i C_{p,i}(\text{prod}) - \sum \nu_j C_{p,j}(\text{react})$ | Always evaluate at the *target* temperature |
| Exothermic Condition | $\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ < 0$ | Heat is *released* to surroundings |
| Endothermic Condition | $\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ > 0$ | Heat is *absorbed* from surroundings |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Reactor Sizing and Design:** The energy balance equation $Q = \Delta H_{rxn} \pm W$ governs heat exchanger sizing, jacket/cooling coil design, and reactor temperature profiles.
2. **Process Safety (Runaway Reactions):** Highly exothermic reactions (e.g., polymerization of styrene, $\Delta H \approx -70 \, \text{kJ/mol}$) require emergency relief systems sized using adiabatic temperature rise.
3. **Energy Integration / Pinch Analysis:** Combining exothermic and endothermic streams in a process plant (heat exchange networks) starts with the standard heats of all reactions.
4. **Fuel Calorific Value:** Standard heat of combustion directly gives the heating value of fuels (GCV/LCV).
5. **Environmental Engineering:** Combustion enthalpy calculations underpin $\text{CO}_2$ emissions accounting and incinerator design.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Heat of Reaction from Standard Heats of Formation

**Starting Point:** A generalized reaction,
$$aA + bB \rightarrow cC + dD$$

We require expressions for the enthalpies of formation of all species. Each species can be hypothetically formed from its constituent elements. Let:
- $A$ formed from elements: $\rightarrow A$, $\Delta H = \Delta H_{f,A}^\circ$
- $B$ formed from elements: $\rightarrow B$, $\Delta H = \Delta H_{f,B}^\circ$
- $C$ formed from elements: $\rightarrow C$, $\Delta H = \Delta H_{f,C}^\circ$
- $D$ formed from elements: $\rightarrow D$, $\Delta H = \Delta H_{f,D}^\circ$

**Hess's Cycle Construction:**

We want $aA + bB \rightarrow cC + dD$. We construct an alternative path: first, decompose reactants into elements; then reform elements into products.

$$\text{Path 1 (Direct):} \quad aA + bB \longrightarrow cC + dD \qquad \Delta H = \Delta H_{rxn}^\circ$$

$$\text{Path 2 (Hess Cycle):} \quad aA + bB \longrightarrow \text{Elements} \longrightarrow cC + dD$$

**Step-by-Step Hess Cycle Analysis:**

**Step 1 — Decompose reactants into their elements.** To go from $aA + bB$ *back* to elements, we must reverse the formation reactions. Reversing a reaction changes the sign of $\Delta H$:
$$aA \rightarrow a \cdot (\text{elements of } A) \qquad \Delta H_1 = -a \cdot \Delta H_{f,A}^\circ$$
$$bB \rightarrow b \cdot (\text{elements of } B) \qquad \Delta H_2 = -b \cdot \Delta H_{f,B}^\circ$$

**Step 2 — Reform elements into products.** Forward formation reactions:
$$c \cdot (\text{elements of } C) \rightarrow cC \qquad \Delta H_3 = c \cdot \Delta H_{f,C}^\circ$$
$$d \cdot (\text{elements of } D) \rightarrow dD \qquad \Delta H_4 = d \cdot \Delta H_{f,D}^\circ$$

**Step 3 — Apply Hess's Law (state function property).** Since enthalpy is a state function, total enthalpy change along Path 2 equals that along Path 1:
$$\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ = \Delta H_1 + \Delta H_2 + \Delta H_3 + \Delta H_4$$

$$\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ = -a \cdot \Delta H_{f,A}^\circ - b \cdot \Delta H_{f,B}^\circ + c \cdot \Delta H_{f,C}^\circ + d \cdot \Delta H_{f,D}^\circ$$

**Final General Form:**

$$\boxed{\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ = \sum_{i=\text{products}} \nu_i \Delta H_{f,i}^\circ - \sum_{j=\text{reactants}} \nu_j \Delta H_{f,j}^\circ}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Heat of Reaction from Standard Heats of Combustion

**Starting Point:** Same generalized reaction,
$$aA + bB \rightarrow cC + dD$$

The combustion reactions for each species (with oxygen as the implicit second reactant) are:
$$A + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Combustion products of } A \qquad \Delta H = \Delta H_{c,A}^\circ$$
$$B + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Combustion products of } B \qquad \Delta H = \Delta H_{c,B}^\circ$$
$$C + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Combustion products of } C \qquad \Delta H = \Delta H_{c,C}^\circ$$
$$D + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Combustion products of } D \qquad \Delta H = \Delta H_{c,D}^\circ$$

**Hess's Cycle Construction:**

All combustion reactions end at the *same* state: the **fully oxidized products** (denoted as $\Sigma \text{Ox}$: mixture of $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, etc.). This common final state is the key to the cycle.

$$\text{Path 1:} \quad aA + bB \longrightarrow cC + dD \longrightarrow \Sigma \text{Ox} \qquad \Delta H = \Delta H_{rxn}^\circ + c\Delta H_{c,C}^\circ + d\Delta H_{c,D}^\circ$$

$$\text{Path 2:} \quad aA + bB \longrightarrow \Sigma \text{Ox} \qquad \Delta H = a\Delta H_{c,A}^\circ + b\Delta H_{c,B}^\circ$$

**Equating the Two Paths** (state function property):
$$\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ + c\Delta H_{c,C}^\circ + d\Delta H_{c,D}^\circ = a\Delta H_{c,A}^\circ + b\Delta H_{c,B}^\circ$$

**Rearranging for $\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ$:**

$$\boxed{\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ = \sum_{j=\text{reactants}} \nu_j \Delta H_{c,j}^\circ - \sum_{i=\text{products}} \nu_i \Delta H_{c,i}^\circ}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The sign reversal relative to the formation method is *intentional*. Because both paths terminate at the same oxidized state, the products' combustion enthalpies appear as a *subtraction* — they must be "un-combusted" to bring them back to the unburned state before they can be formed from the reactants.

### 3.3 Derivation of Kirchhoff's Equation (Temperature Correction)

**Starting Point:** The total differential of enthalpy for a reacting system,
$$dH = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \sum_i \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial n_i}\right)_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}} dn_i$$

The partial derivative $\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P$ is by definition the **heat capacity at constant pressure**, $C_p$.

For the *reaction* mixture, define:
$$C_p = \sum_i n_i C_{p,i}$$

**Step 1 — Enthalpy of products at temperature $T$:**
$$H_{\text{prod}}(T) = H_{\text{prod}}(T_{\text{ref}}) + \int_{T_{\text{ref}}}^{T} C_{p,\text{prod}} \, dT$$

**Step 2 — Enthalpy of reactants at temperature $T$:**
$$H_{\text{react}}(T) = H_{\text{react}}(T_{\text{ref}}) + \int_{T_{\text{ref}}}^{T} C_{p,\text{react}} \, dT$$

**Step 3 — Enthalpy of reaction at $T$:**
$$\Delta H_{rxn}(T) = H_{\text{prod}}(T) - H_{\text{react}}(T)$$

Substituting Steps 1 and 2:
$$\Delta H_{rxn}(T) = \left[H_{\text{prod}}(T_{\text{ref}}) - H_{\text{react}}(T_{\text{ref}})\right] + \int_{T_{\text{ref}}}^{T} \left(C_{p,\text{prod}} - C_{p,\text{react}}\right) dT$$

**Step 4 — Identify the first term as $\Delta H_{rxn}(T_{\text{ref}})$:**
$$\Delta H_{rxn}(T) = \Delta H_{rxn}(T_{\text{ref}}) + \int_{T_{\text{ref}}}^{T} \Delta C_p \, dT$$

**Final General Form (Kirchhoff's Equation):**

$$\boxed{\Delta H_{rxn}(T_2) = \Delta H_{rxn}(T_1) + \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \Delta C_p \, dT}$$

where $\Delta C_p = \sum_i \nu_i C_{p,i}(\text{prod}) - \sum_j \nu_j C_{p,j}(\text{react})$.

**Integrated Forms for Common $C_p$ Correlations:**

If $C_p = a + bT + cT^2 + dT^3$, then:
$$\int_{T_1}^{T_2} C_p \, dT = a(T_2 - T_1) + \frac{b}{2}(T_2^2 - T_1^2) + \frac{c}{3}(T_2^3 - T_1^3) + \frac{d}{4}(T_2^4 - T_1^4)$$

If $C_p = a + bT + c/T^2$, then:
$$\int_{T_1}^{T_2} C_p \, dT = a(T_2 - T_1) + \frac{b}{2}(T_2^2 - T_1^2) - c\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption Underlying Kirchhoff's Equation:** No phase change occurs in any reactant or product between $T_1$ and $T_2$. If a phase change occurs, an additional latent heat term must be added at the transition temperature.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Computational Routes to $\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ$

The following block diagram shows the three principal computational routes for evaluating the standard heat of reaction. Each route requires a different set of tabulated data but converges to the same result by virtue of Hess's Law.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Target: Delta H reaction at standard state])
    startNode --> route1Node{Route 1: Given T equals 298 K}
    startNode --> route2Node{Route 2: Given Heat of Formation Data}
    startNode --> route3Node{Route 3: Given Heat of Combustion Data}
    startNode --> route4Node{Route 4: Reaction Temperature differs from 298 K}

    route1Node --> tab1Node[Tabulated value lookup at 298 K]
    tab1Node --> out1Node[Result: Delta H rxn 298]

    route2Node --> f1Node[Sum of nu times Delta Hf of products]
    f1Node --> f2Node[Sum of nu times Delta Hf of reactants]
    f2Node --> f3Node[Apply: Products minus Reactants]
    f3Node --> out2Node[Result: Delta H rxn 298]

    route3Node --> c1Node[Sum of nu times Delta Hc of reactants]
    c1Node --> c2Node[Sum of nu times Delta Hc of products]
    c2Node --> c3Node[Apply: Reactants minus Products]
    c3Node --> out3Node[Result: Delta H rxn 298]

    route4Node --> k1Node[Compute Delta H rxn 298 first]
    k1Node --> k2Node[Evaluate Delta Cp for the reaction]
    k2Node --> k3Node[Integrate Delta Cp from 298 to T]
    k3Node --> k4Node[Apply Kirchhoff Equation]
    k4Node --> out4Node[Result: Delta H rxn at T]

    out1Node --> finalEnd([Final Answer: Delta H rxn at desired state])
    out2Node --> finalEnd
    out3Node --> finalEnd
    out4Node --> finalEnd

    style startNode fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style finalEnd fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style route1Node fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    style route2Node fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    style route3Node fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    style route4Node fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Hess's Law Cycle — Formation Method (Visualized)

The following block diagram visualizes the thermodynamic cycle used to derive the heat-of-formation method. It shows how the same overall reaction can be performed by an *alternate path* (decompose to elements, then reform) and the total enthalpy change must be the same.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Reactants State: aA plus bB at 298 K]
    A2[Elements State: Constituent elements at 298 K]
    A3[Products State: cC plus dD at 298 K]

    A1 -- Direct Path Delta H rxn 298 --> A3
    A1 -- Step 1: Reverse Formation minus a times Delta Hf A minus b times Delta Hf B --> A2
    A2 -- Step 2: Forward Formation c times Delta Hf C plus d times Delta Hf D --> A3

    style A1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    style A2 fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#808000,color:#000
    style A3 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Process Flow — Application of Kirchhoff's Equation in Reactor Design

This diagram maps the engineering workflow from thermodynamic data to actual reactor heat duty.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inp1[Input: Reaction stoichiometry]
    inp2[Input: Standard Heat of Formation or Combustion table]
    inp3[Input: Cp correlation for each species]
    inp4[Input: Operating temperature of reactor T op]

    inp1 --> calc1[Step 1: Calculate Delta H rxn at 298 K]
    inp2 --> calc1
    calc1 --> calc2[Step 2: Calculate Delta Cp for the reaction]
    inp3 --> calc2
    calc2 --> calc3[Step 3: Integrate Delta Cp from 298 K to T op]
    inp4 --> calc3
    calc3 --> calc4[Step 4: Apply Kirchhoff Equation to obtain Delta H rxn at T op]
    calc4 --> des1[Step 5: Apply Energy Balance to Reactor: Q equals Delta H plus minus W]
    des1 --> des2[Output: Heat Exchanger Duty, Coolant Flowrate, Utility Sizing]

    style inp1 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
    style inp2 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
    style inp3 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
    style inp4 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
    style des2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Method

| **Method** | **Data Requirement** | **Best Used When** | **Common Pitfall** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Heat of Formation | $\Delta H_f^\circ$ of all species | Inorganic reactions, simple organics | Forgetting to look up $\Delta H_f^\circ = 0$ for elements |
| Heat of Combustion | $\Delta H_c^\circ$ of organic species | Organic molecules, fuel chemistry | Sign reversal confusion; miscounting $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ state (l vs g) |
| Kirchhoff's Equation | $C_p$ of all species + $\Delta H_{rxn}$ at $T_1$ | Reactions away from 298 K (most industrial cases) | Phase change in $T_1 \rightarrow T_2$ range not accounted for |
