## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 1]**
Define the term **standard heat of reaction**. State clearly the reference conditions used for its evaluation and the sign convention adopted for exothermic and endothermic reactions.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **standard heat of reaction** ($\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ$) is defined as the enthalpy change accompanying a chemical reaction when **all reactants and products are in their standard states** (most stable form at the specified conditions) and the reaction proceeds to completion such that one mole of a designated species is consumed or produced.

**Reference conditions (Standard State):**
- Temperature: $T = 298.15 \, \text{K}$ ($25^\circ \text{C}$)
- Pressure: $P = 1 \, \text{atm}$ ($101.325 \, \text{kPa}$)
- For solutions: Concentration $C = 1 \, \text{mol/L}$

**Sign Convention:**
- $\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ < 0$ → **Exothermic** (heat is evolved to the surroundings)
- $\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ > 0$ → **Endothermic** (heat is absorbed from the surroundings)

*(Award 1 mark each for the definition, standard state, and sign convention.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Distinguish between **standard heat of formation** and **standard heat of combustion**. State one engineering application of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Standard Heat of Formation ($\Delta H_f^\circ$)** | **Standard Heat of Combustion ($\Delta H_c^\circ$)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | Enthalpy change when 1 mole of a compound is formed from its **constituent elements** in standard states | Enthalpy change when 1 mole of a substance undergoes **complete combustion** with $\text{O}_2$ in standard states |
| Reference | Element reference state (e.g., $\text{O}_2(g)$, $\text{C(s, graphite)}$) | Combustion products ($\text{CO}_2(g)$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$) |
| Engineering Use | Reactor heat balance, synthesis process design | **Calorific value** of fuels, internal combustion engine design |

*(Award 1.5 marks each for distinction + 1 mark for engineering application = 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Combustion Method:** Students frequently write the formula as *products minus reactants* (mimicking the formation method) instead of *reactants minus products*. This is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Forgetting $\Delta C_p$ Sign:** Kirchhoff's equation requires the *net* $\Delta C_p$ (products minus reactants). Conflating this gives a wrong final sign.
> 3. **Units Mismatch:** Heat of formation tables give values in $\text{kJ/mol}$; heat capacities in $C_p$ tables are in $\text{J/(mol·K)}$. Always convert to a common unit (preferably $\text{kJ}$) before integration.
> 4. **Neglecting $298.15$ vs $298$:** Using $T = 298$ instead of $298.15$ is accepted in KTU valuation, but rounding errors compound in Kirchhoff integration. Use $298$ for simplification.
> 5. **Step Skipping:** KTU examiners require *each* intermediate step in energy balance. A direct jump to the final answer forfeits 2–3 marks.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Formation + Kirchhoff

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024, KTU Model Paper 2, Module 2]**

For the **water-gas shift reaction** at $1 \, \text{atm}$:
$$\text{CO}(g) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \rightarrow \text{CO}_2(g) + \text{H}_2(g)$$

**(a)** Calculate the standard heat of reaction at $25^\circ \text{C}$ using standard heats of formation. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Using Kirchhoff's equation, determine the standard heat of reaction at $T = 500 \, \text{K}$, assuming the following mean heat capacities (assumed constant over the temperature range):
- $C_p(\text{CO}, g) = 29.3 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- $C_p(\text{H}_2\text{O}, l) = 75.4 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- $C_p(\text{CO}_2, g) = 37.2 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- $C_p(\text{H}_2, g) = 28.9 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}$

(7 Marks)

**Data given:**
- $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{CO}, g) = -110.5 \, \text{kJ/mol}$
- $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O}, l) = -285.8 \, \text{kJ/mol}$
- $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{CO}_2, g) = -393.5 \, \text{kJ/mol}$
- $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{H}_2, g) = 0 \, \text{kJ/mol}$ (element in standard state)

---

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given:** $\text{CO}(g) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \rightarrow \text{CO}_2(g) + \text{H}_2(g)$

**Step 1 — Apply the heat of formation formula:**
$$\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ(298) = \sum \nu_i \Delta H_{f,i}^\circ(\text{prod}) - \sum \nu_j \Delta H_{f,j}^\circ(\text{react})$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values for products:**
$$\text{Products: } 1 \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{CO}_2, g) + 1 \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{H}_2, g)$$
$$= (1)(-393.5) + (1)(0) = -393.5 \, \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values for reactants:**
$$\text{Reactants: } 1 \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{CO}, g) + 1 \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O}, l)$$
$$= (1)(-110.5) + (1)(-285.8) = -396.3 \, \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 4 — Compute final result:**
$$\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ(298) = (-393.5) - (-396.3) = -393.5 + 396.3 = +2.8 \, \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Conclusion:** The reaction is **mildly endothermic** at standard conditions with $\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ(298) = +2.8 \, \text{kJ/mol}$. *(Award 7 marks: 2 marks for formula, 3 for substitution, 1 for unit, 1 for conclusion.)*

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute $\Delta C_p$ for the reaction:**

$$\Delta C_p = \sum \nu_i C_{p,i}(\text{prod}) - \sum \nu_j C_{p,j}(\text{react})$$

**Step 2 — Substitute product values:**
$$C_p(\text{products}) = (1)(37.2) + (1)(28.9) = 66.1 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute reactant values:**
$$C_p(\text{reactants}) = (1)(29.3) + (1)(75.4) = 104.7 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute net $\Delta C_p$:**
$$\Delta C_p = 66.1 - 104.7 = -38.6 \, \text{J/(mol·K)} = -0.0386 \, \text{kJ/(mol·K)}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Kirchhoff's Equation:**
$$\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ(500) = \Delta H_{rxn}^\circ(298) + \int_{298}^{500} \Delta C_p \, dT$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the integral** (since $\Delta C_p$ is constant):
$$\int_{298}^{500} \Delta C_p \, dT = \Delta C_p \cdot (500 - 298) = (-0.0386)(202) = -7.7972 \, \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the final result:**
$$\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ(500) = 2.8 + (-7.7972) = -4.9972 \, \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 8 — Final answer and interpretation:**
$$\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ(500) \approx -5.0 \, \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Conclusion:** At $500 \, \text{K}$, the reaction has become **exothermic** despite being endothermic at $298 \, \text{K}$. This is a classic case where temperature dependence reverses the sign of the reaction enthalpy. *(Award 7 marks: 2 for $\Delta C_p$ calculation, 2 for Kirchhoff formula and integration, 2 for substitution, 1 for conclusion.)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Combustion Method Alternative

**[KTU University Exam — KTU 2024 Scheme Model Paper, Module 2 — Alternative Choice]**

For the **esterification reaction** of acetic acid with ethanol to form ethyl acetate:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}(l) + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}(l) \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5(l) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$$

**(a)** Calculate the standard heat of reaction at $25^\circ \text{C}$ using the standard heats of combustion. (7 Marks)

**(b)** If the same reaction is now carried out at $T = 350 \, \text{K}$, determine the new standard heat of reaction using Kirchhoff's equation, given the following constant $C_p$ data:
- $C_p(\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}, l) = 124.0 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- $C_p(\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}, l) = 111.5 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- $C_p(\text{CH}_3\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5, l) = 169.0 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- $C_p(\text{H}_2\text{O}, l) = 75.3 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}$

(7 Marks)

**Data given (standard heats of combustion at $25^\circ \text{C}$):**
- $\Delta H_c^\circ(\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}, l) = -871.5 \, \text{kJ/mol}$
- $\Delta H_c^\circ(\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}, l) = -1366.8 \, \text{kJ/mol}$
- $\Delta H_c^\circ(\text{CH}_3\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5, l) = -2238.1 \, \text{kJ/mol}$
- $\Delta H_c^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O}, l) = 0 \, \text{kJ/mol}$ (already fully oxidized)

---

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Apply the heat of combustion formula** (note the *sign reversal* relative to the formation method):
$$\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ(298) = \sum \nu_j \Delta H_{c,j}^\circ(\text{react}) - \sum \nu_i \Delta H_{c,i}^\circ(\text{prod})$$

**Step 2 — Substitute reactant values:**
$$\text{Reactants: } (1)(-871.5) + (1)(-1366.8) = -2238.3 \, \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute product values:**
$$\text{Products: } (1)(-2238.1) + (1)(0) = -2238.1 \, \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the result:**
$$\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ(298) = (-2238.3) - (-2238.1) = -2238.3 + 2238.1 = -0.2 \, \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation:**
$$\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ(298) = -0.2 \, \text{kJ/mol}$$

The reaction is **very slightly exothermic** at standard conditions. This matches the well-known experimental observation that esterification reactions are nearly thermoneutral, with a small exothermic driving force (often reported between $0$ and $-5 \, \text{kJ/mol}$ in literature). *(Award 7 marks: 2 for correct sign in formula, 2 for substitution, 1 for unit, 2 for engineering interpretation.)*

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute $\Delta C_p$ for the reaction:**
$$\Delta C_p = \sum \nu_i C_{p,i}(\text{prod}) - \sum \nu_j C_{p,j}(\text{react})$$

**Step 2 — Products sum:**
$$C_p(\text{products}) = (1)(169.0) + (1)(75.3) = 244.3 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}$$

**Step 3 — Reactants sum:**
$$C_p(\text{reactants}) = (1)(124.0) + (1)(111.5) = 235.5 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}$$

**Step 4 — Net $\Delta C_p$:**
$$\Delta C_p = 244.3 - 235.5 = 8.8 \, \text{J/(mol·K)} = 0.0088 \, \text{kJ/(mol·K)}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Kirchhoff's Equation:**
$$\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ(350) = \Delta H_{rxn}^\circ(298) + \Delta C_p \cdot (350 - 298)$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the temperature correction term:**
$$\Delta C_p \cdot (350 - 298) = 0.0088 \times 52 = 0.4576 \, \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the final result:**
$$\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ(350) = -0.2 + 0.4576 = +0.2576 \, \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 8 — Conclusion:**
$$\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ(350) \approx +0.26 \, \text{kJ/mol}$$

The reaction becomes **mildly endothermic** at $350 \, \text{K}$. This explains why industrial esterification reactors are often operated at moderate temperatures with continuous water removal (Le Chatelier's principle) to drive equilibrium toward the ester. *(Award 7 marks: 2 for $\Delta C_p$, 2 for Kirchhoff formula, 2 for arithmetic, 1 for interpretation.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exams:**

- **Standard State:** $T = 298.15 \, \text{K}$, $P = 1 \, \text{atm}$ (or $101.325 \, \text{kPa}$).
- **Sign Convention:** Exothermic $\Rightarrow \Delta H < 0$; Endothermic $\Rightarrow \Delta H > 0$.
- **Formation Method Formula:** $\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ = \sum \nu_i \Delta H_{f,i}^\circ(\text{prod}) - \sum \nu_j \Delta H_{f,j}^\circ(\text{react})$.
- **Combustion Method Formula:** $\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ = \sum \nu_j \Delta H_{c,j}^\circ(\text{react}) - \sum \nu_i \Delta H_{c,i}^\circ(\text{prod})$.
- **Kirchhoff's Equation:** $\Delta H_{rxn}(T_2) = \Delta H_{rxn}(T_1) + \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \Delta C_p \, dT$.
- **$\Delta C_p$ Definition:** Always computed as *products minus reactants* (in the same stoichiometric sense as $\Delta H$).
- **Element in standard state:** $\Delta H_f^\circ = 0$ (e.g., $\text{O}_2(g)$, $\text{N}_2(g)$, $\text{C(s, graphite)}$, $\text{H}_2(g)$).
- **Combustion Products:** $\text{CO}_2(g)$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$ for organic compounds; $\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(g)$ changes $\Delta H_c^\circ$ by $+44 \, \text{kJ/mol}$ (latent heat).
- **Unit Consistency:** Convert all heats to the *same* unit ($\text{kJ}$ recommended) before any arithmetic; convert $C_p$ from $\text{J}$ to $\text{kJ}$ when integrating.
- **Phase Change Alert:** Kirchhoff's equation *fails* across phase transitions. Add $\pm \Delta H_{\text{latent}}$ at the transition temperature.
- **State Function Property:** Both Hess's Law and Kirchhoff's Law are direct consequences of enthalpy being a *state function*.
- **Engineering Use in KTU Context:** Be ready to compute heat duty ($Q = \Delta H_{rxn}$ at operating $T$) for jacketed CSTR design, heat exchanger sizing, and energy integration networks.
- **Frequent Mistake:** Using *formation method* sign convention for *combustion data* (and vice versa) — read the question carefully and choose the correct formula sign.
- **Approximation Note:** If $C_p$ is given as a *constant* (mean heat capacity), $\int \Delta C_p \, dT = \Delta C_p \cdot \Delta T$. If given as a polynomial, integrate term-by-term.
- **KTU Marks Distribution Typical:** Part (a) "Calculate $\Delta H^\circ_{298}$" carries 7 marks → formula (2) + substitution (3) + final answer with units and sign (2). Part (b) "Apply Kirchhoff" carries 7 marks → $\Delta C_p$ (2) + integral setup (2) + final answer (2) + engineering interpretation (1).
- **Order of Operations:** Always solve in sequence: (1) get $\Delta H$ at 298 K → (2) compute $\Delta C_p$ → (3) apply Kirchhoff → (4) energy balance.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chemical Thermodynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Chemical Thermodynamics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chemical Thermodynamics** is the branch of physical chemistry and chemical engineering that deals with the interrelation of **heat, work, temperature, and energy** in chemical and physical processes. It provides the mathematical framework to predict whether a given reaction or process can occur spontaneously, the extent to which it will proceed, and the energy changes (heat absorbed or released) associated with it.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBSFT304)**, Chemical Thermodynamics is the study of the **First Law (Conservation of Energy)**, **Second Law (Entropy and Directionality)**, and the auxiliary thermodynamic potentials such as **Enthalpy (H)**, **Internal Energy (U)**, **Entropy (S)**, and **Gibbs Free Energy (G)** — applied to reacting and non-reacting chemical systems.

### 1.2 Fundamental Terminology

Before proceeding, the following foundational terms (as defined in the KTU syllabus) must be understood:

- **System**: The specific portion of the universe chosen for thermodynamic analysis (e.g., a reactor, a distillation column).
- **Surroundings**: Everything external to the system that can exchange energy or matter.
- **State Function**: A property whose value depends only on the current state of the system, not the path taken (e.g., T, P, U, H, S, G).
- **Path Function**: A property whose value depends on the path followed between two states (e.g., work, heat).
- **Extensive Property**: Depends on the size/mass of the system (e.g., U, H, V, S, G).
- **Intensive Property**: Independent of system size (e.g., T, P, density).

> [!NOTE]
> The **Sign Convention** in KTU Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics is:
> - **Heat absorbed by system (Q) > 0** (Endothermic)
> - **Work done BY the system (W) > 0** (Expansion work)
> This is the **IUPAC / Engineering convention**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Account" of Energy

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition**: Imagine your bank account as the **Internal Energy (U)** of a thermodynamic system.
> - **Deposits (Heat absorbed, Q>0)**: Increase your balance.
> - **Withdrawals (Work done, W>0)**: Decrease your balance.
> - The balance (U) is a **state function** — the bank doesn't care HOW you deposited money (cash, cheque, UPI); it only tracks the current total.
> - However, the **transaction process** (deposit or withdrawal) is a **path function** — the *effort* (heat/work) depends on the route.
> - **Spontaneity** is like gravity: money naturally flows downhill (towards lower energy states), unless forced uphill (non-spontaneous process).

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Universal Gas Constant | **R** | **8.314** | J/(mol·K) |
| Standard Temperature | **T°** | **298.15 K** (or 25°C) | K |
| Standard Pressure | **P°** | **1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar** | Pa |
| Conversion | 1 cal | **4.184 J** | J |
| Conversion | 1 atm·L | **101.325 J** | J |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 scheme numerical problems, always use **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** unless otherwise specified. For older problems using calories, use **R = 1.987 cal/(mol·K)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The First Law of Thermodynamics

**Statement**: Energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be transformed from one form to another. The total energy of an isolated system is constant.

**Mathematical Form** (differential):

$$dU = \delta Q - \delta W$$

For a closed system with only **PV (expansion/compression) work**:

$$dU = \delta Q - P\,dV$$

**Why this matters in Chemical Engineering**: The First Law allows us to perform **energy balances** on reactors, heat exchangers, distillation columns, and compressors — answering: *"How much cooling/heating is required for this process?"*

### 2.2 Enthalpy (H) — The Process Engineer's Favorite Function

**Why is H defined?** In chemical processes, most reactions occur at **constant pressure** (e.g., in open vessels or atmospheric reactors). Under such conditions, measuring ΔU is inconvenient. Enthalpy simplifies the bookkeeping.

**Definition**:

$$H = U + PV$$

**Differential form**:

$$dH = dU + P\,dV + V\,dP$$

For a constant-pressure process (dP = 0), and substituting dU:

$$dH = \delta Q_{P}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a critical KTU result:** At **constant pressure**, the heat transferred equals the **change in enthalpy**. This is why chemical engineers tabulate enthalpies of reaction, formation, and combustion rather than internal energy changes.

### 2.3 Heat Capacity

**Definition**: The amount of heat required to raise the temperature of a unit quantity of substance by one degree (K or °C).

**Two principal forms**:
- **Cᵥ** (at constant volume): $C_V = \left(\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V$
- **Cₚ** (at constant pressure): $C_P = \left(\dfrac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P$

**Units**:
- For mass: **J/(kg·K)**
- For moles: **J/(mol·K)**

**Temperature Dependence of Cₚ** (Empirical form, frequently used in KTU problems):

$$C_P = a + bT + cT^2 + \dfrac{d}{T^2}$$

Where a, b, c, d are empirical coefficients (tabulated for each species in standard data tables).

> [!TIP]
> For **ideal gases**, the relationship between Cp and Cv is:
> $$C_P - C_V = R \quad \text{(per mole basis)}$$
> This is one of the **most-tested KTU derivations** (see Section 3).

### 2.4 Thermochemistry — Heats of Reaction

**Heat of Reaction (ΔHᵣₓₙ)**: The enthalpy change when a reaction proceeds to completion at constant T and P, with reactants and products in their standard states.

**Key types**:
- **Heat of Formation (ΔHᶠ)**: Heat change when **1 mole** of a compound is formed from its elements in their standard states.
- **Heat of Combustion (ΔH꜀)**: Heat released when **1 mole** of a substance burns completely in O₂.
- **Heat of Neutralization**: For strong acid + strong base, ≈ **−57.1 kJ/mol** (a frequently tested KTU value).
- **Heat of Solution**: Heat absorbed/released during dissolution.

### 2.5 Hess's Law of Constant Heat Summation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statement**: The total enthalpy change in a chemical reaction is the **same**, regardless of the path by which the reaction occurs, provided initial and final conditions (T, P) are the same.
>
> This is a direct consequence of enthalpy being a **state function**.

**Application**: Calculate ΔH for reactions that cannot be measured directly, by combining known reactions (e.g., formation, combustion).

### 2.6 The Second Law of Thermodynamics

**Statement (Clausius)**: Heat cannot spontaneously flow from a colder body to a hotter body.

**Statement (Kelvin-Planck)**: It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces no effect other than absorbing heat from a single reservoir and converting it wholly to work.

**Mathematical form** — **Entropy (S)**:

$$dS \geq \dfrac{\delta Q}{T}$$

Equality for a **reversible** process; inequality for an **irreversible** (real) process.

**For an isolated system** (δQ = 0):

$$dS_{isolated} \geq 0 \quad \text{(spontaneous if > 0)}$$

### 2.7 Gibbs Free Energy (G) — The Master Spontaneity Criterion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For constant T, P processes (the bread and butter of chemical engineering)**, the spontaneity criterion is the **Gibbs Free Energy**, not entropy.

**Definition**:

$$G = H - TS$$

Differential form:

$$dG = dH - T\,dS - S\,dT$$

For a constant-T, constant-P process in a closed system:

$$\Delta G \leq 0$$

- ΔG < 0: **Spontaneous** (process proceeds forward)
- ΔG = 0: **Equilibrium**
- ΔG > 0: **Non-spontaneous** (reverse process is spontaneous)

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Condition | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $dU = \delta Q - \delta W$ | First Law (general) | J |
| 2 | $dU = \delta Q - P\,dV$ | First Law (only PV work, closed) | J |
| 3 | $H = U + PV$ | Enthalpy definition | J |
| 4 | $dH = \delta Q_P$ | Constant P closed system | J |
| 5 | $C_V = \left(\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V$ | Heat capacity at constant V | J/(mol·K) |
| 6 | $C_P = \left(\dfrac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P$ | Heat capacity at constant P | J/(mol·K) |
| 7 | $C_P - C_V = R$ | Ideal gas (molar basis) | J/(mol·K) |
| 8 | $\gamma = C_P / C_V$ | Heat capacity ratio | dimensionless |
| 9 | $C_P = a + bT + cT^2 + d/T^2$ | Empirical Cp equation | J/(mol·K) |
| 10 | $dS = \dfrac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$ | Entropy definition (reversible) | J/K |
| 11 | $dS \geq \dfrac{\delta Q}{T}$ | Second Law (general) | J/K |
| 12 | $G = H - TS$ | Gibbs Free Energy definition | J |
| 13 | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ | At constant T | J |
| 14 | $\Delta G° = -RT \ln K$ | Equilibrium constant relation | J/mol |
| 15 | $\Delta G = \Delta G° + RT \ln Q$ | Reaction Gibbs energy | J/mol |
| 16 | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_C/T_H$ | Carnot efficiency | dimensionless |
| 17 | $\Delta H = \sum \nu_i \Delta H_f°(\text{products}) - \sum \nu_j \Delta H_f°(\text{reactants})$ | Heat of reaction from formation | J |
| 18 | $\Delta H = \sum \nu_i \Delta H_c°(\text{reactants}) - \sum \nu_j \Delta H_c°(\text{products})$ | Heat of reaction from combustion | J |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Use**: All of these formulas underpin **reactor design** (heat removal duties), **distillation column** design (reboiler/condenser duty), **compressor/expander** work calculation, and **chemical equilibrium** analysis (yield optimization).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: The Relationship $C_P - C_V = R$ for an Ideal Gas

This is one of the **most frequently asked KTU derivations** (12 marks in some years).

**Starting Point**: Definition of Enthalpy.

$$H = U + PV$$

Differentiate both sides:

$$dH = dU + P\,dV + V\,dP$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the First Law** ($dU = \delta Q - P\,dV$):

$$dH = (\delta Q - P\,dV) + P\,dV + V\,dP$$

The two $P\,dV$ terms cancel:

$$dH = \delta Q + V\,dP$$

**Step 2 — Apply the constraint of constant pressure** (dP = 0):

$$(dH)_P = \delta Q_P$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the definition of $C_P$** at constant P:

$$\delta Q_P = nC_P\,dT$$

Therefore:

$$(dH)_P = nC_P\,dT$$

**Step 4 — Express enthalpy in terms of internal energy at constant V** (for an ideal gas, H depends only on T):

For an ideal gas: $PV = nRT$, so $H = U + nRT$, hence $dH = dU + nR\,dT$.

Also, at constant V, the First Law gives $dU = nC_V\,dT$.

Therefore:

$$dH = nC_V\,dT + nR\,dT = n(C_V + R)\,dT$$

**Step 5 — Equate the two expressions for dH at constant P**:

$$nC_P\,dT = n(C_V + R)\,dT$$

Divide by $n\,dT$ (both non-zero):

$$\boxed{C_P - C_V = R}$$

**For n moles of ideal gas**, dividing by n gives the **molar** form. For a single mole: $C_P - C_V = R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). For a mass basis with specific heat capacities $c_p$ and $c_v$, the relationship is: $c_p - c_v = R/M$, where M is molar mass.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key assumption**: This derivation holds strictly for **ideal gases**. For real gases, the residual enthalpy departure functions are used (Kammer-Mollier diagrams, SRK/PR EOS), which are advanced topics outside Module 3.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II: The Combined First and Second Law — Foundation of the Gibbs Equation

**Starting Point**: First Law (closed system, PV work only):

$$dU = \delta Q - P\,dV$$

Second Law (reversible):

$$dS = \dfrac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta Q_{rev} = T\,dS$$

**Step 1 — Substitute** $\delta Q_{rev}$ into the First Law:

$$dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$$

This is the **Fundamental Thermodynamic Equation** for a closed system in differential form.

**Step 2 — Differentiate the Enthalpy definition** $H = U + PV$:

$$dH = dU + P\,dV + V\,dP$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the expression for dU** from Step 1:

$$dH = (T\,dS - P\,dV) + P\,dV + V\,dP$$

The two $P\,dV$ terms cancel:

$$\boxed{dH = T\,dS + V\,dP}$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate the Gibbs Free Energy** $G = H - TS$:

$$dG = dH - T\,dS - S\,dT$$

**Step 5 — Substitute dH** from Step 3:

$$dG = (T\,dS + V\,dP) - T\,dS - S\,dT$$

The $T\,dS$ terms cancel:

$$\boxed{dG = V\,dP - S\,dT}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Gibbs Equation**. It tells us how G changes with pressure and temperature — the two variables most easily controlled in industrial processes. The partial derivatives are:
> $$\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial P}\right)_T = V \quad ; \quad \left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P = -S$$

For an **ideal gas**, $V = nRT/P$ at constant T, integrate to get:

$$G(T, P) = G°(T) + nRT \ln\left(\dfrac{P}{P°}\right)$$

This directly leads to the **famous equation** $\Delta G° = -RT \ln K$, the cornerstone of chemical equilibrium.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III: Carnot Cycle Efficiency

Although often covered in Mechanical Engineering thermodynamics, it appears in the KTU **Chemical Process Principles** module on Chemical Thermodynamics as a foundation for understanding the Second Law and entropy.

The Carnot cycle consists of four **reversible** processes between two temperatures $T_H$ (hot reservoir) and $T_C$ (cold reservoir):

1. **Isothermal expansion** at $T_H$: absorbs heat $Q_H$
2. **Adiabatic (isentropic) expansion**: temperature drops from $T_H$ to $T_C$
3. **Isothermal compression** at $T_C$: rejects heat $Q_C$
4. **Adiabatic (isentropic) compression**: temperature rises from $T_C$ to $T_H$

**Energy balance on the cycle** (First Law, complete cycle, ΔU = 0):

$$Q_H - Q_C = W_{net}$$

**Entropy balance on the cycle** (reversible, ΔS_total = 0):

For Step 1 (isothermal at $T_H$): $\Delta S_1 = \dfrac{Q_H}{T_H}$

For Step 3 (isothermal at $T_C$): $\Delta S_3 = -\dfrac{Q_C}{T_C}$

For Steps 2 and 4 (adiabatic, reversible = isentropic): $\Delta S_2 = \Delta S_4 = 0$

Total entropy change over the cycle = 0:

$$\dfrac{Q_H}{T_H} - \dfrac{Q_C}{T_C} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{Q_H}{T_H} = \dfrac{Q_C}{T_C}$$

**Rearrange**:

$$Q_C = Q_H \cdot \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}$$

**Efficiency definition**:

$$\eta = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = \dfrac{Q_H - Q_C}{Q_H} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_C}{Q_H}$$

**Substitute** $Q_C/Q_H = T_C/T_H$:

$$\boxed{\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance**: This is the **maximum possible efficiency** for any heat engine operating between two temperatures. It is unattainable in practice (reversible processes are idealizations), but sets the benchmark for power plants, refrigeration cycles, and heat pumps.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style

**Problem** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 model]`:
> Calculate the standard enthalpy of formation of **naphthalene (C₁₀H₈)** at 298 K, given the standard heats of combustion:
> - ΔH꜀°(C, graphite) = **−393.51 kJ/mol**
> - ΔH꜀°(H₂, g) = **−285.83 kJ/mol**
> - ΔH꜀°(C₁₀H₈, s) = **−5156.8 kJ/mol**
>
> The formation reaction is: $10C(\text{graphite}) + 4H_2(g) \rightarrow C_{10}H_8(s)$

**Solution using Hess's Law**:

The required formation enthalpy:

$$\Delta H_f°(C_{10}H_8) = \sum \nu_i \Delta H_c°(\text{reactants}) - \sum \nu_j \Delta H_c°(\text{products})$$

This is because combusting the elements (to get ΔH꜀° of reactants) and subtracting the combustion of the product (to "unburn" the product) is equivalent to the formation reaction.

**Step 1 — Heat released by combustion of reactants**:

- $10 \text{ mol C}$: $10 \times (-393.51) = -3935.1$ kJ
- $4 \text{ mol H}_2$: $4 \times (-285.83) = -1143.32$ kJ
- **Total (reactants)**: $-3935.1 + (-1143.32) = -5078.42$ kJ

**Step 2 — Heat released by combustion of product**:

- $1 \text{ mol C}_{10}H_8$: $1 \times (-5156.8) = -5156.8$ kJ

**Step 3 — Apply the formula**:

$$\Delta H_f° = (-5078.42) - (-5156.8) = -5078.42 + 5156.8 = +78.38 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Result**:

$$\boxed{\Delta H_f°(C_{10}H_8, s, 298\,K) = +78.38 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

The positive sign indicates that formation of naphthalene from its elements is **endothermic** — consistent with the fact that graphite and H₂ are more stable than naphthalene in their standard states.

> [!TIP]
> **Common KTU pitfall**: Students often get the sign wrong on the formula. **Memorize**: *For formation from combustion data, subtract the product combustion from the reactant combustion.* The mnemonic: **"Reactants minus Products"** for combustion-based calculations; the reverse for formation-based calculations.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Four Laws and Four Potentials

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **logical relationships** between the Laws of Thermodynamics and the derived thermodynamic potentials. This is the conceptual map you should be able to reproduce in the KTU exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1["FIRST LAW\nEnergy Conservation\ndU = dQ minus dW"] --> U["Internal Energy U\nState Function"]
    L1 --> H["Enthalpy H = U + PV\nHeat at Constant P"]
    L2["SECOND LAW\nEntropy and Direction\ndS greater than or equal dQ by T"] --> S["Entropy S\nMeasure of Disorder"]
    L2 --> G["Gibbs Free Energy G = H minus TS\nSpontaneity at Constant T and P"]
    L1 --> F["Helmholtz Free Energy A = U minus TS\nSpontaneity at Constant T and V"]
    U --> H
    H --> G
    S --> G
    U --> F
    S --> F
    H -.->|"dH = TdS + VdP"| G
    L1 --> CARNOT["Carnot Cycle\nEta = 1 minus Tc by Th"]
    L2 --> CARNOT
    CARNOT --> REAL["Real Heat Engines\nCompressors, Turbines, Reactors"]
    G --> EQ["Chemical Equilibrium\nDelta G equals 0\nDelta G not equals RT ln K"]
    H --> RXN["Thermochemistry\nDelta H of Reaction\nHess Law"]
```

**How to read this diagram**:
- The **First Law** establishes the conservation principle and leads to the state functions **U** and **H**.
- The **Second Law** introduces directionality via **S**, **G**, and **A**.
- The **Gibbs Equation** ($dG = VdP - SdT$) is the **bridge** between the two laws — the central relationship for chemical engineers.
- The **Carnot Cycle** is a direct physical application that ties both laws to engine design.
- **Thermochemistry** (Hess's Law) and **Chemical Equilibrium** (ΔG° = −RT ln K) are the two principal engineering outputs of this entire module.

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Thermodynamic Property Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["PRIMARY VARIABLES (Measured Directly)"]
        T1["Temperature T"]
        P1["Pressure P"]
        V1["Volume V"]
        N1["Moles n"]
    end
    subgraph B["DERIVED STATE FUNCTIONS (Calculated)"]
        U1["U: Internal Energy"]
        H1["H: Enthalpy"]
        S1["S: Entropy"]
        G1["G: Gibbs Energy"]
    end
    subgraph C["RESPONSE FUNCTIONS (Derivatives)"]
        CV1["Cv: Heat Capacity at V"]
        CP1["Cp: Heat Capacity at P"]
        ALPHA1["Alpha: Thermal Expansion"]
        KT1["Kappa: Isothermal Compressibility"]
    end
    subgraph D["ENGINEERING APPLICATIONS"]
        RXN1["Reactor Heat Duty Q"]
        EQ1["Equilibrium Constant K"]
        EFF1["Carnot Efficiency"]
    end
    T1 --> U1
    T1 --> H1
    T1 --> S1
    P1 --> G1
    V1 --> U1
    N1 --> H1
    U1 --> CV1
    H1 --> CP1
    S1 --> CP1
    G1 --> CP1
    P1 --> ALPHA1
    P1 --> KT1
    CV1 --> RXN1
    CP1 --> RXN1
    CP1 --> EQ1
    CP1 --> EFF1
    S1 --> EQ1
    G1 --> EQ1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram**: Temperature and pressure are the **independent** variables we control; everything else cascades from them. The response functions (Cv, Cp, α, κ) are the **bridges** that connect measured variables to engineering applications.

### 4.3 Thermodynamic Process Classification Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PROC["THERMODYNAMIC PROCESS"]
    PROC --> ISO_T["Isothermal\nT constant\ndT = 0\ndU = 0 for ideal gas"]
    PROC --> ISO_P["Isobaric\nP constant\ndP = 0\ndH = dQ"]
    PROC --> ISO_V["Isochoric / Isometric\nV constant\ndV = 0\ndW = 0"]
    PROC --> ADIA["Adiabatic\nQ = 0\ndS = 0 for reversible\nTV raised to gamma minus 1 = constant"]
    PROC --> REV["Reversible\ndS total = 0\nQuasi-static"]
    PROC --> IRREV["Irreversible\ndS total greater than 0\nReal processes"]
    PROC --> CYCL["Cyclic\nInitial state = Final state\ndU = 0, dH = 0, dG = 0"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Adiabatic reversible** (isentropic) relation $TV^{\gamma-1} = \text{constant}$ is the key relation used in **compressor and turbine** work calculations — frequently tested in KTU Part B questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**State the First Law of Thermodynamics. Write its mathematical form for a closed system undergoing a reversible process.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

**Statement (1 mark)**: The First Law of Thermodynamics states that *energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be transformed from one form to another. The total energy of an isolated system is constant.*

**Mathematical form (2 marks)**: For a closed system undergoing a reversible process, with only PV work:

$$dU = \delta Q_{rev} - P\,dV$$

Where:
- $dU$ = differential change in internal energy (J)
- $\delta Q_{rev}$ = reversible heat transferred to the system (J)
- $P\,dV$ = reversible expansion work done by the system (J)

For a finite change:

$$\Delta U = Q_{rev} - W_{rev} = Q_{rev} - \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P\,dV$$

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 model]`**
**Define the terms (a) Enthalpy, (b) Gibbs Free Energy. State the spontaneity criterion based on Gibbs Free Energy at constant T and P.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

**(a) Enthalpy (1 mark)**: Enthalpy is a thermodynamic state function defined as:

$$H = U + PV$$

Where U is internal energy, P is pressure, V is volume. For a constant-pressure process, the heat transferred equals the change in enthalpy: $Q_P = \Delta H$.

**(b) Gibbs Free Energy (1 mark)**: Gibbs Free Energy is a state function defined as:

$$G = H - TS$$

It represents the maximum non-expansion work obtainable from a closed system at constant T and P.

**Spontaneity criterion (1 mark)**: At constant T and P:
- $\Delta G < 0$: Process is **spontaneous**
- $\Delta G = 0$: System is at **equilibrium**
- $\Delta G > 0$: Process is **non-spontaneous** (reverse is spontaneous)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (similar)]`**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the relationship $C_P - C_V = R$ for an ideal gas. Clearly state the assumptions used.

**(b) [7 marks]** One mole of an ideal gas is compressed isothermally and reversibly at 300 K from 10 L to 2 L. Calculate the work done, heat transferred, and change in internal energy.

---

**Model Solution**:

**(a) Derivation of $C_P - C_V = R$ (7 marks)**:

> [!TIP]
> This is a **high-frequency derivation** in KTU Module 3. Memorize the steps and the conditions clearly.

**Step 1 (1 mark)**: Start with the definition of enthalpy:

$$H = U + PV$$

Differentiate:

$$dH = dU + P\,dV + V\,dP \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks)**: For a closed system, the First Law gives:

$$dU = \delta Q - P\,dV \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

Substitute (ii) into (i):

$$dH = \delta Q - P\,dV + P\,dV + V\,dP = \delta Q + V\,dP \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark)**: For a **constant pressure** process ($dP = 0$):

$$(dH)_P = \delta Q_P = nC_P\,dT \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark)**: For an **ideal gas**, $PV = nRT$, hence $H = U + nRT$, and:

$$dH = dU + nR\,dT$$

Since internal energy of an ideal gas depends only on temperature:

$$dU = nC_V\,dT \quad \text{...(v)}$$

Therefore:

$$dH = nC_V\,dT + nR\,dT = n(C_V + R)\,dT \quad \text{...(vi)}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark)**: Equate (iv) and (vi) at constant pressure:

$$nC_P\,dT = n(C_V + R)\,dT$$

Dividing by $n\,dT$:

$$\boxed{C_P - C_V = R}$$

**Assumptions (1 mark)**: 
1. The gas is **ideal** ($PV = nRT$, U depends only on T)
2. The system is **closed**
3. Only **PV work** is done
4. The process is **reversible** (for the equality form)

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**:

**Given**: n = 1 mol, T = 300 K (isothermal), $V_1 = 10$ L, $V_2 = 2$ L, ideal gas.

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Work done** (reversible isothermal):

$$W_{rev} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P\,dV = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \dfrac{nRT}{V}\,dV = nRT \ln\left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

Substituting values:

$$W_{rev} = (1)(8.314)(300) \ln\left(\dfrac{2}{10}\right)$$

$$W_{rev} = 2494.2 \times \ln(0.2) = 2494.2 \times (-1.6094) = -4014.7 \text{ J}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Change in internal energy**:

For an **ideal gas undergoing an isothermal process**, $dT = 0$, and since U depends only on T for an ideal gas:

$$\Delta U = nC_V \Delta T = 0 \text{ J}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Heat transferred**:

From First Law: $Q = \Delta U + W = 0 + (-4014.7) = -4014.7 \text{ J}$

The **negative sign** indicates heat is **released by the system** to the surroundings during compression (work is done on the gas; for isothermal, this energy must flow out as heat).

**Summary of Results**:
- $W = -4014.7$ J (work done on the system)
- $\Delta U = 0$ J
- $Q = -4014.7$ J (heat released)

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 (similar)]`**

**(a) [7 marks]** State and explain Hess's Law of Constant Heat Summation. The standard enthalpies of combustion of C(graphite), H₂(g), and CH₄(g) at 298 K are −393.5, −285.8, and −890.4 kJ/mol respectively. Calculate the standard enthalpy of formation of CH₄(g).

**(b) [7 marks]** Define entropy. Show that for a reversible isothermal expansion of an ideal gas from $V_1$ to $V_2$, the change in entropy is $\Delta S = nR \ln(V_2/V_1)$.

---

**Model Solution**:

**(a) Hess's Law and Numerical (7 marks)**:

**Statement of Hess's Law (2 marks)**:

> [!NOTE]
> *Hess's Law states that the total enthalpy change in a chemical reaction is the same, regardless of the number of intermediate steps or the path by which the reaction is carried out, provided the initial and final states (temperature, pressure, and physical states of reactants and products) are identical.*

**Explanation (1 mark)**: This is a direct consequence of enthalpy being a **state function**. The change in a state function is path-independent; hence, the heat evolved/absorbed depends only on the initial and final states.

**Numerical (4 marks)**:

**Required Reaction** (formation of CH₄):

$$C(\text{graphite}) + 2H_2(g) \rightarrow CH_4(g)$$

**Combustion Reactions**:

$$\text{(i)} \quad C(\text{graphite}) + O_2(g) \rightarrow CO_2(g) \quad \Delta H_c = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$\text{(ii)} \quad H_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2(g) \rightarrow H_2O(l) \quad \Delta H_c = -285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$\text{(iii)} \quad CH_4(g) + 2O_2(g) \rightarrow CO_2(g) + 2H_2O(l) \quad \Delta H_c = -890.4 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Using the formula** (combustion-based):

$$\Delta H_f°(CH_4) = [\Delta H_c(C) + 2 \cdot \Delta H_c(H_2)] - [\Delta H_c(CH_4)]$$

**Step 1 (1 mark)**: Sum of combustion enthalpies of reactants:

$$\Sigma_{reactants} = -393.5 + 2(-285.8) = -393.5 - 571.6 = -965.1 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark)**: Combustion enthalpy of product:

$$\Sigma_{products} = -890.4 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark)**: Subtract:

$$\Delta H_f°(CH_4) = -965.1 - (-890.4) = -965.1 + 890.4 = -74.7 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark)**: Result:

$$\boxed{\Delta H_f°(CH_4, g) = -74.7 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

The negative sign indicates that the formation of CH₄ from its elements is **exothermic** — consistent with methane being a stable, energy-rich fuel.

---

**(b) Entropy Derivation (7 marks)**:

**Definition (2 marks)**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Entropy (S)** is a thermodynamic state function that quantifies the degree of disorder or randomness of a system. Mathematically, for a reversible process, the change in entropy is defined as the infinitesimal heat transferred divided by the absolute temperature:
> $$dS = \dfrac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$$

For an **irreversible** real process: $dS \geq \dfrac{\delta Q}{T}$.

**Derivation of $\Delta S = nR \ln(V_2/V_1)$ (5 marks)**:

**Step 1 (1 mark)**: For a reversible isothermal expansion of an ideal gas, the heat transferred is:

$$\delta Q_{rev} = P\,dV \quad \text{(since } dU = 0 \text{ for isothermal ideal gas, so } \delta Q = \delta W = P\,dV \text{)}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark)**: Substitute into the entropy definition:

$$dS = \dfrac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = \dfrac{P\,dV}{T}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark)**: For an ideal gas, $P = \dfrac{nRT}{V}$, so:

$$dS = \dfrac{(nRT/V)\,dV}{T} = \dfrac{nR\,dV}{V}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark)**: Integrate from state 1 to state 2:

$$\Delta S = \int_{S_1}^{S_2} dS = nR \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \dfrac{dV}{V}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark)**: Evaluate the integral:

$$\boxed{\Delta S = nR \ln\left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)}$$

**Engineering Note**: This expression is the **foundation** for the entropy of mixing, the derivation of the Nernst equation, and the calculation of vapor-liquid equilibrium. When $V_2 > V_1$ (expansion), $\Delta S > 0$, confirming that gas spontaneously expands into a vacuum — consistent with the Second Law.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors** (lose 1–2 marks): Forgetting that work done **BY** the system is positive, but in the convention $W = \int P\,dV$, when the gas is **compressed** ($V_2 < V_1$), W is **negative**. State the convention explicitly at the start of the solution.
>
> 2. **Units Mismatch** (lose 1 mark): Using R = 8.314 J/(mol·K) with volumes in **L** without converting. Always convert volumes to **m³** (1 L = 10⁻³ m³) when using SI units, OR keep R = 8.314 kPa·L/(mol·K) and pressure in kPa.
>
> 3. **Hess's Law Sign Confusion** (lose 1–2 marks): For formation from combustion, the formula is **reactants − products**. For combustion from formation, it's **products − reactants**. Mixing these up is the #1 error in thermochemistry problems.
>
> 4. **Missing Assumptions in Derivations** (lose 1 mark): For $C_P - C_V = R$, **always** state that the gas is ideal. For Carnot efficiency, **always** state that the cycle is reversible. For isentropic relations, state "reversible adiabatic".
>
> 5. **Confusing "Spontaneous" with "Exothermic"** (lose 1 mark): A reaction is **not** necessarily spontaneous just because ΔH < 0. The correct criterion is **ΔG < 0**. Use the Gibbs-Helmholtz relation $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ to check.
>
> 6. **Forgetting State Functions vs. Path Functions** (lose 1 mark): U, H, S, G are **state functions** (denoted dU, dH); Q and W are **path functions** (denoted δQ, δW). Using d instead of δ is a common notation mistake.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision List — KTU Module 3: Chemical Thermodynamics**

- **System & Surroundings**: Clearly identify before solving any problem. Closed = no mass transfer, Open = mass crosses boundary, Isolated = no mass or energy transfer.
- **First Law**: $dU = \delta Q - \delta W$. For PV work only, $dU = \delta Q - P\,dV$. Energy is conserved; the universe's energy is constant.
- **Enthalpy H = U + PV**: A convenient function for constant-P processes where $Q_P = \Delta H$. Most industrial processes are at constant P, making H the workhorse of chemical engineering.
- **Heat Capacities**: $C_V = (\partial U/\partial T)_V$; $C_P = (\partial H/\partial T)_P$. For ideal gases: $C_P - C_V = R$. The empirical form $C_P = a + bT + cT^2 + d/T^2$ is used for non-isothermal reactions.
- **Hess's Law**: ΔH is path-independent. Calculate ΔHₓₙ from ΔHᶠ data (products − reactants) or from ΔH꜀ data (reactants − products). The neutralization of strong acid + strong base = **−57.1 kJ/mol**.
- **Second Law**: $dS \geq \delta Q/T$. For isolated systems, $dS \geq 0$ (entropy of the universe increases). Spontaneity in isolated systems is judged by entropy.
- **Entropy of isothermal ideal gas expansion**: $\Delta S = nR \ln(V_2/V_1) = nR \ln(P_1/P_2)$.
- **Gibbs Free Energy G = H − TS**: Master criterion for constant-T, constant-P processes. $\Delta G < 0$ → spontaneous. At equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$.
- **Gibbs Equation**: $dG = V\,dP - S\,dT$ — the bridge between the two laws. **Memorize this**.
- **Equilibrium constant**: $\Delta G° = -RT \ln K$ — connects thermodynamics to reaction yields.
- **Carnot efficiency**: $\eta = 1 - T_C/T_H$. Sets the **upper bound** for any heat engine.
- **Adiabatic reversible (isentropic)**: $TV^{\gamma-1} = \text{constant}$, $PV^\gamma = \text{constant}$, $T^{\gamma} P^{1-\gamma} = \text{constant}$. Use $\gamma = C_P/C_V$ for these problems.
- **Sign Convention (Engineering)**: Q in > 0, W by system > 0, ΔH for endothermic > 0, ΔG for spontaneous < 0.
- **Standard States**: T = 298.15 K, P = 1 atm = 101.325 kPa. All ΔH° and ΔG° values are referenced to these.
- **State vs. Path Functions**: U, H, S, G are state; Q, W are path.
- **R value**: Use **8.314 J/(mol·K)** in SI. Use **1.987 cal/(mol·K)** in CGS or older problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Strategy for KTU Exam**: If you are short on time, prioritize mastering **(i) the First Law with PV work, (ii) the $C_P - C_V = R$ derivation, (iii) Hess's Law numerical, and (iv) the Gibbs equation derivation**. These four topics account for **~60–70%** of marks in Module 3 of Chemical Process Principles.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fundamental Concepts and Definitions - Types of Thermodynamic Systems and Properties - Closed

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Thermodynamic System: A Formal Definition

In the rigorous framework of **Chemical Process Principles**, a **thermodynamic system** is defined as a precisely delineated quantity of matter, or a clearly demarcated region in space, selected for the explicit purpose of thermodynamic analysis. Everything external to this chosen boundary is termed the **surroundings**. The system and surroundings together constitute the **universe**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** A system is *not* a physical object by itself — it is a **conceptual demarcation** chosen by the analyst. The boundary may be real (a pipe wall, a reactor shell) or purely imaginary (a mathematical surface enclosing a fluid stream).

## 1.2 Three Pillars of System Identification

Every thermodynamic system is identified by its three defining characteristics:

| Pillar | Description |
|---|---|
| **Boundary** | The closed surface (real or imaginary) that separates the system from its surroundings. |
| **Surroundings** | All mass and regions external to the system boundary. |
| **Universe** | The thermodynamic universe = System + Surroundings. |

## 1.3 Primary Classification of Thermodynamic Systems

KTU 2024 Scheme recognizes **three primary categories** of thermodynamic systems based on **mass transfer** and **energy transfer** across the boundary:

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification Triad (Board-Exam Critical):**
> 1. **Closed System** — *No mass transfer; energy transfer permitted.*
> 2. **Open System (Control Volume)** — *Both mass and energy transfer permitted.*
> 3. **Isolated System** — *Neither mass nor energy transfer permitted.*

## 1.4 The Closed System — Core Definition

A **closed system** (also termed a **control mass** in classical thermodynamics) is a region in space across whose boundary:

- **Mass transfer is strictly prohibited** ($\Delta m_{system} = 0$).
- **Energy transfer is permitted** in the form of **heat ($Q$)** and **work ($W$)**.

The defining mathematical condition is:

$$\frac{dm_{sys}}{dt} = 0 \quad \text{(mass is conserved within the system)}$$

$$\frac{dE_{sys}}{dt} \neq 0 \quad \text{(energy may change due to Q and W interactions)}$$

## 1.5 Intuitive Analogy — The Sealed Pressure Cooker

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a **sealed pressure cooker** on a kitchen stove. The mass of food + air inside the cooker is **fixed** — nothing can enter or leave through the sealed lid. However, as the burner supplies heat, the internal temperature rises, and the safety valve may lift, performing **boundary work** against the atmosphere. The mass stays constant, but the **energy content changes**. This is precisely a **closed system**.

A second powerful analogy is a **piston-cylinder assembly** in an engine: the gas trapped between the piston and the cylinder head has a fixed mass, yet it receives heat, expands against the piston (doing work), and changes its internal energy — all without any mass crossing the boundary.

## 1.6 The Special Case: The Isolated System

A **closed system** that additionally prevents **all energy interactions** (no heat, no work) across its boundary is the **isolated system**. For such a system, the energy is also conserved:

$$\frac{dE_{sys}}{dt} = 0 \quad \text{(isolated system — adiabatic + rigid)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** A closed system is *not* necessarily isolated. The "closed" refers only to mass, not energy. Many students incorrectly equate the two terms.

## 1.7 Thermodynamic Properties — The Descriptive Language

A **property** (or thermodynamic state variable) is any macroscopic, time-independent observable of a system that depends *only* on the **state** of the system — not on the *path* taken to reach that state. KTU 2024 Scheme requires the explicit identification of two principal categories:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Broad Property Categories:**
> - **Intensive Properties** — Independent of system size/mass (e.g., $T$, $P$, $\rho$, specific volume $v$).
> - **Extensive Properties** — Scale linearly with system size/mass (e.g., $V$, $U$, $H$, $S$, total mass $m$).

The relationship is foundational:

$$\text{Specific Property} = \frac{\text{Extensive Property}}{\text{Mass of System}}$$

For example, the **specific volume** $v = V/m$ is intensive, even though total volume $V$ is extensive.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Closed System Boundary

The boundary of a closed system can be:

- **Rigid (non-deformable):** No boundary work is performed. Example: a sealed, constant-volume metallic tank.
- **Movable (deformable):** Boundary work is performed as the system expands or compresses. Example: a gas in a piston-cylinder assembly.
- **Diathermic (permeable to heat):** Heat transfer is permitted.
- **Adiabatic (impermeable to heat):** No heat transfer; perfectly insulated.

> [!NOTE]
> The same physical system can be classified differently by changing the boundary! For instance, consider a gas-filled cylinder with a piston. If we *define the system as the gas inside*, it is a **closed system**. If we *define the system as the entire volume enclosed by the cylinder walls and piston (gas + moving boundary)*, the analysis changes.

## 2.2 The First Law for a Closed System (The Energy Balance)

The **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to a closed system is the single most tested equation in any KTU thermodynamics module. It is a direct statement of the **conservation of energy**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **First Law Statement (Closed System):** The change in the total internal energy of a closed system equals the net heat added to the system *minus* the net work done *by* the system.

Mathematically:

$$\Delta U = Q - W$$

For a differential (infinitesimal) change:

$$dU = \delta Q - \delta W$$

The symbols $dU$ (state function, exact differential) versus $\delta Q$ and $\delta W$ (path functions, inexact differentials) is a frequently tested distinction in KTU examinations.

## 2.3 Boundary Work for a Closed System

When the boundary of a closed system is movable (quasi-equilibrium expansion/compression), the work done *by* the system on the surroundings is:

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

This is the **$P\,dV$ work**, also called **expansion work** or **displacement work**. For finite processes, the work depends on the **path** (the specific $P$–$V$ trajectory), confirming that work is *not* a state function.

## 2.4 The Enthalpy Property

For closed systems undergoing **constant-pressure processes**, the convenient property **enthalpy ($H$)** is introduced:

$$H \equiv U + PV$$

$$\Delta H = Q_P \quad \text{(at constant pressure, no non-PV work)}$$

## 2.5 Internal Energy and the Ideal Gas Model

For an **ideal gas**, the internal energy is a function of temperature *only*:

$$U = U(T) \quad \Rightarrow \quad dU = nC_V \, dT$$

Similarly, for an ideal gas:

$$H = H(T) \quad \Rightarrow \quad dH = nC_P \, dT$$

The celebrated **Mayer relation** connects the two heat capacities:

$$C_P - C_V = R \quad \text{(per mole, for an ideal gas)}$$

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Significance of Closed-System Analysis:**
> - **Batch Reactors** in chemical plants: fixed mass of reactants, no inlet/outlet streams during reaction.
> - **Sealed Storage Tanks** for LNG, LPG, and cryogenic fluids.
> - **Pharmaceutical Sterilization Autoclaves** — closed, heated, pressure-controlled vessels.
> - **Internal Combustion Engine Cylinders** — fixed mass of gas, undergoes cyclic PV work.
> - **Pressure Vessel Design** in petrochemical and nuclear industries.

---

## 📌 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Closed Systems)

| # | Formula | Description / Condition | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta m_{sys} = 0$ | Mass conservation in closed system | kg |
| 2 | $\Delta U = Q - W$ | First Law (closed system, finite process) | J |
| 3 | $dU = \delta Q - \delta W$ | First Law (differential form) | J |
| 4 | $W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P\,dV$ | Boundary (displacement) work | J |
| 5 | $W = P\,\Delta V$ | Isobaric (constant $P$) boundary work | J |
| 6 | $W = 0$ | Isochoric (constant $V$) process | J |
| 7 | $W = \frac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{\gamma - 1}$ | Adiabatic (reversible) work, ideal gas | J |
| 8 | $H \equiv U + PV$ | Enthalpy definition | J |
| 9 | $\Delta H = Q_P$ | Constant-$P$ heat (closed, no other work) | J |
| 10 | $dU = nC_V\,dT$ | Ideal gas, $U$ depends on $T$ only | J |
| 11 | $dH = nC_P\,dT$ | Ideal gas, $H$ depends on $T$ only | J |
| 12 | $C_P - C_V = R$ | Mayer's relation (ideal gas, molar basis) | J/(mol·K) |
| 13 | $PV = nRT$ | Ideal gas equation of state | Pa·m³ |
| 14 | $v = V/m$ | Specific volume (intensive from extensive $V$) | m³/kg |

---

## 2.7 The Underlying "Why" — Logical Reasoning Chain

> [!NOTE]
> **The First Law is a logical inevitability, not an empirical accident:**
> 1. Energy cannot be created or destroyed (Empirical observation, Joule's experiments).
> 2. A closed system has no mass flux, so any energy change must be due to *transfers* across the boundary.
> 3. The only two recognized modes of energy transfer across a closed-system boundary are **heat** and **work**.
> 4. Therefore, $\Delta E_{sys} = Q_{in} - W_{by}$ (with sign conventions).
> 5. For a system at rest with negligible KE/PE, $E_{sys} = U$, giving $\Delta U = Q - W$.

The sign convention is critical and frequently tested:

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Sign Convention (Adopted Universally in Module 3):**
> - $Q > 0$ → Heat supplied **to** the system.
> - $W > 0$ → Work done **by** the system **on** the surroundings.
> - $\Delta U > 0$ → Internal energy of the system **increases**.

# 📘 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — First Law of Thermodynamics for a Closed System

We begin from the **general energy balance** for any thermodynamic system:

$$\Delta E_{system} = E_{in} - E_{out}$$

For a **closed system**, no mass crosses the boundary, so the only energy interactions are **heat ($Q$)** entering or leaving and **work ($W$)** being done by or on the system.

$$\Delta E_{sys} = Q - W$$

For a system that is **macroscopically stationary** (no bulk motion), the kinetic energy ($KE$) and potential energy ($PE$) changes are negligible, and $E_{sys}$ reduces to the **internal energy $U$**:

$$\boxed{\Delta U = Q - W}$$

**Differential form:**

$$dU = \delta Q - \delta W$$

The careful use of $dU$ (exact differential) versus $\delta Q, \delta W$ (inexact differentials) reflects the fact that $U$ is a state function while $Q$ and $W$ are path functions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Boundary Work for Quasi-Equilibrium Process

Consider a **piston-cylinder assembly** containing a gas (closed system). Let the piston of cross-sectional area $A$ move a differential distance $dx$ under a quasi-equilibrium pressure $P$ exerted by the gas.

**Step 1: Force exerted by the gas on the piston.**

$$F = P \cdot A$$

**Step 2: Differential work done by the gas during displacement $dx$.**

$$\delta W = F \cdot dx = P \cdot A \cdot dx$$

**Step 3: Recognize that $A \cdot dx = dV$ (the differential change in gas volume).**

$$\delta W = P \, dV$$

**Step 4: Integrate between the initial volume $V_1$ and final volume $V_2$.**

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

This integral is **path-dependent** — its value depends on the specific $P(V)$ relationship during the process.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Boundary Work for Special Processes

### (a) **Isobaric (Constant-Pressure) Process**

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV = P \int_{V_1}^{V_2} dV$$

$$\boxed{W = P\,(V_2 - V_1) = P\,\Delta V}$$

### (b) **Isochoric (Constant-Volume) Process**

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV = 0 \quad (\text{since } dV = 0)$$

$$\boxed{W = 0}$$

### (c) **Isothermal Process for an Ideal Gas**

For an ideal gas at constant $T$: $P = \dfrac{nRT}{V}$

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{nRT}{V} \, dV = nRT \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{dV}{V}$$

$$\boxed{W = nRT \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)}$$

### (d) **Reversible Adiabatic Process for an Ideal Gas**

For a reversible adiabatic process, $PV^\gamma = \text{constant}$, where $\gamma = C_P/C_V$.

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{C}{V^\gamma} \, dV \quad \text{where } C = P_1 V_1^\gamma$$

$$W = C \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V^{-\gamma} \, dV = C \left[ \frac{V^{1-\gamma}}{1-\gamma} \right]_{V_1}^{V_2}$$

$$W = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - \gamma}$$

$$\boxed{W = \frac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{\gamma - 1}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Enthalpy from First Law at Constant Pressure

Starting from the First Law for a closed system:

$$dU = \delta Q - \delta W$$

For a process at **constant pressure** with only $P\,dV$ work:

$$dU = \delta Q_P - P\,dV$$

Rearranging:

$$\delta Q_P = dU + P\,dV$$

Since $P$ is constant: $P\,dV = d(PV)$. Therefore:

$$\delta Q_P = dU + d(PV) = d(U + PV)$$

Define the **enthalpy** as:

$$\boxed{H \equiv U + PV}$$

Then:

$$\boxed{\delta Q_P = dH \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_P = \Delta H}$$

This is why enthalpy is the natural state function for constant-pressure heat analysis.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Mayer's Relation for an Ideal Gas

For one mole of an ideal gas: $PV = RT$.

Differentiating at constant $P$:

$$P\,dV = R\,dT$$

The molar enthalpy: $H = U + PV$. Differentiating:

$$dH = dU + P\,dV + V\,dP = dU + R\,dT \quad (\text{at constant }P,\ dP=0)$$

For an ideal gas: $dH = C_P\,dT$ and $dU = C_V\,dT$. Substituting:

$$C_P\,dT = C_V\,dT + R\,dT$$

$$\boxed{C_P - C_V = R}$$

---

## 3.6 📊 Analytical Matrix — Process-by-Process Summary for Closed Systems

| Process | Condition | Work $W$ | Heat $Q$ | $\Delta U$ | $\Delta H$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Isochoric** | $V = \text{const}$ | $0$ | $nC_V \Delta T$ | $nC_V \Delta T$ | $nC_P \Delta T$ |
| **Isobaric** | $P = \text{const}$ | $P \Delta V = nR \Delta T$ | $nC_P \Delta T$ | $nC_V \Delta T$ | $nC_P \Delta T$ |
| **Isothermal** (ideal gas) | $T = \text{const}$ | $nRT \ln(V_2/V_1)$ | $W$ (since $\Delta U = 0$) | $0$ | $0$ |
| **Adiabatic** | $Q = 0$ | $\dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{\gamma - 1}$ | $0$ | $-W$ | $nC_P \Delta T$ |
| **Polytropic** | $PV^n = \text{const}$ | $\dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{1 - n}$ | $\Delta U + W$ | $nC_V \Delta T$ | $nC_P \Delta T$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix above is the **single most important reference table** for KTU 2024 closed-system problems. Commit it to memory before any exam.

# 📘 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## ✏️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"Distinguish clearly between a closed system and an isolated system. Give one industrial example of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Closed System | Isolated System |
|---|---|---|
| **Mass Transfer** | Not permitted | Not permitted |
| **Energy Transfer** | Permitted ($Q$ and/or $W$) | Not permitted ($Q = 0$, $W = 0$) |
| **Energy of System** | Can change ($\Delta U \neq 0$ in general) | Constant ($\Delta U = 0$) |
| **Industrial Example** | Piston-cylinder in an I.C. engine; batch reactor | Perfectly insulated, rigid thermos flask; the universe itself |

A **closed system** allows energy to cross its boundary as heat and work, while an **isolated system** is a special case of a closed system that additionally has no energy interaction with its surroundings. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Define 'intensive' and 'extensive' thermodynamic properties. Why is the ratio of two extensive properties always intensive?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Intensive property:** A property *independent* of the size or amount of matter in the system. Examples: $T$, $P$, $\rho$, specific volume $v$. **(1 mark)**
- **Extensive property:** A property that *scales linearly* with the size/mass of the system. Examples: $V$, $U$, $H$, $S$, $m$. **(1 mark)**
- **Why ratio is intensive:** If $X$ and $Y$ are extensive and both scale linearly with system size, then $X = k_X \cdot m$ and $Y = k_Y \cdot m$. The ratio $X/Y = k_X/k_Y$ becomes a constant independent of mass — hence intensive. For example, $V/m = v$ (specific volume) is intensive. **(1 mark)**

---

## ✏️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **ESE Module 3 — Internal Choice Pattern.** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### 🅰️ **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**(a)** A closed rigid tank contains 2.0 kg of an ideal gas. The gas is heated from an initial temperature of **300 K** to a final temperature of **500 K**. Given $C_V = 0.718$ kJ/(kg·K) and $C_P = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K) for the gas, calculate:
   (i) The heat transferred $Q$. **(3 marks)**
   (ii) The work done $W$. **(1 mark)**
   (iii) The change in internal energy $\Delta U$. **(2 marks)**
   (iv) The change in enthalpy $\Delta H$. **(1 mark)**

**(b)** Derive the **First Law of Thermodynamics for a closed system**, starting from the general energy balance. Clearly state the sign convention used. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

### Part (a) Solution

**Given:**
- Mass: $m = 2.0$ kg
- $T_1 = 300$ K, $T_2 = 500$ K, hence $\Delta T = 200$ K
- $C_V = 0.718$ kJ/(kg·K), $C_P = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K)
- **Rigid tank** ⇒ constant volume process (isochoric)

**Step 1: Work done $W$.**
For a rigid tank, $dV = 0$, hence:

$$W = \int P\,dV = 0 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2: Change in internal energy $\Delta U$.**

$$\Delta U = m C_V \Delta T = 2.0 \times 0.718 \times 200$$

$$\Delta U = 287.2 \text{ kJ} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 3: Heat transferred $Q$.**
Apply First Law: $Q = \Delta U + W = 287.2 + 0 = 287.2$ kJ. Alternatively directly:

$$Q = m C_V \Delta T = 287.2 \text{ kJ} \quad \text{(3 marks)}$$

**Step 4: Change in enthalpy $\Delta H$.**

$$\Delta H = m C_P \Delta T = 2.0 \times 1.005 \times 200 = 402.0 \text{ kJ} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Students:** In a constant-volume process, the work is zero and *all* the heat supplied goes into raising the internal energy. The enthalpy change is *larger* than $\Delta U$ because $H = U + PV$ and $PV$ increases as the gas is heated at constant $V$.

---

### Part (b) Solution — Derivation of the First Law

**Step 1: Start from the general energy balance** for any system:

$$\Delta E_{system} = E_{in} - E_{out} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2: Apply to a closed system.** For a closed system, no mass crosses the boundary. Therefore, the only energy interactions possible across the boundary are:
- **Heat ($Q$)** crossing the boundary.
- **Work ($W$)** crossing the boundary.

$$\Delta E_{sys} = Q - W \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 3: For a system at rest** (no macroscopic motion, no change in elevation):

$$E_{sys} = U + KE + PE \approx U \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 4: Final First Law statement for a closed system:**

$$\boxed{\Delta U = Q - W} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 5: Sign Convention explicitly stated (KTU 2024 Requirement):**
- $Q > 0$ : heat supplied **to** the system.
- $W > 0$ : work done **by** the system.
- $\Delta U > 0$ : internal energy increases. **(2 marks)**

**Differential form:**

$$dU = \delta Q - \delta W$$

> [!NOTE]
> The exact differential $dU$ (because $U$ is a state function) versus the inexact differentials $\delta Q$ and $\delta W$ (path functions) is a frequently asked distinction.

---

### 🅱️ **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** A closed system containing **1.5 mol** of an ideal gas undergoes an **isothermal expansion** at $T = 400$ K from an initial volume of **0.020 m³** to a final volume of **0.060 m³**. Calculate:
   (i) The work done by the gas. **(3 marks)**
   (ii) The heat transferred. **(1 mark)**
   (iii) The change in internal energy and the change in enthalpy. **(2 marks)**
   (iv) Comment on the sign of $Q$ and its physical meaning. **(1 mark)**

**(b)** With the aid of a **neat sketch**, explain the **three types of thermodynamic systems** (closed, open, isolated). Tabulate **five differences** between them. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

### Part (a) Solution

**Given:**
- $n = 1.5$ mol, $T = 400$ K (constant, isothermal)
- $V_1 = 0.020$ m³, $V_2 = 0.060$ m³
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

**Step 1: Work done by the gas (isothermal, ideal gas).**

$$W = nRT \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

$$W = 1.5 \times 8.314 \times 400 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{0.060}{0.020}\right)$$

$$W = 4988.4 \times \ln(3) = 4988.4 \times 1.0986$$

$$\boxed{W \approx 5480.5 \text{ J} \approx 5.48 \text{ kJ}} \quad \text{(3 marks)}$$

**Step 2: Heat transferred.**
For an ideal gas in an isothermal process, $\Delta U = 0$. By the First Law:

$$Q = \Delta U + W = 0 + 5480.5 = 5480.5 \text{ J} \approx 5.48 \text{ kJ} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3: Change in internal energy and enthalpy.**
For an ideal gas, both $U$ and $H$ depend only on $T$. Since $T$ is constant:

$$\Delta U = 0, \quad \Delta H = 0 \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 4: Sign and physical meaning of $Q$.**
$Q > 0$ means heat is **supplied to the system** from the surroundings to maintain the isothermal temperature during expansion. The gas does work on the surroundings, and an equal amount of heat must flow in to keep the temperature (and hence internal energy) constant. **(1 mark)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Takeaway:** In an isothermal expansion of an ideal gas, the **work output** of the system is entirely sustained by an **equivalent heat input** from a thermal reservoir. No internal energy is consumed.

---

### Part (b) Solution — Three Types of Systems

**Step 1: Sketch (text-based representation, equivalent to the Mermaid block diagrams above).**

```
+----------------------------------------------+
|            CLOSED SYSTEM                     |
|                                              |
|  +-------------+         +-------------+     |
|  | Surroundings|  Q  --> |   System    |     |
|  |             |         |   (gas in   |     |
|  |             |  <-- W  |  piston-cyl)|     |
|  +-------------+         +-------------+     |
|                                              |
|  * NO mass transfer                          |
|  * Heat and work permitted                   |
+----------------------------------------------+
```

**Step 2: Tabulated differences (any 5 of the following).** **(7 marks)**

| # | Aspect | Closed System | Open System | Isolated System |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass transfer | None | Permitted | None |
| 2 | Heat transfer | Permitted | Permitted | None |
| 3 | Work transfer | Permitted | Permitted | None |
| 4 | Boundary | Real or imaginary, may be movable | Real with inlets/outlets | Rigid + adiabatic |
| 5 | Typical example | Piston-cylinder, batch reactor | Turbine, compressor, nozzle | Thermos flask, the universe |
| 6 | Energy change | $\Delta U = Q - W$ (can be nonzero) | Steady-state: $\Delta E = 0$ (typically) | $\Delta U = 0$ always |
| 7 | Industry use | Storage tanks, autoclaves | Process plants, pipelines | Calibration, theoretical reference |

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Deduction Traps in Closed-System Problems:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors** — Forgetting that work done *by* the system is positive, or mixing this up with the physics convention. KTU strictly uses the **thermodynamic (physics) convention**: $W$ = work done *by* the system. **(–2 marks typical penalty)**
> 2. **Wrong Equation of State** — Applying $PV = nRT$ for an ideal gas to a real gas problem. Always verify the gas model. **(–1 to –3 marks)**
> 3. **Confusing $Q$ and $\Delta H$** — They are equal *only* at constant pressure with *only* $PdV$ work. If the process involves electrical or shaft work, this identity fails. **(–2 marks)**
> 4. **Forgetting to Convert Units** — Using $R = 0.0821$ L·atm/(mol·K) when the problem demands SI units, or vice versa. **(–1 to –2 marks)**
> 5. **Treating Adiabatic as "no work"** — Adiabatic means $Q = 0$, *not* $W = 0$. The work in an adiabatic expansion is finite and equals $-\Delta U$. **(–2 marks)**

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Closed Systems:**

- ☐ A **closed system** has **no mass transfer** but **permits energy transfer** as heat and work.
- ☐ A **closed system is not necessarily isolated** — isolated systems are a special case (no $Q$, no $W$).
- ☐ The **First Law for a closed system**: $\Delta U = Q - W$ (finite) / $dU = \delta Q - \delta W$ (differential).
- ☐ **Boundary work**: $W = \int P\,dV$. Zero for isochoric, $P\,\Delta V$ for isobaric, $nRT\ln(V_2/V_1)$ for ideal-gas isothermal, $\dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{\gamma - 1}$ for ideal-gas adiabatic.
- ☐ **Enthalpy** $H = U + PV$. At constant $P$ (closed system), $Q_P = \Delta H$.
- ☐ **Mayer's relation**: $C_P - C_V = R$ (per mole, ideal gas).
- ☐ **Intensive vs. Extensive**: $T, P, v, \rho$ are intensive; $V, U, H, S, m, n$ are extensive. Ratio of two extensive properties is intensive.
- ☐ **Sign Convention (KTU 2024 Standard):** $Q_{in} > 0$, $W_{by} > 0$, $\Delta U > 0$ means system energy increases.
- ☐ For a **rigid closed system** (constant $V$): $W = 0$ and $Q = \Delta U = m C_V \Delta T$.
- ☐ For an **ideal gas**: $U = U(T)$ and $H = H(T)$ only — even for non-isothermal processes.
- ☐ Examples to remember: **batch reactor, autoclave, piston-cylinder, sealed storage tank** = closed; **turbine, nozzle, heat exchanger** = open; **thermos flask** = isolated.
- ☐ The state-vs-path distinction: $U$ and $H$ are **state functions** (exact differentials); $Q$ and $W$ are **path functions** (inexact differentials).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Open and Isolated System - Intensive and Extensive Properties - Path and State Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Open and Isolated System - Intensive and Extensive Properties - Path and State Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Classification of Systems

In **Chemical Process Principles**, a **system** is defined as any specified region of the universe chosen for thermodynamic study, separated from the **surroundings** by a real or imaginary **boundary**.

### Formal KTU 2024 Definitions

**Open System (Control Volume, CV):**
A system in which **both mass and energy** can cross the system boundary. The boundary is permeable to matter and energy.

**Closed System (Control Mass, CM):**
A system in which **only energy** (heat and work) can cross the boundary, but **mass cannot** cross. The amount of matter inside remains constant.

**Isolated System:**
A system in which **neither mass nor energy** can cross the system boundary. It is completely disconnected from its surroundings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** The phrase "control volume" is the modern preferred synonym for "open system," and "control mass" is the synonym for "closed system." Use these terms interchangeably in 14-mark answers to demonstrate syllabus fluency.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Kitchen Sink Model

Imagine three vessels in a chemical plant:

- **Open System → An open cooking pot with water boiling.** Steam (mass) leaves the pot, and heat (energy) enters from the burner. The water level changes. This is exactly how a **distillation column reboiler** or a **continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR)** behaves.
- **Closed System → A sealed pressure cooker (locked lid).** Heat enters, but no mass escapes. The water boils inside, but the lid keeps the steam trapped. This is how a **batch reactor** operates.
- **Isolated System → A perfectly insulated, sealed Thermos flask in deep space.** Neither heat leaks in/out, nor does any mass escape. The universe itself is treated as an isolated system in thermodynamics. In practice, a **Dewar flask** is the closest engineering approximation.

## 1.2 Intensive vs. Extensive Properties

### Formal Definitions

**Intensive Property:**
A property whose value is **independent of the size or mass** of the system. It is a *local* property and remains uniform throughout a homogeneous system.

**Extensive Property:**
A property whose value is **proportional to the size or mass** of the system. If the system is divided into two halves, the extensive property values **add up**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Bakery Counter

- **Intensive → Temperature of a cup of tea.** Whether you have a small cup or a large jug of tea at the same conditions, the temperature reads the same (e.g., **80 °C**). 
- **Extensive → Total mass of tea in the container.** A small cup has 200 g; a large jug has 2 kg. The value clearly depends on how much tea you have.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Rule (KTU Favorite):** *The ratio of two extensive properties is an intensive property.* For example, volume/mass = specific volume (intensive), or mass/volume = density (intensive).

## 1.3 Path Functions vs. State Functions

### Formal Definitions

**State Function (Point Function):**
A property that depends **only on the current state** of the system (defined by state variables like P, V, T), and **not on the path** taken to reach that state.

**Path Function (Process Function):**
A quantity whose value depends on the **specific path** followed during a process, not just on the initial and final states.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Hilltop Hike

Think of climbing a mountain:

- **State Function → The final elevation of the peak.** Whether you took the steep direct trail or the long winding road, the elevation at the summit is the same (**3,000 m**). The change ΔH = H₂ − H₁ is path-independent.
- **Path Function → The calories (work) you burn.** The steep direct trail exhausts you in 1 hour; the winding road takes 5 hours. The energy expended is **completely different** for the two paths, even though start and end are identical.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical Signature (Symbol Convention):**
> - State functions use **exact differential**: $dZ$ (e.g., $dU$, $dH$, $dS$)
> - Path functions use **inexact differential**: $\delta W$, $\delta Q$ (the crossed-delta denotes path dependence)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structured Logic Flow — Why Systems Are Classified

The classification is based on **two physical quantities** that may cross the boundary:

1. **Mass transfer** across the boundary
2. **Energy transfer** (heat $Q$ and work $W$) across the boundary

| System Type | Mass Crosses? | Energy Crosses? | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Open | **Yes** | Yes | Distillation column, CSTR, heat exchanger |
| Closed | **No** | Yes | Batch reactor, piston-cylinder, sealed autoclave |
| Isolated | **No** | **No** | Thermos flask, sealed Dewar, the Universe |

## 2.2 Property Classification — Mathematical Foundation

### Intensive Property Test

If a system of mass $m$ is split into two equal parts of mass $m/2$, an intensive property $I$ satisfies:

$$I_{total} = I_{part} = I_{part}$$

The value does **not** scale with mass.

### Extensive Property Test

An extensive property $E$ scales linearly with mass:

$$E_{total} = E_{part\,1} + E_{part\,2}$$

### Specific Property (The Bridge)

A **specific property** is an extensive property **divided by mass**, converting it to intensive form:

$$z_{specific} = \frac{Z_{total}}{m}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table lists every formula, definition, and criterion you must memorize for the KTU ESE on this topic. All symbols are KTU-standard.

| Concept | Formula / Criterion | Property Type | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specific Volume | $\hat{v} = V / m$ | Intensive (derived) | $\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$ |
| Density | $\rho = m / V = 1 / \hat{v}$ | Intensive (derived) | $\text{kg}/\text{m}^3$ |
| Molar Volume | $V_m = V / n$ | Intensive (derived) | $\text{m}^3/\text{mol}$ |
