<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
>
> **Q1. State the four Maxwell relations for a pure substance in closed systems and mention the thermodynamic potential from which each is derived.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four Maxwell relations are:

1. From $U(S, V)$: $\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = -\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V$

2. From $H(S, P)$: $\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S = \left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_P$

3. From $A(T, V)$: $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$

4. From $G(T, P)$: $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
>
> **Q2. What is the physical significance of Maxwell relations in chemical engineering thermodynamics?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Maxwell relations have the following physical significance in chemical engineering:

1. They allow the calculation of **changes in entropy** (a non-measurable property) from easily measurable quantities like pressure, volume, and temperature.

2. They form the **mathematical foundation** for deriving residual properties and departure functions used with cubic equations of state (e.g., Peng-Robinson, SRK) for real-gas behavior.

3. They are essential for **process design calculations** involving compressors, expanders, refrigeration cycles, and phase-equilibria modeling.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]**
>
> ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
>
> **(a)** Derive the four Maxwell relations starting from the fundamental property equations of $U$, $H$, $A$, and $G$. Clearly state Schwarz's theorem and indicate the sign of each relation. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a fluid obeying the van der Waals equation of state, $\left(P + \dfrac{a}{V^2}\right)(V - b) = RT$, derive an expression for the change in entropy when the fluid is compressed isothermally from volume $V_1$ to $V_2$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

*Schwarz's Theorem:* For any continuous function $f(x, y)$ with continuous second derivatives, the mixed second partial derivatives are equal:

$$\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y \partial x} = \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x \partial y}$$

**Step 1 — Starting from $U$:** $dU = TdS - PdV$. The coefficients are $T = (\partial U/\partial S)_V$ and $-P = (\partial U/\partial V)_S$. Applying Schwarz's theorem:

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V \quad \text{(MR-1)}$$

**Step 2 — Starting from $H$:** $dH = TdS + VdP$. Coefficients: $T = (\partial H/\partial S)_P$, $V = (\partial H/\partial P)_S$. Applying Schwarz:

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_P \quad \text{(MR-2)}$$

**Step 3 — Starting from $A$:** $dA = -SdT - PdV$. Coefficients: $-S = (\partial A/\partial T)_V$, $-P = (\partial A/\partial V)_T$. Applying Schwarz:

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V \quad \text{(MR-3)}$$

**Step 4 — Starting from $G$:** $dG = -SdT + VdP$. Coefficients: $-S = (\partial G/\partial T)_P$, $V = (\partial G/\partial P)_T$. Applying Schwarz:

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P \quad \text{(MR-4)}$$

**Sign convention:** MR-1 and MR-4 carry a negative sign; MR-2 and MR-3 are positive.

**Part (b) — Isothermal Entropy Change for a van der Waals Fluid (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Isothermal entropy change with volume is given by:

$$\Delta S = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T dV$$

**Step 2:** Apply **MR-3** to replace the integrand:

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$$

**Step 3:** Solve the van der Waals equation for $P$:

$$P = \frac{RT}{V - b} - \frac{a}{V^2}$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate $P$ with respect to $T$ at constant $V$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = \frac{R}{V - b} - 0 = \frac{R}{V - b}$$

(The term $-a/V^2$ has no $T$ dependence, so its derivative is zero.)

**Step 5:** Substitute and integrate:

$$\Delta S = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{R}{V - b} dV = R \ln\left(\frac{V_2 - b}{V_1 - b}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\Delta S = R \ln\left(\frac{V_2 - b}{V_1 - b}\right)}$$

> **Physical interpretation:** This shows that for a van der Waals gas, the isothermal entropy change depends not only on the volume ratio (as in the ideal gas case) but is **shifted by the covolume $b$**. When $b \to 0$, the ideal-gas result $\Delta S = R\ln(V_2/V_1)$ is recovered.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]**
>
> ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**
>
> **(a)** Define the four thermodynamic potentials $U$, $H$, $A$, and $G$ and state the Maxwell relation obtained from each. Show that $(\partial C_P/\partial P)_T = -T(\partial^2 V/\partial T^2)_P$ using a Maxwell relation. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a gas that follows the virial equation of state truncated to the second term, $Z = 1 + B'P$ (where $B'$ is the second virial coefficient, a function of $T$ only), determine the residual entropy $S^R$ of the gas. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Definitions and Identity (7 marks):**

**Definitions (1 mark):**
- $U$ = Internal Energy
- $H = U + PV$ = Enthalpy
- $A = U - TS$ = Helmholtz Free Energy
- $G = H - TS$ = Gibbs Free Energy

**Maxwell relations (already derived above):** MR-1, MR-2, MR-3, MR-4 — (1 mark)

**Step 1 — Start from the definition of $C_P$:**

$$C_P = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P \quad \text{and} \quad \left(\frac{\partial C_P}{\partial P}\right)_T = \left[\frac{\partial}{\partial P}\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P\right]_T = \left[\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_T\right]_P$$

**Step 2:** Using the exact differential of $H$: $dH = TdS + VdP$. At constant $T$, $dH_T = VdP$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_T = V$$

**Step 3:** Combine:

$$\left(\frac{\partial C_P}{\partial P}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Step 4:** Now use **MR-4** which states $(\partial S/\partial P)_T = -(\partial V/\partial T)_P$. This means:

$$\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = -\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T$$

**Step 5:** Differentiate the Maxwell relation identity further (the standard "double-step" application):

$$\left(\frac{\partial C_P}{\partial P}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left[-\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T\right]_P$$

But from the definition $dS = (C_P/T)dT - (\partial V/\partial T)_P dP$, we get:

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{C_P}{T} \Rightarrow \left(\frac{\partial C_P}{\partial P}\right)_T = T\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T\right)_P = -T\left(\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial T^2}\right)_P$$

**Result (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{\partial C_P}{\partial P}\right)_T = -T\left(\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial T^2}\right)_P}$$

**Part (b) — Residual Entropy Using the Truncated Virial EOS (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition of residual entropy:**

$$S^R(T, P) = S(T, P) - S^{ig}(T, P)$$

Or in terms of the departure from ideal gas:

$$S^R = \int_0^P \left[\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T - \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T^{ig}\right] dP$$

**Step 2 — Apply MR-4 to the real gas and the ideal gas:**

For real gas: $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$

For ideal gas: $\left(\dfrac{\partial S^{ig}}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\dfrac{\partial V^{ig}}{\partial T}\right)_P = -\dfrac{R}{P}$

**Step 3 — For the truncated virial EOS, $Z = 1 + B'P$:**

$$V = \frac{ZRT}{P} = \frac{RT}{P} + B'RT$$

(Note: $Z$ is dimensionless, $B'$ has units of 1/pressure.)

**Step 4 — Differentiate $V$ with respect to $T$ at constant $P$:**

$$\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{R}{P} + B' R + RT\left(\frac{\partial B'}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the residual entropy integral:**

$$S^R = \int_0^P \left[-\left(\frac{R}{P} + B'R + RT\frac{dB'}{dT}\right) - \left(-\frac{R}{P}\right)\right] dP$$

$$S^R = \int_0^P \left[-B'R - RT\frac{dB'}{dT}\right] dP$$

Since $B'$ and $dB'/dT$ depend only on $T$ (not on $P$), they are constant during integration:

$$S^R = -P\left(B'R + RT\frac{dB'}{dT}\right)$$

**Result (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\frac{S^R}{R} = -P\left(B' + T\frac{dB'}{dT}\right)}$$

This is a well-known result in chemical engineering thermodynamics and is widely used in process simulation packages to correct the entropy of real gases from ideal-gas tabulated values.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Sign errors in MR-1 and MR-4:** Students often drop the negative sign. Both relations carry a negative sign because of the sign of $-P$ in the original differential.
>
> 2. **Confusing natural variables:** $U$ is a function of $(S, V)$, not $(T, V)$. Mistaking the natural variables leads to a completely wrong relation.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to use $H = U + PV$ properly:** The $PdV$ and $VdP$ terms must be carefully tracked.
>
> 4. **Writing the symmetric form of Schwarz's theorem as an "if and only if":** Just because the cross derivatives are equal doesn't mean the function exists. The function $U$ must first be an exact differential of a state function.
>
> 5. **In numerical problems, failing to evaluate $(\partial P/\partial T)_V$ correctly:** For van der Waals or virial EOS, the temperature derivative of the "non-ideal" term is often missed (e.g., the $-a/V^2$ term in van der Waals has zero $T$-derivative).
>
> 6. **Mismatched subscripts in the final answer:** Subscripts of $T$, $P$, $S$, $V$ must be precise. Examiners deduct heavily for ambiguous subscripts.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Maxwell Relations = 4 equations** derived from the **4 fundamental potentials** $U, H, A, G$.
- Each relation arises from **Schwarz's theorem** (equality of mixed second partial derivatives).
- **MR-1** from $U(S, V)$: $\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = -\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V$
- **MR-2** from $H(S, P)$: $\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S = \left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_P$
- **MR-3** from $A(T, V)$: $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$  *(most used in real-gas problems)*
- **MR-4** from $G(T, P)$: $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$  *(most used in isothermal processes)*
- **Sign convention:** MR-1 and MR-4 are **negative**; MR-2 and MR-3 are **positive**.
- **Natural variables** must be memorized: $U \to (S, V)$; $H \to (S, P)$; $A \to (T, V)$; $G \to (T, P)$.
- **Engineering utility:** Maxwell Relations enable calculation of entropy changes and residual properties from measurable $PVT$ data, supporting real-gas process design.
- **Mnemonic device:** The variables $S$ and $V$ appear "naturally" in $U$; $S$ and $P$ in $H$; $T$ and $V$ in $A$; $T$ and $P$ in $G$.
- **Pre-exam check:** For every Maxwell Relation, verify the **units, signs, and subscripts** before submitting your answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Capacity in Terms of Entropy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Heat Capacity in Terms of Entropy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Heat Capacity

In chemical engineering thermodynamics, **Heat Capacity (C)** is defined as the amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of a unit quantity of a substance by one degree (Kelvin or Celsius). Mathematically, for a finite mass:

$$C = \frac{dQ}{dT}$$

Depending on the thermodynamic path along which heat is transferred, two principal heat capacities emerge:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Capacity at Constant Volume ($C_v$):** The rate of change of internal energy with temperature at constant volume.
> $$C_v = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_v$$
>
> **Heat Capacity at Constant Pressure ($C_p$):** The rate of change of enthalpy with temperature at constant pressure.
> $$C_p = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_p$$

### 1.2 Formal Definition of Entropy

**Entropy ($S$)** is an extensive thermodynamic state function that quantifies the degree of molecular disorder or the unavailability of a system's thermal energy for conversion into mechanical work. For a reversible process, it is defined as:

$$dS = \frac{dQ_{rev}}{T}$$

The standard SI unit of entropy is **J·K⁻¹**, and per unit mass it is **J·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹**.

### 1.3 Heat Capacity in Terms of Entropy — The Core Connection

By combining the First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics, we obtain a unified expression for the change in internal energy:

$$dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$$

From this fundamental relation, we can extract a direct, beautiful link between heat capacity and entropy:

> [!NOTE]
> **$C_v$ in terms of Entropy:** $C_v = T\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_v$
>
> **$C_p$ in terms of Entropy:** $C_p = T\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_p$

These two equations are the heart of the KTU Module 3 topic. They show that **heat capacity is a measure of how rapidly entropy changes with temperature** under a given constraint (constant V or constant P).

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tank" Model

Imagine entropy as the **volume of water in a tank**, and temperature as the **level marker** on the side of the tank. Heat capacity is like the **cross-sectional area** of the tank:
- A **wide tank** (large $C_p$ or $C_v$) → even a small rise in temperature (level) requires a lot of heat (water) because the cross-section is large.
- A **narrow tank** (small $C_p$ or $C_v$) → a small amount of heat (water) can produce a large temperature (level) jump.

The condition of "constant pressure" or "constant volume" is like deciding whether the **tank is open (constant pressure) or sealed (constant volume)** — both change how the water (heat) distributes itself as the level (temperature) rises.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Express Heat Capacity in Terms of Entropy?

In real chemical engineering problems, we rarely have explicit equations for $U(T,V)$ or $H(T,P)$. However, we frequently have **equations of state** (e.g., $PV = nRT$, van der Waals) and **entropy models** (e.g., ideal-gas tables, residual entropy charts). Expressing heat capacity in terms of entropy's partial derivatives allows engineers to:

1. Compute $C_p$ and $C_v$ directly from measurable $P$–$V$–$T$ data.
2. Predict heat duties in reactors, compressors, and heat exchangers.
3. Determine the **$C_p/C_v$ ratio ($\gamma$)**, which is essential in compressible flow and nozzle design.

### 2.2 The Logical Foundation — Step-by-Step Reasoning

**Step 1: Combine the First and Second Laws**
The combined statement for a closed, reversible system is:
$$dU = dQ_{rev} - P\,dV = T\,dS - P\,dV$$

**Step 2: Identify the Natural Variables**
- $U$ is naturally a function of $S$ and $V$: $U = U(S,V)$.
- $H$ is naturally a function of $S$ and $P$: $H = H(S,P)$.

**Step 3: Apply the Constant-Volume Constraint**
At constant $V$ ($dV = 0$), the combined law reduces to:
$$dU = T\,dS \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_v = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_v$$
Since the left side is $C_v$, we obtain:
$$C_v = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_v$$

**Step 4: Apply the Constant-Pressure Constraint**
At constant $P$, we start from the enthalpy definition $H = U + PV$. Its differential is:
$$dH = T\,dS + V\,dP$$
At constant $P$ ($dP = 0$):
$$dH = T\,dS \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_p = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_p$$
Since the left side is $C_p$, we obtain:
$$C_p = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_p$$

**Step 5: Derive the Famous $C_p - C_v$ Relation**
Subtracting the two expressions:
$$C_p - C_v = T\left[\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_p - \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_v\right]$$

Using the triple-product rule from calculus and a Maxwell relation, this simplifies to:
$$C_p - C_v = T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_p$$

For an **ideal gas** ($PV = nRT$), evaluating the two partial derivatives yields the celebrated result:
$$C_p - C_v = nR \quad \text{(or simply } R \text{ per mole)}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $C_v = T\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_v$ | $C_v$ in terms of entropy | J·K⁻¹ |
| 2 | $C_p = T\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_p$ | $C_p$ in terms of entropy | J·K⁻¹ |
| 3 | $C_p - C_v = T\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v \left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_p$ | General $C_p - C_v$ identity | J·K⁻¹ |
| 4 | $C_p - C_v = nR$ | Ideal-gas relation | J·K⁻¹ |
| 5 | $C_p - C_v = \dfrac{T \beta^2 V}{\kappa_T}$ | Real-fluid form ($\beta$ = thermal expansion coeff., $\kappa_T$ = isothermal compressibility) | J·K⁻¹ |
| 6 | $\gamma = \dfrac{C_p}{C_v}$ | Heat-capacity ratio (adiabatic index) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $dS = \dfrac{C_p}{T}dT - \left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_p dP$ | Entropy change of a pure substance | J·K⁻¹ |
| 8 | $dS = \dfrac{C_v}{T}dT + \left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v dV$ | Alternate entropy differential | J·K⁻¹ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Reactor Design:** $C_p$ of reactant/product streams determines the heat removal rate needed for isothermal operation.
- **Compressor Work:** For isentropic compression, the exit temperature depends directly on $C_p/C_v$.
- **Distillation Column Sizing:** Reboiler and condenser duties are calculated using $C_p$ expressed as a function of entropy tables.
- **Cryogenic Engineering:** Liquefaction of gases (LNG, air separation) relies on accurate $C_p(T)$ integration in the entropy domain.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: $C_v = T(\partial S/\partial T)_v$

**Starting point — Combined First & Second Law:**
$$dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$$

**Step 1:** Recognize that $U$ is fundamentally a function of entropy and volume:
$$U = U(S, V)$$

**Step 2:** Take the total differential:
$$dU = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial S}\right)_v dS + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_s dV$$

**Step 3:** Match coefficients of $dS$ and $dV$ with the combined law $dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$:
$$T = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial S}\right)_v \quad \text{and} \quad -P = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_s$$

**Step 4:** Rewrite $U$ in terms of more practical variables $T$ and $V$ using a Legendre-like transformation. Since $S$ is linked to $T$ via $dQ_{rev} = T\,dS$, we can express:
$$C_v = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_v = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial S}\right)_v \cdot \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_v = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_v$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{\,C_v = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_v\,}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: $C_p = T(\partial S/\partial T)_p$

**Starting point — Enthalpy definition:**
$$H = U + PV$$

**Step 1:** Take the differential:
$$dH = dU + P\,dV + V\,dP$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$:
$$dH = T\,dS - P\,dV + P\,dV + V\,dP = T\,dS + V\,dP$$

**Step 3:** At constant pressure ($dP = 0$):
$$dH = T\,dS$$

**Step 4:** Express $H$ as $H(T,P)$ and take the partial derivative at constant $P$:
$$C_p = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_p = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_p$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{\,C_p = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_p\,}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: $C_p - C_v = T(\partial P/\partial T)_v (\partial V/\partial T)_p$

**Step 1:** Write entropy as a function of $T$ and $V$: $S = S(T, V)$. Its total differential is:
$$dS = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_v dT + \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T dV$$

**Step 2:** Also express $S$ as a function of $T$ and $P$: $S = S(T, P)$. Its total differential is:
$$dS = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_p dT + \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T dP$$

**Step 3:** From the $T,P$ representation, evaluate the partial derivative $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_p$ along a path of constant $P$. But we also want to express this in terms of $V$ variation. Using a cyclic relation:
$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_p = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_v + \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_p$$

**Step 4:** Use the **Maxwell Relation** derived from $dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$:
$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back:
$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_p = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_v + \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_p$$

**Step 6:** Multiply by $T$:
$$T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_p - T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_v = T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_p$$

**Step 7:** Recognize the left side as $C_p - C_v$:

$$\boxed{\,C_p - C_v = T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_p\,}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: $C_p - C_v = nR$ for an Ideal Gas

**Starting point:** Use the previous boxed result with the ideal-gas law $PV = nRT$.

**Step 1:** Compute $\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v$ from $P = \dfrac{nRT}{V}$:
$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v = \frac{nR}{V}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_p$ from $V = \dfrac{nRT}{P}$:
$$\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_p = \frac{nR}{P}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the general identity:
$$C_p - C_v = T \cdot \frac{nR}{V} \cdot \frac{nR}{P} = T \cdot \frac{nR}{V} \cdot \frac{V}{T} = nR$$

**Step 4:** Conclude:
$$C_p - C_v = nR \quad \text{or per mole:} \quad \bar{C}_p - \bar{C}_v = R = 8.314 \text{ J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Putting It All Together

| Path | Starting Equation | Constraint Applied | Resulting Heat Capacity |
|------|-------------------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| Constant Volume | $dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$ | $dV = 0$ | $C_v = T(\partial S/\partial T)_v$ |
| Constant Pressure | $dH = T\,dS + V\,dP$ | $dP = 0$ | $C_p = T(\partial S/\partial T)_p$ |
| Difference (General) | $C_p - C_v$ identity | None | $T(\partial P/\partial T)_v(\partial V/\partial T)_p$ |
| Ideal-Gas Specialization | $PV = nRT$ | Ideal gas | $C_p - C_v = nR$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Heat Capacity in the Thermodynamic Web

The following block diagram illustrates how heat capacity is embedded within the broader network of thermodynamic state functions and how entropy serves as the central bridge.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[First Law: dU = dQ - P dV] --> B[Combined Law: dU = T dS - P dV]
    C[Second Law: dS = dQ_rev / T] --> B
    B --> D[Enthalpy Identity: H = U + P V]
    D --> E[Enthalpy Differential: dH = T dS + V dP]
    B --> F[Constant Volume Path: dV = 0]
    E --> G[Constant Pressure Path: dP = 0]
    F --> H[Cv = T times partial S over partial T at constant V]
    G --> I[Cp = T times partial S over partial T at constant P]
    H --> J[Difference: Cp minus Cv]
    I --> J
    J --> K[General Form: T times partial P over partial T at constant V times partial V over partial T at constant P]
    K --> L[Apply Ideal Gas Law: P V = n R T]
    L --> M[Final Result: Cp minus Cv = n R]
    M --> N[Heat Capacity Ratio gamma = Cp over Cv]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Hierarchy — From Concept to Application

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Heat Capacity] --> B[Constant Volume Cv]
    A --> C[Constant Pressure Cp]
    B --> D[Internal Energy Path]
    C --> E[Enthalpy Path]
    D --> F[Entropy Bridge: dS = Cv dT over T at constant V]
    E --> G[Entropy Bridge: dS = Cp dT over T at constant P]
    F --> H[Derivation of Cp minus Cv]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Engineering Applications]
    I --> I1[Reactor Heat Duty]
    I --> I2[Compressor Design]
    I --> I3[Distillation Reboiler Sizing]
    I --> I4[Cryogenic Liquefaction]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Flow — Inputs, Process, Outputs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Equation of State PVT] --> PROC1[Compute Partial Derivatives]
    IN2[Entropy Model S T P or S T V] --> PROC1
    PROC1 --> PROC2[Apply Maxwell Relations]
    PROC2 --> PROC3[Derive Cv and Cp]
    PROC3 --> OUT1[Heat Capacity Values J per K]
    PROC3 --> OUT2[Heat Capacity Ratio gamma]
    PROC3 --> OUT3[Entropy Change dS]
    OUT1 --> APP1[Heat Exchanger Sizing]
    OUT1 --> APP2[Reactor Energy Balance]
    OUT2 --> APP3[Compressible Flow Analysis]
    OUT3 --> APP4[Isentropic Process Calculations]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1: Define heat capacity at constant pressure in terms of entropy.**
`[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]`

**Model Answer:**
Heat capacity at constant pressure is defined as the rate of change of enthalpy with respect to temperature at constant pressure. In terms of entropy, it is expressed as:
$$C_p = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_p$$
where $T$ is the absolute temperature, $S$ is the entropy, and the subscript $p$ indicates the derivative is evaluated at constant pressure. Its SI unit is J·K⁻¹. **(3 marks)**

---

**Question 2: State the relationship $C_p - C_v = nR$ for an ideal gas. Under what condition does this hold?**
`[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]`

**Model Answer:**
For an ideal gas, the difference between heat capacity at constant pressure and heat capacity at constant volume is equal to the universal gas constant multiplied by the number of moles:
$$C_p - C_v = nR$$
This relation holds strictly under the condition that the gas obeys the ideal gas law $PV = nRT$ and that the heat capacities are evaluated at thermodynamic equilibrium. The relation is independent of temperature and composition for an ideal gas. The value of $R$ is **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> As per the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, the student must answer **one full question** (either Option A or Option B) carrying **14 marks**, typically split into sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

#### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the expression for heat capacity at constant pressure ($C_p$) in terms of entropy. Show all steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the general thermodynamic identity, derive the relation $C_p - C_v = nR$ for an ideal gas. State all assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1:** Start with the combined first and second law of thermodynamics for a closed reversible system:
$$dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$$

**Step 2:** Define enthalpy and take its differential:
$$H = U + PV \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; dH = dU + P\,dV + V\,dP$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the expression for $dU$:
$$dH = T\,dS - P\,dV + P\,dV + V\,dP = T\,dS + V\,dP$$

**Step 4:** Apply the constant-pressure condition $dP = 0$:
$$(dH)_p = T\,(dS)_p$$

**Step 5:** Divide both sides by $dT$ at constant $P$:
$$\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_p = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_p$$

**Step 6:** Recognize the left side as the definition of $C_p$:
$$\boxed{\,C_p = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_p\,}$$

**Final Statement:** This result shows that $C_p$ is proportional to the temperature and the rate at which entropy changes with temperature at constant pressure.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Recall the general identity (which we will derive from Maxwell relations):
$$C_p - C_v = T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_p$$

**Step 2:** Apply the ideal-gas law $PV = nRT$:

**Step 3:** At constant $V$: $P = nRT/V$, so:
$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v = \frac{nR}{V}$$

**Step 4:** At constant $P$: $V = nRT/P$, so:
$$\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_p = \frac{nR}{P}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute:
$$C_p - C_v = T \cdot \frac{nR}{V} \cdot \frac{nR}{P} = T \cdot \frac{nR}{V} \cdot \frac{V}{T} = nR$$

**Assumptions used:**
1. The gas obeys the ideal gas equation $PV = nRT$.
2. The process is quasi-equilibrium (reversible).
3. The system is closed with a fixed number of moles.
4. $C_p$ and $C_v$ are evaluated at the same temperature.

**Final Result:** $C_p - C_v = nR = 8.314\,n$ J·K⁻¹.

---

#### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Derive the expression for heat capacity at constant volume ($C_v$) in terms of entropy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an ideal gas with $\bar{C}_v = \dfrac{3}{2}R$ per mole, calculate the molar heat capacity at constant pressure, the heat-capacity ratio $\gamma$, and the entropy change when 2 mol of the gas is heated from 300 K to 500 K at constant pressure. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1:** Start from the combined law:
$$dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that $U$ is a state function: $U = U(T, V)$.

**Step 3:** Total differential:
$$dU = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_v dT + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T dV$$

**Step 4:** Also write $U$ in terms of $S$ and $V$:
$$dU = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial S}\right)_v dS + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_s dV = T\,dS - P\,dV$$

**Step 5:** Compare coefficients and connect with $C_v$:
$$C_v = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_v$$

**Step 6:** Chain rule transformation:
$$C_v = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial S}\right)_v \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_v = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_v$$

$$\boxed{\,C_v = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_v\,}$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** Monatomic ideal gas, $\bar{C}_v = \dfrac{3}{2}R$, $n = 2$ mol, $T_1 = 300$ K, $T_2 = 500$ K, constant pressure process.

**Step 1:** Molar heat capacity at constant pressure:
$$\bar{C}_p = \bar{C}_v + R = \frac{3}{2}R + R = \frac{5}{2}R$$
$$\bar{C}_p = \frac{5}{2} \times 8.314 = 20.785 \text{ J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$$

**Step 2:** Heat-capacity ratio:
$$\gamma = \frac{\bar{C}_p}{\bar{C}_v} = \frac{(5/2)R}{(3/2)R} = \frac{5}{3} \approx 1.667$$

**Step 3:** Entropy change at constant pressure:
$$\Delta S = n\bar{C}_p \ln\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) = 2 \times 20.785 \times \ln\left(\frac{500}{300}\right)$$
$$\Delta S = 41.57 \times \ln(1.6667) = 41.57 \times 0.5108$$
$$\boxed{\,\Delta S \approx 21.24 \text{ J·K}^{-1}\,}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Missing the Maxwell Relation:** Many students skip the step $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v$ in deriving $C_p - C_v$ and lose **3 marks** outright.
> 2. **Forgetting the "Constant" subscript:** Writing $C_p = T(\partial S/\partial T)$ without the $p$ subscript is treated as a major error — KTU examiners deduct **2 marks** for this oversight.
> 3. **Algebra slip in ideal-gas substitution:** Students often write $\dfrac{nR}{V} \cdot \dfrac{nR}{P}$ and then forget that $P = nRT/V$ (or vice versa). Carefully cancel terms to get $nR$ — losing a factor of $n$ is a **frequent 2-mark penalty**.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing J·K⁻¹ with kJ·K⁻¹ in the final numerical answer of a Part B question is a guaranteed **1-mark deduction**.
> 5. **Skipping assumptions:** In any derivation of $C_p - C_v = nR$, you **must** explicitly state that the gas is ideal and obeys $PV = nRT$. Missing this costs **1–2 marks**.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Result 1:** $C_v = T\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_v$ — derived from $dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$ with $dV = 0$.
- **Core Result 2:** $C_p = T\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_p$ — derived from $dH = T\,dS + V\,dP$ with $dP = 0$.
- **Difference Identity:** $C_p - C_v = T\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v \left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_p$ — general for all pure substances.
- **Ideal-Gas Specialization:** $C_p - C_v = nR = 8.314\,n$ J·K⁻¹.
- **Maxwell Relation Used:** $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v$ — must be quoted in any KTU derivation.
- **Engineering Applications:** Reactor heat duty, compressor work, distillation column design, cryogenic liquefaction, nozzle flow.
- **Units to remember:** Heat capacity in **J·K⁻¹**, molar heat capacity in **J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**, specific heat in **J·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹**, entropy in **J·K⁻¹**.
- **$R$ value:** **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** — memorize it cold.
- **The "Why" question to always be ready for:** *Why is $C_p > C_v$?* → At constant pressure, some heat goes into expansion work $P\,dV$, so more heat is needed for the same temperature rise.
- **Heat-capacity ratio $\gamma = C_p/C_v$:** Always dimensionless; for monatomic ideal gas, $\gamma = 5/3$; for diatomic, $\gamma = 7/5$.
- **Always show the Maxwell relation step explicitly** in KTU 2024 Scheme derivations — it is a high-weightage checkpoint for the examiner.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equation of State of Gases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Equation of State of Gases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Equation of State (EOS)** is a thermodynamic constitutive relation that expresses the functional relationship among the macroscopic state variables of a pure substance or mixture — most commonly **pressure (P)**, **temperature (T)**, and **molar volume (V̂)**. In its most general analytical form, an EOS is written as:

$$f(P, V̂, T) = 0$$

For a pure component, the canonical form is:

$$P = P(T, V̂)$$

This means the pressure of a homogeneous fluid at thermodynamic equilibrium is a state function of temperature and molar volume alone. The simplest and most historically significant EOS is the **Ideal Gas Equation of State**, derived from a combination of Boyle's Law, Charles's Law, and Avogadro's Hypothesis:

$$PV = nRT$$

where:
- **P** = absolute pressure (Pa, kPa, bar, or atm)
- **V** = total volume of the gas (m³, L)
- **n** = number of moles (kmol or mol)
- **R** = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** = **0.08314 L·bar/(mol·K)** = **0.08206 L·atm/(mol·K)**
- **T** = absolute temperature (K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Equation of State is the foundational building block of Module 3. Every subsequent topic — P-V-T behaviour, residual properties, fugacity, vapour-liquid equilibrium — depends on the choice and accuracy of the EOS. Students must master both ideal and real gas EOS derivations.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **balloon filled with gas molecules** inside a closed box. The molecules are like tiny, hyperactive billiard balls zipping around in every direction. Each ball collides with the balloon walls — and the **collective force** of these trillions of collisions per second is what we measure as **pressure**.

Now, think of the gas molecules from two competing perspectives:

1. **The "Ideal Student" (Ideal Gas):** Imagine students in a huge, empty auditorium. They move freely, never bump into each other, and the size of each student is negligible compared to the auditorium. They are also non-attractive — they don't pull each other closer. This is the **ideal gas assumption**: molecules are point masses with no intermolecular forces and zero volume.

2. **The "Real Student" (Real Gas):** Now imagine the same students in a **crowded college corridor during lunch break**. They have actual size (they take up space), and they pull/push each other through attractive and repulsive forces. The corridor is smaller relative to the student size, and behaviours deviate from the "ideal auditorium" model.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we need real gas equations?** Because the ideal gas law is accurate **only at low pressures and high temperatures**. As pressure rises or temperature drops (especially near the **critical point** or during **condensation**), real gases deviate significantly. Chemical engineers need accurate P-V-T relationships to design compressors, expanders, distillation columns, pipelines, and reactors safely.

---

## 1.3 The Need for Equations of State — Physical Constants & Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value | Units |
|----------|--------|---------------|-------|
| Universal Gas Constant | R | **8.314** | J/(mol·K) |
| Standard Temperature | T° | **273.15** | K |
| Standard Pressure | P° | **101.325** | kPa = 1 atm |
| Critical Compressibility Factor (most gases) | Z_c | **0.27 – 0.29** | dimensionless |
| Boltzmann's Constant | k_B | **1.381 × 10⁻²³** | J/K |
| Avogadro's Number | N_A | **6.022 × 10²³** | molecules/mol |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Insight:** In KTU 2024 Scheme, numerical problems frequently require consistent SI units (Pa, m³, K, mol). Always convert given data to SI before applying the EOS, or use a value of R whose units match the input data.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ideal Gas Equation — Origin and Limitations

The **ideal gas law** emerges from three empirical laws combined with Avogadro's principle:

- **Boyle's Law:** $P \propto \dfrac{1}{V}$ at constant $T, n$
- **Charles's Law:** $V \propto T$ at constant $P, n$
- **Avogadro's Law:** $V \propto n$ at constant $T, P$

Combining these: $V \propto \dfrac{nT}{P}$, which gives $PV = nRT$.

### Underlying Molecular Assumptions of the Ideal Gas:
1. Molecules occupy **negligible volume** (treated as point masses).
2. **No intermolecular forces** exist (no attraction, no repulsion).
3. Collisions are perfectly **elastic**.
4. Molecules are in **constant random motion** (kinetic theory).

### Where the Ideal Gas Fails:
- **High pressure** (molecular volume becomes non-negligible).
- **Low temperature** (intermolecular forces become significant).
- **Near phase transitions** (condensation, critical region).

---

## 2.2 Real Gas Equations of State — The Modern Toolkit

Real gases are modelled by introducing two corrections to the ideal gas model:
- **Volume correction** (excluded volume / co-volume, *b*)
- **Pressure correction** (intermolecular attraction, *a*)

The general cubic form is:

$$P = \frac{RT}{V̂ - b} - \frac{a(T)}{f(V̂)}$$

where $f(V̂)$ depends on the specific EOS.

### 2.2.1 Van der Waals Equation (1873)

$$\left(P + \frac{a}{V̂^2}\right)(V̂ - b) = RT$$

- **a** = attraction parameter = $\dfrac{27 R^2 T_c^2}{64 P_c}$
- **b** = co-volume = $\dfrac{R T_c}{8 P_c}$

### 2.2.2 Redlich-Kwong Equation (1949)

$$P = \frac{RT}{V̂ - b} - \frac{a}{\sqrt{T} \, V̂ (V̂ + b)}$$

- $a = \dfrac{0.42748 \, R^2 T_c^{2.5}}{P_c}$
- $b = \dfrac{0.08664 \, R T_c}{P_c}$

### 2.2.3 Soave-Redlich-Kwong (SRK) Equation (1972)

$$P = \frac{RT}{V̂ - b} - \frac{a(T)}{V̂(V̂ + b)}$$

- $a(T) = a_c \, \alpha(T_r, \omega)$
- $a_c = \dfrac{0.42748 \, R^2 T_c^2}{P_c}$
- $b = \dfrac{0.08664 \, R T_c}{P_c}$
- $\alpha = \left[1 + m\left(1 - \sqrt{T_r}\right)\right]^2$
- $m = 0.480 + 1.574\omega - 0.176\omega^2$

### 2.2.4 Peng-Robinson Equation (1976)

$$P = \frac{RT}{V̂ - b} - \frac{a(T)}{V̂(V̂ + b) + b(V̂ - b)}$$

- $a_c = \dfrac{0.45724 \, R^2 T_c^2}{P_c}$
- $b = \dfrac{0.07780 \, R T_c}{P_c}$
- $m = 0.37464 + 1.54226\omega - 0.26992\omega^2$

### 2.2.5 Virial Equation (Truncated)

$$\frac{PV̂}{RT} = Z = 1 + \frac{B(T)}{V̂} + \frac{C(T)}{V̂^2} + \cdots$$

- **B(T)** = second virial coefficient (pair interactions)
- **C(T)** = third virial coefficient (triplet interactions)

### 2.2.6 Compressibility Factor (Z)

$$Z = \frac{PV̂}{RT}$$

- Z = 1 → ideal gas
- Z < 1 → attractive forces dominate (typical at moderate T)
- Z > 1 → repulsive forces dominate (typical at very high P)

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation of State | Formula | Constants (in terms of T_c, P_c, ω) | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ideal Gas | $PV̂ = RT$ | None | Low P, high T |
| Van der Waals | $\left(P + \dfrac{a}{V̂^2}\right)(V̂ - b) = RT$ | $a = \dfrac{27 R^2 T_c^2}{64 P_c}$, $b = \dfrac{R T_c}{8 P_c}$ | Qualitative; vapour-liquid |
| Redlich-Kwong | $P = \dfrac{RT}{V̂-b} - \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{T} \, V̂(V̂+b)}$ | $a = \dfrac{0.42748 R^2 T_c^{2.5}}{P_c}$, $b = \dfrac{0.08664 R T_c}{P_c}$ | Hydrocarbons, gas-phase PVT |
| Soave-Redlich-Kwong (SRK) | $P = \dfrac{RT}{V̂-b} - \dfrac{a\alpha}{V̂(V̂+b)}$ | $a = 0.42748 \dfrac{R^2 T_c^2}{P_c}$, $b = 0.08664 \dfrac{R T_c}{P_c}$ | Vapour-liquid equilibrium |
| Peng-Robinson (PR) | $P = \dfrac{RT}{V̂-b} - \dfrac{a\alpha}{V̂(V̂+b)+b(V̂-b)}$ | $a = 0.45724 \dfrac{R^2 T_c^2}{P_c}$, $b = 0.07780 \dfrac{R T_c}{P_c}$ | Liquid + Vapour phases |
| Virial (truncated) | $Z = 1 + \dfrac{B}{V̂} + \dfrac{C}{V̂^2}$ | Empirical virial coefficients | Moderate P, dilute gases |
| Compressibility | $Z = \dfrac{PV̂}{RT}$ | Read from generalized charts | Any fluid, charts-based |

| Reduced Property | Definition | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Reduced Temperature | $T_r = \dfrac{T}{T_c}$ | How far T is from critical T |
| Reduced Pressure | $P_r = \dfrac{P}{P_c}$ | How far P is from critical P |
| Reduced Volume | $V_r = \dfrac{V̂}{V̂_c}$ | How far V is from critical V |
| Acentric Factor | $\omega$ | Deviation from simple-fluid behaviour |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why EOS matters |
|---|---|
| **Compressor Design** | Accurate discharge T requires precise V̂ (via EOS) |
| **Pipeline Flow** | Compressibility factor Z corrects volumetric flow to standard conditions |
| **Distillation Columns** | SRK and PR predict K-values for VLE calculations |
| **LNG/CNG Storage** | Critical-region accuracy is vital for cryogenic fluids |
| **Reactor Design** | Conversion depends on real molar concentrations, not ideal |
| **Safety Relief Sizing** | Two-phase flashing requires accurate PVT near saturation |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Ideal Gas Law from Kinetic Theory

### Starting Premise:
Consider **N** spherical molecules of mass **m** confined in a cubic box of side length **L** at temperature **T**.

### Step 1: Pressure from Momentum Transfer
The pressure exerted by gas molecules on a wall is the rate of momentum change per unit area. For a single molecule moving with velocity component $c_x$ normal to a wall:

$$\Delta p_x = 2 m c_x \quad \text{(per collision)}$$

The time between successive collisions with the same wall is $\Delta t = \dfrac{2L}{c_x}$.

Therefore, the average force on the wall due to this molecule is:

$$F_{1} = \frac{\Delta p_x}{\Delta t} = \frac{2 m c_x}{2L / c_x} = \frac{m c_x^2}{L}$$

### Step 2: Total Force from N Molecules
Summing over all N molecules:

$$F = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{m c_{x,i}^2}{L} = \frac{N m \overline{c_x^2}}{L}$$

where $\overline{c_x^2}$ is the mean square x-component of velocity. By isotropy, $\overline{c_x^2} = \overline{c_y^2} = \overline{c_z^2} = \dfrac{\overline{c^2}}{3}$, where $\overline{c^2}$ is the mean square speed.

### Step 3: Pressure as Force per Unit Area
Wall area is $A = L^2$, so:

$$P = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{N m \overline{c^2}}{3 L^3} = \frac{N m \overline{c^2}}{3 V}$$

### Step 4: Link to Temperature via Kinetic Energy
The average translational kinetic energy per molecule is:

$$\bar{E}_K = \frac{1}{2} m \overline{c^2} = \frac{3}{2} k_B T$$

Solving for $m \overline{c^2}$:

$$m \overline{c^2} = 3 k_B T$$

### Step 5: Substituting Back

$$P = \frac{N \cdot 3 k_B T}{3 V} = \frac{N k_B T}{V}$$

### Step 6: Converting to Moles
Using $N = n N_A$ and $R = N_A k_B$:

$$P = \frac{n N_A k_B T}{V} = \frac{n R T}{V}$$

Rearranging gives the **Ideal Gas Law**:

$$\boxed{PV = nRT}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** The ideal gas law shows that pressure is proportional to the **number of molecules per unit volume** and to the **average kinetic energy per molecule** (which is itself proportional to T).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Van der Waals Equation

### Step 1: Address the Volume Correction
Ideal gas assumes molecules have **zero volume**, so the available volume for molecular motion is the entire container volume V. In reality, each molecule of finite diameter **σ** excludes a volume around it. The excluded volume per molecule is $\dfrac{4}{3}\pi \sigma^3$, but the excluded volume per **pair** is $\dfrac{2}{3}\pi \sigma^3$. Summing over all pairs:

$$b = \frac{2}{3} \pi N_A \sigma^3 \quad \text{(co-volume per mole)}$$

Replacing V by $(V̂ - b)$ in the ideal gas law accounts for this:

$$P = \frac{RT}{V̂ - b} \quad \text{(repulsion-corrected)}$$

### Step 2: Address the Attraction Correction
Attractive forces between molecules reduce the pressure exerted on the walls (molecules near the wall are pulled inward). The reduction is proportional to:
- The **density of molecules** in the bulk (∝ 1/V̂)
- The **density of molecules** at the wall (∝ 1/V̂)

Thus the pressure correction is $\dfrac{a}{V̂^2}$. The observed (true) pressure is therefore lower than the ideal:

$$P = \frac{RT}{V̂ - b} - \frac{a}{V̂^2}$$

### Step 3: Final Form
Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\left(P + \frac{a}{V̂^2}\right)(V̂ - b) = RT}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Constants of Van der Waals EOS from Critical Point

At the **critical point**, the isotherm has an **inflection point**, meaning both first and second derivatives of P with respect to V̂ vanish:

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial V̂}\right)_{T_c} = 0, \quad \left(\frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial V̂^2}\right)_{T_c} = 0$$

### Step 1: Expanded Form of VdW
Multiplying out:

$$P = \frac{RT}{V̂ - b} - \frac{a}{V̂^2}$$

### Step 2: First Derivative

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial V̂}\right)_T = -\frac{RT}{(V̂ - b)^2} + \frac{2a}{V̂^3}$$

Setting this to zero at the critical point:

$$\frac{RT_c}{(V̂_c - b)^2} = \frac{2a}{V̂_c^3} \quad \text{...(i)}$$

### Step 3: Second Derivative

$$\left(\frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial V̂^2}\right)_T = \frac{2RT}{(V̂ - b)^3} - \frac{6a}{V̂^4}$$

Setting to zero at the critical point:

$$\frac{2RT_c}{(V̂_c - b)^3} = \frac{6a}{V̂_c^4} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

### Step 4: Dividing (ii) by (i)

$$\frac{2}{V̂_c - b} = \frac{3}{V̂_c}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$2 V̂_c = 3 (V̂_c - b) \implies 2V̂_c = 3V̂_c - 3b \implies V̂_c = 3b$$

### Step 5: Substituting Back into (i)

$$\frac{RT_c}{(3b - b)^2} = \frac{2a}{(3b)^3} \implies \frac{RT_c}{4b^2} = \frac{2a}{27b^3}$$

Simplifying:

$$\frac{RT_c}{4b^2} = \frac{2a}{27b^3} \implies RT_c = \frac{8a}{27b} \implies a = \frac{27 R T_c b}{8}$$

### Step 6: Substituting V̂_c = 3b Back into VdW at Critical Point

$$P_c = \frac{RT_c}{3b - b} - \frac{a}{(3b)^2} = \frac{RT_c}{2b} - \frac{a}{9b^2}$$

Substituting $a = \dfrac{27 R T_c b}{8}$:

$$P_c = \frac{RT_c}{2b} - \frac{27 R T_c b}{8 \cdot 9b^2} = \frac{RT_c}{2b} - \frac{3 R T_c}{8b} = \frac{4RT_c - 3RT_c}{8b} = \frac{RT_c}{8b}$$

### Step 7: Final Expressions

$$\boxed{b = \frac{R T_c}{8 P_c}, \quad a = \frac{27 R^2 T_c^2}{64 P_c}, \quad V̂_c = 3b = \frac{3RT_c}{8P_c}}$$

The **critical compressibility factor** for VdW is:

$$Z_c^{VdW} = \frac{P_c V̂_c}{R T_c} = \frac{P_c \cdot 3b}{R T_c} = \frac{P_c \cdot 3 \cdot RT_c/(8P_c)}{RT_c} = \frac{3}{8} = 0.375$$

This is higher than the experimental value of 0.27–0.29 for most real gases, indicating VdW's limitation.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Compressibility Factor and Reduced Form

### Definition:
By definition, the **compressibility factor** is:

$$Z = \frac{PV̂}{RT}$$

For an ideal gas, Z = 1. For real gases, Z deviates from 1.

### Compressibility from Van der Waals:

$$Z = \frac{PV̂}{RT} = \frac{V̂}{V̂ - b} - \frac{a}{RTV̂}$$

### Reduced Form (Principle of Corresponding States):
Dividing P, T, V̂ by their critical values:

$$P_r = \frac{P}{P_c}, \quad T_r = \frac{T}{T_c}, \quad V̂_r = \frac{V̂}{V̂_c}$$

Substituting into VdW and using the critical constant expressions for *a* and *b*:

$$\boxed{\left(P_r + \frac{3}{V̂_r^2}\right)\left(V̂_r - \frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{8 T_r}{3}}$$

This is a **universal equation** — independent of the substance! All substances obeying VdW will collapse onto the same reduced PVT surface. This is the **Two-Parameter Principle of Corresponding States**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The Law of Corresponding States underpins the **generalized compressibility charts** (Z vs. P_r and T_r). The acentric factor ω adds a third parameter to handle substances that deviate from the simple-fluid curve (e.g., H₂O, NH₃, HF).

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A 0.5 m³ rigid tank contains 2 kmol of methane at 300 K. Using the Van der Waals EOS, estimate the pressure inside the tank. Compare with the ideal gas prediction.

**Given:**
- $T = 300$ K, $V = 0.5$ m³, $n = 2$ kmol
- For methane: $T_c = 190.6$ K, $P_c = 4.64$ MPa
- $R = 8.314$ kJ/(kmol·K) = 8.314 kPa·m³/(kmol·K)

### Step 1: Molar Volume

$$V̂ = \frac{V}{n} = \frac{0.5}{2} = 0.25 \text{ m}^3/\text{kmol}$$

### Step 2: Van der Waals Constants

$$b = \frac{RT_c}{8 P_c} = \frac{8.314 \times 190.6}{8 \times 4640} = \frac{1584.6}{37120} = 0.04269 \text{ m}^3/\text{kmol}$$

$$a = \frac{27 R^2 T_c^2}{64 P_c} = \frac{27 \times (8.314)^2 \times (190.6)^2}{64 \times 4640}$$

Calculating numerator: $27 \times 69.12 \times 36328.36 = 27 \times 2{,}510{,}722 = 67{,}789{,}494$

$$a = \frac{67{,}789{,}494}{296{,}960} = 228.28 \text{ kPa·m}^6/\text{kmol}^2$$

### Step 3: Apply VdW Equation

$$P = \frac{RT}{V̂ - b} - \frac{a}{V̂^2} = \frac{8.314 \times 300}{0.25 - 0.04269} - \frac{228.28}{(0.25)^2}$$

$$P = \frac{2494.2}{0.20731} - \frac{228.28}{0.0625} = 12{,}031.6 - 3652.5$$

$$\boxed{P_{VdW} = 8379.1 \text{ kPa} = 8.38 \text{ MPa}}$$

### Step 4: Compare with Ideal Gas

$$P_{ideal} = \frac{RT}{V̂} = \frac{8.314 \times 300}{0.25} = \frac{2494.2}{0.25} = 9976.8 \text{ kPa} = 9.98 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 5: Analysis
- VdW predicts a lower pressure than the ideal gas law.
- This is because the **attractive forces** pull molecules inward, reducing wall impact frequency.
- The **repulsion effect** (V̂ − b term) is partially offsetting, but the net effect here is attractive-dominant at 300 K for methane.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Evolution of Equations of State (Hierarchy Flow)

This Mermaid diagram maps the conceptual progression of EOS, from the simplest ideal model to the most advanced cubic and corresponding-states models.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Need PVT Relation] --> B[Kinetic Theory of Gases]
    B --> C[Ideal Gas Law: PV = nRT]
    C --> D{Real Gas Behavior?}
    D -->|No, dilute at high T| E[Use Ideal Gas Law]
    D -->|Yes, near saturation or critical| F[Introduce Volume and Attraction Corrections]
    F --> G[Van der Waals EOS 1873]
    G --> H[Redlich Kwong EOS 1949]
    H --> I[Soave Redlich Kwong SRK 1972]
    I --> J[Peng Robinson EOS 1976]
    F --> K[Virial Equation: Statistical Mechanics]
    G --> L[Principle of Corresponding States]
    L --> M[Generalized Compressibility Charts Z]
    M --> N[Acentric Factor Extension]
    N --> I
    N --> J
```

---

## 4.2 Cubic Equation Block Architecture

This block diagram shows the generic cubic form of an EOS and the role of each term.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input1[Inputs]
        X1[Temperature T]
        X2[Molar Volume V]
        X3[Critical Constants Tc, Pc]
        X4[Acentric Factor omega]
    end
    X3 --> Y1[Compute Constants a and b]
    X4 --> Y1
    Y1 --> Y2[Compute alpha function of Tr and omega]
    X1 --> Y2
    Y2 --> Z1[Repulsive Term RT over V minus b]
    X2 --> Z1
    Y2 --> Z2[Attractive Term a alpha over V Function]
    X2 --> Z2
    Z1 --> W1[Pressure P]
    Z2 --> W1
    W1 --> Out[Output: P, or solve for V given P and T]
```

---

## 4.3 P-V Diagram Showing the Isotherm Behaviour

This is a schematic representation of P-V isotherms for a pure substance, showing subcritical, critical, and supercritical behaviour relevant to the cubic EOS.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Subcritical[T below Tc]
        S1[Low T Isotherm] --> S2[Liquid Branch High Density]
        S2 --> S3[Two Phase Region: P = P_sat constant]
        S3 --> S4[Vapour Branch Low Density]
    end
    subgraph Critical[T equals Tc]
        C1[Critical Isotherm] --> C2[Inflection Point at Critical Point]
        C2 --> C3[Cubic EOS passes through critical point]
    end
    subgraph Supercritical[T greater than Tc]
        SC1[High T Isotherm] --> SC2[No Phase Transition]
        SC2 --> SC3[Continuous Fluid Region]
    end
    Subcritical --> Critical
    Critical --> Supercritical
```

---

## 4.4 Decision Tree for Selecting an EOS in Chemical Engineering

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose an EOS] --> Q1{Polar or Associating substance?}
    Q1 -->|Yes water, ammonia, alcohols| PR1[Use Peng Robinson or SAFT]
    Q1 -->|No hydrocarbons, nonpolar| Q2{Polar VLE needed?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| SRK1[Use SRK or PR with mixing rules]
    Q2 -->|No, gas phase only| Q3{Is P less than 10 bar?}
    Q3 -->|Yes, low pressure| IDEAL[Use Ideal Gas Law]
    Q3 -->|No, moderate to high P| RK1[Use Redlich Kwong]
    PR1 --> End[Selected EOS]
    SRK1 --> End
    IDEAL --> End
    RK1 --> End
```

---

## 4.5 Cross-Comparison Schematic: P-V Curves for Different EOS

This block-level flow visualizes how each EOS approximates a real P-V isotherm differently from the ideal gas line.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Real[Real Gas PVT Data]
        R1[Experimental Data Points]
    end
    subgraph IdealApprox[Ideal Gas Approximation]
        I1[Straight Isotherm: P equals RT over V]
    end
    subgraph VdWApprox[Van der Waals Approximation]
        V1[Hyperbola: Corrected for V and a]
    end
    subgraph RKApprox[Redlich Kwong Approximation]
        RK1[Improved at high T: Temperature dependent a]
    end
    subgraph PRApprox[Peng Robinson Approximation]
        P1[Best near critical point and liquids]
    end
    Real --> I1
    Real --> V1
    Real --> RK1
    Real --> P1
    I1 --> Compare[Compare with Real: which is closest?]
    V1 --> Compare
    RK1 --> Compare
    P1 --> Compare
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**Define the term "Equation of State." Mention its significance in chemical engineering calculations.**

**Model Answer:**

An **Equation of State (EOS)** is a mathematical relationship that relates the state variables pressure (P), temperature (T), and molar volume (V̂) of a pure substance or mixture in thermodynamic equilibrium. It has the general form $f(P, V̂, T) = 0$.

**Significance in Chemical Engineering:**

1. Enables accurate calculation of fluid densities at operating conditions — vital for sizing pumps, compressors, pipelines, and storage tanks.
2. Forms the basis for thermodynamic property estimation such as enthalpy, entropy, and fugacity.
3. Predicts vapour-liquid equilibria (VLE) for distillation and separation processes.
4. Critical for safety analysis in pressure relief and two-phase flow systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Pattern Tip:** 3-mark questions expect **definition + 2 to 3 crisp points** of application. Avoid lengthy derivations; focus on conceptual clarity.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]**

**State the Van der Waals equation of state. How are the constants a and b related to the critical constants?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Van der Waals equation** for one mole of a real gas is:

$$\left(P + \frac{a}{V̂^2}\right)(V̂ - b) = RT$$

The constants are expressed in terms of critical temperature (T_c) and critical pressure (P_c) as:

$$a = \frac{27 R^2 T_c^2}{64 P_c}, \quad b = \frac{R T_c}{8 P_c}$$

The parameter **a** corrects for **attractive intermolecular forces** (units: Pa·m⁶/mol² or kPa·m⁶/kmol²), while **b** is the **co-volume** representing the excluded volume of the molecules (units: m³/mol or m³/kmol).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**(a) Derive the Van der Waals equation of state starting from the ideal gas law. Explain the physical significance of the constants a and b. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Starting from the ideal gas law:**
For 1 mole, $PV̂ = RT$.

**Step 2: Volume correction (co-volume):**
Real molecules have a finite volume. Each molecule excludes a spherical volume of radius equal to the molecular diameter σ from other molecules. For a pair, the excluded volume is $\frac{2}{3}\pi \sigma^3$. Summing over all pairs, the **effective available volume** is reduced by **b**:

$$V̂_{\text{available}} = V̂ - b$$

**Step 3: Pressure correction (intermolecular attraction):**
Attractive forces between molecules near a wall pull them inward, **reducing the impact pressure** on the wall. The pressure reduction is proportional to (density of bulk molecules) × (density of wall molecules) ∝ $\frac{1}{V̂} \cdot \frac{1}{V̂} = \frac{1}{V̂^2}$. The proportionality constant is **a**.

**Step 4: Combining the corrections:**

$$\boxed{\left(P + \frac{a}{V̂^2}\right)(V̂ - b) = RT}$$

**Physical Significance:**

| Constant | Physical Meaning | Effect on P |
|----------|------------------|-------------|
| **a** | Attractive intermolecular force parameter | Decreases P (attractive pull) |
| **b** | Excluded volume per mole (co-volume) | Increases P (less free space) |

**(b) A gas has critical constants T_c = 405.5 K and P_c = 11.28 MPa. Calculate the Van der Waals constants a and b. If 2 kmol of this gas is confined in a 0.1 m³ vessel at 500 K, estimate the pressure using (i) the ideal gas law, and (ii) the Van der Waals equation. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $T_c = 405.5$ K, $P_c = 11.28$ MPa = 11,280 kPa, $R = 8.314$ kPa·m³/(kmol·K), $T = 500$ K, $V = 0.1$ m³, $n = 2$ kmol.

**Step 1: Calculate a and b:**

$$b = \frac{RT_c}{8P_c} = \frac{8.314 \times 405.5}{8 \times 11280} = \frac{3371.33}{90240} = 0.03736 \text{ m}^3/\text{kmol}$$

$$a = \frac{27 R^2 T_c^2}{64 P_c} = \frac{27 \times (8.314)^2 \times (405.5)^2}{64 \times 11280}$$

Calculating step by step:
- $R^2 = 69.122$
- $T_c^2 = 164{,}430.25$
- Numerator: $27 \times 69.122 \times 164{,}430.25 = 27 \times 11{,}365{,}071 = 306{,}856{,}917$

$$a = \frac{306{,}856{,}917}{721{,}920} = 425.08 \text{ kPa·m}^6/\text{kmol}^2$$

**Step 2: Molar Volume**

$$V̂ = \frac{0.1}{2} = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{kmol}$$

**Step 3: Ideal Gas Pressure**

$$P_{ideal} = \frac{nRT}{V} = \frac{2 \times 8.314 \times 500}{0.1} = \frac{8314}{0.1} = 83{,}140 \text{ kPa} = 83.14 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Van der Waals Pressure**

$$P = \frac{RT}{V̂ - b} - \frac{a}{V̂^2} = \frac{8.314 \times 500}{0.05 - 0.03736} - \frac{425.08}{(0.05)^2}$$

$$P = \frac{4157}{0.01264} - \frac{425.08}{0.0025} = 328{,}878 - 170{,}032$$

$$P_{VdW} = 158{,}846 \text{ kPa} = 158.85 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5: Comparison and Discussion**

| Method | Pressure (MPa) |
|--------|----------------|
| Ideal Gas | 83.14 |
| Van der Waals | 158.85 |
| Deviation | ~91% |

**Discussion:** At such an extremely high reduced pressure ($P_r \approx 14$), the ideal gas law severely underestimates the pressure because repulsive forces (molecular volume) dominate. VdW partially accounts for this but the deviation between VdW and reality is still significant in this region.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]**

**(a) What is the compressibility factor Z? Explain its physical significance and state the principle of corresponding states. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of Compressibility Factor:**

The **compressibility factor Z** is defined as the ratio of the actual molar volume of a real gas to the molar volume predicted by the ideal gas law at the same T and P:

$$\boxed{Z = \frac{PV̂}{RT}}$$

**Physical Significance:**

- Z = 1 → ideal gas behaviour (no deviation).
- Z < 1 → attractive forces dominate; gas is more compressible than ideal.
- Z > 1 → repulsive forces dominate; gas is less compressible than ideal.

**Principle of Corresponding States:**

The principle states that **all fluids, when expressed in terms of reduced properties** ($P_r, T_r, V̂_r$), exhibit approximately the same PVT behaviour. The reduced properties are:

$$P_r = \frac{P}{P_c}, \quad T_r = \frac{T}{T_c}, \quad V̂_r = \frac{V̂}{V̂_c}$$

Therefore, Z is a universal function of $P_r$ and $T_r$:

$$Z = Z(P_r, T_r)$$

This **two-parameter corresponding states principle** is the basis of the **Lee-Kesler and generalized compressibility charts** used in chemical engineering practice.

**Limitation:** It assumes Z depends only on $P_r$ and $T_r$. To improve accuracy for polar and hydrogen-bonding substances, a third parameter — the **acentric factor ω** — is introduced (Pitzer's three-parameter corresponding states).

---

**(b) Steam at 600 K and 2 MPa is to be transported through a pipeline. Estimate the specific volume of steam using (i) the ideal gas law, and (ii) the compressibility chart (generalized Z method). For water, T_c = 647.1 K and P_c = 22.06 MPa. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $T = 600$ K, $P = 2$ MPa, $T_c = 647.1$ K, $P_c = 22.06$ MPa, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) = 0.008314 kJ/(mol·K), molecular weight of water = 18.015 g/mol.

**Specific gas constant:**

$$R_{\text{sp}} = \frac{R}{M} = \frac{8.314}{18.015 \times 10^{-3}} = 461.5 \text{ J/(kg·K)} = 0.4615 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$

**Step 1: Reduced properties**

$$T_r = \frac{600}{647.1} = 0.927$$

$$P_r = \frac{2}{22.06} = 0.0907$$

**Step 2: Compressibility factor from chart**

Using the generalized compressibility chart at $T_r = 0.93$ and $P_r = 0.09$:

$$Z \approx 0.98 \text{ (approximately)}$$

(Students should consult the standard Lee-Kesler or generalized Z chart; the value lies in the range 0.96 to 0.99 for these reduced conditions.)

**Step 3: Ideal gas specific volume**

$$v_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{R_{\text{sp}} T}{P} = \frac{0.4615 \times 600}{2000} = \frac{276.9}{2000} = 0.1385 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Step 4: Real specific volume using Z**

$$v_{\text{real}} = Z \cdot v_{\text{ideal}} = 0.98 \times 0.1385 = 0.1357 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Step 5: Analysis**

| Method | Specific Volume (m³/kg) |
|--------|--------------------------|
| Ideal Gas | 0.1385 |
| Compressibility (Z method) | 0.1357 |
| Deviation | ~2% |

**Discussion:** At $T_r = 0.93$ and $P_r = 0.09$, the deviation from ideal gas is small (~2%) because the conditions are far from the critical point. The ideal gas law gives a reasonable estimate, but the Z method is more accurate for engineering design purposes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (2 to 3 marks lost):** Students frequently mix units — using P in atm with R in J/(mol·K). Always verify that R's units match the input units of P, V, and T.
> 2. **Confusing T with T_c:** Reduced temperature is $T_r = T/T_c$, **not** T − T_c. Read the question carefully.
> 3. **Van der Waals a, b Units:** The Van der Waals constant *a* has units of (pressure)(volume)² — e.g., kPa·m⁶/kmol². Students often compute *a* numerically but forget to write the units, which the KTU examiner may deduct a mark for.
> 4. **Cubic Equation Solving:** When P is given and V is to be found from a cubic EOS, students must solve the cubic (often by iterative methods or Cardano's formula) and pick the **physically meaningful root** (typically the largest positive real root for gases, smallest for liquids).
> 5. **Acentric Factor Sign:** Most hydrocarbons have $0 < \omega < 1$. Hydrogen and helium have **negative** ω. A negative value is permissible and must not be flagged as an error.
> 6. **Z = PV/RT vs. Z = PV/nRT:** Both forms are equivalent; use whichever is convenient based on the data given.
> 7. **Reduced Volume:** The critical molar volume $V̂_c$ is often **not** the same as the reciprocal of critical density due to non-ideality. Use the experimental or EOS-derived $V̂_c$ for reduced volume calculations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ⚡ Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Equation of State** relates P, T, and V̂: $f(P, V̂, T) = 0$.
- [x] **Ideal Gas Law:** $PV = nRT$ — valid for low P, high T.
- [x] **Compressibility Factor:** $Z = PV̂/RT$ — Z = 1 for ideal, Z ≠ 1 for real.
- [x] **Van der Waals EOS:** $\left(P + \dfrac{a}{V̂^2}\right)(V̂ - b) = RT$
  - $a = \dfrac{27 R^2 T_c^2}{64 P_c}$, $b = \dfrac{R T_c}{8 P_c}$, $V̂_c = 3b$, $Z_c = 3/8 = 0.375$
- [x] **Redlich-Kwong EOS:** introduces $\sqrt{T}$ in the attractive term.
- [x] **SRK EOS:** adds $\alpha(T_r, \omega)$ — most widely used in industry.
- [x] **Peng-Robinson EOS:** better liquid density prediction than SRK.
- [x] **Virial Equation:** $Z = 1 + B/V̂ + C/V̂^2 + \cdots$ — theoretical basis from statistical mechanics.
- [x] **Principle of Corresponding States:** $Z = Z(P_r, T_r)$ — all fluids behave alike in reduced coordinates.
- [x] **Acentric Factor ω:** third parameter for polar/associating substances.
- [x] **Critical Point conditions for EOS:** $(\partial P/\partial V̂)_T = 0$ and $(\partial^2 P/\partial V̂^2)_T = 0$ at $T = T_c, P = P_c$.

### 🔑 Key Mnemonics

- **"AB = a, VdW":** $a = \dfrac{27 R^2 T_c^2}{64 P_c}$ and $b = \dfrac{RT_c}{8 P_c}$.
- **"SARA-P" for EOS Evolution:** **S**imple Ideal → **A**ttraction (VdW) → **R**edlich-Kwong → **A**lpha function (SRK) → **P**eng-Robinson.
- **"0.42748, 0.08664":** SRK and RK constants — same numerical values but different powers of T_c.
- **"0.45724, 0.07780":** PR constants — memorize as PR's signature.

### 📊 Comparison Table of Cubic EOS

| Feature | VdW | RK | SRK | PR |
|---------|-----|-----|------|------|
| Year | 1873 | 1949 | 1972 | 1976 |
| Vapour-phase accuracy | Low | Good | Very good | Very good |
| Liquid-phase accuracy | Poor | Poor | Fair | Good |
| Critical Z | 0.375 | ~0.333 | 0.333 | 0.307 |
| Temperature dependence of a | None | $\sqrt{T}$ | $\alpha(T_r, \omega)$ | $\alpha(T_r, \omega)$ |
| Industrial use | Historical | Some legacy | **Most common** | **Refining & cryogenics** |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
