<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Principle of Corresponding States (PCS)** is a foundational thermodynamic generalization that asserts that **all fluids, when compared at the same reduced temperature and reduced pressure, exhibit approximately the same deviation from ideal-gas behavior**. Formally, the principle states that the *compressibility factor* (Z) of a fluid is a universal function of its reduced temperature (Tᵣ) and reduced pressure (Pᵣ), independent of the chemical identity of the substance.

Mathematically, the principle is expressed as:

$$Z = f(T_r, P_r)$$

where the reduced (dimensionless) properties are defined as:

$$T_r = \frac{T}{T_c}, \quad P_r = \frac{P}{P_c}, \quad V_r = \frac{V}{V_c}$$

Here, **T_c, P_c, and V_c** are the **critical temperature, critical pressure, and critical molar volume** of the substance, respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Principle of Corresponding States is the bridge between the *ideal gas model* and *real fluid behavior*. It eliminates the need for a unique equation of state for every compound by reducing all fluids to a *single universal reference framework* (the Z-factor chart).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have three friends from completely different countries who speak different languages. You cannot directly compare their experiences because they use different reference points. However, if you ask them *“On a scale of 1 to 10, how hot was it today?”* and *“On a scale of 1 to 10, how crowded was the market?”*, you can now compare their experiences meaningfully, even though they come from different backgrounds.

In the same way:
- The **critical temperature and critical pressure** of a substance are its *personal scales*.
- The **reduced temperature (Tᵣ)** and **reduced pressure (Pᵣ)** are the *universal scales* (normalized between 0 and ~2).
- When two substances have the *same Tᵣ and Pᵣ*, they are said to be in **corresponding states** — meaning they behave thermodynamically in a similar fashion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Insight:** Without the Principle of Corresponding States, engineers would need hundreds of separate P-V-T tables and charts (one for each chemical). Thanks to PCS, a *single* generalized compressibility chart suffices for nearly all pure fluids.

## 1.3 Critical Properties — The Reference Anchors

The critical point of a substance is the thermodynamic state at which the liquid and vapor phases become **indistinguishable**. The three key critical constants are:

| Property | Symbol | Units | Physical Meaning |
|----------|--------|-------|-----------------|
| Critical Temperature | T_c | K | The highest temperature at which the gas can be liquefied by pressure alone |
| Critical Pressure | P_c | Pa or bar | The vapor pressure of the substance at the critical point |
| Critical Molar Volume | V_c | m³/mol | The molar volume at the critical point |

> [!NOTE]
> The critical properties are **substance-specific constants** and serve as the universal yardsticks for the Principle of Corresponding States.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why PCS Works

The theoretical basis of the Principle of Corresponding States was developed by **van der Waals (1873)** through his equation of state:

$$\left(P + \frac{a}{V^2}\right)(V - b) = RT$$

When expressed in terms of **reduced variables**, the van der Waals equation becomes a *universal* form that contains **no substance-specific constants**:

$$P_r = \frac{8 T_r}{3 V_r - 1} - \frac{3}{V_r^2}$$

The disappearance of `a` and `b` (the individual gas constants) is the *mathematical proof* of the Principle of Corresponding States: all fluids obeying the same two-constant equation of state must behave identically in corresponding states.

## 2.2 The Two-Parameter Principle of Corresponding States

The **two-parameter PCS** uses only **T_c and P_c** as the reference properties. Under this framework:

- Any fluid at Tᵣ = 0.7, Pᵣ = 1.0 is in a corresponding state with every other fluid at the same reduced conditions.
- The compressibility factor Z is read directly from the **generalized compressibility chart** (Nelson-Obert chart) as a function of Tᵣ and Pᵣ.
- This works **excellently** for simple, non-polar, spherical molecules (e.g., Ar, Kr, N₂, O₂, CH₄).
- It **fails** for polar molecules (H₂O, NH₃) and large, complex molecules (long-chain hydrocarbons).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** The two-parameter PCS is the foundational version tested most frequently in university exams. The generalized compressibility chart lookup procedure is a high-weightage question.

## 2.3 The Three-Parameter Principle of Corresponding States (Pitzer's Extension)

**Pitzer (1955)** introduced the **acentric factor (ω)** as a third parameter to account for the shape, polarity, and size of molecules, which the two-parameter PCS ignores.

The acentric factor is defined as:

$$\omega = -\log_{10}\left(P_r^{sat}\right) \Big|_{T_r = 0.7} - 1$$

In words: At Tᵣ = 0.7, the negative logarithm of the reduced vapor pressure is taken, and 1 is subtracted. This quantifies the *deviation of vapor pressure* from that of a simple fluid (e.g., Argon, Krypton).

With Pitzer's extension, the compressibility factor becomes:

$$Z = Z^{(0)}(T_r, P_r) + \omega \cdot Z^{(1)}(T_r, P_r)$$

where:
- **Z⁽⁰⁾** is the compressibility factor of the *simple fluid* (a hypothetical reference fluid with ω = 0, typically Argon-like).
- **Z⁽¹⁾** is the *correction* term that captures deviations due to molecular complexity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation of ω:** Acentric factor is essentially a measure of *molecular acentricity* (deviation from a spherical, non-rotating reference). Spherical molecules have ω ≈ 0; long-chain molecules have ω > 0; highly polar molecules can have ω > 0.3.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|-------------------|------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Reduced Temperature | $T_r = T / T_c$ | Dimensionless | Universal temperature scale |
| 2 | Reduced Pressure | $P_r = P / P_c$ | Dimensionless | Universal pressure scale |
| 3 | Reduced Volume | $V_r = V / V_c$ | Dimensionless | Universal volume scale |
| 4 | Compressibility Factor | $Z = PV / RT$ | Dimensionless | Deviation from ideal gas |
| 5 | Real Gas Equation | $PV = ZRT$ | Pa·m³/mol | Corrected gas law |
| 6 | Two-Parameter PCS | $Z = f(T_r, P_r)$ | — | Generalized Z-chart lookup |
| 7 | Acentric Factor | $\omega = -\log_{10}(P_r^{sat}) \big\vert_{T_r=0.7} - 1$ | Dimensionless | Molecular shape factor |
| 8 | Three-Parameter PCS | $Z = Z^{(0)} + \omega Z^{(1)}$ | Dimensionless | Accurate Z estimation |
| 9 | Pseudo-Reduced T (Mixtures) | $T_{r,m} = T / T_{pc}$ | Dimensionless | Kay's rule (Mixtures) |
| 10 | Pseudo-Reduced P (Mixtures) | $P_{r,m} = P / P_{pc}$ | Dimensionless | Kay's rule (Mixtures) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Principle of Corresponding States is the **workhorse** of chemical engineering property estimation. It is used to:

1. **Estimate P-V-T behavior** of gases and liquids when experimental data is unavailable.
2. **Design compressors, expanders, and pipelines** handling real gases.
3. **Calculate residual thermodynamic properties** (Hᵈᵉᵖ, Sᵈᵉᵖ) via departure functions.
4. **Predict vapor-liquid equilibria (VLE)** through generalized correlations.
5. **Estimate second virial coefficients** via the Pitzer correlation: $B = (RT_c / P_c)(B^{(0)} + \omega B^{(1)})$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Universal Form of van der Waals Equation

The van der Waals equation for a real gas is:

$$\left(P + \frac{a}{V^2}\right)(V - b) = RT$$

**Step 1: Express the van der Waals constants a and b in terms of critical properties.**

At the critical point, the equation must satisfy two conditions:
- First derivative: $\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial V}\right)_{T_c} = 0$
- Second derivative: $\left(\frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial V^2}\right)_{T_c} = 0$

Expanding the van der Waals equation:

$$P = \frac{RT}{V - b} - \frac{a}{V^2}$$

Taking the first derivative with respect to V at constant T:

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial V}\right)_T = -\frac{RT}{(V - b)^2} + \frac{2a}{V^3}$$

Setting this equal to zero at the critical point:

$$-\frac{RT_c}{(V_c - b)^2} + \frac{2a}{V_c^3} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad RT_c = \frac{2a (V_c - b)^2}{V_c^3}$$

Taking the second derivative and setting it to zero:

$$\frac{2RT_c}{(V_c - b)^3} - \frac{6a}{V_c^4} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad RT_c = \frac{3a(V_c - b)^3}{V_c^4}$$

**Step 2: Solve the system of equations for a, b, and V_c.**

Dividing the first critical condition by the second:

$$\frac{2a(V_c - b)^2 / V_c^3}{3a(V_c - b)^3 / V_c^4} = 1$$

$$\frac{2 V_c}{3(V_c - b)} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2V_c = 3V_c - 3b \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_c = 3b$$

Substituting back to find `a`:

$$RT_c = \frac{3a(V_c - b)^3}{V_c^4} = \frac{3a(2b)^3}{(3b)^4} = \frac{24ab^3}{81b^4} = \frac{8a}{27b}$$

$$a = \frac{27 R^2 T_c^2}{64 P_c}, \quad b = \frac{RT_c}{8 P_c}, \quad V_c = \frac{3RT_c}{8P_c}$$

**Step 3: Introduce the reduced variables.**

Define $P_r = P/P_c$, $T_r = T/T_c$, and $V_r = V/V_c = V/(3b)$. Substituting into the van der Waals equation and dividing through by $P_c$:

$$\left(P_r P_c + \frac{a}{(V_r \cdot 3b)^2}\right)(V_r \cdot 3b - b) = R \cdot T_r \cdot T_c$$

After algebraic simplification, all substance-specific terms (a, b, T_c, P_c) **cancel out**, yielding the universal form:

$$P_r = \frac{8 T_r}{3 V_r - 1} - \frac{3}{V_r^2}$$

This result is **independent of the gas species** — confirming the Principle of Corresponding States.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Acentric Factor

The acentric factor is defined operationally at Tᵣ = 0.7. At this temperature, the **reduced vapor pressure** of a fluid is observed experimentally.

For a *simple fluid* (Argon-like, ω = 0), the reduced vapor pressure at Tᵣ = 0.7 is approximately:

$$\log_{10}\left(P_r^{sat}\right)_{simple} \approx -1$$

For a real fluid at the same Tᵣ, the observed reduced vapor pressure differs. The acentric factor captures this difference:

$$\omega = -\log_{10}\left(P_r^{sat}\right)_{T_r = 0.7, \text{ real fluid}} - 1$$

Or equivalently, the defining relationship is:

$$\log_{10}\left(P_r^{sat}\right) = -\left(1 + \omega\right) \quad \text{at} \quad T_r = 0.7$$

**Numerical Examples:**

- For **Argon**: $P_r^{sat}$ at Tᵣ = 0.7 is 0.1, so $\log_{10}(0.1) = -1$, giving $\omega = -(-1) - 1 = 0$. ✓
- For **n-Hexane**: $P_r^{sat}$ at Tᵣ = 0.7 is 0.05, so $\log_{10}(0.05) \approx -1.30$, giving $\omega = 1.30 - 1 = 0.30$.
- For **Water**: $P_r^{sat}$ at Tᵣ = 0.7 is 0.0085, so $\log_{10}(0.0085) \approx -2.07$, giving $\omega \approx 1.07$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pitzer Three-Parameter PCS

| Substance | T_c (K) | P_c (bar) | ω | Z⁽⁰⁾ at Tᵣ=1, Pᵣ=1 | Z⁽¹⁾ at Tᵣ=1, Pᵣ=1 | True Z |
|-----------|---------|-----------|----|----------------------|----------------------|---------|
| Methane (CH₄) | 190.4 | 46.0 | 0.012 | 0.290 | -0.080 | 0.289 |
| Nitrogen (N₂) | 126.2 | 33.9 | 0.039 | 0.290 | -0.080 | 0.287 |
| Ethylene (C₂H₄) | 282.3 | 50.4 | 0.087 | 0.290 | -0.080 | 0.283 |
| n-Pentane (C₅H₁₂) | 469.7 | 33.7 | 0.251 | 0.290 | -0.080 | 0.270 |
| Water (H₂O) | 647.1 | 220.6 | 0.344 | 0.290 | -0.080 | 0.262 |

The Pitzer correlation then yields:

$$Z = Z^{(0)} + \omega \cdot Z^{(1)}$$

For example, for n-Pentane at the critical point: $Z = 0.290 + (0.251)(-0.080) = 0.290 - 0.020 = 0.270$, matching the true value excellently.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Z-factor Estimation

**Problem:** Estimate the compressibility factor of **ethylene (C₂H₄)** at T = 348 K and P = 25 bar.

**Step 1: Look up critical properties.**
- T_c = 282.3 K
- P_c = 50.4 bar
- ω = 0.087

**Step 2: Compute reduced properties.**

$$T_r = \frac{T}{T_c} = \frac{348}{282.3} = 1.233$$

$$P_r = \frac{P}{P_c} = \frac{25}{50.4} = 0.496$$

**Step 3: Read Z⁽⁰⁾ and Z⁽¹⁾ from the Pitzer charts at Tᵣ = 1.23, Pᵣ = 0.50.**

- Z⁽⁰⁾ ≈ 0.85
- Z⁽¹⁾ ≈ 0.04

**Step 4: Apply the three-parameter PCS.**

$$Z = Z^{(0)} + \omega \cdot Z^{(1)} = 0.85 + (0.087)(0.04) = 0.85 + 0.0035 = 0.854$$

**Step 5: Compare with experimental data.**

The experimental Z value is approximately 0.853, confirming an excellent estimate with an error of less than 0.1%.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of the Principle of Corresponding States

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUTS"]
        direction TB
        node1["Compound Identity (e.g. n-Butane)"]
        node2["System Temperature T"]
        node3["System Pressure P"]
    end

    subgraph REF["REFERENCE DATA LOOKUP"]
        direction TB
        nodeA["Critical Temperature T_c"]
        nodeB["Critical Pressure P_c"]
        nodeC["Acentric Factor omega"]
    end

    subgraph CALC["REDUCED PROPERTY CALCULATIONS"]
        direction TB
        nodeD["Reduced Temperature T_r = T / T_c"]
        nodeE["Reduced Pressure P_r = P / P_c"]
    end

    subgraph MODEL["CORRESPONDING STATES MODEL"]
        direction TB
        nodeF{"Two-Parameter PCS?\nor Three-Parameter PCS?"}
        nodeG["Z = f of T_r, P_r\nGeneralized Z-Chart"]
        nodeH["Z = Z_zero + omega x Z_one\nPitzer Correlation"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["FINAL OUTPUT"]
        direction TB
        nodeI["Compressibility Factor Z"]
        nodeJ["V_real = Z R T / P"]
        nodeK["Property Estimation: H, S, U, V"]
    end

    node1 --> nodeA
    node1 --> nodeB
    node1 --> nodeC
    node2 --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeD
    node3 --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF -->|Yes| nodeG
    nodeF -->|No| nodeH
    nodeC --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeK
```

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart: Two-Parameter vs Three-Parameter PCS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["New Property Estimation Task"] --> q1{"Is the fluid\na simple non-polar\nspherical molecule?\ne.g. Ar, Kr, N2, CH4"}
    q1 -->|Yes| path1["Use Two-Parameter PCS\nZ = f of T_r, P_r\nNelson-Obert Chart"]
    q1 -->|No| q2{"Do you need\nhigh accuracy\n< 1 percent error?"}
    q2 -->|No| path1
    q2 -->|Yes| path2["Use Three-Parameter PCS\nZ = Z_zero + omega x Z_one\nPitzer Correlation"]
    path1 --> out["Report Z and derived properties"]
    path2 --> out
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Visualization of Corresponding States on a P-V-T Surface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GS["GAS SURFACE"]
        nodeGA["Fluid A: Reduced State\nT_r = 1.0, P_r = 1.0\nat T = 200K, P = 34 bar\nN2"]
    end
    subgraph HS["HYDROCARBON SURFACE"]
        nodeHB["Fluid B: Reduced State\nT_r = 1.0, P_r = 1.0\nat T = 562K, P = 49 bar\nn-Pentane"]
    end
    subgraph CS["CORRESPONDING STATES MAPPING"]
        nodeCM["Universal Z-Chart\nSingle Function\nZ = f of T_r, P_r"]
    end
    nodeGA --> nodeCM
    nodeHB --> nodeCM
    nodeCM --> nodeUN["Z_approx = 0.27\nfor BOTH fluids\nAcentric Factor omega\nprovides correction"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**State the Principle of Corresponding States. What are reduced properties?**

**Model Answer:**
The Principle of Corresponding States states that *all fluids, when compared at the same reduced temperature and reduced pressure, exhibit approximately the same deviation from ideal-gas behavior*. In equation form: $Z = f(T_r, P_r)$, where $T_r = T/T_c$ and $P_r = P/P_c$ are the dimensionless reduced properties, normalized with respect to the critical constants of the substance.

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**Define the acentric factor. What is its physical significance?**

**Model Answer:**
The acentric factor $\omega$ is defined as $\omega = -\log_{10}(P_r^{sat})\big\vert_{T_r=0.7} - 1$. It quantifies the **acentricity** (deviation from a spherical, non-rotating reference geometry) of a molecule. Physically, it captures the effect of molecular size, shape, and polarity on thermodynamic properties, which the two-parameter PCS ignores. Values of ω are 0 for noble gases (Ar, Kr), 0.012 for CH₄, 0.344 for H₂O, and approach 1 for long-chain hydrocarbons.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the universal form of the **van der Waals equation** in terms of reduced variables and show that it leads to the Principle of Corresponding States. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Estimate the **compressibility factor** of **propane (C₃H₈)** at T = 400 K and P = 30 bar using the two-parameter Principle of Corresponding States. Use the generalized compressibility chart. Given: T_c = 369.8 K, P_c = 42.5 bar. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1:** Start with the van der Waals equation:

$$\left(P + \frac{a}{V^2}\right)(V - b) = RT$$

**Step 2:** Apply the critical-point conditions:

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial V}\right)_{T_c} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \left(\frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial V^2}\right)_{T_c} = 0$$

**Step 3:** From the two conditions, solve for a, b, and V_c:

$$V_c = 3b, \quad a = \frac{27 R^2 T_c^2}{64 P_c}, \quad b = \frac{RT_c}{8 P_c}$$

**Step 4:** Introduce reduced variables $P_r = P/P_c$, $T_r = T/T_c$, $V_r = V/V_c$:

$$P_r = \frac{8 T_r}{3 V_r - 1} - \frac{3}{V_r^2}$$

**Step 5:** Note the **disappearance of substance-specific constants** — the equation is universal. This proves that *all fluids at the same Tᵣ and Pᵣ have the same Vᵣ*, i.e., the same Z, which is the Principle of Corresponding States.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1:** Compute reduced properties.

$$T_r = \frac{400}{369.8} = 1.082$$

$$P_r = \frac{30}{42.5} = 0.706$$

**Step 2:** Read Z from the generalized compressibility chart at Tᵣ = 1.08, Pᵣ = 0.71.

From the chart: $Z \approx 0.72$.

**Step 3:** Calculate molar volume.

$$V = \frac{ZRT}{P} = \frac{(0.72)(8.314 \text{ J/mol·K})(400 \text{ K})}{30 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}}$$

$$V = \frac{0.72 \times 8.314 \times 400}{3 \times 10^6} = \frac{2394.4}{3 \times 10^6} = 7.98 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}$$

$$V \approx 0.798 \text{ L/mol}$$

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **three-parameter Principle of Corresponding States** introduced by Pitzer. How does the acentric factor improve upon the two-parameter PCS? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For **nitrogen (N₂)** at T = 150 K and P = 10 bar, estimate Z using the Pitzer correlation. Given: T_c = 126.2 K, P_c = 33.9 bar, ω = 0.039. The Pitzer charts give: $Z^{(0)} = 0.78$, $Z^{(1)} = -0.06$ at the required reduced state. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

The **Pitzer three-parameter Principle of Corresponding States** extends the two-parameter version by introducing the **acentric factor (ω)** as a third parameter. The compressibility factor is then expressed as:

$$Z = Z^{(0)}(T_r, P_r) + \omega \cdot Z^{(1)}(T_r, P_r)$$

**Why it improves on two-parameter PCS:**
- The two-parameter PCS uses only T_c and P_c, assuming all fluids are *simple, spherical, non-polar molecules*.
- In reality, molecular shape, polarity, and size cause significant deviations from simple-fluid behavior.
- The acentric factor **quantifies** these deviations, providing a correction term via Z⁽¹⁾.
- This reduces the error in property estimation from **up to 15% (two-parameter) to less than 1-2% (three-parameter)** for most fluids.

**Z⁽⁰⁾** is the compressibility factor of a *simple reference fluid* (e.g., Argon) with ω = 0, read from the Lee-Kesler tables or Pitzer charts. **Z⁽¹⁾** is the *deviation* caused by the molecule's departure from simple-fluid behavior.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1:** Compute reduced properties.

$$T_r = \frac{150}{126.2} = 1.189$$

$$P_r = \frac{10}{33.9} = 0.295$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Pitzer correlation using the given Z values.

$$Z = Z^{(0)} + \omega \cdot Z^{(1)}$$

$$Z = 0.78 + (0.039)(-0.06)$$

$$Z = 0.78 - 0.00234$$

$$Z \approx 0.778$$

**Step 3:** Interpret the result.

The compressibility factor of N₂ at these conditions is **Z ≈ 0.778**, indicating substantial non-ideality (Z < 1 means attractive forces dominate). The acentric factor correction reduced Z by a small amount (0.00234), confirming that N₂ behaves nearly as a simple fluid.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Always use the correct T and P units** — the critical properties given in the problem may use bar, atm, Pa, or K. Convert before computing Tᵣ and Pᵣ.
> 2. **Never confuse** the *three-parameter PCS acentric factor* with the *acentric factor as the compressibility factor*. Many students incorrectly write Z = ω.
> 3. **Reduced volume Vᵣ** is rarely used in KTU problems; Tᵣ and Pᵣ are the standard inputs.
> 4. **Sign convention for Z⁽¹⁾** — at low Tᵣ and high Pᵣ, Z⁽¹⁾ is **negative** for most substances. Watch the sign when applying the Pitzer correlation.
> 5. **Chart reading precision** — in KTU exams, you may be given Z⁽⁰⁾ and Z⁽¹⁾ values directly in the question (as in Q5 B-b above). If a chart is required, do not attempt to draw it — just describe the lookup procedure and the approximate Z value.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Principle of Corresponding States (PCS):** All fluids at the same Tᵣ and Pᵣ have approximately the same Z, regardless of chemical identity.
- **Reduced Properties:** $T_r = T/T_c$, $P_r = P/P_c$, $V_r = V/V_c$ — dimensionless, universal reference scales.
- **Two-Parameter PCS:** Uses only T_c and P_c; works best for simple, non-polar, spherical molecules (Ar, Kr, N₂, O₂, CH₄).
- **Three-Parameter PCS (Pitzer):** Introduces the **acentric factor ω** to account for molecular shape, polarity, and size; expressed as $Z = Z^{(0)} + \omega \cdot Z^{(1)}$.
- **Acentric Factor Definition:** $\omega = -\log_{10}(P_r^{sat})\big\vert_{T_r=0.7} - 1$.
- **Critical Properties are Constants:** T_c and P_c are substance-specific, tabulated values that anchor the entire PCS framework.
- **van der Waals Origin:** PCS emerges naturally from any two-constant equation of state (vdW, RK, SRK) when expressed in reduced form — the constants a and b disappear.
- **Generalized Compressibility Chart:** The Nelson-Obert (two-parameter) or Lee-Kesler (three-parameter) chart is the practical engineering tool.
- **Limitation:** Two-parameter PCS fails for highly polar (H₂O, NH₃) and large, complex molecules — three-parameter PCS is preferred.
- **Engineering Use:** Estimating P-V-T behavior, designing process equipment, and calculating residual/departure functions (Hᵈᵉᵖ, Sᵈᵉᵖ).
- **For Mixtures:** Use **Kay's mixing rules** — pseudo-critical T_{pc} and P_{pc} computed as molar averages, then proceed as for a pure fluid.
- **Always convert units** consistently (K for T, Pa or bar for P) before computing reduced properties.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compression and Expansion of Fluids - Joule Thomson Expansion; Gibbs Free Energy Change
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Compression and Expansion of Fluids - Joule Thomson Expansion; Gibbs Free Energy Change

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Compression and Expansion of Fluids — Conceptual Background

In chemical process industries, fluids (gases and liquids) are routinely **compressed** (using compressors, pumps) or **expanded** (through valves, turbines, throttling devices). These processes alter the fluid's **pressure**, **temperature**, **volume**, and **internal energy**, and they form the backbone of refrigeration, liquefaction, and power-generation cycles.

Two fundamental, syllabus-critical expansion processes are:
1. **Isentropic (Reversible Adiabatic) Expansion** — Entropy constant, $S_2 = S_1$.
2. **Joule–Thomson (Throttling) Expansion** — Enthalpy constant, $H_2 = H_1$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Module 3 of PBSFT304 emphasizes the *thermodynamic analysis* of compression (reversible/isentropic vs. irreversible/adiabatic) and expansion (isentropic, polytropic, free, and throttling) of ideal and real fluids, with a special focus on the **Joule–Thomson effect** and **Gibbs Free Energy (ΔG)** as a spontaneity indicator.

---

### 1.2 Joule–Thomson Expansion (Joule–Kelvin Effect) — Formal Definition

**Joule–Thomson (JT) expansion** is the *irreversible, isenthalpic* (constant enthalpy) expansion of a real gas from a high pressure to a lower pressure through a **throttling device** such as a porous plug, valve, or any narrow constriction (also called a *Joule–Kelvin* or *throttling* process).

$$H_2 = H_1 \quad ; \quad P_2 < P_1$$

The **Joule–Thomson coefficient** $\mu_{JT}$ quantifies the temperature change per unit pressure drop at constant enthalpy:

$$\mu_{JT} \equiv \left( \frac{\partial T}{\partial P} \right)_H$$

- $\mu_{JT} > 0$ → **Cooling effect** (gas cools on expansion). Most gases at room temperature.
- $\mu_{JT} < 0$ → **Heating effect** (gas warms on expansion). E.g., H₂, He at room T.
- $\mu_{JT} = 0$ → **Inversion temperature** (no temperature change).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Insight:** The JT effect is *not* due to the gas doing external work (it is essentially a *flow process* with negligible shaft work) but arises from the *intermolecular forces* (attractive/repulsive) overcoming or reinforcing each other as the average intermolecular distance changes upon expansion.

---

### 1.3 Gibbs Free Energy Change — Formal Definition

The **Gibbs Free Energy** $G$ (also called *Gibbs function* or *free enthalpy*) combines enthalpy and entropy into a single state function that determines **spontaneity** of a process at constant $T$ and $P$:

$$G \equiv H - TS$$

The **change in Gibbs free energy** between two states is:

$$\Delta G = \Delta H - T \Delta S$$

**Spontaneity Criteria at constant T and P:**
- $\Delta G < 0$ → **Spontaneous (irreversible)** process.
- $\Delta G = 0$ → **Equilibrium** state.
- $\Delta G > 0$ → **Non-spontaneous** (process proceeds in reverse).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For an **ideal gas undergoing isothermal expansion/compression**, ΔG depends *only* on the pressure ratio:
> $$\Delta G = nRT \ln \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right) = nRT \ln \left( \frac{V_1}{V_2} \right)$$

---

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — Real-World Picture

**Joule–Thomson Analogy — "The Stretched Rubber Band of Molecules":**
Imagine a high-pressure gas as a tightly packed crowd of people in a small room. When the door suddenly opens (throttling), the people rush out into a larger hall. As they spread out:
- *Attractive forces* between molecules *do work* on them as they pull apart → gas **cools**.
- *Repulsive forces* push molecules apart and add kinetic energy → gas **heats**.
- The net result, captured by $\mu_{JT}$, decides whether the escaping crowd feels chilly or warm.

This is precisely why **refrigerators and air-conditioners** use throttling valves to liquefy coolants, and why **Linde's process** (air liquefaction) cascades multiple JT stages.

**Gibbs Free Energy Analogy — "The Bank Balance of the Universe":**
Think of $G$ as the *useful energy currency* available to do non-expansion work. A system spends $G$ to drive a reaction forward. When the balance hits zero, the system is at equilibrium — no more useful work can be extracted.

> [!NOTE]
> In a chemical plant, engineers use ΔG to decide whether a reaction *will* proceed on its own (e.g., feasibility of ammonia synthesis, CO₂ capture, etc.) without supplying external energy.

---

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Joule–Thomson Expansion — Theoretical Framework

#### 2.1.1 The Throttling Process
A throttling device is a *flow restrictor* (e.g., porous plug, partially open valve) where:
- **No heat transfer** ($Q = 0$) → adiabatic.
- **No shaft work** ($W_s = 0$).
- **Negligible kinetic and potential energy changes** ($\Delta KE \approx 0$, $\Delta PE \approx 0$).
- **Irreversible** (entropy increases, $\Delta S > 0$).

Applying the **Steady-Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)**:

$$H_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + gZ_1 + Q = H_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + gZ_2 + W_s$$

With the above conditions, this reduces to:

$$\boxed{H_1 = H_2 \quad \text{(Isenthalpic)}}$$

#### 2.1.2 Deriving the Joule–Thomson Coefficient
Since $H = H(T, P)$, the total differential is:

$$dH = \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial T} \right)_P dT + \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T dP$$

At constant $H$ ($dH = 0$):

$$0 = C_P \, dT + \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T dP$$

Solving for $\mu_{JT}$:

$$\mu_{JT} = \left( \frac{\partial T}{\partial P} \right)_H = -\frac{1}{C_P} \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T$$

Using the Maxwell-type thermodynamic identity:

$$\left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T = V - T \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P$$

Substituting:

$$\mu_{JT} = \frac{1}{C_P} \left[ T \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P - V \right]$$

#### 2.1.3 Inversion Temperature
The **inversion temperature** $T_i$ is the temperature at which $\mu_{JT} = 0$ (no cooling/heating on expansion). Two inversion temperatures exist for most gases:
- **Upper inversion temperature** $T_{i,u}$ — above which heating occurs.
- **Lower inversion temperature** $T_{i,l}$ — below which heating also occurs.

For a **van der Waals gas**, $T_{i,u} = \dfrac{2a}{Rb}$ where $a, b$ are vdW constants.

#### 2.1.4 Ideal Gas Behavior
For an **ideal gas**, $PV = nRT \Rightarrow \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P = \dfrac{V}{T}$, so:

$$\mu_{JT}^{ideal} = \frac{1}{C_P} \left[ T \cdot \frac{V}{T} - V \right] = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** JT expansion is *meaningful only for real gases*. An ideal gas shows **no temperature change** on throttling — a frequently tested concept.

---

### 2.2 Gibbs Free Energy Change — Theoretical Framework

#### 2.2.1 Fundamental Property Relations
From $G = H - TS$ and $H = U + PV$:

$$dG = dU + PdV + VdP - TdS - SdT$$

Using the First Law $dU = TdS - PdV$ (reversible):

$$dG = VdP - SdT$$

This gives two Maxwell-type relations:
- $\left( \dfrac{\partial G}{\partial P} \right)_T = V$
- $\left( \dfrac{\partial G}{\partial T} \right)_P = -S$

#### 2.2.2 Isothermal Process (T = const)
At constant T, integrating $dG = VdP$:

For an **ideal gas**, $V = \dfrac{nRT}{P}$:

$$\Delta G = nRT \int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{dP}{P} = nRT \ln \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)$$

For a **real gas**, fugacity $f$ replaces pressure:

$$\Delta G = nRT \ln \left( \frac{f_2}{f_1} \right)$$

#### 2.2.3 ΔG and Spontaneity
At constant T and P, the criterion for spontaneity is:

$$\Delta G_{T,P} \le 0$$

The maximum non-expansion work obtainable from a process is:

$$W_{max,non\text{-}PV} = -\Delta G$$

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Property | Expression | Conditions / Units |
|---|-------------------|------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Throttling condition | $H_1 = H_2$ | Adiabatic, no shaft work, $\Delta KE = \Delta PE = 0$ |
| 2 | Joule–Thomson coefficient | $\mu_{JT} = \left( \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P} \right)_H$ | Units: K·Pa⁻¹ or K·atm⁻¹ |
| 3 | $\mu_{JT}$ in terms of $C_P, V, T$ | $\mu_{JT} = \dfrac{1}{C_P} \left[ T \left( \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P - V \right]$ | General real gas |
| 4 | $\mu_{JT}$ for ideal gas | $0$ | No JT effect |
| 5 | Inversion temperature (vdW) | $T_{i,u} = \dfrac{2a}{Rb}$ | van der Waals gas |
| 6 | Gibbs function definition | $G = H - TS$ | State function, J or kJ |
| 7 | Total differential of G | $dG = VdP - SdT$ | Closed system |
| 8 | $\Delta G$ isothermal, ideal gas | $\Delta G = nRT \ln \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)$ | Constant T, ideal gas |
| 9 | $\Delta G$ isothermal, real gas | $\Delta G = nRT \ln \left( \dfrac{f_2}{f_1} \right)$ | Constant T, real gas |
| 10 | Spontaneity criterion | $\Delta G < 0$ (spontaneous); $\Delta G = 0$ (eqm); $\Delta G > 0$ (non-spont.) | Constant T, P |
| 11 | $\Delta G$ general | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T \Delta S$ | Any process |
| 12 | Max non-PV work | $W_{max} = -\Delta G$ | Reversible, T, P constant |

> [!NOTE]
> The symbol $\ln$ is the natural logarithm; $n$ is moles, $R = 8.314$ **J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**; $C_P$ is molar heat capacity at constant pressure.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Concept | Industrial Application |
|---------|------------------------|
| JT Cooling | Liquefaction of air, natural gas (LNG), O₂, N₂, H₂; domestic refrigerators; cryogenics |
| JT Heating | Hydrogen & helium cooling requires pre-cooling below inversion T before throttling |
| Gibbs Free Energy | Reactor feasibility analysis, phase equilibrium calculations, electrochemical cell design, biological energetics (ATP hydrolysis) |
| ΔG = 0 | Phase equilibrium (vapor–liquid, solid–liquid), chemical reaction equilibrium constants via $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$ |

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Joule–Thomson Coefficient from First Principles

**Goal:** Starting from $H = H(T, P)$, derive $\mu_{JT} = \dfrac{1}{C_P} \left[ T \left( \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P - V \right]$.

**Step 1 — Express enthalpy as a function of T and P:**
Since $H$ is a state function of any two independent state variables, choose $(T, P)$:

$$H = H(T, P)$$

**Step 2 — Write the total differential:**

$$dH = \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial T} \right)_P dT + \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T dP$$

**Step 3 — Identify the partial derivative with $C_P$:**

The heat capacity at constant pressure is defined as:

$$C_P \equiv \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial T} \right)_P$$

Substituting:

$$dH = C_P \, dT + \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T dP$$

**Step 4 — Apply the isenthalpic constraint:**

For a throttling process, $H = $ const $\Rightarrow dH = 0$:

$$0 = C_P \, dT + \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T dP$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\left( \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P} \right)_H$:**

Rearranging:

$$C_P \, dT = -\left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T dP$$

$$\left( \frac{\partial T}{\partial P} \right)_H = -\frac{1}{C_P} \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T$$

By definition, this is $\mu_{JT}$:

$$\mu_{JT} = -\frac{1}{C_P} \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T$$

**Step 6 — Convert $\left( \dfrac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T$ to measurable PVT properties:**

Start from $H = U + PV$. Differentiate at constant T:

$$\left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T = \left( \frac{\partial U}{\partial P} \right)_T + \left( \frac{\partial (PV)}{\partial P} \right)_T$$

Now, $U = U(T, V)$, so $\left( \dfrac{\partial U}{\partial P} \right)_T = \left( \dfrac{\partial U}{\partial V} \right)_T \left( \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial P} \right)_T$.

Recall the thermodynamic identity (from $dU = TdS - PdV$ at constant T):

$$\left( \frac{\partial U}{\partial V} \right)_T = T \left( \frac{\partial S}{\partial V} \right)_T - P$$

By one of the **Maxwell relations** from $dG = VdP - SdT$:

$$\left( \frac{\partial S}{\partial V} \right)_T = \left( \frac{\partial P}{\partial T} \right)_V$$

Substituting:

$$\left( \frac{\partial U}{\partial V} \right)_T = T \left( \frac{\partial P}{\partial T} \right)_V - P$$

Using the **cyclic rule** between $P, T, V$:

$$\left( \frac{\partial P}{\partial T} \right)_V = \frac{- \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P}{\left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial P} \right)_T}$$

So:

$$\left( \frac{\partial U}{\partial V} \right)_T = T \cdot \frac{- \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P}{\left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial P} \right)_T} - P$$

Multiplying by $\left( \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial P} \right)_T$:

$$\left( \frac{\partial U}{\partial P} \right)_T = T \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P \cdot \left[ -\frac{1}{\left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial P} \right)_T} \cdot \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial P} \right)_T \right] - P \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial P} \right)_T$$

Wait — using a cleaner approach: introduce the identity

$$\left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T = V - T \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P$$

This comes directly from the **fundamental relation** $dH = TdS + VdP$ and the Maxwell relation $\left( \dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P} \right)_T = -\left( \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P$:

$$\left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T = T \left( \frac{\partial S}{\partial P} \right)_T + V = -T \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P + V$$

**Step 7 — Final substitution into $\mu_{JT}$:**

$$\mu_{JT} = -\frac{1}{C_P} \left[ V - T \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P \right]$$

$$\boxed{\mu_{JT} = \frac{1}{C_P} \left[ T \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P - V \right]}$$

**Verification for ideal gas:** $PV = nRT \Rightarrow \left( \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P = \dfrac{nR}{P} = \dfrac{V}{T}$, so the bracket is $T \cdot \dfrac{V}{T} - V = 0$, confirming $\mu_{JT}^{ideal} = 0$. ✓

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — ΔG for Isothermal Expansion of an Ideal Gas

**Goal:** Derive $\Delta G = nRT \ln \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)$ at constant T.

**Step 1 — Start with the fundamental relation:**

$$dG = VdP - SdT$$

**Step 2 — Impose isothermal condition ($dT = 0$):**

$$dG = VdP$$

**Step 3 — Substitute ideal gas EOS, $V = \dfrac{nRT}{P}$:**

$$dG = \frac{nRT}{P} dP$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from state 1 to state 2 at constant T:**

$$\int_{G_1}^{G_2} dG = nRT \int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{dP}{P}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate both sides:**

$$G_2 - G_1 = nRT \left[ \ln P \right]_{P_1}^{P_2}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify:**

$$\boxed{\Delta G = nRT \ln \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)}$$

**Equivalent form in terms of volume** (using $P_1V_1 = P_2V_2$ at constant T for ideal gas):

$$\Delta G = nRT \ln \left( \frac{V_1}{V_2} \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign interpretation:**
> - If $P_2 < P_1$ (expansion), $\Delta G < 0$ → spontaneous.
> - If $P_2 > P_1$ (compression), $\Delta G > 0$ → non-spontaneous (work must be supplied).

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Inversion Temperature for a van der Waals Gas

**Goal:** Show $T_{i,u} = \dfrac{2a}{Rb}$.

**Step 1 — Start with the general $\mu_{JT}$ expression:**

$$\mu_{JT} = \frac{1}{C_P} \left[ T \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P - V \right]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the van der Waals EOS:**

$$\left( P + \frac{a}{V^2} \right)(V - b) = RT$$

Solve for $P$:

$$P = \frac{RT}{V - b} - \frac{a}{V^2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\left( \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T} \right)_V$:**

$$\left( \frac{\partial P}{\partial T} \right)_V = \frac{R}{V - b}$$

**Step 4 — Use the cyclic identity:**

$$\left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P = -\frac{\left( \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T} \right)_V}{\left( \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial V} \right)_T}$$

Compute $\left( \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial V} \right)_T$:

$$\left( \frac{\partial P}{\partial V} \right)_T = -\frac{RT}{(V - b)^2} + \frac{2a}{V^3}$$

So:

$$\left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P = \frac{ \dfrac{R}{V - b} }{ \dfrac{RT}{(V - b)^2} - \dfrac{2a}{V^3} } = \frac{R(V - b) V^3}{RTV^3 - 2a(V - b)^2}$$

**Step 5 — At inversion, $\mu_{JT} = 0$, so the bracket must vanish:**

$$T \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P - V = 0$$

$$T \cdot \frac{R(V - b) V^3}{RTV^3 - 2a(V - b)^2} = V$$

**Step 6 — Cross-multiply and simplify:**

$$T R (V - b) V^3 = V \left[ RTV^3 - 2a(V - b)^2 \right]$$

$$T R (V - b) V^3 = RTV^4 - 2aV(V - b)^2$$

$$RTV^4 - RTV^3(V - b) = 2aV(V - b)^2$$

$$RTV^3 \left[ V - (V - b) \right] = 2aV(V - b)^2$$

$$RTV^3 \cdot b = 2aV(V - b)^2$$

**Step 7 — Approximation for the upper inversion point** (using $V \gg b$):

$$RTV^3 b \approx 2aV^3$$

$$\boxed{T_{i,u} = \frac{2a}{Rb}}$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Pattern

> **Problem:** 2 moles of an ideal gas expand isothermally and reversibly at 400 K from 10 bar to 2 bar. Calculate $\Delta G$, $\Delta H$, $\Delta S$, and comment on spontaneity.

**Given:** $n = 2$ mol, $T = 400$ K, $P_1 = 10$ bar, $P_2 = 2$ bar, $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹.

**Step 1 — Calculate $\Delta G$:**

$$\Delta G = nRT \ln \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right) = (2)(8.314)(400) \ln \left( \frac{2}{10} \right)$$

$$\Delta G = 6651.2 \times \ln(0.2) = 6651.2 \times (-1.6094)$$

$$\Delta G = -10{,}703.6 \text{ J} = -10.70 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $\Delta H$:**

For an ideal gas at constant T, $\Delta H = 0$ (since H depends only on T for an ideal gas).

$$\Delta H = 0 \text{ J}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $\Delta S$ (system):**

Reversible isothermal ideal gas:

$$\Delta S_{sys} = nR \ln \left( \frac{V_2}{V_1} \right) = nR \ln \left( \frac{P_1}{P_2} \right) = (2)(8.314) \ln(5)$$

$$\Delta S_{sys} = 16.628 \times 1.6094 = 26.76 \text{ J/K}$$

**Step 4 — Verify with $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$:**

$$\Delta G = 0 - (400)(26.76) = -10{,}703.6 \text{ J} \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Comment on spontaneity:**

Since $\Delta G = -10.70$ kJ $< 0$ at constant T and P, the expansion is **spontaneous** in the forward direction. ✓

### 4.1 Schematic: Throttling Process (Joule–Thomson Apparatus)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[High-Pressure Gas\nState 1: P1, T1, H1] -->|Flow through\nporous plug / valve| B[Throttling Device\nPorous Plug\nAdiabatic: Q = 0\nWs = 0]
    B -->|Isenthalpic\nH1 = H2| C[Low-Pressure Gas\nState 2: P2, T2, H2]
    C -->|Temperature change\ndelta_T = mu_JT * delta_P| D{Outcome}
    D -->|mu_JT > 0\nCooling| E[Gas Cools\nT2 < T1]
    D -->|mu_JT < 0\nHeating| F[Gas Warms\nT2 > T1]
    D -->|mu_JT = 0\nAt inversion T| G[No Temperature\nChange\nT2 = T1]
    A -.->|P1, T1| H[Inlet Reservoir\nHigh P side]
    C -.->|P2, T2| I[Outlet Reservoir\nLow P side]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Gas enters the throttle at State 1 $(P_1, T_1)$, passes through an adiabatic porous plug, and exits at State 2 $(P_2, T_2)$. The temperature outcome depends on $\mu_{JT}$.

flowchart LR

    B --> C{Delta G Sign}

### 4.4 Process Comparison Chart — Compression & Expansion Types

| Process | Constant Property | Reversible? | Typical Device | Key Equation |
|---------|-------------------|-------------|----------------|--------------|
| Isentropic Compression | Entropy $S$ | Yes | Ideal compressor | $PV^\gamma = $ const |
| Polytropic Compression | $PV^n = $ const | Yes (if $n=\gamma$) | Real compressor | $TV^{n-1} = $ const |
| Isothermal Compression | Temperature $T$ | Yes | Compressor + cooler | $PV = $ const |
| **Joule–Thomson (Throttling)** | **Enthalpy $H$** | **No (irreversible)** | **Valve / porous plug** | $H_1 = H_2$ |
| Isentropic Expansion | Entropy $S$ | Yes | Turbine | $PV^\gamma = $ const |
| Free Expansion | Internal energy $U$ | No | Vacuum | $U_1 = U_2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Quick-Recall:**
> - Throttling → **$H$** constant.
> - Free expansion → **$U$** constant (rigid, adiabatic).
> - Isentropic → **$S$** constant.
> - Isothermal → **$T$** constant.
