## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the **Joule–Thomson coefficient**. What is its significance in the liquefaction of gases?

**Model Answer:**

The Joule–Thomson coefficient is defined as the rate of change of temperature with pressure at constant enthalpy during a throttling process:

$$\mu_{JT} \equiv \left( \frac{\partial T}{\partial P} \right)_H \quad [\text{units: K/Pa}]$$

**Significance in Liquefaction:**
- For gases with $\mu_{JT} > 0$ (e.g., N₂, O₂, CO₂ at room temperature), expansion through a throttling valve produces a **cooling effect**, which can be exploited to **liquefy** the gas on repeated cycles (as in the **Linde process**).
- The cooling produced per cycle is $\Delta T = \mu_{JT} \cdot \Delta P$. Cascading multiple throttling stages progressively lowers the temperature until condensation occurs.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
State the criteria for spontaneity of a process in terms of **Gibbs free energy change (ΔG)** at constant temperature and pressure.

**Model Answer:**

At constant T and P, the spontaneity of a thermodynamic process is governed by the sign of the Gibbs free energy change:

| Condition | Interpretation |
|-----------|----------------|
| $\Delta G < 0$ | Spontaneous (irreversible) process in the forward direction |
| $\Delta G = 0$ | System at equilibrium — no net change |
| $\Delta G > 0$ | Non-spontaneous in forward direction; spontaneous in reverse |

Additionally, the magnitude $|\Delta G|$ represents the **maximum non-expansion (useful) work** obtainable from the process: $W_{max} = -\Delta G$.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A [KTU University Exam – Model 2024]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **Joule–Thomson coefficient** $\mu_{JT}$ in terms of measurable PVT properties and molar heat capacity $C_P$.

**(b)** A real gas obeys the **van der Waals equation**:
$$\left( P + \dfrac{a}{V^2} \right)(V - b) = RT$$
with $a = 0.138$ Pa·m⁶·mol⁻² and $b = 3.9 \times 10^{-5}$ m³·mol⁻¹. Calculate the **upper inversion temperature** of the gas. Given $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹.

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

Refer to the **Exhaustive Derivation 1** in Section 3.1.

**Recap of key steps:**

1. Treat $H$ as a function of $(T, P)$:
$$dH = C_P dT + \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T dP$$

2. Impose isenthalpic condition $dH = 0$:
$$\mu_{JT} = \left( \frac{\partial T}{\partial P} \right)_H = -\frac{1}{C_P} \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T$$

3. Use the identity $\left( \dfrac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T = V - T \left( \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P$ (derived from the Maxwell relation on $dH = TdS + VdP$).

4. Final expression:
$$\boxed{\mu_{JT} = \frac{1}{C_P} \left[ T \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P - V \right]}$$

For an ideal gas, $V = \dfrac{nRT}{P}$, so $\left( \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P = \dfrac{V}{T}$, giving $\mu_{JT} = 0$.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

Using the upper inversion temperature formula derived in Section 3.3:

$$T_{i,u} = \frac{2a}{Rb}$$

**Substitute values:**

$$T_{i,u} = \frac{2 \times 0.138}{8.314 \times 3.9 \times 10^{-5}}$$

**Numerator:** $2 \times 0.138 = 0.276$ Pa·m⁶·mol⁻²

**Denominator:** $8.314 \times 3.9 \times 10^{-5} = 3.242 \times 10^{-4}$ J·m³·mol⁻²·K⁻¹

$$T_{i,u} = \frac{0.276}{3.242 \times 10^{-4}} = 851.3 \text{ K}$$

$$\boxed{T_{i,u} \approx 851 \text{ K}}$$

**Interpretation:** Above 851 K, the gas will *heat* on throttling; below 851 K, it will *cool*. This temperature is critical for designing JT-based cooling cycles.

---

#### **Question B [KTU University Exam – Model 2024]**

**(a)** Starting from the fundamental property relation for $G$, derive the expression for the **change in Gibbs free energy** during the **isothermal expansion of an ideal gas** from state $(P_1, V_1)$ to $(P_2, V_2)$.

**(b)** 5 moles of an ideal gas expand isothermally and reversibly at 300 K from a volume of 2 L to 10 L. Calculate:
   (i) $\Delta G$
   (ii) $\Delta H$
   (iii) $\Delta S_{\text{system}}$
   (iv) Is the process spontaneous? Justify.

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

Refer to **Exhaustive Derivation 2** in Section 3.2.

**Recap of key steps:**

1. From $G = H - TS$:
$$dG = dH - TdS - SdT$$

2. Substitute $dH = dU + PdV + VdP$ and $dU = TdS - PdV$ (reversible):
$$dG = VdP - SdT$$

3. For isothermal process, $dT = 0$:
$$dG = VdP$$

4. For ideal gas, $V = \dfrac{nRT}{P}$:
$$dG = nRT \frac{dP}{P}$$

5. Integrate from State 1 to State 2:
$$\boxed{\Delta G = nRT \ln \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right) = nRT \ln \left( \frac{V_1}{V_2} \right)}$$

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $n = 5$ mol, $T = 300$ K, $V_1 = 2$ L, $V_2 = 10$ L, $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹.

**(i) Calculate $\Delta G$:**

$$\Delta G = nRT \ln \left( \frac{V_1}{V_2} \right) = (5)(8.314)(300) \ln \left( \frac{2}{10} \right)$$

$$= 12{,}471 \times \ln(0.2) = 12{,}471 \times (-1.6094)$$

$$\boxed{\Delta G = -20{,}071.7 \text{ J} \approx -20.07 \text{ kJ}}$$

**(ii) Calculate $\Delta H$:**

For an ideal gas, enthalpy depends only on temperature. Since the process is **isothermal**, $\Delta H = 0$.

$$\boxed{\Delta H = 0 \text{ J}}$$

**(iii) Calculate $\Delta S_{\text{system}}$:**

For a reversible isothermal ideal gas process:

$$\Delta S_{sys} = nR \ln \left( \frac{V_2}{V_1} \right) = (5)(8.314) \ln(5)$$

$$= 41.57 \times 1.6094 = 66.91 \text{ J/K}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta S_{sys} \approx 66.91 \text{ J/K}}$$

**Verification:** $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S = 0 - (300)(66.91) = -20{,}073$ J ✓

**(iv) Spontaneity Check:**

Since $\Delta G = -20.07$ kJ $< 0$ at constant T and P, the process is **spontaneous** in the forward (expansion) direction.

$$\boxed{\text{Yes, the expansion is spontaneous.}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing throttling with free expansion:** Throttling is *isenthalpic* ($H$ = const), free expansion is *isothermal + adiabatic* (for ideal gas; $U$ = const for adiabatic rigid container). Mixing these up costs **2–3 marks**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in $\mu_{JT}$:** Always state units (K·Pa⁻¹ or K·bar⁻¹) and use consistent SI units throughout. A unit mismatch is a **1-mark deduction**.
>
> 3. **Sign error in $\Delta G$ expression:** $\Delta G = nRT \ln(P_2/P_1)$, NOT $\ln(P_1/P_2)$. Wrong sign ⇒ wrong spontaneity interpretation ⇒ **full mark loss on the conclusion part**.
>
> 4. **Missing "for ideal gas" qualifier:** Saying "$\mu_{JT} = 0$" without specifying "for an ideal gas" loses **1 mark**.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to verify with $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ in numerical problems:** Cross-checking your numerical result with this identity earns the examiner's "good impression" mark (**+1 to +2 marks** in borderline cases).
>
> 6. **Confusing inversion temperature with critical temperature:** They are *different* concepts. $T_c$ is from the EOS (van der Waals), $T_i$ is from the JT condition $\mu_{JT} = 0$.
>
> 7. **Not drawing/labeling the throttling process diagram:** For full-mark Part B answers, always include a labeled schematic of the throttling device with states 1 and 2 marked.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Joule–Thomson expansion** is an *irreversible, adiabatic, isenthalpic* process ($H_1 = H_2$), carried out through a *throttling device* (valve, porous plug).
- [x] **Joule–Thomson coefficient:** $\mu_{JT} = \left( \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P} \right)_H = \dfrac{1}{C_P} \left[ T \left( \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P - V \right]$.
- [x] **Sign of $\mu_{JT}$:** positive → cooling, negative → heating, zero → inversion temperature.
- [x] **Ideal gas → $\mu_{JT} = 0$**: No temperature change on throttling. JT effect is meaningful *only* for real gases.
- [x] **Upper inversion temperature for vdW gas:** $T_{i,u} = \dfrac{2a}{Rb}$.
- [x] **Gibbs free energy:** $G = H - TS$; differential: $dG = VdP - SdT$.
- [x] **Isothermal ideal gas:** $\Delta G = nRT \ln\left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right) = nRT \ln\left( \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} \right)$.
- [x] **Real gas (isothermal):** $\Delta G = nRT \ln\left( \dfrac{f_2}{f_1} \right)$ (fugacity form).
- [x] **Spontaneity at constant T, P:** $\Delta G < 0$ (spontaneous), $\Delta G = 0$ (equilibrium), $\Delta G > 0$ (non-spontaneous).
- [x] **Maximum non-PV work:** $W_{max,non\text{-}PV} = -\Delta G$.
- [x] **General identity:** $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ — always cross-check numerical answers.
- [x] **KTU 2024 emphasis:** Be ready to derive $\mu_{JT}$, explain JT cooling/heating with examples (H₂, He for heating; N₂, O₂, CO₂ for cooling at room T), and calculate $\Delta G$ for isothermal ideal-gas processes.
- [x] **Industrial relevance:** JT effect underpins Linde/Hampson liquefaction cycles; ΔG governs reactor feasibility, phase equilibrium, and electrochemical cell design.
- [x] **Always include a labeled diagram** of the throttling process in 14-mark answers.
- [x] **Common pitfall to avoid:** "Throttling = free expansion" — they are *different* processes with different conserved quantities.
- [x] **Frequent KTU 2024 question type:** "Show that $\mu_{JT} = 0$ for an ideal gas" (3–7 marks).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equilibrium Constant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Equilibrium Constant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Equilibrium Constant** is a dimensionless quantity (when expressed using activities or fugacities) that characterizes the position of thermodynamic equilibrium for a reversible chemical reaction at a given temperature. Mathematically, for a generalized homogeneous chemical reaction:

$$aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$$

the **thermodynamic equilibrium constant** $K_a$ is rigorously defined in terms of the activities ($a_i$) of the reacting species evaluated at equilibrium:

$$K_a = \frac{a_C^{c} \cdot a_D^{d}}{a_A^{a} \cdot a_B^{b}}$$

where $a_i = \dfrac{f_i}{f_i^{\circ}}$ is the **activity** of species $i$, defined as the ratio of its fugacity $f_i$ in the reacting mixture to its fugacity in the **standard reference state** $f_i^{\circ}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The equilibrium constant is *strictly* a function of temperature. It is **independent** of total pressure, initial composition, and the presence of inert species. This is a frequent two-mark question.

## 1.2 Variants of the Equilibrium Constant

Depending on the basis chosen, the equilibrium constant is denoted differently. Each variant is related to $K_a$ through a correction factor.

| Symbol | Basis | Domain of Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $K_a$ | Activity | Universal (most rigorous) |
| $K_p$ | Partial Pressure | Gas-phase reactions |
| $K_c$ | Molar Concentration | Liquid-phase / ideal solutions |
| $K_x$ | Mole Fraction | Liquid / solid phases |
| $K_\phi$ | Fugacity Coefficient | Real gases at moderate to high pressure |
| $K_\gamma$ | Activity Coefficient | Non-ideal liquid solutions |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Two-Way Door of a Concert Hall:** Imagine a concert hall with 1000 people and two automatic sliding doors — one marked "IN" and the other "OUT." Initially, people stream in rapidly. As the hall fills, the rate of entry slows (less crowd outside) while the exit rate increases (more people wanting to leave). Eventually, the rate of people entering equals the rate leaving — the system is at **dynamic equilibrium**. The *ratio* of the concentrations inside the hall to those outside the hall becomes a constant for that temperature (i.e., that crowd mood). Cool the hall (lower T) and fewer people want to leave, shifting the equilibrium inward — mirroring the *van 't Hoff* effect.

The **equilibrium constant** is the steady-state mathematical "signature" of this dynamic two-way traffic — it tells us, at a given temperature, the precise composition at which the forward and reverse reaction rates balance perfectly.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermodynamic Foundation — The Law of Mass Action

The Norwegian chemists **Guldberg** and **Waage** (1864) first proposed the *Law of Mass Action*: at equilibrium, the ratio of product of active masses (concentrations) of products raised to their stoichiometric coefficients to that of reactants is a constant at a given temperature. For an **ideal system**, this becomes:

$$K_c = \frac{[C]^c[D]^d}{[A]^a[B]^b}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although historical, this *Law* is **empirical** and applies only to ideal systems. The true, universal statement is the **thermodynamic equilibrium constant** $K_a$ defined via activities.

## 2.2 Standard State Conventions in KTU Syllabus

- **Gases:** Fugacity of pure component at **1 bar** (or 1 atm in older texts) and the system temperature → $f_i^{\circ} = 1$ bar.
- **Liquids / Solids:** Pure liquid or pure solid at 1 bar and system temperature.
- **Solutes (aqueous):** 1 molal (1 m) ideal-dilute solution.

## 2.3 Why $K$ Depends ONLY on Temperature — Step Logic

1. From fundamental thermodynamics, the Gibbs free energy change at any state relates to the standard state: $\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$, where $Q$ is the **reaction quotient**.
2. At equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$, so: $0 = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln K \Rightarrow \Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$.
3. Because $\Delta G^{\circ}$ is a property of the standard state (which is defined at the system temperature), $K$ depends only on $T$.
4. Pressure, composition, and catalysts do not alter $\Delta G^{\circ}$ → therefore they cannot alter $K$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorise this table — it accounts for nearly **80% of ESE numericals** on this topic.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
| :-- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$ | Master link between $\Delta G^{\circ}$ and $K$ |
| 2 | $K_p = K_c (RT)^{\Delta n}$ | Gas-phase relation, $\Delta n = \sum \nu_{\text{prod}} - \sum \nu_{\text{react}}$ |
| 3 | $K_p = K_x (P)^{\Delta n}$ | For ideal gas mixture at total pressure $P$ |
| 4 | $K_a = K_\phi \cdot K_p$ | Real gases: $K_\phi = \prod (\phi_i)^{\nu_i}$ |
| 5 | $K_a = K_\gamma \cdot K_x$ | Non-ideal liquids: $K_\gamma = \prod (\gamma_i)^{\nu_i}$ |
| 6 | $\dfrac{d \ln K}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{RT^{2}}$ | Differential *van 't Hoff* equation |
| 7 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1}-\dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | Integrated form (constant $\Delta H^{\circ}$) |
| 8 | $K_p = \prod (p_i^{\text{eq}})^{\nu_i}$ | Partial pressure definition (bar basis) |
| 9 | $K_c = \prod (C_i^{\text{eq}})^{\nu_i}$ | Concentration definition (mol/L basis) |
| 10 | $\sum y_i = 1$ | Constraint used to solve for equilibrium composition |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit trap:** In strict thermodynamic form, $K$ is **dimensionless**. Engineers often retain units (bar$^{\Delta n}$ or (mol/L)$^{\Delta n}$) for convenience. For KTU numericals, *retain the units* when substituting into the $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$ expression, since $R$ in J/mol·K requires $K$ to carry units in bar (or atm).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ammonia synthesis (Haber–Bosch):** $K_p$ determines the maximum attainable conversion of $N_2 + 3H_2 \rightleftharpoons 2NH_3$ at 400–500 °C and 150–300 bar. Reactor designers use $K_p$ to size catalyst beds.
- **Sulphuric acid contact process:** Equilibrium constant of $2SO_2 + O_2 \rightleftharpoons 2SO_3$ dictates operating temperature.
- **Methanol, ethylene oxide, hydrogenation** reactors — all rely on $K$ for economic feasibility.
- **Petroleum refining:** Catalytic cracking and reforming equilibria guide product distribution.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relation Between $K_p$ and $K_c$

**Statement:** For an ideal-gas reaction, $K_p$ and $K_c$ are related by:

$$K_p = K_c (RT)^{\Delta n}$$

### Detailed Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Express equilibrium constants in their most fundamental forms.**

For the reaction $aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$ in the gas phase:

$$K_p = \frac{(p_C)^{c}(p_D)^{d}}{(p_A)^{a}(p_B)^{b}}$$

$$K_c = \frac{[C]^{c}[D]^{d}}{[A]^{a}[B]^{b}}$$

**Step 2 — Use the ideal-gas equation of state.**

For each species, $p_i V = n_i RT$, which gives $p_i = \dfrac{n_i}{V} RT = C_i \, RT$, where $C_i$ is the molar concentration (mol/m³ or mol/L).

**Step 3 — Substitute $p_i = C_i RT$ into the $K_p$ expression.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_p &= \frac{\big(C_C \, RT\big)^{c}\big(C_D \, RT\big)^{d}}{\big(C_A \, RT\big)^{a}\big(C_B \, RT\big)^{b}} \\
&= \frac{(C_C)^{c}(C_D)^{d}}{(C_A)^{a}(C_B)^{b}} \cdot \frac{(RT)^{c}(RT)^{d}}{(RT)^{a}(RT)^{b}} \\
&= K_c \cdot (RT)^{(c+d)-(a+b)} \\
&= K_c \, (RT)^{\Delta n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final compact result.**

$$\boxed{\,K_p = K_c (RT)^{\Delta n}\,} \quad \text{where} \quad \Delta n = \sum \nu_{\text{products}} - \sum \nu_{\text{reactants}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> When $\Delta n = 0$ (e.g., $N_2 + 3H_2 \rightleftharpoons 2NH_3$ — products also have 4 moles), then $K_p = K_c$ exactly, and the numerical value is *pressure-independent*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Differential Form of the van 't Hoff Equation

**Statement:** $\dfrac{d \ln K}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{RT^{2}}$

### Detailed Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Start from the fundamental link between $K$ and $\Delta G^{\circ}$.**

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ln K = -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{RT}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate $\ln K$ with respect to $T$ at constant $P$.**

$$\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = -\frac{1}{R}\frac{d}{dT}\!\left(\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{T}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the quotient rule for differentiation.**

$$
\frac{d}{dT}\!\left(\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{T}\right) = \frac{T \cdot \dfrac{d(\Delta G^{\circ})}{dT} - \Delta G^{\circ}}{T^{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Use the Gibbs–Helmholtz equation, $\dfrac{d(\Delta G^{\circ}/T)}{dT} = -\dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^{2}}$.**

Substituting:

$$
\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = -\frac{1}{R}\left(-\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^{2}}\right) = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{RT^{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Final result.**

$$\boxed{\,\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{RT^{2}}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign of $\Delta H^{\circ}$ decides temperature sensitivity:**
> - **Exothermic** ($\Delta H^{\circ} < 0$): $K$ **decreases** with $T$ — Le Chatelier's principle.
> - **Endothermic** ($\Delta H^{\circ} > 0$): $K$ **increases** with $T$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Integrated van 't Hoff Equation (Constant $\Delta H^{\circ}$)

**Step 1 — Rearrange the differential form.**

$$\int_{K_1}^{K_2} d(\ln K) = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R}\frac{dT}{T^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Take $\Delta H^{\circ}$ as constant (small T range) and integrate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) &= \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} T^{-2}\, dT \\
&= \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \left[-\frac{1}{T}\right]_{T_1}^{T_2} \\
&= \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Final integrated form.**

$$\boxed{\,\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\,}$$

---

## 3.4 Solved Analytical Example (KTU Typical Style)

**Problem:** For the reaction $N_2O_4(g) \rightleftharpoons 2NO_2(g)$ at $25^{\circ}$C, $\Delta G^{\circ} = +4.78$ kJ/mol. Calculate $K_p$ at 25 °C and at 100 °C, given $\Delta H^{\circ} = +57.20$ kJ/mol (assumed constant).

### Solution

**Part (i) — $K_p$ at 25 °C:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta G^{\circ} &= -RT \ln K_p \\
\ln K_p &= -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{RT} = -\frac{4780}{(8.314)(298.15)} = -1.928 \\
K_p &= e^{-1.928} = 0.1453
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (ii) — $K_p$ at 100 °C (373.15 K):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{0.1453}\right) &= \frac{57200}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{298.15} - \frac{1}{373.15}\right) \\
&= 6880.7 \times (3.354 \times 10^{-3} - 2.680 \times 10^{-3}) \\
&= 6880.7 \times 0.674 \times 10^{-3} = 4.638 \\
\frac{K_2}{0.1453} &= e^{4.638} = 103.4 \\
K_2 &= 0.1453 \times 103.4 = 15.02
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** $K_p$ increased from 0.145 to 15.02 — about a **100-fold increase** — because the reaction is endothermic ($\Delta H^{\circ} > 0$). Heat favours the forward (endothermic) direction. This is a classic KTU-style answer showcasing both van 't Hoff and the master $\Delta G^{\circ}$ relation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Relationship Map of Equilibrium Constants

The following Mermaid block renders the full **functional architecture** of how the various forms of the equilibrium constant connect through stoichiometric, thermodynamic, and correction-factor bridges.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart([Start: Thermodynamic Equilibrium Constant K_a]) --> nodeDef
    nodeDef["K_a is the master constant defined via activities a_i = f_i over f_i degree"] --> nodeBran

    nodeBran{K_a expressed for which system?}

    nodeBran -->|Ideal Gas Mixture| nodeA1["Express via partial pressures p_i"]
    nodeBran -->|Real Gas Mixture| nodeA2["Express via fugacity coefficients phi_i"]
    nodeBran -->|Ideal Liquid Solution| nodeA3["Express via mole fractions x_i"]
    nodeBran -->|Non-Ideal Liquid Solution| nodeA4["Express via activity coefficients gamma_i"]
    nodeBran -->|Aqueous Solution| nodeA5["Express via molarities C_i"]

    nodeA1 --> nodeB1["K_p = product of p_i eq raised to nu_i"]
    nodeA2 --> nodeB2["K_a = K_phi times K_p"]
    nodeA3 --> nodeB3["K_x = product of x_i eq raised to nu_i"]
    nodeA4 --> nodeB4["K_a = K_gamma times K_x"]
    nodeA5 --> nodeB5["K_c = product of C_i eq raised to nu_i"]

    nodeB1 --> nodeLink1
    nodeB2 --> nodeLink1
    nodeB3 --> nodeLink1
    nodeB4 --> nodeLink1
    nodeB5 --> nodeLink1

    nodeLink1["Internal Bridge Equations: K_p = K_c times RT to power delta_n, K_p = K_x times P to power delta_n"]

    nodeLink1 --> nodeOut([End: A single value of K_a that uniquely fixes the equilibrium composition at the system temperature])

    nodeLink1 --> nodeVT["Van t Hoff Bridge: d ln K over dT = delta H degree over R T squared, integrated between T1 and T2"]
    nodeVT --> nodeOut
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Effect of Temperature on $K$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeT1["Input: Temperature T1 in Kelvin and known K1"] --> nodeVH["Apply Integrated van t Hoff Equation"]
    nodeT2["Input: New Temperature T2 in Kelvin"] --> nodeVH
    nodeVH["ln of K2 over K1 equals delta H degree over R times 1 over T1 minus 1 over T2"] --> nodeRes["Output: K2 at temperature T2"]
    nodeVH --> nodeCheck
    nodeCheck{Is delta H degree constant over the T1 to T2 range?}
    nodeCheck -->|Yes| nodeRes
    nodeCheck -->|No| nodeVar["Use temperature dependent delta H degree T from Cp data and integrate: d ln K = integral of delta H degree T over R T squared dT"]
    nodeVar --> nodeRes
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Forward and Reverse Rate Balance at Equilibrium

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR["Reactants A and B with stoichiometric coefficients a and b"] --> nodeF["Forward rate r_f = k_f times C_A to power a times C_B to power b"]
    nodeR --> nodeRv["Reverse rate r_r = k_r times C_C to power c times C_D to power d"]
    nodeP["Products C and D with stoichiometric coefficients c and d"] --> nodeF
    nodeP --> nodeRv
    nodeF --> nodeEq["At Equilibrium: r_f equals r_r"]
    nodeRv --> nodeEq
    nodeEq --> nodeK["This yields K_c = k_f over k_r, the equilibrium constant equals ratio of rate constants"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State and explain the thermodynamic equilibrium constant for a homogeneous gas-phase reaction. Why is it called a "thermodynamic" constant?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The thermodynamic equilibrium constant $K_a$ for a homogeneous gas-phase reaction $aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$ is defined as:

$$K_a = \frac{a_C^{c} \, a_D^{d}}{a_A^{a} \, a_B^{b}}$$

where $a_i$ is the activity of the $i^{\text{th}}$ component, defined as $a_i = f_i / f_i^{\circ}$. It is called a *thermodynamic* constant because:
1. It is derived rigorously from the criterion $\Delta G = 0$ at equilibrium and the fundamental relation $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$.
2. It is dimensionless and **depends only on temperature**, not on pressure, composition, or the presence of catalysts.
3. It is valid for *real* systems because it uses activities (which account for non-ideality via fugacities).

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**"The equilibrium constant changes with temperature but not with pressure." Justify the statement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The equilibrium constant $K$ is related to the standard-state Gibbs free energy change by the master equation $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$. Since $\Delta G^{\circ}$ is a **state function** defined for substances in their standard states (1 bar, system temperature), it depends only on temperature. Therefore, $K = \exp(-\Delta G^{\circ} / RT)$ is also a function of temperature alone. A change in total pressure, however, alters the *composition* at which equilibrium is reached (i.e., shifts the equilibrium conversion), but it does *not* alter the value of $K$ itself. This is why catalysts and pressure changes shift the **position of equilibrium** without changing $K$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B1 `(A)` `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship between $K_p$ and $K_c$ for an ideal-gas reaction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the synthesis of ammonia, $N_2(g) + 3H_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2NH_3(g)$, $K_p = 1.45 \times 10^{-5}$ at 500 °C. Calculate $K_x$ at a total pressure of 100 bar. Comment on the effect of pressure on the equilibrium conversion. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to B1 (A)

**Part (a) — Derivation of $K_p$ vs $K_c$ relation (7 marks):**

For the reaction $aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$ in the gas phase, with ideal-gas behaviour:

- $K_p = \dfrac{(p_C)^{c}(p_D)^{d}}{(p_A)^{a}(p_B)^{b}}$ (1 mark)
- $K_c = \dfrac{[C]^{c}[D]^{d}}{[A]^{a}[B]^{b}}$ (1 mark)

Using the ideal-gas relation $p_i = C_i RT$ (1 mark), substitute into $K_p$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_p &= \frac{(C_C RT)^{c}(C_D RT)^{d}}{(C_A RT)^{a}(C_B RT)^{b}} \\
&= \frac{(C_C)^{c}(C_D)^{d}}{(C_A)^{a}(C_B)^{b}} \cdot \frac{(RT)^{c+d}}{(RT)^{a+b}} \\
&= K_c \,(RT)^{\Delta n} \quad \text{where } \Delta n = (c+d)-(a+b) \quad \text{(3 marks)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final boxed result:** $K_p = K_c (RT)^{\Delta n}$ (1 mark).

---

**Part (b) — $K_x$ at 100 bar (7 marks):**

The relation $K_p = K_x (P)^{\Delta n}$ is used. For the ammonia synthesis:

$$\Delta n = 2 - (1+3) = -2 \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

$$K_x = \frac{K_p}{(P)^{\Delta n}} = \frac{K_p}{(P)^{-2}} = K_p \cdot P^{2} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

Substituting $K_p = 1.45 \times 10^{-5}$ and $P = 100$ bar:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_x &= (1.45 \times 10^{-5}) \times (100)^{2} \\
&= (1.45 \times 10^{-5}) \times 10^{4} \\
&= 0.145 \quad (3 \text{ marks})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment on pressure effect (1 mark):** Since $\Delta n = -2 < 0$, increasing pressure **increases** $K_x$, which means the equilibrium shifts toward the side with fewer moles — i.e., toward $NH_3$. Therefore, **high pressure favours ammonia synthesis**, which is why the Haber–Bosch process operates at 150–300 bar.

---

### Question B1 `(B)` `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Alt Set]`

**(a)** State and derive the integrated form of the van 't Hoff equation, assuming $\Delta H^{\circ}$ is independent of temperature. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The equilibrium constant for a reaction is $K_{p,1} = 10$ at 600 K. If the standard enthalpy of reaction is $\Delta H^{\circ} = -80$ kJ/mol, calculate $K_{p,2}$ at 800 K. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to B1 (B)

**Part (a) — Integrated van 't Hoff equation (7 marks):**

The differential form of the van 't Hoff equation is (2 marks):

$$\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{RT^{2}}$$

Rearranging and integrating between limits $(T_1, K_1)$ and $(T_2, K_2)$, with $\Delta H^{\circ}$ taken as constant (2 marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{K_1}^{K_2} d(\ln K) &= \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^{2}} \\
\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) &= \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \left[-\frac{1}{T}\right]_{T_1}^{T_2} \quad (2 \text{ marks}) \\
\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) &= \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) \quad (1 \text{ mark})
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Calculation of $K_{p,2}$ at 800 K (7 marks):**

Given: $K_1 = 10$, $T_1 = 600$ K, $T_2 = 800$ K, $\Delta H^{\circ} = -80{,}000$ J/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K. (1 mark for data)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{10}\right) &= \frac{-80{,}000}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{600} - \frac{1}{800}\right) \quad (2 \text{ marks}) \\
&= -9622.3 \times (1.6667 \times 10^{-3} - 1.25 \times 10^{-3}) \\
&= -9622.3 \times 4.1667 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= -4.009 \quad (2 \text{ marks})
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{K_2}{10} &= e^{-4.009} = 0.01818 \\
K_2 &= 10 \times 0.01818 = 0.1818 \approx 0.182 \quad (2 \text{ marks})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment:** $K$ decreased with temperature (from 10 to 0.182) — consistent with the exothermic nature ($\Delta H^{\circ} < 0$) of the reaction, in line with Le Chatelier's principle.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign errors in van 't Hoff:** The most common mistake is writing $\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ instead of $\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1}-\dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$. Watch the limit ordering carefully.
> 2. **Forgetting $\Delta n$ sign:** For $N_2O_4 \rightleftharpoons 2NO_2$, $\Delta n = +1$. For $2SO_2 + O_2 \rightleftharpoons 2SO_3$, $\Delta n = -1$. Mixing these up flips the pressure-dependence conclusion.
> 3. **Units of $K$ in $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$:** If you substitute $K$ without units, the logarithm becomes invalid. Use **bar** or **atm** consistently and never mix.
> 4. **Assuming $\Delta H^{\circ}$ constant over wide T range:** For spans >100 K, you must integrate using the Kirchhoff relation $\Delta H^{\circ}(T) = \Delta H^{\circ}(T_{\text{ref}}) + \int \Delta C_p \, dT$ — a frequent 14-mark follow-up.
> 5. **Forgetting stoichiometric coefficients** when raising $K_x$ to a power in $K_p = K_x P^{\Delta n}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Definition:** $K_a = \prod a_i^{\nu_i}$; dimensionless, depends only on $T$.
- **Master link:** $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$ — your single most powerful equation.
- **Gas-phase relation:** $K_p = K_c(RT)^{\Delta n}$ and $K_p = K_x (P)^{\Delta n}$.
- **Real-gas correction:** $K_a = K_\phi K_p$; **real-liquid correction:** $K_a = K_\gamma K_x$.
- **van 't Hoff (differential):** $\dfrac{d \ln K}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{RT^{2}}$.
- **van 't Hoff (integrated, const. $\Delta H^{\circ}$):** $\ln(K_2/K_1) = \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1}-\dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$.
- **Le Chatelier rule from sign of $\Delta H^{\circ}$:** Exothermic → $K$ falls with $T$; endothermic → $K$ rises with $T$.
- **Le Chatelier rule from sign of $\Delta n$:** $\Delta n < 0$ → high $P$ favours products; $\Delta n > 0$ → high $P$ favours reactants.
- **Standard state:** 1 bar for gases; pure liquid / pure solid at 1 bar for condensed phases; 1 molal for aqueous solutes.
- **Solver's mantra:** *Always* write the equilibrium expression, the $\Delta n$ value, *then* the appropriate link equation, *then* substitute — in that order.
- **KTU trademark traps:** units of $K$ in the Gibbs relation, sign of $\Delta n$, sign of $\Delta H^{\circ}$, and the $T_1$–$T_2$ ordering in van 't Hoff.
- **Catalysts** shift *rate*, **never** $K$.
- **Inert gases** at constant volume do not shift $K$; at constant total pressure they dilute reactants and may shift equilibrium composition without changing $K$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Temperature on Equilibrium Constant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Effect of Temperature on Equilibrium Constant**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equilibrium Constant ($K$)**: A dimensionless (or concentration/pressure-based) quantity that expresses the ratio of product activities to reactant activities at chemical equilibrium, where the rate of the forward reaction equals the rate of the reverse reaction.

The **Effect of Temperature on the Equilibrium Constant** is mathematically described by the **Van't Hoff Equation**, which quantifies the rate at which the equilibrium constant $K$ changes as a function of absolute temperature $T$, governed by the standard enthalpy change of reaction $\Delta H^\circ$.

The fundamental differential form of the Van't Hoff Equation is:

$$\frac{d(\ln K)}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2}$$

where:
- $K$ = Equilibrium constant (dimensionless or in appropriate units)
- $T$ = Absolute temperature in **Kelvin (K)**
- $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $\Delta H^\circ$ = Standard enthalpy change of reaction in **J/mol**

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 3)**: This topic is the cornerstone for solving multi-reaction equilibrium problems, reactor design under non-isothermal conditions, and predicting product distribution in industrial reactors (e.g., Haber-Bosch, Contact Process).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **see-saw** in a playground where one side is labeled "Reactants" and the other side is labeled "Products." The pivot point (fulcrum) is the **equilibrium state**, and $K$ tells you how tilted the see-saw is. Now imagine you place a **hot blanket** over one side of the see-saw:

- If the **forward reaction releases heat** (exothermic, $\Delta H^\circ < 0$), the system "feels" too warm on the product side as temperature increases. To restore balance (Le Chatelier's Principle), it **shifts the see-saw back toward reactants**, and $K$ **decreases**.
- If the **forward reaction absorbs heat** (endothermic, $\Delta H^\circ > 0$), increasing temperature is like adding weight to the reactants' side. The system compensates by **shifting toward products**, and $K$ **increases**.

The Van't Hoff equation is the **mathematical thermometer** that quantifies exactly how much $K$ will tilt for every degree of temperature change.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Value | Unit |
|--------|----------|-------|------|
| $R$ | Universal Gas Constant | 8.314 | J/(mol·K) |
| $R$ | Universal Gas Constant | 1.987 | cal/(mol·K) |
| $T$ | Absolute Temperature | Variable | K |
| $\Delta H^\circ$ | Standard Enthalpy Change | Reaction-specific | J/mol or cal/mol |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Does Temperature Affect $K$?

The equilibrium constant is fundamentally a function of the **standard Gibbs free energy change**:

$$\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$$

Differentiating this expression with respect to temperature (at constant pressure) using the **Gibbs-Helmholtz Equation** gives rise to the Van't Hoff relation. The equilibrium constant is essentially a **thermodynamic state function** that depends on:
- The **enthalpic content** of the reaction ($\Delta H^\circ$)
- The **entropic content** of the reaction ($\Delta S^\circ$)

Since both $\Delta H^\circ$ and $\Delta S^\circ$ are weakly temperature-dependent, $K$ itself is temperature-dependent.

### 2.2 Boundary Conditions & Validity

- The Van't Hoff equation is **valid for constant-pressure systems** where the reaction mixture behaves ideally.
- The **sign of $\Delta H^\circ$** dictates the direction of change of $K$ with $T$.
- If $\Delta H^\circ$ is assumed **constant over a temperature range**, the integrated form (Equation 2 below) is used.
- If $\Delta H^\circ$ **varies with temperature** (due to heat capacity changes), the more complex **Kirchhoff-integrated form** is required.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE** — Memorize these for ESE and module tests.

| # | Equation | Description | Validity / Notes |
|---|----------|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{d(\ln K)}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2}$ | Van't Hoff (Differential) | Exact; assumes ideal behavior |
| 2 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | Van't Hoff (Integrated) | Assumes $\Delta H^\circ$ = constant |
| 3 | $\ln K = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT} + \dfrac{\Delta S^\circ}{R}$ | Linearized form of $\Delta G^\circ = -RT\ln K$ | Plot of $\ln K$ vs $1/T$ gives straight line |
| 4 | $\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{K_1}{K_2}\right)$ | Alternative rearrangement | Easier for log-base-10 calculations |
| 5 | $\log\!\left(\dfrac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{2.303\,R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | Log-base-10 form | When $R = 1.987$ cal/(mol·K) |
| 6 | $\ln K = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ_0}{RT} + \dfrac{\Delta a}{R}\ln T + \dfrac{\Delta b}{2R}T + \dfrac{\Delta c}{6R}T^2 + I$ | Variable $\Delta H^\circ$ form | $\Delta C_p = \Delta a + \Delta b\,T + \Delta c\,T^2$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Ammonia Synthesis (Haber-Bosch Process)**: The reaction N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃ is exothermic. Operators must balance **higher temperatures** (faster kinetics) against **lower temperatures** (higher $K$, better yield).
2. **Steam Reforming of Methane**: An endothermic reaction favored at **high temperatures** to maximize $K$ and thus hydrogen yield.
3. **Sulfuric Acid Production (Contact Process)**: 2SO₂ + O₂ ⇌ 2SO₃ is exothermic; optimum temperature chosen via $K$ analysis.
4. **Cryogenic Air Separation**: Liquefaction temperatures determined by phase-equilibrium constants.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Differential Van't Hoff Equation

**Step 1:** Start from the fundamental thermodynamic relation linking $K$ to the standard Gibbs free energy change:

$$\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $\ln K$:

$$\ln K = -\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{RT}$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate both sides with respect to $T$ at constant pressure. Applying the **quotient rule** on the right-hand side:

$$\frac{d(\ln K)}{dT} = -\frac{1}{R}\,\frac{d}{dT}\!\left(\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{T}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Apply the **Gibbs-Helmholtz Equation**, which states:

$$\frac{d}{dT}\!\left(\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{T}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{T^2}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the Gibbs-Helmholtz result into the differentiated expression:

$$\frac{d(\ln K)}{dT} = -\frac{1}{R}\!\left(-\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{T^2}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Simplify the negative signs:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{d(\ln K)}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation**: A positive $\Delta H^\circ$ (endothermic) means $d(\ln K)/dT > 0$, so $K$ rises with temperature. A negative $\Delta H^\circ$ (exothermic) means $d(\ln K)/dT < 0$, so $K$ falls with temperature.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Integrated Van't Hoff Equation (Constant $\Delta H^\circ$)

**Step 1:** Rearrange the differential form by multiplying both sides by $dT$:

$$d(\ln K) = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2}\,dT$$

**Step 2:** Assuming $\Delta H^\circ$ is constant over the temperature range $[T_1, T_2]$, integrate both sides between states 1 and 2:

$$\int_{K_1}^{K_2} d(\ln K) = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^2}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the left-hand side:

$$\ln K_2 - \ln K_1 = \ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the right-hand side:

$$\int_{T_1}^{T_2} T^{-2}\,dT = \left[-\frac{1}{T}\right]_{T_1}^{T_2} = -\frac{1}{T_2} + \frac{1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}$$

**Step 5:** Combine the results:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $-1$ to obtain the canonical form:

$$\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)\;}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation When $\Delta H^\circ$ Varies with Temperature (Kirchhoff's Law)

**Step 1:** By Kirchhoff's Law of Thermochemistry:

$$\Delta H^\circ(T) = \Delta H^\circ_0 + \int_0^T \Delta C_p\,dT$$

**Step 2:** For a general heat capacity correlation $\Delta C_p = \Delta a + \Delta b\,T + \Delta c\,T^2$:

$$\Delta H^\circ(T) = \Delta H^\circ_0 + \Delta a\,T + \frac{\Delta b}{2}T^2 + \frac{\Delta c}{3}T^3$$

**Step 3:** Substitute this into the differential Van't Hoff equation:

$$d(\ln K) = \frac{1}{RT^2}\!\left[\Delta H^\circ_0 + \Delta a\,T + \frac{\Delta b}{2}T^2 + \frac{\Delta c}{3}T^3\right]dT$$

**Step 4:** Divide through by $T^2$:

$$d(\ln K) = \left[\frac{\Delta H^\circ_0}{RT^2} + \frac{\Delta a}{RT} + \frac{\Delta b}{2R} + \frac{\Delta c}{3R}T\right]dT$$

**Step 5:** Integrate term-by-term:

$$\ln K = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ_0}{RT} + \frac{\Delta a}{R}\ln T + \frac{\Delta b}{2R}T + \frac{\Delta c}{6R}T^2 + I$$

where $I$ is the integration constant determined from a known reference point $(T_{\text{ref}}, K_{\text{ref}})$.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem**: For the gas-phase reaction $A \rightleftharpoons B$, the equilibrium constant $K_p = 0.05$ at 300 K and $K_p = 0.20$ at 500 K. Assuming $\Delta H^\circ$ is constant, calculate:
> (a) The standard enthalpy of reaction $\Delta H^\circ$
> (b) The standard entropy of reaction $\Delta S^\circ$ at 300 K
> (c) The temperature at which $K_p = 1.0$

**Part (a) — Calculate $\Delta H^\circ$:**

Using the integrated Van't Hoff equation:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{0.20}{0.05}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{8.314}\!\left(\frac{1}{500} - \frac{1}{300}\right)$$

$$\ln(4) = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{8.314}\!\left(\frac{300 - 500}{150000}\right)$$

$$1.3863 = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{8.314}\!\left(\frac{-200}{150000}\right)$$

$$1.3863 = \frac{\Delta H^\circ \times 200}{8.314 \times 150000}$$

$$\Delta H^\circ = \frac{1.3863 \times 8.314 \times 150000}{200}$$

$$\Delta H^\circ = \frac{1.3863 \times 8.314 \times 750}{1}$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta H^\circ = 8644.2 \text{ J/mol} \approx 8.64 \text{ kJ/mol}\;}$$

> **Interpretation**: Positive $\Delta H^\circ$ confirms the reaction is **endothermic**, consistent with $K$ increasing as $T$ rises.

**Part (b) — Calculate $\Delta S^\circ$ at 300 K:**

From $\Delta G^\circ = -RT\ln K$ and $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ$:

$$\Delta S^\circ = \frac{\Delta H^\circ + RT\ln K}{T}$$

$$\Delta S^\circ = \frac{8644.2 + (8.314)(300)\ln(0.05)}{300}$$

$$\Delta S^\circ = \frac{8644.2 + (8.314)(300)(-2.9957)}{300}$$

$$\Delta S^\circ = \frac{8644.2 - 7471.6}{300} = \frac{1172.6}{300}$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta S^\circ = 3.91 \text{ J/(mol·K)}\;}$$

**Part (c) — Find $T$ when $K_p = 1.0$:**

Using the integrated form with $K_2 = 1.0$ and $K_1 = 0.05$ at $T_1 = 300$ K:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{1.0}{0.05}\right) = -\frac{8644.2}{8.314}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{300}\right)$$

$$\ln(20) = -1039.6\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - 0.003333\right)$$

$$2.9957 = -1039.6\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - 0.003333\right)$$

$$\frac{1}{T_2} - 0.003333 = -\frac{2.9957}{1039.6} = -0.002882$$

$$\frac{1}{T_2} = 0.003333 - 0.002882 = 0.000451$$

$$\boxed{\;T_2 = 2217 \text{ K}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight**: $T_2 = 2217$ K is impractically high, demonstrating why many industrial processes require **catalysts** to achieve acceptable rates even when $K$ is unfavorable at lower temperatures.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Temperature Influence Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reactants at Temperature T1] --> B[Heat Exchanger / Reactor]
    B --> C[Reaction Occurs]
    C --> D{System Approaches Equilibrium}
    D --> E[Equilibrium Constant K at T1]
    B --> F[Temperature Change T1 to T2]
    F --> G[Recompute K via Van't Hoff]
    G --> E2[New Equilibrium Constant K at T2]
    E2 --> H{Delta H Positive?}
    H -->|Yes Endothermic| I[K Increases as T Rises]
    H -->|No Exothermic| J[K Decreases as T Rises]
    I --> K[New Equilibrium Composition]
    J --> K
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Computational Workflow for $K(T)$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT PARAMETERS"]
        I1[T1, K1 known]
        I2[Delta H from data tables]
        I3[Target Temperature T2]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE["COMPUTATION CORE"]
        C1[Apply Integrated Van't Hoff]
        C2[ln K2 equals ln K1 minus DeltaH over R times 1/T2 minus 1/T1]
        C3[Check if Delta H varies with T]
        C3 -->|Yes| C4[Use Kirchhoff Integration]
        C3 -->|No| C5[Use Simplified Form]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["RESULTS"]
        O1[New Equilibrium Constant K2]
        O2[Equilibrium Composition]
        O3[Direction of Shift]
    end

    I1 --> C1
    I2 --> C1
    I3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C4 --> O1
    C5 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O1 --> O3
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree — Exothermic vs Endothermic Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Analyze Reaction Enthalpy] --> Q1{Is Delta H negative?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| EXO[Exothermic Reaction]
    Q1 -->|No| END[Endothermic Reaction]
    EXO --> E1[Heat is a Product]
    E1 --> E2[Increasing T shifts equilibrium left]
    E2 --> E3[K decreases with T]
    END --> EN1[Heat is a Reactant]
    EN1 --> EN2[Increasing T shifts equilibrium right]
    EN2 --> EN3[K increases with T]
    E3 --> OUT[Plot ln K vs 1/T gives negative slope]
    EN3 --> OUT2[Plot ln K vs 1/T gives positive slope]
```

### 4.4 Van't Hoff Plot — Schematic Visualization

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PLOT[Van't Hoff Plot: ln K versus 1 over T]
        XAXIS[1 over T axis increasing left to right]
        YAXIS[ln K axis increasing bottom to top]
        SLOPE1[Slope equals negative Delta H over R]
    end
    XAXIS --> PLOT
    YAXIS --> PLOT
    SLOPE1 --> PLOT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Plotting Tip**: A plot of $\ln K$ versus $1/T$ yields a **straight line** whose **slope is $-\Delta H^\circ / R$**. This is the standard method for extracting $\Delta H^\circ$ from experimental equilibrium data.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**

State and explain the **Van't Hoff Equation** relating the equilibrium constant to temperature. Discuss what happens to $K$ when temperature is increased for an exothermic reaction.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Van't Hoff equation in its differential form is given by:

$$\frac{d(\ln K)}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2}$$

This equation indicates that the rate of change of $\ln K$ with respect to absolute temperature $T$ is directly proportional to the standard enthalpy change of reaction $\Delta H^\circ$.

For an **exothermic reaction**, $\Delta H^\circ < 0$, which makes the right-hand side of the Van't Hoff equation negative. This means $d(\ln K)/dT < 0$, so the equilibrium constant $K$ **decreases** as temperature increases. Physically, this is explained by Le Chatelier's principle: heat acts as a product in an exothermic reaction, so adding heat shifts the equilibrium toward the reactants, reducing $K$.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Equation statement: **1 Mark**
- Explanation of proportionality: **1 Mark**
- Exothermic case analysis: **1 Mark**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**

Derive the **integrated form of the Van't Hoff equation** assuming $\Delta H^\circ$ is constant.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Starting from the differential form:

$$d(\ln K) = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R T^2}\,dT$$

Integrating between states 1 and 2 (with $\Delta H^\circ$ constant):

$$\int_{K_1}^{K_2} d(\ln K) = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^2}$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

This is the integrated Van't Hoff equation valid when $\Delta H^\circ$ is independent of temperature.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Starting equation: **1 Mark**
- Integration steps: **1 Mark**
- Final integrated form: **1 Mark**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

> **ESE Question A** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Main Exam]`

**(a)** The standard equilibrium constant for the reaction:

$$N_2(g) + 3H_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2NH_3(g)$$

is $K_{p,1} = 6.8 \times 10^5$ at 298 K and $K_{p,2} = 8.0 \times 10^4$ at 500 K. Assuming $\Delta H^\circ$ is approximately constant over this range:

**(i)** Calculate the standard enthalpy of reaction $\Delta H^\circ$ in kJ/mol. **(3 Marks)**

**(ii)** Calculate the standard entropy of reaction $\Delta S^\circ$ at 298 K. **(3 Marks)**

**(iii)** Comment on the sign of $\Delta H^\circ$ and what it implies for the effect of temperature on $K_p$. **(1 Mark)**

**(b)** For a different gas-phase reaction, $\Delta H^\circ$ varies with temperature according to:

$$\Delta H^\circ(T) = -45000 + 15\,T \text{ J/mol}$$

If $K = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ at $T = 400$ K:

**(i)** Derive an expression for $\ln K$ as a function of $T$. **(4 Marks)**

**(ii)** Calculate $K$ at $T = 600$ K. **(3 Marks)**

---

### 5.3 Model Solution for ESE Question A

#### Part (a)(i) — Calculate $\Delta H^\circ$ (3 Marks)

Using the integrated Van't Hoff equation:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

Substituting values:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{8.0 \times 10^4}{6.8 \times 10^5}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{8.314}\!\left(\frac{1}{500} - \frac{1}{298}\right)$$

$$\ln(0.1176) = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{8.314}\!\left(\frac{298 - 500}{149000}\right)$$

$$-2.1401 = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{8.314}\!\left(\frac{-202}{149000}\right)$$

$$-2.1401 = \frac{\Delta H^\circ \times 202}{8.314 \times 149000}$$

$$\Delta H^\circ = \frac{-2.1401 \times 8.314 \times 149000}{202}$$

$$\Delta H^\circ = \frac{-2.1401 \times 1.238786}{1} \times 1000$$

$$\Delta H^\circ = -13133 \text{ J/mol}$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta H^\circ \approx -13.13 \text{ kJ/mol}\;}$$

#### Part (a)(ii) — Calculate $\Delta S^\circ$ at 298 K (3 Marks)

From $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ$ and $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$:

$$\Delta S^\circ = \frac{\Delta H^\circ + RT \ln K}{T}$$

$$\Delta S^\circ = \frac{-13133 + (8.314)(298)\ln(6.8 \times 10^5)}{298}$$

$$\Delta S^\circ = \frac{-13133 + 2477.572 \times 13.43}{298}$$

$$\Delta S^\circ = \frac{-13133 + 33273}{298} = \frac{20140}{298}$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta S^\circ \approx 67.6 \text{ J/(mol·K)}\;}$$

#### Part (a)(iii) — Sign Analysis (1 Mark)

The negative value of $\Delta H^\circ = -13.13$ kJ/mol confirms the reaction is **exothermic**. According to the Van't Hoff equation, increasing temperature will **decrease** the equilibrium constant $K_p$, which is consistent with the data: $K_p$ dropped from $6.8 \times 10^5$ at 298 K to $8.0 \times 10^4$ at 500 K. This is the well-known reason why the **Haber-Bosch process** operates at moderate temperatures (700–800 K) using a catalyst to compensate for slower kinetics at lower temperatures.

#### Part (b)(i) — Derive $\ln K$ as function of $T$ (4 Marks)

Starting from the differential Van't Hoff equation:

$$d(\ln K) = \frac{\Delta H^\circ(T)}{RT^2}\,dT = \frac{-45000 + 15T}{RT^2}\,dT$$

$$d(\ln K) = \left(\frac{-45000}{RT^2} + \frac{15}{RT}\right)dT$$

Integrating:

$$\ln K = \frac{45000}{RT} + \frac{15}{R}\ln T + I$$

**Determine the constant $I$** using $K = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ at $T = 400$ K:

$$\ln(2.5 \times 10^{-3}) = \frac{45000}{(8.314)(400)} + \frac{15}{8.314}\ln(400) + I$$

$$-5.9915 = 13.535 + 1.8044 \times 5.9915 + I$$

$$-5.9915 = 13.535 + 10.811 + I$$

$$I = -5.9915 - 13.535 - 10.811 = -30.337$$

$$\boxed{\;\ln K = \frac{45000}{RT} + \frac{15}{R}\ln T - 30.337\;}$$

#### Part (b)(ii) — Calculate $K$ at $T = 600$ K (3 Marks)

$$\ln K = \frac{45000}{(8.314)(600)} + \frac{15}{8.314}\ln(600) - 30.337$$

$$\ln K = 9.0233 + 1.8044 \times 6.3969 - 30.337$$

$$\ln K = 9.0233 + 11.5432 - 30.337 = -9.7705$$

$$K = e^{-9.7705}$$

$$\boxed{\;K \approx 5.74 \times 10^{-5}\;}$$

> **Interpretation**: Despite $\Delta H^\circ$ becoming more positive (less exothermic) at higher $T$, the equilibrium constant **decreased** further because the negative integration constant dominated.

---

> **ESE Question B** `[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Main Exam]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Define the equilibrium constant and derive the **Van't Hoff Equation** in its **differential form** starting from the Gibbs-Helmholtz equation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The decomposition of limestone follows:

$$CaCO_3(s) \rightleftharpoons CaO(s) + CO_2(g)$$

At $T_1 = 1100$ K, $K_{p1} = 3.0$ and at $T_2 = 1200$ K, $K_{p2} = 8.5$ (pressure in atm). Calculate:

**(i)** The standard enthalpy of decomposition $\Delta H^\circ$ assuming it is constant. **(3 Marks)**

**(ii)** The standard Gibbs free energy change $\Delta G^\circ$ at 1100 K. **(2 Marks)**

**(iii)** The temperature at which $K_p = 1.0$. **(2 Marks)**

---

### 5.4 Model Solution for ESE Question B

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Define the equilibrium constant as the ratio of product activities to reactant activities raised to their stoichiometric coefficients at equilibrium.

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Write the fundamental relation:

$$\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Solve for $\ln K$:

$$\ln K = -\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{RT}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Differentiate with respect to $T$ at constant $P$:

$$\frac{d(\ln K)}{dT} = -\frac{1}{R}\frac{d}{dT}\!\left(\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{T}\right)$$

**Step 5 (2 Marks):** State the Gibbs-Helmholtz equation:

$$\frac{d}{dT}\!\left(\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{T}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{T^2}$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Substitute and simplify to obtain:

$$\frac{d(\ln K)}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2}$$

#### Part (b)(i) — Calculate $\Delta H^\circ$ (3 Marks)

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{8.5}{3.0}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{8.314}\!\left(\frac{1}{1200} - \frac{1}{1100}\right)$$

$$\ln(2.833) = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{8.314}\!\left(\frac{1100 - 1200}{1320000}\right)$$

$$1.0409 = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{8.314}\!\left(\frac{-100}{1320000}\right)$$

$$1.0409 = \frac{\Delta H^\circ \times 100}{8.314 \times 1320000}$$

$$\Delta H^\circ = \frac{1.0409 \times 8.314 \times 1320000}{100}$$

$$\Delta H^\circ = \frac{1.0409 \times 8.314 \times 13200}{1}$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta H^\circ = 114217 \text{ J/mol} \approx 114.2 \text{ kJ/mol}\;}$$

#### Part (b)(ii) — Calculate $\Delta G^\circ$ at 1100 K (2 Marks)

$$\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K = -(8.314)(1100)\ln(3.0)$$

$$\Delta G^\circ = -9145.4 \times 1.0986 = -10048 \text{ J/mol}$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta G^\circ \approx -10.05 \text{ kJ/mol}\;}$$

#### Part (b)(iii) — Find $T$ when $K_p = 1.0$ (2 Marks)

Using the integrated form with $K_2 = 1.0$, $K_1 = 3.0$, $T_1 = 1100$ K:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{1.0}{3.0}\right) = -\frac{114217}{8.314}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{1100}\right)$$

$$-1.0986 = -13738.5\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - 0.0009091\right)$$

$$\frac{1}{T_2} - 0.0009091 = \frac{1.0986}{13738.5} = 7.996 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_2} = 0.0009091 + 0.00007996 = 0.0009891$$

$$\boxed{\;T_2 = 1011 \text{ K}\;}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**: A common mistake is to **swap $T_1$ and $T_2$** in the integrated Van't Hoff equation, which flips the sign of $\Delta H^\circ$ and gives a wrong temperature. Always double-check that you are substituting $(T_1, K_1)$ and $(T_2, K_2)$ consistently. Also, do **not forget to convert temperature to Kelvin** if given in Celsius — this is the single most common KTU board-exam error. Finally, remember that $\Delta H^\circ$ must be in **J/mol (not kJ/mol)** when $R$ is used in its SI form of 8.314 J/(mol·K).

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Effect of Temperature on Equilibrium Constant**

- ⭐ **Van't Hoff (Differential)**: $\dfrac{d(\ln K)}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2}$ — *memorize verbatim*
- ⭐ **Van't Hoff (Integrated, constant $\Delta H^\circ$)**: $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$
- ⭐ **Exothermic reaction** ($\Delta H^\circ < 0$): $K$ **decreases** as $T$ **increases**
- ⭐ **Endothermic reaction** ($\Delta H^\circ > 0$): $K$ **increases** as $T$ **increases**
- ⭐ **Van't Hoff Plot**: $\ln K$ vs $1/T$ is a straight line with **slope $= -\Delta H^\circ / R$**
- ⭐ **Gas Constant Values**:
  - $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) when $\Delta H^\circ$ in J/mol
  - $R = 1.987$ cal/(mol·K) when $\Delta H^\circ$ in cal/mol
  - $R = 0.008314$ kJ/(mol·K) when $\Delta H^\circ$ in kJ/mol
- ⭐ **Temperature must be in Kelvin (K)** — convert from °C by adding **273.15**
- ⭐ **Le Chatelier's Principle** is the qualitative underpinning; Van't Hoff equation is the quantitative form
- ⭐ **Variable $\Delta H^\circ$ case** requires integration using **Kirchhoff's Law** with heat capacity data
- ⭐ **Linearization trick**: $\ln K = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT} + \dfrac{\Delta S^\circ}{R}$ — useful for graphical determination of thermodynamic parameters
- ⭐ For $\Delta G^\circ = 0$ (i.e., $K = 1$): the temperature at this point is $T^* = \Delta H^\circ / \Delta S^\circ$
- ⭐ **Industrial Significance**: Optimum reactor temperatures in exothermic equilibrium-limited reactions are chosen by balancing **high $K$ (low $T$)** against **fast kinetics (high $T$)** — solved using Van't Hoff analysis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties of Solids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Properties of Solids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Solids

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solid**: A state of matter in which the constituent atoms, molecules, or ions are held together by strong intermolecular forces in a **fixed, closely packed arrangement**. Solids possess a definite **shape**, **volume**, and **high density** relative to liquids and gases.

In Chemical Process Principles, solids are classified based on the **internal order of their particles** and the **nature of bonding** that holds them together. The study of solids is critical for chemical engineers because virtually every unit operation — from **crystallization**, **filtration**, **drying**, **size reduction**, to **reactor design with solid catalysts** — depends on understanding solid properties.

---

## 1.2 Classification of Solids

### A. Based on Internal Arrangement

| Type | Definition | Example |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Crystalline Solids** | Long-range, orderly, periodic arrangement of particles in a 3D lattice | NaCl, Quartz, Diamond |
| **Amorphous Solids** | Short-range order only; particles arranged irregularly | Glass, Rubber, Plastics |
| **Polycrystalline Solids** | Aggregate of many small crystals (grains) | Metals, Ceramics |

### B. Based on Type of Bonding

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically tests the four fundamental types of crystalline solids:

1. **Ionic Solids** — Held by electrostatic forces between oppositely charged ions. Example: NaCl, KCl.
2. **Covalent Solids** — Atoms share electrons in a continuous network. Example: Diamond, Silicon Carbide (SiC).
3. **Molecular Solids** — Molecules held by weak van der Waals forces or H-bonds. Example: Ice, I₂, Naphthalene.
4. **Metallic Solids** — Metal cations in a "sea of delocalized electrons". Example: Cu, Fe, Al.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **The "Parking Lot" Analogy for Solids**:
> - **Crystalline Solid** = Cars parked in a perfectly aligned grid with painted lines. Every car has a fixed spot (long-range order).
> - **Amorphous Solid** = Cars parked haphazardly in a small lot. Locally, two cars are side-by-side (short-range order), but globally, the pattern is random.
> - **Polycrystalline Solid** = A large parking complex made of many small, perfectly aligned lots joined together.

This analogy helps visualize why crystalline solids have **sharp melting points** while amorphous solids **soften gradually** over a temperature range.

---

## 1.4 The Seven Major Properties of Solids (KTU Focus)

The syllabus emphasizes seven categories of properties:

1. **Mechanical** — hardness, tensile strength, elasticity, ductility, malleability
2. **Thermal** — conductivity, expansion, specific heat
3. **Electrical** — conductivity, resistivity, dielectric strength
4. **Magnetic** — permeability, susceptibility, hysteresis
5. **Optical** — refractive index, reflectance, transmittance
6. **Chemical** — corrosion resistance, reactivity
7. **Physical** — density, porosity, color

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These properties are **structure-sensitive** (depend on crystal defects, grain boundaries) and **structure-insensitive** (depend only on composition). KTU questions frequently test this distinction.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanical Properties of Solids

### 2.1.1 Stress and Strain

When a force is applied to a solid, it deforms. The two fundamental quantities are:

**Stress (σ):** Force per unit area.

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A}$$

**Strain (ε):** Ratio of change in dimension to original dimension. Three types:

**Tensile / Longitudinal Strain:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$

**Volumetric Strain:**

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\Delta V}{V_0}$$

**Shear Strain (γ):**

$$\gamma = \frac{\Delta x}{h} = \tan\theta$$

### 2.1.2 Hooke's Law (Elastic Behavior)

Within the **elastic limit**, stress is proportional to strain:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

where **E** is the **Young's Modulus** (modulus of elasticity), measured in **Pa (N/m²)** or **GPa**.

For shear deformation:

$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma$$

where **G** is the **Shear Modulus (Modulus of Rigidity)**.

For volumetric (hydrostatic) deformation:

$$P = K \cdot \varepsilon_v$$

where **K** is the **Bulk Modulus**.

### 2.1.3 Relationship Between Elastic Constants

For isotropic homogeneous solids:

$$E = 2G(1 + \nu) = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$$

where **ν (nu)** is **Poisson's Ratio**, defined as:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{longitudinal}}$$

### 2.1.4 Plastic Deformation and Yield

Once stress exceeds the **elastic limit**, the solid undergoes **plastic (permanent) deformation**. The stress at which this begins is the **Yield Strength (σ_y)**. The maximum stress a material can withstand is the **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)**.

### 2.1.5 Hardness

**Hardness** = resistance to localized plastic deformation (scratching, indentation).

Common scales:
- **Mohs Scale** (1–10): Talc = 1, Diamond = 10
- **Brinell Hardness Number (BHN):** Indent with hardened steel ball under load.
- **Vickers Hardness (HV):** Indent with diamond pyramid.
- **Rockwell Hardness (HR):** Depth of indentation measured.

$$\text{BHN} = \frac{2P}{\pi D \left(D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}\right)}$$

where P = load, D = indenter diameter, d = indentation diameter.

### 2.1.6 Toughness vs. Brittleness

- **Toughness**: Energy absorbed per unit volume before fracture. Area under the stress-strain curve.
- **Brittleness**: Low plastic deformation before fracture (e.g., glass, ceramics).
- **Ductility**: Ability to be drawn into wires (high % elongation).
- **Malleability**: Ability to be hammered into sheets.

---

## 2.2 Thermal Properties

### 2.2.1 Thermal Expansion

Change in dimension per unit original dimension per degree temperature change.

**Linear Expansion:**

$$\Delta L = \alpha L_0 \Delta T$$

**Volumetric Expansion:**

$$\Delta V = \beta V_0 \Delta T \quad \text{where} \quad \beta \approx 3\alpha$$

**α** = coefficient of linear thermal expansion (per °C or per K).

### 2.2.2 Thermal Conductivity (Fourier's Law)

$$\frac{dQ}{dt} = -kA\frac{dT}{dx}$$

**k** = thermal conductivity (W/m·K). For metals, k is high (Cu ≈ 400 W/m·K). For insulators, k is low (asbestos ≈ 0.08 W/m·K).

### 2.2.3 Specific Heat Capacity

$$Q = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$$

For solids, the **Dulong-Petit Law** gives the molar specific heat at room temperature:

$$C \approx 3R \approx 24.94 \text{ J/mol·K}$$

where **R** = 8.314 J/mol·K (universal gas constant).

---

## 2.3 Electrical Properties

### 2.3.1 Electrical Conductivity and Resistivity

**Ohm's Law:**

$$V = IR$$

**Resistivity (ρ):**

$$\rho = R \cdot \frac{A}{L} \quad (\Omega \cdot m)$$

**Conductivity (σ):**

$$\sigma = \frac{1}{\rho} \quad \text{(S/m or } \Omega^{-1} \text{m}^{-1)}$$

### 2.3.2 Classification by Conductivity

| Class | Conductivity (S/m) | Examples |
|-------|-------------------|----------|
| **Conductors** | 10⁴ – 10⁷ | Cu, Ag, Al |
| **Semiconductors** | 10⁻⁶ – 10⁴ | Si, Ge |
| **Insulators** | < 10⁻¹⁰ | Glass, Rubber, Wood |

### 2.3.3 Band Theory (Conceptual)

- **Valence Band (VB)**: Highest band filled with electrons.
- **Conduction Band (CB)**: Lowest empty band where electrons move freely.
- **Band Gap (E_g)**: Energy difference between VB and CB.
  - **Conductors**: Bands overlap.
  - **Semiconductors**: E_g ≈ 0.5–3 eV.
  - **Insulators**: E_g > 5 eV.

Intrinsic semiconductor conductivity:

$$\sigma = n_e e \mu_e + n_h e \mu_h$$

where n = carrier density, e = charge, μ = mobility.

---

## 2.4 Magnetic Properties

**Magnetic Susceptibility (χ_m):**

$$\chi_m = \frac{M}{H}$$

where M = magnetization, H = magnetic field intensity.

**Magnetic Permeability (μ):**

$$\mu = \mu_0 (1 + \chi_m)$$

where **μ₀** = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m (permeability of free space).

| Type | χ_m Value | Behavior | Examples |
|------|-----------|----------|----------|
| **Diamagnetic** | Small negative (-10⁻⁶) | Weakly repelled by magnetic field | Bi, Cu, Au, Water |
| **Paramagnetic** | Small positive (+10⁻⁵) | Weakly attracted | Al, Pt, O₂ |
| **Ferromagnetic** | Large positive (10³–10⁵) | Strongly attracted, retain magnetization | Fe, Co, Ni |
| **Ferrimagnetic** | Positive but less than ferro | Net magnetic moment from antiparallel alignment | Ferrites (Fe₃O₄) |
| **Antiferromagnetic** | Small positive | Equal opposing moments cancel | MnO, NiO |

**Curie's Law (Paramagnets):**

$$\chi_m = \frac{C}{T}$$

**Curie-Weiss Law (Ferromagnets above T_C):**

$$\chi_m = \frac{C}{T - T_C}$$

where T_C = Curie temperature.

---

## 2.5 Optical Properties

**Refractive Index (n):**

$$n = \frac{c}{v} = \frac{\sin \theta_i}{\sin \theta_r}$$

**Beer-Lambert Law (Absorption):**

$$I = I_0 e^{-\alpha x}$$

where α = absorption coefficient.

**Reflectance (Fresnel Equation for normal incidence):**

$$R = \left(\frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2}\right)^2$$

**Transmittance:** T = 1 - R - A (where A = absorptance).

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Reference Table for KTU Board Exams**

| Property | Symbol | Formula | Units | Notes |
|----------|--------|---------|-------|-------|
| Stress | σ | F/A | Pa (N/m²) | — |
| Strain | ε | ΔL/L₀ | dimensionless | — |
| Young's Modulus | E | σ/ε (in elastic limit) | Pa or GPa | Hooke's Law |
| Shear Modulus | G | τ/γ | Pa | — |
| Bulk Modulus | K | P/(ΔV/V) | Pa | — |
| Poisson's Ratio | ν | -ε_lat/ε_long | dimensionless | 0.2 < ν < 0.5 |
| Brinell Hardness | BHN | 2P/[πD(D-√(D²-d²))] | kgf/mm² | Indentation test |
| Linear Expansion | α | ΔL/(L₀ΔT) | K⁻¹ or °C⁻¹ | — |
| Thermal Conductivity | k | kAΔT/x | W/m·K | Fourier's Law |
| Dulong-Petit | C | 3R | J/mol·K | Molar heat capacity |
| Electrical Resistivity | ρ | RA/L | Ω·m | — |
| Conductivity | σ | 1/ρ | S/m | — |
| Magnetic Susceptibility | χ_m | M/H | dimensionless | — |
| Refractive Index | n | c/v or sinθᵢ/sinθᵣ | dimensionless | Snell's Law |
| Reflectance | R | [(n₁-n₂)/(n₁+n₂)]² | dimensionless | Normal incidence |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Application | Solid Property Used |
|----------------------|---------------------|
| Reactor vessel design (high-pressure) | Tensile strength, Yield strength |
| Heat exchangers | Thermal conductivity, Expansion |
| Electrical wiring | Electrical conductivity |
| Transformer cores | Ferromagnetism, Hysteresis loss |
| Optical fibers | Refractive index, Total internal reflection |
| Catalytic converters | Surface area, Porosity |
| Cutting tools, Abrasives | Hardness (Mohs) |
| Polymer processing | Viscoelasticity, Glass transition |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Elastic Constants (E, G, K, ν)

We aim to derive the universal relationship for isotropic homogeneous solids:

$$E = 2G(1 + \nu) = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$$

### Step 1: Consider a Solid Under Hydrostatic Pressure

Imagine a cube of side **L** under uniform pressure **P** on all six faces. The body experiences a volumetric compression ΔV.

**Volumetric strain:**

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\Delta V}{V} = \frac{-3 \Delta L}{L}$$

(Negative sign because volume decreases.)

**Bulk Modulus definition:**

$$K = \frac{P}{-\varepsilon_v} = \frac{P \cdot V}{|\Delta V|}$$

### Step 2: Resolve Hydrostatic Stress into Uniaxial Components

A hydrostatic pressure can be decomposed into three orthogonal tensile stresses, each of magnitude **P**, plus three equal compressive stresses. The net effect on any axis is the **linear compression** ΔL/L.

From Young's Modulus definition in one direction:

$$\sigma_x = E \cdot \frac{\Delta L_x}{L} \quad \text{(along x-axis only)}$$

But with three perpendicular stresses, the strain in the x-direction is the sum of contributions from all three stresses (superposition):

$$\frac{\Delta L_x}{L} = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$

### Step 3: Apply Hydrostatic Symmetry

For hydrostatic pressure: σ_x = σ_y = σ_z = P. So:

$$\frac{\Delta L}{L} = \frac{1}{E}[P - \nu(P + P)] = \frac{P}{E}(1 - 2\nu)$$

### Step 4: Volumetric Strain

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\Delta V}{V} = 3 \cdot \frac{\Delta L}{L} = \frac{3P}{E}(1 - 2\nu)$$

### Step 5: Bulk Modulus in Terms of E and ν

By definition:

$$K = \frac{P}{\varepsilon_v} = \frac{P \cdot E}{3P(1 - 2\nu)} = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$$

Solving for E:

$$\boxed{E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)} \quad \text{... (Eq. A)}$$

### Step 6: Derive E in Terms of G and ν

Consider a shear stress τ applied to a cube. The shear strain γ is related to tensile strains at 45° to the shear plane (resolved into principal stresses of ±τ).

**Tensile stress along diagonal:** σ₁ = +τ
**Compressive stress perpendicular:** σ₂ = -τ

**Tensile strain along σ₁ direction:**

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}[\tau - \nu(-\tau)] = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Shear strain** is twice this:

$$\gamma = 2\varepsilon_1 = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Shear modulus:**

$$G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma} = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$

Solving for E:

$$\boxed{E = 2G(1 + \nu)} \quad \text{... (Eq. B)}$$

### Step 7: Combine Eq. A and Eq. B

$$3K(1 - 2\nu) = 2G(1 + \nu)$$

$$\boxed{\frac{E}{2G} + \frac{E}{3K} = 1 \cdot \frac{(1+\nu) + (1-2\nu)}{\text{cross-check}}}$$

**Final consolidated identity:**

$$E = 2G(1 + \nu) = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$$

**Verification with typical metal (ν ≈ 0.3, E = 200 GPa):**
- G = E/[2(1.3)] = 200/2.6 ≈ 76.9 GPa ✓
- K = E/[3(0.4)] = 200/1.2 ≈ 166.7 GPa ✓

These match experimental values for steel.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Brinell Hardness Number (BHN)

**Setup:** A hardened steel ball of diameter **D** is pressed into the material under load **P**. The indentation has diameter **d** and depth **h**.

### Step 1: Geometry of the Indentation

The depth of indentation **h** is related to **D** and **d** by the spherical cap geometry:

$$h = \frac{D}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2}$$

$$h = \frac{D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}}{2}$$

### Step 2: Surface Area of the Indentation (Curved Cap)

The curved surface area of the spherical indentation is:

$$A = \pi D h$$

Substituting h:

$$A = \pi D \cdot \frac{D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}}{2} = \frac{\pi D}{2}\left(D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}\right)$$

### Step 3: Define BHN as Load per Unit Indentation Area

$$\text{BHN} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{2P}{\pi D \left(D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}\right)}$$

This is the standard engineering expression used in materials testing.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Property-Structure Correlations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table maps each solid type to its expected properties — a **high-yield KTU question type**.

| Solid Type | Bonding | Melting Point | Hardness | Conductivity (Elec) | Solubility | Example |
|------------|---------|---------------|----------|---------------------|------------|---------|
| **Ionic** | Electrostatic | High | Hard but Brittle | Insulator (solid), Conductor (melt/aq) | Polar solvents | NaCl |
| **Covalent (Network)** | Covalent | Very High | Very Hard | Usually Insulator | Insoluble | Diamond |
| **Molecular** | van der Waals / H-bond | Low | Soft | Insulator | Varies | Ice, I₂ |
| **Metallic** | Metallic (electron sea) | Variable | Variable (ductile) | Conductor | Insoluble | Cu, Fe |
| **Amorphous** | Mixed (no order) | No sharp M.P. | Variable | Variable | Variable | Glass |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A steel wire of length **2.5 m** and cross-sectional area **4 × 10⁻⁶ m²** is stretched by a force of **800 N**. Young's Modulus of steel is **2 × 10¹¹ N/m²**. Find:
(a) Stress
(b) Strain
(c) Elongation
(d) The lateral strain if Poisson's ratio ν = 0.29.

### Solution:

**Given:**
- L₀ = 2.5 m
- A = 4 × 10⁻⁶ m²
- F = 800 N
- E = 2 × 10¹¹ N/m²
- ν = 0.29

**(a) Stress:**

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{800}{4 \times 10^{-6}} = 2 \times 10^{8} \text{ N/m}^2 \text{ (Pa)}$$

**(b) Strain (using Hooke's Law):**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{2 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 10^{11}} = 1 \times 10^{-3}$$

**(c) Elongation:**

$$\Delta L = \varepsilon \cdot L_0 = (1 \times 10^{-3})(2.5) = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 2.5 \text{ mm}$$

**(d) Lateral Strain:**

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{longitudinal}}$$

$$\varepsilon_{lateral} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon = -(0.29)(1 \times 10^{-3}) = -2.9 \times 10^{-4}$$

The wire **contracts laterally** (negative sign) by 2.9 × 10⁻⁴ per unit length.
