# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define Young's Modulus, Bulk Modulus, and Shear Modulus. State the relationship between them for an isotropic solid.**

**Model Answer:**

**Young's Modulus (E):** The ratio of longitudinal (tensile/compressive) stress to longitudinal strain within the elastic limit.

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{F/A}{\Delta L/L_0}$$

**Bulk Modulus (K):** The ratio of volumetric stress (pressure) to volumetric strain.

$$K = \frac{P}{\Delta V/V_0}$$

**Shear Modulus (G):** The ratio of shear stress to shear strain.

$$G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma}$$

**Relationship (for isotropic solids):**

$$\boxed{E = 2G(1 + \nu) = 3K(1 - 2\nu)}$$

where ν is Poisson's ratio.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between crystalline and amorphous solids. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Crystalline Solids | Amorphous Solids |
|----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Order** | Long-range periodic order | Only short-range order |
| **Geometry** | Definite geometric shape | Irregular shape |
| **Melting** | Sharp, fixed melting point | Soften gradually over a range |
| **Anisotropy** | Anisotropic (properties vary with direction) | Isotropic (properties same in all directions) |
| **Cleavage** | Clean cleavage along planes | Irregular fracture |
| **Examples** | NaCl, Diamond, Quartz | Glass, Rubber, Plastics |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`

**Q: (a)** With the help of a stress-strain diagram for a ductile metal, explain the following terms: **Elastic limit, Yield point, Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS), and Fracture point**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A copper wire of length **3 m** and cross-sectional area **2 × 10⁻⁶ m²** is subjected to a tensile force of **600 N**. If Young's Modulus for copper is **1.2 × 10¹¹ N/m²**, calculate: **(i)** Stress, **(ii)** Strain, **(iii)** Elongation, **(iv)** Bulk modulus if Poisson's ratio is **0.34**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Stress-Strain Diagram Explanation:**

```
Stress (σ)
    |              UTS
    |             /‾‾‾‾\         F (Fracture)
    |            /      \       /
    |       Y  /         \    /
    |        /            \ /
    |       /              X
    |      / Elastic
    |     /  region
    |    /
    |   /
    |  / O
    |/________________________ Strain (ε)
        E  C
    E = Proportional limit
    C = Elastic limit
    Y = Yield point
    UTS = Ultimate tensile strength
    F = Fracture point
```

**Description:**

1. **Proportional Limit (E):** The maximum stress up to which stress is **strictly proportional to strain** (Hooke's law region: σ = Eε). The straight line from O to E.

2. **Elastic Limit (C):** The maximum stress up to which the body **completely regains its original shape and size** upon removal of load. Beyond this, permanent deformation begins.

3. **Yield Point (Y):** The stress at which the material begins to deform **plastically** without any increase in load. For mild steel, there is a distinct upper and lower yield point.

4. **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS):** The **maximum stress** the material can withstand. Beyond UTS, the cross-section begins to **neck** (localized reduction in area).

5. **Fracture Point (F):** The stress at which the material **finally breaks** into two pieces.

**Key Regions:**
- **O–C:** Elastic region (recoverable)
- **C–Y:** Elastic-to-plastic transition
- **Y–UTS:** Plastic region with strain hardening
- **UTS–F:** Necking and fracture

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- L₀ = 3 m
- A = 2 × 10⁻⁶ m²
- F = 600 N
- E = 1.2 × 10¹¹ N/m²
- ν = 0.34

**(i) Stress:**

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{600}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ N/m}^2 \text{ (Pa)}$$

**(ii) Strain:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{1.2 \times 10^{11}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

**(iii) Elongation:**

$$\Delta L = \varepsilon \cdot L_0 = (2.5 \times 10^{-3})(3) = 7.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 7.5 \text{ mm}$$

**(iv) Bulk Modulus:**

Using the relationship:

$$E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$$

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{11}}{3(1 - 2 \times 0.34)} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{11}}{3(0.32)} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{11}}{0.96}$$

$$K = 1.25 \times 10^{11} \text{ N/m}^2 \text{ (Pa)}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the **band theory** of solids. With the help of diagrams, differentiate between conductors, semiconductors, and insulators based on band structure. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The resistivity of a material is **1.7 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m**, and its electron mobility is **4.3 × 10⁻³ m²/V·s**. If the charge of an electron is **1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**, find the **number density of charge carriers** in the material. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Band Theory of Solids:**

In isolated atoms, electrons occupy **discrete energy levels**. When N atoms come together to form a solid, the energy levels split into **N closely spaced levels** forming **energy bands**. The two most important bands are:

1. **Valence Band (VB):** The highest energy band that is **completely filled** with electrons at 0 K. These electrons are bound to atoms.

2. **Conduction Band (CB):** The lowest energy band that is **empty or partially filled** at 0 K. Electrons in this band are free to move and conduct electricity.

3. **Forbidden Energy Gap (E_g):** The energy difference between the top of VB and the bottom of CB. No electron states exist in this region.

**Classification Based on Band Structure:**

**Conductor (Metals):**
- VB and CB **overlap** OR CB is partially filled.
- E_g ≈ 0 eV.
- Plenty of free electrons → high conductivity.
- Example: Cu, Ag, Al.

**Semiconductor:**
- Small band gap: **0.5 eV < E_g < 3 eV**.
- At 0 K: insulator-like; at room T: some electrons jump to CB.
- Conductivity **increases with temperature**.
- Example: Si (E_g = 1.1 eV), Ge (E_g = 0.67 eV).

**Insulator:**
- Large band gap: **E_g > 5 eV**.
- Electrons cannot jump from VB to CB at ordinary temperatures.
- Very low conductivity.
- Example: Diamond (E_g = 5.5 eV), Glass.

**Band Diagrams:**

```
CONDUCTOR:               SEMICONDUCTOR:           INSULATOR:
                         
___                      ___                     ___
   CB (partially filled)  CB (empty)               CB (empty)
___                      
   VB (overlaps CB)      E_g ≈ 1 eV              E_g > 5 eV
___                      
                         ___                     ___
                           VB (filled)             VB (filled)
```

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Resistivity: ρ = 1.7 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m
- Electron mobility: μ = 4.3 × 10⁻³ m²/V·s
- Charge: e = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C

**Formula:**

$$\sigma = n \cdot e \cdot \mu$$

where n = number density of charge carriers (electrons per m³).

**Step 1: Calculate Conductivity**

$$\sigma = \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{1}{1.7 \times 10^{-8}} = 5.882 \times 10^{7} \text{ S/m}$$

**Step 2: Rearrange for n**

$$n = \frac{\sigma}{e \cdot \mu} = \frac{5.882 \times 10^{7}}{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(4.3 \times 10^{-3})}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Denominator**

$$e \cdot \mu = (1.6 \times 10^{-19})(4.3 \times 10^{-3}) = 6.88 \times 10^{-22}$$

**Step 4: Final Division**

$$n = \frac{5.882 \times 10^{7}}{6.88 \times 10^{-22}} = 8.55 \times 10^{28} \text{ electrons/m}^3$$

This value (~10²⁸ m⁻³) is typical for **copper**, confirming the material.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Units Confusion (–2 marks typical):** Forgetting to convert cm² to m², or mm² to m² in stress calculations. **Always work in SI units (Pa, m², N).**
> 2. **Poisson's Ratio Sign Error (–1 mark):** Lateral strain is **negative** when longitudinal strain is positive. The minus sign in ν = -ε_lateral/ε_longitudinal matters!
> 3. **Bulk Modulus Formula (–2 marks):** Many students write E = 3K(1 + 2ν) instead of E = 3K(1 − 2ν). The **minus** sign is critical.
> 4. **Band Theory Diagrams (–2 marks):** Examiner expects distinct **labeled** band structures (VB, CB, E_g) with the **relative magnitudes** of gaps correctly indicated. Avoid drawing all three with the same gap.
> 5. **Dulong-Petit Units (–1 mark):** C_molar = 3R has units of **J/mol·K**, NOT J/kg·K. Don't confuse molar and specific heat.
> 6. **Hardness Formula (–2 marks):** In BHN, do NOT use πd²/4 (the area of the circular indentation face). Use the **curved spherical cap area** = πDh.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Must-Know Summary Points for Last-Minute Revision:**

### A. Mechanical Properties
- ☐ **Stress** = F/A; **Strain** = ΔL/L; both are tensor quantities in 3D
- ☐ **Hooke's Law** σ = Eε valid **only in elastic region**
- ☐ **E = 2G(1+ν) = 3K(1−2ν)** — master this identity
- ☐ **Poisson's ratio** of most metals ≈ 0.3; rubber ≈ 0.5; cork ≈ 0
- ☐ **BHN** uses curved surface area, NOT projected area
- ☐ **Stress-strain curve** has 5 key points: Proportional limit, Elastic limit, Yield, UTS, Fracture
- ☐ **Toughness** = area under stress-strain curve; **Brittleness** = little plastic deformation

### B. Thermal Properties
- ☐ **α** (linear) ≈ **β/3** (volumetric) for isotropic solids
- ☐ **Fourier's Law** for heat conduction: q = -k(dT/dx)
- ☐ **Dulong-Petit Law**: C_molar ≈ 3R = 24.94 J/mol·K (fails at low T for light elements)

### C. Electrical Properties
- ☐ **σ = 1/ρ = neμ** (for metals with single type of carrier)
- ☐ **Conductors** have E_g ≈ 0; **Semiconductors** E_g = 0.5–3 eV; **Insulators** E_g > 5 eV
- ☐ **Semiconductor conductivity** ↑ with **temperature** (opposite to metals)
- ☐ **Doping** creates n-type (donors) and p-type (acceptors) semiconductors

### D. Magnetic Properties
- ☐ **Diamagnetic**: χ_m < 0, **no unpaired electrons** (Bi, Cu, Au)
- ☐ **Paramagnetic**: χ_m > 0 small, **unpaired electrons** (Al, O₂)
- ☐ **Ferromagnetic**: χ_m ≫ 0, exhibits **hysteresis**, T_C (Fe, Co, Ni)
- ☐ **Curie's Law** for paramagnets: χ_m = C/T
- ☐ **Curie-Weiss Law** for ferromagnets above T_C: χ_m = C/(T − T_C)

### E. Optical Properties
- ☐ **n = c/v** (in medium) — high n means slow light
- ☐ **Snell's Law**: n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂
- ☐ **Total internal reflection** when θ₁ > critical angle: sin θ_c = n₂/n₁
- ☐ **Beer-Lambert Law** for absorption: I = I₀ e^(−αx)

### F. Solid Classification
- ☐ **Ionic**: high MP, hard, brittle, insulators (solid) — **NaCl**
- ☐ **Covalent network**: very high MP, very hard — **Diamond**
- ☐ **Molecular**: low MP, soft — **I₂, Ice**
- ☐ **Metallic**: variable MP, ductile, conductors — **Cu, Fe**
- ☐ **Amorphous**: no sharp MP, isotropic — **Glass**

### G. Constants to Remember
- ☐ R = 8.314 J/mol·K
- ☐ μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m
- ☐ e = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C
- ☐ E_g of Si = 1.1 eV; Ge = 0.67 eV
- ☐ C_molar (Dulong-Petit) ≈ 25 J/mol·K
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Screening
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Screening ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition **Screening** is a unit operation in chemical engineering that involves the **mechanical separation of a mixture of solid particles of varying sizes** into two or more fractions by means of a **perforated surface (screen/sieve)** through which the material flows. The separation is strictly based on the **geometric size of the particles** relative to the **aperture (opening size)** of the screen. > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology (KTU Standard):** > - **Aperture ($d_a$):** The clear opening dimension in the screen cloth. > - **Mesh Number ($M$):** Number of openings per linear inch. > - **Feed ($F$):** The heterogeneous mixture entering the screen. > - **Oversize / Coarse ($O$):** Particles **retained** on the screen (larger than the aperture). > - **Undersize / Fines ($U$):** Particles **passing through** the screen (smaller than the aperture). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **kitchen colander draining pasta from water**. The water (small molecules) slips through the tiny holes, while the pasta (large particles) stays behind. Screening works on the **exact same principle**, but at an industrial scale and with much more precise size cut-offs. Another everyday analogy is a **coin sorter in a bank** — coins of different diameters are sorted because they cannot pass through holes designed for smaller denominations. In chemical engineering, screens do the same thing for powders, granules, and crushed ores. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 scheme, Module 4 focuses on **Mechanical Separation Techniques**. Screening is the **simplest and most economical** size-separation method and is almost always attempted before more energy-intensive processes like filtration or centrifugation. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Standard Aperture Series:** ISO 565 / Tyler Series (e.g., 75 µm, 150 µm, 425 µm, 1 mm, 4.75 mm). - **Standard Screen Motion:** 5 Hz to 20 Hz (vibrational frequency in industrial vibratory screens). - **Typical Capacity Range:** 1 to 100 tonnes/(m²·h) depending on particle size and moisture content. - **Standard Efficiency Benchmark:** A "good" industrial screen operates at **85%–95%** overall efficiency. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Feed Presentation:** The feed mixture ($F$) is delivered uniformly across the screen surface (e.g., by a vibrating feeder) to prevent localized overloading. 2. **Stratification Mechanism:** Vibration or agitation causes fine particles to migrate **downward** through the bulk of the bed, while coarse particles migrate **upward** (a phenomenon known as **B stratification**). 3. **Passage Criterion:** A particle will pass through the screen if and only if its **smallest cross-sectional dimension** is less than the aperture $d_a$. Note: a long, thin needle-like particle may pass through a hole smaller than its length, if oriented diagonally. 4. **Blinding (Pinning):** If a particle is **only slightly smaller** than the aperture, it can wedge in the opening and block it. This phenomenon is called **blinding**, and it reduces screen efficiency drastically. 5. **Discharge:** Coarse particles travel along the inclined/vibrating screen and discharge at the end. Fines pass through to the collection pan below. ### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step - **Why stratify?** Without stratification, fines would remain trapped beneath coarse particles, and the screen would either need excessive area or would never achieve a clean cut. - **Why vibrate?** Vibration provides the kinetic energy required to overcome inter-particle friction, fluid drag, and surface adhesion (especially in fine, moist, or sticky powders). - **Why uniform feed?** A localized pile concentrates mass flux, which leads to bed-depth blinding. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Description & Units | |---|---|---| | 1 | $F = O + U$ | **Overall material balance.** Mass flow of feed equals sum of oversize and undersize. Units: kg/s or kg/h. | | 2 | $F \cdot x_{F,A} = O \cdot x_{O,A} + U \cdot x_{U,A}$ | **Component balance** for size fraction $A$ (e.g., the coarse fraction). | | 3 | $D_p = \dfrac{1}{M}$ | **Aperture–mesh relationship.** $D_p$ is aperture size (inches); $M$ is mesh number (openings per inch). | | 4 | $R_o = \dfrac{O \cdot (1 - x_{U,o})}{F \cdot x_{F,o}}$ | **Recovery of Oversize.** Mass of coarse actually recovered ÷ mass of coarse in feed. | | 5 | $R_u = \dfrac{U \cdot (1 - x_{O,u})}{F \cdot (1 - x_{F,o})}$ | **Recovery of Undersize.** Mass of fines actually recovered ÷ mass of fines in feed. | | 6 | $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{R_o + R_u}{2}$ | **Overall Screen Efficiency** (arithmetic mean of recoveries). | | 7 | $C = K \cdot \rho_b \cdot A_s \cdot d_a$ | **Capacity equation** (empirical). $C$ = capacity (t/h), $K$ = empirical constant, $\rho_b$ = bulk density (t/m³), $A_s$ = screen area (m²), $d_a$ = aperture size (mm). | | 8 | $\eta_{L} = 1 - e^{-k L}$ | **Probabilistic (Lenthwaite) efficiency model.** $L$ = screen length; $k$ = constant depending on aperture, particle size, and vibration. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Units Discipline:** In all KTU calculations, **always** convert everything to consistent units (e.g., kg for mass, s for time) before plugging into the formula. Examiners will deduct marks for unit inconsistency. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Mining Industry:** Pre-concentration of iron ore, coal, and aggregates before crushing — the "scalping" screen removes fines before they overload the crusher. - **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Particle size control of granules and excipients to ensure uniform tablet dissolution. - **Cement Industry:** Separation of raw meal and clinker into size fractions for kilns and mills. - **Food Industry:** Flour sifting, sugar refining, salt classification. - **Wastewater Treatment:** Removal of coarse debris in **bar screens** at the headworks of treatment plants. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Screen Efficiency Formula We begin with a **two-product screen** (one feed splits into oversize $O$ and undersize $U$). Let the mass fraction of the **coarse component ($A$)** in each stream be denoted as $x_{F,A}$, $x_{O,A}$, and $x_{U,A}$. **Step 1: Overall Mass Balance** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total mass in} &= \text{Total mass out} \\ F &= O + U \end{aligned} $$ This is the **first principle** (conservation of mass). **Step 2: Component Mass Balance for Fraction $A$ (the coarse fraction)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Mass of A in} &= \text{Mass of A out} \\ F \cdot x_{F,A} &= O \cdot x_{O,A} + U \cdot x_{U,A} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Express Recovery of Oversize ($R_o$)** The *ideal* screen would put **all** of fraction $A$ into the oversize stream, and **none** into the undersize. The actual recovery is: $$ \begin{aligned} R_o &= \dfrac{\text{Mass of } A \text{ in oversize stream}}{\text{Mass of } A \text{ in feed}} \\ R_o &= \dfrac{O \cdot x_{O,A}}{F \cdot x_{F,A}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Express Recovery of Undersize ($R_u$)** The fraction $B$ (the fines) is what we want in the undersize. Its mass fraction is $(1 - x_{F,A})$ in the feed, $(1 - x_{O,A})$ in the oversize, and $(1 - x_{U,A})$ in the undersize. $$ \begin{aligned} R_u &= \dfrac{\text{Mass of fines in undersize stream}}{\text{Mass of fines in feed}} \\ R_u &= \dfrac{U \cdot (1 - x_{U,A})}{F \cdot (1 - x_{F,A})} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Combine Into Overall Efficiency** By convention, the KTU board examiner accepts the **arithmetic mean** of the two recoveries: $$ \eta_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{R_o + R_u}{2} $$ Substituting the expressions from Steps 3 and 4: $$ \eta_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \left[ \dfrac{O \cdot x_{O,A}}{F \cdot x_{F,A}} + \dfrac{U \cdot (1 - x_{U,A})}{F \cdot (1 - x_{F,A})} \right] $$ ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Exam-Style) > **Problem (KTU University Exam - May 2024 pattern):** > A screen receives a feed of **600 kg/h** containing **45% coarse particles** (size > aperture). The screen produces **270 kg/h of oversize** containing **92% coarse material**, and the rest is the undersize. Calculate: > (i) The mass of undersize produced per hour. > (ii) The composition of the undersize. > (iii) The recovery of oversize and undersize. > (iv) The overall screen efficiency. **Given Data:** - $F = 600 \text{ kg/h}$, $x_{F,A} = 0.45$ - $O = 270 \text{ kg/h}$, $x_{O,A} = 0.92$ **Solution:** **Part (i): Mass of undersize** $$ \begin{aligned} F &= O + U \\ U &= F - O = 600 - 270 = 330 \text{ kg/h} \end{aligned} $$ **Part (ii): Composition of undersize (using component balance for coarse fraction $A$)** $$ \begin{aligned} F \cdot x_{F,A} &= O \cdot x_{O,A} + U \cdot x_{U,A} \\ (600)(0.45) &= (270)(0.92) + (330) \cdot x_{U,A} \\ 270 &= 248.4 + 330 \cdot x_{U,A} \\ 330 \cdot x_{U,A} &= 270 - 248.4 = 21.6 \\ x_{U,A} &= \dfrac{21.6}{330} = 0.0654 \;\; (\text{or } 6.54\% \text{ coarse}) \end{aligned} $$ So the undersize contains **6.54% coarse** and **93.46% fines**. **Part (iii): Recovery Calculations** Recovery of oversize: $$ \begin{aligned} R_o &= \dfrac{O \cdot x_{O,A}}{F \cdot x_{F,A}} = \dfrac{270 \times 0.92}{600 \times 0.45} = \dfrac{248.4}{270} = 0.9200 = 92.00\% \end{aligned} $$ Recovery of undersize (note: fines mass fraction is $1 - x$): $$ \begin{aligned} R_u &= \dfrac{U \cdot (1 - x_{U,A})}{F \cdot (1 - x_{F,A})} = \dfrac{330 \times (1 - 0.0654)}{600 \times (1 - 0.45)} \\ R_u &= \dfrac{330 \times 0.9346}{600 \times 0.55} = \dfrac{308.42}{330} = 0.9349 = 93.49\% \end{aligned} $$ **Part (iv): Overall Screen Efficiency** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_{\text{overall}} &= \dfrac{R_o + R_u}{2} = \dfrac{0.9200 + 0.9349}{2} = \dfrac{1.8549}{2} \\ \eta_{\text{overall}} &= 0.9275 = 92.75\% \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** A 92.75% efficiency is excellent for industrial screening — it means the screen is recovering >92% of *both* the coarse and the fine fractions where they belong. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of an Industrial Screening Operation The diagram below illustrates the full material flow architecture of a typical two-product screening system, including the auxiliary feed-handling and discharge subsystems. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Material Hopper] --> B[Vibratory Feeder] B --> C{Inclined Vibrating Screen} C -- Retained fraction --> D[Oversize Discharge Chute] C -- Passing fraction --> E[Undersize Collection Pan] D --> F[Coarse Product Conveyor] E --> G[Fines Collection Hopper] F --> H[Crusher / Next Stage] G --> I[Storage Silo] J[Drive Motor with Eccentric Weights] -. Vibration energy .-> C K[Dust Extraction Hood] -. Captures airborne fines .-> C ``` ### 4.2 Mass-Balance Block Diagram (Sankey-style Architecture) This is the canonical material-balance schematic that examiners expect on the answer script for any screen-related numerical problem. ```mermaid flowchart LR feed[/"F = 600 kg/h
xF,A = 0.45"/] --> screen{SCREEN} screen -- "O = 270 kg/h" --> oversize[/"Oversize
xO,A = 0.92"/] screen -- "U = 330 kg/h" --> undersize[/"Undersize
xU,A = 0.0654"/] ``` ### 4.3 Classification of Industrial Screens (Tree Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD scr[Screens] --> s1[Grizzlies
Parallel bars, 50-300 mm aperture] scr --> s2[Stationary Screens
Inclined, low capacity] scr --> s3[Vibrating Screens
Most common industrial type] scr --> s4[Rotary Trommels
Cylindrical rotating drum] scr --> s5[Probability Screens
High throughput, low efficiency] s3 --> s3a[Mechanical Vibratory] s3 --> s3b[Electromagnetic Vibratory] s3 --> s3c[Inclined vs Horizontal] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why these diagrams matter:** KTU valuation scripts often deduct marks if the student does not draw a clear **feed/oversize/undersize** flow block diagram before starting material-balance calculations. Always include the schematic! ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Define screening. List any two industrial applications of screening.** **Model Answer:** Screening is a mechanical size-separation technique in which a mixture of solid particles is passed over a perforated surface, and particles smaller than the aperture pass through (undersize) while larger particles are retained (oversize). **Two industrial applications:** 1. **Coal Preparation Plant:** Screening removes fines before washing to reduce water and energy consumption. 2. **Cement Industry:** Raw meal is screened to ensure uniform particle size for the rotary kiln feed. *(Any two valid applications accepted.)* **[3 Marks]** #### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2022] **Distinguish between oversize and undersize with a neat sketch.** **Model Answer:** - **Oversize (Coarse):** The fraction of feed that is **retained on the screen** because its particle size is **larger** than the screen aperture. Symbol: $O$. - **Undersize (Fines):** The fraction of feed that **passes through the screen** because its particle size is **smaller** than the aperture. Symbol: $U$. **Sketch:** A horizontal screen with feed $F$ entering from one side, $O$ discharged from the top of the screen, and $U$ collected in a pan below. **[3 Marks]** --- ### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) #### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2023] A crusher produces **800 kg/h of crushed limestone**, of which **40% passes through a 2 mm screen** (the desired undersize). The crushed product is fed to a vibrating screen. The oversize analysis shows that the oversize contains **95% material greater than 2 mm** and the undersize contains **5% material greater than 2 mm**. **(a)** [7 Marks] Calculate the mass of oversize and undersize, and verify using overall material balance. **(b)** [7 Marks] Calculate the recovery of oversize, recovery of undersize, and the overall screen efficiency. **Given:** - $F = 800$ kg/h - $x_{F,A} = 0.40$ (fraction of coarse in feed) - $x_{O,A} = 0.95$ (fraction of coarse in oversize) - $x_{U,A} = 0.05$ (fraction of coarse in undersize) **Part (a) Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Component balance for the coarse fraction $A$ (>2 mm): $$ \begin{aligned} F \cdot x_{F,A} &= O \cdot x_{O,A} + U \cdot x_{U,A} \\ 800 \times 0.40 &= 0.95\,O + 0.05\,U \\ 320 &= 0.95\,O + 0.05\,U \quad \text{...(i)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Overall mass balance: $$ \begin{aligned} F &= O + U \\ 800 &= O + U \quad \Rightarrow \quad U = 800 - O \quad \text{...(ii)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Substitute (ii) into (i): $$ \begin{aligned} 320 &= 0.95\,O + 0.05\,(800 - O) \\ 320 &= 0.95\,O + 40 - 0.05\,O \\ 320 - 40 &= 0.90\,O \\ 280 &= 0.90\,O \\ O &= \dfrac{280}{0.90} = 311.11 \text{ kg/h} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Find $U$: $$ \begin{aligned} U &= 800 - 311.11 = 488.89 \text{ kg/h} \end{aligned} $$ **Part (a) Final Answer:** $O = 311.11$ kg/h, $U = 488.89$ kg/h. **[7 Marks]** **Part (b) Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Recovery of Oversize: $$ \begin{aligned} R_o &= \dfrac{O \cdot x_{O,A}}{F \cdot x_{F,A}} = \dfrac{311.11 \times 0.95}{800 \times 0.40} = \dfrac{295.56}{320} = 0.9236 = 92.36\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Recovery of Undersize: $$ \begin{aligned} R_u &= \dfrac{U \cdot (1 - x_{U,A})}{F \cdot (1 - x_{F,A})} = \dfrac{488.89 \times (1 - 0.05)}{800 \times (1 - 0.40)} \\ R_u &= \dfrac{488.89 \times 0.95}{800 \times 0.60} = \dfrac{464.45}{480} = 0.9676 = 96.76\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Overall Efficiency: $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_{\text{overall}} &= \dfrac{R_o + R_u}{2} = \dfrac{0.9236 + 0.9676}{2} = \dfrac{1.8912}{2} \\ \eta_{\text{overall}} &= 0.9456 = 94.56\% \end{aligned} $$ **Part (b) Final Answer:** $R_o = 92.36\%$, $R_u = 96.76\%$, $\eta_{\text{overall}} = 94.56\%$. **[7 Marks]** --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] *(Alternative Choice)* A vibrating screen is used to classify **1000 kg/h of crushed ore**. The feed contains **60% particles larger than the screen aperture**. The screen produces an oversize stream of **580 kg/h** containing **98% coarse particles**. **(a)** [7 Marks] Determine the mass flow rate of undersize and its coarse-fraction content. **(b)** [7 Marks] Calculate the overall efficiency of the screen, and comment on whether the screen is operating satisfactorily. **Given:** $F = 1000$ kg/h, $x_{F,A} = 0.60$, $O = 580$ kg/h, $x_{O,A} = 0.98$. **Part (a) Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Overall mass balance: $$ U = F - O = 1000 - 580 = 420 \text{ kg/h} $$ **Step 2:** Component balance: $$ \begin{aligned} 1000 \times 0.60 &= 580 \times 0.98 + 420 \times x_{U,A} \\ 600 &= 568.4 + 420 \cdot x_{U,A} \\ 420 \cdot x_{U,A} &= 31.6 \\ x_{U,A} &= 0.0752 = 7.52\% \end{aligned} $$ **Part (a) Final Answer:** $U = 420$ kg/h, $x_{U,A} = 7.52\%$. **[7 Marks]** **Part (b) Model Solution:** Recovery of Oversize: $$ R_o = \dfrac{580 \times 0.98}{1000 \times 0.60} = \dfrac{568.4}{600} = 0.9473 = 94.73\% $$ Recovery of Undersize: $$ R_u = \dfrac{420 \times (1 - 0.0752)}{1000 \times (1 - 0.60)} = \dfrac{420 \times 0.9248}{1000 \times 0.40} = \dfrac{388.42}{400} = 0.9710 = 97.10\% $$ Overall Efficiency: $$ \eta_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{0.9473 + 0.9710}{2} = \dfrac{1.9183}{2} = 0.9592 = 95.92\% $$ **Comment:** An efficiency of **95.92%** is **highly satisfactory** for an industrial screen, as it lies well within the typical good-performance range of 90%–96%. The screen is operating efficiently. **[7 Marks]** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting to draw the feed–oversize–undersize diagram** before the numerical — the examiner **will deduct 1 mark** even if the rest is correct. > 2. **Mixing up $x_{O,A}$ and $x_{U,A}$:** Students often confuse the *fraction of coarse in the undersize* (a measure of *contamination*) with the *fraction of fines in the undersize*. Always re-read the question: "coarse in undersize" ≠ "fines in undersize". > 3. **Forgetting the factor of $1/2$ in overall efficiency:** This single omission costs a full mark in the final step. > 4. **Unit errors:** If $F$ is given in kg/h, all other flow rates must be in kg/h — never mix kg/h with kg/s unless you explicitly convert. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Screening = mechanical size separation** based on a perforated surface; no chemical or thermal action involved. - The two product streams are **Oversize (coarse, $O$)** and **Undersize (fines, $U$)**. - **Two fundamental balances** must always be written first: (i) overall mass balance $F = O + U$, and (ii) component balance $F \cdot x_{F,A} = O \cdot x_{O,A} + U \cdot x_{U,A}$. - **Recovery of oversize** $R_o$ and **Recovery of undersize** $R_u$ are calculated separately; the **overall efficiency** is the arithmetic mean $\eta = (R_o + R_u)/2$. - **Efficiency benchmarks:** Industrial screens typically operate at **85%–96%** overall efficiency. Below 80% indicates excessive blinding or incorrect vibration parameters. - **Blinding** (particles wedging in apertures) is the single biggest cause of efficiency loss — it is mitigated by proper vibration, anti-blinding balls, or heated decks for moist materials. - **Aperture–mesh relation:** $D_p = 1/M$ (in inches); for mm units, $D_p \approx 25.4/M$ mm. - **Probabilistic model (Lenthwaite):** $\eta_L = 1 - e^{-kL}$ is useful for design — efficiency increases with screen length up to a point of diminishing returns. - Always convert all **mass fractions to decimals** (e.g., 45% → 0.45) before substituting into equations. - Always **label the diagram** with stream names, mass flow rates, and compositions — this is a non-negotiable step in KTU answer scripts. - The most common **industrial screen** is the **vibrating screen** (mechanical or electromagnetic drive), with aperture ranges from 50 µm to 300 mm. - **Screening efficiency is dimensionless** and is always expressed as a percentage in the final answer.

topics.title: Screening Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Screening Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Screening** is a **mechanical size separation** unit operation in which a mixture of solid particles of varying sizes is passed over a **perforated surface (screen)** possessing apertures of definite dimension, in order to divide the feed into two (or more) fractions based on a single boundary size called the **cut size** or **mesh of separation**.

The portion of feed that **passes through** the screen apertures is termed the **undersize** (or **fines/throughs**), while the fraction that is **retained on** the screen surface is called the **oversize** (or **coarse/overs**). The boundary size separating these two fractions is known as the **screen aperture** or **cut diameter** ($d_{50}$).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pouring a mixture of **rice grains and large dal** onto a traditional **chhalni** (Indian kitchen sieve). When you shake it:
- **Small rice grains (undersize)** fall *through* the perforations into the bowl below.
- **Large dal pieces (oversize)** stay *on top* of the sieve and are collected separately.

This is essentially **screening**. In a chemical plant, the same principle is applied — but the sieve is made of **woven wire mesh, punched plates, or parallel bars**, and the shaking is done by **vibratory or gyratory motion** powered by electric motors. Screens are the **workhorses of solids-handling industries** — quarrying, mineral processing, fertilizer manufacture, cement, food processing, and even pharmaceutical granulation rely on them.

## 1.3 Key Nomenclature and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Definitions to Remember:**
>
> - **Mesh Number (M):** Number of openings per linear inch of screen. e.g., **100 mesh** ⇒ 100 openings/inch.
> - **Aperture (a):** Clear opening dimension between adjacent wires/bars, expressed in **mm** or **µm**.
> - **Wire Diameter (d):** Thickness of the wire strands. Aperture + wire = pitch.
> - **Open Area (%) = $\dfrac{a^2}{(a+d)^2} \times 100$** — higher open area = higher capacity.
> - **Cut Size ($d_{50}$):** Particle size at which 50 % reports to oversize and 50 % to undersize.
> - **Capacity (C):** Mass of feed handled per unit time — typically expressed in **tonnes/hr** or **kg/s**.
> - **Standard constant for capacity:** For dry screening, the **bulk density ($\rho_b$)** in **kg/m³** is mandatory in design.

## 1.4 Classification of Screening Equipment

Screens are broadly classified based on the **mechanism of motion** and the **type of screening surface**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Focus:** Out of all categories, the *Vibrating Screen* carries the maximum weightage in numerical problems, while *Grizzlies* and *Trommels* appear in **Part A (2–3 mark)** conceptual questions.

| S.No. | Type | Motion | Typical Use |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Grizzlies** | Stationary / Inclined | Primary scalping of ROM (Run-of-Mine) ore |
| 2 | **Trommels (Revolving Screens)** | Rotational drum | Municipal solid waste, fertilizer |
| 3 | **Vibrating Screens** | Linear / Elliptical vibration | Mineral, coal, aggregates |
| 4 | **Oscillating Screens** | Reciprocating (mechanical) | Chemical powders, food |
| 5 | **Sieves (Test Screens)** | Manual / Shaker | Laboratory size analysis |
| 6 | **Gyratory Screens** | Circular gyration | Fine powders, pharmaceuticals |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of a Screen

A particle fed onto a vibrating screen is subjected to **two simultaneous competing effects**:

1. **Stratification (Segregation):** Smaller particles migrate *downward* through the bed, while larger ones remain on top — driven by vibration acceleration.
2. **Passage Probability:** A particle can pass through the aperture **only if** its *diameter < aperture* AND it is presented to an aperture within the time of contact.

The **efficiency of a screen** is therefore determined by the **probability of presentation + probability of passage**.

## 2.2 Factors Affecting Screen Performance (High-Yield List)

- **Feed Characteristics:** Particle size distribution, shape (round vs. flaky), moisture content, bulk density, **toughness**.
- **Screen Surface:** Open area, aperture shape (square/rectangular/slot), wire/material of construction.
- **Operating Variables:** Vibration amplitude, frequency, angle of inclination, feed rate (tonnes/hr), bed depth, moisture.
- **Maintenance:** Blinding (pegging) — particles lodging in apertures, drastically reducing effective open area.

> [!WARNING]
> **Blinding** is the #1 reason for poor screen efficiency in KTU plant case studies. Always mention it as a deduction point in design problems.

## 2.3 The Mass Balance (Material Balance) Over a Screen

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal law for any screening operation:**
> $$\boxed{F = U + O}$$
> where $F$ = feed mass rate, $U$ = undersize mass rate, $O$ = oversize mass rate.

Similarly, for the **oversize (coarse) component** in the feed:
$$\boxed{F \cdot x_{F} = O \cdot x_{O} + U \cdot x_{U}}$$
where $x_F$, $x_O$, $x_U$ are the **mass fractions of the coarse species** in feed, oversize, and undersize respectively. For an *ideal* screen, $x_O = 1$ and $x_U = 0$.

For the **fines (undersize) component**, by analogy:
$$F(1 - x_F) = O(1 - x_O) + U(1 - x_U)$$

## 2.4 Screen Efficiency — The Two Definitions

There are **two commonly used definitions** in chemical engineering textbooks. KTU examiners *love* switching between them.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition 1 — Mass-Recovery Efficiency (also called "Recovery of fines into undersize"):**
> $$\boxed{\eta_1 = \dfrac{U \, x_{u,\text{fine}}}{F \, x_{F,\text{fine}}} \times 100 \ \%}$$
> — measures how much of the *desired undersize material in the feed* actually reports to the undersize stream.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition 2 — Comparative (or Overall) Efficiency:**
> $$\boxed{\eta_2 = \dfrac{\text{Actual amount of undersize obtained}}{\text{Amount of undersize that would be obtained with perfect separation}}}$$
> 
> Expressed as:
> $$\boxed{\eta_2 = \eta_{u} \cdot \eta_{o}}$$
> where $\eta_u$ = recovery of fines in undersize, and $\eta_o$ = recovery of coarse in oversize.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas required for solving **Screening Equipment** problems in KTU 2024 Scheme:

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:--|:--------|:------------|:------|
| 1 | $F = O + U$ | Total mass balance | kg/s or t/h |
| 2 | $F x_F = O x_O + U x_U$ | Component balance (coarse) | — |
| 3 | $\eta_u = \dfrac{U x_{u}}{F x_{F,\text{fine}}} \times 100$ | Recovery of fines (where $x_u$ = fraction of fines in undersize stream) | % |
| 4 | $\eta_o = \dfrac{O x_{O,\text{coarse}}}{F x_{F,\text{coarse}}} \times 100$ | Recovery of coarse | % |
| 5 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_u \cdot \eta_o$ | Combined efficiency | % |
| 6 | $\text{Open Area \%} = \dfrac{a^2}{(a+d)^2} \times 100$ | Mesh geometry | % |
| 7 | Mesh $\approx \dfrac{25.4 \text{ mm}}{a + d}$ | Mesh–aperture relation | mesh |
| 8 | $\eta_d = 1 - e^{-k t}$ | Probability of passage of a particle (k = constant) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $C = f(\rho_b, A, v, \theta)$ | Capacity function | t/h |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Screens are used as:
- **Primary scalpers** ahead of crushers in mining.
- **Sizing screens** between crushing stages.
- **Dewatering screens** in coal preparation.
- **Classifying screens** in cement, fertilizer, and food industries.
- **Safety/scalping screens** in bagging operations (remove oversize before packing).

The **screen aperture selection** directly affects downstream equipment: too coarse → crusher overload; too fine → low throughput, high blinding.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Two Product Material Balance

**Given:** A feed stream $F$ of known mass rate (e.g., **kg/h**) and known mass fraction of coarse material $x_F$. A perfect screen would send *all* coarse to oversize and *all* fines to undersize. A *real* screen, however, has some misplacement of particles.

We define:
- $x_O$ = mass fraction of coarse in the **oversize** stream.
- $x_U$ = mass fraction of coarse in the **undersize** stream.

**Total Balance (no reaction, no accumulation):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Input} &= \text{Output} \\
F &= O + U \quad \text{...(1)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Coarse-component balance:**
$$\begin{aligned}
F \cdot x_F &= O \cdot x_O + U \cdot x_U \quad \text{...(2)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Fine-component balance (since $1 - x$ = fine fraction):**
$$\begin{aligned}
F(1 - x_F) &= O(1 - x_O) + U(1 - x_U) \quad \text{...(3)}
\end{aligned}$$

Equations (1), (2), (3) are **not independent** — any two may be used to solve the unknowns ($O$, $U$, $x_O$, $x_U$) when the others are given.

## 3.2 Derivation of Screen Recovery Equations

Let the **fines** be the desired product in the **undersize** stream. The mass flow of fines in the feed = $F \cdot (1 - x_F)$ (if $x_F$ = mass fraction of coarse in feed). The mass flow of fines in the undersize = $U \cdot (1 - x_U)$.

The **recovery of fines** is:
$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_u &= \dfrac{\text{Fines in undersize}}{\text{Fines in feed}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{U(1 - x_U)}{F(1 - x_F)} \times 100
\end{aligned}$$

Similarly, the **recovery of coarse** to oversize:
$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_o &= \dfrac{\text{Coarse in oversize}}{\text{Coarse in feed}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{O \, x_O}{F \, x_F} \times 100
\end{aligned}$$

The **overall (comparative) efficiency**, assuming an *ideal* screen gives 100 % recovery of each stream:
$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{overall} &= \eta_u \cdot \eta_o \\
&= \dfrac{U(1 - x_U)}{F(1 - x_F)} \times \dfrac{O \, x_O}{F \, x_F} \times 10^4 \ \%
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (Model Problem — KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):**
> 
> A vibrating screen receives **600 kg/h** of a feed containing **30 % by mass** of coarse material (oversize). The screen produces an oversize stream containing **80 % coarse** and an undersize stream containing **5 % coarse** (by mass). Calculate:
> 
> **(a)** Mass flow rates of oversize and undersize streams.
> **(b)** Recovery of coarse to oversize.
> **(c)** Recovery of fines to undersize.
> **(d)** Overall screen efficiency.

### 3.3.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Given Data:**
- $F = 600$ kg/h
- $x_F = 0.30$ (mass fraction of coarse in feed)
- $x_O = 0.80$ (mass fraction of coarse in oversize)
- $x_U = 0.05$ (mass fraction of coarse in undersize)

**Part (a): Mass flow rates of O and U.**

Apply total balance:
$$\begin{aligned}
F &= O + U \\
600 &= O + U \quad \text{...(i)}
\end{aligned}$$

Apply coarse component balance:
$$\begin{aligned}
F \, x_F &= O \, x_O + U \, x_U \\
600 \times 0.30 &= O \times 0.80 + U \times 0.05 \\
180 &= 0.80 \, O + 0.05 \, U \quad \text{...(ii)}
\end{aligned}$$

Substitute $U = 600 - O$ into (ii):
$$\begin{aligned}
180 &= 0.80 \, O + 0.05(600 - O) \\
180 &= 0.80 \, O + 30 - 0.05 \, O \\
180 - 30 &= 0.75 \, O \\
150 &= 0.75 \, O \\
O &= \dfrac{150}{0.75} = 200 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:
$$\begin{aligned}
U &= 600 - 200 = 400 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}$$

> ✅ **Answer (a):** Oversize $O = 200$ kg/h; Undersize $U = 400$ kg/h.

---

**Part (b): Recovery of coarse to oversize.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_o &= \dfrac{O \, x_O}{F \, x_F} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{200 \times 0.80}{600 \times 0.30} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{160}{180} \times 100 = 88.89 \ \%
\end{aligned}$$

> ✅ **Answer (b):** $\eta_o \approx 88.89$ %.

---

**Part (c): Recovery of fines to undersize.**

Mass fraction of fines in feed = $1 - x_F = 0.70$
Mass fraction of fines in undersize = $1 - x_U = 0.95$

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_u &= \dfrac{U(1 - x_U)}{F(1 - x_F)} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{400 \times 0.95}{600 \times 0.70} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{380}{420} \times 100 = 90.48 \ \%
\end{aligned}$$

> ✅ **Answer (c):** $\eta_u \approx 90.48$ %.

---

**Part (d): Overall screen efficiency.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{overall} &= \eta_u \times \eta_o \\
&= 0.9048 \times 0.8889 \\
&= 0.8042 = 80.42 \ \%
\end{aligned}$$

> ✅ **Answer (d):** $\eta_{overall} \approx 80.4$ %.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Strategy Table:**

| Scenario in Question | What to Use |
|:---|:---|
| Feed rate and compositions of all 3 streams given | Direct total + component balance |
| Asked for "efficiency" — single value | Compute $\eta_u \cdot \eta_o$ (Definition 2) |
| Asked for "recovery of undersize" only | Use $\eta_u$ formula |
| Asked for "capacity" with bulk density | Use $C = \rho_b \times A \times v \times \sin\theta$ |
| Mesh size and wire diameter given | Use Open Area formula first |
| Asked for probability of passage | Use $\eta_d = 1 - e^{-kt}$ |
| Given aperture and asked for mesh | Mesh $= \dfrac{25.4}{a + d}$ mm |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Vibrating Screening System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ROM Feed Hopper] --> B[Feed Conveyor Belt]
    B --> C[Vibrating Screen Unit]
    C --> D{Screen Aperture Cut}
    D -->|Particles > Aperture| E[Oversize Discharge Chute]
    D -->|Particles < Aperture| F[Undersize Collection Bin]
    E --> G[Crusher or Recycle Loop]
    F --> H[Finished Product Stockpile]
    I[Vibration Motor Drive] --> C
    J[Suspension Springs] --> C
    K[Dust Collection Hood] --> C
    L[Control Panel and Amplitude Adjuster] --> I
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Trommel (Drum Screen)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feed Inlet Chute] --> B[Rotating Cylindrical Drum]
    B --> C{Internal Lifters and Lift Flights}
    C -->|Tumbling Action| D[Stratification of Feed]
    D --> E[Oversize Exits Drum End]
    D --> F[Undersize Passes Through Drum Walls]
    F --> G[Undersize Conveyor]
    E --> H[Oversize Discharge Conveyor]
    I[Drive Motor and Gear Reducer] --> B
```

## 4.3 Process Flow Block for a Two-Deck Screen System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Feed] --> B[Top Deck Coarse Screen]
    B -->|Oversize| C[Coarse Product Storage]
    B -->|Undersize| D[Second Deck Fine Screen]
    D -->|Oversize| E[Mid-size Product]
    D -->|Undersize| F[Fines Collection Hopper]
    G[Vibrator Exciter] --> B
    G --> D
    H[Isolation Springs] --> B
    H --> D
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Screen

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Choose Screen] --> B{Feed Moisture?}
    B -->|Dry| C{Particle Top Size?}
    B -->|Wet or Sticky| D[Grizzly with Water Spray]
    C -->|> 100 mm| E[Primary Grizzly]
    C -->|1 to 100 mm| F{Required Cut Size?}
    C -->|< 1 mm| G[Ultrafine Sieve or Air Classifier]
    F -->|Coarse 5 to 25 mm| H[Vibrating Screen Inclined]
    F -->|Fine 0.1 to 5 mm| I[Horizontal Vibrating Screen]
    F -->|Ultrafine| J[Gyratory Screener]
```

## 4.5 Material Balance Sankey-style Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[F equals 600 kg per hour] --> B[O equals 200 kg per hour]
    A --> C[U equals 400 kg per hour]
    B --> D[Coarse 80 percent]
    B --> E[Fines Carryover 20 percent]
    C --> F[Coarse Carryover 5 percent]
    C --> G[Fines 95 percent]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between oversize and undersize in a screening operation. What is meant by cut size?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Oversize** is the fraction of feed **retained on the screen** — particles whose diameter is *greater* than the screen aperture. **Undersize** is the fraction that **passes through** the apertures — particles *smaller* than the aperture. The **cut size ($d_{50}$)** is the particle diameter at which **50 % by mass** reports to oversize and **50 %** to undersize. It is the *boundary size* that defines the separation. The cut size is governed by the screen aperture and the probability of passage of a particle approaching that aperture.

*(3 marks — KTU standard: 1 mark definition, 1 mark oversize vs undersize, 1 mark cut size.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: List any three factors that affect the efficiency of a vibrating screen.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The three major factors are:
> 1. **Feed characteristics** — particle size distribution, shape, moisture content, bulk density, and clay content.
> 2. **Operating variables** — vibration amplitude, frequency, deck angle (inclination), and feed rate (tonnes/hr).
> 3. **Screen surface condition** — open area, aperture size, blinding (pegging) of apertures, and wear of wire.

*(3 marks — One mark for each valid factor.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> **Q (a)** A vibrating screen is fed with a feed slurry containing **40 % moisture** (by mass wet basis) at a rate of **10 t/h**. The screen produces an oversize of **2.5 t/h** with **70 % solids** and an undersize of **7.5 t/h** with **50 % solids**. Calculate the **mass of solids in the oversize** and **mass of solids in the undersize** streams. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q (b)** Define **screen efficiency** and derive the expression for **overall efficiency** in terms of recovery of coarse and fines. Compute the efficiency of the screen if the feed contained **45 % fines**, oversize contains **20 % fines** and undersize contains **5 % coarse**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution for A(a):

**Given:**
- Feed rate: 10 t/h with 40 % moisture → Solids in feed = 60 % of 10 = **6 t/h**
- Oversize rate: 2.5 t/h with 70 % solids → Solids in oversize = $2.5 \times 0.70 = 1.75$ t/h
- Undersize rate: 7.5 t/h with 50 % solids → Solids in undersize = $7.5 \times 0.50 = 3.75$ t/h

**Verification by mass balance on solids:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Solids in feed} &= \text{Solids in oversize} + \text{Solids in undersize} \\
6 &= 1.75 + 3.75 = 5.5 \text{ t/h}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> There is a **mismatch of 0.5 t/h** because some liquid reports with oversize and some with undersize — the difference reflects **moisture redistribution** during dewatering. In a KTU answer, the student should report both calculated values and the material balance closure.

**Answer:** Solids in oversize = **1.75 t/h**; Solids in undersize = **3.75 t/h**.

---

#### Solution for A(b):

**Definition of Screen Efficiency:**
Screen efficiency is the measure of how closely a real screen approaches the performance of an *ideal* screen (which sends 100 % of coarse to oversize and 100 % of fines to undersize).

**Derivation:**
Let $F$ = feed rate, $O$ = oversize rate, $U$ = undersize rate.
Let $x_F$ = mass fraction of coarse in feed, $x_O$ = mass fraction of coarse in oversize, $x_U$ = mass fraction of coarse in undersize.

**Recovery of coarse to oversize:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_o = \dfrac{O \cdot x_O}{F \cdot x_F} \times 100
\end{aligned}$$

**Recovery of fines to undersize:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_u = \dfrac{U \cdot (1 - x_U)}{F \cdot (1 - x_F)} \times 100
\end{aligned}$$

**Overall Efficiency** (comparative) = product of the two recoveries:
$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{overall} = \eta_u \times \eta_o
\end{aligned}$$

**Numerical Computation:**

Given:
- Feed contains 45 % fines → Coarse fraction in feed $x_F = 0.55$
- Oversize contains 20 % fines → Coarse fraction in oversize $x_O = 0.80$
- Undersize contains 5 % coarse → Coarse fraction in undersize $x_U = 0.05$

Apply total balance: $F = O + U$, and component balance: $F x_F = O x_O + U x_U$.

Let $F = 1$ (basis for calculation). Then:
$$\begin{aligned}
1 &= O + U \quad \text{...(i)} \\
1 \times 0.55 &= 0.80 \, O + 0.05 \, U \quad \text{...(ii)}
\end{aligned}$$

Substitute $U = 1 - O$:
$$\begin{aligned}
0.55 &= 0.80 \, O + 0.05(1 - O) \\
0.55 &= 0.80 \, O + 0.05 - 0.05 \, O \\
0.55 - 0.05 &= 0.75 \, O \\
0.50 &= 0.75 \, O \\
O &= \dfrac{0.50}{0.75} = 0.667 \\
U &= 1 - 0.667 = 0.333
\end{aligned}$$

**Compute Recoveries:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_o &= \dfrac{0.667 \times 0.80}{1 \times 0.55} = \dfrac{0.533}{0.55} = 0.9691 = 96.91 \ \% \\
\eta_u &= \dfrac{0.333 \times 0.95}{1 \times 0.45} = \dfrac{0.3164}{0.45} = 0.7030 = 70.30 \ \%
\end{aligned}$$

**Overall Efficiency:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{overall} &= 0.9691 \times 0.7030 = 0.6813 = 68.13 \ \%
\end{aligned}$$

> ✅ **Answer:** $\eta_o \approx 96.91$ %; $\eta_u \approx 70.30$ %; $\eta_{overall} \approx 68.13$ %.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **Q (a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **vibrating screen**. List two advantages over a stationary grizzly. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q (b)** A crushing plant processes **1200 t/h** of limestone. The feed contains **35 %** material coarser than 50 mm. After screening, the oversize contains **85 % coarse** and the undersize contains **8 % coarse**. Calculate the mass rates of oversize and undersize, and the **overall screen efficiency**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution for B(a):

**Construction and Working of a Vibrating Screen:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Constructional Features:**
> 
> 1. **Screen surface (deck):** Single or multiple decks made of woven wire mesh or perforated plate, supported on a rigid frame.
> 2. **Vibration mechanism:** A **vibrating motor** or **eccentric shaft** with counterweights creates high-frequency (600–7000 rpm) linear or elliptical vibration.
> 3. **Suspension system:** Coil springs or rubber isolators suspend the vibrating frame from the stationary structure, isolating vibration.
> 4. **Inclination:** Usually inclined at **15° to 25°** to the horizontal.
> 5. **Drive:** Electric motor with V-belt drive to the eccentric shaft.

**Working Principle:**
- Feed is introduced at the top end of the inclined deck.
- Vibration causes the bed to **stratify** — small particles percolate down through the bed to the screen surface.
- Particles **smaller than the aperture** pass through and discharge as undersize.
- Particles **larger than the aperture** travel along the deck to discharge as oversize.
- Amplitude and frequency are tuned so that particles get **multiple opportunities** to find an aperture (high presentation probability).

**Two Advantages of Vibrating Screen over a Stationary Grizzly:**
1. **Higher efficiency** — vibration prevents blinding, ensures higher throughput per unit area.
2. **Better separation sharpness** — produces a cleaner cut at a well-defined particle size, with reduced misplacement.
3. *(Optional third point)* Handles a wider range of feed sizes and moisture contents.

---

#### Solution for B(b):

**Given:**
- $F = 1200$ t/h
- Coarse in feed $x_F = 0.35$ (since 35 % is coarser than 50 mm)
- Coarse in oversize $x_O = 0.85$
- Coarse in undersize $x_U = 0.08$

**Total Balance:**
$$\begin{aligned}
1200 &= O + U \quad \text{...(i)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Coarse-component balance:**
$$\begin{aligned}
1200 \times 0.35 &= 0.85 \, O + 0.08 \, U \\
420 &= 0.85 \, O + 0.08 \, U \quad \text{...(ii)}
\end{aligned}$$

Substitute $U = 1200 - O$:
$$\begin{aligned}
420 &= 0.85 \, O + 0.08(1200 - O) \\
420 &= 0.85 \, O + 96 - 0.08 \, O \\
420 - 96 &= 0.77 \, O \\
324 &= 0.77 \, O \\
O &= \dfrac{324}{0.77} = 420.78 \text{ t/h} \approx 421 \text{ t/h}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:
$$\begin{aligned}
U &= 1200 - 420.78 = 779.22 \text{ t/h}
\end{aligned}$$

**Compute Recoveries:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_o &= \dfrac{O \cdot x_O}{F \cdot x_F} = \dfrac{420.78 \times 0.85}{1200 \times 0.35} = \dfrac{357.66}{420} = 0.8516 = 85.16 \ \% \\
\eta_u &= \dfrac{U \cdot (1 - x_U)}{F \cdot (1 - x_F)} = \dfrac{779.22 \times 0.92}{1200 \times 0.65} = \dfrac{716.88}{780} = 0.9191 = 91.91 \ \%
\end{aligned}$$

**Overall Efficiency:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{overall} &= \eta_u \times \eta_o = 0.8516 \times 0.9191 = 0.7827 = 78.27 \ \%
\end{aligned}$$

> ✅ **Answer:** $O \approx 421$ t/h; $U \approx 779$ t/h; $\eta_{overall} \approx 78.27$ %.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically LOSE marks in Screening Equipment problems:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing the two efficiency definitions** — KTU may ask for either recovery of fines OR overall efficiency. The 14-mark question's part (b) *always* expects $\eta_u \cdot \eta_o$, but Part A asks for a single recovery.
> 2. **Forgetting the 1 − x conversion** — many students compute $\eta_u$ using $x_U$ instead of $(1 - x_U)$. The bookwork and the substitution step are different.
> 3. **Not choosing a consistent basis** — always take $F = 1$ (or 100 kg) for cleaner arithmetic; mixing units (kg/h with t/h) loses the unit-consistency mark.
> 4. **Mesh vs aperture confusion** — when asked "what mesh size is needed for 2 mm aperture?" the answer is **NOT** simply $1/2$ mm — wire diameter must be added: $a + d$, then divided into 25.4 mm.
> 5. **Skipping the material balance check** — KTU examiners award 1 bonus mark if you verify $F = O + U$ numerically.
> 6. **Neglecting to mention blinding** in design questions — losing 1 conceptual mark easily.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision summary — Screening Equipment:**
>
> - **Screening = mechanical size separation** using a perforated surface.
> - **Two product streams:** Oversize (retained) and Undersize (passed).
> - **Cut size ($d_{50}$)** = boundary particle size; controls the separation.
> - **Master equations to memorize:**
>   * $F = O + U$ (total balance)
>   * $F x_F = O x_O + U x_U$ (component balance)
>   * $\eta_u = \dfrac{U(1-x_U)}{F(1-x_F)} \times 100$ (fines recovery)
>   * $\eta_o = \dfrac{O x_O}{F x_F} \times 100$ (coarse recovery)
>   * $\eta_{overall} = \eta_u \cdot \eta_o$
>   * $\text{Open Area} = \dfrac{a^2}{(a+d)^2} \times 100$ %
>   * $\text{Mesh} = \dfrac{25.4}{a + d}$ (in mm)
> - **Screen types (in order of exam importance):** Vibrating screen > Grizzly > Trommel > Gyratory > Oscillating.
> - **Vibrating screens dominate** industrial applications — high frequency, inclined deck, vibrator motor.
> - **Blinding (pegging)** = the #1 practical problem — apertures blocked by near-size particles.
> - **Efficiency depends on:** feed PSD, moisture, vibration amplitude/frequency, deck angle, open area, blinding.
> - **Capacity** scales with **bulk density × deck area × feed velocity × sin(θ)**.
> - **Standard bulk density** for limestone ≈ **1600 kg/m³**; for coal ≈ **800–900 kg/m³**; for iron ore ≈ **2500–3000 kg/m³**.
> - **Always convert all mass rates to the same units** (kg/h or t/h) before applying balance.
> - **For 14-mark problems:** always (i) draw a labeled block diagram (1 mark), (ii) state assumptions (1 mark), (iii) apply total + component balance (4 marks), (iv) compute recoveries and overall efficiency (4 marks), (v) discuss real-world implications and validation (4 marks).
> - **Real-world examples to cite in answers:** Coal preparation plants, iron ore crushing circuits, cement raw mix, fertilizer granulation, aggregate quarries — these give context marks in long answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effectiveness of Screens
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Effectiveness of Screens

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Screen Effectiveness (η):** A dimensionless metric, expressed as a percentage, that quantifies the ability of a screening machine to separate a feed mixture into correctly classified **oversize** (coarse/retained on screen) and **undersize** (fine/passing through screen) fractions, while minimizing the quantity of *misplaced material* (oversize in fines stream or undersize in coarse stream).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PBSFT304 — Chemical Process Principles, Module 4: *Size Reduction, Size Enlargement and Mechanical Separations*), screens are classified as **Screening / Sifting Operations** under the umbrella of *Mechanical Separations based on Size*. The **effectiveness** of a screen is the engineering yardstick that converts a qualitative "good/bad" separation into a quantifiable, design-grade parameter (typically **70 % – 95 %** for industrial units).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bouncer at a Club"

Imagine a nightclub with two doors:
- **VIP Door** (the screen aperture / mesh) → lets only "small" guests pass (undersize).
- **Main Door** → where the bouncer (the vibrating screen deck) directs all "big" guests (oversize).

A *perfect* bouncer (η = 100 %) never lets a small guest into the main door and never blocks a big guest from going VIP. But a *real* bouncer is imperfect:
- Some short guests sneak past the bouncer into the main door → these are **misplaced fines** in the oversize stream.
- Some tall guests squeeze through VIP → these are **misplaced coarse** in the undersize stream.

**Effectiveness** simply asks: *"How close to the perfect bouncer is my screen, in %?"* The closer to 100 %, the better the screen.

## 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics (KTU 2024 Highlight)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants & Definitions used in KTU Valuation:**
> - **Aperture (a):** Clear opening between screen wires, in **mm** or **μm**.
> - **Mesh Number:** Number of openings per linear inch (Tyler standard) or per cm (BSS).
> - **Cut Size (d₅₀):** The aperture size at which 50 % of particles report to each stream.
> - **Misplaced Material:** Particles of size `> aperture` found in undersize, or `< aperture` found in oversize.
> - **Capacity (Q):** Mass flow rate of feed, in **tonnes/hr** or **kg/s**.
> - **Typical Industrial η Range:** Grizzly < 70 %, Vibrating Screen 80 – 90 %, High-Frequency Screen > 95 %.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stepwise Logic of Screen Performance

A screen is fundamentally a **two-output classifier**. To evaluate it, we sample three streams:
1. **Feed (F)** — material entering the screen.
2. **Oversize (O)** — material retained on the screen (also called "coarse" or "plus" material).
3. **Undersize (U)** — material passing through the screen (also called "fines" or "minus" material).

Every particle in the feed is classified as either "should pass" (true undersize) or "should not pass" (true oversize). The screen's job is to physically execute this ideal classification. Effectiveness measures the **deviation from the ideal**.

### The Four Streams of Material — A 2×2 Matrix

| | **Reports to Oversize (O)** | **Reports to Undersize (U)** |
|---|---|---|
| **True Undersize (fines, m·F)** | ❌ *Misplaced — should have passed* | ✅ *Correctly placed* |
| **True Oversize (coarse, n·F)** | ✅ *Correctly placed* | ❌ *Misplaced — should have been retained* |

Where:
- **m** = mass fraction of true undersize in feed
- **n** = mass fraction of true oversize in feed = `1 − m`

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` / `\mid` in table cells — never the vertical pipe `|`.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Overall Mass Balance | $F = O + U$ | kg/hr | Conservation of mass |
| 2 | Component Mass Balance (Fines) | $F \cdot m = O \cdot o + U \cdot u$ | kg/hr | Where `o` = fines fraction in O, `u` = fines fraction in U |
| 3 | Component Mass Balance (Coarse) | $F \cdot n = O \cdot (1-o) + U \cdot (1-u)$ | kg/hr | Equivalent to row 2 |
| 4 | Recovery of Fines (η_f) | $\eta_f = \dfrac{U \cdot u}{F \cdot m} \times 100$ | % | Fraction of true fines recovered as undersize |
| 5 | Recovery of Coarse (η_c) | $\eta_c = \dfrac{O \cdot (1-o)}{F \cdot n} \times 100$ | % | Fraction of true coarse recovered as oversize |
| 6 | **Overall Effectiveness (Taggart)** | $\eta = \eta_f + \eta_c - 100$ | % | **Most-tested KTU formula** |
| 7 | Alternative Closed Form | $\eta = 100 \cdot \dfrac{m \cdot u - m \cdot o}{u \cdot (1-o)}$ simplified as $\eta = \dfrac{(u-m)(1-o)}{m(1-m)(1-o)} \cdot 100$ | % | Used when O/U not given, only fractions known |
| 8 | **Simplified Fines Recovery** | $\eta_f = \dfrac{u(o-m)}{m(u-o)} \cdot 100$ | % | When O/F or U/F is unknown |
| 9 | Capacity Criterion (Carter) | $Q = 0.6 \cdot A \cdot \rho_b \cdot v$ | tonnes/hr | Design check; `A` = screen area (m²), `v` = velocity (m/s) |
| 10 | Cut-Size Relation (for ideal screen) | $d_{50} = a$ (aperture) | m | For perfect separation |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **mineral beneficiation** (e.g., iron ore, coal, limestone), screen effectiveness directly translates to:
- **Product purity** (e.g., silica in iron-ore fines).
- **Recycling efficiency** in **crushed-aggregate quarries**.
- **Pharmaceutical tablet manufacturing** — size uniformity for granulation.
- **Cement industry** — pre-blending bed consistency.
- **Food processing** — flour/sugar sifting, salt grading.

A high η means downstream crushers/grinders do *less redundant work* on already-fine material, saving **₹ lakhs in energy** annually.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Component Mass Balance (Fines Balance)

Consider a screen with feed `F`, oversize `O`, undersize `U`. Let `m` = mass fraction of undersize in feed. The total mass of fines in the feed must equal the mass of fines appearing in the oversize stream plus the mass of fines appearing in the undersize stream.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fines in Feed} &= \text{Fines in Oversize} + \text{Fines in Undersize} \\
F \cdot m &= O \cdot o + U \cdot u \quad \text{...(Equation A)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the overall balance $F = O + U$, we solve for $O$ and $U$ in terms of `F` and one stream split. Let $r = U/F$ (undersize fraction of feed).

$$
\begin{aligned}
O &= F(1-r), \quad U = F \cdot r \\
\text{Substituting in Eq. A:} \quad F \cdot m &= F(1-r) \cdot o + F \cdot r \cdot u \\
m &= (1-r) \cdot o + r \cdot u \\
\text{Solving for } r: \quad r &= \dfrac{m - o}{u - o}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Insight:** The fraction of feed that exits as undersize `r` depends purely on the three measured mass fractions `m`, `o`, `u`. This is a *fingerprint* of the screen's performance.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Recovery of Fines (η_f)

By definition, fines recovery is the *ratio of correctly-collected fines* to *total fines available in feed*.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_f &= \dfrac{\text{Mass of fines in undersize}}{\text{Mass of fines in feed}} \times 100 \\
      &= \dfrac{U \cdot u}{F \cdot m} \times 100 \\
\text{Substituting } U/F = r = (m-o)/(u-o): \quad \eta_f &= \dfrac{r \cdot u}{m} \times 100 \\
      &= \dfrac{u(m-o)}{m(u-o)} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Overall Effectiveness (Taggart Form)

The overall effectiveness penalises the screen for *both* types of misplaced material.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \eta_f + \eta_c - 100 \\
    &= \left[ \dfrac{U \cdot u}{F \cdot m} + \dfrac{O \cdot (1-o)}{F \cdot n} \right] \times 100 - 100 \\
    &= 100 \left[ \dfrac{r \cdot u}{m} + \dfrac{(1-r)(1-o)}{(1-m)} - 1 \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $r = (m-o)/(u-o)$ and simplifying algebraically (a tedious but standard reduction shown in Coulson & Richardson Vol. 2, but derivable by direct expansion):

$$
\eta = 100 \cdot \dfrac{(u-m)(1-o)}{m(1-m)(1-o)}
$$

> **Memory Tip for KTU Exam:** *Numerator = product of (recovery surplus) and (coarse purity). Denominator = product of (max possible fines) and (coarse purity in feed).*

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Problem:** A vibrating screen processes **1000 kg/hr** of crushed limestone. Analysis shows:
- Feed: **40 %** is finer than the screen aperture (m = 0.40).
- Oversize stream (620 kg/hr) contains **8 %** fines (o = 0.08).
- Undersize stream (380 kg/hr) contains **94 %** fines (u = 0.94).

**Required:** (a) Verify the mass balance. (b) Calculate fines recovery. (c) Calculate coarse recovery. (d) Calculate overall screen effectiveness.

### Part (a) — Mass Balance Check

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 1000 \text{ kg/hr}, \quad O = 620 \text{ kg/hr}, \quad U = 380 \text{ kg/hr} \\
F - (O + U) &= 1000 - (620 + 380) = 0 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

Fines balance check:
$$
\begin{aligned}
F \cdot m &= O \cdot o + U \cdot u \\
1000 \times 0.40 &= 620 \times 0.08 + 380 \times 0.94 \\
400 &= 49.6 + 357.2 = 406.8
\end{aligned}
$$

> Small discrepancy of 6.8 kg/hr is due to **experimental sampling error** — accept within ±2 % in KTU valuation.

### Part (b) — Fines Recovery (η_f)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_f &= \dfrac{U \cdot u}{F \cdot m} \times 100 = \dfrac{380 \times 0.94}{1000 \times 0.40} \times 100 \\
      &= \dfrac{357.2}{400} \times 100 = \mathbf{89.30 \, \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Part (c) — Coarse Recovery (η_c)

True oversize in feed: $n = 1 - m = 0.60$. Oversize in O: $1 - o = 0.92$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_c &= \dfrac{O \cdot (1-o)}{F \cdot n} \times 100 = \dfrac{620 \times 0.92}{1000 \times 0.60} \times 100 \\
      &= \dfrac{570.4}{600} \times 100 = \mathbf{95.07 \, \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Part (d) — Overall Effectiveness (η)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \eta_f + \eta_c - 100 \\
    &= 89.30 + 95.07 - 100 \\
    &= \mathbf{84.37 \, \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The screen is performing **above the industrial average** (typical vibrating screen: 80 – 90 %). The 6.8 % fines in oversize are the main source of inefficiency — the operator should consider **reducing feed rate** or **increasing vibration amplitude**.

---
