# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define sieve analysis. State any two standard sieve series used in particle size analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

Sieve analysis is a **particle size distribution (PSD) determination technique** in which a known mass of granular solid is passed through a stack of standard testing sieves arranged in descending order of aperture size, and the mass retained on each sieve is measured to express the size distribution as a percentage of the total sample mass.

**Two standard sieve series:**
1. **ISO 565 / ISO 3310-1** (international standard) — uses aperture sizes in geometric progression with ratio $\sqrt{2}$ or $\sqrt[4]{2}$.
2. **ASTM E11** (American standard) — equivalent to ISO with aperture values in mm or µm; sieve designation in mesh number (openings per inch) or µm.
3. *(Alternate answer)* **Tyler standard series** — the original US series, basis of modern standards.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the Sauter mean diameter? Write its mathematical expression.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Sauter mean diameter** is defined as the diameter of a hypothetical sphere that has the **same volume-to-surface-area ratio** as the entire population of particles. It is particularly used in processes where **surface area controls the rate** — such as heterogeneous reactions, dissolution, and mass transfer.

**Expression:**

$$
d_{sv} = \frac{1}{\displaystyle\sum_{i} \frac{x_i}{d_i}}
$$

where $x_i$ is the mass fraction of particles in size class $i$ and $d_i$ is the mean diameter of that size class.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **complete procedure** for conducting a sieve analysis, listing the apparatus required, the sample preparation steps, and the data reduction methodology. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 400 g sample of silica sand is sieved through a stack of standard sieves. The retained masses are:

| Sieve (µm) | 710 | 500 | 355 | 250 | 180 | 125 | 90 (pan) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mass retained (g) | 18 | 42 | 78 | 96 | 84 | 52 | 30 |

Calculate:
- (i) The mass fraction retained on each sieve,
- (ii) The cumulative % passing through each sieve,
- (iii) The mass median diameter $d_{50}$ (by log-linear interpolation). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A(a):**

**Apparatus required:**
1. Set of standard testing sieves (with lid and pan) — typically nested 6–8 sieves.
2. **Mechanical sieve shaker** (vibratory type) with timer.
3. **Analytical balance** (accuracy ±0.01 g).
4. **Oven** for sample drying (105–110 °C).
5. **Riffler** or **coning-and-quartering device** for representative sampling.
6. **Brush** for cleaning sieves; **desiccator** for cooling dried sample.

**Procedure:**

1. **Sampling:** From the bulk lot, obtain a representative sample by **riffling** or **coning-and-quartering**. The sample size should be chosen such that no sieve is overloaded (the rule of thumb is that the mass retained on any sieve should not exceed the value of $d^2 \cdot k$, where $k$ is an empirical constant).
2. **Drying:** Dry the sample in a hot-air oven at **105–110 °C for 1 hour** (or until constant mass) to remove moisture, then cool in a desiccator.
3. **Weighing the initial mass ($M$):** Record $M$ accurately on the balance.
4. **Stack assembly:** Place sieves in **descending aperture order** (coarsest on top, finest immediately above the pan). Fit a lid on top.
5. **Sieving:** Mount the stack on the sieve shaker. Run for **15–20 minutes** (or until the rate of mass passing the bottom sieve is < 0.1 % of $M$ per minute).
6. **Weighing retained fractions:** Carefully remove each sieve, tap and brush out the retained material, and weigh on the balance. Record $m_i$ for each sieve.
7. **Mass balance check:** Verify that $\sum m_i = M$ within $\pm 1\%$. If not, discard the data and repeat.
8. **Data reduction:** Compute $x_i = m_i / M$, then cumulative % retained and cumulative % passing.
9. **Plotting:** Plot cumulative % passing (Y-axis, linear) *versus* aperture size (X-axis, **logarithmic**). This is the **S-curve**.
10. **Reporting:** Read off $d_{10}, d_{50}, d_{90}$ and compute the Sauter mean.

**Engineering significance:** The S-curve provides a quick visual assessment of distribution width and skewness; narrow distributions (steep S-curve) indicate a well-classified product, while broad/shallow curves indicate polydisperse material.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A(b):**

**Total mass:** $M = 400$ g.

**Mass balance check:** $18 + 42 + 78 + 96 + 84 + 52 + 30 = 400$ g ✓ (perfect, no loss).

**(i) Mass fraction retained on each sieve:** Using $x_i = m_i / 400$:

| Sieve $d_i$ (µm) | $m_i$ (g) | $x_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 710 | 18 | 0.0450 |
| 500 | 42 | 0.1050 |
| 355 | 78 | 0.1950 |
| 250 | 96 | 0.2400 |
| 180 | 84 | 0.2100 |
| 125 | 52 | 0.1300 |
| 90 (pan) | 30 | 0.0750 |
| **Sum** | **400** | **1.0000** |

**(ii) Cumulative % passing:**

| $d_i$ (µm) | $x_i$ | Cum. fraction retained | Cum. % passing |
|---|---|---|---|
| 710 | 0.0450 | 0.0450 | **95.50** |
| 500 | 0.1050 | 0.1500 | **85.00** |
| 355 | 0.1950 | 0.3450 | **65.50** |
| 250 | 0.2400 | 0.5850 | **41.50** |
| 180 | 0.2100 | 0.7950 | **20.50** |
| 125 | 0.1300 | 0.9250 | **7.50** |
| 90 | 0.0750 | 1.0000 | **0.00** |

**(iii) Mass median diameter $d_{50}$:**
The 50 % passing point lies **between 250 µm (41.50 %) and 355 µm (65.50 %)**.

Using log-linear interpolation:

$$
\log d_{50} = \log d_a + \frac{(50 - P_a)}{(P_b - P_a)} \cdot \log\!\left(\frac{d_b}{d_a}\right)
$$

where $d_a = 250$ µm, $P_a = 41.50$ %; $d_b = 355$ µm, $P_b = 65.50$ %.

$$
\log d_{50} = \log 250 + \frac{50 - 41.50}{65.50 - 41.50} \cdot \log\!\left(\frac{355}{250}\right)
$$

$$
\log d_{50} = 2.3979 + \frac{8.50}{24.00} \cdot \log(1.4200)
$$

$$
\log d_{50} = 2.3979 + 0.3542 \times 0.1523 = 2.3979 + 0.0539 = 2.4518
$$

$$
\boxed{\;d_{50} \approx 10^{2.4518} \approx 283.0\;\mu\text{m}\;}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a)** Define the following terms with their formulas, where applicable:
**(i) Mass median diameter (MMD)**
**(ii) Arithmetic mean diameter**
**(iii) Volume-surface mean (Sauter) diameter**
**(iv) Specific surface area of a powder**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The results of a sieve analysis on a 200 g sample of crushed quartz are given below. Determine the **Sauter mean diameter** and the **mass median diameter** from the data.

| Sieve (µm) | 850 | 600 | 425 | 300 | 212 | 150 | 75 (pan) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mass retained (g) | 6 | 18 | 30 | 50 | 46 | 32 | 18 |

**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B(a):**

**(i) Mass Median Diameter (MMD):** It is the particle diameter such that **50 % of the mass of the sample is finer (smaller) than this size and 50 % is coarser (larger)**. It is read directly from the cumulative % passing curve at the 50 % point. For a distribution of size classes, log-linear interpolation is used:

$$
\log d_{50} = \log d_a + \frac{(50 - P_a)}{(P_b - P_a)} \cdot \log\!\left(\frac{d_b}{d_a}\right)
$$

**(ii) Arithmetic Mean Diameter:**

$$
d_{mean} = \sum_{i} x_i \, d_i
$$

where $x_i$ is the mass fraction in size class $i$ and $d_i$ is the geometric (or arithmetic) mean of the upper and lower aperture bounds of that class. This is the simple **weighted average** of all size classes.

**(iii) Volume-Surface (Sauter) Mean Diameter:** Defined as the diameter of a sphere having the same volume-to-surface ratio as the population:

$$
d_{sv} = \frac{1}{\displaystyle\sum_{i} \frac{x_i}{d_i}}
$$

It is also called the **specific surface mean diameter**.

**(iv) Specific Surface Area of a Powder:** Total surface area per unit mass. For a monodisperse powder of diameter $d$ and density $\rho_p$:

$$
S_v = \frac{A}{m} = \frac{\pi d^2}{\tfrac{\pi}{6} d^3 \, \rho_p} = \frac{6}{d \, \rho_p} \quad \left[\frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{kg}}\right]
$$

For a polydisperse powder, the **Sauter mean** $d_{sv}$ is used in place of $d$:

$$
S_v = \frac{6}{d_{sv}\,\rho_p}
$$

This is the most operationally useful definition in catalysis and pharmaceutical engineering.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B(b):**

**Total mass:** $M = 6+18+30+50+46+32+18 = 200$ g ✓ (perfect balance).

**Step 1 — Mass fractions $x_i = m_i / 200$ and cumulative % passing:**

| $d_i$ (µm) | $m_i$ (g) | $x_i$ | Cum. fraction retained | Cum. % passing |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 850 | 6 | 0.030 | 0.030 | 97.0 |
| 600 | 18 | 0.090 | 0.120 | 88.0 |
| 425 | 30 | 0.150 | 0.270 | 73.0 |
| 300 | 50 | 0.250 | 0.520 | 48.0 |
| 212 | 46 | 0.230 | 0.750 | 25.0 |
| 150 | 32 | 0.160 | 0.910 | 9.0 |
| 75 (pan) | 18 | 0.090 | 1.000 | 0.0 |

**Step 2 — Sauter mean diameter:**

Compute $\sum x_i / d_i$:

| $d_i$ (µm) | $x_i$ | $x_i/d_i$ ($\mu\text{m}^{-1}$) |
|---|---|---|
| 850 | 0.030 | $3.529 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| 600 | 0.090 | $1.500 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| 425 | 0.150 | $3.529 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| 300 | 0.250 | $8.333 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| 212 | 0.230 | $1.085 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 150 | 0.160 | $1.067 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 75 | 0.090 | $1.200 \times 10^{-3}$ |

Summing:

$$
\sum \frac{x_i}{d_i} = (0.3529 + 1.500 + 3.529 + 8.333 + 10.85 + 10.67 + 12.00) \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\sum \frac{x_i}{d_i} = 47.234 \times 10^{-4}\;\mu\text{m}^{-1} = 4.7234 \times 10^{-3}\;\mu\text{m}^{-1}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;d_{sv} = \frac{1}{4.7234 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 211.7\;\mu\text{m}\;}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass median diameter $d_{50}$:**
The 50 % passing point lies between **300 µm (48 % passing)** and **425 µm (73 % passing)**. Log-linear interpolation:

$$
\log d_{50} = \log 300 + \frac{50 - 48}{73 - 48} \cdot \log\!\left(\frac{425}{300}\right)
$$

$$
\log d_{50} = 2.4771 + \frac{2}{25} \cdot \log(1.4167)
$$

$$
\log d_{50} = 2.4771 + 0.08 \times 0.1514 = 2.4771 + 0.0121 = 2.4892
$$

$$
\boxed{\;d_{50} \approx 10^{2.4892} \approx 308.4\;\mu\text{m}\;}
$$

**Observations:**
- $d_{sv} \approx 211.7\;\mu\text{m} < d_{50} \approx 308.4\;\mu\text{m}$ — confirms the **Sauter mean is biased toward fines** (larger contribution of small particles to total surface area).
- The distribution is **fine-skewed** (significant mass below 200 µm).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Sieve Analysis Questions:**
>
> 1. **Mass Balance Check (–1 mark):** Students frequently *omit* the verification that $\sum m_i = M$. Examiners explicitly look for this as a sign of laboratory discipline. **Always write it explicitly in your solution.**
>
> 2. **Log-Linear vs Linear Interpolation (–2 marks):** The 50 % point **must be interpolated in log space**, since the X-axis (aperture size) is logarithmic by KTU convention. Linear interpolation gives a *wrong* value and is the #1 reason students lose marks on MMD problems.
>
> 3. **Pan Inclusion (–1 mark):** The **bottom pan** is *itself* a sieve (it has a defined boundary — its contents are "finer than the second-smallest sieve"). Always treat it as the last data row.
>
> 4. **Units in $d_{sv}$ Calculation (–1 mark):** When summing $x_i/d_i$, the result carries the units of $\mu\text{m}^{-1}$. Failing to invert correctly yields a wrong order of magnitude ($10^{3}$ µm instead of $10^{2}$ µm). **Double-check the exponent.**
>
> 5. **Forgetting to State Sieve Standard (–1 mark):** When asked about apparatus, always name a specific standard (e.g., *ISO 565*, *ASTM E11*). Writing only "standard sieves" loses a mark.
>
> 6. **Confusing $d_{sv}$ with the median:** Examiners love asking "Why is $d_{sv} < d_{50}$?" or "Which mean is relevant for a mass-transfer-controlled process?" — answer must invoke the **surface-area bias** of the Sauter mean.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Sieve Analysis**
>
> - **Definition:** Sieve analysis is the **PSD determination method** by mechanically separating a granular sample through a stack of standard wire-mesh sieves.
> - **Aperture range:** ~ **75 µm to 100 mm**. Below 75 µm → use sedimentation or laser diffraction; above 100 mm → use direct caliper measurement.
> - **Sieve order:** **Largest aperture on top**, pan at the bottom. **Always** with a lid to prevent loss.
> - **Sample mass rule:** Top sieve should not be overloaded — typical rule: $m_{max} \approx 20 \cdot d_{aperture}^2$ (in g if $d$ is in cm).
> - **Sieving time:** Until mass loss in the pan is < **0.1 % of $M$ per minute**, typically **15–20 min** on a vibratory shaker.
> - **Mass balance check:** $\sum m_i = M \pm 1\%$ — **mandatory** for a valid run.
> - **S-curve plot:** Cumulative % passing (Y, linear) *versus* aperture $d_i$ (X, **logarithmic**).
> - **Mass Median Diameter $d_{50}$:** Read at 50 % passing on the S-curve. **Log-linear interpolation** is required between adjacent sieves.
> - **Sauter Mean Diameter $d_{sv}$:** $d_{sv} = 1 \big/ \sum (x_i / d_i)$ — biased toward **fines**, used when **surface area controls** the process.
> - **Arithmetic mean:** $\sum x_i d_i$ — used for **count-based** averaging.
> - **Volume mean:** $\sum x_i d_i^3 \big/ \sum x_i d_i^2$ — used for **volume-based** calculations.
> - **Specific surface area:** $S_v = 6 / (d_{sv} \rho_p)$ — key in catalysis, dissolution, and drying.
> - **Standard series:** ISO 565 / ASTM E11 / Tyler — use a **geometric ratio** of $\sqrt{2}$ or $\sqrt[4]{2}$.
> - **Why $d_{sv} < d_{50}$ in fine-skewed distributions:** Because small particles contribute *disproportionately* to total surface area; the Sauter mean is an **inverse-weighted** diameter.
> - **Key error sources in lab:** Inadequate sampling, overloaded sieves, insufficient shaking time, dirty/clogged meshes, electrostatic charging of fines (use antistatic agent or humid air for cohesive powders).
> - **Engineering link:** Sieve analysis directly feeds into the **Ergun equation** for packed-bed pressure drop, **classification efficiency** curves, and **crusher/ball-mill** product specifications.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Average Diameter and Specific Surface; Size Reduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Average Diameter and Specific Surface; Size Reduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Average Particle Diameter

### Formal Definition
The **average particle diameter** of a particulate solid is a single representative diameter that characterizes a population of particles having a distribution of sizes. Since any given powder is a mixture of particles of different sizes, an "average" or "mean" diameter must be defined based on the property being measured (e.g., number, length, surface, volume, or mass).

For a sample containing $N$ particles of size $D$ and $N_i$ particles of size $D_i$, different averaging methods yield different mean diameters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Different averaging methods are not interchangeable — the choice depends on the application. For example, surface-related phenomena use the **Sauter mean diameter** ($D_{vs}$), while mass-flow calculations use the **mass mean diameter** ($D_w$).

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a class of 60 students of different heights. If you want to find the "average height" for buying T-shirts, you would use the **arithmetic mean**. But if you want to know the total cloth needed to cover the entire class, you would need a **surface-weighted mean**. Similarly, for particles, the "right" average depends on *what property* matters to your process.

---

## 1.2 Specific Surface Area

### Formal Definition
**Specific surface area** is the surface area of the particles per unit mass (or per unit volume) of the solid. It is a critical property in operations involving heat transfer, mass transfer, reaction rates, and drying, because a higher specific surface means more contact area per unit mass.

For spherical particles of diameter $D$ and density $\rho_p$:

$$S_w = \frac{\text{Surface of one particle} \times N}{\text{Mass of N particles}} = \frac{6}{\rho_p D}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Specific surface is *inversely* proportional to particle diameter. Halving the particle size doubles the specific surface — this is why **size reduction is so crucial** in chemical processes.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a sugar cube (one large particle) vs. powdered sugar of the same mass. Powdered sugar dissolves in water almost instantly because it has an enormous specific surface area. This is the practical power of fine particles.

---

## 1.3 Size Reduction

### Formal Definition
**Size reduction** refers to the unit operation in which solid particles are mechanically broken into smaller fragments. It is achieved by applying **crushing, impact, attrition, shearing, or cutting** forces. Size reduction is essential for:
- Increasing surface area for reaction/dissolution
- Separating different components
- Improving flow/handling characteristics
- Meeting product specifications

> [!NOTE]
> The **energy required** for size reduction is a critical design parameter and is governed by three classical laws: **Rittinger's, Kick's, and Bond's Law**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of cracking a walnut with your hand (Rittinger — surface area dominates) vs. crushing a boulder with a hydraulic press (Kick — volumetric strain dominates). Different materials and size ratios obey different laws.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Mean Diameters (Number-Average Bases)

Given $N$ particles total, with $N_i$ particles of size $D_i$:

| Mean Type | Formula | Physical Meaning |
|-----------|---------|------------------|
| **Arithmetic Mean** | $D_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum N_i D_i}{\sum N_i}$ | Simple length average |
| **Geometric Mean** | $D_g = \left(\prod D_i^{N_i}\right)^{1/\sum N_i}$ | Log-normal average |
| **Harmonic Mean** | $D_h = \dfrac{\sum N_i}{\sum \left(\dfrac{N_i}{D_i}\right)}$ | Reciprocal-based average |
| **Length Mean** | $D_L = \dfrac{\sum N_i D_i^2}{\sum N_i D_i}$ | Weighted by length |
| **Surface Mean** | $D_S = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum N_i D_i^2}{\sum N_i}}$ | Surface-area based |
| **Volume Mean** | $D_V = \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{\sum N_i D_i^3}{\sum N_i}}$ | Volume-based |

> [!NOTE]
> **Proof of relation (Surface Mean):** For a single particle, $S \propto D^2$, so $D^2 \propto S$, hence $D_S = \sqrt{\sum x_i D_i^2}$ where $x_i$ is the number fraction.

## 2.2 Mass-Average and Sauter Mean

When sieve analysis is available, fractions are by **mass ($x_i$)**, not by number. The most important KTU-mean diameters are:

| Mean | Formula | Application |
|------|---------|-------------|
| **Mass Mean Diameter** | $D_w = \dfrac{\sum x_i}{\sum \left(\dfrac{x_i}{D_i}\right)}$ | Mass flow problems |
| **Sauter Mean Diameter** | $D_{vs} = \dfrac{1}{\sum \left(\dfrac{x_i}{D_i}\right)}$ | Surface-area-based processes |
| **Volume Mean** | $D_v = \sqrt[3]{\sum \dfrac{x_i}{D_i^2}\Big/\sum \dfrac{x_i}{D_i^3}}$ | Volumetric analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sauter mean diameter ($D_{vs}$)** is the most frequently asked in KTU exams because it directly connects specific surface area to sieve data.

## 2.3 Number of Particles in a Mixture

For a mass $M$ of solid with density $\rho_p$ and uniform diameter $D$:

$$N = \frac{M}{\rho_p \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} D^3} = \frac{6M}{\pi \rho_p D^3}$$

For a mixture, sum contributions: $N_T = \sum N_i$.

## 2.4 Specific Surface Area

| Basis | Formula (Spherical) |
|-------|---------------------|
| **Volume basis** $S_v$ | $S_v = \dfrac{6}{D_{vs}} \;\; [m^2/m^3]$ |
| **Mass basis** $S_w$ | $S_w = \dfrac{6}{\rho_p D_{vs}} \;\; [m^2/kg]$ |
| **For mixture** | $S_w = \dfrac{6}{\rho_p} \sum \left(\dfrac{x_i}{D_i}\right)$ |

## 2.5 Laws of Size Reduction

### Rittinger's Law (Surface Area Dominant — fine grinding)
$$E = K_R \left( \frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1} \right) = K_R (S_2 - S_1)$$

- Energy is proportional to **new surface created**.
- Best for **fine grinding** where $D_2/D_1$ is large.

### Kick's Law (Volumetric Strain Dominant — coarse crushing)
$$E = K_K \ln\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)$$

- Energy is proportional to **log of reduction ratio**.
- Best for **coarse crushing**.

### Bond's Law (Intermediate — general use)
$$E = E_i \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}} \right) = W_i \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F}} \right)$$

where $W_i$ = **Work Index** (kWh/ton) — the energy to reduce from infinite size to 100 μm.

### Bond's Power Formula
$$P = 1.46 \, W_i \cdot Q \left( \frac{\sqrt{d} - \sqrt{D}}{\sqrt{d \cdot D}} \right)$$

where $Q$ = capacity (tons/hr), $d$ = discharge size, $D$ = feed size.

## 2.6 Crushing Efficiency

$$\eta_c = \frac{\text{Surface energy created}}{\text{Energy actually supplied}} = \frac{E_{surface}}{E_{actual}}$$

$$\eta_c = \frac{\sigma \cdot \Delta S}{E_a}$$

where $\sigma$ = surface tension (J/m²) of the solid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Real crushing efficiencies are very low (typically **0.1% to 2%**) — most energy is lost as heat, sound, and deformation. This is a very common KTU question topic.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units |
|----------|---------|-------|
| Sauter mean | $D_{vs} = 1 / \sum (x_i / D_i)$ | m |
| Mass mean | $D_w = \sum x_i / \sum (x_i / D_i)$ | m |
| Specific surface (mass) | $S_w = 6 / (\rho_p D_{vs})$ | m²/kg |
| Number of particles | $N = 6M / (\pi \rho_p D^3)$ | – |
| Rittinger | $E = K_R(1/D_2 - 1/D_1)$ | kWh/ton |
| Kick | $E = K_K \ln(D_1/D_2)$ | kWh/ton |
| Bond | $E = W_i (1/\sqrt{P} - 1/\sqrt{F})$ | kWh/ton |
| Crushing efficiency | $\eta_c = \sigma \Delta S / E_a$ | – |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Sauter Mean Diameter

The **Sauter mean diameter** is the diameter of a sphere that has the same volume-to-surface ratio as the particle mixture.

**Step 1 — Setup**
Let mass fraction of particles in size interval $i$ be $x_i$ with mean diameter $D_i$. Total specific surface:

$$S_w = \frac{\text{Total Surface}}{\text{Total Mass}} = \frac{\sum (\text{No.}_i \times \pi D_i^2)}{\sum (\text{No.}_i \times \rho_p \frac{\pi}{6} D_i^3)}$$

**Step 2 — Express in terms of mass fraction**
The mass of fraction $i$ is $m_i = x_i M_{total}$. Number of particles in that fraction:

$$N_i = \frac{x_i M}{\rho_p \frac{\pi}{6} D_i^3} = \frac{6 x_i M}{\pi \rho_p D_i^3}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into $S_w$**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_w &= \frac{\sum \left( \frac{6 x_i M}{\pi \rho_p D_i^3} \cdot \pi D_i^2 \right)}{\sum x_i M} \\
&= \frac{\sum \dfrac{6 x_i M}{\rho_p D_i}}{\sum x_i M} \\
&= \frac{6}{\rho_p} \cdot \frac{\sum \dfrac{x_i}{D_i}}{\sum x_i}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $\sum x_i = 1$:

$$S_w = \frac{6}{\rho_p} \sum \frac{x_i}{D_i}$$

**Step 4 — Define Sauter mean**
For a single equivalent sphere: $S_w = \dfrac{6}{\rho_p D_{vs}}$.

Equating:

$$\frac{6}{\rho_p D_{vs}} = \frac{6}{\rho_p} \sum \frac{x_i}{D_i} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \boxed{D_{vs} = \frac{1}{\sum \dfrac{x_i}{D_i}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a high-yield KTU derivation; expect it in 7-mark questions.

## 3.2 Derivation: Rittinger's Law

**Step 1 — Assumption**
Energy required is proportional to **new surface area created**.

**Step 2 — For one particle of diameter $D$**
Surface $S = \pi D^2$ (or $\pi D^2$ for sphere). New surface from $D_1 \to D_2$:

$$\Delta S = \pi (D_2^2 - D_1^2)$$

**Step 3 — Energy ∝ ΔS**
$E = K_R (S_2 - S_1) = K_R \pi (D_2^2 - D_1^2)$

**Step 4 — Factor for simplification**
Factoring $\pi (D_1 + D_2)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= K_R \pi (D_1 + D_2)(D_2 - D_1) \\
&= K_R' (D_1 + D_2)(D_2 - D_1) \quad \text{where } K_R' = K_R \pi
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Rittinger equation for spherical particles** used when $D_1, D_2$ are explicitly given.

**Step 5 — General form using $S \propto 1/D$**
For unit mass: $S = \dfrac{6}{\rho_p D}$, so:

$$E = \frac{K_R}{\rho_p} \left(\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Don't confuse $K_R$ (with $\pi$ embedded) and $K_R/\rho_p$ (specific). Always check which one is given in the problem data.

## 3.3 Derivation: Bond's Law (from Kick + Rittinger)

**Step 1 — Bond's observation**
Energy is proportional to **square root of new surface per unit volume**, or equivalently the **work to break a unit cube of material**.

**Step 2 — Differential form**
For an infinitesimal reduction $D \to D - dD$:

$$dE = -\frac{2 K_B}{D^{3/2}} dD$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $D_1$ to $D_2$**

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \int_{D_2}^{D_1} -\frac{2 K_B}{D^{3/2}} dD \\
&= -2K_B \left[\frac{D^{-1/2}}{-1/2}\right]_{D_2}^{D_1} \\
&= 4K_B \left[ D^{-1/2} \right]_{D_2}^{D_1} \\
&= 4K_B \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} \right)
\end{aligned}$$

Defining $W_i = 4 K_B$ (the **work index**):

$$\boxed{E = W_i \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} \right)}$$

This is **Bond's Law** as it appears in KTU problems. $W_i$ is experimentally determined; some standard values (in kWh/ton): Limestone ≈ 11.9, Granite ≈ 15.1, Iron ore ≈ 15.4, Coal ≈ 11.4.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A material is crushed from 50 mm to 10 mm using Rittinger's Law. If the same material is crushed from 50 mm to 5 mm, calculate the ratio of energy required in the second case to the first.

**Solution:**

For Rittinger: $E = K_R \left(\dfrac{1}{D_2} - \dfrac{1}{D_1}\right)$

**Case 1:** $D_1 = 50$ mm, $D_2 = 10$ mm

$$E_1 = K_R \left(\frac{1}{10} - \frac{1}{50}\right) = K_R \left(\frac{5-1}{50}\right) = K_R \cdot \frac{4}{50} = 0.08 K_R$$

**Case 2:** $D_1 = 50$ mm, $D_2 = 5$ mm

$$E_2 = K_R \left(\frac{1}{5} - \frac{1}{50}\right) = K_R \left(\frac{10-1}{50}\right) = K_R \cdot \frac{9}{50} = 0.18 K_R$$

**Ratio:**

$$\frac{E_2}{E_1} = \frac{0.18}{0.08} = 2.25$$

> **Answer:** The second crushing requires **2.25 times** the energy of the first.

## 3.5 Numerical Example: Sauter Mean & Specific Surface

> **Problem:** A powder has the following sieve analysis: 30% retained on 1.0 mm, 40% on 0.5 mm, 30% on 0.25 mm. Particle density = 2500 kg/m³. Calculate the Sauter mean diameter and mass-specific surface.

**Solution:**

| Fraction $x_i$ | $D_i$ (m) | $x_i/D_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0.30 | $1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ | 300 |
| 0.40 | $0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ | 800 |
| 0.30 | $0.25 \times 10^{-3}$ | 1200 |
| **Sum** | – | **2300** |

$$D_{vs} = \frac{1}{2300} = 4.348 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.4348 \text{ mm}$$

$$S_w = \frac{6}{\rho_p D_{vs}} = \frac{6}{2500 \times 4.348 \times 10^{-4}} = 5.52 \text{ m}^2/\text{kg}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Size Reduction Equipment Hierarchy

The following block diagram shows the functional classification of size reduction equipment, organized by feed size and reduction ratio.

```mermaid
graph TD
    F[Raw Feed Solid] --> A{Feed Size Classification}
    A -->|Coarse mm to cm| B[Coarse Crushers]
    A -->|Intermediate mm| C[Intermediate Crushers]
    A -->|Fine 50 to 500 micrometer| D[Grinders]
    A -->|Colloidal sub 50 micrometer| E[Colloidal Mills]

    B --> B1[Jaw Crusher]
    B --> B2[Gyratory Crusher]

    C --> C1[Cone Crusher]
    C --> C2[Roll Crusher]

    D --> D1[Ball Mill]
    D --> D2[Rod Mill]
    D --> D3[Hammer Mill]
    D --> D4[Attrition Mill]

    E --> E1[Colloidal Mill]
    E --> E2[Fluid Energy Mill]

    B1 --> R[Reduction Ratio 3 to 10]
    C1 --> R
    D1 --> R
    E1 --> R
```

## 4.2 Energy Flow in Size Reduction (Block Architecture)

This block-level architecture maps the input-output energy pathways during size reduction — useful for understanding efficiency calculations.

```mermaid
graph TD
    INPUT[Mechanical Energy Input Ea] --> EFF{Energy Partition}

    EFF -->|Useful Work| US[New Surface Energy sigma x Delta S]
    EFF -->|Heat Loss| HL[Heat Generation]
    EFF -->|Sound Noise| SL[Acoustic Loss]
    EFF -->|Vibration| VL[Mechanical Vibration]
    EFF -->|Deformation| DF[Plastic Deformation]

    US --> OUT[Output: Reduced Particle Size]
    HL --> OUT
    SL --> OUT
    VL --> OUT
    DF --> OUT

    EFF -.->|Eta c| EF[Crushing Efficiency Indicator]
    EF -.-> US
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic: Choice of Size Reduction Law

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q[Given Reduction Ratio D1 by D2] --> R{Reduction Ratio?}
    R -->|Greater than 6| RT[Use Rittinger's Law]
    R -->|Around 4 to 6| BD[Use Bond's Law]
    R -->|Less than 4| KC[Use Kick's Law]
    RT --> APP1[Best for fine grinding]
    BD --> APP2[General use and ball mills]
    KC --> APP3[Coarse crushing]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Sauter mean diameter. Why is it significant in chemical process calculations?**

**Model Answer:**
The Sauter mean diameter, $D_{vs}$, is defined as the diameter of a sphere having the same volume-to-surface ratio as the entire particle mixture:

$$D_{vs} = \frac{1}{\sum \left( \dfrac{x_i}{D_i} \right)}$$

**Significance:**
1. It directly relates **specific surface area** to sieve analysis data: $S_w = \dfrac{6}{\rho_p D_{vs}}$.
2. It is the most relevant mean for processes governed by **surface phenomena** — e.g., reaction kinetics, mass transfer, drying, and combustion.
3. It allows design of size reduction equipment using simple sieve tests rather than complex particle counting.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State Rittinger's Law of size reduction. For what kind of operation is it most suitable?**

**Model Answer:**
**Rittinger's Law (1867):** *The energy consumed in size reduction is proportional to the new surface area created.*

$$E = K_R (S_2 - S_1) = K_R \left( \frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1} \right)$$

**Suitability:** Most suitable for **fine grinding** operations where a large amount of new surface is created (e.g., ball milling, micronization). It is **not** suitable for coarse crushing because in that case, deformation energy dominates over surface energy.

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A quartz sample is crushed from a $D_1 = 5$ mm feed to $D_2 = 0.5$ mm product. The screen analysis of the feed and product is given below. Calculate the **work index** of the material using Bond's Law. Use density of quartz $\rho_p = 2650$ kg/m³.

| Sieve Aperture (mm) | Feed Mass % | Product Mass % |
|---|---|---|
| 5.0 | 8 | – |
| 3.0 | 32 | – |
| 2.0 | 26 | – |
| 1.0 | 18 | – |
| 0.5 | 10 | 8 |
| 0.25 | 6 | 22 |
| 0.125 | – | 30 |
| 0.075 | – | 25 |
| Pan | – | 15 |

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the working principle of a **Ball Mill**. State its advantages and limitations.

---

### **Solution to Question A(a):**

**Step 1 — Calculate 80% passing size ($D_{80}$).**
The 80% passing size is found by interpolating in the cumulative weight fraction table.

**For Feed:**
Cumulative % passing: 92% (5 mm), 60% (3 mm), 34% (2 mm), 16% (1 mm), 6% (0.5 mm), 0% (0.25 mm).
- 80% lies between 5 mm (92% passing) and 3 mm (60% passing). Interpolating:

$$D_{80,feed} = 5 - \frac{(92-80)}{(92-60)} \times (5-3) = 5 - \frac{12}{32} \times 2 = 5 - 0.75 = 4.25 \text{ mm}$$

**For Product:**
Cumulative % passing: 92% (0.5 mm), 70% (0.25 mm), 40% (0.125 mm), 15% (0.075 mm), 0% (pan).
- 80% lies between 0.5 mm (92% passing) and 0.25 mm (70% passing). Interpolating:

$$D_{80,product} = 0.5 - \frac{(92-80)}{(92-70)} \times (0.5-0.25) = 0.5 - \frac{12}{22} \times 0.25 = 0.5 - 0.1364 = 0.364 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bond's Law**

$$E = W_i \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_{80,P}}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_{80,F}}} \right)$$

Assume $E = 1$ kWh/ton for a unit-reference calculation (since work index is defined per unit energy):

$$1 = W_i \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.364}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{4.25}} \right)$$

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{0.364}} = 1.657, \quad \frac{1}{\sqrt{4.25}} = 0.485$$

$$1 = W_i (1.657 - 0.485) = 1.172 \, W_i$$

$$\boxed{W_i = 0.853 \text{ kWh/ton}}$$

(For quartz the standard literature value is ≈ 13.5–15.5 kWh/ton; the low value here indicates the assumption of 1 kWh/ton reference energy. In practice, $E$ is measured experimentally.)

**Recomputed with measured $E$:**
If, for example, $E$ measured experimentally = 12.5 kWh/ton:

$$W_i = \frac{12.5}{1.172} = 10.66 \text{ kWh/ton}$$

---

### **Solution to Question A(b):**

**Working Principle of a Ball Mill:**
A ball mill consists of a horizontal rotating cylindrical shell, partially filled with grinding media (balls) made of steel, ceramic, or flint. The material to be ground is fed through one end and discharged through the other. As the mill rotates:

1. **Cataracting action** — At low speeds, balls cascade over each other (attrition/grinding by friction).
2. **Cascading action** — At higher speeds, balls are lifted and fall, producing impact crushing.

The combined impact and attrition break the feed particles.

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    SHELL[Rotating Cylindrical Shell] --> MEDIA[Steel Balls]
    MEDIA --> IMP[Impact Action]
    MEDIA --> ATT[Attrition Action]
    IMP --> PROD[Ground Product]
    ATT --> PROD
    FEED[Feed Inlet] --> SHELL
    SHELL --> DISCH[Discharge Outlet]
```

**Advantages:**
1. Can grind a wide range of materials (wet or dry).
2. High capacity; can be operated in batch or continuous mode.
3. Produces very fine product (down to ~10 μm).
4. Low capital cost per unit capacity.

**Limitations:**
1. High energy consumption; low efficiency (often < 2%).
2. Wear of balls and liners; contamination of product.
3. Noise and vibration; not suitable for heat-sensitive materials without cooling.
4. Long residence time for fine grinding.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A 10 kg sample of limestone ($\rho_p = 2700$ kg/m³) is crushed and the following size distribution is obtained:

| Size (μm) | Mass (kg) |
|---|---|
| 1000 – 500 | 2.0 |
| 500 – 250 | 3.0 |
| 250 – 125 | 3.5 |
| 125 – 75 | 1.5 |

Calculate: **(i)** the **Sauter mean diameter** $D_{vs}$, **(ii)** the **specific surface area** $S_w$ (in m²/kg), and **(iii)** the **total surface area** of the sample.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **Rittinger's, Kick's, and Bond's Laws** of size reduction. State the working conditions under which each law is most applicable.

---

### **Solution to Question B(a):**

**Step 1 — Compute mass fractions and geometric mean diameters.**

| Interval (μm) | $D_i$ (μm) | Mass (kg) | $x_i$ | $x_i/D_i$ (1/μm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1000 – 500 | $\sqrt{1000 \times 500} = 707.1$ | 2.0 | 0.20 | $2.828 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| 500 – 250 | $\sqrt{500 \times 250} = 353.6$ | 3.0 | 0.30 | $8.485 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| 250 – 125 | $\sqrt{250 \times 125} = 176.8$ | 3.5 | 0.35 | $1.980 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 125 – 75 | $\sqrt{125 \times 75} = 96.8$ | 1.5 | 0.15 | $1.550 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| **Total** | – | 10.0 | 1.00 | $\sum = 4.661 \times 10^{-3}$ |

**(i) Sauter mean diameter:**

$$D_{vs} = \frac{1}{\sum (x_i / D_i)} = \frac{1}{4.661 \times 10^{-3} \text{ μm}^{-1}} = 214.5 \text{ μm} = 2.145 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

**(ii) Specific surface area:**

$$S_w = \frac{6}{\rho_p D_{vs}} = \frac{6}{2700 \times 2.145 \times 10^{-4}} = 10.35 \text{ m}^2/\text{kg}$$

**(iii) Total surface area of the 10 kg sample:**

$$S_{total} = S_w \times M = 10.35 \times 10 = 103.5 \text{ m}^2$$

> **Answers:** $D_{vs} = 214.5$ μm, $S_w = 10.35$ m²/kg, $S_{total} = 103.5$ m².

---

### **Solution to Question B(b): Comparison Table**

| Aspect | Rittinger's Law | Kick's Law | Bond's Law |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Statement** | $E \propto$ new surface | $E \propto \ln(D_1/D_2)$ | $E \propto (1/\sqrt{D_2} - 1/\sqrt{D_1})$ |
| **Assumption** | Energy → new surface | Energy → volume strain | Energy → new crack length |
| **Best for** | Fine grinding | Coarse crushing | Intermediate (general use) |
| **Reduction ratio** | High (> 6) | Low (< 4) | Moderate (4–6) |
| **Equipment** | Ball mill, jet mill | Jaw crusher, gyratory | Ball mill (general) |
| **Formula constant** | $K_R$ (Rittinger constant) | $K_K$ (Kick constant) | $W_i$ (Work index, kWh/ton) |
| **Year / Origin** | 1867 | 1885 | 1952 |

**Working Conditions:**
- **Rittinger:** Applicable when feed is already fine and large new surface is produced (e.g., micronization, ball milling of fines).
- **Kick:** Applicable when crushing large, brittle materials with low reduction ratio (e.g., primary jaw crusher taking run-of-mine ore to 10 cm).
- **Bond:** Most general — works for both coarse and fine; the **work index $W_i$** is widely used in design of crushing circuits.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Valuation Warning):**
> 1. **Unit trap:** $W_i$ is in kWh/**short ton** in some texts and kWh/**metric ton** in others. Always check given units. 1 short ton = 907.18 kg.
> 2. **80% passing size:** When sieve data is given, $D_1$ and $D_2$ in Bond's Law are the **80% passing sizes** ($D_{80}$), not the largest particle sizes.
> 3. **Log base:** Kick's Law uses natural log ($\ln$) or log base 10 ($\log$)? KTU expects **natural log** unless otherwise stated. Read the question!
> 4. **Geometric mean diameter:** When sieve intervals are given, use the **geometric mean** $\sqrt{D_{upper} \cdot D_{lower}}$ for $D_i$ — not the arithmetic mean. This is the most common marking trap.
> 5. **Density of mixture:** If multiple materials are given, use the **mass-averaged density**, not the density of one component.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Sauter Mean Diameter** $D_{vs} = 1 / \sum (x_i / D_i)$ — connects sieve data to specific surface.
- ✅ **Specific Surface (mass basis)** $S_w = 6 / (\rho_p D_{vs})$ — spherical assumption; multiply by surface-volume shape factor for irregular particles.
- ✅ **Number of particles** $N = 6M / (\pi \rho_p D^3)$ — single size; for mixtures, sum over fractions.
- ✅ **Rittinger** — energy ∝ new surface; best for **fine grinding**.
- ✅ **Kick** — energy ∝ $\ln(D_1/D_2)$; best for **coarse crushing**.
- ✅ **Bond** — energy ∝ $(1/\sqrt{D_2} - 1/\sqrt{D_1})$; introduces **Work Index $W_i$**; the most general and most asked.
- ✅ **80% passing size** $D_{80}$ is the standard diameter used in Bond's equation with sieve data.
- ✅ **Crushing efficiency** $\eta_c = \sigma \cdot \Delta S / E_a$ — typically 0.1–2% in real processes.
- ✅ Use **geometric mean** of sieve interval for $D_i$ in mass-fraction calculations.
- ✅ Particle density is required for specific surface area calculations — don't forget to include units conversion (μm → m).
- ✅ Reduction Ratio $R = D_1/D_2$ — high R favors Rittinger; low R favors Kick.
- ✅ Size reduction equipment hierarchy: **Crushers (Coarse) → Grinders (Fine) → Colloidal mills (Ultrafine)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** For any size reduction problem, always identify (1) the **type of law** from the data given ($W_i$, $K_R$, or $K_K$), (2) the **80% passing sizes** from sieve analysis, and (3) the **specific surface** if $D_{vs}$ is required. These three steps solve 95% of KTU problems on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Equipment Used in the Various Stages of Reductions; Laws of Crushing & Grinding Power Requirements; Belt Conveyer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Equipment Used in the Various Stages of Reductions; Laws of Crushing & Grinding Power Requirements; Belt Conveyer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Size Reduction – Formal Definition

**Size reduction** is a unit operation in chemical engineering that involves the mechanical breakdown of solid particles into smaller fragments through the application of crushing, grinding, or impact forces. The operation increases the **specific surface area** (surface area per unit mass) of the material, which is critical for enhancing rates of subsequent processes such as leaching, dissolution, heat transfer, drying, and chemical reaction.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context (PBSFT304 – Chemical Process Principles), size reduction is classified into stages based on the **feed-to-product size ratio (reduction ratio, R = D_f / D_p)** and the **mechanism of force application**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Students must be able to *classify size reduction equipment by stage*, *apply the three classical laws of crushing/grinding*, and *compute power requirements and belt conveyor capacity* — these are direct Part B (14-mark) hot-spots in University ESE.

## 1.2 Stages of Size Reduction

The size reduction is broadly divided into **four stages** based on the size of feed and product:

| Stage | Feed Size | Product Size | Typical Equipment | Reduction Ratio (R) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|
| **Primary Crushing** | 1500 mm – 200 mm | 200 mm – 50 mm | Jaw Crusher, Gyratory Crusher | 3 to 10 |
| **Secondary Crushing** | 50 mm – 5 mm | 5 mm – 1 mm | Roll Crusher, Hammer Mill | 5 to 30 |
| **Grinding (Coarse/Fine)** | 5 mm – 1 mm | 1 mm – 75 μm | Ball Mill, Rod Mill, Attrition Mill | 50 to 100 |
| **Ultra-fine Grinding** | < 75 μm | < 1 μm | Fluid Energy Mill, Colloid Mill | > 100 |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy – "Why Do We Crush?"

Imagine trying to dissolve a single large cube of sugar in your tea versus a spoonful of powdered sugar. The powder dissolves **almost instantly**, while the cube takes a long time. This is because the powder has a much higher **surface area exposed** to the tea.

> **Real-world analogy:** A large log of wood is hard to ignite. But if you chop it into small splinters (increased surface area), the fire starts quickly and burns efficiently. **Size reduction works on the same principle** — breaking solids into smaller pieces to accelerate physical and chemical processing.

Similarly, in industry, iron ore is crushed before leaching; grains are ground before extraction of oil; and coal is pulverized before combustion — all because smaller particles react, dissolve, or burn faster.

## 1.4 Mechanism of Size Reduction

The four fundamental mechanisms by which force is applied to break a solid are:

1. **Compression (Crushing)** — Force applied between two hard surfaces, suitable for **brittle materials** and **coarse sizes**.
2. **Impact (Grinding)** — Sudden dynamic force from a hammer or high-velocity particle, suitable for **tough materials**.
3. **Attrition (Rubbing)** — Particles rub against each other or a surface, suitable for **fine grinding of soft materials**.
4. **Cutting (Shearing)** — Sharp blades slice through fibrous or soft materials (e.g., knife cutters, rotary cutters).

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Reduction Ratio (R):**
> $$R = \frac{D_f}{D_p}$$
> where $D_f$ = average size of feed, $D_p$ = average size of product. This is the **single most important design parameter** in selecting a crusher.

## 1.5 Belt Conveyors – Formal Definition

A **belt conveyor** is a continuous mechanical handling device that transports bulk solids (and sometimes packaged items) horizontally or at an inclined angle, using a continuous belt that runs over two or more pulleys. It is one of the most economical and widely used methods for moving bulk materials over short to medium distances in a chemical plant.

**Key engineering metrics**:
- **Belt width (W)** in meters
- **Belt speed (v)** in m/s
- **Capacity (Q)** in tonnes/hour
- **Power (P)** in kW

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard metric used in KTU problems:** Bulk density $\rho_b$ is given in **kg/m³** or **tonnes/m³**, and throughput is usually in **tonnes/hour (TPH)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Size Reduction Equipment

### A. Primary Crushers (Coarse – 1500 mm → 50 mm)

#### 1. Jaw Crusher (Toggle Crusher)
- **Operating principle:** Compression between a fixed jaw and a swinging (toggle) jaw.
- **Feed opening:** The **gape** determines the maximum feed size.
- **Discharge opening (CSS – Closed Side Setting):** Determines the product size.
- **Types:**
  - **Blake Jaw Crusher** — Swing jaw pivoted at the top, produces coarse product.
  - **Dodge Jaw Crusher** — Swing jaw pivoted at the bottom, produces uniform product.
- **Capacity equation (Taggart):**
  $$Q = 0.6 \, N \, W \, S$$
  where $N$ = rps of swing jaw, $W$ = width of jaw, $S$ = CSS (set).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Think of a jaw crusher like a nutcracker. The nut is the rock, and the two arms of the cracker are the jaws. As the swing jaw moves toward the fixed jaw, the rock is squeezed until it breaks at its weakest point.

#### 2. Gyratory Crusher
- **Operating principle:** A conical crushing head (mantle) oscillates inside a concave bowl.
- **Continuous crushing** — unlike jaw crusher, it operates continuously because crushing happens on the entire circumference.
- **Higher capacity** than jaw crusher for the same gape.
- **Capacity (Taggart):**
  $$Q = 0.785 \, D^2 \, N \, d$$
  where $D$ = diameter of mantle, $d$ = CSS, $N$ = rps.

### B. Secondary Crushers (50 mm → 1 mm)

#### 3. Smooth Roll Crusher
- **Two counter-rotating smooth rolls** (equal speed) compress the feed.
- **Best for:** Soft, non-abrasive, friable materials (e.g., coal, limestone).
- **Maximum size reduction ratio (R_max):**
  $$R_{max} = \frac{D}{d} \approx 4$$
  where $D$ = roll diameter, $d$ = gap between rolls.

> **Intuition:** Imagine feeding paper through a rolling pin — the paper is compressed to the gap thickness. Smooth rolls act on the entire material layer, so the product is uniform in size.

#### 4. Toothed Roll Crusher
- **Rolls are studded with teeth or knobs** — used for **soft, sticky** materials (e.g., coal, clay).
- Can handle larger feeds than smooth rolls; $R_{max}$ up to 10.

#### 5. Hammer Mill
- **Hammers rotate at high speed** (1000–4000 rpm) inside a casing.
- **Impact + attrition** — the impact breaks the material; attrition polishes it.
- **Reduction ratio:** 5 to 40.
- **Best for:** Moderate hardness materials (limestone, coal, ores).
- **Power (gross):**
  $$P = 0.1 \, D^2 \, L \, N$$
  where $D$ = rotor diameter, $L$ = length, $N$ = rpm.

### C. Grinders (5 mm → 75 μm)

#### 6. Ball Mill
- **A rotating horizontal cylinder** partially filled with **grinding media** (balls of steel, ceramic, or rubber).
- Tumbling action causes impact and attrition.
- **Operating modes:**
  - **Wet grinding** (with slurry) — preferred in chemical plants; avoids dust.
  - **Dry grinding** — used when material must remain dry.
- **Critical speed $N_c$** — the speed at which centrifuging occurs and no grinding takes place:
  $$N_c = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{R}} = \frac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}} \text{ (rpm)}$$
  where $D$ = mill diameter in meters, $R = D/2$ = radius.
- **Operating speed:** typically **50–80%** of $N_c$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** A ball mill works like a clothes dryer filled with marbles. As the drum spins, the marbles rise on the wall, then cascade down under gravity. The cascading balls shatter the particles by impact and friction.

#### 7. Rod Mill
- Uses **steel rods** instead of balls — produces relatively coarse, uniform product.
- **Preferred for:** First-stage grinding; avoids over-grinding.
- Operates at lower speed (~ 50–65% of $N_c$).

#### 8. Attrition Mill
- Two **flat or slightly convex disks** rotating against each other.
- Feed is between the disks; one disk rotates, the other is stationary.
- **Best for:** Soft, fibrous materials (e.g., spices, starch).
- Produces very fine product.

#### 9. Fluid Energy Mill (Jet Mill)
- Particles are **accelerated by high-velocity fluid (air/steam)** in opposing jets.
- Particles collide and shatter by impact.
- **No moving parts** in the grinding zone.
- Produces **ultra-fine** particles (1–10 μm).
- Used in pharmaceuticals, pigments, cosmetics.

### D. Specialized Cutters

#### 10. Rotary Cutter (Knife Mill)
- **Rotating blades** cut fibrous / sheet materials.
- Used for plastics, paper, food materials.

#### 11. Colloid Mill
- Produces **colloidal suspensions** by shearing between a rotor and stator.
- Particle size: < 1 μm.

## 2.2 The Three Classical Laws of Crushing and Grinding

These laws relate the **energy required for size reduction** to the change in particle size. Each is valid in a different size range.

### A. Kick's Law (1885) — Coarse Crushing
**Assumption:** Energy required is proportional to the **ratio** of volumes (sizes) — i.e., energy per unit mass required to produce a given change is **constant** per unit mass ratio.

$$E = K_K \ln\left(\frac{D_f}{D_p}\right) = K_K \ln R$$

where:
- $E$ = energy per unit mass (kWh/tonne or J/kg)
- $K_K$ = Kick's constant (depends on material & crusher)
- $D_f, D_p$ = feed and product sizes
- $R = D_f / D_p$ = reduction ratio

**Power required:**
$$P = K_K \, m \, \ln\left(\frac{D_f}{D_p}\right)$$

> **Intuition:** Imagine crushing a 100 mm rock to 50 mm. Kick's law says the energy is the same as crushing a 50 mm rock to 25 mm — because the *ratio* is the same. This works for **coarse particles** where cracks don't initiate easily.

**Validity:** Best for **coarse crushing (R < 10)** — e.g., primary crushers.

### B. Rittinger's Law (1867) — Fine Grinding
**Assumption:** Energy required is proportional to the **new surface area created**. Since the new surface is inversely proportional to particle size, energy is proportional to $\left(\frac{1}{D_p} - \frac{1}{D_f}\right)$.

$$E = K_R \left(\frac{1}{D_p} - \frac{1}{D_f}\right)$$

where $K_R$ = Rittinger's constant.

> **Intuition:** Breaking a large candy into two pieces creates *new surface*. Breaking each of those two pieces into two creates *four* new surfaces. Rittinger says: **the more new surface you create, the more energy you need.** This dominates when the **newly created surface area is the controlling factor**, i.e., during fine grinding.

**Validity:** Best for **fine grinding (R > 100)** — e.g., ball mills, fine pulverizers.

### C. Bond's Law (1952) — Intermediate (Most Widely Used)
**Assumption:** Energy required is proportional to the **square root of the new surface area**, which leads to a formula in terms of the inverse square root of size.

$$E = K_B \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_p}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_f}}\right)$$

where $K_B$ = Bond's constant.

**Power required:**
$$P = K_B \, m \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_p}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_f}}\right)$$

> **Bond's Work Index ($W_i$):** $K_B$ is related to the **Work Index**, a fundamental material property.

**Bond's Third Theory Equation (for actual mills):**

$$W = \frac{10 \, W_i}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{10 \, W_i}{\sqrt{F}}$$

where:
- $W$ = work required (kWh/tonne)
- $W_i$ = Bond Work Index (kWh/tonne)
- $F$ = 80% passing size of feed (μm)
- $P$ = 80% passing size of product (μm)

**Alternatively, for ball mills:**
$$W = W_i \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{d_{80,p}}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{d_{80,f}}}\right) \times 1.102 \text{ (with sizes in mm)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bond's Work Index Table (selected materials — memorize these for KTU):**
>
> | Material | $W_i$ (kWh/tonne) |
> |:---|:---:|
> | Limestone | 11.5 – 13.0 |
> | Coal | 11.0 – 14.0 |
> | Iron Ore (Hematite) | 12.0 – 14.0 |
> | Cement Clinker | 13.0 – 15.0 |
> | Dolomite | 11.3 – 14.3 |
> | Sandstone | 11.5 – 14.5 |
> | Quartz | 13.5 – 16.0 |
> | Granite | 15.0 – 18.0 |

> **Intuition for Bond's Law:** Kick and Rittinger are extreme cases. Bond's law is the **engineering compromise** — it works in the middle range (most industrial grinding). The exponent on size is $-\frac{1}{2}$, between Kick's logarithm (very weak size dependence) and Rittinger's inverse (very strong size dependence).

### D. Comparison of the Three Laws

| Feature | Kick's Law | Rittinger's Law | Bond's Law |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Best for** | Coarse crushing | Fine grinding | Intermediate |
| **Size range** | > 1 cm | < 100 μm | 1 cm – 100 μm |
| **Mathematical form** | $E \propto \ln(D_f/D_p)$ | $E \propto (1/D_p - 1/D_f)$ | $E \propto (1/\sqrt{D_p} - 1/\sqrt{D_f})$ |
| **Surface area basis** | Volume change | New surface area | Geometric mean |
| **Exponent on size** | 0 (log) | −1 | −1/2 |
| **Practical use** | Rare (old) | Limited | **Most used** |

## 2.3 Power Requirements in Size Reduction

### A. General Power Equation

For a mill, the gross power is given by:
$$P_{gross} = m \cdot E$$

where $m$ = mass feed rate, $E$ = specific energy (Wh/kg or kWh/tonne).

**Net power (motor input):**
$$P_{net} = P_{gross} / \eta$$

where $\eta$ = mechanical efficiency of the drive (~ 0.7–0.9).

### B. Bond's Power for a Ball Mill

$$P = 2 \, W_i \, m \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{d_{80,p}}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{d_{80,f}}} \right)$$

(where $d_{80}$ is in μm, $P$ in kW if $m$ is in tonnes/h)

### C. Hukki's Approach (Modern View)
Hukki (1975) proposed a unified differential form:
$$dE = -C \cdot D^{-n} \cdot dD$$

where $C$ is a constant, and $n$ varies from 1 (Rittinger) at fine sizes to 0 (Kick) at coarse sizes, with Bond's law at $n = 1.5$ (between).

## 2.4 Belt Conveyors – Theoretical Background

### A. Components of a Belt Conveyor

1. **Belt** — Continuous loop of rubber, PVC, or fabric; carries the load.
2. **Idlers** — Rollers supporting the belt; **carrying idlers** (top) and **return idlers** (bottom).
3. **Pulleys** — **Drive pulley** (motor-driven) and **tail pulley**.
4. **Take-up device** — Maintains belt tension.
5. **Loading and discharge chutes.**
6. **Drive unit** — Motor + gearbox + coupling.

### B. Capacity of Belt Conveyor

$$Q = 3600 \, A \, v \, \rho_b$$

where:
- $Q$ = mass throughput (tonnes/hour) — using $\rho_b$ in **tonnes/m³**
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of the load (m²) — depends on trough angle and belt width
- $v$ = belt speed (m/s) — typically **1–2.5 m/s** for bulk solids
- $\rho_b$ = bulk density (tonnes/m³)

**Cross-sectional area of the load (troughed belt):**
- For a 3-idler trough, a typical empirical relation:
  $$A \approx 0.16 \, W^2 + 0.03 \, W$$
  (simplified for 30° trough angle, $W$ = belt width in m)
- Or the more general:
  $$A = W^2 \cdot f(\theta)$$
  where $f(\theta)$ is a function of the trough angle and surcharge angle.

**Standard simplification for KTU problems:**
$$Q = 3600 \, A \, v \, \rho_b \quad \text{(A in m}^2, v \text{ in m/s, } \rho_b \text{ in kg/m}^3 \text{ gives } Q \text{ in kg/h)}$$

$$Q_{TPH} = A \, v \, \rho_b \times 3.6 \quad \text{(with } \rho_b \text{ in kg/m}^3\text{)}$$

### C. Belt Tension and Power

**Effective tension (T_e):** The net force the belt must transmit.
$$T_e = T_1 - T_2$$
where $T_1$ = tight side tension, $T_2$ = slack side tension.

**Power at drive pulley:**
$$P = T_e \cdot v = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v$$

**Mass of material on belt (per meter length):**
$$m_{load} = A \cdot \rho_b \quad \text{(kg/m)}$$

**Total moving mass per meter:**
$$m_{total} = m_{load} + m_{belt}$$

**Effective tension (fundamental formula):**
$$T_e = (m_{load} + m_{belt}) \cdot L \cdot g \cdot f$$
where $L$ = horizontal length (for horizontal conveyor), $f$ = coefficient of friction between belt and idler (~ 0.02–0.04 for low-friction idlers, ~ 0.05 for plain idlers).

For an **inclined conveyor** at angle $\alpha$:
$$T_e = L \cdot g \cdot f \cdot (m_{load} + m_{belt}) + m_{load} \cdot g \cdot H$$
where $H = L \sin\alpha$ = vertical lift.

### D. Belt Selection

Trough angle: typically 20°, 30°, or 45°.
Belt width selection: depends on **lump size** of the material (largest particle should be ≤ 0.6 × belt width for 30° trough).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these formulas appear in 90% of KTU Part B questions on Module 4.**

| # | Formula | Variables | Units | Applicable Law / Equipment |
|:--|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $E = K_K \ln(D_f / D_p)$ | $E$ = specific energy, $D_f, D_p$ = feed/product size | kWh/tonne | **Kick's Law** (coarse crushing) |
| 2 | $E = K_R (1/D_p - 1/D_f)$ | same | kWh/tonne | **Rittinger's Law** (fine grinding) |
| 3 | $E = K_B (1/\sqrt{D_p} - 1/\sqrt{D_f})$ | same | kWh/tonne | **Bond's Law** (intermediate) |
| 4 | $W = (10 \, W_i / \sqrt{P}) - (10 \, W_i / \sqrt{F})$ | $W$ = kWh/tonne, $F, P$ = 80% passing sizes in μm | kWh/tonne | **Bond's Work Index** equation |
| 5 | $N_c = 42.3 / \sqrt{D}$ | $D$ = mill diameter | rpm | **Ball mill critical speed** |
| 6 | $R = D_f / D_p$ | sizes | – | **Reduction Ratio** |
| 7 | $R_{max} \approx D/d$ for roll crusher | $D$ = roll diam, $d$ = gap | – | **Roll crusher max ratio** |
| 8 | $Q = 0.6 \, N \, W \, S$ | $N$ = rps, $W$ = width, $S$ = set | TPH | **Taggart's** jaw crusher |
| 9 | $Q = 0.785 \, D^2 \, N \, d$ | $D$ = mantle diam, $d$ = CSS, $N$ = rps | TPH | **Taggart's** gyratory |
| 10 | $Q = 3600 \, A \, v \, \rho_b$ | $A$ = m², $v$ = m/s, $\rho_b$ = kg/m³ | kg/h | **Belt conveyor** capacity |
| 11 | $P = T_e \cdot v = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v$ | $P$ = W, $T$ = N, $v$ = m/s | W | **Belt conveyor** power |
| 12 | $T_e = (m_{load} + m_{belt}) \cdot L \cdot g \cdot f$ | $m$ = kg/m, $L$ = m, $f$ = dimensionless | N | **Belt tension** horizontal |

> **Engineering Application:** These laws are used by **Metso, FLSmidth, KHD, and Polysius** in designing crushing and grinding circuits. Belt conveyor design follows **CEMA (Conveyor Equipment Manufacturers Association)** standards — used worldwide in mining and bulk solids handling.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Kick's Law from Energy-Volume Assumption

**Starting principle (Kick, 1885):** *The energy required to crush a given mass of material is proportional to the logarithm of the ratio of initial to final size.*

**Step 1: Differential form**
The energy required for an infinitesimal size reduction $dD$ is:
$$dE = -C \cdot \frac{dD}{D}$$

where $C$ = constant, $D$ = particle size, and the negative sign indicates energy is consumed as size decreases.

**Step 2: Integrate from $D_f$ to $D_p$**
$$E = \int_{D_f}^{D_p} -C \cdot \frac{dD}{D}$$

**Step 3: Carry out integration**
$$E = -C \cdot [\ln D]_{D_f}^{D_p} = -C \cdot (\ln D_p - \ln D_f) = C \cdot \ln\left(\frac{D_f}{D_p}\right)$$

**Step 4: Define Kick's constant**
$$E = K_K \cdot \ln\left(\frac{D_f}{D_p}\right)$$

This is the **final form of Kick's Law**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Rittinger's Law from Surface Area Assumption

**Starting principle (Rittinger, 1867):** *The energy required is proportional to the newly created surface area.*

**Step 1: Surface area of a cube of side $D$**
A single particle of side $D$ has surface area $6D^2$ and volume $D^3$.

**Step 2: Number of particles per unit mass**
Consider unit mass. If density is $\rho$, the volume of material is $V = 1/\rho$. If we split it into $N$ cubes each of side $D_p$:
$$N \cdot D_p^3 = \frac{1}{\rho} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N = \frac{1}{\rho \, D_p^3}$$

**Step 3: Total new surface area per unit mass**
$$A_s = 6 \, D_p^2 \cdot N = \frac{6}{\rho \, D_p}$$

**Step 4: Change in surface area from $D_f$ to $D_p$**
$$\Delta A_s = \frac{6}{\rho} \left(\frac{1}{D_p} - \frac{1}{D_f}\right)$$

**Step 5: Apply Rittinger's assumption $E \propto \Delta A_s$**
$$E = K_R \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D_p} - \frac{1}{D_f}\right)$$

where $K_R = 6 \, K_R' / \rho$ = Rittinger's constant. **Final form of Rittinger's Law.**

## 3.3 Derivation: Bond's Law as Geometric Mean

**Bond's hypothesis:** *The energy required is proportional to the square root of the new surface area created.*

**Step 1: Following Rittinger, new surface per unit mass is proportional to $1/D$.**

**Step 2: Energy proportional to $\sqrt{\Delta A_s}$:**
$$E \propto \sqrt{\frac{1}{D_p} - \frac{1}{D_f}}$$

**Step 3: Define Bond's constant and write the final form:**
$$E = K_B \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_p}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_f}}\right)$$

**Step 4: Relating $K_B$ to Work Index $W_i$:**

Bond defined the **Work Index** as the energy (kWh/tonne) required to reduce the material from a theoretical infinite size ($D_f \to \infty$) to a size such that 80% passes 100 μm (so $D_p = 100 \,\mu m$).

If $D_f \to \infty$, $1/\sqrt{D_f} \to 0$, so:
$$W_i = K_B \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{100 \, \mu m}}$$

Solving for $K_B$ (with sizes in μm):
$$K_B = W_i \cdot \sqrt{100} = 10 \, W_i$$

**Step 5: Final Bond equation in 80% passing size form:**
$$W = \frac{10 \, W_i}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{10 \, W_i}{\sqrt{F}}$$

where $F$ and $P$ are 80% passing sizes in **μm**, $W$ is in **kWh/tonne**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on units:** In KTU problems, sizes are often in **mm**. To convert Bond's equation to mm units, multiply the right side by $\sqrt{1000}$ (since $\sqrt{1000 \, \mu m} = 1 \, mm$ when $F, P$ are in mm we need to scale). The exact numerical constants depend on the textbook — always use units consistently!

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Solving a Typical KTU Numerical

**Model Problem (Step-by-Step Solution Format):**

> **Problem:** 10 tonnes/h of limestone ($W_i = 12$ kWh/tonne) is to be ground from a feed with 80% passing 1500 μm to a product with 80% passing 75 μm. Calculate the power requirement using Bond's Law. Take mill efficiency $\eta = 0.8$.

**Step 1: Identify given data**
- $m = 10$ tonnes/h
- $W_i = 12$ kWh/tonne
- $F = 1500 \,\mu m$
- $P = 75 \,\mu m$
- $\eta = 0.8$

**Step 2: Apply Bond's equation**
$$W = \frac{10 \, W_i}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{10 \, W_i}{\sqrt{F}}$$

**Step 3: Compute each term**
$$\frac{10 \times 12}{\sqrt{75}} = \frac{120}{8.660} = 13.856 \, \text{kWh/tonne}$$

$$\frac{10 \times 12}{\sqrt{1500}} = \frac{120}{38.730} = 3.098 \, \text{kWh/tonne}$$

**Step 4: Subtract**
$$W = 13.856 - 3.098 = 10.758 \, \text{kWh/tonne}$$

**Step 5: Power input**
$$P_{gross} = m \cdot W = 10 \times 10.758 = 107.58 \, \text{kW}$$

**Step 6: Net power (motor rating)**
$$P_{net} = \frac{P_{gross}}{\eta} = \frac{107.58}{0.8} = 134.5 \, \text{kW}$$

> **Final Answer:** The ball mill requires a motor of approximately **134.5 kW**.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Belt Conveyor Calculation

> **Problem:** A troughed belt conveyor carries 250 tonnes/h of crushed limestone ($\rho_b = 1600$ kg/m³). The belt width is 1.0 m, belt speed is 1.5 m/s, the conveyor is 50 m long and horizontal, and the friction coefficient $f = 0.03$. Find the power required at the drive pulley. Belt mass = 15 kg/m. Use $A \approx 0.16 W^2 + 0.03 W$.

**Step 1: Compute cross-sectional area of load**
$$A = 0.16 \times (1.0)^2 + 0.03 \times 1.0 = 0.19 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2: Verify capacity**
$$Q = 3600 \cdot A \cdot v \cdot \rho_b = 3600 \times 0.19 \times 1.5 \times 1600$$

$$Q = 1,641,600 \, \text{kg/h} = 1641.6 \, \text{tonnes/h}$$

Hmm, this exceeds 250 TPH. **In practice, $A$ is the *maximum* cross-section at surcharge angle**; the *actual* $A$ depends on material properties. For a 250 TPH requirement, we use a smaller $W$ or set $A$ by the formula:
$$A = \frac{Q}{3600 \cdot v \cdot \rho_b} = \frac{250 \times 1000}{3600 \times 1.5 \times 1600} = 0.0289 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 3: Mass of load per meter**
$$m_{load} = A \cdot \rho_b = 0.0289 \times 1600 = 46.3 \, \text{kg/m}$$

**Step 4: Total moving mass per meter**
$$m_{total} = m_{load} + m_{belt} = 46.3 + 15 = 61.3 \, \text{kg/m}$$

**Step 5: Effective tension (horizontal conveyor)**
$$T_e = m_{total} \cdot L \cdot g \cdot f = 61.3 \times 50 \times 9.81 \times 0.03$$

$$T_e = 901.4 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 6: Power at drive pulley**
$$P = T_e \cdot v = 901.4 \times 1.5 = 1352.1 \, \text{W} = 1.35 \, \text{kW}$$

**Step 7: Motor power with $\eta = 0.85$**
$$P_{motor} = 1.35 / 0.85 = 1.59 \, \text{kW}$$

> **Final Answer:** A 1.6 kW motor is required to drive the belt conveyor.

## 3.6 Validation of Laws Against Each Other

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In practice, the same grinding job may yield **different energy predictions** from each law. Always:
>
> 1. Use **Bond's law** for design calculations (industry standard).
> 2. Use **Kick's law** for primary crushing.
> 3. Use **Rittinger's law** for fine grinding (drying, leaching feeds).
> 4. Cross-check with experimental Work Index values.

The actual energy in any real operation is closer to the **geometric mean** of the three predictions — that is precisely why Bond's law is most successful.
