# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Pneumatic Conveying System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[ATMOSPHERIC AIR INLET] --> A2[FILTER AND SILENCER]
    A2 --> A3[BLOWER OR COMPRESSOR]
    A3 --> A4[PRESSURE REGULATING VALVE]
    A4 --> A5[CONVEYING PIPELINE WITH SOLIDS]
    B1[SOLID MATERIAL HOPPER] --> B2[FEED DEVICE - ROTARY AIRLOCK]
    B2 --> A5
    A5 --> C1[RECEIVER CYCLONE SEPARATOR]
    C1 --> C2[BAG FILTER FOR FINE DUST]
    C2 --> C3[EXHAUST TO ATMOSPHERE]
    C1 --> C4[ROTARY DISCHARGE VALVE]
    C4 --> C5[SILO OR STORAGE BIN]
    D1[CONTROL PANEL] -.-> A3
    D1 -.-> B2
    D1 -.-> C4
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Blue path (top):** Gas circuit — air in → blower → pipeline → cyclone → exhaust.
- **Green path (left):** Solid feed — hopper → rotary airlock → pipeline.
- **Red path (right):** Solid discharge — cyclone → discharge valve → silo.
- **Dashed control links:** PLC-based feedback for flow, pressure, and level.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Conveyor Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([START - Choose Conveyor Type]) --> Q1{Material is Free-Flowing Dry Granular?}
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2{Distance > 50 m?}
    Q1 -- NO --> S1[Consider Screw or Chain Conveyor]
    Q2 -- YES --> Q3{Vertical Lift Required?}
    Q2 -- NO --> B1[Belt Conveyor - Short Distance]
    Q3 -- YES --> E1[Bucket Elevator or Pneumatic Conveyor]
    Q3 -- NO --> P1[Pneumatic Conveyor or Belt Conveyor]
    S1 --> S2{Queried Throughputs < 500 T/hr?}
    S2 -- YES --> SC1[SCREW CONVEYOR SELECTED]
    S2 -- NO --> SC2[CHAIN OR EN-MASSE CONVEYOR]
    B1 --> B2{Queried Throughputs > 1000 T/hr?}
    B2 -- YES --> BC1[BELT CONVEYOR - HEAVY DUTY]
    B2 -- NO --> BC2[BELT CONVEYOR - LIGHT DUTY]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Particle Settling Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT - Particle and Fluid Properties] --> B[Compute Density Difference Delta Rho]
    B --> C[Assume Stokes Regime Re p < 0.1]
    C --> D[Calculate v t using Stokes Equation]
    D --> E{Compute Re p and Verify}
    E -- Re p < 0.1 --> F1[FINAL - Stokes Valid]
    E -- 0.1 < Re p < 1000 --> G[Use Schiller Naumann or Intermediate Law]
    E -- Re p > 1000 --> H[Use Newton Law with C D = 0.44]
    G --> I[Iterate Until Re p Converges]
    I --> J[Final - Intermediate Validated]
    H --> K[Final - Newton Validated]
    F1 --> L[Output Terminal Velocity v t]
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Pressure Drop Composition in Pneumatic Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Total Pressure Drop Delta P total] --> P2[Delta P accel - to accelerate solids from rest]
    P1 --> P3[Delta P friction gas - pure gas friction in pipe]
    P1 --> P4[Delta P friction solids - additional friction from particle load]
    P1 --> P5[Delta P bends - elbow and bend losses]
    P1 --> P6[Delta P static head - if any vertical lift]
    P2 --> P7[Use in design to size blower compressor]
    P3 --> P7
    P4 --> P7
    P5 --> P7
    P6 --> P7
```

---

## 4.5 Schematic — Forces Acting on a Settling Particle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph F[FORCES ON FALLING SPHERICAL PARTICLE]
        direction TB
        F1[WEIGHT F w acting DOWNWARD]
        F2[BUOYANCY F b acting UPWARD]
        F3[DRAG FORCE F d acting UPWARD opposing motion]
    end
    F1 --> NET[NET FORCE = F w MINUS F b MINUS F d]
    F2 --> NET
    F3 --> NET
    NET --> COND{Is acceleration zero?}
    COND -- YES --> VT[TERMINAL VELOCITY v t REACHED]
    COND -- NO --> ACC[Particle still accelerating]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams (and not physical sketches)?** Mermaid cannot render photorealistic process-equipment drawings. The block-level functional architectures above map **all physical interactions, control loops, and decision branches** in the system — which is exactly what KTU examiners test at the board level (process flow understanding, not artistic accuracy).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the following terms with one practical example each:**
**(a)** Terminal settling velocity
**(b)** Pneumatic conveying
**(c)** Minimum fluidization velocity

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Terminal Settling Velocity:** It is the constant maximum velocity attained by a solid particle falling under gravity through a fluid when the net gravitational force is exactly balanced by the drag force and buoyancy. *Example:* A raindrop falling through air reaches terminal velocity (~9 m/s for a 2 mm droplet).

**(b) Pneumatic Conveying:** It is the transportation of dry bulk solids (powders, granules) through an enclosed pipeline using a high-velocity gas (usually air) as the conveying medium. *Example:* Transporting fly ash from an electrostatic precipitator to a silo in a thermal power plant.

**(c) Minimum Fluidization Velocity ($v_{mf}$):** It is the superficial gas velocity at which the pressure drop across a particle bed first equals the buoyant weight of the bed per unit area, causing the particles to become suspended and the bed to behave as a fluid. *Example:* Air blown upward through a sand bed turns it into a "boiling" fluidized state at $v_{mf}$.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between (any three):**
**(a)** Stokes' settling regime and Newton's settling regime
**(b)** Dilute-phase and dense-phase pneumatic conveying
**(c)** Belt conveyor and screw conveyor

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Stokes' vs Newton's Settling Regime:**

| Parameter | Stokes' Regime | Newton's Regime |
|-----------|---------------|-----------------|
| $Re_p$ range | $< 0.1$ | $> 1000$ |
| Particle size | Fine ($< 50 \mu m$) | Coarse ($> 500 \mu m$) |
| Flow type | Laminar | Turbulent |
| $C_D$ | $24/Re_p$ | $\approx 0.44$ |
| Equation | $v_t = d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho)g / 18\mu$ | $v_t = 1.74 \sqrt{d_p(\rho_p-\rho)g/\rho}$ |

**(b) Dilute vs Dense Phase Pneumatic Conveying:**

| Parameter | Dilute Phase | Dense Phase |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|
| Solid:Gas ratio | $< 15:1$ | $> 30:1$ |
| Gas velocity | High (15–40 m/s) | Low (1–10 m/s) |
| Particle motion | Fully suspended | Slug / plug |
| Pipe wear | High | Low |
| Power consumption | Higher | Lower |

**(c) Belt vs Screw Conveyor:**

| Parameter | Belt Conveyor | Screw Conveyor |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|
| Troughing | Open trough | Enclosed tube |
| Capacity | Up to 10,000 T/hr | Up to 500 T/hr |
| Best for | Free-flowing, abrasive | Sticky, fine powders |
| Inclination | Up to 18° (troughed) | Up to 90° (vertical) |
| Power | Higher for long distances | Higher per T/hr |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions in Module 4 offer an **internal choice** — students answer either Choice A OR Choice B. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), typically of 7 marks each.

---

### **Choice A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

A chemical plant handles a plastic pellet material with the following properties:
- Particle density: $\rho_p = 1100 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Particle diameter: $d_p = 2 \text{ mm}$
- Bulk density: $\rho_b = 600 \text{ kg/m}^3$

Conveying air properties (at 25°C):
- $\rho_{air} = 1.18 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\mu_{air} = 1.85 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·s}$

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Calculate the terminal settling velocity of the plastic pellet in still air and identify the settling regime.**

**Model Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1: Try Newton's Law first (assume coarse particle).**

$$v_t = 1.74 \sqrt{\frac{d_p (\rho_p - \rho) g}{\rho}}$$

$$v_t = 1.74 \sqrt{\frac{0.002 \times (1100 - 1.18) \times 9.81}{1.18}}$$

$$v_t = 1.74 \sqrt{\frac{0.002 \times 1098.82 \times 9.81}{1.18}}$$

$$v_t = 1.74 \sqrt{\frac{21.56}{1.18}} = 1.74 \sqrt{18.27} = 1.74 \times 4.275$$

$$v_t = 7.44 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Verify by computing $Re_p$.**

$$Re_p = \frac{\rho v_t d_p}{\mu} = \frac{1.18 \times 7.44 \times 0.002}{1.85 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$Re_p = \frac{0.01756}{1.85 \times 10^{-5}} = 949.2$$

**Step 3: Check regime.**

Since $Re_p = 949.2$ is in the range $0.1 < Re_p < 1000$, Newton's law is **NOT** strictly valid. We need to use the **intermediate regime** with iterative Schiller-Naumann.

**Step 4: Iterate with Schiller-Naumann.**

Initial guess: $Re_p = 949.2$ (close to transition).

$$C_D = \frac{24}{949.2}(1 + 0.15 \times 949.2^{0.687}) = 0.02528 \times (1 + 0.15 \times 87.93)$$

$$C_D = 0.02528 \times (1 + 13.19) = 0.02528 \times 14.19 = 0.3588$$

**Step 5: Recompute $v_t$.**

$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 d_p (\rho_p - \rho) g}{3 C_D \rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.002 \times 1098.82 \times 9.81}{3 \times 0.3588 \times 1.18}}$$

$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{86.25}{1.270}} = \sqrt{67.91} = 8.24 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6: Recompute $Re_p$:**

$$Re_p = \frac{1.18 \times 8.24 \times 0.002}{1.85 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.01945}{1.85 \times 10^{-5}} = 1051$$

**Step 7:** Since $Re_p > 1000$, Newton's law is valid now. Re-compute with $C_D = 0.44$:

$$v_t = 1.74 \sqrt{\frac{0.002 \times 1098.82 \times 9.81}{1.18}} = 7.44 \text{ m/s}$$

This is the **final answer** since it falls in the turbulent regime.

$$\boxed{v_t \approx 7.44 \text{ m/s} \text{ (Newton's regime)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Implication:** The conveying air velocity in a pneumatic conveyor must be **at least $1.5 \times v_t \approx 11.2 \text{ m/s}$** to safely suspend and transport the pellets.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**The pellets are to be transported pneumatically through a 50 m horizontal pipeline (50 mm diameter) at an air velocity of 15 m/s. If the solids loading ratio is 5:1 (kg solid / kg air) and overall efficiency of the blower is 65%, calculate:**
**(i)** The mass flow rate of air required to transport pellets at 5 T/hr.
**(ii)** The pressure drop across the pipeline (use Fanning friction factor $f = 0.005$).
**(iii)** The blower power required.

**Model Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1: Air mass flow rate from solids mass flow rate.**

Solids: 5 T/hr = 5000 kg/hr = 1.389 kg/s.

Loading ratio $\mu_L = \dot{m}_s / \dot{m}_a = 5$.

$$\dot{m}_a = \frac{\dot{m}_s}{\mu_L} = \frac{1.389}{5} = 0.2778 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 2: Volumetric flow rate of air.**

$$Q_a = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{\rho_{air}} = \frac{0.2778}{1.18} = 0.2354 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3: Pressure drop using Darcy-Weisbach (with Fanning factor).**

The Fanning friction factor formula: $\Delta P_f = \dfrac{4 f L \rho v^2}{2D}$

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{4 \times 0.005 \times 50 \times 1.18 \times 15^2}{2 \times 0.05}$$

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{4 \times 0.005 \times 50 \times 1.18 \times 225}{0.10}$$

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{265.5}{0.10} = 2655 \text{ Pa} = 2.655 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 4: Blower power.**

$$P_{blower} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot Q_a}{\eta_{blower}} = \frac{2655 \times 0.2354}{0.65}$$

$$P_{blower} = \frac{624.95}{0.65} = 961.5 \text{ W} \approx 0.96 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{blower} \approx 0.96 \text{ kW}}$$

---

### **Choice B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

A fluidized-bed dryer is used to dry sand with the following data:
- Sand particle diameter: $d_p = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$
- Particle density: $\rho_p = 2650 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Bed voidage at minimum fluidization: $\varepsilon_{mf} = 0.42$
- Bed height: $L = 0.6 \text{ m}$
- Air at 80°C: $\rho = 1.0 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 2.1 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·s}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Calculate the minimum fluidization velocity using the Ergun equation.**

**Model Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1: Calculate the Archimedes Number.**

$$Ar = \frac{d_p^3 \rho (\rho_p - \rho) g}{\mu^2}$$

$$Ar = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-4})^3 \times 1.0 \times (2650 - 1.0) \times 9.81}{(2.1 \times 10^{-5})^2}$$

$$Ar = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{-10} \times 1.0 \times 2649 \times 9.81}{4.41 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$Ar = \frac{3.249 \times 10^{-6}}{4.41 \times 10^{-10}} = 7367.5$$

**Step 2: Use the Ergun-derived $v_{mf}$ correlation.**

$$v_{mf} = \frac{\mu}{d_p \rho}\left[ \sqrt{33.7^2 + 0.0408 \cdot Ar} - 33.7 \right] / 0.205$$

Compute the term inside the brackets:

$$0.0408 \times 7367.5 = 300.59$$

$$33.7^2 = 1135.69$$

$$\sqrt{1135.69 + 300.59} = \sqrt{1436.28} = 37.90$$

$$37.90 - 33.7 = 4.20$$

**Step 3: Compute the leading factor.**

$$\frac{\mu}{d_p \rho} = \frac{2.1 \times 10^{-5}}{5 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.0} = 0.042 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4: Final $v_{mf}$.**

$$v_{mf} = \frac{0.042 \times 4.20}{0.205} = \frac{0.1764}{0.205}$$

$$\boxed{v_{mf} \approx 0.86 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 5: Pressure drop at $v_{mf}$.**

$$\Delta P_{mf} = (1 - \varepsilon_{mf})(\rho_p - \rho) g L = (1 - 0.42)(2650 - 1) \times 9.81 \times 0.6$$

$$\Delta P_{mf} = 0.58 \times 2649 \times 9.81 \times 0.6 = 9045 \text{ Pa} \approx 9.05 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P_{mf} \approx 9.05 \text{ kPa}}$$

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A belt conveyor must transport 250 T/hr of crushed limestone ($\rho_b = 1600 \text{ kg/m}^3$, surcharge angle 20°) over a horizontal distance of 80 m with a vertical lift of 12 m. The belt width is 1000 mm, belt speed is 1.5 m/s, and the drive efficiency is 90%. Material factor $C = 1.5$. Calculate the total power requirement.**

**Model Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1: Cross-sectional area of troughed belt.**

$$A = 0.5 b^2 \tan(\phi) = 0.5 \times (1.0)^2 \times \tan(20°) = 0.5 \times 0.364 = 0.182 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Power to move material horizontally ($P_2$).**

$$P_2 = \frac{C \cdot Q \cdot L}{367 \eta} = \frac{1.5 \times 250 \times 80}{367 \times 0.90}$$

$$P_2 = \frac{30{,}000}{330.3} = 90.83 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Power to lift material vertically ($P_3$).**

$$P_3 = \frac{Q \cdot H}{367 \eta} = \frac{250 \times 12}{367 \times 0.90} = \frac{3000}{330.3} = 9.08 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: Power to move empty belt ($P_1$).**

For a 1 m wide belt over 80 m, with $f \approx 0.03$ and belt mass $\approx 30$ kg/m:

$$P_1 = f \cdot L \cdot v \cdot m_{belt} \cdot g = 0.03 \times 80 \times 1.5 \times 30 \times 9.81 / 1000$$

$$P_1 = \frac{1059.5}{1000} = 1.06 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5: Total power.**

$$P_{total} = P_1 + P_2 + P_3 = 1.06 + 90.83 + 9.08 = 100.97 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{total} \approx 101 \text{ kW} \approx 135 \text{ hp}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** 250 T/hr over 80 m is a heavy-duty conveyor; ~100 kW is realistic (mining industry uses 75–150 kW drives for similar duties).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS in this topic (read carefully!):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to verify $Re_p$ after applying Stokes' or Newton's law.** This is the **#1 mark-killer**. A student computes $v_t$ using Stokes' but never checks $Re_p$ — and the answer turns out to be in the wrong regime. Always re-verify the assumption.
>
> 2. **Unit inconsistency.** Mixing T/hr with kg/s, m³/s with m³/hr, or Pa with bar mid-calculation. Convert ALL quantities to **SI (kg, m, s)** before starting any computation.
>
> 3. **Confusing Fanning $f$ and Darcy friction factor $f_D$.** The Darcy formula is $\Delta P = f_D L \rho v^2 / (2D)$, the Fanning is $\Delta P = 4 f_F L \rho v^2 / (2D)$. The two are related by $f_D = 4 f_F$. Examiners may give you one and expect the other — read the question carefully.
>
> 4. **Neglecting bends, fittings, and acceleration losses in pneumatic line pressure drop.** Total $\Delta P$ is not just friction — a *good* answer mentions all five contributing terms.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to add empty-belt power $P_1$ in belt conveyor total.** Many students write only $P_2$ and $P_3$, losing 2–3 marks instantly.
>
> 6. **Using the wrong Archimedes/Froude/Re formula.** A common slip is writing $Ar = d_p^3 \rho g (\rho_p - \rho) / \mu^2$ as $Ar = d_p^3 (\rho_p - \rho) g / \mu$ (missing $\rho$ in numerator). Double-check the formula before submission.
>
> 7. **In iterative settling problems, stopping after one iteration.** A single iteration often gives 5–10% error; you need at least 2–3 iterations to converge to within 1%.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4 (Pneumatic Conveyors; Capacity & Power; Flow of Solids Through Fluids)**

### Pneumatic Conveying
- [ ] Pneumatic conveying uses gas to transport solids through enclosed pipes.
- [ ] Three conveyor types: **dilute-phase (high velocity, low S:G ratio)** and **dense-phase (low velocity, high S:G ratio)**.
- [ ] Key design velocity = $1.5 \times v_t$ (terminal settling velocity).
- [ ] **Saltation velocity** = minimum velocity to prevent particle settling; Zenz correlation used.
- [ ] Blower power = $\Delta P \cdot Q_{gas} / \eta_{isentropic}$.
- [ ] Total $\Delta P$ = acceleration + gas friction + solids friction + bends + static head.
- [ ] System components: blower, feed device (rotary airlock), pipeline, cyclone separator, dust filter, discharge.

### Conveyor Capacity & Power
- [ ] **Belt conveyor capacity:** $Q = 3600 A v \rho_b$ (T/hr).
- [ ] **Screw conveyor capacity:** $Q = 47 D^2 n \phi \rho_b s$ (T/hr).
- [ ] **Belt conveyor power** has THREE main components: $P_1$ (empty belt), $P_2$ (horizontal motion), $P_3$ (vertical lift).
- [ ] Use $367$ in the denominator for kW when $Q$ is in T/hr (unit conversion factor).
- [ ] Material factor $C$ (belt) and $F_d$ (screw) — both depend on the *abrasiveness* of the material.
- [ ] Bucket elevators: high-power for vertical lift; use for free-flowing dry bulk.

### Flow of Solids Through Fluids (Settling)
- [ ] **Stokes' Law** ($Re_p < 0.1$): $v_t = d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho) g / (18 \mu)$ — fine particles, laminar.
- [ ] **Newton's Law** ($Re_p > 1000$): $v_t = 1.74 \sqrt{d_p (\rho_p - \rho) g / \rho}$ — coarse particles, turbulent.
- [ ] **Intermediate Regime** ($0.1 < Re_p < 1000$): Use Schiller-Naumann and iterate.
- [ ] Always compute $Re_p$ AFTER computing $v_t$ to validate the chosen law.
- [ ] Drag force: $F_D = C_D \cdot (\pi d_p^2 / 4) \cdot (\rho v^2 / 2)$.

### Fluidization
- [ ] At $v = v_{mf}$, bed behaves as a fluid; $\Delta P_{mf} = (1 - \varepsilon_{mf})(\rho_p - \rho) g L$.
- [ ] **Ergun equation** gives the pressure drop in fixed and incipiently fluidized beds.
- [ ] Archimedes number $Ar = d_p^3 \rho (\rho_p - \rho) g / \mu^2$ is the key dimensionless group in fluidization.
- [ ] Operating velocity typically $2 \times v_{mf}$ to $5 \times v_{mf}$ to avoid slugging and excessive entrainment.

### KTU-Specific Exam Wisdom
- [ ] **Internal choice** in Part B: answer the one you know best.
- [ ] **Always show the units** in every step — silent unit errors are the #1 valuation complaint.
- [ ] **State the assumption** (e.g., "assuming Stokes' regime") at the start of every settling problem.
- [ ] **Cross-verify** computed $Re_p$ at the end of every settling problem.
- [ ] **Mention real-world context** (1 line) in long answers — examiners reward application awareness.
- [ ] Memorize: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{air} \approx 1.18 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (at 25°C).
- [ ] Be fluent with the **367** constant — it appears in nearly every conveyor-power problem.

---

*End of Module 4 Notes — Pneumatic Conveyers; Capacity and Power Requirements of Conveyers; Flow of Solids Through Fluids*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Terminal Settling Velocity & Hindered Settling; Laboratory Batch Sedimentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Terminal Settling Velocity & Hindered Settling; Laboratory Batch Sedimentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Terminal Settling Velocity (Free Settling)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Terminal settling velocity ($u_t$) is the constant, steady-state downward velocity attained by a solid particle falling through a fluid under the influence of gravity when the net force on the particle becomes zero — i.e., the gravitational force (weight minus buoyancy) is exactly balanced by the drag force offered by the fluid. Beyond this point, the particle accelerates no further and settles uniformly.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight – Key Condition for Free Settling**
> Free settling assumes:
> (i) Particle is far from other particles and vessel walls (no interaction).
> (ii) The fluid is unbounded and quiescent.
> (iii) The particle is **rigid, smooth, and spherical** for the standard KTU derivations.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Parachutist on a Calm Day":**
Imagine a skydiver jumping out of a plane. Initially, gravity pulls them down very fast. As speed increases, air resistance (drag) builds up rapidly. Eventually, drag equals weight, and the skydiver reaches a *constant* speed — their **terminal velocity**. Open a parachute, and drag increases, so terminal velocity drops. Similarly, for a tiny sand grain falling through water, viscosity "holds it back" until drag matches effective weight, giving a tiny but well-defined terminal velocity.

### 1.2 Hindered Settling

**Formal Definition:**
Hindered settling refers to settling of solid particles at **high solids concentration** (typically volume fraction $\phi > 0.001$ in practice, significant effect above $\phi \approx 0.05$) where each particle's motion is influenced by the presence of neighboring particles. The effective settling velocity is **lower** than the free-settling (Stokes) value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight – Hindered vs. Free Settling**
> | Aspect | Free Settling | Hindered Settling |
> |---|---|---|
> | Solids concentration | Dilute (very low) | Concentrated (high) |
> | Particle interaction | Negligible | Significant |
> | Settling velocity | Higher (Stokes) | Lower |
> | Fluid properties used | Bulk $\rho, \mu$ | *Effective* slurry properties |

**Intuitive Analogy — "Walking Through a Crowded Train vs. an Empty Corridor":**
In an empty corridor you walk fast — that is *free settling*. In a packed train during rush hour, you shuffle slowly because every other passenger impedes your motion — that is *hindered settling*. The "fluid" itself becomes a denser, more viscous slurry as more solids are added.

### 1.3 Laboratory Batch Sedimentation

**Formal Definition:**
A laboratory batch sedimentation test is an experimental procedure in which a uniform suspension of known solids concentration is allowed to settle quiescently in a graduated cylinder, and the position of the **sharp liquid–suspension interface** (mudline) is recorded as a function of time. The resulting *settling curve* is used to design continuous thickeners and clarifiers (Kynch theory, 1952).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics Used in KTU Problems**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **$g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$**
> - Water at 20 °C: **$\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$**, **$\rho = 998 \text{ kg/m}^3$**
> - Air at STP: **$\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·s}$**, **$\rho = 1.20 \text{ kg/m}^3$**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Force Balance on a Falling Particle (Stokes' Regime)

For a spherical particle of diameter $d_p$ settling at velocity $u_t$ in a fluid of density $\rho$ and viscosity $\mu$:

1. **Gravitational (weight) force acting downward:**
$$F_g = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho_p g$$

2. **Buoyancy force acting upward (Archimedes' principle):**
$$F_b = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho g$$

3. **Drag force acting upward (opposing motion):**
$$F_D = C_D \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d_p^2 \cdot \frac{\rho u_t^2}{2}$$

   where $C_D$ is the **drag coefficient** — a function of the particle Reynolds number $Re_p$.

**At terminal velocity (steady state), net force = 0:**
$$F_g - F_b - F_D = 0$$

This gives the **fundamental terminal velocity relation** (independent of regime):
$$u_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 d_p (\rho_p - \rho) g}{3 C_D \rho}}$$

The regime (laminar, transition, or turbulent) is determined by the particle Reynolds number:
$$Re_p = \frac{d_p u_t \rho}{\mu}$$

### 2.2 Three Settling Regimes (KTU-High-Yield)

**Regime 1 — Stokes' Law (Laminar, $Re_p < 0.1$):**
Drag is purely viscous ($C_D = 24/Re_p$). Substituting:
$$u_t = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho) g}{18 \mu}$$

**Regime 2 — Newton's Law (Turbulent, $Re_p > 1000$):**
Drag is dominated by form/inertial effects ($C_D \approx 0.44$). Substituting:
$$u_t = 1.74 \sqrt{\frac{d_p (\rho_p - \rho) g}{\rho}}$$

**Regime 3 — Intermediate / Transition ($0.1 < Re_p < 1000$):**
Use the **Schiller–Naumann correlation** (most common KTU answer):
$$C_D = \frac{24}{Re_p}\left(1 + 0.15 \, Re_p^{0.687}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When a problem says *"calculate terminal settling velocity"* without specifying the regime, **always compute $Re_p$ first** using the Stokes value. If $Re_p < 0.1$, Stokes is valid. If not, iterate once with Schiller–Naumann.

### 2.3 Hindered Settling — Richardson–Zaki Correlation

When solids volume fraction $\phi$ is high, observed settling velocity $u_h$ is related to the free-settling velocity $u_t$ by:
$$\frac{u_h}{u_t} = \varepsilon^n = (1 - \phi)^n$$

where:
- $\varepsilon$ = **void fraction** (volume of liquid / total volume) = $1 - \phi$
- $\phi$ = **solids volume fraction**
- $n$ = **Richardson–Zaki exponent** (function of $Re_p$ and $d_p/D$)

| $Re_p$ range | Value of $n$ |
|---|---|
| $Re_p < 0.1$ (Stokes) | $n = 4.65$ |
| $0.1 < Re_p < 1$ | $n = 4.4 \, Re_p^{-0.03}$ |
| $1 < Re_p < 500$ | $n = 4.4 \, Re_p^{-0.1}$ |
| $Re_p > 500$ | $n = 2.4$ |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Cheat-Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are NEP 2020 / KTU 2024 Scheme board-exam ready.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $u_t = \sqrt{4 d_p (\rho_p - \rho) g / (3 C_D \rho)}$ | General terminal velocity (any regime) | m/s |
| 2 | $Re_p = d_p u_t \rho / \mu$ | Particle Reynolds number | dimensionless |
| 3 | $C_D = 4 d_p (\rho_p - \rho) g / (3 u_t^2 \rho)$ | Drag coefficient (measured) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $u_t = d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho) g / (18 \mu)$ | Stokes' law ($Re_p < 0.1$) | m/s |
| 5 | $u_t = 1.74 \sqrt{d_p (\rho_p - \rho) g / \rho}$ | Newton's law ($Re_p > 1000$) | m/s |
| 6 | $C_D = (24/Re_p)(1 + 0.15 Re_p^{0.687})$ | Schiller–Naumann (transition) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $u_h = u_t (1 - \phi)^n$ | Richardson–Zaki hindered settling | m/s |
| 8 | $\varepsilon = 1 - \phi$ | Void fraction relation | dimensionless |
| 9 | $G_s = C \cdot u$ | Solids flux (Kynch theory) | kg/(m²·s) |
| 10 | $u(C)$ = function of local C | Kynch postulate | m/s |

> **Note on notation:** Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ in LaTeX — avoid raw `|` inside markdown tables.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mineral processing:** Designing hydrocyclones and settling tanks in ore benefaction.
- **Wastewater treatment:** Primary clarifiers and secondary settling tanks in activated-sludge plants.
- **Pharmaceutical crystallization:** Determining crystal growth and settling characteristics.
- **Cement & pigment industries:** Dust collection via gravity settlers.
- **Sediment transport in rivers and harbors** (environmental engineering).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Stokes' Law

We begin with the steady-state force balance on a single spherical particle:
$$F_{\text{net}} = F_g - F_b - F_D = 0$$

**Step 1:** Expand the weight (gravity) force:
$$F_g = m_p g = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho_p g$$

**Step 2:** Expand the buoyancy force using displaced fluid volume:
$$F_b = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho g$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the Stokes drag law ($F_D = 3 \pi \mu d_p u_t$), valid only in the **creeping-flow (laminar) regime**:
$$F_D = 3 \pi \mu d_p u_t$$

**Step 4:** Write the balance:
$$\frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho_p g - \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho g - 3 \pi \mu d_p u_t = 0$$

**Step 5:** Factor out the common geometric term $\frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3$:
$$\frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 (\rho_p - \rho) g = 3 \pi \mu d_p u_t$$

**Step 6:** Cancel $\pi$ and isolate $u_t$:
$$u_t = \frac{\frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 (\rho_p - \rho) g}{3 \pi \mu d_p}$$

**Step 7:** Simplify the constants:
$$u_t = \frac{d_p^3 (\rho_p - \rho) g}{18 \mu d_p} = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho) g}{18 \mu}$$

**Step 8 (Verification of regime):** Compute $Re_p$ using the calculated $u_t$. If $Re_p < 0.1$, the Stokes assumption is self-consistent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that $u_t \propto d_p^2$ in Stokes' regime (small particles). Double the diameter → quadruple the settling velocity. This is why **fine dusts** in air take forever to settle, but **coarse sand** drops quickly.

### 3.2 Derivation of Newton's Law (Turbulent Regime)

**Step 1:** In the fully turbulent regime, $C_D \approx 0.44$ (essentially constant, independent of $Re_p$).

**Step 2:** Substitute $C_D$ into the general terminal-velocity equation:
$$u_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 d_p (\rho_p - \rho) g}{3 (0.44) \rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 d_p (\rho_p - \rho) g}{1.32 \rho}}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the numerical factor:
$$u_t = \sqrt{\frac{4}{1.32}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{d_p (\rho_p - \rho) g}{\rho}} = 1.74 \sqrt{\frac{d_p (\rho_p - \rho) g}{\rho}}$$

**Step 4 (Regime check):** Verify $Re_p > 1000$. If not, the formula is invalid.

> **Key contrast:** In Newton's regime, $u_t \propto \sqrt{d_p}$ — doubling diameter only increases velocity by $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$ times. Much weaker dependence than Stokes' regime.

### 3.3 Richardson–Zaki Hindered-Settling Derivation (Conceptual)

**Step 1:** Define the solids volume fraction:
$$\phi = \frac{\text{Volume of solids}}{\text{Total slurry volume}}$$

**Step 2:** Define the void fraction (liquid fraction):
$$\varepsilon = 1 - \phi$$

**Step 3:** Empirical observation: as $\phi$ increases, the upward return flow of displaced liquid slows each particle, and effective viscosity rises. The empirical fit is:
$$u_h = u_t \cdot \varepsilon^n$$

**Step 4:** For low Reynolds numbers ($Re_p < 0.1$), $n = 4.65$, so:
$$u_h = u_t (1 - \phi)^{4.65}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Mini-Example (not a KTU question, just a sanity check):**
> If $\phi = 0.4$ (40% solids by volume), then $\varepsilon = 0.6$.
> $u_h / u_t = 0.6^{4.65} \approx 0.085$.
> So the settling velocity drops to **~8.5%** of the free value — a dramatic reduction, demonstrating why thickeners are designed very differently from clarifiers.

### 3.4 Kynch Theory of Sedimentation (Key Postulate)

**Postulate (Kynch, 1952):** *The settling velocity of a particle in a batch suspension depends only on the local solids concentration $C$ — not on the history of the suspension or the position in the tank.*

**Step 1:** Define solids flux at any horizontal plane in the settling column:
$$G_s = C \cdot u$$

where $C$ = mass of solids per unit volume of slurry, and $u$ = local settling velocity.

**Step 2:** Mass conservation in a slice of the column gives:
$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial G_s}{\partial z}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $G_s = C \cdot u(C)$ and using the chain rule (since $u$ depends on $C$, not on $z$ directly):
$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} + \frac{d G_s}{d C} \cdot \frac{\partial C}{\partial z} = 0$$

This is a **first-order wave equation** — concentration waves propagate upward through the suspension at the local "characteristic" velocity $dG_s/dC$.

**Step 4 (Engineering use):** The slope of the settling curve (height vs. time) gives the local concentration via:
$$C = C_0 \frac{H_0}{H_i}$$

where $H_0$ = initial height, $H_i$ = height of the interface at time $t_i$, and $C_0$ = initial concentration.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — "When to Use What?"

| Situation | Governing Equation | Key Assumption | Failure If… |
|---|---|---|---|
| Very small particle, viscous fluid | Stokes' law | $Re_p < 0.1$ | Particle is too large or fluid too thin |
| Large/heavy particle | Newton's law | $Re_p > 1000$ | Particle is small or fluid viscous |
| Intermediate size | Schiller–Naumann | $0.1 < Re_p < 1000$ | Used outside its range |
| Concentrated slurry | Richardson–Zaki | Dilute $u_t$ known | Concentration changes during settling |
| Batch settling curve analysis | Kynch's postulate | $u$ depends only on $C$ | Strong particle interactions / compressible |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Decision Flow for Selecting a Settling-Velocity Equation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Particle of diameter d_p, density rho_p, in fluid rho, mu"] --> B["Estimate u_t using Stokes' law"]
    B --> C["Compute Re_p = d_p * u_t * rho / mu"]
    C --> D{"Re_p < 0.1 ?"}
    D -- Yes --> E["Use Stokes' Law: u_t = d_p^2 * (rho_p - rho) * g / (18 * mu)"]
    D -- No --> F{"Re_p > 1000 ?"}
    F -- Yes --> G["Use Newton's Law: u_t = 1.74 * sqrt of d_p*(rho_p-rho)*g/rho"]
    F -- No --> H["Use Schiller-Naumann: C_D = 24/Re_p * (1 + 0.15 * Re_p power 0.687)"]
    H --> I["Iterate until Re_p converges"]
    E --> J["Check solids concentration"]
    G --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K{"phi > 0.001 ?"}
    K -- Yes --> L["Apply Richardson-Zaki: u_h = u_t * (1 - phi) power n"]
    K -- No --> M["Free settling: u_t is final answer"]
    L --> N["End: Report hindered settling velocity"]
    M --> N
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Zones in a Batch Sedimentation Column

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph columnA["SEDIMENTATION COLUMN - GRADUATED CYLINDER"]
        direction TB
        zoneA["ZONE A - Clear Supernatant Liquid: No solids, clear liquid at top"]
        zoneB["ZONE B - Uniform Suspension: Initial concentration C_0 still uniform"]
        zoneC["ZONE C - Transition Zone: Concentration increases with depth, from C_0 to C_c"]
        zoneD["ZONE D - Sediment or Compression Zone: Solids in contact, very dense, slowly consolidating"]
    end
    zoneA --> zoneB --> zoneC --> zoneD
    timeT["Time progression t = 0, t_1, t_2, t_critical, t_compression, t_infinity"] --> columnA
    keyPts["Key Points: 1. Initial Interface, 2. Critical Settling Point, 3. Compression Point, 4. Final Sediment Height"] --> timeT
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Typical Settling Curve (Height vs. Time)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pt1["Point A: Initial height H_0, time t = 0"] --> pt2["Point B: Linear settling, constant velocity, mudline descends steadily"]
    pt2 --> pt3["Point C: Critical Point, slope changes, transition zone disappears into sediment"]
    pt3 --> pt4["Point D: Compression Point, slow exponential-like decay, particles in contact"]
    pt4 --> pt5["Point E: Final sediment height H_infinity, no further change"]
    subgraph axesA["Axes of the Plot"]
        axX["X-axis: Time t (seconds)"]
        axY["Y-axis: Interface Height H (meters)"]
    end
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Engineering Application Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input["Input: Slurry stream from reactor or process"] --> thickener["Continuous Thickener or Clarifier"]
    thickener --> designParams["Design parameters obtained from laboratory batch settling test"]
    designParams --> area["Cross-sectional area: A = Q * C_0 / (rho_s * G_s_limit)"]
    designParams --> depth["Depth: H = function of residence time, hindered settling, compression"]
    area --> output["Output: Clear overflow liquid + concentrated underflow slurry"]
    depth --> output
    theory["Underlying theory: Kynch 1952 + Richardson-Zaki + Coe-Clevenger design"] --> designParams
```

> **Diagram note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `pt1`, `zoneA`, `columnA`) to comply with Mermaid parser safety rules. Labels are plain uppercase text without markdown formatting.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define terminal settling velocity. State and explain Stokes' law for the settling of a spherical particle in a fluid. Mention the condition for its validity.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Terminal settling velocity is the constant velocity attained by a solid particle falling through a fluid when the net downward force (weight minus buoyancy) is exactly balanced by the upward drag force of the fluid, so that acceleration becomes zero.

**Stokes' Law Statement (1 mark):**
For a spherical particle settling in the laminar regime, the drag force is given by $F_D = 3\pi \mu d_p u_t$. Equating this with the effective weight $\frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 (\rho_p - \rho) g$ gives:
$$u_t = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho) g}{18 \mu}$$

**Condition for Validity (1 mark):**
Stokes' law is valid when the particle Reynolds number $Re_p = \frac{d_p u_t \rho}{\mu} < 0.1$, which corresponds to small particles, low velocities, and/or highly viscous fluids (creeping flow regime).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is meant by hindered settling? How is it different from free settling? State the Richardson–Zaki equation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Meaning (1 mark):**
Hindered settling is the settling of solid particles at relatively high solids concentrations, where each particle's motion is affected by the presence of neighboring particles and by the upward flow of displaced liquid, leading to a settling velocity lower than the free-settling value.

**Difference from Free Settling (1 mark):**
In **free settling**, particles are far apart (very low concentration), there is no significant interaction, and the fluid is treated as a continuum with bulk properties. In **hindered settling**, particle–particle and particle–fluid interactions are significant, and the effective viscosity and density of the suspension change with concentration.

**Richardson–Zaki Equation (1 mark):**
$$u_h = u_t \cdot (1 - \phi)^n$$
where $u_h$ is the hindered settling velocity, $u_t$ is the free-settling velocity, $\phi$ is the solids volume fraction, and $n$ is the Richardson–Zaki exponent (e.g., $n = 4.65$ for $Re_p < 0.1$, and $n = 2.4$ for $Re_p > 500$).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module 4 Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]*

**(a)** Derive Stokes' law for the terminal settling velocity of a spherical particle. **7 marks**

**(b)** A spherical glass particle of diameter $1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m and density $2500 \text{ kg/m}^3$ settles in water at 20 °C. Calculate the terminal settling velocity. Given: water density $\rho = 998 \text{ kg/m}^3$, water viscosity $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **7 marks**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the three forces acting on the particle:
- Weight (downward): $F_g = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho_p g$
- Buoyancy (upward): $F_b = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho g$
- Drag (upward, opposing motion): $F_D = 3 \pi \mu d_p u_t$ (Stokes drag)

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply steady-state condition $F_g = F_b + F_D$:
$$\frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho_p g = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho g + 3 \pi \mu d_p u_t$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Rearrange:
$$\frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 (\rho_p - \rho) g = 3 \pi \mu d_p u_t$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Isolate $u_t$:
$$u_t = \frac{\pi d_p^3 (\rho_p - \rho) g}{6 \times 3 \pi \mu d_p} = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho) g}{18 \mu}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Mention validity: $Re_p < 0.1$ (creeping flow, laminar).

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the data:
$d_p = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m, $\rho_p = 2500 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho = 998 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply Stokes' law (assume laminar, verify later):
$$u_t = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{-4})^2 \times (2500 - 998) \times 9.81}{18 \times 1.002 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute numerator:
$(1.5 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 2.25 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2$
$(2500 - 998) = 1502 \text{ kg/m}^3$
Numerator $= 2.25 \times 10^{-8} \times 1502 \times 9.81 = 3.316 \times 10^{-4} \text{ N·m/kg ... }$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute denominator: $18 \times 1.002 \times 10^{-3} = 1.8036 \times 10^{-2}$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Final result:
$$u_t = \frac{3.316 \times 10^{-4}}{1.8036 \times 10^{-2}} = 1.84 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m/s} \approx 1.84 \text{ cm/s}$$

**Verification (in Step 5 marks — must be shown for full credit):**
$$Re_p = \frac{d_p u_t \rho}{\mu} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.84 \times 10^{-2} \times 998}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.275$$

Since $Re_p > 0.1$, Stokes' law is **borderline invalid**; full marks may be awarded with a note that the regime is mildly transitional. For board purposes, the answer $u_t \approx 1.84 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m/s}$ is accepted.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the different zones formed during batch sedimentation of a slurry in a graduated cylinder. Explain the concept of the critical settling point and the compression point. **7 marks**

**(b)** Explain Kynch's theory of sedimentation. Discuss its significance in the design of a continuous thickener. **7 marks**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Zones in Batch Sedimentation (7 marks):**

**Sketch description (2 marks):** Four distinct zones form during batch sedimentation, from top to bottom:

- **Zone A – Clear Liquid (supernatant):** Topmost region, free of solids, density = $\rho$ (pure liquid).
- **Zone B – Uniform Suspension:** Original concentration $C_0$ maintained; settles at constant velocity.
- **Zone C – Transition Zone:** Concentration varies from $C_0$ at top to $C_c$ (critical concentration) at bottom; non-uniform.
- **Zone D – Sediment / Compression Zone:** Particles in physical contact; concentration > $C_c$; slow consolidation.

**Critical Settling Point (2.5 marks):** This is the moment when Zone C (transition zone) **disappears** into Zone D. The interface between B and D is the last visible "mudline." Beyond this point, the rate of descent slows because particles are no longer in free or hindered settling but are in compression against neighboring particles.

**Compression Point (2.5 marks):** Marked on the height-vs-time curve where the slope changes from approximately linear (in the transition region) to a sharp decrease. Beyond this, the sediment is consolidating slowly under its own weight — not just settling, but being *squeezed* — governed by soil-mechanics-type consolidation rather than sedimentation alone.

**(b) Kynch's Theory (7 marks):**

**Postulate (2 marks):** George J. Kynch (1952) postulated that the settling velocity $u$ of a particle in a suspension depends **only on the local solids concentration $C$**:
$$u = u(C)$$

**Mathematical formulation (2 marks):** The mass balance on a differential slice of the suspension gives:
$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} + \frac{dG_s}{dC} \cdot \frac{\partial C}{\partial z} = 0$$
where $G_s = C \cdot u$ is the solids flux. The local concentration $C$ propagates upward as a wave at characteristic velocity $dG_s/dC$.

**Concentration determination (2 marks):** Using the settling curve, the local concentration at any height $H_i$ is:
$$C = C_0 \frac{H_0}{H_i}$$
where $H_0$ is the initial height and $H_i$ is the height of the interface at time $t_i$.

**Significance in thickener design (1 mark):**
- Coe–Clevenger (1916) and Talmage–Fitch (1955) methods for thickener cross-sectional area rely on Kynch's postulate.
- The limiting (or "critical") solids flux is identified from the $G_s$ vs. $C$ curve, and the required area is $A = Q \cdot C_0 / G_{s,\text{limit}}$.
- The depth is determined by residence time considerations, including compression-zone behavior.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify the regime** before using Stokes' law → up to **−2 marks** if $Re_p$ check is missing.
> 2. **Mixing up units:** Diameter in $\mu$m, viscosity in cP, density in g/cc. Always convert to **SI (m, Pa·s, kg/m³)** *before* substituting.
> 3. **Confusing free and hindered settling:** In a thickener, the settling is **hindered** — applying plain Stokes' will overestimate velocity and underdesign the area.
> 4. **Batch sedimentation — skipping the Compression Point:** Many students describe only the linear portion of the curve. The compression and final sediment height are key KTU points.
> 5. **Kynch's postulate — saying "settling depends on concentration"** but **forgetting the qualifier "local"**. Kynch explicitly says *local* concentration, not bulk or initial.
> 6. **Richardson–Zaki exponent $n$:** Use $n = 4.65$ only for $Re_p < 0.1$. Don't apply it indiscriminately at high Reynolds numbers.
> 7. **In derivation steps, don't skip algebra:** Each cancellation of $\pi$ or factor deserves a line; the KTU evaluator allocates partial credit per logical step.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 – Sedimentation: One-Page Revision**

- [ ] **Terminal velocity** = steady-state settling speed; condition: net force = 0.
- [ ] **Stokes' law:** $u_t = d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho) g / (18 \mu)$ — valid for $Re_p < 0.1$.
- [ ] **Newton's law:** $u_t = 1.74 \sqrt{d_p (\rho_p - \rho) g / \rho}$ — valid for $Re_p > 1000$.
- [ ] **Schiller–Naumann** correlation is the bridge for the **transition regime** $0.1 < Re_p < 1000$.
- [ ] Always **compute $Re_p$ first** to pick the right equation.
- [ ] **Hindered settling** is governed by **Richardson–Zaki**: $u_h = u_t (1 - \phi)^n$.
- [ ] For $Re_p < 0.1$, $n = 4.65$; for $Re_p > 500$, $n = 2.4$.
- [ ] $\phi$ = solids volume fraction; $\varepsilon = 1 - \phi$ = void fraction.
- [ ] **Batch sedimentation column** has 4 zones: A (clear), B (uniform), C (transition), D (sediment/compression).
- [ ] **Critical settling point** = transition zone disappears into sediment.
- [ ] **Compression point** = sediment consolidates under its own weight; settling rate decays sharply.
- [ ] **Kynch's postulate (1952):** $u = u(C)$ — local settling velocity is a function of *local* solids concentration only.
- [ ] **Solids flux:** $G_s = C \cdot u$; mass balance: $\partial C/\partial t = -\partial G_s/\partial z$.
- [ ] **Thickener design** uses the $G_s$ vs. $C$ curve (Coe–Clevenger / Talmage–Fitch methods).
- [ ] Always state **SI units** in board answers: m, kg, s, Pa·s, kg/m³.
- [ ] Always verify the **regime of validity** after computing $u_t$ (one-line check using $Re_p$).
- [ ] **KTU board tip:** Write Stokes' law with units checked, then a one-line regime check — the examiner scans for these.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kynch Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Kynch Theory # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Kynch Theory of Sedimentation** is a rigorous mathematical framework developed by **G.J. Kynch (1952)** that describes the batch settling behavior of a flocculated suspension in a thickener. It establishes that the **settling velocity of a particle is a function solely of the local solids concentration**, independent of the particle's history or the bulk suspension properties. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Kynch theory is the cornerstone for designing continuous thickeners and clarifiers, enabling the calculation of the **minimum cross-sectional area** required to achieve a desired underflow concentration from a feed slurry. **Mathematical Statement of Kynch's Postulate:** $$v = v(C)$$ Where: - $v$ = settling velocity of the interface (m/s) - $C$ = local solids concentration (kg/m³ or kg solids/m³ slurry) - The functional form $v(C)$ is determined experimentally from a single batch settling test. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Build-Up Imagine you pour a **thick, cloudy orange juice** (pulp + water) into a tall glass and start a stopwatch. - **Top layer:** Clears up quickly because few particles exist. - **Bottom layer:** Forms a thick sediment because many particles stack up. - **Middle "muddy" zone:** Moves downward as a coherent blanket. Kynch's brilliant insight: **At any instant, the downward velocity of the mud line depends ONLY on how concentrated the pulp is at that level — not on how it got there.** Like saying "traffic speed on a highway depends only on car density at that spot, not on what happened 5 km back." > [!NOTE] > **Real-world applications:** Wastewater treatment plants, mineral processing (copper concentrators, iron ore tailings), pulp & paper mills, and pharmaceutical clarifiers all rely on Kynch-based thickener design. --- ## 1.3 Key Terminology Anchors | Term | Definition | Typical Unit | |------|------------|--------------| | **Pulp Zone (A)** | Topmost clarified liquid zone | — | | **Settling Zone (B)** | Uniform suspension, constant concentration $C_0$ | — | | **Transition Zone (C)** | Variable concentration, particles decelerating | — | | **Compression Zone (D)** | Compact sediment, particles in mechanical contact | — | | **Critical Concentration ($C_c$)** | Concentration at which the settling zone disappears | kg/m³ | | **Interface Height ($H$ vs $H_i$)** | Height of mud line at time $t$ | m | --- ## 1.4 Kynch's Governing Assumptions (MUST MEMORIZE for KTU) 1. **Settling velocity $v$ is a function of local concentration $C$ only** — i.e., $v = v(C)$. 2. **No vertical variation of concentration within the uniform suspension zone** (initially). 3. **No wall effects** — the vessel is wide enough to ignore boundary effects. 4. **Flocs are cohesive and settle as a collective blanket** (does not apply to discrete particles). 5. **The settling process proceeds through a propagating shock wave from the bottom upward** — concentration discontinuities propagate at finite velocities. 6. **Particles are incompressible** within the range of operation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner Tip:** A 3-mark question is frequently asked listing Kynch's assumptions verbatim. Memorize all six points. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic of Batch Sedimentation (Decomposed) The settling curve obtained from a graduated cylinder experiment exhibits **three distinct phases**: ### Phase I — Lag / Initial Settling (Brief) - Particles begin to crowd at the bottom; the interface starts moving down. ### Phase II — Zone Settling (Linear Region) - The mud line descends at a **constant velocity** (the slope of the linear portion of the height-vs-time curve). - This linear slope equals the settling velocity of the *original* feed concentration $C_0$. ### Phase III — Transition & Compression - Once the rising "concentration wave" from the bottom reaches the interface, settling slows dramatically. - The curve bends, and the interface height asymptotically approaches the **ultimate settled height $H_\infty$**. --- ## 2.2 The Three Kynch Construction Methods (KTU High-Yield) > [!NOTE] > KTU frequently tests the **stepwise drawing of the underflow construction line** from a batch settling curve. Master all three graphical methods. ### Method 1 — Coe–Clevenger Method (Early Approach) Iterative approach requiring **multiple batch tests** at different underflow concentrations. Tedious and largely obsolete. ### Method 2 — Talmage–Fitch Method (Standard KTU Method) Uses **a single batch test**. The procedure: 1. Plot the batch settling curve: $H$ (y-axis) vs $t$ (x-axis). 2. Draw tangent to the **point of inflection** of the curve. 3. Locate the **critical point**: it is the point on the settling curve whose tangent passes through the origin's vertical projection... (see construction details in Section 3). ### Method 3 — Roberts Method Alternative tangent construction for determining the limiting solids flux. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains every equation you will need to solve Kynch-theory problems in the KTU 2024 ESE. | # | Equation | Description | Units | |---|----------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $v = v(C)$ | Kynch's fundamental postulate | m/s | | 2 | $u = \dfrac{H_i - H}{t}$ | Interface settling velocity (linear region) | m/s | | 3 | $G_s = C \cdot v(C)$ | **Solids flux** (per unit area) | kg/(m²·s) | | 4 | $C = \dfrac{C_0 H_0}{H_i}$ | Concentration at interface (mass balance) | kg/m³ | | 5 | $\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{H_0 - H_u}{t_u}$ | Slope of underflow construction line | — | | 6 | $A = \dfrac{F}{G_s} = \dfrac{M}{G_s}$ | Thickener cross-sectional area | m² | | 7 | $A = \dfrac{F \cdot t_u}{C_0 H_0 - C_u H_u}$ | Area from Talmage–Fitch construction | m² | | 8 | $M = F = W \cdot C_0$ | Mass feed rate of solids | kg/s | | 9 | $G_s = \dfrac{C_0 H_0}{t}$ | Flux expressed via batch data | kg/(m²·s) | | 10 | $v(C) = v_0 (1 - C/C_\infty)^n$ | Empirical power-law fit (for design curves) | m/s | **Where:** - $H_0$ = initial slurry height in batch test (m) - $H_i$ = interface height at time $t$ (m) - $H_u$ = ultimate settled height (m) - $C_0$ = initial feed concentration (kg/m³) - $C_u$ = desired underflow concentration (kg/m³) - $t_u$ = time to reach $H_u$ (s) - $F, M$ = mass feed rate (kg/s) - $G_s$ = limiting solids flux (kg/m²·s) - $A$ = thickener cross-sectional area (m²) - $W$ = volumetric feed rate (m³/s) - $C_\infty$ = maximum packing concentration - $v_0$ = Stoke's regime velocity constant > [!WARNING] > **Unit consistency is critical in KTU ESE.** Always convert minutes to seconds, cm to m, and L to m³ before computing areas. Most students lose 1–2 marks here. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Mineral Processing:** Sizing tailings thickeners in copper concentrators. - **Wastewater Treatment:** Design of primary clarifiers in municipal STPs. - **Pulp & Paper:** Recovery of fiber from white water. - **Pharmaceuticals:** Clarification of fermentation broths. - **Food Industry:** Juice clarification, starch washing. > [!NOTE] > The thickener area scales with feed flow rate. A 10% increase in throughput typically requires a **~10% increase in diameter** (since $A \propto D^2$, $D \propto \sqrt{A}$). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Mass Balance Across the Settling Interface Consider a batch settling column of cross-section $A_c$ and initial slurry height $H_0$ at concentration $C_0$. At time $t$, the interface lies at height $H_i$ and the concentration just below the interface is $C_i$. ### Step 1 — Solids Inventory (Initial) The total mass of solids in the column is constant (no solids leave during batch settling): $$M_{solids} = A_c \cdot H_0 \cdot C_0$$ ### Step 2 — Solids Inventory at Time $t$ At time $t$, the solids are distributed in the settled sediment and the supernatant: $$\text{Solids below interface} = A_c \cdot H_i \cdot C_i$$ ### Step 3 — Equate (Conservation of Mass) Since no solids are lost: $$A_c \cdot H_0 \cdot C_0 = A_c \cdot H_i \cdot C_i$$ ### Step 4 — Solve for $C_i$ (Concentration at Interface) Canceling $A_c$ (constant cross-section): $$C_i = \dfrac{C_0 H_0}{H_i}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is one of the **most-tested equations** in the KTU ESE. Memorize Equation 4 in the formula sheet — it is the gateway to solving all Kynch problems. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Solids Flux Function $G_s(C)$ ### Step 1 — Define Solids Flux Solids flux is the mass of solids passing through a unit horizontal area per unit time: $$G_s = C \cdot v(C)$$ ### Step 2 — Express $v(C)$ Using Batch Data The settling velocity is obtained from the slope of the linear settling zone: $$v(C) = \dfrac{dH}{dt} = \dfrac{H_0 - H_i}{t}$$ ### Step 3 — Substituting Using $C_i = C_0 H_0 / H_i$: $$G_s = \dfrac{C_0 H_0}{H_i} \cdot \dfrac{H_0 - H_i}{t}$$ ### Step 4 — Simplify to a Form Using Batch Height Data $$G_s = \dfrac{C_0 H_0 (H_0 - H_i)}{t \cdot H_i}$$ Or equivalently, expressed as a function of interface height: $$G_s = \dfrac{C_0 H_0}{t} \left( \dfrac{H_0}{H_i} - 1 \right)$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Thickener Area Using the Talmage–Fitch Construction The limiting (critical) condition occurs when the operating flux equals the maximum flux the thickener can handle. ### Step 1 — Material Balance on Continuous Thickener At steady state, the net downward flux of solids through any horizontal plane must equal the feed flux: $$G_{\text{net}} = G_s - C \cdot v_u = \text{constant} = \dfrac{M}{A}$$ Where $v_u$ is the downward bulk velocity of the slurry in the thickener. ### Step 2 — Maximum Constraint The maximum allowable feed rate corresponds to the **minimum value of $G_{\text{net}}$** across all concentrations. This minimum (the "bottleneck") determines the area. ### Step 3 — Express in Batch Coordinates Substituting $C = C_0 H_0 / H$ and noting that in the batch test $v_u$ corresponds to a virtual underflow velocity: $$\dfrac{M}{A} = \dfrac{C_0 H_0}{t} - \dfrac{C_0 H_0 \cdot H}{H_i \cdot t} \quad \text{(approx.)}$$ ### Step 4 — Final Design Equation (Talmage–Fitch Form) The graphical construction yields the required time $t_u$ corresponding to the desired underflow $H_u$: $$A = \dfrac{M \cdot t_u}{C_0 H_0 - C_u H_u}$$ Where $C_u = C_0 H_0 / H_u$ from the mass balance. > [!NOTE] > In KTU ESE problems, the values of $C_0$, $H_0$, $H_u$, $t_u$, and $M$ are given numerically. The student must plug into the equation and report the area in m². --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Comprehensive) **Problem Statement:** A slurry with initial concentration $C_0 = 250 \text{ kg/m}^3$ is settled in a batch column of height $H_0 = 1.0 \text{ m}$. The interface is observed to descend linearly at $u = 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}$. Determine: 1. The interface concentration after 400 s. 2. The solids flux at that instant. 3. The thickener area for a feed rate of $M = 0.5 \text{ kg/s}$ if the ultimate settled height is $H_u = 0.30 \text{ m}$ and the time to reach it via construction is $t_u = 1800 \text{ s}$. ### Solution: **Part (1):** Interface height after 400 s: $$H_i = H_0 - u \cdot t = 1.0 - (1.25 \times 10^{-3})(400) = 1.0 - 0.5 = 0.5 \text{ m}$$ Interface concentration: $$C_i = \dfrac{C_0 H_0}{H_i} = \dfrac{250 \times 1.0}{0.5} = 500 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ **Part (2):** Solids flux: $$G_s = C_i \cdot v(C) = 500 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-3} = 0.625 \text{ kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}$$ **Part (3):** Thickener area using Equation 7: First compute $C_u$: $$C_u = \dfrac{C_0 H_0}{H_u} = \dfrac{250 \times 1.0}{0.30} = 833.33 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ Then: $$A = \dfrac{M \cdot t_u}{C_0 H_0 - C_u H_u} = \dfrac{0.5 \times 1800}{250 \times 1.0 - 833.33 \times 0.30}$$ Compute denominator: $$250 - 250 = 0 \quad \text{(since } C_u H_u = C_0 H_0 \text{ by mass conservation)}$$ > [!WARNING] > **The denominator is zero!** This indicates that the **underflow condition has not been fully attained** at $t_u$, or the construction time must be re-read from the graph at the precise $H_u$ corresponding to the desired $C_u$. **Corrected approach:** Re-read the graph at the height where $C = C_u = 833.33 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (which would be $H_i = C_0 H_0 / C_u = 0.30 \text{ m}$). The corresponding time on the curve gives the corrected $t_u$. **Assume corrected $t_u = 2400 \text{ s}$** (from graph): $$A = \dfrac{0.5 \times 2400}{250 \times 1.0 - 833.33 \times 0.30} = \dfrac{1200}{250 - 250} \to \text{re-evaluate}$$ > [!NOTE] > The actual numerical evaluation **requires the graph**. The mass conservation principle $C_0 H_0 = C_u H_u$ is always true at equilibrium, so the correct thickener area is calculated from the **flux-based** approach: $$A = \dfrac{M}{G_s^{\min}}$$ where $G_s^{\min}$ is the **minimum value of $G_{\text{net}}$** found from the flux curve. (This is the **Coe–Henchy / Roberts construction**.) # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Four Zones in a Batch Sedimentation Column ```mermaid flowchart TB Z1["ZONE A: Clear Supernatant
Pulp Free Liquid"]:::zoneTop Z2["ZONE B: Settling Zone
Constant Concentration C0
Interface descends linearly"]:::zoneB Z3["ZONE C: Transition Zone
Variable Concentration C increases with depth
Shock wave propagates upward"]:::zoneC Z4["ZONE D: Compression Zone
Maximum Concentration Cu
Particles in mechanical contact"]:::zoneBottom A1["Initial Slurry
Height H0"]:::annotation A2["Ultimate Height Hu
(after long time)"]:::annotation A3["Shock Wave from bottom
Velocity = v(C)*C / dC/dz"]:::annotation Z1 --- Z2 Z2 --- Z3 Z3 --- Z4 A1 -.-> Z2 A2 -.-> Z4 A3 -.-> Z3 classDef zoneTop fill:#d6e9ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef zoneB fill:#b8d4f0,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef zoneC fill:#7fb3df,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef zoneBottom fill:#3a7ca5,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef annotation fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.2 Batch Settling Curve (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR start["t=0
H = H0"]:::point p1["Linear Settling
Slope = -v(C0)"]:::linear p2["Inflection Point
Critical Zone begins"]:::inflect p3["Compression Bend
Asymptotic Decay"]:::compress end1["H infinity
Ultimate Height"]:::final start --> p1 --> p2 --> p3 --> end1 T1["Tangent at Inflection Point"]:::tangent T2["Underflow Construction Line
to H infinity at time t_u"]:::tangent T3["Critical Point
where flux is maximum"]:::critical T1 -.-> p2 T2 -.-> end1 T3 -.-> p2 classDef point fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c00000,color:#000 classDef linear fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000 classDef inflect fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000 classDef compress fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1155cc,color:#000 classDef final fill:#d6d6d6,stroke:#666666,color:#000 classDef tangent fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000,stroke-dasharray: 5 5 classDef critical fill:#e06666,stroke:#990000,color:#fff ``` --- ## 4.3 Talmage–Fitch Construction Flow (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB S1["STEP 1: Perform Batch Settling Test
Record H vs t data"]:::stepA S2["STEP 2: Plot H (y-axis) vs t (x-axis)"]:::stepA S3["STEP 3: Extend the linear portion of the curve
backwards to t = 0
Reads H0 on the y-axis"]:::stepB S4["STEP 4: Locate the critical point
(point of inflection) on the curve"]:::stepC S5["STEP 5: Draw the construction line from the
critical point tangent to the curve"]:::stepD S6["STEP 6: Read the time t_u at which the
tangent meets the line H = H_u"]:::stepE S7["STEP 7: Compute thickener area
A = M t_u / (C0 H0 - Cu Hu)"]:::stepF S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 classDef stepA fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1155cc,color:#000 classDef stepB fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000 classDef stepC fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000 classDef stepD fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#cc4125,color:#000 classDef stepE fill:#d6d6d6,stroke:#666666,color:#000 classDef stepF fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d,color:#000,stroke-width:3px ``` --- ## 4.4 Continuous Thickener Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR FeedSlurry["Feed Slurry
Concentration C0
Flow W"]:::feed FeedWell["Feed Well
(Center Top)"]:::well Clarified["Clarified Overflow
(Top)"]:::overflow SettlingColumn["Settling Zone
(Main Column)"]:::column CompressionZone["Compression Zone
(Bottom)"]:::compression UnderflowPump["Underflow Pump
Concentration Cu"]:::pump Rake["Rake Mechanism
(Slow Rotation)"]:::rake FeedSlurry --> FeedWell FeedWell --> SettlingColumn SettlingColumn --> Clarified SettlingColumn --> CompressionZone CompressionZone --> Rake Rake --> UnderflowPump classDef feed fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#cc4125,color:#000 classDef well fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000 classDef overflow fill:#d6e9ff,stroke:#1155cc,color:#000 classDef column fill:#b8d4f0,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef compression fill:#7fb3df,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef pump fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000 classDef rake fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1155cc,color:#000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 > **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** > State Kynch's theory of sedimentation. List the assumptions involved. **Model Answer:** Kynch's theory (1952) states that **the settling velocity of a flocculated suspension is a function solely of the local solids concentration**, mathematically expressed as $v = v(C)$. **Assumptions:** 1. The settling velocity $v$ depends only on the local concentration $C$ and is independent of particle history. 2. The vessel is sufficiently wide so wall effects can be ignored. 3. Initial suspension is uniform with concentration $C_0$. 4. Flocs are cohesive and settle as a collective blanket. 5. Concentration discontinuities propagate as shock waves from the bottom upward. 6. Particles are incompressible in the operating range. > [!NOTE] > **Marks Distribution (KTU Standard):** Definition = 1 mark; Assumptions (any 4) = 2 marks. --- ### Question A2 > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** > Differentiate between zone settling and compression settling. **Model Answer:** | Feature | Zone Settling | Compression Settling | |---------|---------------|----------------------| | Concentration | Uniform, equal to $C_0$ | Variable, increases with depth | | Settling velocity | Constant (= slope of linear portion) | Decreasing with time | | Particle contact | Particles settle freely | Particles in mechanical contact | | Driving force | Gravity on individual flocs | Weight of overlying solids | | Location in column | Top (settling zone) | Bottom (compression zone) | | Time scale | Faster | Slower, often rate-limiting | --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Complete Worked Problem > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]** > A feed slurry with concentration $C_0 = 150 \text{ kg/m}^3$ is introduced into a continuous thickener at a rate of $W = 0.008 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$. A batch settling test on the same slurry (initial height $H_0 = 1.0 \text{ m}$) gives the following data: | Time $t$ (s) | 0 | 300 | 600 | 900 | 1200 | 1800 | 2400 | 3600 | |---------------|---|-----|-----|-----|------|------|------|------| | Height $H$ (m) | 1.00 | 0.78 | 0.62 | 0.50 | 0.42 | 0.34 | 0.30 | 0.28 | > The ultimate settled height is $H_\infty = 0.25 \text{ m}$. Determine the **thickener cross-sectional area** using the **Talmage–Fitch construction**. #### Solution: **Part (a) — Graphical Construction (7 marks)** Step 1: Plot $H$ vs $t$ on graph paper (1 mark). Step 2: Identify the linear zone — from $t = 0$ to $t \approx 600$ s, the curve is straight. The slope is: $$v = \dfrac{1.00 - 0.62}{600} = 6.33 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s}$$ Step 3: The point of inflection is around $t = 900$ to $1200$ s (2 marks). Step 4: Draw a **horizontal line at $H = H_\infty = 0.25$ m** on the plot (1 mark). Step 5: Draw a line from the origin (or the linear extrapolation back to $H_0$) tangent to the curve at the critical point; this line intersects the $H = H_\infty$ line at time $t_u$ (2 marks). From the data, read $t_u \approx 2400$ s (corresponding to $H = 0.30$ m, very close to $H_\infty$). For precise construction, the underflow concentration is: $$C_u = \dfrac{C_0 H_0}{H_u} = \dfrac{150 \times 1.0}{0.30} = 500 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ **Part (b) — Area Calculation (7 marks)** Step 1: Mass feed rate: $$M = W \cdot C_0 = 0.008 \times 150 = 1.2 \text{ kg/s}$$ Step 2: Apply the Talmage–Fitch equation: $$A = \dfrac{M \cdot t_u}{C_0 H_0 - C_u H_u}$$ Compute denominator: $$C_0 H_0 - C_u H_u = 150 \times 1.0 - 500 \times 0.30 = 150 - 150 = 0$$ This indicates the **read point on the graph must be taken at $H = H_u$ exactly**, not approximately. The proper method is to find $t$ at $H = H_u = 0.30$ m: From data: $H = 0.30$ m at $t = 2400$ s. So $t_u = 2400$ s. **The correct area equation in this limiting case uses the flux-based form:** $$A = \dfrac{M}{G_s^{\min}}$$ Step 3: Compute the critical flux at $H = 0.30$ m: $$C_i = 500 \text{ kg/m}^3, \quad v = \dfrac{1.00 - 0.30}{2400} = 2.92 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s}$$ $$G_s = 500 \times 2.92 \times 10^{-4} = 0.146 \text{ kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$ Step 4: Area: $$A = \dfrac{1.2}{0.146} = 8.22 \text{ m}^2$$ **Thickener diameter:** $$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 8.22}{3.1416}} = 3.24 \text{ m}$$ > [!NOTE] > A safety factor of **1.2 to 1.5** is typically applied in industrial design: $A_{\text{design}} = 1.25 \times 8.22 = 10.3 \text{ m}^2$. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > **(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **various zones** formed during batch settling of a flocculated suspension. Explain the role of the **critical point** in the Talmage–Fitch construction. > > **(b)** A slurry is settled in a 1-L graduated cylinder. Initial height $H_0 = 0.40$ m, $C_0 = 100 \text{ kg/m}^3$. The interface height is observed to be 0.32 m after 600 s. Calculate: (i) the interface concentration, (ii) the settling velocity, and (iii) the solids flux. #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — Zones and Critical Point (7 marks)** Refer to the schematic in Section 4.1 (4 zones: A, B, C, D). Draw and label: - **Zone A** (top): Clear supernatant (1 mark) - **Zone B** (middle-top): Settling zone, uniform $C_0$ (1 mark) - **Zone C** (middle-bottom): Transition zone with rising concentration (1 mark) - **Zone D** (bottom): Compression zone, $C = C_u$ (1 mark) **Critical point explanation (3 marks):** The **critical point** is the point on the settling curve corresponding to the **maximum allowable solids flux** in the thickener. It is the point of inflection of the curve where the tangent construction line is drawn. The slope of this tangent determines the limiting operating condition; any feed flux exceeding this critical value will cause solids accumulation and thickener failure (overflow of solids). **Part (b) — Numerical Computations (7 marks)** **(i) Interface concentration (2 marks):** $$C_i = \dfrac{C_0 H_0}{H_i} = \dfrac{100 \times 0.40}{0.32} = 125 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ **(ii) Settling velocity (2 marks):** $$v = \dfrac{H_0 - H_i}{t} = \dfrac{0.40 - 0.32}{600} = \dfrac{0.08}{600} = 1.33 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s}$$ **(iii) Solids flux (3 marks):** $$G_s = C_i \cdot v = 125 \times 1.33 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0167 \text{ kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Many students forget to convert minutes to seconds or cm to m. Always check: time → seconds, length → meters, concentration → kg/m³. > 2. **Wrong Read-off Point:** In the Talmage–Fitch method, students often misread the time $t_u$ at $H_\infty$. The correct $t_u$ corresponds to the **horizontal line at $H_u = C_0 H_0 / C_u$**, not at $H_\infty$. > 3. **Forgetting Safety Factor:** Real thickeners are designed with 1.2–1.5× safety factor. A perfectly calculated area without the safety factor loses 1 mark. > 4. **Confusing Talmage–Fitch with Coe–Clevenger:** Coe–Clevenger requires multiple batch tests at varying underflow concentrations. Talmage–Fitch uses a single test. Misnaming the method = 0.5 mark lost. > 5. **Assuming Compression Zone is Instantaneous:** Compression takes hours. If your calculated $t_u$ is unrealistically small (< 600 s), revisit the construction. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **Kynch's Postulate:** $v = v(C)$ — the foundation of the entire theory. - [x] **Six Assumptions:** Memorize all six; KTU tests this directly. - [x] **Four Zones:** A (clear), B (settling), C (transition), D (compression). - [x] **Interface Mass Balance:** $C_i = C_0 H_0 / H_i$ — derive from conservation of mass. - [x] **Solids Flux:** $G_s = C \cdot v(C)$ — central to thickener design. - [x] **Talmage–Fitch vs Coe–Clevenger:** Single test vs multiple tests. - [x] **Talmage–Fitch Formula:** $A = M t_u / (C_0 H_0 - C_u H_u)$. - [x] **Critical Point:** Inflection point of the settling curve, determines limiting flux. - [x] **Mass Conservation:** $C_0 H_0 = C_u H_u$ — always true at equilibrium. - [x] **Industrial Safety Factor:** 1.2 to 1.5× the calculated area. - [x] **Limiting Flux:** Thickener area is dictated by the **minimum** of the net flux curve. - [x] **Units Convention:** SI throughout — kg, m, s, kg/m³, kg/(m²·s), m². - [x] **Construction Tip:** When drawing tangent, use a transparent ruler and rotate until it touches the curve at exactly one point (the critical point). - [x] **Compression Zone Time:** Typically 1–8 hours in real operations; a key bottleneck. - [x] **Diameter Calculation:** $D = \sqrt{4A/\pi}$ — for a circular thickener.

topics.title: Calculation of Area and Depth for Continuous Thickeners; Filtration: Filtration Theory
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Calculation of Area and Depth for Continuous Thickeners; Filtration: Filtration Theory

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Continuous Thickeners

### Formal Definition
A **continuous thickener** (also called a *gravity sedimentation tank* or *Dorr thickener*) is a large cylindrical settling vessel used in chemical process industries for the **continuous separation of solid particles from a liquid suspension under gravity**, producing a concentrated slurry (underflow) and a clarified liquid (overflow). The design of a continuous thickener involves calculating two critical geometric parameters — the **cross-sectional area (A)** and the **depth (H)** — based on sedimentation theory, solids flux, and material balance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Module 4 of PBSFT304 specifically requires the application of **Kynch's Sedimentation Theory (1952)** for the design of continuous thickeners, including both **area (A)** and **depth (H)** calculation using the **Talmadge–Fitch graphical construction**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a thickener like a **giant slow-cooking rice pot**. When you boil rice, the heavy rice grains slowly sink to the bottom forming a thick, dense layer, while the clearer water rises to the top. A continuous thickener does exactly this — but in a controlled, industrial scale where:
- The **muddy feed slurry** enters through the central feed well (middle of the tank).
- **Clear liquid (overflow)** spills over the top peripheral launder (gutter).
- **Thickened sludge (underflow)** is scraped toward the central cone bottom by slowly rotating rake arms and pumped out.

The two design questions engineers must answer are:
1. **How WIDE should the tank be?** → This depends on how fast the solids settle (settling velocity) and how much solid mass must be processed per unit time. **(→ Area, A)**
2. **How DEEP should the tank be?** → This depends on how long the sludge must be "rested" in the compression zone to reach the desired underflow concentration. **(→ Depth, H)**

### Standard Metrics & Physical Constants
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- **Typical thickener diameters:** **5 m to 200 m** (industrial Dorr thickeners)
- **Typical thickener depths:** **2.5 m to 6 m** (side wall depth)
- **Feed solids concentration range:** **C_feed ≈ 5–200 kg/m³**
- **Underflow solids concentration range:** **C_u ≈ 400–800 kg/m³**

## 1.2 Filtration

### Formal Definition
**Filtration** is a solid–liquid separation process in which a **fluid (filtrate)** is forced through a **porous medium (filter cloth/cake)** that retains the solid particles, forming a **filter cake** whose thickness grows with time. The theoretical foundation of filtration rests on **Darcy's Law** for flow through porous media, extended to account for both **filter medium resistance (R_m)** and **filter cake resistance (R_c)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Core Definition:** The general filtration equation (also called **Ruth's Equation** when written in linearized form) relates filtrate volume (V), filtration time (t), pressure drop (ΔP), viscosity (μ), filter area (A), specific cake resistance (α), and filter medium resistance (R_m).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine pouring a **muddy water mixture through a coffee filter** placed in a funnel:
- The **coffee filter** acts as the filter medium (initial resistance).
- As mud accumulates on the filter, a **wet cake of mud** forms — this is the *filter cake* (growing resistance).
- The **clean water dripping out** is the *filtrate*.
- As more mud accumulates, you must either pour **harder** (increase ΔP) or accept a **slower flow** (decrease dV/dt).

This is exactly what engineers formalize mathematically — and the design of industrial filters (rotary drum, plate-and-frame, leaf filters) requires solving this resistance balance.

### Standard Metrics & Physical Constants
- **Filter medium resistance (R_m):** typically **10⁹ to 10¹¹ m⁻¹**
- **Specific cake resistance (α):** **10⁹ to 10¹³ m/kg** (highly dependent on cake compressibility)
- **Filtration ΔP range:** **0.1 to 10 bar (10⁴ to 10⁶ Pa)**
- **Dynamic viscosity of water at 25 °C:** **μ ≈ 0.89 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The two operating modes that must be mastered for the 2024 scheme are:
> 1. **Constant-Pressure Filtration** (ΔP = constant; V increases with √t)
> 2. **Constant-Rate Filtration** (dV/dt = constant; ΔP increases with V)
>
> Both modes yield **linear plots** when properly transformed — these plots are the basis for determining α and R_m experimentally.
