<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Continuous Thickener Design Theory

### 2.1.1 Kynch's Sedimentation Theory (Foundation)

Kynch (1952) postulated that the **settling velocity (v_s) of a particle depends only on the local solids concentration (C)** in a hindered-settling suspension:

$$v_s = v_s(C)$$

This is the fundamental postulate that allows us to convert settling-test data into a **solids flux** function for thickener design.

### 2.1.2 Solids Flux Definition (Mass Balance Foundation)

The **solids flux (G)** is defined as the mass of solids passing per unit area per unit time (units: **kg/(m²·s)**). It has two contributions:

| Flux Component | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Settling flux** | $G_s$ | Solids moving downward due to gravity settling | $G_s = C \cdot v_s(C)$ |
| **Bulk / Underflow flux** | $G_b$ | Solids being dragged downward by underflow withdrawal | $G_b = u \cdot C$ |
| **Total solids flux** | $G$ | Vector sum of the two | $G = C \cdot v_s + u \cdot C = C(v_s + u)$ |

Where:
- $C$ = local solids concentration (kg/m³ or kg of solids per m³ of slurry)
- $v_s$ = settling velocity at concentration C (m/s)
- $u$ = downward bulk velocity of liquid + solids (m/s) = underflow volumetric rate / cross-sectional area

### 2.1.3 Critical Concentration & Limiting Flux

For a given underflow velocity $u$, the **total flux G(C)** typically exhibits a **maximum** at some critical concentration $C^*$. This is the **limiting (critical) solids flux**, $G_L$:

$$G_L = G(C^*) \quad \text{where} \quad \frac{dG}{dC}\bigg|_{C=C^*} = 0$$

**Why does this matter?** A continuous thickener operating at steady state CANNOT process more solids per unit area than $G_L$. Therefore, the **minimum cross-sectional area** required to process a solids mass rate $\dot{m}$ is:

$$\boxed{A_{\min} = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{solids}}}{G_L}}$$

### 2.1.4 Operating Line (Talmadge–Fitch Construction)

Since the underflow concentration $C_u$ is **fixed by design**, the operating point on the flux curve is **also fixed**. The operating line on a G vs. C plot connects two points:

| Point | Coordinates | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Point 1** | $(C=0, \, G=0)$ | Origin (no solids → no flux) |
| **Point 2** | $(C=C_u, \, G=G_u = C_u \cdot u)$ | Operating point at underflow |

The **slope of this line** = underflow bulk velocity $u$. The **vertical distance** between the curve and the line = settling flux $G_s$ at concentration $C$.

### 2.1.5 Talmadge–Fitch Method for Thickener Depth

The **depth (H)** of a thickener is calculated by decomposing it into three functional zones:

| Zone | Function | Depth Contribution |
|---|---|---|
| **Clarification zone (top)** | Clear liquid above the sludge | $H_1$ (small, often neglected) |
| **Settling zone (middle)** | Free settling per Kynch's theory | $H_2$ (computed from settling data) |
| **Compression zone (bottom)** | Solids densification under rake action | $H_3$ (compression, often dominant) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Talmadge–Fitch Construction (Critical Concept for KTU):** The compression zone depth is determined by tracking a **"rising solids interface"** from the initial feed concentration down to the design underflow concentration. The depth is then:

$$H_{\text{total}} = H_1 + H_2 + H_3 \approx H_{\text{compression zone}}$$

This is calculated by graphical integration of the **interface height vs. time** settling curve, scaled by the ratio of thickener area to the batch settling area.

### 2.1.6 Material Balance Over a Thickener

For a continuous thickener at steady state:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{feed}} = \dot{m}_{\text{overflow}} + \dot{m}_{\text{underflow}}$$

In terms of volumetric and mass flow rates:

$$Q_f C_f = Q_o C_o + Q_u C_u$$

with the liquid (water) balance:

$$Q_f (1 - c_f) = Q_o (1 - c_o) + Q_u (1 - c_u)$$

where $c_i$ = mass fraction of solids in stream $i$ (sometimes called $x_i$).

---

## 2.2 Filtration Theory

### 2.2.1 Darcy's Law Foundation

Filtration theory begins with **Darcy's Law** for flow through a porous bed:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot R}$$

where $R$ is the **total resistance** (m⁻¹), comprising:
- $R_m$ = filter medium resistance (constant)
- $R_c$ = filter cake resistance (grows with time)

### 2.2.2 Filter Cake Resistance (The Key Concept)

The cake resistance is proportional to the **mass of cake deposited per unit filtrate volume (w)** and inversely related to the **filter area (A)**:

$$R_c = \alpha \cdot \frac{w \cdot V}{A}$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = **specific cake resistance** (m/kg) — a property of the cake
- $w$ = mass of dry cake solids per unit volume of filtrate (kg/m³)
- $V$ = cumulative filtrate volume (m³)
- $A$ = filter area (m²)

The total resistance becomes:

$$R = R_m + \alpha \cdot \frac{w \cdot V}{A}$$

### 2.2.3 General Filtration Equation (Ruth's Equation — Unrestricted Form)

Substituting into Darcy's law:

$$\boxed{\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A^2 \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \left( \alpha w V + A R_m \right)}}$$

This is the **fundamental differential equation of cake filtration** — it is the "mother equation" from which all filtration design formulas are derived.

### 2.2.4 Constant-Pressure Filtration (ΔP = Constant)

When ΔP is held constant and integrated from $t=0, V=0$ to $t, V$:

$$\boxed{\frac{t}{V} = \frac{\mu \alpha w}{2 A^2 \Delta P} \cdot V + \frac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P}}$$

This is a **straight line** of the form $y = mx + c$ when plotting $t/V$ vs. $V$:

| Linear Plot Component | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|
| **y-axis** | $t/V$ (s/m³) |
| **x-axis** | $V$ (m³) |
| **Slope (m)** | $\dfrac{\mu \alpha w}{2 A^2 \Delta P}$ |
| **Intercept (c)** | $\dfrac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P}$ |

From experimental data, **α** and **R_m** can be back-calculated.

### 2.2.5 Constant-Rate Filtration (dV/dt = Constant)

When the flow rate is held constant, the required ΔP must increase with time as the cake grows:

$$\boxed{\Delta P = \frac{\mu \alpha w v}{A} \cdot V + \mu R_m v}$$

where $v = dV/dt$ = constant filtrate flux (m³/s) divided by A.

Since $V = v \cdot A \cdot t$, this can be rewritten as:

$$\Delta P = \mu \alpha w v^2 \cdot t + \mu R_m v$$

This is a **straight line** when plotting ΔP vs. $V$ (or vs. $t$).

### 2.2.6 Filter Cycle Time & Optimization (Optional Advanced)

For batch filters, the **optimal cycle time** that maximizes the average filtrate rate balances:
- **Filtration time** (V increases)
- **Washing time**
- **Disassembly/cleaning time** (cake removal)

For constant-pressure operation, the **average rate** is:

$$\left(\frac{V}{t}\right)_{\text{avg}} = \frac{V}{t_f + t_w + t_d}$$

### 2.2.7 Compressible vs. Incompressible Cakes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cake Compressibility — KTU Frequently Tested:**
> 
> **Incompressible cake:** α = constant (independent of ΔP).
> 
> **Compressible cake:** α varies with ΔP, typically modeled as:
> $$\alpha = \alpha_0 (\Delta P)^s$$
> where $s$ is the **compressibility index** (0 ≤ s ≤ 1).
> - $s = 0$: completely incompressible
> - $s = 1$: highly compressible
> - Typical values: $s = 0.2$ to $0.8$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Application / Mode | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G_s = C \cdot v_s(C)$ | Settling flux definition | kg/(m²·s) |
| 2 | $G_b = u \cdot C$ | Bulk (underflow) flux | kg/(m²·s) |
| 3 | $G = C \cdot v_s + u \cdot C$ | Total solids flux | kg/(m²·s) |
| 4 | $dG/dC = 0 \Rightarrow G_L$ | Limiting flux criterion | kg/(m²·s) |
| 5 | $A_{\min} = \dot{m} / G_L$ | Minimum thickener area | m² |
| 6 | $Q_f c_f = Q_u c_u + Q_o c_o$ | Mass balance (solids) | kg/s |
| 7 | $Q_f = Q_u + Q_o$ | Volumetric balance (total) | m³/s |
| 8 | $\Delta t = \Delta h \cdot C / G$ | Talmadge–Fitch time interval | s |
| 9 | $dV/dt = A \Delta P / (\mu R)$ | Darcy's law for filtration | m³/s |
| 10 | $R = R_m + \alpha w V / A$ | Total filtration resistance | m⁻¹ |
| 11 | $t/V = (\mu \alpha w / 2A^2 \Delta P) V + \mu R_m / (A \Delta P)$ | **Constant-ΔP** linear form | s/m³ |
| 12 | $\Delta P = (\mu \alpha w v / A) V + \mu R_m v$ | **Constant-rate** linear form | Pa |
| 13 | $\alpha = \alpha_0 (\Delta P)^s$ | Compressible cake law | m/kg |
| 14 | $w = \rho_s c / (1 - mc - c)$ | Mass of cake per filtrate vol. | kg/m³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Continuous thickeners are used in **mineral processing (tailings, red mud), wastewater treatment, sugar refining, and pulp & paper** industries. Filtration is fundamental to **pharmaceutical API recovery, catalyst recovery, beer/wine clarification, lube oil processing, and polymer slurry dewatering**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Kynch's Limiting Flux Condition

### Starting Postulate
For hindered settling, Kynch assumed $v_s$ depends only on $C$:

$$v_s = f(C) \quad \text{(a monotonically decreasing function of C)}$$

### Step 1 — Express Total Flux
The total mass flux of solids downward (per unit area) is the sum of the flux carried by settling and the flux carried by the bulk downward motion:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{\text{total}} &= G_s + G_b \\
&= C \cdot v_s(C) + C \cdot u \\
&= C \cdot f(C) + u \cdot C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** $G_s$ represents solids falling under gravity (concentration times settling velocity). $G_b$ represents all material — both liquid and solids — being pulled down by the underflow pump at velocity $u$.

### Step 2 — Identify the Maximum
For a given $u$ (fixed by the underflow pump rate), the function $G(C) = C \cdot f(C) + uC$ typically has a **single maximum**. To find it, set $dG/dC = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dG}{dC} &= \frac{d}{dC}[C \cdot f(C)] + \frac{d}{dC}[u \cdot C] \\
&= f(C) + C \cdot \frac{df}{dC} + u \\
&= 0 \quad \text{(at the maximum)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This gives the **critical concentration condition**:

$$f(C^*) + C^* \cdot \left.\frac{df}{dC}\right|_{C^*} = -u$$

In graphical terms: draw a **tangent line from the operating point $(C_u, G_u)$** on the $G$ vs. $C$ curve. The point of tangency is the **critical concentration $C^*$**, and the **slope of the tangent equals $-u$** (the operating line slope).

### Step 3 — Calculate Limiting Flux
Read $G$ at $C = C^*$ from the curve. This is $G_L$.

### Step 4 — Compute Minimum Area
A thickener must have area such that the actual flux ≤ limiting flux:

$$
\boxed{A_{\min} = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{solids, feed}}}{G_L}}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Mass flow rate has units kg/s, flux has units kg/(m²·s). Division gives m² (area). The **safety factor** (typically 1.2 to 1.5) is then applied: $A_{\text{design}} = f_s \cdot A_{\min}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Ruth's Constant-Pressure Equation

### Step 1 — Start from Darcy's Law with Growing Resistance

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dV}{dt} &= \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot R} \\
R &= R_m + \alpha \cdot \frac{w \cdot V}{A} \\
\Rightarrow \frac{dV}{dt} &= \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \left( R_m + \dfrac{\alpha w V}{A} \right)} \\
&= \frac{A^2 \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \left( A R_m + \alpha w V \right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** We cross-multiplied and multiplied numerator and denominator by $A$ to consolidate terms.

### Step 2 — Separate Variables and Integrate

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left( A R_m + \alpha w V \right) dV &= \frac{A^2 \cdot \Delta P}{\mu} \cdot dt \\
\int_0^V \left( A R_m + \alpha w V \right) dV &= \frac{A^2 \cdot \Delta P}{\mu} \int_0^t dt
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Rearranged the differential form to group all $V$-terms on the left and all $t$-terms on the right.

### Step 3 — Perform the Integration

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left[ A R_m \cdot V + \frac{\alpha w V^2}{2} \right]_0^V &= \frac{A^2 \cdot \Delta P}{\mu} \cdot t \\
A R_m \cdot V + \frac{\alpha w V^2}{2} &= \frac{A^2 \cdot \Delta P}{\mu} \cdot t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The integral of a constant is the constant times the variable; the integral of $V$ is $V^2/2$.

### Step 4 — Solve for t/V (Linear Form)

Divide both sides by $V$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A R_m + \frac{\alpha w V}{2} &= \frac{A^2 \cdot \Delta P}{\mu} \cdot \frac{t}{V} \\
\frac{t}{V} &= \frac{\mu}{A^2 \cdot \Delta P} \left( A R_m + \frac{\alpha w V}{2} \right) \\
\frac{t}{V} &= \frac{\mu \alpha w}{2 A^2 \Delta P} \cdot V + \frac{\mu R_m}{A \cdot \Delta P}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Distributing the $\mu/(A^2 \Delta P)$ factor across the two terms inside the parenthesis gives the classic **linear (y = mx + c) form** of Ruth's constant-pressure filtration equation.

### Step 5 — Interpretation (Engineering Meaning)

| Term | Symbol | What it tells us |
|---|---|---|
| $\mu \alpha w / (2A^2 \Delta P)$ | $m$ (slope) | Contains the cake resistance; proportional to $1/\Delta P$ |
| $\mu R_m / (A \Delta P)$ | $c$ (intercept) | Contains only the medium resistance; inversely proportional to $A$ and $\Delta P$ |

By measuring $t/V$ at several $V$ at a fixed $\Delta P$, a **straight line plot** is obtained whose slope and intercept yield $\alpha$ and $R_m$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Constant-Rate Equation

### Starting Point (Same Darcy's Law)

For constant rate, $dV/dt = v \cdot A$ (constant), so the cake builds linearly with time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v \cdot A &= \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \left( R_m + \alpha w V / A \right)} \\
\Delta P &= \mu v \left( R_m + \frac{\alpha w V}{A} \right) \\
\Delta P &= \frac{\mu \alpha w v}{A} \cdot V + \mu R_m v
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** At constant rate, ΔP must rise to overcome the growing cake resistance. Plotting ΔP vs. V gives a **straight line** with slope $\mu \alpha w v / A$ and intercept $\mu R_m v$.

### Alternative Form in Time
Substitute $V = v \cdot A \cdot t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= \frac{\mu \alpha w v}{A} \cdot (v A t) + \mu R_m v \\
\Delta P &= \mu \alpha w v^2 \cdot t + \mu R_m v
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Continuous Thickener Area Calculation

**Given:**
- Feed flow rate: $Q_f = 100 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$
- Feed concentration: $C_f = 50 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (mass per slurry volume)
- Underflow concentration: $C_u = 250 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Settling velocity data: $v_s = k \cdot C^{-n}$ with $k = 0.5$, $n = 1.0$ (hypothetical)

**Step 1 — Compute Solids Mass Rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{solids}} &= Q_f \cdot C_f = \frac{100 \times 50}{3600} = 1.389 \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Underflow Velocity $u$**

First, compute the underflow volumetric rate from the mass balance. Using $Q_f C_f = Q_u C_u$ (neglecting solids in overflow):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_u &= \frac{Q_f C_f}{C_u} = \frac{100 \times 50}{250} = 20 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr} = 5.556 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

For an assumed thickener diameter, say $D = 10 \text{ m}$ (initial guess for area), $A = 78.54 \text{ m}^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u &= \frac{Q_u}{A} = \frac{5.556 \times 10^{-3}}{78.54} = 7.07 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Express the Total Flux**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G(C) &= C \cdot v_s(C) + u \cdot C \\
&= C \cdot (0.5 \cdot C^{-1}) + (7.07 \times 10^{-5}) C \\
&= 0.5 + 7.07 \times 10^{-5} \cdot C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Find the Limiting Flux**

For this ideal $v_s = 0.5/C$ model, $G(C)$ is **monotonically increasing** in $C$! This is a degenerate case. In realistic cases, $n > 1$ and the curve exhibits a maximum.

**Let us re-try with $n = 2$ and $k = 5$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_s(C) &= 5 \cdot C^{-2} \text{ m/s} \\
G(C) &= C \cdot 5 C^{-2} + 7.07 \times 10^{-5} C \\
&= \frac{5}{C} + 7.07 \times 10^{-5} C
\end{aligned}
$$

Differentiate and set to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dG}{dC} &= -\frac{5}{C^2} + 7.07 \times 10^{-5} = 0 \\
C^{*2} &= \frac{5}{7.07 \times 10^{-5}} = 70{,}720 \\
C^* &= 266 \text{ kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $C^* \approx C_u = 250 \text{ kg/m}^3$, the underflow is right at the limiting concentration. Compute $G_L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_L &= \frac{5}{266} + 7.07 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 266 \\
&= 0.0188 + 0.0188 = 0.0376 \text{ kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate Minimum Area**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\min} &= \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{solids}}}{G_L} = \frac{1.389}{0.0376} = 36.94 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Safety Factor (1.25)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{design}} &= 1.25 \times 36.94 = 46.18 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{design}} &= \sqrt{\frac{4 A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 46.18}{\pi}} = 7.67 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Constant-Pressure Filtration Analysis

**Given:**
- Filter area: $A = 0.1 \text{ m}^2$
- ΔP = $2 \times 10^5$ Pa
- μ (filtrate viscosity) = $10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- $w$ (cake mass per filtrate) = 25 kg/m³
- Filter medium resistance: $R_m = 1.2 \times 10^{10}$ m⁻¹

**Step 1 — Calculate the Intercept of $t/V$ vs. $V$ Plot**

$$
\begin{aligned}
c = \frac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P} &= \frac{10^{-3} \times 1.2 \times 10^{10}}{0.1 \times 2 \times 10^5} \\
&= \frac{1.2 \times 10^7}{2 \times 10^4} = 600 \text{ s/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Slope for a Given α**

If $\alpha = 10^{11}$ m/kg:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m = \frac{\mu \alpha w}{2 A^2 \Delta P} &= \frac{10^{-3} \times 10^{11} \times 25}{2 \times (0.1)^2 \times 2 \times 10^5} \\
&= \frac{2.5 \times 10^9}{4 \times 10^3} = 6.25 \times 10^5 \text{ s/m}^6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Predict Time to Collect V = 0.05 m³ of Filtrate**

Using $t/V = mV + c$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{t}{0.05} &= 6.25 \times 10^5 \times 0.05 + 600 \\
&= 3.125 \times 10^4 + 600 = 3.131 \times 10^4 \text{ s/m}^3 \\
t &= 0.05 \times 3.131 \times 10^4 = 1565.5 \text{ s} \approx 26.1 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify Resistance Contribution**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_m &= 1.2 \times 10^{10} \text{ m}^{-1} \\
R_c &= \frac{\alpha w V}{A} = \frac{10^{11} \times 25 \times 0.05}{0.1} = 1.25 \times 10^{12} \text{ m}^{-1} \\
\frac{R_c}{R_m} &= \frac{1.25 \times 10^{12}}{1.2 \times 10^{10}} = 104.2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Observation:** The cake resistance is **~100× the medium resistance** — a clear sign that the cake is the dominant resistance, which is typical in industrial filtration. The medium resistance becomes negligible early in the cycle and the cake resistance dominates.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Continuous Thickener Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    feedIn["FEED SLURRY Qf x Cf"] --> feedWell["CENTRAL FEED WELL"]
    feedWell --> clarZone["ZONE 1 CLARIFICATION ZONE Top Clear Liquid Region"]
    feedWell --> settlZone["ZONE 2 SETTLING ZONE Hindered Settling Per Kynch"]
    settlZone --> compZone["ZONE 3 COMPRESSION ZONE Solids Densification"]
    compZone --> rakeMech["RAKE MECHANISM Slow Rotation 0.02 to 0.1 rpm"]
    rakeMech --> underflow["UNDERFLOW SLURRY Qu x Cu High Concentration"]
    underflow --> pumpOut["UNDERFLOW PUMP"]
    clarZone --> overWeir["PERIPHERAL WEIR / LAUNDER"]
    overWeir --> overOut["OVERFLOW Qo x Co Clarified Liquid"]
    overOut --> recirc["RECYCLE OR DISCHARGE"]
    
    feedWell -.->|"Settling Velocity vs"| fluxAnalysis["KYNCH FLUX ANALYSIS G vs C Curve"]
    compZone -.->|"Interface vs Time"| talmadgeFitch["TALMADGE FITCH CONSTRUCTION Depth Calculation"]
    fluxAnalysis --> areaCalc["A_min = m_dot / G_L"]
    talmadgeFitch --> depthCalc["H_total = H1 + H2 + H3"]
    areaCalc --> finalDesign["FINAL THICKENER GEOMETRY"]
    depthCalc --> finalDesign
    finalDesign --> outputD["Diameter D and Depth H"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Filtration Process Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feedSlurry["FEED SLURRY Sl + Lq"] --> pump["FEED PUMP"]
    pump --> filterMedium["FILTER MEDIUM Initial Resistance Rm"]
    filterMedium --> cakeBuild["CAKE BUILDUP Growing Resistance R_c"]
    cakeBuild --> filtrateOut["FILTRATE V increases with t"]
    filtrateOut --> collect["FILTRATE COLLECTION TANK"]
    collect --> analyze["MEASURE V vs t EXPERIMENTAL"]
    
    analyze --> modeSelect{"OPERATING MODE"}
    modeSelect -->|"dP = constant"| constP["CONSTANT PRESSURE t/V vs V is Linear"]
    modeSelect -->|"dV/dt constant"| constR["CONSTANT RATE dP vs V is Linear"]
    
    constP --> extract["EXTRACT Slope m and Intercept c"]
    constR --> extract
    extract --> alphaCalc["alpha from Slope"]
    extract --> rmCalc["Rm from Intercept"]
    alphaCalc --> designOut["FILTER DESIGN PARAMETERS"]
    rmCalc --> designOut
    designOut --> filterType["SELECT FILTER TYPE Rotary Drum or Plate Frame"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Solids Flux vs Concentration Curve (Conceptual Graph)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FLUXCURVE["SOLIDS FLUX G vs CONCENTRATION C"]
        origin["ORIGIN C=0 G=0"]
        gsLine["Gs curve bell shaped from Kynch data"]
        opLine["OPERATING LINE from origin to Cu Gu"]
        tanPoint["TANGENT POINT C* G_L Limiting Flux"]
        maxFlux["MAXIMUM FLUX G_L"]
    end
    
    origin --> gsLine
    gsLine --> tanPoint
    tanPoint --> maxFlux
    origin --> opLine
    opLine --> tanPoint
    
    gsLine -.->|"Below tangent"| safeZone["SAFE OPERATING ZONE G less than G_L"]
    opLine -.->|"Above tangent"| failZone["FAILURE ZONE Solids Accumulate"]
```

    bestFit --> intercept

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**  
**State Kynch's postulate for hindered settling. Why is it the foundation of continuous thickener design?**

**Model Answer:**

Kynch's postulate (1952) states that the **settling velocity $v_s$ of a particle in a hindered-settling suspension depends only on the local solids concentration $C$** of the suspension, and not on the history of the suspension:

$$v_s = f(C)$$

**Significance for thickener design:**
- Allows the **batch settling curve** to be converted into a **continuous flux curve** $G(C) = C \cdot f(C)$.
- Permits the calculation of the **limiting solids flux** $G_L$ — the maximum rate at which a thickener of a given area can process solids.
- Forms the theoretical basis for the **Talmadge–Fitch construction** used to compute thickener depth.
- Reduces a complex 3D time-dependent sedimentation problem to a tractable 1D flux analysis.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse Kynch's postulate with the older **Coe–Clevenger method** (1916), which assumed that the settling rate depends on the average concentration. In Kynch's modern approach, the rate depends only on the **local** concentration — and this distinction is critical for the flux-based design.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**  
**Differentiate between constant-pressure and constant-rate filtration. Give the linearized form of the governing equation in each case.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Constant-Pressure (ΔP = constant) | Constant-Rate (dV/dt = constant) |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating variable held fixed** | ΔP (driving force) | dV/dt (flow rate) |
| **Variable that changes** | dV/dt decreases with time | ΔP increases with time |
| **Linear plot** | $t/V$ vs. $V$ | $\Delta P$ vs. $V$ (or $t$) |
| **Slope** | $\dfrac{\mu \alpha w}{2 A^2 \Delta P}$ | $\dfrac{\mu \alpha w v}{A}$ |
| **Intercept** | $\dfrac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P}$ | $\mu R_m v$ |
| **Equation** | $\dfrac{t}{V} = \dfrac{\mu \alpha w}{2 A^2 \Delta P} V + \dfrac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P}$ | $\Delta P = \dfrac{\mu \alpha w v}{A} V + \mu R_m v$ |
| **Industrial example** | Vacuum drum filter, gravity sand filter | Positive-displacement pump-fed filter press |

**Key physical insight:** In constant-pressure mode, the **flow rate decays** as cake builds. In constant-rate mode, the **pump must work harder** as the cake resistance rises.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question B1 (Option A)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, 14 Marks]**  

**(a)** [7 Marks] A continuous thickener is to be designed to treat **150 m³/hr** of a slurry containing **40 kg/m³** of suspended solids. Laboratory settling tests yield the following data for settling velocity $v_s$ as a function of concentration $C$:

| $C$ (kg/m³) | 20 | 50 | 100 | 150 | 200 | 250 | 300 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $v_s$ (m/s × 10⁻⁴) | 5.0 | 3.5 | 2.4 | 1.7 | 1.2 | 0.8 | 0.5 |

The underflow concentration is to be **$C_u = 250$ kg/m³**. Determine:
1. The **limiting solids flux** $G_L$ and critical concentration $C^*$.
2. The **minimum thickener area** $A_{\min}$.
3. The **design area** (with safety factor 1.25) and the corresponding **diameter**.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Describe the **Talmadge–Fitch construction** for computing the **depth of a continuous thickener**. List the three functional zones in a thickener and explain the role of the compression zone in determining the total depth.

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1 — Compute Mass Flow Rate of Solids**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{solids}} &= Q_f \cdot C_f = \frac{150 \times 40}{3600} \\
&= \frac{6000}{3600} = 1.667 \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Underflow Volumetric Flow Rate**

Neglecting solids in the overflow, the mass balance $Q_f C_f = Q_u C_u$ gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_u &= \frac{Q_f C_f}{C_u} = \frac{150 \times 40}{250} = 24 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr} \\
&= \frac{24}{3600} = 6.667 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Build the Solids Flux Curve**

We need $G_s = C \cdot v_s$ at each data point. For example, at $C = 50$ kg/m³, $v_s = 3.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_s(50) &= 50 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-4} = 1.75 \times 10^{-2} \text{ kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Computing for all data:

| $C$ (kg/m³) | $v_s$ (m/s) | $G_s = C v_s$ (kg/m²·s) |
|---|---|---|
| 20 | 5.0 × 10⁻⁴ | 1.00 × 10⁻² |
| 50 | 3.5 × 10⁻⁴ | 1.75 × 10⁻² |
| 100 | 2.4 × 10⁻⁴ | 2.40 × 10⁻² |
| 150 | 1.7 × 10⁻⁴ | 2.55 × 10⁻² |
| 200 | 1.2 × 10⁻⁴ | 2.40 × 10⁻² |
| 250 | 0.8 × 10⁻⁴ | 2.00 × 10⁻² |
| 300 | 0.5 × 10⁻⁴ | 1.50 × 10⁻² |

**Step 4 — Determine Underflow Velocity $u$ (Iteration Required)**

Since $u$ depends on the area $A$, which depends on $G_L$, this is a **circular problem**. The standard approach is to:

(i) Plot the **settling flux curve** $G_s(C)$.
(ii) Draw the **operating line** from the origin $(0,0)$ to the point $(C_u, G_u)$ where $G_u = C_u \cdot u$.
(iii) For a given assumed $u$, find the tangent from $(C_u, G_u)$ to the $G_s$ curve.
(iv) Read off $C^*$ and $G_L$.

**Algebraic approach** (parametric guess): Suppose we try $u = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s. Then $G_u = 250 \times 10^{-4} = 0.025$ kg/(m²·s). The line from origin to $(250, 0.025)$ has slope $1.0 \times 10^{-4}$, so:

$$
G_{\text{line}}(C) = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \cdot C
$$

Total flux: $G(C) = G_s(C) + 1.0 \times 10^{-4} C$

| $C$ | $G_s$ | $G_b = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} C$ | $G$ (total) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 20 | 1.00 × 10⁻² | 0.20 × 10⁻² | 1.20 × 10⁻² |
| 50 | 1.75 × 10⁻² | 0.50 × 10⁻² | 2.25 × 10⁻² |
| 100 | 2.40 × 10⁻² | 1.00 × 10⁻² | 3.40 × 10⁻² |
| 150 | 2.55 × 10⁻² | 1.50 × 10⁻² | 4.05 × 10⁻² |
| 200 | 2.40 × 10⁻² | 2.00 × 10⁻² | 4.40 × 10⁻² |
| 250 | 2.00 × 10⁻² | 2.50 × 10⁻² | 4.50 × 10⁻² |
| 300 | 1.50 × 10⁻² | 3.00 × 10⁻² | 4.50 × 10⁻² |

The maximum $G$ is at $C \approx 200\text{–}300$ kg/m³. Interpolating between $C=250$ and $C=300$ where $G = 4.50 \times 10^{-2}$, the **peak appears to be in the range $C^* \approx 250$–$300$ kg/m³** with $G_L \approx 4.50 \times 10^{-2}$ kg/(m²·s).

**Step 5 — Compute Minimum Area**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\min} &= \frac{\dot{m}}{G_L} = \frac{1.667}{4.50 \times 10^{-2}} \\
&= 37.04 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Safety Factor and Compute Diameter**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{design}} &= 1.25 \times 37.04 = 46.3 \text{ m}^2 \\
D_{\text{design}} &= \sqrt{\frac{4 A_{\text{design}}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 46.3}{\pi}} \\
&= \sqrt{58.95} = 7.68 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result Summary:**
- $G_L \approx 0.045$ kg/(m²·s) at $C^* \approx 250$ kg/m³
- $A_{\min} = 37.04 \text{ m}^2$, $A_{\text{design}} = 46.3 \text{ m}^2$
- $D_{\text{design}} \approx 7.68 \text{ m}$

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Talmadge–Fitch Construction Procedure:**

1. **Perform a batch settling test** in a 1-L graduated cylinder. Record the **height of the solids–liquid interface (h)** as a function of time (t).

2. **Construct the plot** of $h$ vs. $t$ (typically a three-stage curve: clarification, transition, compression).

3. **Identify the compression point** — the inflection where the curve transitions from linear settling to asymptotic compression.

4. **For each concentration level** $C_i$ (calculated from mass balance as $C_i = C_0 h_0 / h_i$ where $h_0, C_0$ are initial values):
   - Compute the **time to reach that concentration** $t_i$.
   - Compute the **equivalent continuous-thickener depth** for that layer:
     $$\Delta H_i = \frac{t_i \cdot G_L}{C_i \cdot A_{\text{design}} / \dot{m}_{\text{solids}}}$$
     Or equivalently, the **Talmadge–Fitch formula**:
     $$\Delta H_i = \frac{t_i - t_{i-1}}{\frac{C_{i+1} - C_i}{G_L} \cdot \frac{C_{\text{avg}} A_{\text{design}}}{\dot{m}}}$$

5. **Sum the layer depths** to obtain the total compression-zone depth $H_3$:
   $$H_3 = \sum_i \Delta H_i$$

**Three Functional Zones in a Continuous Thickener:**

| Zone | Location | Function | Typical Depth |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Clarification zone (H₁)** | Top, above sludge blanket | Clear liquid (overflow) decants over peripheral launder | 0.3 – 1.0 m |
| **Settling zone (H₂)** | Middle, between feed well and sludge blanket | Free hindered settling per Kynch's theory | 0.5 – 1.5 m |
| **Compression zone (H₃)** | Bottom, beneath sludge blanket | Solids densify under their own weight + rake action | 1.5 – 3.0 m |

**Role of Compression Zone:** The compression zone is where the **final thickening** (solids concentration increasing from feed value to underflow value) takes place. The **rake arms** slowly rotate, breaking the cake structure, releasing trapped liquid, and creating channels for upward water migration. **The depth of this zone typically dominates the total thickener depth** and is calculated by the **Talmadge–Fitch method** using the **batch settling curve**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Thickener Depth):** Many students forget to **scale the batch settling depth by the ratio of areas**. A 1-L cylinder test cannot be directly used for an industrial thickener — the depth must be scaled by:
> $$H_{\text{industrial}} = H_{\text{lab}} \times \frac{A_{\text{lab}}}{A_{\text{industrial}}}$$
> This scaling factor is a frequent source of errors in KTU answers.

---

### Question B1 (Option B) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the **Ruth filtration equation** for **constant-pressure filtration** starting from Darcy's law. Clearly state the assumptions, the linearized form of the equation, and explain how the **specific cake resistance α** and **filter medium resistance R_m** are obtained experimentally.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A plate-and-frame filter handles a slurry with the following data:  
- Filter area: $A = 5 \text{ m}^2$ (total for all frames)  
- ΔP = $3 \times 10^5$ Pa  
- Filtrate viscosity: $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s  
- Cake solids per filtrate volume: $w = 20$ kg/m³  
- Constant-pressure test gives slope $m = 8.0 \times 10^5$ s/m⁶ and intercept $c = 1500$ s/m³  

Calculate:
1. The **specific cake resistance α**.
2. The **filter medium resistance R_m**.
3. The **time required to collect 2.5 m³ of filtrate**.
4. Comment on whether the **medium resistance** is significant compared to **cake resistance** at $V = 2.5$ m³.

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Assumptions:**
1. The filter cake is **incompressible** (α = constant).
2. The **filter medium resistance** $R_m$ is constant.
3. The filtrate has a **constant viscosity** μ (isothermal operation).
4. Flow through the cake is in the **Darcy regime** (laminar, low Re).
5. The cake is **uniformly distributed** over the filter area.
6. The solids concentration in the feed is low enough that the **filtrate volume ≈ liquid volume in feed**.

**Derivation:**

Start from Darcy's law with the resistance model:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dV}{dt} &= \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot R} = \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \left( R_m + \alpha w V / A \right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Rearranging and separating variables:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left( R_m + \frac{\alpha w V}{A} \right) dV &= \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu} \, dt
\end{aligned}
$$

Integrating from $(0, 0)$ to $(V, t)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_m V + \frac{\alpha w V^2}{2A} &= \frac{A \cdot \Delta P \cdot t}{\mu}
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing both sides by $V$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_m + \frac{\alpha w V}{2A} &= \frac{A \cdot \Delta P \cdot t}{\mu V} \\
\Rightarrow \frac{t}{V} &= \frac{\mu \alpha w}{2 A^2 \Delta P} \cdot V + \frac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Ruth constant-pressure equation** in linear form: $y = mx + c$ with:
- $y = t/V$
- $x = V$
- $m = \dfrac{\mu \alpha w}{2 A^2 \Delta P}$
- $c = \dfrac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P}$

**Experimental Determination:**

Conduct a constant-pressure run at a fixed ΔP, recording (V, t) data pairs at multiple times. Plot $t/V$ vs. $V$ — the best-fit straight line yields slope $m$ and intercept $c$. Then:

$$
\alpha = \frac{2 m A^2 \Delta P}{\mu w} \qquad R_m = \frac{c \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu}
$$

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Calculate α from the slope**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{\mu \alpha w}{2 A^2 \Delta P} \\
\alpha &= \frac{2 m A^2 \Delta P}{\mu w} \\
&= \frac{2 \times (8.0 \times 10^5) \times (5)^2 \times (3 \times 10^5)}{(1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times 20} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 8.0 \times 10^5 \times 25 \times 3 \times 10^5}{2.0 \times 10^{-2}} \\
&= \frac{1.2 \times 10^{13}}{2.0 \times 10^{-2}} = 6.0 \times 10^{14} \text{ m/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate R_m from the intercept**

$$
\begin{aligned}
c &= \frac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P} \\
R_m &= \frac{c \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu} \\
&= \frac{1500 \times 5 \times (3 \times 10^5)}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= \frac{2.25 \times 10^{9}}{10^{-3}} = 2.25 \times 10^{12} \text{ m}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate time to collect V = 2.5 m³ of filtrate**

Using $t/V = mV + c$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{t}{2.5} &= (8.0 \times 10^5)(2.5) + 1500 \\
&= 2.0 \times 10^6 + 1500 = 2.0015 \times 10^6 \text{ s/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= 2.5 \times 2.0015 \times 10^6 = 5.004 \times 10^6 \text{ s} \\
&\approx 1389.9 \text{ hours} \approx 57.9 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Wait — this result is suspiciously large!** Let us recheck. The high $R_m$ and high $\alpha$ are unusual. Let us verify units:

Slope $m$ has units s/m⁶ (per Ruth's equation). The intercept is s/m³. With $V = 2.5$ m³:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{t}{V} &= m V + c \\
\frac{t}{2.5} &= (8.0 \times 10^5 \text{ s/m}^6)(2.5 \text{ m}^3) + 1500 \text{ s/m}^3 \\
&= 2.0 \times 10^6 \text{ s/m}^3 + 1500 \text{ s/m}^3 \\
&\approx 2.0015 \times 10^6 \text{ s/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
t = 2.5 \text{ m}^3 \times 2.0015 \times 10^6 \text{ s/m}^3 = 5.004 \times 10^6 \text{ s}
$$

This is indeed a very high resistance filter. The data is consistent but indicates that the cake has unusually high specific resistance — this is a **highly compressible cake** (e.g., a fine precipitate like aluminum hydroxide or ferric hydroxide).

**Step 4 — Compare medium vs cake resistance at V = 2.5 m³**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_m &= 2.25 \times 10^{12} \text{ m}^{-1} \\
R_c &= \frac{\alpha w V}{A} = \frac{(6.0 \times 10^{14}) \times 20 \times 2.5}{5} \\
&= \frac{3.0 \times 10^{16}}{5} = 6.0 \times 10^{15} \text{ m}^{-1} \\
\frac{R_c}{R_m} &= \frac{6.0 \times 10^{15}}{2.25 \times 10^{12}} = 2667
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** The cake resistance is **~2667 times** the medium resistance. The medium resistance is **completely negligible** compared to the cake resistance — a classic signature of a heavily loaded industrial filter. This justifies using filter aids (e.g., diatomaceous earth) in practice to reduce cake compressibility.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Filtration):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — μ in Pa·s, ΔP in Pa, A in m², V in m³. Sloppy unit handling is the #1 cause of mark loss.
> 2. **Confusing α (specific cake resistance, m/kg) with R_m (medium resistance, m⁻¹)**. They are dimensionally different and serve different roles in the equations.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the constant-pressure equation ($V^2/2$ becomes $V$ after linearization but the slope retains the "2" in the denominator).
> 4. **For Ruth's equation, the linear form uses $t/V$ vs $V$, NOT $t$ vs $V$**. This is the single most common error in KTU answer sheets.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### Continuous Thickeners
- ✅ **Kynch's postulate:** $v_s$ depends only on local $C$, not history.
- ✅ **Two fluxes:** Settling flux $G_s = C v_s$ + Bulk flux $G_b = uC$.
- ✅ **Total flux:** $G = C(v_s + u)$.
- ✅ **Limiting flux condition:** $\dfrac{dG}{dC} = 0$ → find $G_L$ and $C^*$.
- ✅ **Minimum area:** $A_{\min} = \dot{m}_{\text{solids}} / G_L$.
- ✅ **Apply safety factor:** 1.2 to 1.5.
- ✅ **Talmadge–Fitch method** for depth — uses batch settling test data.
- ✅ **Three zones:** Clarification + Settling + Compression.
- ✅ **Compression zone** typically dominates the total depth.
- ✅ **Operating line** connects origin $(0,0)$ to $(C_u, G_u)$ with slope $u$.
- ✅ **Tangent from $(C_u, G_u)$** to $G_s$ curve defines $C^*$ and $G_L$.

### Filtration
- ✅ **Foundation:** Darcy's law + resistance model $R = R_m + \alpha w V / A$.
- ✅ **Incompressible cake:** $\alpha$ = constant.
- ✅ **Compressible cake:** $\alpha = \alpha_0 (\Delta P)^s$, $0 \le s \le 1$.
- ✅ **Constant-ΔP equation:** $\dfrac{t}{V} = \dfrac{\mu \alpha w}{2 A^2 \Delta P} V + \dfrac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P}$ — plot $t/V$ vs $V$.
- ✅ **Constant-rate equation:** $\Delta P = \dfrac{\mu \alpha w v}{A} V + \mu R_m v$ — plot $\Delta P$ vs $V$.
- ✅ **Slope** of constant-ΔP plot gives $\alpha$ (contains cake info).
- ✅ **Intercept** of constant-ΔP plot gives $R_m$ (contains medium info).
- ✅ **Cake resistance grows with V**; **medium resistance is constant**.
- ✅ **Typical α range:** $10^9$ to $10^{13}$ m/kg.
- ✅ **Typical $R_m$ range:** $10^9$ to $10^{11}$ m⁻¹.
- ✅ **Cake compressibility** matters for **pressure filter design**; vacuum filters favor incompressible cakes.

### Critical KTU Traps to Avoid
- ❌ Mixing up **specific cake resistance (m/kg)** with **medium resistance (m⁻¹)**.
- ❌ Using **average concentration** instead of **local concentration** in Kynch's theory.
- ❌ Forgetting to **scale** batch settling data to industrial thickener size.
- ❌ Neglecting the **safety factor** in thickener area.
- ❌ Confusing **operating line slope** ($u$) with the **settling flux curve**.
- ❌ Forgetting the **factor of 2** in Ruth's constant-pressure slope formula.
- ❌ Ignoring the **compression zone** when sizing thickener depth.
- ❌ Using the **wrong linearized plot** (e.g., plotting $V$ vs $t$ instead of $t/V$ vs $V$).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Equipment for Filtration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

**Filtration** is a mechanical or physical operation used for the separation of solids from fluids (liquids or gases) by interposing a **filter medium** through which only the fluid can pass. The fluid that passes through is called the **filtrate**, while the solids retained on the medium form the **filter cake**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Aligned):**
> *Filtration equipment* refers to the engineered devices employed to carry out solid–fluid separation based on size exclusion, where the driving force (pressure drop) overcomes the resistance offered by the filter medium and the accumulating cake of solids.

**Equipment for Filtration** are classified broadly into two operating categories:

| Operating Class | Driving Force | Typical Pressure Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Pressure Filters** | Positive pressure on feed side | **1 to 10 atm** (gauge) |
| **Vacuum Filters** | Negative pressure on filtrate side | **0.3 to 0.8 atm** (absolute) |
| **Gravity Filters** | Hydrostatic head | **< 0.1 atm** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Tea Strainer**
> Think of making tea with a metal strainer. The strainer (filter medium) holds back the tea leaves (solids) while allowing the liquid tea (filtrate) to pass through. Now, in industry, imagine the strainer is **10 meters tall**, the leaves are **sticky wet sludge**, and the liquid is being forced through under high pressure using a pump. That is industrial filtration!

A more relatable industrial analogy:
- **Filter Medium** = Kitchen Sieve/Jaalini
- **Filter Cake** = Wet residue left on the sieve
- **Filtrate** = Clean liquid that drips out
- **Driving Force** = Pressure from the pump pushing the liquid through

## 1.3 Classification of Industrial Filters

Industrial filtration equipment is classified based on:

1. **Driving force applied** → Pressure, Vacuum, Gravity, Centrifugal
2. **Mechanism of operation** → Cake filtration, Depth filtration, Cross-flow filtration
3. **Batch vs Continuous** → Batch (e.g., plate and frame), Continuous (e.g., rotary drum)
4. **Filter medium type** → Rigid, Flexible, Granular bed
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Principle of Filtration

Filtration follows the **general filtration equation** derived from Darcy's Law analogously applied to cake filtration:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A^2 \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot (c \cdot V + c' \cdot A)}$$

Where:
- $V$ = volume of filtrate collected (m³)
- $t$ = filtration time (s)
- $A$ = filtration area (m²)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure drop across cake and medium (Pa or N/m²)
- $\mu$ = viscosity of filtrate (Pa·s)
- $c$ = mass of solids deposited per unit volume of filtrate (kg/m³)
- $c'$ = filter medium resistance (1/m)
- $\alpha$ = specific cake resistance (m/kg)
- $R_m$ = filter medium resistance (1/m)

## 2.2 Resistance Model — Cake + Medium

The total resistance to flow is composed of two components:

$$R_{total} = R_{cake} + R_{medium}$$

Expanding these:

$$R_{cake} = \alpha \cdot \nu \cdot \frac{V}{A}$$

$$R_{medium} = R_m \text{ (constant)}$$

Where $\nu$ is the volume of cake formed per unit volume of filtrate.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A \Delta P}{\mu (R_c + R_m)}$ | Basic filtration rate equation | m³/s |
| 2 | $R_c = \alpha \cdot c \cdot \frac{V}{A}$ | Cake resistance | 1/m |
| 3 | $\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A^2 \Delta P}{\mu(\alpha c V + \mu R_m A)}$ | Combined resistance form | m³/s |
| 4 | $t = \frac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^2 \Delta P} V^2 + \frac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P} V$ | Constant pressure integration | s |
| 5 | $K_p = \frac{2 \Delta P \cdot A^2}{\mu \alpha c}$ | Constant pressure constant | m⁶/s |
| 6 | $K_s = \frac{\Delta P \cdot A}{\mu R_m}$ | Constant pressure constant (linear term) | m³/s |
| 7 | $\frac{t}{V} = \frac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^2 \Delta P} V + \frac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P}$ | Linearized form (t/V vs V) | s/m³ |
| 8 | $\alpha = \alpha_0 \cdot \Delta P^s$ | Compressible cake correction (s = compressibility, 0 ≤ s ≤ 1) | m/kg |
| 9 | $V^2 + 2 V V_e = K \cdot t$ | With filter medium correction (Vₑ = equivalence volume) | m⁶ |
| 10 | $R_m = \frac{\Delta P \cdot A}{\mu \cdot (dV/dt)_0}$ | Medium resistance from initial rate | 1/m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM TIP:** Equation (4) is the **most frequently asked** constant-pressure filtration integrated form. Note that it is a **parabolic** (quadratic) relation between $t$ and $V$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceuticals**: Sterile filtration using cartridge filters
- **Water Treatment**: Sand filters and membrane filters
- **Chemical Industry**: Plate and frame filters for catalyst recovery
- **Sugar Industry**: Rotary vacuum filters for sugar juice clarification
- **Mineral Processing**: Thickener underflow dewatering
- **Food Industry**: Filter presses for edible oil refining
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Constant-Pressure Filtration Equation

### Step 1: Start with the basic rate equation

The volumetric flow rate of filtrate through a filter cake of resistance $R_c$ and medium resistance $R_m$ is:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu (R_c + R_m)}$$

### Step 2: Express cake resistance in terms of filtrate volume

The cake resistance depends on the mass of solids deposited. As filtration proceeds, more cake accumulates. Assuming all solids in the feed are captured by the filter:

$$R_c = \alpha \cdot \frac{W}{A} = \alpha \cdot \frac{c \cdot V}{A}$$

Where $W = c \cdot V$ is the mass of dry cake and $c$ = mass of solids per unit volume of filtrate.

### Step 3: Substitute into the rate equation

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \left(\frac{\alpha c V}{A} + R_m\right)}$$

### Step 4: Multiply numerator and denominator by $A$

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A^2 \cdot \Delta P}{\mu (\alpha c V + R_m A)}$$

### Step 5: Separate variables for integration at constant $\Delta P$

Rearranging:

$$(\alpha c V + R_m A) \, dV = \frac{A^2 \Delta P}{\mu} \, dt$$

### Step 6: Integrate from $V = 0$ at $t = 0$ to $V = V$ at $t = t$

$$\int_0^V (\alpha c V + R_m A) \, dV = \frac{A^2 \Delta P}{\mu} \int_0^t dt$$

### Step 7: Perform the integration

$$\frac{\alpha c V^2}{2} + R_m A V = \frac{A^2 \Delta P}{\mu} \cdot t$$

### Step 8: Final form — solve for t

$$\boxed{t = \frac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^2 \Delta P} V^2 + \frac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P} V}$$

This is the **fundamental constant-pressure filtration equation**, which is parabolic in $V$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Linearized Form (t/V vs V)

Dividing both sides of the final equation by $V$:

$$\frac{t}{V} = \frac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^2 \Delta P} V + \frac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P}$$

This is a **straight line** of the form $y = m x + b$:

- **Slope** $m = \dfrac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^2 \Delta P}$
- **Intercept** $b = \dfrac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P}$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** A plot of $t/V$ vs $V$ yields a straight line. The slope and intercept are used to determine $\alpha$ and $R_m$ experimentally.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Standard)

**Problem:** A filter press operates at a constant pressure of **$3 \times 10^5$ Pa**. The filtration area is **$0.5$ m²**. The viscosity of the filtrate is **$10^{-3}$ Pa·s**. The specific cake resistance is **$1.2 \times 10^{11}$ m/kg** and the cake solids concentration parameter is **$25$ kg/m³**. The filter medium resistance is **$1.5 \times 10^{10}$ 1/m**. Find the time required to collect **0.05 m³** of filtrate.

**Solution:**

Using the equation:
$$t = \frac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^2 \Delta P} V^2 + \frac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P} V$$

### Compute the quadratic coefficient:

$$\frac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^2 \Delta P} = \frac{(10^{-3})(1.2 \times 10^{11})(25)}{2 (0.5)^2 (3 \times 10^5)}$$

$$= \frac{3 \times 10^{9}}{1.5 \times 10^{5}} = 2 \times 10^{4} \text{ s/m}^6$$

### Compute the linear coefficient:

$$\frac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P} = \frac{(10^{-3})(1.5 \times 10^{10})}{(0.5)(3 \times 10^5)} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{7}}{1.5 \times 10^{5}} = 100 \text{ s/m}^3$$

### Substitute $V = 0.05$ m³:

$$t = (2 \times 10^4)(0.05)^2 + (100)(0.05)$$

$$t = (2 \times 10^4)(2.5 \times 10^{-3}) + 5$$

$$t = 50 + 5 = \boxed{55 \text{ seconds}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Filter Equipment

| Parameter | Plate & Frame | Leaf Filter | Rotary Drum Vacuum | Sand Filter | Centrifugal Filter |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Driving Force** | Pressure | Pressure/Vacuum | Vacuum | Gravity/Pressure | Centrifugal force |
| **Batch/Continuous** | Batch | Batch | Continuous | Continuous | Batch/Continuous |
| **Cake Removal** | Manual | Manual | Automatic (scraper) | Backwash | Automatic |
| **Typical $\Delta P$** | 3–10 atm | 2–5 atm | 0.5–0.8 atm | 0.05–0.2 atm | 1000+ × gravity |
| **Cake Washing** | Excellent | Moderate | Good | N/A | Moderate |
| **Capital Cost** | Low | Moderate | High | Low | High |
| **Solids Recovery** | Dry cake | Wet cake | Wet cake | Wet sludge | Dry cake |
| **Industries** | Pharma, Chemicals | Beverages, Chemicals | Mining, Sugar | Water Treatment | Salt, Sugar, Chemicals |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**
**Define the term "filter cake" and "filtrate" with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Filter Cake (1 Mark):** The accumulated layer of solid particles retained on the filter medium during a filtration operation is called the filter cake. Example: The wet mass of solids collected on a plate and frame filter cloth during juice clarification.
- **Filtrate (1 Mark):** The clear liquid that has passed through the filter medium, free of suspended solids, is called the filtrate. Example: The clear sugar juice that drains from the filter press in a sugar manufacturing plant.
- **Process Linkage (1 Mark):** The filter cake increases in thickness with time, increasing flow resistance, which is why cake resistance $R_c = \alpha c V / A$ is proportional to filtrate volume $V$.

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
**List any three advantages and three disadvantages of a Rotary Drum Vacuum Filter.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Advantages (1.5 Marks):**
1. Continuous operation — suitable for large-scale production.
2. Fully automatic cake discharge — minimal manual labor.
3. Good for slurries with high solid content and coarse particles.

**Disadvantages (1.5 Marks):**
1. High capital and maintenance cost due to vacuum pump and rotating parts.
2. Not suitable for very fine particles — filter cloth gets blinded.
3. Cake washing efficiency is moderate compared to plate and frame filters.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full Module Question with Internal Choice (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Internal Choice Format:** Answer any ONE full question from the two given. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b) for 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Module 4 Pattern]**

**Part (a)** — 7 Marks

With a neat labeled diagram, explain the **construction, working, and industrial applications of a Plate and Frame Filter Press**. State its advantages and limitations.

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. **Construction (2 Marks):**
   - Alternating plates and frames arranged in series on a frame
   - Filter cloth (medium) is fixed on each plate
   - Plates have corrugated or studded surface with filtrate drainage channels
   - Frames are hollow and provide space for cake accumulation
   - Plates and frames are pressed together using hydraulic or manual screw

2. **Working (3 Marks):**
   - Slurry is fed under pressure (typically 3–10 atm) through a central feed channel
   - Filtrate passes through the cloth and drains out through filtrate outlets
   - Solids deposit on the cloth forming the cake inside the frame chamber
   - When frames are full, feed is stopped and cake is removed by opening the press
   - Cake is dropped manually or by scraping

3. **Applications and Merits/Demerits (2 Marks):**
   - Applications: Pharmaceutical, dyestuff, chemical catalyst recovery, edible oil refining
   - Advantages: High pressure operation, dry cake, excellent cake washing, simple design
   - Disadvantages: Batch operation, manual labor for cake removal, cloth wear

**Part (b)** — 7 Marks

A plate and frame filter press has a total filtration area of **$8$ m²** and operates at a constant pressure of **$4 \times 10^5$ Pa**. The filtrate viscosity is **$1.1 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s**. The specific cake resistance is **$1.8 \times 10^{11}$ m/kg** and solids concentration parameter $c = 20$ kg/m³. The filter medium resistance is **$2.0 \times 10^{10}$ 1/m**.

**Calculate the volume of filtrate collected in 1800 seconds.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Write the constant-pressure filtration equation.**

$$t = \frac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^2 \Delta P} V^2 + \frac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P} V$$

**Step 2: Compute the quadratic coefficient.**

$$\frac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^2 \Delta P} = \frac{(1.1 \times 10^{-3})(1.8 \times 10^{11})(20)}{2 (8)^2 (4 \times 10^5)}$$

$$= \frac{3.96 \times 10^{9}}{5.12 \times 10^{7}} = 77.34 \text{ s/m}^6$$

**Step 3: Compute the linear coefficient.**

$$\frac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P} = \frac{(1.1 \times 10^{-3})(2.0 \times 10^{10})}{(8)(4 \times 10^5)}$$

$$= \frac{2.2 \times 10^{7}}{3.2 \times 10^{6}} = 6.875 \text{ s/m}^3$$

**Step 4: Form the quadratic equation in V.**

$$1800 = 77.34 \, V^2 + 6.875 \, V$$

**Step 5: Solve the quadratic equation.**

$$77.34 \, V^2 + 6.875 \, V - 1800 = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula:
$$V = \frac{-6.875 \pm \sqrt{(6.875)^2 + 4(77.34)(1800)}}{2(77.34)}$$

$$V = \frac{-6.875 \pm \sqrt{47.27 + 556848}}{154.68}$$

$$V = \frac{-6.875 \pm \sqrt{556895.27}}{154.68}$$

$$V = \frac{-6.875 + 746.26}{154.68} = \frac{739.39}{154.68}$$

$$\boxed{V \approx 4.78 \text{ m}^3}$$

(Only the positive root is physically meaningful.)

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - Module 4 Pattern - Alternative]**

**Part (a)** — 7 Marks

Explain with a neat labeled diagram the **construction, working, and applications of a Rotary Drum Vacuum Filter**. Discuss its advantages over a plate and frame filter.

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. **Construction (2 Marks):**
   - Hollow rotating drum mounted horizontally, partially submerged in a slurry trough
   - Drum surface divided into sectors, each connected to a vacuum line via a rotary valve
   - Filter cloth (medium) covers the drum surface
   - Scraper blade, wash spray nozzles, and discharge mechanism

2. **Working (3 Marks):**
   - As the drum rotates slowly, each sector passes through different zones:
     - **Filtration zone** (submerged in slurry): Vacuum pulls filtrate inside, cake forms on surface
     - **Dewatering zone** (above slurry level): Continued vacuum removes residual liquid
     - **Washing zone**: Wash water sprays wash the cake
     - **Discharge zone**: Cake is scraped off the cloth
   - Continuous operation with rotary valve controlling vacuum application

3. **Applications and Comparison (2 Marks):**
   - Applications: Sugar industry, mining, mineral processing, pigment manufacture
   - Advantages over P&F: Continuous, lower labor, handles slurries with high solid content
   - Disadvantages: Higher capital cost, moderate cake dryness

**Part (b)** — 7 Marks

A rotary drum vacuum filter operates at a constant vacuum of **$0.7$ atm** (gauge). The drum area is **$4$ m²** and the drum submergence is **$30\%$**. The drum rotates at **$0.5$ rpm**. Calculate the **filtration cycle time per revolution** and the **theoretical filtrate volume per revolution** if $\frac{dV}{dt} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s under operating conditions.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate cycle time per revolution.**

The drum completes 1 revolution in:
$$T = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2 \text{ minutes/rev} = 120 \text{ s/rev}$$

**Step 2: Calculate effective filtration time per revolution.**

Filtration occurs only during the submerged fraction of the cycle:
$$t_{filtration} = T \times \text{submergence fraction}$$

$$t_{filtration} = 120 \times 0.30 = 36 \text{ s per revolution}$$

**Step 3: Calculate filtrate volume per revolution.**

$$V = \left(\frac{dV}{dt}\right) \times t_{filtration}$$

$$V = (5 \times 10^{-4}) \times 36 = 0.018 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\boxed{V = 0.018 \text{ m}^3 \text{ per revolution (18 litres)}}$$

**Step 4: Convert atmospheric units (additional KTU credit).**

$$\Delta P_{vacuum} = 0.7 \text{ atm} = 0.7 \times 101325 = 70{,}927.5 \text{ Pa} \approx 7.09 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Many students mix atm, bar, and Pa. Always convert to **SI (Pa)** before substitution.
> 2. **Quadratic Sign Error:** In the constant-pressure equation, the linear term is **+ve**, not negative. The equation is $t = aV^2 + bV$, NOT $aV^2 - bV$.
> 3. **Forgetting $\mu$:** Viscosity appears in the numerator — students sometimes drop it in dimensional haste.
> 4. **Confusing $c$ and $c'$:** $c$ is solids-to-filtrate concentration (kg/m³); $c'$ is medium resistance correction (1/m). These are NOT the same.
> 5. **Picking the wrong root:** Quadratic in $V$ has two roots; only the **positive root** is physically meaningful.
> 6. **In drum filter problems:** The "cycle time" is the time per revolution, but **filtration occurs only during the submergence fraction** — students often forget this.
> 7. **Filter medium resistance unit:** It is **1/m** (inverse length), not N·s/m or any other unit. Don't confuse with mechanical friction resistance.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Equipment for Filtration**

- **Core Equation (Constant Pressure):**
  $t = \dfrac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^2 \Delta P} V^2 + \dfrac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P} V$

- **Linearized Form:** $\dfrac{t}{V}$ vs $V$ is a **straight line** — used for graphical determination of $\alpha$ and $R_m$.

- **Filter Cake Resistance:** $R_c = \alpha \cdot c \cdot \dfrac{V}{A}$ — proportional to filtrate volume.

- **Filter Medium Resistance:** $R_m$ is a constant depending on the cloth properties.

- **Compressible Cakes:** $\alpha = \alpha_0 \cdot \Delta P^s$ where $s$ is the compressibility index (0 = incompressible, 1 = highly compressible).

- **Plate and Frame Filter Press:** Batch, high pressure (3–10 atm), dry cake, manual discharge, used in pharma/chemicals.

- **Leaf Filter:** Batch, enclosed, used for clarification of beverages and chemicals.

- **Rotary Drum Vacuum Filter:** Continuous, vacuum-driven, automatic cake discharge, used in sugar/mining.

- **Sand Filter:** Granular bed, gravity or pressure, used in water treatment.

- **Centrifugal Filter:** Uses g-forces (1000+ × g), high dewatering, used for coarse crystals.

- **Nutsche Filter:** Closed-vessel batch filter with agitation, used in pharmaceutical/API manufacturing.

- **Driving Force Hierarchy:** Centrifugal > Pressure > Vacuum > Gravity.

- **Cake Formation vs Depth Filtration:** Cake filtration forms an explicit cake layer; depth filtration traps particles within the medium.

- **KTU Favorite Numerical:** $t = aV^2 + bV$ form, solve for $V$ given $t$ using quadratic formula.

- **Unit Mastery:** $1$ atm = $101325$ Pa; viscosity in Pa·s; area in m²; volume in m³; time in seconds.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constant Pressure and Constant Rate Filtration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBSFT304 - CHEMICAL PROCESS PRINCIPLES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Constant Pressure and Constant Rate Filtration # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Filtration? **Filtration** is a mechanical, physical separation process used to separate an insoluble solid phase (suspended particles) from a fluid (liquid or gas) by passing the mixture through a porous medium that retains the solids and allows the fluid (filtrate) to pass through. In chemical process industries, the two most common industrial filtration operations are governed by two distinctly different process control philosophies: 1. **Constant Pressure Filtration** (ΔP held constant, flow rate decreases with time) 2. **Constant Rate Filtration** (flow rate held constant, ΔP increases with time) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PBSFT304 – Module 4):** > Filtration is the unit operation of separating solids from fluids (liquids or gases) by interposing a medium through which only the fluid can pass. The two operating modes — **Constant Pressure** and **Constant Rate** — describe how the driving force (ΔP) and the throughput (dV/dt) are related during the cycle. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Coffee Maker Story Imagine you are making filter coffee at home using a paper filter and a funnel: - **Constant Pressure Mode**: You pour water from a height that is always the same (the water column above the filter stays at the same level). As the coffee powder cake builds up on the filter paper, the drip rate **slows down** automatically. You do nothing — the pressure is constant, but the flow decreases. This is **Constant Pressure Filtration**. - **Constant Rate Mode**: You use a small hand pump and push the water through the filter at a **fixed, steady rate** (one squirt per second). As the cake gets thicker, the resistance increases, and you must **press harder** (increase the pressure) to maintain that steady flow. This is **Constant Rate Filtration**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Industry Insight (Why it matters):** > Rotary vacuum filters usually operate at **constant pressure** (vacuum level is fixed). Filter presses and most modern membrane/ultrafiltration systems often operate at **constant rate** (using positive displacement pumps) because cake compressibility control is critical. ## 1.3 Fundamental Components of Any Filtration System | Component | Description | Typical Material | |-----------|-------------|------------------| | **Suspension (Slurry)** | The feed mixture of solids + fluid | e.g., CaCO₃ slurry, juice with pulp | | **Filter Medium** | The porous barrier that retains solids | Canvas, sintered metal, ceramic, polymer membrane | | **Filter Cake** | The accumulated solids on the medium | Compressible (gelatinous) or incompressible (rigid) | | **Filtrate** | The clarified fluid that passes through | Purified liquid product | | **ΔP (Pressure Drop)** | The driving force across the medium + cake | 1 bar to 50 bar (industrial) | | **μ (Viscosity)** | Viscosity of the filtrate | Pa·s (SI units) | > [!NOTE] > **Mandatory Units (as per KTU board examiners):** > - Volume: m³ (or litres) > - Area: m² > - Time: s > - Pressure: Pa (= N/m²) > - Specific cake resistance (α): m/kg > - Cake mass per unit filtrate volume (ν or w or s): kg/m³ --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Physical Theory Behind Filtration Filtration is governed by **Darcy's Law**, which states that the superficial velocity of a fluid through a porous bed is directly proportional to the applied pressure drop and inversely proportional to the fluid viscosity and total resistance. ### 2.1.1 Darcy's Law Applied to Filter Cake The instantaneous rate of filtration is given by: $$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot (R_c + R_m)}$$ where: - $V$ = volume of filtrate collected (m³) - $A$ = total filter area (m²) - $\Delta P$ = pressure drop across the cake + medium (Pa) - $\mu$ = viscosity of the filtrate (Pa·s) - $R_c$ = resistance of the filter cake (m⁻¹) - $R_m$ = resistance of the filter medium (m⁻¹) ### 2.1.2 Filter Cake Resistance (R_c) As filtration proceeds, solids accumulate on the medium. The mass of dry cake deposited per unit volume of filtrate is a constant of the slurry, denoted by $\nu$ (kg dry solid / m³ filtrate). $$R_c = \alpha \cdot \frac{\nu \cdot V}{A}$$ where: - $\alpha$ = specific cake resistance (m/kg) - $\nu$ = mass of dry cake per unit filtrate volume (kg/m³) ### 2.1.3 Specific Cake Resistance — Compressible vs. Incompressible Cakes For **incompressible cakes** (rigid particles, e.g., sand, CaCO₃, diatomaceous earth): $$\alpha = \alpha_0 = \text{constant (independent of } \Delta P\text{)}$$ For **compressible cakes** (soft, deformable particles, e.g., flocs, biological sludges, metal hydroxides), the specific resistance is a power-law function of pressure: $$\alpha = \alpha_0 \cdot (\Delta P)^s$$ where: - $\alpha_0$ = specific cake resistance at unit pressure (m/kg) - $s$ = compressibility coefficient (0 for incompressible, ~0.2 to 0.8 for compressible) ### 2.1.4 Filter Medium Resistance (R_m) The filter medium itself offers a finite resistance, often modelled as: $$R_m = \text{constant}$$ or expressed as the equivalent filtrate volume that would pass through the clean medium to deposit a fictitious cake of equal resistance: $$V_e = \frac{A \cdot R_m}{\alpha \cdot \nu}$$ ## 2.2 The General (Fundamental) Filtration Equation Substituting $R_c$ into Darcy's equation: $$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot \left(\alpha \cdot \frac{\nu \cdot V}{A} + R_m\right)}$$ Rearranging: $$\left(\frac{\mu \cdot \alpha \cdot \nu}{A^2}\right) \cdot V \cdot dV + \frac{\mu \cdot R_m}{A} \cdot dV = \Delta P \cdot A \cdot dt$$ This is the **mother equation** from which both operating modes (constant pressure and constant rate) are derived. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Why" Behind The Two Modes:** > The product $V \cdot dV$ (or $dV^2$) couples volume and time, making filtration inherently **non-linear**. Whether you hold $\Delta P$ constant or $dV/dt$ constant fundamentally changes the differential equation you must integrate — and hence the V–t relationship you get. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Equation | Applicable To | Units | |---|---------|----------|---------------|-------| | 1 | Darcy's Law | $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{A \Delta P}{\mu (R_c + R_m)}$ | All filtration | m³/s | | 2 | Cake Resistance | $R_c = \dfrac{\alpha \nu V}{A}$ | All filtration | m⁻¹ | | 3 | Constant P: Combined Eq. | $\dfrac{t}{V} = \dfrac{\mu \alpha \nu}{2 A^2 \Delta P} V + \dfrac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P}$ | Constant Pressure | s/m³ | | 4 | Constant P: Slope | $K_p = \dfrac{\mu \alpha \nu}{2 A^2 \Delta P}$ | Constant Pressure | s/m⁶ | | 5 | Constant P: Intercept | $C = \dfrac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P}$ | Constant Pressure | s/m³ | | 6 | Constant P: y-axis | $\dfrac{t}{V}$ vs. $V$ → straight line | Constant Pressure | — | | 7 | Constant Rate: ΔP form | $\Delta P = \dfrac{\mu \alpha \nu q^2 t}{A} + \mu R_m q$ | Constant Rate | Pa | | 8 | Constant Rate: Slope | $K_r = \dfrac{\mu \alpha \nu q^2}{A}$ | Constant Rate | Pa/s | | 9 | Specific Resistance (incomp.) | $\alpha = \dfrac{K_s (1-\varepsilon)}{\varepsilon^3}$ | Incompressible cake | m/kg | | 10 | Specific Resistance (comp.) | $\alpha = \alpha_0 (\Delta P)^s$ | Compressible cake | m/kg | | 11 | Cake Mass per Filtrate | $\nu = \dfrac{\rho_s \cdot \varepsilon}{1-\varepsilon}$ (sometimes) | Slurry analysis | kg/m³ | | 12 | Filtrate Velocity | $q = \dfrac{V}{A \cdot t}$ | Constant Rate | m³/(m²·s) | | 13 | Wash Water Rate | $\left(\dfrac{dV}{dt}\right)_{wash} = \dfrac{1}{4}\left(\dfrac{dV}{dt}\right)_{end}$ (leaf filter) | Washing | m³/s | | 14 | Wash Water Rate | $\left(\dfrac{dV}{dt}\right)_{wash} = \left(\dfrac{dV}{dt}\right)_{end}$ (filter press) | Washing | m³/s | > [!TIP] > **Examination Trick (Always remember):** > - In **Constant Pressure** problems, plot $t/V$ on y-axis and $V$ on x-axis → **straight line** with slope $K_p$ and intercept $C$. > - In **Constant Rate** problems, plot $\Delta P$ on y-axis and $V$ (or $t$) on x-axis → **straight line** with slope involving $\alpha$ and intercept involving $R_m$. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Pharmaceutical Industry**: Constant rate filtration is preferred for sterile filtration to maintain consistent throughput and avoid over-pressing the cake. 2. **Sugar Industry**: Rotary vacuum filters operate at constant pressure (~50 kPa vacuum). 3. **Wastewater Treatment**: Cake filtration is studied with compressible sludges (high $s$ values, $s \approx 0.7$). 4. **Mineral Processing**: Constant pressure filtration of metal hydroxides, tailings. 5. **Beverage Industry**: Constant rate filtration of fruit juices using rotary vacuum drum filters. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The General Filtration Equation (Foundation) ### Starting from Darcy's Law: $$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot (R_c + R_m)}$$ ### Substituting $R_c$: $$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot \left(\dfrac{\alpha \nu V}{A} + R_m\right)}$$ ### Cross-multiplying: $$\mu \left(\frac{\alpha \nu V}{A} + R_m\right) dV = A \cdot \Delta P \cdot dt$$ ### Expanding the bracket: $$\frac{\mu \alpha \nu}{A} \cdot V \cdot dV + \mu R_m \cdot dV = A \cdot \Delta P \cdot dt$$ This is the **General Filtration Equation** in differential form. From here, two integration paths exist: - **Path A (Constant Pressure):** Treat $\Delta P$ as constant, integrate with respect to $V$ and $t$. - **Path B (Constant Rate):** Treat $dV/dt = $ constant, rearrange for $\Delta P$ as a function of $V$ or $t$. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Constant Pressure Filtration Equation **Assumption:** $\Delta P = $ constant throughout the operation, $R_m = $ constant, $\alpha = $ constant (incompressible cake). ### Starting differential form: $$\frac{\mu \alpha \nu}{A} \cdot V \cdot dV + \mu R_m \cdot dV = A \cdot \Delta P \cdot dt$$ ### Integrating from $0 \to V$ and $0 \to t$: $$\frac{\mu \alpha \nu}{A} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2} + \mu R_m \cdot V = A \cdot \Delta P \cdot t$$ ### Dividing both sides by $A \cdot \Delta P \cdot V$: $$\frac{\mu \alpha \nu}{2 A^2 \Delta P} \cdot V + \frac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P} = \frac{t}{V}$$ ### Defining the constants: $$K_p = \frac{\mu \alpha \nu}{2 A^2 \Delta P} \quad \text{(slope constant, s/m}^6\text{)}$$ $$C = \frac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P} \quad \text{(intercept, s/m}^3\text{)}$$ ### Final linear form: $$\boxed{\dfrac{t}{V} = K_p \cdot V + C}$$ This is the **Constant Pressure Filtration Equation** — a linear relationship between $t/V$ and $V$. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation of $K_p$ and $C$:** > - $K_p$ contains the cake-related terms ($\alpha, \nu$) — it characterizes how quickly resistance builds up. > - $C$ contains $R_m$ — it characterizes the initial medium resistance. If $C = 0$, the medium is "ideal" (resistance-less). ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Constant Rate Filtration Equation **Assumption:** $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = q \cdot A = $ constant (i.e., $V = q \cdot A \cdot t$). ### Starting from Darcy's Law, solving for $\Delta P$: $$\Delta P = \mu (R_c + R_m) \cdot \frac{1}{A} \cdot \frac{dV}{dt}$$ ### Substituting $R_c = \alpha \nu V / A$: $$\Delta P = \mu \left(\frac{\alpha \nu V}{A} + R_m\right) \cdot \frac{1}{A} \cdot \frac{dV}{dt}$$ ### Using $dV/dt = q \cdot A$ and $V = q \cdot A \cdot t$: $$\Delta P = \mu \left(\frac{\alpha \nu \cdot q A t}{A} + R_m\right) \cdot q$$ $$\Delta P = \mu \alpha \nu q^2 t + \mu R_m q$$ ### Final linear form: $$\boxed{\Delta P = \left(\mu \alpha \nu q^2\right) \cdot t + \mu R_m q}$$ Or equivalently, in terms of $V$ (using $t = V / (qA)$): $$\Delta P = \frac{\mu \alpha \nu q}{A^2} \cdot V + \mu R_m q$$ Plot of $\Delta P$ vs. $V$ (or $t$) gives a straight line of slope $K_r$ and intercept equal to the initial pressure needed to overcome medium resistance. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Filter Medium Resistance from Constant Pressure Data When $V = 0$, $t/V = C$ theoretically. In practice, we measure $V_0$ — the "equivalent filtrate volume" such that: $$C = \frac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P} = \frac{\mu \alpha \nu}{2 A^2 \Delta P} \cdot V_e$$ Solving for $R_m$: $$R_m = \frac{\alpha \nu V_e}{A}$$ ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — How to Decide Which Equation to Use | Given Information | What to Plot | Slope (m) | Intercept (b) | What to Find | |-------------------|--------------|-----------|---------------|--------------| | $t$ vs. $V$ data at constant $\Delta P$ | $t/V$ vs. $V$ | $K_p = \dfrac{\mu \alpha \nu}{2 A^2 \Delta P}$ | $C = \dfrac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P}$ | $\alpha$ and $R_m$ | | $\Delta P$ vs. $V$ at constant $q$ | $\Delta P$ vs. $V$ | $\dfrac{\mu \alpha \nu q}{A^2}$ | $\mu R_m q$ | $\alpha$ and $R_m$ | | $\Delta P$ vs. $t$ at constant $q$ | $\Delta P$ vs. $t$ | $\mu \alpha \nu q^2$ | $\mu R_m q$ | $\alpha$ and $R_m$ | | $V_e$ from intercept | — | — | $C = K_p \cdot V_e$ | $R_m$ | ## 3.6 Numerical Demonstration (Solved Example) > **Problem (KTU Standard):** > A slurry is filtered at constant pressure of 1.5 × 10⁵ Pa on a filter of area 0.05 m². The volume vs. time data is: > > | V (litres) | t (s) | > |------------|-------| > | 0.5 | 17 | > | 1.0 | 41 | > | 1.5 | 72 | > | 2.0 | 109 | > | 2.5 | 153 | > > The filtrate viscosity is 1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s and $\nu = 100$ kg/m³. Find $\alpha$ and $R_m$. ### Step 1 — Convert to SI and compute $t/V$: | V (m³) | t (s) | t/V (s/m³) | |--------|-------|------------| | 0.0005 | 17 | 34,000 | | 0.0010 | 41 | 41,000 | | 0.0015 | 72 | 48,000 | | 0.0020 | 109 | 54,500 | | 0.0025 | 153 | 61,200 | ### Step 2 — Plot $t/V$ vs. $V$ (linear fit): - Slope $K_p = \dfrac{61{,}200 - 34{,}000}{0.0025 - 0.0005} = \dfrac{27{,}200}{0.0020} = 1.36 \times 10^{10}$ s/m⁶ - Intercept $C \approx 27{,}200$ s/m³ (from extrapolation) ### Step 3 — Solve for $\alpha$: $$K_p = \frac{\mu \alpha \nu}{2 A^2 \Delta P}$$ $$\alpha = \frac{2 A^2 \Delta P \cdot K_p}{\mu \nu} = \frac{2 \cdot (0.05)^2 \cdot (1.5 \times 10^5) \cdot (1.36 \times 10^{10})}{(1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (100)}$$ $$\alpha = \frac{2 \cdot 0.0025 \cdot 1.5 \times 10^5 \cdot 1.36 \times 10^{10}}{0.1}$$ $$\alpha = \frac{1.02 \times 10^{13}}{0.1} = 1.02 \times 10^{14} \text{ m/kg}$$ ### Step 4 — Solve for $R_m$: $$R_m = \frac{C \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu} = \frac{27{,}200 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 1.5 \times 10^5}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$R_m = 2.04 \times 10^{11} \text{ m}^{-1}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Both Modes Matter in Practice:** > The above experiment is typically called a **"leaf test"** or **"pilot filtration test"**. The $\alpha$ and $R_m$ values obtained are then used to **design full-scale filters** — choosing between constant pressure (rotary vacuum) or constant rate (filter press) based on the cake's compressibility and the required throughput. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Filtration Process Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Slurry Feed Tank] --> B[Filtration Mode Selection] B --> C1[Constant Pressure Filter
e.g. Rotary Vacuum Drum] B --> C2[Constant Rate Filter
e.g. Filter Press with Pump] C1 --> D1[Filter Cake buildup
Resistance increases with time] C1 --> E1[Filtrate Flow Rate
decreases exponentially] C2 --> D2[Filter Cake buildup
Resistance increases with time] C2 --> E2[Driving Pressure DeltaP
increases linearly with time] D1 --> F[Cake Washing and Drying] D2 --> F[Cake Washing and Drying] E1 --> G[Plot t by V vs V
Linear with slope Kp and intercept C] E2 --> H[Plot DeltaP vs t or V
Linear with intercept mu Rm q] G --> I1[Evaluate alpha and Rm] H --> I1[Evaluate alpha and Rm] I1 --> J[Full Scale Filter Design] F --> J[Full Scale Filter Design] C1 --> K[Compressible cake: alpha varies with DeltaP] C2 --> K[Compressible cake: alpha varies with DeltaP] K --> I1 ``` ## 4.2 Conceptual Comparison: The Two Operating Modes ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph S1[CONSTANT PRESSURE MODE] direction TB A1[Fixed DeltaP = constant] --> A2[Variable dV by dt: decreases] A2 --> A3[Plot t by V vs V linear] A3 --> A4[Slope Kp contains alpha and nu] A3 --> A5[Intercept C contains Rm] end subgraph S2[CONSTANT RATE MODE] direction TB B1[Fixed dV by dt = qA = constant] --> B2[Variable DeltaP: increases] B2 --> B3[Plot DeltaP vs t or V linear] B3 --> B4[Slope contains alpha and q squared] B3 --> B5[Intercept equals mu Rm q] end ``` ## 4.3 The V–t and P–t Behaviour Comparison (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Start of Filtration Cycle] --> Q1{Operating Mode?} Q1 -->|Constant Pressure| CP[dV by dt decreases
asymptotic curve of V vs t] Q1 -->|Constant Rate| CR[DeltaP increases
linearly with V or t] CP --> CP1[t by V vs V = straight line
with positive slope and intercept] CR --> CR1[DeltaP vs V = straight line
with positive slope and intercept] CP1 --> Result[Slope yields alpha] CR1 --> Result CP1 --> Result2[Intercept yields Rm] CR1 --> Result2 Result --> Final[Full-scale design constants] Result2 --> Final ``` > [!TIP] > **Diagram Reading Tip (Board Exam):** > When the question states *"At constant pressure, V vs. t data is given…"*, immediately convert to $t/V$ vs. $V$ plot. When *"at constant rate"* is given, plot $\Delta P$ vs. $V$ (or $t$). This single recognition step differentiates 7 marks of correct solution from a wasted attempt. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define specific cake resistance. How does it differ for compressible and incompressible filter cakes?** **Model Answer:** **Specific cake resistance ($\alpha$)** is defined as the resistance offered per unit mass of dry cake deposited on the filter medium. It is a property of the solids being filtered, expressed in units of **m/kg**. For an **incompressible cake** (rigid, non-deforming particles such as sand, CaCO₃, diatomite), $\alpha$ is **independent of pressure** and remains a constant throughout filtration: $$\alpha = \alpha_0 = \text{constant}$$ For a **compressible cake** (soft, deformable particles such as biological flocs, metal hydroxide sludges, organic colloids), $\alpha$ varies with the applied pressure drop according to the empirical relation: $$\alpha = \alpha_0 \cdot (\Delta P)^s$$ where $s$ is the **compressibility coefficient** (typically $0.2$ to $0.8$; $s = 0$ for incompressible, $s = 1$ for highly compressible). Compressibility causes the cake to compact at higher pressures, increasing resistance disproportionately. --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **A filter press operates at constant rate. Sketch the variation of (a) $\Delta P$ vs. $V$, and (b) $dV/dt$ vs. $t$. Justify your answer briefly.** **Model Answer:** **(a) $\Delta P$ vs. $V$:** The relationship is a **straight line** with positive slope and a positive intercept (not zero). This arises from the constant rate equation: $$\Delta P = \frac{\mu \alpha \nu q}{A^2} \cdot V + \mu R_m q$$ At $V = 0$, $\Delta P = \mu R_m q > 0$, hence the intercept is non-zero. The slope $\mu \alpha \nu q / A^2$ is positive because $\Delta P$ must increase to maintain the constant rate as cake builds up. **(b) $dV/dt$ vs. $t$:** Since the filter is operating at **constant rate**, $dV/dt = $ **constant** (a horizontal straight line parallel to the time axis). By definition of constant rate operation, the throughput does not change with time; only $\Delta P$ increases to overcome the growing cake resistance. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Note (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** *Internal choice between two questions, each with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Full solutions are given below for both alternatives.* ### Question A (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set A]`** **(a) Starting from Darcy's Law, derive the constant pressure filtration equation and explain the significance of the slope and intercept of the $t/V$ vs. $V$ plot.
